sections.en: 3.12.1 Process Overview When performing the confirmation step against a production order, whether it’s for an entire order or a milestone confirmation, the system makes actual data updates of the following objects:  Yield  Scrap  Rework Depending on the settings already made, when confirming an order, the system updates the following data in the production order:  Quantities (yield, scrap, rework), activities, dates, and order status  Backflush of the components  Automatic GR  Reduction of the capacity of the relevant work centers  Costs based on actual data entered during confirmation  Excess/shortage quantities for MRP-relevant materials On the configuration side of confirmation, several checks and controls can be incorpo- rated during the confirmation, including but not limited to the following:  Time when the system should backflush components  Checking the operations sequence 3 Discrete Manufacturing Configuration 118  Checking underdelivery and overdelivery quantity tolerances  Checking whether QM in-process results (during production) are entered for an operation  GR control for coproducts  Actual cost of error handling during confirmation  Quantity (goods movements) of error handling during confirmation It’s also possible to control the screen layout of the confirmation to suit any unique business needs. You can even place controls on the fields for which data entry is required, optional, or hidden. See Section 3.12.3 for how to manage this. Now let’s discuss the specifics of confirmation.

sections.title: Parameters for Order Confirmation
sections.en: 3.12.2 Parameters for Order Confirmation To set up parameters for order confirmation, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Confirmation  Define Confirmation Parameters. For Plant 1710 and Order Type PP10 (see Figure 3.42), the Process Control field controls when the system should perform some or all of the following steps:  Goods movements, such as backflush  Automatic GR  Actual costs calculation Figure 3.42 Generally Valid Settings Tab of Confirmation Parameters © 119 3.12 Confirmation If the Process Control field is left blank, then the system performs all activities online and immediately. Refer to Section 3.12.4 for how to control the settings as online, immediate, or in the background. The Checks area controls whether the system should ensure that the user adheres to the Operation Sequence, with varying degrees of controls, from no check to warning to error. Similarly, if the minimum and maximum tolerance limits in the Underdelivery and Overdelivery fields are maintained in the Work Scheduling view of the material master, the system copies this information during production order creation. When users are performing confirmation of the order and entering the yield, the system checks to see if it’s within the tolerance limits and acts according to the level of control set. If there is an error message, the system won’t allow users to proceed further or save the confirmation until the error is corrected. The system considers the sum of yield and rework quantities for underdelivery/overdelivery calculation. Note During the SAP S/4HANA implementation project, you should discuss and come to agreement with the client on the level of control required and then make the necessary settings. In general, the control should be neither too loose nor too stringent to meet the day-to-day working needs of business process owners. The level of control also depends on the stability of production processes and business maturity to reflect that optimum production should be within the defined tolerance limits. If there are large variations and deviations in yield, then a less stringent control will suffice. Tips and Tricks If the underdelivery or overdelivery control is set as an error setting, then the system won’t allow the production supervisor to exceed the defined tolerance limits. To attend to this, you should make changes in the Goods Receipt tab of the production order and perform the confirmation process again. This enables the user to deviate from a one- time or limited deviation to the standard tolerance limits. In the Results Rec. (QM) field and for in-process (during production) inspection, the sys- tem creates a quality inspection lot for one or more operations. This is an integration point between PP and QM processes. It’s possible to control whether the system should check to see if the inspection results of an operation for the inspection lot are already entered before it allows users to perform the confirmation of the next operation. In the Goods Movements area of the screen, you should check the box to set the neces- sary controls for whether the system should perform goods movement for all of the components. 3 Discrete Manufacturing Configuration 120 Choose the Individual Entry General tab, and the screen shown in Figure 3.43 will appear. Here, select the Confirmation Type that the system makes available during the confirmation process. It can be a Partial confirmation, Final confirmation, or Automatic final confirmation. Figure 3.43 Individual Entry General Tab of Confirmation Parameters The settings depend on how frequently the business performs the confirmation pro- cess. If there are large and frequent numbers of confirmations against a production order, then it makes sense to set Partial confirmation as the default. When performing the final confirmation against the production order, you should manually set the Con- firmation Type to Final confirmation. For Automatic final confirmation, the system checks whether the confirmed quantity is equal to or less than the production order quantity. If it’s less, then the system automatically sets the status to Partial confirma- tion, whereas if the confirmation quantity is equal to or greater than the production order quantity, then it automatically sets the status to Final confirmation. For Actual Costs and Goods Movements in the Error Handling/Logs area, you should set whether the system should terminate the confirmation processing until the error is corrected. Choose the Indiv. Entry of Operation w. Init. Screen tab, and the screen shown in Figure 3.44 will appear. The system offers a large number of checkboxes to facilitate data entry work. For example, if you check the Propose box in the Quantities area, the system will © 121 3.12 Confirmation propose the quantity that a production supervisor needs to confirm for an operation or an order. Check the Display boxes to display any information for reference purposes. We suggest that you click the Actual Data radio button in the Screen Control Time Ticket area so that the system will automatically propose the data (quantity and activi- ties) that needs to be confirmed. Otherwise, on the actual confirmation screen, you can manually click the Actual Data button to view the data that needs to be confirmed. Figure 3.44 Indiv. Entry of Operation w. Init. Screen Tab of Confirmation Parameters

sections.title: Single Entry Screen for Confirmation
sections.en: 3.12.3 Single Entry Screen for Confirmation It’s also possible to control how the system displays the information on the confirma- tion screen (use Transaction CO11N for discrete manufacturing or Transaction COR6N for process industries). In this step, you set up a profile and assign that profile to an individual user in the CORUPROF user parameter. A profile for a single-entry screen controls the user-defined layout for confirmation. In the layout, you define the fields and the sequence in which they appear on the confir- mation screen. For example, if the production supervisor likes to have a provision to enter long text in the confirmation screen to enable the process operator to write extensive shift details, you select the relevant option. Similarly, you can set a control for whether the system should list the activities to be confirmed as individual fields or in a tabular form. To define a single-entry screen for confirmation, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Confirmation  Single Screen Entry  Define Single Screen Entry or use Transaction OPK0. On the screen shown in Figure 3.45, you define the header area so that during the actual confirmation, the system will bring up the relevant fields so the user can enter the details. For example, choose whether the system should only allow 3 Discrete Manufacturing Configuration 122 the user to enter the confirmation number at the header level or should allow the user to enter the production order number together with its operation. Tips and Tricks At any time, to see how the chosen option will look on the confirmation screen, you can choose Display Detail Areas (the glasses icon), and the system will show you a preview. When choosing the dropdown list options in the Area section (see Figure 3.45), you can select from a large number of options, such as quantities, activities, HR data, short and long text entry provision, dates, and even external IDs if a third-party product is used to integrate the confirmation process with the production process. You can also choose the sequence in which these details appear on the confirmation screen. Figure 3.45 Single-Entry Screen of Confirmation In the Pushbutton Text fields, you can define the text of the pushbuttons to suit your business needs. Checking the Closed box enables a minimized (closed) view of the information on the confirmation screen, which can be opened when the production supervisor is ready for data entry. Tips and Tricks To check that settings are correct, with no duplication or other layout error, you can click the Detail Areas button to make a consistency check. Also, you can click the Pre- view button to simulate the confirmation screen. © 123 3.12 Confirmation Note You can activate the CONFPP07 enhancement if there’s a need for more than one reason for variances. As a practical workaround (without activating the enhancement), on the screen shown in Figure 3.45 under Detail Areas, you can add an option that says “Addi- tional data: Reason” as many times as needed from the dropdown list.

sections.title: Time of Confirmation
sections.en: 3.12.4 Time of Confirmation If there is a large number of confirmations that the production supervisor needs to per- form on a daily basis, then it’s practical to perform confirmations (such as quantity updates or backflushes) in the background, when there is less of a workload on the sys- tem. However, if an immediate update of all quantity and activity data posted is required, then you should make the necessary settings. These are the same settings used for the Process Control field at the beginning of Section 3.12.2. To set up the time of confirmation, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Confirmation  Single Screen Entry  Define Time for Confirmation Processes or use Transaction OPKC. On the screen shown in Figure 3.46, choose when the system should update the following:  Auto GR (automatic GR)  Backflush  Actual Costs Figure 3.46 Process Control for Confirmation The available update options are listed here:  Immediately Online  Immediately in Update Program  Later in Background Job We’ll cover how to set up background jobs in Section 3.16. 3 Discrete Manufacturing Configuration 124

sections.title: Reasons for Variances
sections.en: 3.13 Reasons for Variances During confirmation against the production order, when the yield, scrap, and rework is recorded, the user can assign a reason for any variance between planned and actual production. For example, assume that due to machine malfunction or defective com- ponents, the actual production was less than planned. In that case, the user can assign the specific reason for this deviation or variance. Similarly, when entering the scrap quantity, the user can give the reason for the variance. Later, when executing the pro- duction order information system (Transaction COOIS) or even when using embedded SAP Fiori analytics, the user can filter out the information based on the reason for vari- ance. This helps clarify the reasons behind the problems and helps the production supervisor take corrective or preventive actions. As a PP consultant during an SAP S/4HANA implementation project, you should main- tain a complete list of plant-related reasons for variances and configure them accord- ingly in the system. You should also perform multiple partial confirmations and enter different reasons for variance for each deviation. Note If there’s a business need to enter multiple reasons for variances, you should engage an ABAP resource and activate the CONFPP07 enhancement. You can also assign a user status to a reason for variance to add further control in the confirmation. Note To set up a new status profile, use Transaction BS02. Figure 3.47 Reasons for Variance © 125

sections.title: Defining Print Control
sections.en: 3.15 Defining Print Control To configure a new reason for the variance for a given plant, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Confirmation  Define Reasons for Vari- ances or use Transaction OPK5. Figure 3.47 lists the reasons for variance for Plant 1710. Once you select a specific reason for variance and click the User Status folder, the system prompts you to enter the status profile and then the user status.

sections.title: Trigger Points
sections.en: 3.14 Trigger Points Trigger points are automatic functions that the system can perform when it’s able to meet a user-defined condition. For example, when a user releases an operation, the sys- tem can automatically release all preceding operations via a trigger point. Minimal configuration steps are involved in trigger points: you simply define the trigger point usage and then define a group for the trigger points. The Trigger Point Usage field provides a unique identification to list trigger points that will have a common usage. This enables a production supervisor to quickly select the relevant trigger point based on its usage. To define the (standard) trigger points usage, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Master Data  Trigger Point  Define (Standard) Trigger Point Usage. On the initial screen, enter an alphanu- meric key and give a short description of the trigger point usage. You can group all of the trigger points together and then assign them to an operation. Doing so saves you the time and effort involved in individually creating and assigning trigger points. To define a group for standard trigger points, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Master Data  Trigger Point  Define Group for Standard Trigger Points. On the initial screen, enter an alphanumeric key and give a short description of the trigger points group. 3.15 Defining Print Control During the production process, there’s often a business need to print several shop floor papers—for example, a production order, a pick list, and a GI slip. The SAP S/4HANA system offers standard layouts for each shop floor paper. During an SAP S/4HANA sys- tem project implementation, you should engage an ABAP resource to develop new scripts or modify the scripts of the shop floor papers to fulfill a client’s requirements. We recommend that you to discuss and evaluate all of the shop floor papers needed as printouts with the client. For example, you can eliminate the layout development of a confirmation and its printout requirement if there’s an SAP S/4HANA system terminal from which to make live data entries, or you can use an upload program to automati- cally enter the confirmation. Furthermore, if Adobe Document Services is installed, then you can also create PDF printouts of the shop floor papers. 3 Discrete Manufacturing Configuration 126 To make the necessary settings to print shop floor papers, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Define Print Controls or use Transaction OPK8. The resulting screen, shown in Figure 3.48, lists the shop floor papers that you can individually work on and for which you can make the desired settings. Figure 3.48 Printing Shop Floor Papers Note Chapter 6 shows how we used list LG01 (Object list), shown on the right-hand side of Figure 3.48, to get printouts with the SAP S/4HANA system standard layout.

sections.title: Background Jobs
sections.en: 3.16 Background Jobs To optimize the SAP system’s resources, which will help to improve performance and minimize undue burdens, there are several processes that you can set up as back- ground jobs. A background job enables you to define the frequency with which the sys- tem automatically performs the desired function without manual intervention. For example, if MRP is run on a weekly basis and planners are generally satisfied with the results, then you can set up a background job to automatically convert planned orders into production orders. To set up a background job, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Schedule Background Jobs or use Transaction SM36. We suggest that you choose the Job Wizard icon and use the wizard because it provides a step-by-step approach for setting up a background job. Table 3.1 lists the program names of selected background jobs that you can set up, and the system asks for this information. Before setting up the background job, you should also set up the variant of the background job so that the system processes only the relevant data. © 127

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 3.17 Summary Tips and Tricks On the initial screen of Transaction SE38, you should enter a program name listed in Table 3.1 and choose Execute. The system then navigates to the screen where you can set screen parameters and save them by pressing (Ctrl)+(S) or choosing Goto  Vari- ants  Save as Variant. You can read additional documentation on the background program by choosing the Information icon, where available. 3.17 Summary The logical and sequential configuration steps that we undertook in this chapter will enable a production planner and production supervisor to perform the same set of steps and activities in configuration steps in process manufacturing. If they follow all of the steps covered in this chapter in a sequential and logical order, then all of the major business processes and scenarios of production involved in production orders (shop floor control) will be covered. See Chapter 6 for more details that will align with the business processes. The next chapter covers process manufacturing configuration. Functions Program Names Convert planned orders into production orders PPBICO40 Print production orders PPPRBTCH Print shop floor papers PPPRBSEL Mass processing COWORKDISPATCHNEW Confirmation: Componentized processes (GR, GI, HR, actual) CORUPROC Confirmation: Fast entry records CORUPROC1 Table 3.1 Background Jobs ©

sections.title: Master Data in Process Industries
sections.en: 4.1 Master Data in Process Industries The master data in process manufacturing is similar to the master data in discrete man- ufacturing. For detailed configuration settings and business processes, refer to Chapter 3 and Chapter 6, respectively. The following master data are specific to process industries and will be the main topics covered in this section:  Resource  Materials list (BOM)  Master recipe  Production version The resource in process manufacturing is similar to the work center in discrete manu- facturing, and the creation of the materials list in process manufacturing is similar to the creation of the BOM in discrete manufacturing, so we won’t cover these master data items here (see Chapter 3 and Chapter 6 for those). For process industries, it’s man- datory to have a master recipe and a production version in the system. While the con- figuration of the master recipe has some uniqueness and is covered in this chapter, the creation of the production version is once again the same for discrete manufacturing and therefore isn’t covered here.

sections.title: Master Recipe Profile
sections.en: 4.1.1 Master Recipe Profile Although most of the details in the master recipe profile are the same as in routing, the one exception is the option available for process management. Depending on the mas- ter recipe profile used during master recipe creation, the system offers the following options for process management:  Process Instructions  XSTEPS  XSTEPS OPTIONAL © 131 4.1 Master Data in Process Industries If you choose the XSTEPS OPTIONAL option when creating the master recipe, then the system provides you with the option to select either Process Instructions or XSTEPS. You cannot use both of them at the same time. In addition, if you use process instruc- tions when creating the master recipe and the company later decides that it wants to transition to XSteps, the system will issue a warning message saying that it won’t be possible to revert back to process instructions after saving the master recipe with XSteps activated. To set up the master recipe profile, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Master Data  Master Recipe  Settings for the Task List Type  Define Master Recipe Profiles with Default Values or use Transaction OPN1. On the initial screen, choose Profile/Recipe, which brings up the overview of the master recipe profile with three entries. Select Profile PI01_XS and choose the Details icon, which leads you to the screen shown in Figure 4.1. Here, you can update the available fields, which then apply when a master recipe is created. Assigning a profile is a mandatory requirement when creating a mas- ter recipe. Notice the Process instruction maintenance dropdown list in the Process management area and the available options, including XSTEPS OPTIONAL. Figure 4.1 Master Recipe Profile 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 132

sections.title: Task List Assignment to Material Types
sections.en: 4.1.2 Task List Assignment to Material Types Task list type 2 is used for a master recipe. When a process order is created, the system looks for the relevant master recipe and for task list type 2 to assign it to the process order. A task list consists of operations, phases, activities, control keys, and the relation- ship to the BOM. If a company has material types other than those available in a stan- dard SAP S/4HANA system, then you should maintain the task list relationship for each material type. To define the task list assignment to the material type, follow menu path SAP IMG  Pro- duction Planning for Process Industries  Master Data  Master Recipe  Settings for the Task List Type  Assign Material Types. On the screen shown in Figure 4.2, choose New Entries to define the task list type (TLType) as 2 and the company-specific material types used in process manufacturing. Figure 4.2 Task List Assignment with Material Type

sections.title: Task List Status
sections.en: 4.1.3 Task List Status The standard SAP S/4HANA system offers task list statuses to cater to various business scenarios. For example, when creating a master recipe of a material in the initial stages, you set the status to 1, which is for Creation. The system won’t make this task list avail- able for selection during process order creation or in production version creation. You can configure a task list that’s specific to product cost controlling, and in that case, it becomes available for costing purposes but not for production. To define a task list status at the header level, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Master Data  Master Recipe  Data for the Recipe Header  Define Recipe Status or use Transaction OP46. Figure 4.3 shows that task list Status 4 enables a user to perform all of the steps, such as using it in production (RelInd), using it for costing (Cstng), and performing consistency checks (Cons.chk.). You can perform a consistency check during master recipe creation to enable the system to point out any deviations or shortcomings in the master recipe that can be dealt with before saving. You can even save a master recipe if the system issues warning or error messages that occur during consistency checks, and then, you can correct the errors later on. © 133

sections.title: Order Type–Dependent Parameters
sections.en: 4.2 Order Type–Dependent Parameters Figure 4.3 Task List Status 4.2 Order Type–Dependent Parameters For better control of various order types configured in the SAP S/4HANA system, you can create different order types within a plant. For example, there may be one order type for finished goods, another for semifinished goods, and a third one to capture rework details from normal production. Each order type can have a separate number range and its own order type settings. Furthermore, you can control authorizations to users based on the order type and plant combination. In order type–dependent param- eters, you should make settings that are applicable each time a user creates a process order for that specific order type and plant combination. To set up order type–dependent parameters, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Order  Master Data  Order  Define Order Type-Dependent Parameters or use Transaction COR4. On the initial screen, select Plant 1710 and Order Type PI01, which leads you to the Master Data tab. We’ll discuss each of the tabs for configuring parameters in the following sections.

sections.title: Master Data
sections.en: 4.2.1 Master Data Figure 4.4 shows the Master Data tab of order type–dependent parameters. Here, you select Automatic Selection of Production Version from the Production Version drop- down list, and the system assigns the first available and valid production version during the planning and production execution steps. Alternatively, you can use the Manual Selection of Production Version option, and the system will open a popup during pro- cess order creation to enable the user to select a production version manually. If you check the Approval Required box, then the process order must be approved before it can be released. When a user creates a process order, the system will require the order to be approved before it will allow the user to release it. Similarly, if you set up the automatic process order release during creation (in the production scheduling pro- file), the system will overrule the automatic release settings if the approval process is set up (see Section 4.3). 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 134 Figure 4.4 Order Type–Dependent Parameters: Master Data Tab Note To grant approval to a process order during its creation, follow menu path Process Order  Functions  Approval  Individual Approval.

sections.title: Planning
sections.en: 4.2.2 Planning In the Planning tab, check the Scrap box if a business process has a need for it (see Figure 4.5). Normally, in process industries, there’s less need to use or record the scrap option. For example, in the fertilizer industry, any visible or overflow material (spilled-over material) is automatically sent back in the production process chain, so there is no scrap recording during production. However, in the steel rerolling industry, it’s com- mon to have production scrap, so the Scrap option needs to be activated in such busi- ness scenarios. For a PP-QM integration scenario, you assign the relevant inspection type for in-process inspection in the Planning tab and make settings for batch determi- nation. Chapter 10 covers batch management. © 135 4.2 Order Type–Dependent Parameters Figure 4.5 Planning Tab

sections.title: Implementation
sections.en: 4.2.3 Implementation In the Implementation tab, make sure to check all the relevant boxes, especially in the Shop Floor Information System and Documentation of Goods Movement areas (see Figure 4.6). Checking these boxes will enable the system to update the LIS, and the user will be able to see the documented goods movements (GI or GR) for the process orders. The documented goods movements in the process order information system (Transac- tion COOISPI) also will show all the relevant details. Figure 4.6 Implementation Tab 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 136

sections.title: Cost Accounting
sections.en: 4.2.4 Cost Accounting In the Cost Accounting tab, we suggest that you coordinate with a controlling resource (who is responsible for cost object controlling) to ensure that the necessary configura- tion settings are in place.

sections.title: Production Scheduling Profile
sections.en: 4.3 Production Scheduling Profile The production scheduling profile in process manufacturing is similar to that in dis- crete manufacturing. However, in process manufacturing, it provides an option to automatically generate the control recipe when a process order is released. A control recipe enables the process operator to record important plant parameters. We cover the details of control recipes in Section 4.4.1. To set up the production scheduling profile, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Order  Master Data  Define Production Sched- uling Profile or use Transaction CORY (see Figure 4.7). On the initial screen, select Plant 1710 and PS Profile (production scheduling profile) YB0002. In this example, the Generate Cntrl Recipe box in the On Release area (within the Automatic Actions area) isn’t checked. In Chapter 7, we’ll show you how to manually generate a control recipe when a process order is released. Figure 4.7 Production Scheduling Profile © 137

sections.title: Process Management
sections.en: 4.4 Process Management Using Transaction MM02 to change the material master, you assign the production scheduling profile in the Work scheduling tab (see Figure 4.8). Whenever a process order of this material and plant combination is created, the system automatically trig- gers the settings made in the production scheduling profile. Figure 4.8 Production Scheduling Profile in Material Master 4.4 Process Management Process management falls within the purview of PP-PI, which covers all the important elements involved in recording the process parameter information and performing specific functions. Creating a process order forms the basis of the generation of the control recipe (see Figure 4.9). Figure 4.9 Process Management Overview Process Planning Process Order Further Message Destinations Process Management Process Control Execution E-1 E-2 E-3 SAP S/4HANA or External System Control Recipe Process Messages PI Sheet Process Control System 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 138 The control recipe is sent to the process control in the form of a PI sheet, and the pro- cess operator not only follows the instructions given on the PI sheet but also fills in the PI sheet with relevant plant parameters and other important data. The process opera- tor then returns the PI sheet as a process message to either the SAP S/4HANA system or an external system. Although this section applies more to the business process side of process manage- ment, it’s important for you to gain a basic understanding of the concepts and termi- nologies used in the rest of the chapter before we move on to discussing some standard settings and tools.

sections.title: Key Terminology
sections.en: 4.4.1 Key Terminology The important terms used in process management are as follows:  Control recipe destination The control recipe destination specifies the technical address to which a control rec- ipe is transferred. An operation in a master recipe can have several phases, and each phase can have a different control recipe destination. In the process order, one con- trol recipe is created for each control recipe destination. Therefore, if there are three different phases, it’s possible to have three different control recipe destinations (e.g., mixing, blending, liquid ammonia sections). Typically, at the process order release, the system creates control recipes for each control recipe destination in the process order. All phases of a process order with the same control recipe destination are combined into the same control recipe. A control recipe destination defines whether the process instruction of a phase is transferred to an external system (a process control system) or is converted to a PI sheet for a line operator. It also specifies which system or line operator executes the process instructions of a phase.  Process instruction characteristic A released characteristic within a characteristic group is available for use in process instruction. Process instruction characteristics are allocated to process instructions either directly in the master recipe or by process instruction category. Process instruction, together with the corresponding characteristic value, determines the information transferred or requested—that is, the status of the control recipe. It also determines how the requested data is processed (e.g., the message category to be used to report the data).  Process instruction category The process instruction category specifies the type of process instruction (i.e., pro- cess parameter or process data request). It also specifies the characteristics of this process instruction—that is, information to be sent via this instruction.  Process message characteristic A released characteristic within the characteristic group is available for use in process messages. Process message characteristics are allocated to process message © 139 4.4 Process Management categories. Process message characteristics, together with the corresponding charac- teristic values, determine the content of the process message. The process message can be sent to an external system, SAP Office mail recipients, or custom-defined ABAP tables.  Process message category The process message category specifies the characteristics to be allocated to a pro- cess message—that is, the information to be sent. It also specifies the message desti- nations for the process message. Every process message sent via process manage- ment must be assigned to a process message category.  PI sheet A PI sheet is used as an input and output form for plant parameters and is customiz- able according to individual production processes. This customized PI sheet is part of either the configuration activity or master data management. Due to its intuitive features, a PI sheet can be used for many functions, such as calling up a transaction while remaining in a PI sheet, displaying documents, recording shift highlights, posting GI/GR, undertaking confirmation of a phase, calculating results, and jump- ing to record results for quality inspections. To create a custom process message or a custom process instruction, a user can lever- age knowledge of and experience in creating characteristics in the classification sys- tem. The only difference between classification and process message/instruction cre- ation is that the former doesn’t offer the option to incorporate details specific to pro- cess management. In the example in the following section, we’ll show you how to use process instruction to fetch the Process Order Start Date field from the process order automatically and integrate it into the PI sheet. Now, let’s discuss the important one-time settings to make in the SAP S/4HANA system to activate process management. This entails a sequence of steps in which the standard settings delivered by the SAP S/4HANA system are copied to specific plants.

sections.title: Standard Settings and Tools
sections.en: 4.4.2 Standard Settings and Tools The system offers a large number of standard process messages, process message cate- gories, process instructions, and process instruction categories. These are all available in the SAP S/4HANA system’s plant 0001. You can copy all of these standard settings from plant 0001, except for process instruction categories, which you’ll need to copy manually. An easier way to do this is to copy the SAP S/4HANA system reference plant 0001 to the desired plants. You’ll need to perform a few important steps in the right sequence to ensure that min- imal work and effort are involved in integrating process management in the business processes. Use menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Pro- cess Management  Standard Settings, as shown in Figure 4.10. 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 140 Figure 4.10 Standard Settings in Process Management Next, follow these steps: 1. Use Transaction O23C to transport predefined characteristics from the SAP system reference client, which is 0001, to the SAP S/4HANA system client. The Copy Object Lists screen appears when you successfully perform the first step. 2. Access Transaction O22C to transport preconfigured process message categories from the SAP S/4HANA system reference plant 0001 to the desired plants. The screen shown in Figure 4.11 appears after you enter the target plant. 3. Enter the target plant (in this example, “1710”). Use the Copy icon to copy it to the tar- get plant. The system will then issue a warning message: The SAP standard settings are copied to your target plant. Objects with identical names are overwritten. 4. When the Do You Want to Copy? message appears, choose Yes. 5. Access Transaction CO60_VM to transport the PI sheet’s display variants from the SAP S/4HANA reference plant to the login client. Make sure to select all available dis- play variants and then choose Save before exiting the screen. Figure 4.11 Predefined Objects of Process Messages The SAP S/4HANA system also offers standard characteristics groups in process management, both for process instructions and for process messages. The system groups the process instruction characteristics in group PPPI_01, and it groups the © 141

sections.title: Process Messages
sections.en: 4.5 Process Messages process message characteristics in group PPPI_02. When you’re creating a self-defined process instruction or process message characteristic, ensure that you assign it to the relevant characteristics group to enable it to work correctly. Tips and Tricks To ensure that you see the characteristics groups (PPPI_01 and PPPI_02) in the drop- down list, on the main screen, select More  Interaction Design  Visualization1 and then choose Show keys within the dropdown lists. When implementing process management, ensure that you release the process instruction and process message groups to the requisite plants. To release a process messages group, use menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Process Messages  Define Characteristics Groups for Process Messages  Release for Process Messages or use Transaction O08C. To release a process instructions group, use menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Process Instructions  Define Characteristics Groups for Process Instructions  Release for Process Instructions or use Transaction O09C. If the process messages or process instruction groups aren’t defined, available, or released for the desired plant, simply copy them with reference to another plant, where these are available and released. To copy all settings from reference plant 0001 to the desired plant, use menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Tools  Copy Settings between Plants or use Transaction O20C. In the ensuing screen, enter the ref- erence plant (which is “0001”) and the target plant to copy the standard system’s set- tings (“1710” in this example). Tips and Tricks If you’re using a new SAP client for the first time (e.g., production system [PRD]), then there’s a possibility that the process management won’t work. If that happens, just run Transaction O23C and then Transaction O22C once (in that order), and that should solve the problem. Alternatively, make it a part of the SAP S/4HANA go-live checklist to run these two trans- actions in the PRD system before starting any PP-related data entry into the system. 4.5 Process Messages Process messages enable the process operator to send the requested data back to the SAP S/4HANA system, which then performs the necessary functions. For example, say a production supervisor wants the process operator to inform them (through process messages) about the consumption (GI) of all the materials used in the production pro- cess (against a process order). The process operator fills out the PI sheet, which contains 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 142 the provision to record actual material consumption details, and marks the status of the PI sheet as Complete. The process operator then sends the process message con- taining the consumption details to the process message destination in the SAP S/4HANA system, after which, the system will perform the actual GI posting against the process order in the system. The system offers many standard process messages and process message categories, but it’s also possible to define your own process messages to attend to specific business needs. For example, there might be some specific information that the system should send back to the SAP S/4HANA system based on the process operator’s input on the PI sheet. In the following sections, we’ll go through the details of how to create or define and set up a process message. The first step is to create a process message characteristic.

sections.title: Creating a Process Message Characteristic
sections.en: 4.5.1 Creating a Process Message Characteristic The first step is to follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Indus- tries  Process Management  Process Messages  Define Characteristics for Process Mes- sages  Create Characteristics for Process Messages or use Transaction O25C. For this example, you should create process message characteristic ZPI_Creation_Date and define the characteristics group (in the Chars Group field) as Process Message Char- acteristic in the Basic Data tab. Define the Data Type as Date. In Chapter 7, we’ll show you how to use this self-defined process message to send details back to the SAP S/4HANA system.

sections.title: Process Message Destination
sections.en: 4.5.2 Process Message Destination To create a new process message destination, use menu path Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Process Messages  Process Messages Desti- nations  Define and Set up Process Messages Destinations or use Transaction O03C. For this example, you should create a new Destination PP20 process message for Plant 1710 and then choose Target Fields/Message destinations on the left-hand side of the screen. The Change View screen shown in Figure 4.12 will appear, and there, the system will allow you to enter the process message characteristic “ZPI_CREATION_DATE,” which was created in the previous step. It’s possible to set a self-defined process message destination so that the system can send process messages to the defined destination. When the system receives informa- tion from process messages, it processes them in the SAP S/4HANA system using func- tion modules. For external systems, process messages with information are sent back to the SAP S/4HANA system using remote function call (RFC) connections. Check the Individual box (not shown) if the system should process each process message individ- ually. This option is generally used in complex process messages involving function module destinations. © 143 4.5 Process Messages Figure 4.12 Target Field in Process Message Destination Note We suggest that you engage an SAP NetWeaver resource to manage the configuration of RFC destinations.

sections.title: Process Message Categories
sections.en: 4.5.3 Process Message Categories In process management, the system first sends the control recipe to the control recipe destination as a manual or automatic (background) job. The plant operator receives the control recipe in the form of a PI sheet and fills in the requisite data. When the data entry is complete, the process operator sets the status of the PI sheet as Complete. This enables the system to communicate the data back into the SAP S/4HANA system in the form of process messages, which are instructions to proceed with performing the nec- essary functions, such as data entry or data updates. The system embeds the process message category within the process instruction cate- gory. For example, the system embeds the PI_PROD process message category within the APROD_1 process instruction category. The APROD_1 process instruction category enables a process operator to record the production and also perform GR against the process order through the PI_PROD process message category. When a user creates a process order, generates a control recipe, sends it to the control recipe destination, maintains the PI sheet, and sets the status to Complete, the system processes the pro- cess messages by sending the completed data (such as quantity produced or GI) to the SAP system, which then process the data using function modules or Business Applica- tion Programming Interfaces (BAPIs). There are several process message categories that cater to different business scenarios, such as the material consumption message, messages about the control recipe status, confirmation of a phase, or even new batch creation messages (provided that batch management is already active for the material). 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 144 Figure 4.13 shows the list of selected and standard process message categories available in the SAP S/4HANA system (Transaction O13C). The system automatically assigns the relevant process message category in the process instruction category in the standard offering. For self-defined process message and process instruction categories, you should ensure that you incorporate the relevant process message category within the process instruction category. Figure 4.13 Overview of Process Message Categories The screen shown in Figure 4.14 appears when you select the PI_CONS process message category and you double-click Destinations/Message categories. On the right-hand side of the screen, the system assigns destination (Dest) PI04, to which it will send the process messages. It’s also possible to map the source and target characteristics to be sent to specific destinations. Figure 4.14 Process Message Categories To view the list of standard process message destinations available in the SAP S/4HANA system, place the cursor on the Dest field and press (F4). © 145 4.5 Process Messages The screen shown in Figure 4.15 appears when you select PI_CONS in the ProcMessage Cat field and you select PI04 Goods issue posting in the Destination field. Figure 4.15 Process Message Category PP_CONS and Destination PP04 On the right-hand side of the screen, the system maps each process instruction charac- teristic (source characteristic) with the process message characteristics (target charac- teristic/field). The Characteristic column lists all of the process instruction characteris- tics, whereas the Target Field column enables mapping of each process instruction characteristic to its respective process message characteristic. Now, let’s walk through the entire set of activities involved in process messages with an example. The example in Figure 4.16 defines the PP20 process message category. Figure 4.16 Process Message Category PP20 for Plant 1710 Follow these steps to set up process message categories: 1. Select PP20 and click Characteristics/Message categories to open the screen shown in Figure 4.17. Now, you can see PP20 in the ProcMessage Cat field and 1710 in the Plant field. 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 146 Figure 4.17 Process Instructions Characteristic in Process Message Category 2. Choose Characteristics/Message categories on the left-hand side of the screen, and then, on the right-hand side, enter “ZPI_CREATION_DATE” for the process instruc- tion Characteristic. 3. Check the Req box to denote that it’s required (mandatory) to enter details in this field. 4. Double-click Destinations/Message categories on the left-hand side, and the screen shown in Figure 4.18 will appear. Incorporate the Destination PP20 (Chem - Mixing) process message. Figure 4.18 Process Message Destination in Process Message Category 5. Select Destination PP20 and choose Characteristics/destination-specific target fields on the left-hand side. On the right-hand side of this screen, perform one-to- one mapping of the process instruction characteristic with the process message characteristic for the Destination PP20 process message. For this example, map the ZPI_CREATION_DATE process instruction characteristic with the ZPI_PI_ CREATION_ DATE process message characteristic. © 147

sections.title: Process Instruction Categories
sections.en: 4.6 Process Instruction Categories 4.6 Process Instruction Categories A process instruction category consists of different process instruction types. In the master recipe, you have to assign the process instruction category, which then auto- matically incorporates the different process instruction types assigned to the process instruction category. In the following section, we deliberately took a longer route to explain the various pro- cess instruction types in order to cover the concepts and fundamentals involved in each type. Toward the end of this section, we also show you how to use a wizard to cre- ate any of the seven different process instruction categories for various business sce- narios.

sections.title: Process Instruction Types
sections.en: 4.6.1 Process Instruction Types When you define a process instruction category, it’s mandatory to assign a process instruction type to enable the system to process the data accordingly. Eight different process instruction types are available:  Process parameters  Process data requests  Process message subscriptions  Calculations  Inspection results requests  Dynamic function calls  Sequence definitions  Universal Table 4.1 summarizes the different process instruction types, their descriptions, and their usages, which we explain in detail later. Universal isn’t included here because it applies to all types. We discuss each of them in the following sections. Process Instruction Type Process Instruction Type Description Usage in PI Sheets (Types 1 and 4) Usage in External Process Control System (Types 2 and 3) Sending Process Parameter Details Process Message Requests to Process Message Destinations 1 Process parameters    2 Process data requests    3 Process message subscriptions   Table 4.1 Process Instruction Categories 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 148 Process Instruction Type 1: Process Parameters Process instruction type 1 is the simplest of all because it only requires the user to enter parameter values. In the PI sheet, there is a label for the process parameter, which is where the user enters the parameter value. Figure 4.19 shows the screen for process instruction category AMAT_1 for process instruction type 1. If this process instruction type is used in the process order (or in the master recipe), the system automatically fills in most of the details, such as the material number, short text, issuance quantity for the operation, and phase. In column A (for automatic), the system automatically checks all of the required boxes. Figure 4.19 Process Instruction Type 1: Material Assignment 4 Process data calcula- tion formulas   5 Inspection results requests   6 Dynamic function calls   7 Sequence definitions    Process Instruction Type Process Instruction Type Description Usage in PI Sheets (Types 1 and 4) Usage in External Process Control System (Types 2 and 3) Sending Process Parameter Details Process Message Requests to Process Message Destinations Table 4.1 Process Instruction Categories (Cont.) © 149 4.6 Process Instruction Categories Process Instruction Type 2: Process Data Requests Process instruction type 2 requires the user on the shop floor to enter the required data and send it back to the SAP S/4HANA system through process messages so that the sys- tem can perform the necessary data entry or updates. Either the system requires the user to enter the process parameter value manually, or the system can fill in the value automatically, depending on how you’ve set up the process instruction characteristics. We’ll cover both types of process parameter value entry (manual input and automatic). Figure 4.20 shows the screen for process instruction category AQMSMR_1 and process instruction type 2. It contains process message category PI_QMSMR to enable the sys- tem to send back the parameter values that the shop floor operator has entered. Figure 4.20 Overview of Control Instruction Category for AQMSMR_1 This example is for quality inspection results for in-process (during production) inspec- tion. The system automatically fills in each Characteristic Value, such as the operation number and inspection lot number, except that the quality inspector user will have to maintain the characteristic value for PPPI_INSPECTION_CHARACTERISTIC. This value is basically the master inspection characteristic (in QM) against which a process operator is requesting the quality inspector to enter values. The process operator also enters val- ues (PPPI_Requested_Value) in the PI sheet. During process message sending, the sys- tem sends the entered information back to the SAP system for further processing. Another example for process instruction type 2 is process instruction category AREAD1. It contains process message category READ to enable the system to send back the parameter values that the shop floor operator has entered. On the PI sheet, when the process operator enters the parameter value for characteristic value PPPI_Material_ Quantity, the system sends this parameter value during the process message sending. The system automatically fills in the characteristic values for the PPPI_Event_Date and 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 150 PPPI_Event_Time with the date and time when the user enters the requested process parameter values. Later, we’ll also show you how the system automatically fills in the characteristic value for a self-defined process instruction characteristic. Process Instruction Type 3: Process Message Subscriptions Figure 4.21 shows the screen for process instruction category APROD_1 and process instruction type 3. Figure 4.21 Process Instruction Type 3: Process Message Subscription It contains the process message category PI_PROD to enable the system to send back the parameter values that the shop floor operator has entered. In this specific example, the process operator sends the quantity produced against the process order for which the operator wants to record GR against the process order. The process operator records the goods-produced information on the PI sheet and sets the status of the PI sheet as Complete. Once the process operator processes the process message—that is, sends it back to the SAP S/4HANA system—the system updates the goods-produced information in the process order and also creates the material and accounting docu- ments. Process Instruction Type 4: Calculations In this process instruction type, the system can perform calculations on the PI sheet based on the calculation formula defined. The system uses the process message cate- gory to send back the calculated value to the predefined destination. The standard pro- cess message category is NREAD. © 151 4.6 Process Instruction Categories Table 4.2 provides the details of some of the important process instruction characteris- tics that you can use for calculation. You can also refer to Figure 4.22 for more informa- tion. On the screen shown in Figure 4.22, you can maintain a field description for Character- istic PPPI_INPUT_REQUEST and the system will display it in the PI sheet. Then, define the formula that the system will use in the calculation in the value field for Character- istic PPPI_CALCULATION_FORMULA. If the formula is too long to fit on one line, you can enter it in up to eight characteristics: just add a new line, enter the “PPPI_CALCULA- TION_FORMULA” characteristic, and continue entering formula details in the Charac- teristic Value field until the formula is complete. Characteristics Characteristic Values Details PPPI_INPUT_REQUEST KS PRODUCTION Define any characteristic value, and the system displays it accordingly in the PI sheet. PPPI_VARIABLE B This is the variable that is defined for the calculated value. PPPI_EVENT PARAMETER_ CHANGED If this characteristic and its value are used, the system automatically does the calculation. If not, the user will have to manually choose the Calculator icon to trigger calculation. PPPI_ CALCULATED_ VALUE NH3_02 This characteristic value was already cre- ated using Transaction CT04 and is assigned here. It governs and controls sev- eral parameters, such as whether the field input is mandatory, whether a negative data entry is possible, or whether a user can select a value from the dropdown list on the PI sheet. PPPI_ CALCULATION_ FORMULA A*20 This is the calculation formula. The vari- able A needs to be previously defined in the PI sheet. Up to eight lines of charac- teristics can be used to define a long or complex formula. PPPI_UNIT_ OF_MEASURE KG This is the unit of measure of the calcu- lated value. Table 4.2 Process Instruction Characteristics for Calculation 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 152 Figure 4.22 Process Instruction Type 4: Calculation Process Instruction Type 5: Inspection Results Requests In this process instruction type, you can navigate to the screen or transaction within the PI sheet in which a quality inspector can record inspection results of an operation or a phase. In other words, this provides the option to record quality results. The stan- dard process instruction category that’s available for operations is QMJUM, and for phase, it’s QMJUM_PH. Depending on the process instruction category you select, you define the operation, the phase (see Table 4.3), or both. Process Instruction Type 6: Dynamic Function Calls Process instruction type 6 uses the dynamic function call, wherein a user can call any SAP S/4HANA system transaction on the PI sheet to facilitate a business process. For example, before initiating the process of GI against a process order within the PI sheet, the process operator wants to quickly check the stock overview of the materials. Simi- larly, while remaining in the PI sheet, a user also wants to perform the phase confirma- tion of the process order. In the first example, the relevant transaction is MMBE for a stock overview. In the second example, the user can call or navigate to the phase con- firmation Transaction COR6N. Characteristic Characteristic Value Details PPPI_OPERATION – The operation for which you want to record quality inspection results PPPI_PHASE – The phase for which you want to record quality inspection results Table 4.3 Process Instruction Type 5 for Phase and Operation © 153 4.6 Process Instruction Categories Table 4.4 provides an overview of each process instruction characteristic and its func- tion. In Figure 4.23, process instruction category C_DURAT enables calculating the duration. Figure 4.23 Process Instruction Type 6: Calculation of Duration Characteristics Characteristic Values Details PPPI_FUNCTION_NAME COPF_CALL_ TRANSACTION The system uses this function module to call up a transaction. PPPI_BUTTON_TEXT Display Process Order The system displays the text Display Process Order (or any other text) on the pushbutton on the PI sheet. PPPI_FUNCTION_DURING_ DISPLAY Allowed This control function determines whether the system should display or forbid display of the desired informa- tion. PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER NEW_SESSION – PPPI_INSTRUCTION – – PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER TCODE – PPPI_TRANSACTION_CODE COR3 This is the transaction code that you want to use to display a process order. Table 4.4 Process Instruction of Type 6 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 154 For each export parameter (PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER), such as I_START_DATE or I_ START_TIME, you enter the values in PPPI_STRING_VARIABLE and the system automatically calculates the duration. You can use this duration to calculate machine hours or produc- tion duration. The system uses the COPF_DETERMINE_DURATION function module. Process Instruction Type 7: Sequence Definitions When process instruction type 7 is incorporated into the process instruction of a master recipe, the system prompts the user to enter the phases’ relationships with each other. This enables the system to provide the business process owner with the necessary con- trol so that unless a predecessor phase is complete, the successor phase can’t start or the user can’t enter its data into the PI sheet. Only when a process operator sends a process message of a predecessor phase will the system enable the user to enter the details of operation of the next (successor) phase. In this process instruction type, you assign the following process instruction character, along with its value (see Figure 4.24). Figure 4.24 Process Instruction Type 7: Sequence Definition Process Instruction Type 0: Universal We’ve discussed how each process instruction type differs from the others—but you can also use process instruction type 0 to attend to specific business processes that the standard process instruction types don’t cover. In addition, if a company is planning to implement the process manufacturing cockpit, all of the process instruction categories will need to be defined as type 0 to enable the system to use them. See Section 4.9 for more on configuring the process manufacturing cockpit in the SAP S/4HANA system.

sections.title: Using a Wizard or Process Instruction Assistant
sections.en: 4.6.2 Using a Wizard or Process Instruction Assistant Using a wizard helps with defining the category of each process instruction type, except for process instruction type 0. For example, if a user wants to create a new pro- cess instruction type 4 (calculation) for a new process instruction category, you use the PI Assistant button to open this step-by-step instruction tool, which eliminates the need to know the complexities involved, such as a characteristic’s PPPI_INPUT_VALUE. © 155 4.6 Process Instruction Categories Table 4.5 lists the standard process instruction categories that are available in the SAP system to enable business users to perform routine process management functions. sections.title: Creating a Self-Defined Process Instruction Category
sections.en: 4.6.3 Creating a Self-Defined Process Instruction Category Now that we’ve covered the basics of the process instruction category, let’s create a self- defined process instruction category of type 0 as an example. In Chapter 7, we’ll show you how to use this self-defined process instruction category, PP20. To create a new process instruction category, use menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Process Instructions  Define Process Instruction Categories (General) or use Transaction O12C. On the initial screen that appears, enter “1710” in the Plant field and choose Continue to open the screen shown in Figure 4.25. The screen that appears shows the list of available process instruction categories for Plant 1710. Notice the ProcInstr Type field; if you place your cursor on this field and press (F4), the system will show a dropdown list of process instruction types. These are the same process instructions that we covered in Section 4.6.1. Choose New Entries and the screen shown in Figure 4.25 will appear. On the initial screen that appears, define process instruction category PP20 as type 0 (universal) and give a short Description. Next, select Process instruction categories and double-click the Characteristics/Process instruction category field on the left-hand side of the screen to enter specific process instruction characteristics. For this example, enter the characteristics as shown in Table 4.6. 158© 159 4.6 Process Instruction Categories Figure 4.25 Overview Screen of Process Instruction Category PP20 Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_DATA_REQUEST_TYPE Simple Data Request PPPI_MESSAGE_CATEGORY PP20 ZPI_CREATION_DATE PPPI_OUTPUT_TEXT P.O. Basic Start Date PPPI_OUTPUT_CHARACTERISTIC ZPI_CREATION_DATE PPPI_PROCESS_ORDER PPPI_CONTROL_RECIPE Table 4.6 Self-Defined Process Instruction Category PP20 Table 4.6 consolidates the configuration made so far. It incorporates process mes- sage category PP20 as well as the self-defined process instruction characteristic ZPI_ CREATION_DATE. See Section 4.6.4 to create a self-defined process instruction characteris- tic. Also note that the system automatically fills in the characteristic values that are blank in the preceding table.

sections.title: Creating a Self-Defined Process Instruction Characteristic
sections.en: 4.6.4 Creating a Self-Defined Process Instruction Characteristic Although the SAP system offers many standard process instruction (and process mes- sage) characteristics, unique business processes or business needs often require custom- defined or self-defined characteristics. For our example, you create a simple self-defined process instruction characteristic that automatically fetches the process order basic start date when the user creates the process order. Use this characteristic to display this information (process order end date) on the PI sheet. Although this example uses automatic value assignment—that is, the system automatically fills in the date field—you can also use it to ask the process operator to send the requisite information in the form of a process message. You can also use process instruction characteristics to define the RFC destination to which a production supervisor wants the process operator to send process instructions for an external control system. To create a new process instruction characteristic, follow menu path SAP IMG  Produc- tion Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Process Instructions  Define Characteristics for Process Instructions  Create Characteristics for Process Instructions or use Transaction O25C. Figure 4.26 displays the initial screen you use to create the process instruction charac- teristic. Notice the similarities between creating a standard characteristic (in Transac- tion CT04) and creating a process instruction characteristic. For this example, you create ZPI_CREATION_DATE and click the Basic data tab. In this tab, it’s important to ensure that the Char. Group (characteristics group) is defined as a process instruction characteristic (PPPI_02). This is the same characteristic group that we covered in Section 4.4.2. Then, define the Data Type as DATE Date Format and click the Proc. mgmt icon. The sys- tem will then display a popup that allows you to incorporate additional process instructions and process message details. If you place the cursor on the Table field and press (F4), or if you click the dropdown option, then the dialog box shown in Figure 4.27 will appear. This screen provides you with the option to select any of the five tables available: Process Order (Header), Operation, Phase, Resource, and Material (Header). The system will display the fields that are associated with the table you select. 160 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration Example The process operator wants to refer to specific information for a process order or its operations in the PI sheet, and this isn’t available in the standard offering. Similarly, there are some standard fields for which a production supervisor wants the process operator to send back the requisite information through process messages. The option to self-define the process instruction characteristic offers the flexibility to choose from a large number of fields in the production process and use them to bring together dif- ferent information on the PI sheet. © 161 4.6 Process Instruction Categories Figure 4.26 Creating Process Instruction Characteristic Figure 4.27 Automatic Value Assignment for Process Order Tables 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 162 For this example, select 01 Process Order (Header) and the system will bring back the previous screen. This time, place the cursor in the Field field and again press (F4) to see a limited selection of fields that are available in the process order table. Select GSTRP, which is the technical name of the process order start date (see Figure 4.28). Figure 4.28 Automatic Value Assignment of Basic Start Date Apart from entering the Table and the Field, you should check the Only automatic box to enable the system to automatically fill in the requisite information in the process instruction characteristic and save it. Then, when the user creates the process order and the system assigns the basic start and finish dates of the process order, the system will fetch the basic start date of the process order and make it an integral part of the PI sheet. Tips and Tricks If a business process involves the creation of a simple process instruction characteris- tic, then use Transaction CT04 (which doesn’t have a process management option) to create characteristics and ensure selection of the correct characteristic group.

sections.title: Control Recipe/Process Instruction Sheets
sections.en: 4.7 Control Recipe/Process Instruction Sheets There are four types of control recipe destinations. Two of them are used when transfer- ring information within the SAP S/4HANA system, and the other two facilitate the trans- fer of information to external systems. The system creates a control recipe when a pro- cess order is released, and this can be automatic (controlled in order type−dependent parameters; Section 4.2) or generated manually. Creating the control recipe and then sending the PI sheet to its destination enables the process operator to begin recording important plant parameters. Table 4.7 provides the necessary details for the different types of control recipes. Note Although they’re still available in SAP S/4HANA 2023, ABAP list−based PI sheets are already obsolete and may not be available in future releases of SAP S/4HANA. © 163 4.7 Control Recipe/Process Instruction Sheets If the control recipe destination is of either type 1 or type 4, then the system sends the recipes to different destinations within the SAP system. The system creates a control recipe, either driven by the user (manually) or automatically, and consolidates the pro- cess instructions for a given order and destination in the form of the PI sheet. The PI sheet contains user-defined details—for example, process notes, process steps, input fields, calculations, options to sign the phases of the PI sheet, details to enter GI (con- sumption), and navigation to the confirmation screen or to record in-process quality results. When the process operator sets the status of the PI sheet to Complete, the sys- tem sends the data back to the SAP system in the form of process messages. The system then uploads the information from the process messages into the SAP S/4HANA system tables using function modules and BAPIs. If the control recipe destination is of either type 2 or type 3, then the system consolidates the process instructions in the control rec- ipe of the process order and sends them to an external process control system. An internal SAP S/4HANA system destination can be any logical or virtual destination where the process operator receives the PI sheet and fills in the data. For example, inter- nal destinations could include a mixing unit, packing unit, blending section, ammonia section, urea section, or quality section. For external destinations—that is, external pro- cess control systems—these are the RFC destinations of the external system. It’s also possible to integrate a digital signature using an SAP Universal ID and pass- word. The following options are available for use with digital signatures:  Activating or deactivating the PI sheet  Locking or unlocking the PI sheet  Changing the status of the PI sheet to Complete  Entering each parameter value into the PI sheet Digital signature options are also available in case a parameter value deviates from pre- defined limits, as well as for completion of each phase in the PI sheet. If there’s a busi- ness need to automatically generate process instructions to minimize maintaining them in the master recipe, then you can achieve this when creating the control recipe destination. See Section 4.7.2 for information on the scope of generation. In the follow- ing sections, we’ll explain how to set up a control recipe destination and how to make the necessary settings for the scope of generation for process instructions. Control Recipe Destination Types Destination Locations Output Types 1 SAP S/4HANA system ABAP list−based PI sheet 2 External control system External control system 3 External control system External system 4 SAP system Browser-based PI sheet Table 4.7 Control Recipe Destination Types 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 164

sections.title: Creating a Control Recipe Destination
sections.en: 4.7.1 Creating a Control Recipe Destination To create a new control recipe destination, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Control Recipe/PI Sheets  Con- trol Recipe Destinations  Define and Set Up Control Recipe Destinations. On the screen shown in Figure 4.29, create a new control recipe destination PP of Type 4 (a browser-based PI sheet). Give a short description and set the Destination address to Chemical Mixing. Check the SortMat box to enable the system to display materials in the same order in which they appear in the master recipe or in the process order. If you place the cursor on the Type field and press (F4), or if you use the dropdown option, then the system will display the four different types of control recipe destinations that are available to choose from. (This is the same as in Table 4.7.) Figure 4.29 Control Recipe Destination: Overview Figure 4.30 Maintaining Control Recipe Destination © 165 4.7 Control Recipe/Process Instruction Sheets Figure 4.30 shows the details screen where you create the control recipe destination, and it also shows the options for incorporating digital signatures in some or all of the processes.

sections.title: Scope of Generation
sections.en: 4.7.2 Scope of Generation In some situations, the process operator enters the same data, but you can use scope data to automate the entry of this data so that it’s the same every time. This is helpful when you want to do the following:  Confirm the quantity produced or activities consumed at the end of each phase.  Enter shift details at the end of every phase or operation.  Ensure that each master recipe created for a specific destination has standard instructions and notes, which then become visible on the PI sheet. Figure 4.31 shows the New Entries: Overview of Added Entries screen that appears when you’re creating control recipe destination 10 for Plant 1710 and when you choose Pro- cess instructions to be generated on the left-hand side. Figure 4.31 Process Instructions to Be Generated during Control Recipe Creation Place the cursor on the Position field and press (F4) and the dialog box will appear. Here, you can define where in the control recipe you want to place the process instruc- tion category. It can be at the start or end of a control recipe, an operation, or a phase. Once you’ve defined a position, the next step is to assign a process instruction cate- gory, place the cursor on the Gen.scope field, and press (F4). This opens the screen shown in Figure 4.31, where you can use the Scope of generation to enable the system to generate process instructions based on the details you provide. For example, you 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 166 can select the 01 option for the GI process instruction category. When the system cre- ates a process order and incorporates the materials list based on the BOM, it simultane- ously creates a reservation with item numbers. To record consumption (GI) against the process order using the PI sheet, select the relevant process instruction category (CH_ CONS or CH_CONS2) and define the scope of generation as 01 (For all reservations). For this example, assign the instruction category SIGN, which the system will generate once at the end of the PI sheet (refer to Figure 4.31). Tips and Tricks When creating the process instruction category SIGN, make sure to assign a character- istic value (e.g., PI Completion Signoff) to the process instruction characteristic PPPI_ INPUT_VALUE.

sections.title: Background Jobs
sections.en: 4.8 Background Jobs Background jobs help to automate processes that otherwise require manual interven- tion. For example, when a user creates a process order and generates its control recipe, the user will have to manually send it to the control recipe destination. The system can be configured to automatically send the control recipe as soon as the system generates it or even periodically. Similarly, sending back process messages to the SAP S/4HANA system can be a manual effort that users can manage as a background (automatic) job. Apart from sending control recipes and process messages, it’s also possible to delete them periodically to save precious system resources. For example, there may not be any need to preserve process messages older than six months, in which case, those can be deleted. In this section, we show you how to set up a background job for sending a control rec- ipe. The same steps apply to sending process messages and deleting process messages as background jobs.

sections.title: Sending Control Recipes
sections.en: 4.8.1 Sending Control Recipes To set up the background job for sending a control recipe as soon as the system creates it, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Control Recipe/PI Sheet  Define Background Job for Sending or use Transaction SM36. Figure 4.32 shows the initial Define Background Job screen to define the background job. Enter “ZPP_PI_SEND” in the Job Name field and press (Enter). On the next screen, enter “SAP_NEW_CONTROL_RECIPES” in the Event field, which causes the system to send the control recipe as soon as the system creates it. If you click the Start Condition button, you can define the schedule for sending control recipes to © 167 4.8 Background Jobs various destinations. You can also assign the program name RCOCB006 to set up the background job. Figure 4.32 Defining Background Job to Send Control Recipe Tips and Tricks Use the Job Wizard button to enable the system to provide guidance in a step-by-step process to set up a background job. This works not just for sending a control recipe to a destination but for any background job. It’s important to know the program name for which to define the background job.

sections.title: Sending Process Messages
sections.en: 4.8.2 Sending Process Messages In Transaction SM36, you can use program RCOCB004 to send newly created process messages as a background job. The system also processes the process messages that have statuses of To Be Submitted and To Be Submitted with Warning. This program is for sending process messages within your plant. If you need to send process messages to different plants, then use program RCOCB002. The corresponding event is SAP_NEW_ PROCESS_MESSAGES. Tips and Tricks Whenever you define a background job by using Transaction SM36, you need to assign a variant to it. Use Transaction SE38 to create a new variant for any background job (and its associated program).
