On the initial screen, enter the program name, click the Variants radio button, and then click the Change button. On the next screen, give the variant name and click Create.

sections.title: Deleting Process Messages
sections.en: 4.8.3 Deleting Process Messages The background program also lets you delete process messages as a background job. Use Transaction SM36 to set up the background job. 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 168

sections.title: Process Manufacturing Cockpit
sections.en: 4.9 Process Manufacturing Cockpit The process manufacturing cockpit finds diverse applications in areas where the activ- ities performed, actions taken, or results recorded don’t reference any specific process order. In the cockpit, it’s possible to use function calls—for example, to call up a trans- action or display a document. You can also create a cockpit for a predefined set of activ- ities that a process operator performs on a specific resource or in a specific work center. The cockpit also facilitates input or output data entry as well as calculations. The sys- tem creates a PI sheet with reference to a process order, and the system reflects any changes or updates made to it. In a cockpit, there’s no need to give reference to a pro- cess order. Here are some examples in which creating process manufacturing cockpits meets business needs:  There’s a need to record the values of five specific process parameters of the plant and report them back to the main control room. The reporting for the parameters doesn’t reference a specific process order but is used to ensure that there is regular recording and consistent transmission of this information.  The production process generates an ancillary product (a by-product of low value), but the production supervisor doesn’t want to record it with reference to any spe- cific process order or book the cost of generating the by-product to a process order. There’s also a need to record the quantity of the by-product every three days, along with other details, such as the date of recording, the name of the person who recorded the information, and that person’s signature.  A company has installed a new production line for which it wants to ensure that the latest technical drawings and manuals are available for process operators to refer to. These drawings are for each work center or work station. In the process manufactur- ing cockpit, the process operator only has to click the relevant drawing’s icon, and all details will become visible. You can create process manufacturing cockpits for as many business applications or activities as needed. Because the cockpit uses a process instruction category, it’s also possible to incorporate a process message category into it. When you make entries are into the cockpit, the system generates process messages that you can send to pre- defined destinations. To define a process manufacturing cockpit, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Define Process Manufacturing Cockpits (see Figure 4.33). Here, you define Cockpit PP10 for Plant 1710. Notice that you can check a newly created cockpit for correctness by choosing the Check icon, and it you can simulate it by choos- ing Simulate at the bottom of the screen. Finally, you need to activate the process man- ufacturing cockpit so that it becomes available and changes you make to the cockpit become effective. To activate the cockpit, choose Activate. © 169 4.9 Process Manufacturing Cockpit Figure 4.33 Process Manufacturing Cockpit For this example, check the box for Cockpit PP10 and double-click Assign Process Instructions to Cockpits. This brings up the screen in Figure 4.34, where two different process instruction categories are maintained. The system only allows process instruc- tion categories of type 0 (universal). The Line no. column defines the sequence in which the process instruction categories will appear in the process manufacturing cockpit. You should then create a new process instruction category with reference to the exist- ing ones and assign it type 0. Checking the Ref (reference) box ensures that the system will automatically reflect any changes made to the specific process instruction category. Figure 4.34 Assigning Process Instruction Categories to Cockpits 4 Process Manufacturing Configuration 170 Tips and Tricks A quick and easy way to use the desired process instruction category in the process manufacturing cockpit is to copy the original process instruction category as a refer- ence and define it as type 0 (universal) during the copy function. In this example, the process instruction category CONS_PI is used as a reference to create a new process instruction category, CONSPP, which is defined as type 0 (universal). In Chapter 7, we’ll show the outcome and the output of the PP10 process manufactur- ing cockpit created in this chapter.

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 4.10 Summary Most of the configuration settings that are applicable in discrete manufacturing also apply to process manufacturing. However, the process management area in PP for pro- cess industries requires detailed coverage. The concepts and fundamentals covered in this chapter apply equally whether a company plans to implement XSteps in discrete manufacturing or use process manufacturing cockpits. The next chapter covers the configuration basics of the last main manufacturing type we’ll discuss in this book: repetitive manufacturing. ©

sections.title: Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
sections.en: 5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile The REM profile offers several controls for important business functions that form the basis of regular or periodic data recording in the system. This includes options to post activities online or at a later date, automatic goods movements of all or some of the materials, and stock and/or automatic batch determination, among other things. To create an REM profile using the REM profile assistant, follow SAP menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Control  Create Repetitive Manufac- turing Profile Using Assistant or use Transaction OSPT. The following sections will explain the steps involved in setting up an REM profile.

sections.title: Repetitive Manufacturing Production Type
sections.en: 5.1.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Production Type When creating a RE M profile, you need to decide whether to use the profile for the MTO or MTS production method. Alternately, you can cater separate REM profiles to meet MTO or MTS business scenarios. Note Chapter 12 covers MTO and MTS production methods. Depending on the option you select, the system will display the relevant screens and options to choose from. If you choose MTO production, then the system will create a profile suited to REM that references the sales order. If you choose MTS production, the system will create a profile suited to REM that doesn’t reference the sales order. It’s also possible to use an REM profile template to copy previously created and avail- able REM profiles to create a new REM profile. This step copies all the settings of the REM template profile into the new REM profile, and then, you can make the desired changes as needed. © 173 5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile Tips and Tricks Click the Meaning icon on each REM profile creation screen to get information on the underlying concept for that functionality or area. Click the Recommendation icon on each REM profile creation screen to get system advice on recommended settings. For this example, you create an REM profile for an MTS production method. Figure 5.1 shows the initial screen where you create an REM profile using the assistant. For this example, click the Make-to-stock - REM radio button and then click Next. Figure 5.1 Initial Screen of Repetitive Manufacturing Profile Assistant

sections.title: Reporting Points
sections.en: 5.1.2 Reporting Points A reporting point enables the system to record consumption or other details, such as identifying the work in process (WIP) and better stock visibility in inventory manage- ment (IM), which are specific to actual information in time and equally helpful in report- ing. As shown in Figure 5.2, you need to select whether the system should include no reporting points or make the reporting points mandatory or optional. A reporting point serves the same purpose as a milestone does in routing for discrete manufacturing. If 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 174 you select the Mandatory Reporting Points option, then you need to define a reporting point at the time of assembly or component backflush. Figure 5.2 Reporting Points You can use reporting points in one of the following situations:  If you select the mandatory reporting points option, then a backflush must take place at every reporting point. It makes sense to backflush at reporting points if the GI is to be posted as close as possible to the actual issuance of the materials or if WIP is to be calculated.  If you select the optional reporting points option, then it entails carrying out the normal final backflush and using a reporting point backflush only in certain situa- tions. With optional reporting points, you use the standard GR posting procedures in REM for backflushing and then only backflush at reporting points for special purposes—for example, when backflushing scrap or calculating WIP. The optional reporting point makes sense when, for example, there’s usually no business need for a reporting point at all, but due to technical issues in the production line, the pro- duction supervisor wants to evaluate extra material issuance or extra scrap genera- tion. For this example, choose Mandatory reporting points and then click Next.

sections.title: Automatic Goods Movements
sections.en: 5.1.3 Automatic Goods Movements The automatic goods movement option applies to reporting point backflush only. Without reporting points, you can post the GR on final confirmation. On the screen shown in Figure 5.3, you choose whether the system should perform automatic GR at the time of assembly backflush. When assembly backflush is recorded and the yield is confirmed, the system automatically performs the GR at the last reporting point. When you’re using optional reporting points, choose the Automatic GR setting because usually, the components are backflushed along with the GR posting. When you’re using mandatory reporting points, the production supervisor can only backflush at reporting points. The normal GR posting isn’t available here, so for man- datory reporting points, we recommend that you define the last workplace on the © 175 5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile production line as a reporting point with the Automatic GR setting. If you choose the Automatic GR option, the production supervisor has to post activities and GIs manu- ally for operations that occur after the last reporting point. This is an exception to the preceding recommendation. Figure 5.3 Automatic GR For this example, choose Automatic GR and then choose Next. Note While you’re configuring the REM profile using the assistant, the system displays either the screen shown in Figure 5.3 or the screen shown in Figure 5.4. Which one it shows depends on the option you chose while creating an REM profile.

sections.title: Reporting Points: Confirmations and Kanban
sections.en: 5.1.4 Reporting Points: Confirmations and Kanban Depending on the system release, you select whether to use reporting points with or without Kanban, as shown in Figure 5.4. Using reporting points with Kanban enables you to monitor the production process at the intermediate level by using Kanban to process products in individual operations. The reporting points record the confirma- tion that an operation has been completed. For this example, choose RP Confirmation w/o Kanban and then click Next. Figure 5.4 RP Confirmation for Kanban 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 176

sections.title: Activity Posting
sections.en: 5.1.5 Activity Posting On the next screen, you choose whether or not the system will also post the activities to a product cost collector at the time of assembly backflush. You do this by choosing either the Post activities radio button or the or No activity posting radio button (see Figure 5.5). Activities can be machine hours, labor hours, or processing time, and they are defined in rate routing. If a company follows a lean manufacturing approach, in which even the data entry effort is minimal, activity posting involves additional use of the system’s resources, such as material calculation and price updates. Activity posting should only be considered if it’s significant when compared with cost. We suggest that you engage a product cost controlling resource to help with the decision-making pro- cess about activity posting. If you instruct the system to post production activities, it will calculate these activities either on the basis of details from the standard cost estimate or from preliminary cost- ing in the cost collector for material. The system also posts the activities during the confirmation process. For this example, choose Post activities and then click Next. Figure 5.5 Activities Backflush

sections.title: Separated Backflush
sections.en: 5.1.6 Separated Backflush In this step, you choose whether or not the system should perform separated back- flushes by selecting the Separated Backflush radio button or the No Separated Back- flush radio button. This option is primarily used to improve a system’s performance, especially if it becomes slow due to a large number of confirmations. With separated backflush, you can uncouple certain aspects of backflushing during the confirmation process by scheduling them as separate background jobs offline. You should use separated backflushing in the following cases:  To improve system performance  If you’re dealing with large BOMs  For sales orders with many schedule lines for small quantities For this example, choose Separated Backflush and then click Next. © 177 5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile If you select the No Separated Backflush option, then Figure 5.6 will appear, with an option to choose whether the system should simultaneously post GR and GI in the same assembly confirmation transaction or post them separately. In REM, the final confirmation also encompasses GR. You should choose whether to also post GI during the confirmation process. If you select the Post GR Only option, then you can post the GIs for the components collectively—for example, by using Transaction MIGO in REM. For this example, choose Post GR and GI in the assembly confirmation. Figure 5.6 Automatic GR and GI

sections.title: Process Control
sections.en: 5.1.7 Process Control On the screen shown in Figure 5.7, you choose when GI and the calculation of costs are performed and whether GI and the calculation of costs are aggregated during confirma- tion. While the option to let the system perform several steps and functions together is available, you can use the Process Control option to customize the process according to your business needs. Click Customizing on this screen to make the necessary changes. (We’ll cover the customization of process controls later in Section 5.5.). Figure 5.7 Process Control in Backflushing It’s important to note that this option is only available if you choose Separated Back- flush in the previous step. If you do, then you need to define the process control for this separated backflush, and there are four options available to maintain. For this example, enter “SAP1” for the Process Control and click Next. 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 178

sections.title: Firming Planned Orders
sections.en: 5.1.8 Firming Planned Orders It makes sense to firm planned orders when creating them on the REM planning table because a firmed planned order won’t change during the MRP run. There are also options to not firm the planned order at the time of its creation and to firm planned orders in the planning time fence only. For this example, you choose Firm planned orders in plng time fence and then click Next (see Figure 5.8). Figure 5.8 Firming Planned Orders in REM

sections.title: Automatic Stock Determination
sections.en: 5.1.9 Automatic Stock Determination You can take advantage of the system’s ability to perform automatic stock determina- tion at the time of component or assembly backflush. You do this by selecting the Use stock determination radio button (see Figure 5.9). The prerequisite is that you must have already set up the automatic stock determination procedure in the system. Stock determination enables the system to suggest available stock for consumption based on the defined criteria. For this example, you choose Do not use stock determination and then click Next. Figure 5.9 Stock Determination

sections.title: Batch Determination Procedure
sections.en: 5.1.10 Batch Determination Procedure Similar to the previous automatic stock determination procedure, you must now choose whether or not the system should perform the batch determination procedure in REM. You do this by selecting Separate batch search procedure or No separate batch © 179 5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile search proc. The material in this case must be batch managed to use this functionality. Batch determination enables the system to suggest available batches for consumption based on the defined criteria. For this example, choose No separate batch search proc. and then click Next. Note Batch determination is covered in Chapter 10.

sections.title: Reduction in Planned Order Quantities
sections.en: 5.1.11 Reduction in Planned Order Quantities On the screen shown in Figure 5.10, you choose whether the system should reduce the production quantities in the planned order or in the run schedule quantity while per- forming assembly confirmation of a material. You also choose whether the system should reduce the planned order quantity only if it pertains to a specific production version. As each production version reflects a production line, this option enables the reduction of planned order quantities as soon as the goods on the production line (in direct relationship to the production version) are produced. For this example, you choose All, which causes the system to reduce planned order quantities in all cases. Then click Next. Figure 5.10 Reduction in Planned Order Quantities

sections.title: Reduction Period
sections.en: 5.1.12 Reduction Period On the screen shown in Figure 5.11, choose the period in days during which the system should reduce the planned order quantities. The system considers the reduction period from today and expands it to consider the defined dates in the future. For example, if a company is consistently faced with overproduction and wants to avoid additional pro- duction for the defined days in the future, then you maintain the reduction period here. In addition, note that the system reduces the planned order quantities dates from the past in any case and accords them higher priority than the current date or future dates. For this example, you enter “3” in the Reduction Period field and then click Next. 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 180 Figure 5.11 Reduction Period for Planned Orders

sections.title: Creating New Planned Orders on Goods Receipts Reversals
sections.en: 5.1.13 Creating New Planned Orders on Goods Receipts Reversals On the screen shown in Figure 5.12, you choose whether the system should automati- cally create planned orders for reversed quantities. If you do not choose this option, then the system automatically creates planned orders during the MRP run if necessary. For this example, choose No planned order creation and then click Next. Figure 5.12 Planned Order Creation on GR Reversals

sections.title: Online Error Correction
sections.en: 5.1.14 Online Error Correction In this step, you choose how the system processes errors encountered during transac- tion postings, such as a failed component backflush due to a mismatch in IM. Other types of errors may include an insufficient quantity of a component’s stock in a com- ponent backflush, a missing storage location for GI or GR, and so on. If you select the No online correction or Optional online correction option, then you’ll use a separate trans- action to address errors separately, provided that you create the reprocessing records (see the next step). You can also choose Online correction mandatory. For this example, choose Optional online correction and then click Next. © 181 5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile

sections.title: Error Log Maintenance for Reprocessing
sections.en: 5.1.15 Error Log Maintenance for Reprocessing During automatic goods movement processing, such as backflushing, sometimes the processing isn’t successful due to insufficient stock, an incorrect storage location, or missing or nonupdated rates for activity types. All such unsuccessful records end up in error log maintenance for reprocessing, and you need to note that they haven’t been resolved yet. On the screen shown in Figure 5.13, you choose how the system should manage all reprocessing records of all items that have caused errors. The available options are that the system won’t maintain any reprocessing records, will maintain collective repro- cessing records, or will maintain individual and collective reprocessing records of errors. For this example, choose Online correction mandatory and then click Next. Figure 5.13 Reprocessing Incorrect Records

sections.title: Movement Types for Stock Postings
sections.en: 5.1.16 Movement Types for Stock Postings On the screen shown in Figure 5.14, the system provides standard movement types that it uses to perform various IM transactions. For example, for GI against a REM planned order, the movement type is 261, and its reversal is reflected in movement type 262. Figure 5.14 Movement Types for MTS Production If there’s any customized movement type that you need to record an inventory trans- action, then you enter it on this screen. The prerequisite for using a customized move- ment type is that you’ve previously maintained it in the system. You can then assign it on this screen and click Next. 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 182

sections.title: Naming the Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
sections.en: 5.1.17 Naming the Repetitive Manufacturing Profile Finally, the last screen of the REM profile creation process using the assistant appears. On this screen, you enter “PP10” for the REM Profile and give it a short description. You can also go back and make changes to any of the settings you’ve made so far.

sections.title: Summary of Repetitive Manufacturing Profile Settings
sections.en: 5.1.18 Summary of Repetitive Manufacturing Profile Settings Figure 5.15 shows the overview/summary screen of REM profile PP10. Here, you choose Continue and then Save. Scroll down to review the remaining options you configured for the REM profile on the previous screens. After you create an REM profile, you can’t use the same transaction to make changes to it; that transaction is only for the creation of the REM profile. Once you’re familiar with the REM profile, you can create it directly without using the REM profile assistant. That saves you time and effort because all of the selection options are available on one screen and in different tabs. Figure 5.15 Overview of REM Profile after All Settings Are Made © 183

sections.title: Scheduling Planned Orders
sections.en: 5.2 Scheduling Planned Orders To make changes or update any field in the already created REM profile, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Control  Define Repetitive Man- ufacturing Profiles or use Transaction OSP2. Here, you make the desired changes to REM profile PP10. For this example, scroll down and choose Backflush Using Standard Cost Estimate for Material (not shown) and then save the REM profile (see Figure 5.16). Figure 5.16 Overall Maintenance of REM Profile 5.2 Scheduling Planned Orders The next basic configuration topic we’ll discuss is scheduling planned orders. As with the discrete manufacturing and process manufacturing types, a production planner can take advantage of the available scheduling options. You set the scheduling param- eters for planned orders in REM by following menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repet- itive Manufacturing  Planning  Define Scheduling Parameters for Planned Orders. The initial screen then appears where you maintain the scheduling parameters for a planned order. Because the order type for the REM planned order is PE, you enter “1710” for Plant and “PE” for Order type, and then, you double-click the Plant field (1710). It’s important to note here that the PE order type is identical to the run schedule quantity. After making this selection, you choose the Detail icon. This brings up the detailed screen where you set scheduling parameters for the REM planned order for order type PE and plant 1710 (see Figure 5.17). 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 184 Because REM uses rate-based scheduling, you check the Takt Time/Rate-Based Sched. box. If your company decides to use detailed scheduling for all non-REM applications, you check the relevant scheduling and capacity requirements boxes (both the Schedul- ing and Generate Capacity Reqs. boxes) to meet your business needs. You also select the Scheduling Type, which for this example is Backwards. Choose from the Backward Scheduling and Current Date scheduling types. Refer to Chapter 3 for more details about scheduling. Although the details covered in Chapter 3 are applicable to scheduling production orders, they apply equally to planned orders. Figure 5.17 Detailed Screen of Scheduling Parameters for Planned Orders

sections.title: Display
sections.en: 5.3 Display You can select different options to control how different settings will appear in the SAP S/4HANA system. The following sections cover two display options.

sections.title: Entry Parameters for a Planning Table
sections.en: 5.3.1 Entry Parameters for a Planning Table You can choose how the display entry parameters of the REM planning table should look and how to present the requisite information. The settings you make in this sec- tion will eventually be available on the initial parameters selection screen of the REM planning table (Transaction MF50). To maintain or change the entry parameters of the © 185 5.3 Display planning table, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Planning Table  Maintain Entry Parameters (see Figure 5.18). Figure 5.18 Entry Parameters of REM Planning Table We will discuss the impact of the preceding configuration on the parameters selection screen of the planning table (Transaction MF50) in Chapter 8.

sections.title: Maintaining Row Selection
sections.en: 5.3.2 Maintaining Row Selection You can control what information is displayed in the planning table, including receipts, requirements, and stock situations. To configure the selection of rows in the planning table, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Planning Table  Maintain Rows Selection. For this example, check all the boxes (see Figure 5.19); in a real-world situation, you’d check only the boxes that are relevant to your business needs to keep things lean when dealing with your company’s unique situation. 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 186 Figure 5.19 Determining Visible Rows in REM Planning Table

sections.title: Material Staging
sections.en: 5.4 Material Staging A pull list is basically a movement of stock (stock transfer) from storage location to stor- age location that uses inventory movement type 311. It’s also known as material stag- ing. You can assign the previously configured stock determination and batch search procedures on the screen shown in Figure 5.20. To make the necessary settings, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Material Staging  Define Control Data for Pull List. Figure 5.20 Pull List Control © 187

sections.title: Global Settings for Confirmation and the Logistics Information System
sections.en: 5.5 Global Settings for Confirmation and the Logistics Information System 5.5 Global Settings for Confirmation and the Logistics Information System Some settings are maintained at the global level. This includes the period the system is to consider when automatically updating the statistics (the logistics information sys- tem [LIS]) of the planning figures. It also includes an option allowing a production plan- ner to require the system to display the planned order number of the run schedule quantity number during backflushing. To configure the necessary settings, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Backflushing  Maintain Global Settings for Confirmation and LIS. In the Start: current date+ and Finish: current date+ fields (see Figure 5.21), you can enter “1” to start from the current date (the earliest) and enter any number of days for the finish date. Figure 5.21 Field Selection Table in REM When the process control for confirmation is maintained, it can control when the sys- tem should update the transactions, such as during backflush or activity costs calcula- tion. The system can update the information immediately and online by using an update program or later in a background program. It’s therefore important to select the process controls while creating the REM profile. On the screen shown in Figure 5.22, you place the cursor in the Backflush field and press (F4); the dropdown list on the right side of the screen then appears. From the drop- down list, choose one of the three backflushing options available. To set the relevant parameters, use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Back- flushing  Separate Backflushing Processes  Define Confirmation Processes. 5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration 188 Figure 5.22 Process Control Confirmation Parameters

sections.title: Operational Methods Sheet
sections.en: 5.6 Operational Methods Sheet The operational methods sheet supports business tasks throughout the entire REM cycle. An operational methods sheet shows the production details that a plant operator needs to produce goods, and you can make several layout and configuration settings to customize the display layout of an operational methods sheet. For example, you can specifically select the desired fields from the work center, operation, component, pro- duction resources/tools (PRT), or SAP Document Management service that must appear in the operational methods sheet. You can also separately customize the header and table (item) details. To create an operational methods sheet, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Basic Data  Line Design  Operational Method Sheet or use Transaction OLDPS. In the Table Assignments area shown in Figure 5.23, select COMP (component), MAT (material), OPR (operation), or PRT from the Table ID column. Next, use the Assign fields folder on the left-hand side of the screen to choose the fields that you want to appear on the opera- tional methods sheet. In Chapter 8, we’ll show you how the settings you’ve made here let you display (and print) the operational methods sheet in REM. Integration with Microsoft Word must be in place as well, and the prerequisite is to have Microsoft Word and macros enabled. The template file is placed within the SAP S/4HANA system, which the system refers to while creating the OMS. © 189

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 5.7 Summary Figure 5.23 Operational Methods Sheet 5.7 Summary The REM profile is the main configuration activity, and it controls the various func- tions that you can perform in REM. You assign the configured REM profile in the mate- rial master. You can configure the scheduling parameters of run schedule quantities, and you can alter the display layout of the planning table. You can maintain controls for when the system will perform automated functions, such as backflushing, either online or in the background. The next chapter will cover the business processes associated with discrete manufac- turing in the SAP S/4HANA system. It will build upon the configuration concepts and understandings that were developed in Chapter 3. © PART III Production Planning Workflows ©

sections.title: Process Overview
sections.en: 6.1 Process Overview Figure 6.1 illustrates the end-to-end process flow in discrete manufacturing. The pro- cess begins when requirements from manually created planned orders, MRP, or SAP S/4HANA Sales flow in, based on which production the process must initiate. Figure 6.1 Process Flow in Discrete Manufacturing To initiate the production process, you need a production order 1. The system sched- ules the order 2 and checks for material and PRT requirements and their availability 3 to see if it can produce the required quantities or not. It also checks for the capacity 4 needed to produce the material. If the system is unable to find the requisite capacity, it reflects an overload. The next step is to release the production order 5 to enable the system to perform additional functions. Several functions can’t be performed until the order is released, including printing shop floor papers 6. The production execution step is the actual and Completion Scheduling Availability Checks Capacity Requirements Planning Order Release Shop Paper Printing Material Withdrawal Confirmation Goods Receipt Order Settlement Order Creation © 195

sections.title: Master Data
sections.en: 6.2 Master Data physical production of material, and the first step toward production is material with- drawal from the warehouse 7. The plant operator then enters the operation or order confirmation to record the actual processing of material or production, including scrap produced or rework required 8. The production department delivers the produced material to the warehouse, which performs the GR against the production order 9. The last two steps reflect the integra- tion of SAP S/4HANA Finance’s CO functionality with PP, in which order settlement j takes place and the production order is closed k. 6.2 Master Data Before you run processes and transactions in the SAP S/4HANA system, it’s imperative to set up complete and comprehensive master data. In discrete manufacturing (and in other manufacturing types), if the master data is set up in the right sequence, then it’s much easier to connect with other types of master data because the predecessor-suc- cessor relationship is already taken care of. The following steps outline the procedure that you need to follow to set up master data correctly and that we discuss in more detail in the following sections: 1. Create the material master of the product (a finished good or an assembly) that needs to be produced. Also, create the material master for the raw material that will eventually become part of the production process. 2. Create the BOM of the product that needs to be produced and assign components, together with the quantities needed to produce the product. If applicable, also define a scrap percentage at the operation and component levels. Also, if required, main- tain details of coproducts or by-products in the BOM. 3. Create the work center. 4. Create a routing for the material and assign the previously created work center. 5. Create the production version for the material and assign the material’s BOM and routing to it. Due to the highly diverse nature of the material master creation process, we cover it briefly in this chapter while offering much more detailed coverage of the PP-specific master data (specifically, the BOM, work center, routing, and production version). Note Refer to Chapter 3 for a more in-depth understanding of the configuration specifics for discrete manufacturing. 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 196

sections.title: Material Master
sections.en: 6.2.1 Material Master A material master is whatever the company wants to maintain as an inventory item. Examples are far ranging and include the following: a finished product that the com- pany first produces in house and then sells, a raw material it procures and consumes in the production process, consumables (e.g., greases, lubricants, gloves), trade goods that it buys and then sells, and spare parts for maintenance of machines. Creating and maintaining information on a material master is the collective and com- bined responsibility of a company’s various departments and divisions. A material master is a central element, so everyone contributes their share of maintaining differ- ent information. In the SAP S/4HANA system, maintaining logically structured infor- mation absolutely helps the relevant departments efficiently perform their day-to-day business functions. Example A company produces a finished good (a product). For this product, the company needs to maintain information for producing the product, selling it, and maintaining plan- ning and design data, quality control and life span information, costing, and more. More than likely, one person or department won’t have all of the information related to many business functions. For example, sales personnel may not know what quality tests a product goes through. Similarly, the production department won’t necessarily care about the selling price of the product because it’s primarily concerned with or focused on producing it. However, salespeople need instant access to the selling price and the applicable taxes or discounts upon creation of a sales order. Similarly, the pro- duction supervisor wants better control over the underdelivery and overdelivery toler- ances allowed for a material, whereas the warehouse in charge needs instant informa- tion about storing the product. To add greater visibility to the entire material master creation process, the SAP S/4HANA system provides views in which the relevant departments of the company (depending on which SAP S/4HANA functionality they’re working with) can fill in their share of information. The Basic Data view contains basic information that is applicable to the entire material master, such as base units of measure, gross and net weights, vol- ume, product hierarchy, and design/drawing information. We cover the details of these views later in this section. When you’re creating a material master, it’s also important to define the material type—that is, whether it’s a raw material, a semifinished product, a trading good, a spare part, or a consumable item. The material type controls the views that the system makes available for the user to maintain information for later use. The views contain either organization-independent or organization-dependent details. The organization- independent information includes, for example, the material description or its weight and volume. The organization-dependent information—that is, information specific © 197 6.2 Master Data to a plant or storage location—may include the planning or forecasting data that is unique to a given plant. For a different plant (organization dependent), the planning or forecasting data may be different. Table 6.1 provides an overview of plant-independent and plant-dependent views of the material master. A checkmark in a column denotes the data that needs to be main- tained for a given function. The following list provides broad explanations of the data in some of the views of the material master: Material View Organization- Independent Plant Storage Location Storage Number SAP S/4HANA Sales Basic Data 1 and 2  Classification  Sales: Sales Org.   Sales: General/Plant   Foreign Trade/Export Data   Sales Text   Purchasing  Foreign Trade/Import Data  Purchase Order Text  MRP 1–4  Advanced Planning  Forecast   Work Scheduling  Plant Data/Storage 1 and 2    Warehouse Manage- ment 1 and 2   Quality Management  Accounting 1 and 2  Costing 1 and 2  Table 6.1 Material Master Views 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 198  Basic data These views consist of information such as base unit of measure, which is the small- est unit of measure in which a company maintains the product’s inventory. It con- sists of gross weight, net weight, volume, product hierarchy, size/dimension, and design/drawing details if you’re using the SAP Document Management service.  Classification This view enables classifying specific materials with identical or similar characteris- tics into groups and categorizing those groups based on specific characteristics. Due to this view’s diverse and practical nature, it’s available in several areas of the SAP S/4HANA system. You can classify materials as well as customers and equipment. When there isn’t any other option to do so, you can use classification to maintain material-specific information.  Sales These views contain details such as the delivering plant, selling unit of measure, taxes, conditions (e.g., gross price, taxes), sales order text, transportation group, loading group, and item category. These views are maintained for materials that a company wants to sell.  Purchasing This view contains the purchase unit of measure, purchase group, material group that groups together materials with similar attributes, purchasing value key that incorpo- rates underdelivery and overdelivery tolerances, GR processing time, and batch man- agement. For externally procured materials, maintaining this view is mandatory.  MRP These views enable a materials planner to define how to procure a material and how to plan and control the material within the production process. The definition of the procurement type specifies whether a company procures the material externally, produces it in house, or can do both. It enables specifying whether the material fol- lows reorder point planning or is demand driven. Using a strategy group, you can maintain controls for whether a material uses make-to-stock (MTS), make-to-order (MTO), or assemble-to-order (ATO) production. Material planning for a procured or produced material requires you to maintain some or all of the MRP views. Note Chapter 13 covers MRP.  Advanced planning Production planning and detailed scheduling (PP/DS) with SAP S/4HANA introduces the advanced capacity planning and heuristics for materials planning that were pre- viously available in SAP Advanced Planning and Optimization (SAP APO). PP/DS transactions are executed directly in SAP S/4HANA, thereby eliminating the need for a separate installation to run PP/DS transactions. The material master and work center creation are also synchronous, which means that a material or a work center that has just been created can be immediately available for planning in PP/DS. © 199 6.2 Master Data  Forecast This view enables a materials planner to define forecasting parameters and models, such as constant, seasonal, moving average, and trend. If you already know the fore- casting data properties, then you can select from one of the available forecasting models. If you don’t know which model to select, the system provides an option for automatic model selection. It requires you to define the number of historical and forecast periods, based on which the system runs forecasting. Note Chapter 11, Section 11.4 covers forecasting.  Work scheduling This view contains details for underdelivery and overdelivery tolerances for in- house production, the production scheduler, the production scheduling profile that controls several business functions, the production unit of measure, and the pro- duction time in days. For material produced in house, maintaining this view is man- datory. It’s possible to have a different unit of measure, which in this case is the pro- duction unit of measure and may be different from the base unit of measure.  Plant data This view contains information such as the storage bin, temperature conditions, and shelf life data for batch-managed materials, including maximum storage period, minimum remaining shelf life, and total shelf life.  Quality management This view provides the option to select different types of inspections that a material may go through—for example, finished goods inspection, raw material inspection, and in-process (during production) inspection. The QM procurement key denotes whether raw material must be quality managed and also denotes the relevant certif- icate type.  Accounting These views contain the price control indicator—that is, whether the material will be valued at a standard price or a moving average price—and the valuation class.  Costing These views contain details such as overhead group, origin group, and variance key. Note The creation of material types and also the material master is largely driven by MM processes, with other functional areas supporting and facilitating it. Now, let’s create a new material and maintain some of the important views. To create a new material master, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  Material Master  6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 200 Create (General)  Immediately or use Transaction MM01. On the initial screen that appears, select M Mechanical Engineering as the Industry sector and FERT Finished Product from the dropdown list (not shown) as the Material Type. Then, choose Org. (organizational) Levels from the top menu bar so that Figure 6.2 appears. Figure 6.2 Initial Screen for Material Master Creation Next, click Select View(s) and the screen shown in Figure 6.3 will appear, showing the list of views that were briefly covered earlier. Figure 6.3 Selecting View Options in Material Master Creation © 201 6.2 Master Data You don’t need to select and maintain all the views, just those that will eventually be used in your company’s business processes. For example, if material planning is not required, then there’s no need to maintain MRP views of the material. Select the Basic Data views and the screen shown in Figure 6.4 will appear, showing the Basic Data 1 tab of the material master. Here, the system automatically assigns it an internal material number, FG129. In this view, you maintain information such as Base Unit of Measure, Gross Weight, and Net Weight. The right-hand side of the screen shows the various views that can be maintained for this material. Figure 6.4 Basic Data View of Material Master

sections.title: Bill of Materials
sections.en: 6.2.2 Bill of Materials A material’s BOM consists of components grouped together with the relevant quanti- ties that are needed to produce the material in the production process. For example, if vanilla ice cream is the main product, then its BOM will consist of all the ingredients (components) needed to produce it. Similarly, to produce an automobile, there’s a need for an engine, a chassis, a body, and a large number of other components. To create the BOM of a material, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data  Bills of Material  Bill of Material  Material BOM  Create or use Transaction CS01. On the initial screen, enter “FG129” for Material and “1710” for Plant, as shown in Figure 6.5. This is the material number for which you want to create the BOM. In the BOM Usage field, enter “1” to specify the purpose for which you’re creating and using a BOM. BOM 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 202 Usage 1 denotes that it’s for production purposes and will be available when creating a production order for this material. Other BOM usages are SD BOM, PM BOM, and Cost- ing BOM. In the Valid From field, enter “01/01/2026” to denote the date from which the BOM becomes available in the production process. In this example, the BOM’s validity starts on January 01, 2026 (01/01/2026), when a user creates a production order. Therefore, the BOM won’t be available if the user creates a production order in the month of December 2025. If no date is given, the system automatically assigns the BOM’s cre- ation date as the Valid From date. The purpose of BOM validity is to ensure that if the company has a new BOM starting at a later date, then the validity of the new BOM can be set accordingly and will become available during the production process. In the Alternative BOM field, you can specify up to 99 different alternative BOMs for a material. This means that the same material can be produced in up to 99 different ways by having an alternative BOM option available. For example, when one component of a material’s BOM is unavailable in stock, a production planner can select another alter- native BOM to continue the production process. When a material’s BOM is created for the first time, the system automatically assigns the alternative BOM as 1. When a new BOM for the same material is created, then the system issues a warning message and internally assigns the next alternative BOM number to it. You choose Header to open the Create Material BOM: Header Overview screen. The base quantity of a material denotes the quantity based on which the material’s BOM components are defined. The system then automatically divides the component’s quantity by the base quantity during production order creation. This is especially use- ful when component quantities are very small and defined in, for example, milligrams, as with vanilla flavor. For this example, you assign a base quantity of “10 PC” in the BOM header as shown in Figure 6.5. The components and their quantities that you define in the BOM now denote the requirements to produce ten pieces of material FG129. The BOM Status field controls whether the BOM is active and is available for usage, and the value 1 denotes that it’s active. If there’s a business need to deactivate a BOM, then you can simply change the status of the BOM to Inactive at any time. Next, click the Item button to go to the screen shown in Figure 6.6, which shows the item overview of the BOM. Enter the material numbers for the individual components, together with the quantity for each. The system will divide the component’s quantity by the base quantity (if any) during production order creation to calculate the quantity of the component needed to produce the main material, which in this case is FG129. © 203 6.2 Master Data Figure 6.5 BOM Header Figure 6.6 BOM Item Overview Select line item 0010 and then choose Item, which opens the screen shown in Figure 6.7. Some of the important fields on this screen are as follows:  Fixed Quantity If you check this box, you’re denoting that regardless of production order quantity, the component quantity remains fixed. For example, to produce 50 kg of a main 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 204 material, the component quantity required is 50 kg. To produce 35 kg of the main material, the system calculates the component quantity of 35 kg, as defined in the material’s BOM. However, if you check the Fixed Quantity box and select a compo- nent Quantity of, say, 35 kg, then the system will calculate the component quantity required as 35 kg, even if the main material’s production quantity is 50 kg.  Operation scrap in % Operation scrap is defined as a percentage increases the issuance quantity of a com- ponent to account for scrap during the production process. For example, if you define Operation scrap as 5% and the component quantity as 100 kg, then the sys- tem automatically calculates the required component quantity as 105 kg to account for the operation (during production) scrap. It’s mandatory to select Net ID (not shown) when defining operation scrap.  Component scrap (%) Component scrap is defined as a percentage increase of the issuance quantity of a component to account for scrap during the production process. For example, if you define Component Scrap as 10% and the component Quantity as 100 kg, then the system automatically calculates the required component quantity as 110 kg to account for the operation scrap. Figure 6.7 Detailed View of BOM Item If you define and select both scrap types (operation scrap and component scrap), the system will consider both scrap types during component quantity calculation. During the MRP run, the system will take all of these scrap definitions into account to suggest planning proposals. Next, scroll down the screen (see Figure 6.8). If the normal production of a product gen- erates a coproduct, then you should check the Co-product box. © 205 6.2 Master Data If the production process in a business scenario entails that the main material can also be one of the process’s BOM components, then this will only be possible if you check the Recurs. (recursive) allowed box. Figure 6.8 Additional Details in BOM Item In the MRP Data area of the screen, the Lead-time offset field helps you plan a compo- nent’s availability. For example, if a production supervisor wants to start producing a material as soon as the first shift starts on a given morning, then they will need to approach the warehouse supervisor on the day before to issue the requisite compo- nents. To handle this scenario, you enter “–1” in the Lead-time offset field so that the component’s required availability date is one day earlier than the planned start date of the production. The negative number of the workday reflects that the production supervisor needs components earlier than the start date of production, and a positive number of days is used to show that there’s no need to have the components until the defined number of days after the start of production. The system ignores this option if the production process follows lead-time scheduling (and not just basic date schedul- ing). To enable this behavior for lead-time scheduling, you assign the components to the corresponding operations in the routing. Note that such a positive or negative lead time is always independent of lot size. The Special Procurement field enables a materials planner to define a component- specific special procurement, like a phantom assembly or a procurement from another plant. Maintaining a special procurement type at the component level gives it a higher precedence than at the material level. During an MRP run, the system explodes the material BOM and evaluates parameters maintained at the component level for plan- ning purposes before it looks for the information at the material level. Next, choose the Status/Long Text tab shown in Figure 6.7 to see the fields in Figure 6.9. Enter the Storage Location. During the backflush process in SFC, the system looks for information in this field because it acts as an issue storage location. If the customer or vendor supplies the material instead of the company using its own material in produc- tion, then you should select the relevant option from the Mat. Provision Ind. dropdown list. Finally, you can also control whether the component is considered during product 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 206 cost calculation or whether the system excludes it. You do this by selecting the relevant option in the CostingRelevncy field. Figure 6.9 Status/Long Text Tab of BOM Item Details Tips and Tricks You can make mass changes to BOMs to attend to any new business requirements. For example, a materials planner might want to replace an old and obsolete component number in all of the material BOMs in which it’s currently in use or change the compo- nent’s quantity in all of the material BOMs in which it’s in use. For such business sce- narios, you use Transaction CS20 to make mass changes to the BOM. In the upper half of the screen, you define the selection criteria; in the lower half, you define the changes that need to be made. When making the desired changes, be sure to select one of the many radio buttons that are available.

sections.title: Work Center
sections.en: 6.2.3 Work Center A work center can be a production facility, a processing unit, a machine or group of machines, or even a laborer or group of laborers directly involved in the production process. It’s also possible to group together machines and laborers in a single work cen- ter. To create a work center, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data  Work Centers  Work Center  Create or use Transaction CR01. Note For process industries, create a resource by following menu path Logistics  Production – Process  Master Data  Resources  Resource  Resource  Create or using Transaction CRC1. © 207 6.2 Master Data On the initial screen, enter Plant “1710” for the relevant new work center. It’s manda- tory to assign a plant while creating a work center. Enter the Work Center as “ASSEM- BLY.” This is an alphanumeric identification to denote a work center. Enter “0001” in the Work Center Category field; this is a control function used to ensure that only the relevant screens are eventually available during work center creation. Work center cat- egory 0001 denotes a machine, whereas 0007 denotes a production line. Other important functions on the screen are Copy from Plant and Copy from Work Cen- ter. Check the boxes of the reference work center views that need to be copied to the new work center. These functions help in reducing data entry efforts during the new work center creation by automatically making available all of the necessary informa- tion from the reference work center to the new work center, which you can change as needed. In the following sections, we’ll cover the various tabs that are available in a work center and the data to be maintained, as well as the data’s impact on business processes. Basic Data Choose the Basic Data tab of the work center and enter a short description of the work center. In the Usage field, enter “009” (see Figure 6.10). A usage is a control function that controls whether this work center eventually becomes available for various other tasks, such as the creation of a routing or the production version. Entering usage 009 denotes using this work center in all task list types. Two important fields on this screen are as follows (but not shown):  Shift Note Type A shift note is an option to record the important shift-related details of a work center. These can be general details of the shift, as well as any specific problems encoun- tered at this specific work center during the production process.  Shift Report Type A shift report compiles and consolidates all of the information from a single shift note or multiple shift notes. It is available either in PDF format or other formats, as well as in the layout defined for a company. The user can also digitally sign a shift report before a printout is possible and send a shift report by email. Recall from Chapter 3 that we discussed the configuration of the Standard Value Key field, in which you entered “SAP1.” The standard value key consists of three activities to note and record during the production process when the ASSEMBLY work center was used. A standard value key denotes activities performed at the work center, as well as the option to record them or not. Some of the activities are production time, setup time, processing time, and labor time. If you enter “SAP1,” the system automatically brings up three activities (Setup, Machine, and Labor) for which the actual details in the confirmation process are recorded (see Figure 6.10). 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 208 Figure 6.10 Basic Data View of Work Center You should enter “Machine” under Key Word and select 3 must be entered under Rule for Maint. If you mark this activity as must be entered, then during the creation of a routing, the production supervisor will be required to enter the actual duration of the activity; otherwise, the system won’t allow you to save the routing. Other maintenance options are may not be entered, should not be entered, and should be entered, which offer various degrees of control, from an information message to a warning message, if the requisite information (duration, in this case) isn’t recorded during confirmation. In addition, you should check the Backflush box on this screen so that the system will automatically issue components at the work center (in this example, ASSEMBLY) during the confirmation process. © 209 6.2 Master Data Note In Chapter 3, we made the field settings that require the selection of the Backflush field. Default Values Choose the Default Values tab to see the options shown in Figure 6.11. Figure 6.11 Default Values View of Work Center Assign the Control key “PP01” to the work center. A control key is a control function that determines whether a production supervisor can perform an activity or a task associ- ated with the work center or not. A control key controls several functions, such as scheduling, cost, automatic GR, print confirmation, and print. If you don’t check any of the boxes of the control key, then those steps or functions can’t be performed at this work center (ASSEMBLY). For example, if the Scheduling box isn’t checked in the con- trol key, then the system won’t perform scheduling at this work center. Note Chapter 3, Section 3.3.6, covers how to configure a control key. Capacities Choose the Capacities tab to see the options shown in Figure 6.12. Enter “SAP005” in Setup Formula, which denotes the setup formula that the system will use during capacity calculation. For the Processing Formula, enter “SAP006,” and for the Teardown Formula, enter “SAP007.” These formula entries are for Capacity category 001, which is Machine. 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 210 Figure 6.12 Capacities View of Work Center Note See Chapter 16 for details about capacity requirements planning. Choose Capacity Header on the toolbar at the bottom of the Capacities tab to open the Change Work Center Capacity: Header screen for Work Center ASSEMBLY (see Figure 6.13). Figure 6.13 Capacity Header © 211 6.2 Master Data The capacity header contains comprehensive capacity details, such as the start and end time of shifts and breaks, the overload percentage allowed for capacity, shifts and inter- vals, and the factory calendar. Note Refer to Chapter 2 for information about setting up the factory calendar and capacity planner group. For this example, you should maintain the following in each field:  Short Text for Capacity: “Assembly” This short text describes the capacity category—for example, a specific production line or machine.  Capacity Responsible: “A” It’s important to assign a capacity planner group to a work center so that during evaluation of capacities, the capacity planner group can be one of the evaluation cri- teria. The capacity planner group shouldn’t be the name of the person but instead the name of the area or domain of responsibilities.  Grouping If, apart from the normal shift timings during the entire calendar year (per the fac- tory calendar), a company has any specific slow or busy seasons in which either an increase or a decrease in the number of shifts or intervals takes place, then a produc- tion planner can logically group these by using the grouping function. For example, in several countries, workers have longer breaks on Fridays, and in other countries, Saturday is a half working day. You can address such business scenarios by using grouping, which doesn’t fall under normal start, finish, and break timings.  Factory Calendar ID: “US” This is the same factory calendar configured in Chapter 2; now, you’re assigning it to the work center.  Capacity Base Unit: “MIN” (for minutes) This is the unit of measure (in hours) in which the system displays the capacity of a work center.  Start Time: “07:00:00” This is the start time of a shift of a work center.  End Time: “16:00:00” This is the end time of a shift of a work center.  Length of breaks: “01:00:00” This is the total length of all breaks during the start and end of shifts. Break timing deducts the availability time of the capacity of a production line. For example, if a machine runs 12 hours a day, with two hours of daily breaks, then the available capacity of the production line is 10 hours. 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 212  Capacity Utilization: “100” (percent) This is the actual capacity utilization factor. This factor has to be as realistic and as close to the actual capacity of a production line as possible to enable the system to calculate the available capacity of the production line. We recommend that you model for statistically unforeseen downtimes. When the SAP S/4HANA system has built up a significant amount of data, use one of many available statistics reports on capacities to update the information in this field. For example, say that a capacity planner has maintained a value of 100% in this field but the statistical reports reveal that the actually utilized capacity is never more than 90%. In this case, updating the information to 90% here ensures realistic and practical capacity planning, and there will be greater confidence in the system-generated results. (Refer to Chapter 19 on reporting.)  No. Ind. Capacities: “1” This is the number of individual capacities associated with a production line. For example, if four individual ice cream mixers are created as one production line, then the individual capacities are four in number and directly multiplied by the timings of the capacity of the production line. As another example, if the available capacity of a production line is 10 hours in a day, and if there are four individual capacities, then the total available capacity of the production line is 40 hours. If you will be using splitting in a routing and you will also define the number of splits, then it’s important to also define the number of capacities because the system checks for this information during scheduling.  Relevant to Finite Scheduling (not shown) Checking this box causes the work center to consider capacity overload during capacity evaluation.  Overload: “5%” (not shown) Setting a capacity overload in percentage terms directly increases the capacity avail- ability of the production line by the defined percentage. If, for example, the total capacity of a production line is 40 hours with 10% overload, then the available capac- ity is 44 hours. A 10% overload of 40 hours is four hours, and it adds up to 40 hours to show the total available capacity at 44 hours.  Long-Term Planning (not shown) Checking this box enables the system to consider the production line availability during long-term planning. Long-term planning typically is a simulative planning of material and production lines over a longer period. Next, click the Intervals button, and then, on the screen shown in Figure 6.14, you can assign specific intervals or shifts that are applicable to the defined duration. For this example, you should define that from 01/01/2025 until 04/02/2025, there will be a shift Sequence consisting of a seven-day cycle (Length of cycle). © 213 6.2 Master Data Figure 6.14 Interval in Capacity Header Scheduling Next, click the Back icon, and then, choose the Scheduling tab to see the options shown in Figure 6.15. Enter the standard formulas available for discrete manufacturing, which we covered in detail in Chapter 3. Figure 6.15 Scheduling View of Work Center Costing Choose the Costing tab to see the options shown in Figure 6.16. Because PP compre- hensively integrates with product cost controlling’s cost object controlling (part of SAP S/4HANA Finance), each work center needs to have a cost center. One cost center can be assigned to multiple work centers, but multiple cost centers cannot be assigned to a work center. 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 214 Figure 6.16 Costing View of Work Center Note You should coordinate with a CO expert to ensure that each cost center and associated activity type for the given controlling area has price planning. In the same step, you should ensure that activity types and cost centers are already in place so that you can assign them in the work center. This is an integration point between PP and CO. Enter the following details:  Start date: “01/01/2024” This is the start date of the availability of the cost center for the work center.  End Date: “12/31/9999” This is the end date of the availability of the cost center for the production line.  Cost Center: “17101301” Every work center needs to have a cost center directly assigned to it so that when work is performed using the work center, the system is able to accumulate all of the costs in the designated cost center.  Activity Type (Alt: Activity Text Setup): “3” The activity type denotes the predefined rate at which the system calculates the costs incurred to perform machine setup. Repeat the same process of entering activity types and formulas for the other remain- ing two activities, Machine and Labor, and then save the work center. © 215 6.2 Master Data

sections.title: Routing
sections.en: 6.2.4 Routing The routing of a material can contain a series of operations. The operation details are defined for each operation based on the production quantity, production time, and associated unit of measure. You assign work centers to the routing, which should also contain an SVK, in which the standard values for setup time, production time, and tear- down time are defined. The routing is the sequence of logical production processes through which a product will, for example, be transformed from a raw material into a finished product. To create the routing of a material, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data  Routings  Routings  Standard Routings  Create or use Transaction CA01. Figure 6.17 shows the initial screen you use to create a routing. Enter the Material as “FG129” and the Plant as “1710.” In the Key Date field, enter “06/27/2024” to denote the date when the routing becomes available in the production process. Because the rout- ing validity starts on June 27, 2024 (06/27/2024), if a production planner creates a pro- duction order for the month of January 2024, this routing won’t be available. Figure 6.17 Initial Screen for Routing If you don’t enter a date during routing creation, then the system automatically assigns the routing’s creation date as the valid from date. The following sections will discuss the various elements and options that are available in the routing. Header Details Next, choose Routings. In the header details of the routing, shown in Figure 6.18, enter the following: 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 216  Usage: “1” (for Production) This makes the routing available during subsequent production activities, in which the system uses the routing during production order creation.  Overall Status: “4” (for Released) This makes the routing available during subsequent production activities, in which the system uses the routing during creation of the production order. If the overall status of the routing is set to Being Created (1), it won’t be available when you create a production order for the material.  Planning Work Center: “ASSEMBLY” This is the work center that was previously created and is now used in the planning and production of the material. Figure 6.18 Header Details in Routing Note Normally, a production planner should have several work centers already created before creating a routing. You should either leave the Planning Work Center field open or blank or make it include the “bottleneck” work center that the production planner wants to evaluate during the planning process. © 217 6.2 Master Data The Lot Size From field denotes that the smallest quantity of the production order must be at least 1 PC for the system to consider using this routing. The Lot Size To field denotes the maximum quantity of the production/order. The Parameters for Dynamic Modification/Inspection Points area of the screen (not shown but located further down the same screen) is the integration point between PP and QM in SAP S/4HANA. The parameters entered there determine whether the system carries out an inspection for the inspection type, for the individual inspection lot, or for the master inspection characteristics. Note Because QM also integrates with discrete manufacturing, we recommend that you engage a QM expert for this activity. Next, click the Operation button to access the screen in Figure 6.19, where you can maintain the Work Center as “ASSEMBLY.” This denotes assigning a previously created work center to an operation, which is used in the production of material and for which the routing is being created. An operation defines, for example, a single-level detail of a production step. The second work center, Work, denotes the second step in the produc- tion process. Then, you should double-click Operation 0010, which opens the screen in Figure 6.20. Figure 6.19 Operations Overview of Routing Operations Figure 6.20 shows the detailed operations view for maintaining the following entries:  Setup: “50 MIN” Here, you define the time it takes to set up the machine for this task. Any deviation in setup time, either positive or negative, has a direct impact on the costs of goods manufactured (COGM).  Act. Type: “3” An activity type is a result of the direct integration of PP with the product cost con- trolling functionality. In fact, PP completely integrates with product cost con- trolling. An activity type contains the financial rate (value based) to perform an 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 218 activity during production. So, five hours of production time has an associated cost of producing the material. Any deviation in production time, either positive or neg- ative, has a direct implication for the COGM.  Machine: “150 MIN” Recall that you defined in the work center (in the rule for maintenance, when you selected the Must Be Entered option) that recording duration information in the routing is mandatory, so it’s mandatory to define the time the operation will take in this work center’s manufacturing process.  Labor: “150 MIN” Here, you define the time it takes to dismantle or tear down the setup of the machine or the production line. Any deviation in teardown time, either positive or negative, has a direct implication on COGM. Figure 6.20 Operation Details in Routing Enter the Act. Type for each of the three activities: Setup, Machine, and Labor. These durations are for the Base Quantity of 10 PC of material FG129 produced. Notice that the system automatically incorporates the Control Key that was defined in the work center, ASSEMBLY, in the previous step. © 219 6.2 Master Data Next, scroll down to see the screen area shown in Figure 6.21. There, you assign the reduction strategy that was configured for plant 1710 in Chapter 3. If you check the Tear- down/Wait Parallel (simulation) box here, it enables a planner to see simulated details during scheduling. You can also define normal and minimum interoperation times. The interoperation times consist of wait time, queue time, and move (transport) time. During reduction—that is, during scheduling conflicts in basic dates—the system switches to minimum interoperation times and performs further reduction based on the reduction strategy and the defined reduction levels. Next, scroll down the same screen to see the screen areas shown in Figure 6.22. With Splitting, a production planner can divide a large production lot into several smaller lots, provided that there are several individual capacities to manage it. The system checks the number of splits with individual capacities defined in the work center. With Overlapping, the system passes the processed parts of the total lot directly on to the next machine. You should define the minimum overlap time and the minimum send-ahead quantity to the next operation if there’s a business need for it. Figure 6.21 Operation Details in Routing: Interoperation Times 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 220 Figure 6.22 Item Details in Routing: Splitting and Overlapping The information in the General data tab (see Figure 6.23) relates to the percentage scrap (planned) for the operation and integrating with the human resources (HR) system. For example, the person doing confirmation on the shop floor can be integrated with the HR system. Figure 6.23 Item Details in Routing: General Data © 221 6.2 Master Data Next, scroll down further on the same screen to see the screen area shown in Figure 6.24. Here, you can check the Subcontracting box to reflect the details that can be main- tained in case the material processing involves subcontracting. A subcontracting pro- cess entails engaging the services of a service provider to perform some of the steps in the production process, which happens when either there isn’t an in-house expert available or the company finds it more cost effective to outsource those steps. Figure 6.24 Item Details in Routing: Subcontracting Check the Subcontracting box and maintain the procurement details in the remaining fields. When a production planner creates a production order for the material, the sys- tem will simultaneously create a purchase requisition for the subcontracting opera- tions. You can use the User-Specific Fields section (not shown) to maintain additional details related to routing that can’t be maintained elsewhere. Components Assignment Next, press (F6) or follow menu path Goto  Operations Overview, and the system will bring you back to the Operations Overview screen. On this screen, choose Components Allocation to open the screen shown in Figure 6.25. Here, you can find the component allocation in routing. These components are directly taken from a material’s BOM, 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 222 which you created in the previous step. If you don’t assign a specific component to a specific operation, then the system assigns all components to the first operation of the routing during lead-time scheduling. This means that all components must be avail- able at the first operation so that during backflush, the system can consume all compo- nents in the first operation during its confirmation. Figure 6.25 Material Component Overview If your company’s business process entails that a few components are used or needed in the later stages of the production process, then you should select them and use the Reassign button to assign specific components to the operation. For this example, assign Component 50 to Oper./Act. 0020. This means that while the system will con- sume other components in the confirmation of the first operation (0010) using the backflush functionality, it will consume component 50 when the user confirms opera- tion 0020. To ensure that the system uses the backflush functionality, check the Back- flushing box. If a component’s quantity is maintained with a negative value—say, 50,000 KG—it denotes a by-product being produced in the production process. So, although all components in a material BOM are consumed during the production pro- cess, the coproducts or by-products are generated or produced. Material Assignment If you go back to the routing header screen and choose MatlAssignment, the popup shown in Figure 6.26 will appear. Material FG129 in Plnt 1710 is assigned an internal group of 41710010 with the group counter (GrC) set to 1. Later, we’ll use this group (41710010) and the group counter (1) to create the next PP master data element, which is the production version. It’s important to add that every combination of a group and a group counter reflects certain planned working processes. You can assign several materials together with their respective plants to this routing group. All such materials belonging to this routing group will have the same group but will have ascending group counter numbers. © 223 6.2 Master Data Figure 6.26 Material Assignment Tips and Tricks If there are several materials undergoing the same production process, there’s no longer a need to create separate routings for each.
