sections.en: 6.9.2 Cost Calculation Similar to automatic goods movement errors, cost calculation errors arise during the confirmation process, when the system updates the costs based on entered data, such as activities. Depending on how the system is configured, errors occurring during the confirmation process and cost calculation can appear as warnings or errors. For warn- ings, the system allows saving the confirmation so that the errors can be processed later on. For errors during cost calculation in confirmation, the system doesn’t allow saving the confirmation. To reprocess the actual costs of the production order, follow menu path Logistics  Pro- duction  Shop Floor Control  Confirmation  Reprocessing  Actual Costs or use Transac- tion COFC. Figure 6.73 shows the postprocessing error log that requires you to engage and coordinate with the product cost controlling resource to manage it. Figure 6.73 Postprocessing Confirmations with Errors in Cost Calculations © 271

sections.title: Settlement and Completion
sections.en: 6.10 Settlement and Completion 6.10 Settlement and Completion When a production order is created, the system calculates the planned cost of the order. Thereafter, every business function that is performed will directly be reflected as costs, which will continue to accrue. This occurs, for example, when components are withdrawn against a production order, when an operation’s activities are confirmed, or when any external processing (subcontracting) is involved in the production process. The system debits all of these actual costs and only credits them when GR against the production order is undertaken. The basis for crediting an order is either the standard price or the moving average price that has been maintained in the Accounting 1 view of the material master. The standard price control may not reflect the actual costs of pro- duction and so must be passed on to settle the production order. The product cost controlling team normally proceeds with the settlement process after all production steps are ensured and the order has a status of Delivery Completed. With the right settings in place, the system assigns a settlement rule to the production order as soon as the order is created. A settlement rule defines the recipient to which the sys- tem will settle the order and distributes the percentages of costs to various settlement receivers. Some of the settlement receivers can be a material, a cost center, a project, or a sales order. Normally, the product cost controlling team assigns the settlement receiver of the production order to the material account—so the settlement receiver is the material, and the settlement share is 100%. To display the settlement rule of a pro- duction order, you choose Header  Settlement Rule. When GR against the production order is performed, the system credits the production order and debits the material stock account. If the price control in the material master is the standard price, then the system valuates the material at the standard price. Because the actual production costs often vary, the system posts the difference to the price difference account during the settlement process. For the moving average price control in the material master, the system automatically valuates the material at the moving average that it determines. For the moving average calculation, it takes the total value of the material stock account and divides it by the total quantity of material available in all of the storage locations. The overheads can be separately evaluated, and that must be part of product cost con- trolling. Overheads can be management or employee salaries, administration, and even utilities, such as electricity. The variances can also be separately evaluated between planned and actual costs that occur due to the actual components and activities consumed that are different from (either larger than or smaller than) the plan, according to the BOM and routing. Similarly, as soon as the GI for the production order takes place, the system will reflect this as WIP, and it will remain so until the GR is posted and the stocks are updated. A 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing production supervisor or even a cost controller can individually or collectively view the WIP details of production orders. The completion of an order is logically divided into two distinct areas to distinguish logistical completion from accounting-based completion. When an order is technically completed and assigned the status TECO, the system updates the MRP-relevant details and deletes all reservations of components and capacity requirements. A production supervisor or even the cost controller can technically complete an order at any time by choosing Functions  Restrict Processing  Complete Technically on the order screen. After that, it isn’t possible to make changes to the production order. Furthermore, with a technically completed order, it also isn’t possible to post costs to the order or perform any further confirmation. While on the production order screen, a production planner can set the completion sta- tus by choosing Functions  Restrict Processing  Close. The system denotes the comple- tion with the status CLSD. Both the completion and the technical completion steps can be achieved in mass processing steps. We explain the mass processing function in Section 6.11.4.

sections.title: Additional Functions and Information Systems
sections.en: 6.11 Additional Functions and Information Systems In this section, we cover several of the functions that you can use to optimize business processes. We suggest checking out the features and functionalities available in each area, such as master data, transactional data, and reports. You can do this by navigating through each menu path and checking out the available options in the relevant folders (e.g., for the routing or work center).

sections.title: From Planned Order: Individual Conversion
sections.en: 6.11.1 From Planned Order: Individual Conversion Although this entire chapter is primarily focused on covering the master data and the transactional data in the production processes, a production planner can also effec- tively use the planning data, which the system generates when the MRP is run. The out- put of MRP is the generation of planned orders, which can be converted into produc- tion orders for in-house production and purchase requisitions for external procure- ment. For in-house produced materials, it’s important that the system is able to find the PP master data, such as the BOM, routing, and production version. There are two options for converting planned orders into production orders: interac- tive conversion in Transaction MD04 and individual conversion using Transaction CO40. With production order creation, all of the remaining functions are the same as covered in this chapter, such as release, cost calculation, control instruction genera- tion, and scheduling. 272© 273 6.11 Additional Functions and Information Systems Note See Chapter 13 on MRP.

sections.title: From Planned Orders: Collective Conversion
sections.en: 6.11.2 From Planned Orders: Collective Conversion You can convert several planned orders into production orders collectively by using Transaction CO41. On the initial screen, enter the selection parameters that the system will consider in bringing up the relevant planned orders. Then, sign an Order Type on the initial screen so that the collective conversion of planned orders to production orders is all of the same order type. Note You can also use report PPBICO40 for the automatic conversion of planned orders into production orders in a batch job.

sections.title: Production Order Creation without a Material
sections.en: 6.11.3 Production Order Creation without a Material You can also create a production order without reference to a material; for example, there may be a business need to create a capacity placeholder for postprocessing or maintenance works without using the maintenance orders. You can also use this option to record extra production costs incurred during rework, and you can reference the main (parent) production order for settlement. Use Transaction CO07 to create production orders without a material. On the initial screen, enter the short text and assign a routing. If no routing is available, the system generates the operation from the default value that was defined in configuration.

sections.title: Mass Processing
sections.en: 6.11.4 Mass Processing To process a large volume of transaction data or simply optimize routine business pro- cesses, a production supervisor can use the mass processing functionality for the fol- lowing functions:  Performing confirmation  Creating capacity requirements  Picking  Costing  Checking material availability  Generating control instructions  Printing shop floor papers 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 274 For this example, you perform the technically complete function using mass process- ing by following menu path Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Control  Mass Reprocessing or by using Transaction COHV. Figure 6.74 shows the initial parameters entry screen, in which the system offers a large number of parameters to select from. Here, you enter Production Plant “1710,” enter Order Type “PP10,” and choose the Mass Processing tab. Figure 6.74 Initial Screen of Mass Processing Function On the next screen, select Technically Complete from the Function dropdown and click Execute (see Figure 6.75). Figure 6.75 Confirmation through Mass Processing Function © 275 6.11 Additional Functions and Information Systems The system then opens the worklist, where you can select Production Order 80000001 and click Execute. The system then displays the mass processing log (see Figure 6.76). Figure 6.76 Mass Processing Log

sections.title: Information Systems
sections.en: 6.11.5 Information Systems Due to the highly integrated nature of the SAP S/4HANA system, any activity or busi- ness function performed with reference to the production order automatically updates the relevant information. Figure 6.77 shows the screen that appears after you choose Goto  Documented Goods Movements in the production order display option (Transaction CO03), in which all of the GIs and receipts performed in MM are automatically updated. Figure 6.77 Documented Goods Movement in Production Order 6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing 276 Figure 6.78 shows the itemized costs of the production order, providing a detailed cost breakdown. It also keeps on updating the planned costs with actual costs to provide a quick evaluation. This is an integration point between PP and the product cost con- trolling team. Figure 6.78 Item Cost Overview of Production Order

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 6.12 Summary The production process in discrete manufacturing entails the creation of important master data followed by production execution that itself consists of order creation, GI, confirmation, and GR. Handling scrap, coproducts, by-products, completion, and set- tlement processes occurs in both manufacturing types (discrete and process). Shop floor papers, such as object lists, can also be printed. A large number of tools and func- tionalities can be used to optimize business processes, such as backflush, automatic GR, mass processing, and postprocessing. WIP batches can also be implemented to gain better control over the entire production process. The next chapter discusses the business processes in production planning for process industries (PP-PI). ©

sections.title: Process Manufacturing Overview
sections.en: 7.1 Process Manufacturing Overview Figure 7.1 shows an overview of the end-to-end process involved in process manufac- turing. The business processes involved can be divided broadly into the following areas:  Process planning  Process order execution  Process management  Order closure Production planning in PP-PI begins when a materials planner converts the output of MRP, which in this case is a planned order, into a process order. This is then followed by a material availability check to ensure that the required quantities of components needed to produce the material are available. If the material quantity calculation func- tionality is enabled in the master recipe of the material, the system calculates the com- ponents’ quantities. If not, the system reads the information from the material’s BOM. At this stage, the system can perform batch determination of the components that are going to be used in the production process. The next step is to proceed with releasing the process order, as well as printing the pro- cess order. With a released process order, a control recipe can be generated. A generated control recipe takes the form of a PI sheet. Several of these process order management activities can run automatically or in the background to minimize the need to manage them manually. For example, say a planner determines that when they create a process order, the system can automatically release it too. If it can’t, the process order will have to be released manually. Alternatively, they can use a separate transaction to release a large number of process orders (mass processing), which again they can do as a manual task. The MM functionality plays an important role when raw materials and components need to be issued against a process order. QM processes, if integrated with PP, enable extensive in-process (during production) quality inspection checks. During this time, the planner also maintains the PI sheet and assigns it a Complete status. Then, they undertake confirmation of the process order, either at the individual phase level or at the entire process order level. When goods are produced, it’s again time to engage MM to ensure GR against the process order. Sending the process messages back to the SAP S/4HANA system is the last step of process management. © 279

sections.title: Master Data in Process Manufacturing
sections.en: 7.2 Master Data in Process Manufacturing Figure 7.1 Production Planning and Execution in Process Industries Cost object controlling activities—such as WIP determination, variance calculation, and settlement—are order specific in nature and usually processed in the background. PP completely integrates with cost object controlling in the SAP S/4HANA system, so it’s imperative to ensure extensive coordination for comprehensive business processes mapping. To optimize and bring greater visibility to the business processes, you can implement and integrate several additional processes and functionalities, such as digital signa- tures, engineering change management, SAP Document Management service, coprod- ucts and by-products, shift notes, and shift reports. You can also integrate QM during production (in-process quality inspection) or at the time of GR. 7.2 Master Data in Process Manufacturing Process manufacturing has its own unique master data that often overlaps with other production types, such as discrete manufacturing and (REM). If the master data is set up in the right sequence, it’s much easier and more logical to interconnect the master data because the predecessor-successor relationship is already taken care of. Master data creation for process manufacturing begins with the material master of the product (a finished good or an assembly). You create a BOM for the product, via which Order Request Process Order Creation Availability Check Resources/Line Scheduling Resource Selection Batch Determination Process Order Release Order Printing Send Control Recipe Maintain PI Sheet Process Messages for Different Destinations Material Staging/ Material Withdrawals Order Confirmations Goods Receipt Variance Calculation Process Order Settlement Batch Record Archive/Delete Material Quantity Calculation Order Closing Process Planning Process Management Process Order Execution In-Process Quality Inspection 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 280 the company will produce and assign components, together with the quantities needed to produce the product. If needed, you can define a scrap percentage at the operation or component level. The next master data creation step is to create the resource and then create the master recipe for the material, in which the previously created resource is assigned. Finally, a production version for the material is created and the material’s BOM and the master recipe are assigned to it. When all the master data is in place, the CO team can create a product cost estimate of the material and release it. Note We strongly recommend that you maintain close coordination and liaison with the CO team to ensure that when they’re working with the PP team, both teams are com- pletely aligned with their working and reporting needs. For example, for each resource, there’s a need to assign a cost center, which the CO team should provide to the PP team. It may provide one cost center for multiple resources or one cost center for an individual resource, depending on how the CO team wants to see the cost center reporting and evaluation. The following make up the important master data in PP-PI:  Material master  BOM  Resource  Production version  Master recipe We’ll discuss each in detail in the following sections.

sections.title: Material Master
sections.en: 7.2.1 Material Master The material master is the central master record in logistics and the supply chain. The system defines a material as a substance or commodity that a company buys or sells on a commercial basis. A material can also be either consumed or produced. Some exam- ples of materials are raw materials, packing materials, consumables, semifinished goods, and finished goods. The material isn’t just restricted to production-based pro- cesses but is used in all those processes for which the company wants to maintain inventory (stock items). Therefore, a company may also have materials that are used in plant maintenance (PM) processes, or it may even have nonvaluated materials. In PP-PI, there is extensive use of batch management. A batch is a uniquely identifiable partial quantity of a material, and batches of a material are managed in separate stocks. In a production process, a batch is a quantity of a specific material produced during a standardized production run. This quantity therefore represents a nonreproducible © 281 7.2 Master Data in Process Manufacturing unit with unique specifications. The key properties of a batch are homogeneity and nonreproducibility. A batch can be traced across the entire supply chain—that is, from the receipt of the raw material to processing in production and the creation of the final product, all the way through sales and delivery to the customer. Complete batch traceability, batch determination, and batch derivation functionalities are available. The batch informa- tion cockpit (Transaction BMBC) can be used for complete top-down or bottom-up evaluation of material batches. The system creates batches for a material, and the data of the material master is valid for all batches assigned to it. In contrast to the material master, a batch master record contains data that uniquely identifies the corresponding batch and characterizes the unit as one that can’t be reproduced. The characteristic batch specifications are assigned using characteristics from the classification system in the material master and are inherited by the corresponding batch master records. Note Chapter 10 covers batch management.

sections.title: Bill of Materials
sections.en: 7.2.2 Bill of Materials The BOM in PP-PI is the same as in discrete manufacturing. Refer to Chapter 3 and Chapter 6 for a detailed understanding of the configuration of BOMs and the business processes they’re involved in. The material quantity calculation is unique only to PP-PI and uses components of the material defined in its BOM. When you’re calculating the components’ quantities that the system should use in reference to each other, it refers to the information in the BOM. See Section 7.2.5 concerning the master recipe for a detailed understanding of material quantity calculation. To create a BOM, you use Transaction CS01.

sections.title: Resource
sections.en: 7.2.3 Resource The resource in process manufacturing is the same as the work center in discrete man- ufacturing. Refer to Chapter 3 and Chapter 6 for a detailed understanding of the config- uration and business processes involved in work centers (resources in PP-PI). To create a resource, use Transaction CRC1. The system offers and makes available a standard configuration for PP-PI that you can use if your company’s business processes aren’t too complex. For example, you can set the Usage as “008” (for master recipe + process order) and the Standard Value Key as “SAP4” (process manufacturing), in which only Duration is listed as an activity. The con- trol key that’s available for use is PI01 (Master Recipe/Process Order). 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 282

sections.title: Production Version
sections.en: 7.2.4 Production Version A production version determines which alternative BOM the system should use in combination with the master recipe for process manufacturing. In PP-PI, it’s manda- tory to define a production version. The system uses the production version during the creation of a master recipe to identify the BOM for the material and pull the BOM details from the master recipe. When creating the master recipe for a material and plant combination, you must also enter the production version for the material on the initial screen. You should create the production version prior to creating the master recipe and then use it to create the master recipe. To create a new production version, use Transaction C223. You can also create a produc- tion version in the MRP 4 view of the material master or even in the Work Scheduling view. Refer to Chapter 6, Section 6.2.5, for a detailed understanding of the business pro- cess of a production version and how to create one in the SAP system. Note Creating a production version directly from Transaction MM02 should be an exception because there may still be some incomplete data at this stage. Use Transaction C223 to achieve this objective instead.

sections.title: Master Recipe Creation
sections.en: 7.2.5 Master Recipe Creation Before creating a master recipe, you can create a production version and include BOM details only (without the master recipe details because these aren’t available at that time). Next, create the master recipe and give reference to the production version because it’s mandatory to enter a production version during master recipe creation. Then, go back to the production version and incorporate the master recipe details, including the group number and group counter that the system generated when the master recipe was saved. The system suggests the master recipe group number and the group counter when the production version is again called up. With this approach, cre- ating a master recipe makes a materials list (BOM) available in the master recipe, and you can then use it in the material quantity calculation. A second approach that you can use to creating the master recipe is to first create a master recipe group without reference to a material and plant combination. When the system generates the recipe group number, create a production version of the material and enter the BOM and master recipe details. Finally, when the header material num- ber is assigned in the change master recipe option for the master recipe group, the sys- tem prompts you to enter a production version to enable it to explode the BOM. To create a master recipe for which the production version already exists, follow menu path Logistics  Production—Process  Master Data  Master Recipes  Recipe and © 283 7.2 Master Data in Process Manufacturing Material List  Create or use Transaction C201. On the initial screen of the master rec- ipe, enter the Material, Plant, and Profile (see Figure 7.2), and the header screen will appear. Recall from Chapter 4 that the master recipe’s profile is a control function to allow or disallow certain functions, such as XSteps being optional or mandatory. The Key date controls when the master recipe will be available for production process. You can enter the key date manually or, if no entry is maintained, the system will use the date of master recipe creation as the key date. Figure 7.2 Initial Screen of Master Recipe Creation We’ll discuss the different elements of this screen in the following sections. Recipe Header Figure 7.3 shows the master recipe header details screen. The Charge Quantity Range area is valid for the lot size quantities in the master recipe, and it contains the default values for the operation, the phase, and secondary resources. A proportional relation- ship exists between the default values for operation quantities and their unit of mea- sure and the recipe quantities and their unit of measure. In contrast to master recipes, you maintain this relationship directly in the operation details in routing and rate routings. For example, say the master recipe unit of measure is pieces (PC) and the operational unit of measure is kilograms (kg). That means that for every 7 kg of the operation, there are 4 PC of the master recipe, with a quotient of 4/7. The charge quantity is 4 PC, and the operation quantity is 7 kg. The system also provides the option to maintain a base quantity for detailed work. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 284 Figure 7.3 Header Details of Master Recipe Materials The master recipe integrates the details of the operations and the BOM together into one master data element by using the production version. The system explodes the BOM in the master recipe to bring up the details of the material BOM. The material BOM details in the task list (the master recipe) help enable a feature unique to process manufacturing known as material quantity calculation. Note It’s crucial to adopt one of the two approaches mentioned previously to create a pro- duction version in relation to the master recipe to ensure the master recipe contains the materials (the BOM). Material Quantity Calculation In a process order, the system calculates the components’ quantities directly from the BOM and takes the material quantity calculation into account. With the material quantity calculation, the following options are available:  Change the header product quantity with reference to the components’ quantities or even with respect to the active ingredient proportions. © 285 7.2 Master Data in Process Manufacturing  Calculate the planned scrap at the phase level; the system includes it in the planned production cost.  Change the components’ quantities with reference to each other, the header prod- uct, or the active ingredient proportions (the batch characteristics and their values).  Change operation or phase quantities when these aren’t in proportion to the prod- uct quantity. For material quantity calculation to work effectively, you need to make sure to create the master recipe with reference to the BOM consisting of components and quantities. Because the planned scrap of the component is entered either in the material master or in the BOM, the system automatically increases the component quantity during planned order or process order creation. You should therefore use the planned scrap of a component as a variable to calculate the other component’s quantity using the mate- rial quantity calculation formula. When a process order is created, the system automatically calculates the quantities based on the formulas. For a formula that is processed at the batch level and that also uses active ingredient proportions (batch characteristics values), the material quantity calculation needs to be triggered manually in the process order and after batch deter- mination. Note Note that the system only considers batch characteristics with numeric values. When the system explodes the BOM in the master recipe, you should go to the Material Quantity Calculation screen shown in Figure 7.4 by choosing Goto  Material Quantity Calculation or by clicking the Material Quantity Calc. (calculation) icon in the Materials tab of the master recipe. Figure 7.4 Material Quantity Calculation in Master Recipe 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 286 In general, you perform the following steps when entering the formula for the material quantity calculation: 1. On the screen shown in Figure 7.4, place the cursor on the field for which you want to change the quantity using a formula and choose the Select Formula button in the menu bar. 2. In the Formula Definition box, enter the formula or equation, which derives the out- put field value. 3. While creating a formula, double-click the variables to be included in the formula or place the cursor on the variable and choose the Insert in formula button on the menu bar. You can also use formula operators such as +, –, *, /, DIV, and MOD, as well as exponen- tial, rounding (ROUND), absolute values (ABS), truncation (TRUNC), EXP, LOG, SIN, COS, TAN, square roots (SQRT), IF THEN ELSE conditions, and IF THEN NOT conditions. Operations and Phases Master recipes use a phase, which works in the same manner as operations do in rout- ing for discrete manufacturing. It’s easier to maintain detailed levels working at the phase level in the master recipe because a production supervisor can manage and incorporate more production details, including process management. In the master recipe, you assign activities such as production duration and labor hours at the phase level and not at the operation level. Hence, the confirmation of a process order is recorded for a phase and not an operation. A resource (work center) is assigned at the operation level, and the phases below the operation then adopt the resource that was assigned at the operation level. The system assigns the standard values and activi- ties (controlled by a control key in the resource) as active at the phase level and not at the operation level. The sum total of standard values at a phase is in fact the total time required to process the operation. The system assigns the components of the BOM (materials list) to phases and not to operations. However, integration of in-process quality inspections of QM can take place either at the operation level or the phase level. To create a phase below an operation, you check the Phase box in the Operations tab, which then automatically copies the resource from the operation. At the same time, when you’re defining a phase, you must assign the superior operation so that the sys- tem knows which specific phase relates to which operation. You can maintain relationships among various phases, such as start-finish, finish-start, finish-finish, and start-start. The phases can work either in parallel or in overlapping sequences. In the Operations tab of the master recipe, you can access the phase rela- tionship screen for phases by selecting the phases and choosing Goto  Relationships. © 287 7.2 Master Data in Process Manufacturing You can assign individual control recipe destinations at the phase level and assign the process instructions in the respective phases of the master recipe. If the scope of gener- ation is defined in the configuration of the process instructions, it reduces data mainte- nance efforts at the master recipe level. Alternatively, you can maintain the desired pro- cess instruction details either in the master recipe or in the process order. You can assign process instructions that have characteristic values based on a material at the master recipe level. To assign process instructions to the phases in the Operations tab of the master recipe, use the menu path Goto  Process Management  Process Instructions. Figure 7.5 shows the Operations tab of the master recipe, in which the Operation is 0010. Enter the “PACKING” Resource at the operation level and the system will auto- matically copy it to all of the phases below it. The phase is 0020, you denote it by check- ing the Phase Indicator box. When you define an operation as a phase, you also have to define the Sup. Operation, which, in this example, is 0010 (the operation). The control recipe Destinatn (destination) is PP. This is the same control recipe destina- tion that was configured in Chapter 4. Figure 7.5 Operations Overview in Master Recipe Notice that the system automatically copies the Control key, PI01, from the PACKING Resource. Select the phase 0020 and double-click the line item 0020 (the operation, which is basically a phase), and the system will bring up the screen shown in Figure 7.6. This shows the Standard values tab of the master recipe, where you can maintain the duration of the activities, such as the Machine, Labor, and Setup (not shown) hours required to produce the material in each operation. From here, choose the Process instructions tab to configure the process instructions that are an integral part of pro- cess management. We’ll visit this screen a bit later, in the upcoming section. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 288 Figure 7.6 Standard Values in Master Recipe Because characteristics have such an important role to play in process management, we’ll cover them in the next section.

sections.title: Characteristics
sections.en: 7.3 Characteristics A characteristic describes an object and can have one value or a range of values that are valid. In this section, we’ll begin by covering the steps required to create a characteristic and the available options. We’ll also cover the associated configuration settings that can help users effectively create characteristics and put relevant controls in place. You use Transaction CT04 to create a characteristic, via the menu path SAP Menu  Cross-Application Components  Classification System  Master Data  Characteristics. On the screen shown in Figure 7.7, information related to the characteristic is defined, such as description, data type, number of characteristics, decimal places (if any), and unit of measure. Table 7.1 lists descriptions some of the important fields seen in the var- ious tabs shown in Figure 7.7. Field Names Field Descriptions and Values Description Description of the characteristic; for example, thickness. Char. Group Groups together similar characteristics in one group. Status Status of the characteristic, for example, Released, to indicate it’s ready for use. Table 7.1 Details of Individual Fields of Characteristic © 289 7.3 Characteristics Figure 7.7 Basic Data Screen for Transaction CT04 Data Type Indicates the type of data this characteristic has; for example, date, time format, or numeric format. Number of Chars Indicates the number of characters a value of this characteristic can contain. Decimal Places Indicates the number of decimal places if the format is numeric. Unit of Measure Unit of measure of the characteristic (quantitative/numeric), if any. Single-value/Multiple Values (radio buttons) Option for whether a single value or multiple values are allowed for a characteristic. An example of multiple value usage is when you can select a surface finish as coarse, pitted, and uneven. For multi- ple values, the system offers a checkbox for each value to select from the dropdown list. Negative Vals Allowed (checkbox) Option for whether negative values are allowed. You can’t enter a negative value for a characteristic if you don’t check this box. Entry Required (checkbox) Option to indicate whether the user must enter a value for this characteristic. If you don’t check this box, then entering a value is optional. Field Names Field Descriptions and Values Table 7.1 Details of Individual Fields of Characteristic (Cont.) 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 290 Next, switch over to the Values tab (see Figure 7.8) and maintain values for this charac- teristic. For example, say a characteristic provides an option to record the pump value, which you can search for in the material master along with any other characteristic. Check the Additional Values box to ensure that values other than the prefixed 10, 20, or 30 are also allowed for entry as input values. Next, choose the Addnl Data tab in Figure 7.7 to see more options. Enter the relevant SAP Document Management service document’s number, document type, document part, and document version, if any. The following are the details of the various check- boxes and their functions:  Not Ready for Input No value can be maintained for this characteristic. In other words, a preassigned default or calculated value automatically fills the characteristic field, and the user has no option to make an entry.  No Display The system won’t display a characteristic.  Display Allowed Values If several values are already available from the Values tab of the characteristic, the system displays those values to help the user with data entry.  Unformatted Entry If this box is checked, the system makes the provision to set the characteristic’s field length to a maximum of 30 so the user can make multiple entries, separated by colons. If it’s not checked, then the system displays the exact length of the character- istic for data entry.  Proposed Template If this box is checked, the system proposes or displays a template to help the user during data entry. Choose the Basic Data tab to view the template details. Finally, on the Restrictions tab, you can restrict the use of a characteristic to specific class types; otherwise, it will be available for use in all class types. For example, entering class type “001” will restrict this characteristic to the material class type only. Save the entries. Note If a characteristic with a date format is created and then used in any class, the system will not provide the standard option of bringing up a calendar from which to choose a date. The user will have to enter the date manually. If a user wants to have the calen- dar option in the dropdown list of the date characteristic, then you need to engage the assistance of an ABAP consultant. © 291 7.3 Characteristics Figure 7.8 Allowed Values for Characteristic Tips and Tricks The standard system offers the provision to use a separate batch input program to upload large numbers of characteristics. SAP S/4HANA includes a number of templates that you can use to enter information into the characteristic values. If a new template is required for a specific characteristic value, you can configure it. Many characters are configured for use in the templates, and you can find them by using the configuration navigation path SAP IMG  Cross-Application Components  Classification System  Characteristics  Define Template Characters. Figure 7.9 shows the characters that are defined for use in the templates. The Usage field determines how the character is used via the following options:  1: A numeric character that is valid for numeric and character formats  2: An alphanumeric character that is valid for character formats  3: A character that is valid for the character format  4: A preliminary sign that is valid for the character format  5: A separator that is valid for the character format By accessing the transaction using configuration navigation path SAP IMG  Cross- Application Components  Classification System  Characteristics  Define Templates, you can configure new characteristic value templates or modify existing templates. Figure 7.10 shows the templates that have been configured for use with characteristics. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 292 Figure 7.9 Defining Characters for Characteristic Templates Figure 7.10 Creation and Modification of New Characteristic Value Templates

sections.title: Process Management
sections.en: 7.4 Process Management Because a large number of features and functionalities of process instructions exist within an operation’s phase of the master recipe, the topic of process instructions war- rants a separate section in this chapter. This section deals with process instructions (which are part of process management) that need to be defined in the Process instruc- tions tab shown previously in Figure 7.6. In a manufacturing organization, there is a permanent requirement to monitor system performance and plant parameters. For example, when the production of a certain item is scheduled, the plant operator needs to have a series of clear and comprehensive instructions to follow. Similarly, the plant operator is required to record and report back data, such as steam temperature twice a shift or an abnormal vibration in the © 293 7.4 Process Management suction pump, so that it will be available for future reference or to help with corrective action. Therefore, a functionality is needed in the SAP system that can transfer and communi- cate all such information in a timely manner from the plant operator back to the pro- cess control system. This has been made possible by the process management func- tionality. Note Transmitting information between an SAP S/4HANA system and a process control sys- tem is made possible by defining the type of the control recipe destination. We focus on the transfer of information to PI sheets to show that implementing process man- agement can still yield significant added value without the need to integrate SAP S/4HANA with a process control system. Process management completely integrates with core functional areas such as MM, QM, and SAP Document Management service. It offers functionality such as GIs and GRs, process order confirmations, and results recording of quality inspection data. All of this information helps in analysis and report generation functions, and it also bene- fits business process owners who directly use the information. The following list summarizes the functions supported by process management in PP-PI:  Receiving control recipes from released process orders  Sending control recipes to process operators or process control systems  Preparing process instructions as texts so that the process operators can display them on their computer screens  Receiving, checking, and sending process messages with actual process data  Monitoring process messages and control recipes  Manually creating process messages In the following sections, we’ll cover some of the elements of process management and how to intuitively create a PI sheet.

sections.title: Elements in Process Management
sections.en: 7.4.1 Elements in Process Management Figure 7.11 illustrates the various elements involved in process management for data flow. Starting from the top left, creating a process order forms the basis for the genera- tion of the control recipe. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 294 Figure 7.11 Overview of Process Management The system sends the control recipe to the predefined control recipe destinations, in the form of a PI sheet. The process operator follows the instructions given on the PI sheet and also fills out the PI sheet with relevant plant parameters and other important data. The process operator then returns the PI sheet as a process message to either the SAP S/4HANA system or an external system.

sections.title: Integrating Process Management
sections.en: 7.4.2 Integrating Process Management In an automated environment, object linking and embedding (OLE) for process control (Open Platform Communications [OPC]) and SAP OPC Data Access enable the system to read and write data points and events using the OPC server for the SAP S/4HANA sys- tem. This function is also available in production orders (discrete manufacturing). Note OPC is a standard that uses component object model/distributed component object model (COM/DCOM) technology to define interfaces independent of the manufacturer for use in an industry. SAP designed the OPC standard especially for the process control level. OPC servers allow access to various data sources, such as process control systems, programmable logic controllers, and temperature sensors, and thus, they provide pro- cess data that can be requested by OPC clients. SAP Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence offers an adaptive manufacturing solution for production. SAP Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence provides manufacturing companies increased flexibility through improved linking of the SAP S/4HANA system to the production process level and by making real-time information available, and SAP Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence can also be used in both the process order environment and the production order environment. It provides standardized, preconfigured connectors to enable real-time data integration in manu- facturing execution systems and supervisory control and data acquisition systems. Process Planning Process Order Further Message Destinations Process Management Process Control Execution E-1 E-2 E-3 SAP S/4HANA or External System Control Recipe Process Messages PI Sheet Process Control System © 295 7.4 Process Management A process operator can run real-time analyses and display the results in browser- and role-based dashboards. These analyses provide important information for checking and supporting decision-making, such as warnings, job lists, analyses, reports, and real- time messages about production variance.

sections.title: Process Instructions
sections.en: 7.4.3 Process Instructions An operation in the master recipe may have several phases, and each phase requires a control recipe destination. After a control recipe destination is defined for an opera- tion, it automatically applies to all phases of that operation. Within each phase, process management-related information is incorporated, including process message catego- ries, process instruction characteristics, and control recipe destinations. You can assign process instructions to the phases in the Operations tab of the master recipe. To do this, select the specific phase and use the following path in the Operations tab: Master Recipe—Goto  Process Management  Process Instructions. In the resulting screen, shown in Figure 7.12, there are two process instruction catego- ries: AREAD1 and PP20. AREAD1 relates to the request to the shop floor to get the mea- sured value of the process parameter. The second process instruction category, PP20, is the same one that you configured in Chapter 4. The Control Recipe Destination PP (Chemical Mixing) is also one that you configured in Chapter 4. Figure 7.12 Process Instructions for Control Recipe Destination PP Note You should implement SAP Note 2508866 in SAP S/4HANA if you encounter an error during the selection of process instruction categories via (F4) help. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 296 Double-click Proc. Instr. (process instruction) 0010 (with Proc.instr. category AREAD1) to go to the screen shown in Figure 7.13. Figure 7.13 Process Instruction Characteristics for PI Category AREAD1 A major benefit that process management offers is that you can check its results for consistency and simulate them to ensure completeness and correctness. Choose the Check Process Instruction icon located at the top right of Figure 7.13 to check the consis- tency of the sequence of process instruction characteristics and the value of each defined characteristic. Then, choose the Simulate Process Instruction Sheet icon to show the simulated version of what the field and other information will eventually look like in a PI sheet. Note If you encounter an issue while simulating or displaying the data shown in Figure 7.15 and the screen goes blank with the top right side of the screen showing Loading…, then it is due to unsupported browsers. Here are some tips to resolve this issue: 1. Engage an SAP NetWeaver expert to implement SAP Note 2224356. 2. If after you’ve implemented the SAP Note, the problem still isn’t solved, then it is browser based. The PI sheets and process manufacturing cockpits do not work with the SAP GUI for Java or the SAP GUI for HTML. © 297 7.4 Process Management For the system to be able to display browser-based PI sheets and process manufactur- ing cockpits correctly, you must install Microsoft Internet Explorer version 5 or higher. You must also be using a Microsoft operations system. Since Internet Explorer has been deprecated and replaced with the Microsoft Edge browser, you must go to Settings  Default Browser in the Edge browser and force all websites and links to open in Inter- net Explorer only. Refer to the following SAP Help link for more details: http://s-prs.co/v603100 Then, once you’re on the main screen of SAP GUI, choose More  SAP GUI Settings and Actions  Options. On the left-hand side of the screen, choose Interaction Design  Con- trol Settings. In the dropdown list in the Browser Control field, choose Internet Explorer (see Figure 7.14). Figure 7.14 Internet Explorer as Browser Control Figure 7.15 shows message category PP20, which you configured in Chapter 4. It also con- tains the output characteristic ZPI_CREATION_DATE process instruction created earlier in Chapter 4. In the PI sheet, this field should show the PO Basic Start Date of the process order. The output characteristic also has the same value (ZPI_CREATION_DATE) assigned. Figure 7.15 Self-Defined Process Instruction Category 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 298

sections.title: Process Instruction Sheet
sections.en: 7.4.4 Process Instruction Sheet Figure 7.16 and Figure 7.17 show a general example of different sections of a PI sheet that include options like input fields, calculations, tables, values to choose from a dropdown list, and operator’s notes. Figure 7.16 Sample PI Sheet, Part 1 Figure 7.17 Sample PI Sheet, Part 2 © 299 7.4 Process Management The following sections explain some of the options available on the PI sheet and the data or other information that you need to maintain to use a specific function/option. Input Value Table 7.2 contains the PP-PI characteristics needed for input field functionality on the PI sheet, as shown in Figure 7.18. Figure 7.18 Process Instructions Characteristics for Input Values Figure 7.19 shows a simulation of the Thickness field and how it will look in the PI sheet. Hence, the PP-PI characteristic PPPI_INPUT_REQUEST is the display field in the PI sheet. The value (any numeric value) will be given a tag of PPPI_VARIABLE as “A,” and the out- put format of the numeric value will be governed by characteristic MM01. For example, characteristic MM01 stipulates having a field length of four characters with two deci- mal places and no negative values. In such a case, values such as 5.35 and 5.88 are Characteristics Characteristic Values Descriptions PPPI_INPUT_REQUEST Thickness This is the standard PP-PI characteristic to use whenever a field is required to have an input value. The field value corresponding to this characteristic is given as Thickness and will be the display name of the field in the PI sheet. PPPI_VARIABLE A Each field can be tagged as a variable whose value can subsequently be used in calculations, if needed. For this example, you give the vari- able tag as “A,” as defined in the characteristic value. PPPI_REQUESTED_VALUE MM01 This PP-PI characteristic is the output value of a field. However, what governs the format and other details actually comes from characteris- tic value MM01. Recall that we just covered cre- ating characteristic MM01 in Section 7.3. Table 7.2 Input Fields as Defined in Process Management 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 300 acceptable, but –5.28 isn’t acceptable on the PI sheet. The decimal point is also counted as a character. Figure 7.19 Input Value in PI Sheet Tips and Tricks If a PP-PI characteristic isn’t going to be used in any subsequent calculation, and if it’s being used only for data entry purposes, then eliminate the entire PPPI_VARIABLE row and its value A. Calculated Value A PI sheet can be extensively used for all kinds of calculations, as long as all of the rele- vant parameters required for calculation are available in the same PI sheet. Table 7.3 contains the PP-PI characteristics needed for the calculation field functional- ity on the PI sheet. If the calculation formula is too long for a single line, it can be con- tinued on the next line. (Up to eight lines can be used for the calculation formula.) In addition, if you want to use variables in a calculation formula, you must first define them in the same PI sheet. Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_INPUT_REQUEST Revised thickness PPPI_VARIABLE B PPPI_CALCULATED_VALUE MM01 PPPI_CALCULATION_FORMULA A*1.05 PPPI_EVENT Parameter_changed Table 7.3 Calculation Fields in Process Management © 301 7.4 Process Management The simulated version of the calculated field will appear as shown in Figure 7.20. The system automatically calculates the Revised Thickness field by using the formula that you defined in the master recipe for this field. Notice that there is a small calculator icon right next to the Revised Thickness field to denote that it’s a calculated field. This field is also noneditable. If you make changes to the Thickness parameter value, the sys- tem will automatically update the calculated values. Figure 7.20 Calculation Field in PI Sheet Repeat Data Request Table 7.4 contains the PP-PI characteristics needed for the repeat data input request functionality in the PI sheet. Using them will result in a display as shown in Figure 7.21. Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_DATA_REQUEST_TYPE Repeated Data Request PPPI_MESSAGE_CATEGORY DPREAD PPPI_PROCESS_ORDER PPPI_DATA_POINT_NAME 10 PPPI_REQUESTED_VALUE PPPI_DATA_POINT_VALUE PPPI_REQUESTED_VALUE PPPI_EVENT_DATE PPPI_REQUESTED_VALUE PPPI_EVENT_TIME Table 7.4 Input Groups as Defined in Process Management 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 302 Figure 7.21 PP-PI Characteristics for Input Group Field Functionality in PI Sheet Call Function As explained in Table 7.5, you can use a PI sheet to call up a transaction while remaining within the PI sheet’s screen. The process instruction characteristics together with their values call up the Display Process Order transaction while you remain in the PI sheet. The PPPI_Button_Text enables you to define a meaningful description of the icon while remaining in the PI sheet. You should set the icon text as Display Process Order and set the PPPI_Transaction_Code as COR3 for this example, but note that these fields are flexible and you can set them to whatever you need. Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_FUNCTION_NAME COPF_CALL_TRANSACTION PPPI_BUTTON_TEXT Display Process Order PPPI_FUNCTION_DURING_DISPLAY Allowed PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER New_Session PPPI_INSTRUCTION PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER TCODE PPPI_TRANSACTION_CODE COR3 Table 7.5 PP-PI Characteristics and Their Values for Call Functions as Defined in Process Management © 303 7.4 Process Management The simulated version of the characteristics is shown in Figure 7.22. When the DISPLAY PROCESS ORDER button shown here is clicked, it brings up Transaction COR3 (Display Process Order). Figure 7.22 Call Function in PI Sheet Table Entry Often, there is a business need to enter multiple values in a tabular form for a single value or multiple values of parameters. Table 7.6 lists all the PP-PI characteristics you need to use the table-entry format in the PI sheet. Notice that you can control the table size (with a minimum of four values and a maximum of six values in this example). Figure 7.23 illustrates the simulated version of the table entry format and shows the option to enter six values. The option to maintain decimal places for each value is con- trolled via characteristic MM03. Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_DATA_REQUEST_TYPE Repeated Data Request PPPI_MINIMUM_TABLE_SIZE 4 PPPI_MAXIMUM_TABLE_SIZE 6 PPPI_INPUT_REQUEST Shift-wise Flow Reading PPPI_VARIABLE ABC PPPI_REQUESTED_VALUE MM03 Table 7.6 Activating Format with Multiple Values 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 304 Figure 7.23 Table in PI Sheet Long Text Input In general, at the end of every shift, a company requires the process operator to record shift highlights and/or other important details. To do this, you can use the long text functionality in the PI sheet. Table 7.7 lists the PP-PI characteristics needed for the long text functionality in a PI sheet. Figure 7.24 shows the text box that becomes available in the PI sheet, in which the pro- cess operator has entered shift details. Figure 7.24 Shift Details (Notes) in PI Sheet Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_INPUT_REQUEST Shift Highlights PPPI_REQUESTED_VALUE PPPI_MESSAGE_TEXT Table 7.7 Required PP-PI Fields for Activating Text Box in PI Sheet © 305 7.4 Process Management Instructions and Notes In general, businesses want to make sure that the line operator has easy and instant access to various instructions and other notes during the shift. The functionality of instructions and notes is useful in such scenarios. Table 7.8 provides a list of the PP-PI characteristics you need to maintain instructions and notes in a PI sheet. To enter the desired text that you want to display on the PI sheet, select the PPPI_INSTRUCTION (or PPPI_NOTE) characteristic and choose the Value icon at the bottom of the screen shown in Figure 7.18. You can enter the text that you want to see displayed on the PI sheet , and you can also use some of the formatting options, such as bold, italic, large fonts, or text alignment center/right. SAP Document Management Service in the Process Instruction Sheet Sometimes, the line operator is required to refer to a specific user manual or machine instruction manual to perform a task or rectify a production problem during opera- tions. For this purpose, process management completely integrates with SAP Docu- ment Management service (see Figure 7.25), which is a central repository used to store important documents and other references for use. Table 7.9 contains the PP-PI characteristics that you need to maintain to activate SAP Document Management service in a PI sheet. Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_INSTRUCTION – PPPI_NOTE – Table 7.8 PP-PI Fields for Activating Instructions and Notes in PI Sheet Characteristics Characteristic Values PPPI_FUNCTION_NAME COPF_DOCUMENT_SHOW_DIRECT PPPI_BUTTON_TEXT Instruction Manual – Complete PPPI_FUNCTION_DURING_DISPLAY Allowed PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER DOCUMENT_TYPE PPPI_STRING_CONSTANT DRW PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER DOCUMENT PPPI_STRING_CONSTANT 10000000001 PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER DOCUMENT_PART PPPI_STRING_CONSTANT 000 Table 7.9 PP-PI Characteristics for Activating SAP Document Management Service 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 306 Figure 7.25 SAP Document Management Service in Process Management Digital Signature on Completion of the Process Instruction Sheet A digital signature in the SAP S/4HANA system is a process users can employ to authen- ticate information, and it requires the user to enter their name along with a password before the system can accept an entry or perform a function. For example, when a user executes a function, they are required to digitally sign the PI sheet in the popup box that appears.

sections.title: Process Order Execution
sections.en: 7.5 Process Order Execution Process order execution has the following differences from and similarities to produc- tion order execution in discrete manufacturing:  Batch allocation, either automatic or manual, can occur within the process order.  The system automatically executes the formulas of the material quantity calcula- tion that is defined in the master recipe using batch allocations.  When a process order is released, the system can create the control recipe automati- cally, or the user can choose the relevant icon within the process order creation screen to do it manually.  A user can also create a test control recipe in a process order to check or validate set- tings.  The system creates a control recipe for each control recipe destination of the process order. If there are three control recipe destinations in the process order, then the sys- tem creates three control recipes for the process order. PPPI_EXPORT_PARAMETER DOCUMENT_VERSION PPPI_STRING_CONSTANT 00 Characteristics Characteristic Values Table 7.9 PP-PI Characteristics for Activating SAP Document Management Service (Cont.) © 307

sections.title: Process Management in Action
sections.en: 7.6 Process Management in Action  Integration points—such as in-process inspection, finished goods inspection, capac- ity requirements planning, material requirements planning (MRP), and sales and operations planning (S&OP)—also remain the same. Figure 7.26 illustrates the important elements in a process order. Figure 7.26 Elements of Process Order 7.6 Process Management in Action The main steps involved in process management are listed here, with numbers corre- sponding to each step, as shown in Figure 7.27: 1. The first step is the process order creation. The process order reads the material mas- ter data, including the master recipe and materials list (the BOM). It also takes the material quantity calculation into account. The master recipe contains all the infor- mation related to process management and is used for the generation of a control recipe. 2. When the process order is released and the control recipe is generated, the system creates a PI sheet. 3. The next step is to send the generated control recipe to the predefined destination in the master recipe (in this example, it’s PP—Chemical Mixing). Order Header Assignments Costs Settlement Rule Process Instructions XSteps Confirmation • Order Number • Plant • Material Number • Batch • Status • Prod. Qty (Plan/ Actual) • Dates (Plan/ Actual) • Production Version • Resources Network • Production • Scheduler • Planned Order • Sales Order • Insp. Lot (QM) • Settlement Profile • Settlement Receiver • Planned Costs • Actual Costs • Chars. For Process • Control Level • Process Instructions • Control Recipe Dest. • Activities • Point in Time • Operation Quantities Material Components • Material Number • Req. Qty (Planned/Actual) • Requirement Dates • Reservation • Batch Secondary Resources • Control Key • Standard Values Operations • Resources Phases • Control Key • Standard Values • Relationships • Control Recipe • Destination Either/Or Material Quantity Calculation 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 308 4. The PI sheet is now available at the destination, and the PI sheet can be maintained. 5. The status of the PI sheet can be updated to Complete. Once that happens, no further changes to the PI sheet are possible. If necessary, an incomplete PI sheet can also be saved. 6. The last step is to send the process messages to their required locations so that the system can perform the necessary predefined actions. Figure 7.27 Main Steps in Process Management You’ll learn how to accomplish each of these steps in the following sections.

sections.title: Creating and Releasing a Process Order
sections.en: 7.6.1 Creating and Releasing a Process Order To create a process order, follow menu path Logistics  Production – Process  Process Order  Process Order  Create  Create with Material or use Transaction COR1. Enter Material number “2252,” Plant “1710,” and order Type “PI01,” and then press (Enter). Figure 7.28 shows the detailed Create Process Order: Header—General Data screen. Figure 7.28 Header Screen for Process Order Creation Create process order Generate control recipe Send control recipe to predefined destination Maintain process instruction sheet Complete process instruction sheet Send the process messages to their destinations © 309 7.6 Process Management in Action On this screen, enter the production quantity in the Total Qty field and the order finish date in the End field. Notice that the Start date of the process order is 08/15/2024. In the PI sheet, the system should automatically be able to fetch this date in the Process Order Start Date field. Release the process order by clicking Release (not shown).

sections.title: Generating a Control Recipe
sections.en: 7.6.2 Generating a Control Recipe After releasing the process order, you can choose the Generate Control Recipe button at the top of the Change Process Order screen (refer to Figure 7.28) to generate a control recipe. An information message appears at the bottom of the screen to inform you that the system created a control recipe (see Figure 7.29) and also generated a log. Figure 7.29 Control Recipes Created The system has now created a PI sheet. With the control recipe having been generated, you save the process order by clicking Save on the process order screen. The system then generates a process order number, which in this example is 1002221. Tips and Tricks You can automate control recipe creation on process order release by making the rele- vant settings in the production scheduling profile (refer to Chapter 4) and then assign- ing this production scheduling profile in the Work Scheduling view of the material master. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 310 Generating a control recipe creates a PI sheet. All the necessary checks and balances are maintained, such as defining tolerance limits for parameter values, requiring sign-off at the end of each operation or phase, and integrating digital signatures in process management. If you realize that incorrect information was provided on the PI sheet, then you can create a new PI sheet (by generating a new control recipe) to attend to this scenario. This situation arises after a user has already performed all the necessary steps in process management. To generate a new PI sheet, follow these steps: 1. Use Transaction CO67. On the Worklist for PI Sheets screen containing the PI sheet with the Complete status, select the PI sheet. Then choose PI Sheet  Delete. The system then issues a message stating that the selected entry (PI sheet) was deleted. 2. Use Transaction CO57 to create a process message manually. In this option, enter the Plant and use Process Message Category PI_CRST. Enter the process order number of the deleted control recipe, enter the number of the deleted control recipe (from the previous step), and set the Control Recipe Status to 00007 (Discarded). 3. Save the process message, and the system will state that a new process message has been created and will give the process message number. 4. Use Transaction CO54XT, and on the initial parameter selection screen, enter the process order number and the process message category as “PI_CRST.” Press (F8) or choose the Message List icon. 5. Select the process message number created in the previous step and choose Send to send the process message. The system will issue an information message to inform you that the process message has been processed. 6. Use Transaction CO53XT. On the initial screen, enter the Plant, the process order number, and the destination address (in this example, it’s “Chemical Mixing”). Press (F8) or choose Display. 7. On the Control Recipe Monitor screen, select the relevant control recipe number and choose the Delete icon. The system issues an information message that the control recipe has been deleted and, at the same time, assigns the status of the control recipe as Discarded. 8. Use Transaction COR2 for the change in process order and click the Generate Control Recipe icon to create a new PI sheet for the same process order.

sections.title: Downloading and Sending a Control Recipe
sections.en: 7.6.3 Downloading and Sending a Control Recipe You can manually download a control recipe to a destination in a nonautomated envi- ronment (as a background job). To do so, follow menu path Logistics  Production – Process  Process Management  Control Recipe  Control Recipe Monitor or use Transac- tion CO53XT. Figure 7.30 shows the initial screen with selection parameters for the © 311 7.6 Process Management in Action control recipe. For this example, use the same Manufacturing Order number (1002221) and assign the Destination Address as Chemical Mixing, which you configured in Chap- ter 4. Then, click the Created, Sent button to view the generated control recipe. Figure 7.30 Initial Screen of Control Recipe Monitor On the screen shown in Figure 7.31, select the line item and choose the Send button to send the control recipe to destination PP. The status will be updated from Created to Sent. Figure 7.31 Control Recipe Monitor The control recipe has the statuses detailed in Table 7.10. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 312 When the PI sheet is set to the Completion status, the system automatically sends the process message category PI_CRST to the SAP S/4HANA system. This process message category has the process message characteristic PPPI_CONTROL_RECIPE_STATUS, for which the system automatically sets the value to Processed. In the process order, the system sets the status of the control recipe to CRFI. This is one of the statuses that you’ll find in Table 7.10. The Control Recipe Monitor screen provides comprehensive information on the discarded, terminated, and sent control recipes. When the system downloads control recipes of destination type 1 or 4, it’s known as a PI sheet. When the system downloads control recipes of destination type 2 or 3, it reaches an external system or an external PCS. Note You can schedule a background job for downloading and sending control recipes by using program RCOCB006 (batch job name: SAP_NEW_CONTROL_RECIPE). Detailed set- tings for the background job are covered in Chapter 4, Section 4.8.

sections.title: Maintaining Process Instruction Sheets
sections.en: 7.6.4 Maintaining Process Instruction Sheets To maintain the PI sheet, follow menu path Logistics  Production—Process  Process Management  Process Instruction Sheet  Worklist—Maintain or use Transaction CO55. You can choose All Selections so that the system can offer you more selection options on the initial screen than are visible when you first execute Transaction CO55. For example, apart from new and in-process PI sheets, you can select PI sheets that you’ve discarded and terminated (see Figure 7.32). You can also check the relevant box to use this transaction to display completed PI sheets. Control Recipe Statuses Control Recipe Descriptions Details CRCR Created This status appears in the process order when the system generates a control recipe. CRFI Processed/finished This status appears upon comple- tion of all details in the PI sheet and after you set the status of the PI sheet to Complete. CRAB Discarded The system assigns this status to the control recipe when you discard the PI sheet before the start of the execution. Table 7.10 Control Recipe Statuses © 313 7.6 Process Management in Action Figure 7.32 Initial Screen for PI Sheets Enter the Plant as “1710” and the Processor Group (which is the description of the con- trol recipe destination) as “CHEMICAL MIXING” and then click Execute or press (F8). The corresponding PI sheet’s work list will then be available for maintenance (see Figure 7.33). Figure 7.33 Work List of PI Sheets for Operating Group Production Floor This is the process instruction work list, which meets the selection criteria. For this example, there is only one PI sheet in the work list. You select the relevant PI sheet and choose Maintain PI Sheet (see Figure 7.33) to start data input (data maintenance). Figure 7.34 shows the PI sheet with all of the input fields available for maintenance. The PI sheet may contain several fields and other important functions already filled in, such as default views or calculation fields. Next, enter the value for the Thickness parameter. A red asterisk next to the parameter denotes that it’s a mandatory field that needs to be maintained. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 314 Figure 7.34 Maintaining PI Sheet Tips and Tricks You can control whether an entry in the field is optional or mandatory by checking the relevant box in the characteristic (Transaction CT04). In SHIFT HIGHLIGHTS text box (not shown, but located further down the same screen), a process operator can enter detailed text. They can also make entries in the table of the PI sheet. Notice that P.O. Basic Start Date is automatically filled, based on the configu- rations you made in Chapter 4.

sections.title: Completing Process Instruction Sheets
sections.en: 7.6.5 Completing Process Instruction Sheets When you’ve finished making all entries into the PI sheet, you can either save it or mark it as Complete by using the light-blue buttons at the top of the PI sheet (refer to Figure 7.34). You can use the Save function in a PI sheet as many times as needed to save partial and incomplete information. You use the Complete function when you’ve incorporated all details into the PI sheet. Note that after you use the Complete function, you won’t be able to make changes to the PI sheet. © 315 7.6 Process Management in Action Note If the relevant settings were made in the configuration (see Chapter 4) of the control recipe destination, then the system will show you a popup message that will prompt you to digitally sign the PI sheet when you complete it.

sections.title: Sending Process Messages
sections.en: 7.6.6 Sending Process Messages The PI sheet automatically creates a process message with all the information that the system needs to send to specific destinations. This information may be a GI against a process order, a GR against a process order, or a confirmation of a process order. Send- ing the process message executes the function module with the gathered information. Process messages in a productive system are typically sent via a background job of pro- gram RCOCB002 (cross-plant) or program RCOCB004 (plant-specific). Warning Be sure to monitor process messages; sometimes, a process message sending fails if, for example, data is missing from the PI sheet. You can also monitor and delete process messages with Transaction CO54XT and manually create them with Transaction CO57. Figure 7.35 Process Message Monitor 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 316 You can also create, test, check, and monitor process messages, although the system doesn’t process these process test messages at the destination as it does for normal process messages. Test process messages are created to check whether the system can transfer all the relevant information from various systems and destinations. To send a process message, follow menu path Logistics  Production – Process  Process Management  Message  Message Monitor or use Transaction CO54XT. Enter the Plant and the Creation Date range for the process message (see Figure 7.35) and then click the To Be Sent button to send the messages. In the process messages work list shown in Figure 7.36, there is a process message cate- gory, SIGN, that the system needs to send. Notice that the sender of these process mes- sages is Chemical Mixing (which you configured in Chapter 4). Select the first process message, SIGN, and choose Change (the pen icon). Figure 7.36 Process Messages from Production Floor Sender In Figure 7.37, the system automatically incorporates the Char. Value, which is the date (08/15/2024) for the ZPI_CREATION_DATE characteristic. Figure 7.37 Process Message with Message Category PP © 317

sections.title: Execution Steps
sections.en: 7.7 Execution Steps Some of the other salient features of process messages are as follows:  Process message categories that have errors or are terminated during processing can be resubmitted for processing using the Process Messages Monitor screen.  Process messages can be created manually using Transaction CO57. Then these man- ually created process messages will be visible on the Process Messages Monitor screen, where they can be processed further.  The system uses Business Application Programming Interfaces (BAPIs) or function modules to update the process messages. The system processes the received process messages using these function modules.  The system uses a remote function call (RFC) connection to communicate with an external process control system using process messages. It’s important to define the RFC destination for every external system, so we recommended that you engage an SAP NetWeaver (basis) consultant for this task.  The system issues alerts for critical situations or milestones in the production run. The alerts can be from either the external control system or the internal shop floor operator.  A special destination type, SAP Office user, is used in the process message to inform specific SAP S/4HANA system users about the status of process messages (and also control recipes). 7.7 Execution Steps XSteps are extensively used not only in PP-PI but also in discrete manufacturing. In fact, all of the concepts, fundamentals, features, and functionalities covered in Chapter 4 and this chapter apply if there’s a business need to integrate XSteps with discrete manufacturing. In the following sections, we’ll discuss where you can find a repository of standard XSteps that you can use as a reference to create your own XSteps and how to set up a specific function or parameter in the PI sheet. We then address the transition required from process management to XSteps. The remaining sections then focus on how to leverage the configuration made in Chapter 4 and the process management concepts developed earlier in this chapter for XSteps.

sections.title: Repository for Standard XSteps
sections.en: 7.7.1 Repository for Standard XSteps The standard XSteps repository is an invaluable resource that you can use to gain a bet- ter understanding of how to use XSteps. It contains detailed step-by-step instructions for how to effectively use all the features and functionalities of XSteps. To access the repository of standard XSteps, follow menu path Logistics  Production— Process  Master Data  Standard XStep Repository or use Transaction CMXSV. For this example, enter Plant “1710” and click Execute. The screen shown in Figure 7.38 will then appear, containing comprehensive details on XSteps. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 318 Figure 7.38 XSteps Repository Note SAP provides a standard XSteps library that you can downloaded from the SAP for Me website (https://me.sap.com) in an XML format and import into the SAP system. You can use the XSteps provided in this library as a starting point or even as a model for cre- ating your own XSteps. Refer to SAP Note 3156358 to gain a detailed understanding of and step-by-step instructions to implement the technical and functional areas of XSteps. It is also important to use Transaction CMX21 to create plant-wise entries with necessary con- trols in place that the system will check while importing XML files into the SAP system.

sections.title: Importing the Standard XSteps Library
sections.en: 7.7.2 Importing the Standard XSteps Library To import the XML file containing the standard XSteps library, you use Transaction CMXSV. After executing Transaction CMXSV, define the plant by populating the Plant field in the initial screen that the system displays. Then, click Execute or press the (F8) key to access the XSteps repository. With the repository open, right-click the Standard XSteps folder and then choose Cre- ate  Folder to create a new folder in which to store the standard XSteps. The newly created folder appears on the next screen with the description <Folder>. After double-clicking this folder, you should change the description in the popup screen that appears. The next step is to import the downloaded XML file containing the standard XSteps library to the newly created folder.
