To complete this step, right-click the folder you just created and select the Import Standard XStep option. © 319 7.7 Execution Steps In the popup screen that appears, select the file named SXS Library BASICS V1_0.XML under the previously mentioned folder (XML_files_V_1_0) and then click the Open button.

sections.title: Switching from Process Instructions to XSteps
sections.en: 7.7.3 Switching from Process Instructions to XSteps Whenever you’re transitioning from process instructions to XSteps in the master rec- ipe, the system issues the warning message that all previously created process instruc- tions become invalid when the changes made in the master recipe are saved. In addi- tion, the profile in the master recipe (see Chapter 4, Section 4.1.1) should allow for the creation of XSteps. A warning message appears when you click the XSteps icon in the master recipe. (This icon is located on the top menu bar in Figure 7.39.) Choose Yes to proceed further (see Figure 7.39). Figure 7.39 Switching from Process Instructions to XSteps

sections.title: General Information
sections.en: 7.7.4 General Information On the XSteps creation screen in the master recipe, choose XStep Tree and the system will create an XSteps icon on the left-hand side of the screen. Select the XStep Tree icon again, right-click on it, and select Properties from the dropdown list. Tips and Tricks You can also select relevant fields in XSteps, such as process instruction or calculation. You can then press (Enter) and the system will bring up the Properties option, wherein you can make the desired changes. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 320 On the Node Attributes screen that appears as shown in Figure 7.40, you enter a short description that then becomes visible throughout the XSteps tree. Choose the Parame- ters tab to bring up the next screen, which is described in the next section. Figure 7.40 Node Attributes in XSteps

sections.title: Parameters
sections.en: 7.7.5 Parameters Figure 7.41 shows the screen on which you can define all the parameters that will be used in the XSteps for data entry or calculation. If the parameters aren’t defined on this screen, then it won’t be possible to select them when needed; for example, you won’t be able to create an input value in the process instructions. If it’s only a numeric value that won’t be subsequently used in calculation, then there’s no need to define the parameter. Figure 7.41 Parameters in XSteps © 321 7.7 Execution Steps For this example, you define two parameters, T01 and T02, and give a short description of each one. It’s important to enter the Characteristic (“PPPI_PARAMETER_VALUE,” in this example). You can also use the characteristics that you previously created using Transaction CT04 (in the classification system). However, in this case, you use charac- teristic value “PPPI_PARAMETER_VALUE,” which is delivered by the SAP system. If you need to maintain the parameter values in a table, then check the Tabular box.

sections.title: Valuation
sections.en: 7.7.6 Valuation On the same screen, choose the Valuation tab to bring up the screen shown in Figure 7.42, which provides the options for how the system should valuate the parameters. You can even define a fixed value in the Value field, if the value remains constant. In addition, if the value refers to another parameter or value, then you refer to the rele- vant parameter here. Figure 7.42 Valuation in XSteps

sections.title: Control Recipe Destination
sections.en: 7.7.7 Control Recipe Destination Next, choose the Destination tab, and on the screen shown in Figure 7.43, assign the control recipe destination you defined in Chapter 4. For this example, double-click the control recipe destination, Chemical Mixing, which you defined in Chapter 4 and is now assigned here. Then, choose the Close icon (not shown). After you’ve successfully maintained the details in the previous four figures, the screen appears as shown in Figure 7.44. Then, you can perform the next step, which is the cre- ation of process instructions in the XStep tree. To do so, place the cursor on the Chem- ical Mixing field (in the XStep tree) and right-click it to bring up the available options. From the available options, choose Create  Process Instruction. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 322 Figure 7.43 Destination in XSteps Figure 7.44 Process Instruction Creation in XSteps

sections.title: Process Instructions
sections.en: 7.7.8 Process Instructions You can create a new process instruction d by right-clicking New Process Instruction, choosing Create  Control Data, and then choosing the appropriate option. Here, you can freely define the input parameters, output parameters, function modules, and even control data. For example, you can manage calculations, process messages, com- mands, and function calls. © 323 7.7 Execution Steps The screen shown in Figure 7.45 appears when you select a process instruction and bring up the properties (by either pressing (Enter) or right-clicking to select the Prop- erties option). For this example, enter a description to identify the process instruction. Note Now that we’ve explained how to access the Properties option of any element in XSteps, we’ll only cover the details of the element, such as input value, output value, and calculation, without repeatedly referring to the Properties option. Figure 7.45 General Tab in Calculations The following sections cover some XSteps features that we previously touched upon to help you understand the similarities between process management and XSteps. We’ll also show additional features in XSteps. Parameter Values in XSteps The screen shown in Figure 7.46 appears when you create the first parameter by follow- ing menu path Create  Entry  Parameter Value. In Description, enter “Viscosity_Ini- tial”, and in the Parameters area, select T01 Viscosity_Initial from the Name dropdown list. Notice that this is one of the same parameters defined when the XStep tree was first created. The remaining three tabs provide in-depth options to validate the values entered for a parameter. A user can choose to always accept a value, accept it with a dig- ital signature, or never accept an out-of-range value. You can also create your own text that the system will display in case of a failed check. Repeat the same process to create the second parameter value, with a description of “Viscosity_Final,” and select the parameter’s name from the Name dropdown list. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 324 Tips and Tricks As an alternative, you can choose Create  Entry  Numeric Value to achieve the same results as just described, but make sure you select the Parameters option from the dropdown list in the Default Value tab. Figure 7.46 Characteristic Details and Validation in XSteps Calculation in Execution Steps Now that you’ve created two parameter values in the XStep tree, let’s use them in calcu- lations. You can perform any kind of calculation, as long as the parameters are already defined in the XStep tree. For this example, use the simple calculation of subtracting the value of one parameter from another to display the result as a calculated field. To perform a calculation, follow menu path Create  Control Data  Calculation. The screen shown in Figure 7.47 will then appear. Enter a short description: “Difference.” On the lower half of the screen, enter “T02 - T01” in the Calculation Formulas box. An important aspect to note is the Event field, in which “PARAMETER_CHANGED” is entered. An event is a control function that triggers the necessary action when the user or the system fulfills a specific and predefined event. For this example, you stipulate that whenever there is a change (an event) in the Viscosity Difference field, the system should automatically calculate and update the value in the field. If an event isn’t defined, then the user will have to manually choose the Calculator icon to update the calculation in the Viscosity Difference field, based on any changes made to the param- eter values. Other examples of events include the system unlocking the table fields when the user maintains the PI sheet, locking the tables when all entries in the table are made, and activating or deactivating the PI sheet. © 325 7.7 Execution Steps Tips and Tricks To view all the available options in events, place the cursor on the Event field and press (F4) or choose the dropdown option. Figure 7.47 Calculation Formula in XSteps To simulate the fields, place the cursor on either the XStep tree or the process instruc- tion and then either press (F8) or right-click and choose Simulate. Figure 7.48 shows how the screen looks when you enter not only the two parameters but also the calculation. Notice that the calculated Difference field has a small Calcula- tor icon next to it to denote that the value in the field is a calculated value. Many input, output, and control options are available in the XStep tree, so we briefly discuss the most relevant ones in the following sections. Note We encourage you to explore the large number of options available in XSteps, includ- ing the option to incorporate website addresses and images/figures (e.g., hazard or gloves signs), the option to enter long text or a short note, and so on. You can immedi- ately simulate the results to see if the settings are correct. If they aren’t correct, the sys- tem provides guidance to correct them or points out the shortcomings or deviations. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 326 Figure 7.48 Simulation of Calculation Formula in XSteps Output Characteristics and Values in Execution Steps If there is a business need to display a fixed value in an XStep or subsequently on the PI sheet, then you should use the Output Characteristic option. For example, you can enter the description “Value Output (Fixed)” and use the PPPI_Parameter_Value charac- teristic with a fixed value of 40. On the left-hand side of the same figure, you can again use the Output Characteristic option, but this time, select Note as the Characteristic, and in the Long Text field, enter the text shown. This is fixed text that will display when the PI sheet is processed. Control Instruction You can also change the description of how each control instruction in the PI sheet appears. This option helps combine the relevant process instructions in one visual tab so that it’s easier for the line operator to either enter the details of the process parame- ters or simply use the available functions. To ensure that the system displays control instructions as a tab, you first enter the desired description of the control instruction and then select the Control Instruction characteristic from the dropdown menu. Tables in Execution Steps To generate a table in the PI sheet using XSteps, you first create a process instruction and then follow menu path Create  Control Data  Table. The screen shown in Figure 7.49 provides the option to enter the minimum and maximum number of lines (rows) that must appear in the table. When you create a table, you maintain the input value of the parameters by following menu path Create  Entry  Numeric Value (or Parameter Value). Figure 7.50 shows an example of a table created in XSteps with calculation and a signature option. © 327 7.7 Execution Steps Figure 7.49 Tables Settings in XSteps Figure 7.50 Tables in XSteps Scope of Generation in Execution Steps Whenever an XStep is created, the system provides the option to define the scope of generation, just as it does for process instructions. In this step, define the scope of gen- eration for materials, resources/work centers, documents, or orders. XSteps offer far greater generation options as compared to process instructions. Figure 7.51 lists the available options for scope of generation. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 328 Figure 7.51 Scope of Generation in XSteps Signature in Execution Steps At the process instruction level in XSteps, you can incorporate a signature at various steps of data entry (or other input) to validate the information entered (see Figure 7.50 again). You can even enter the details of a digital signature; the digital signature func- tionality is already configured in the system, and you can choose it by following menu path Create  Control Data  Signature. Process Messages in Execution Steps At the same level on which you enter a signature in the process instruction, you can select the associated process message. To do so, follow menu path Create  Control Data  Process Message. Use the same process message example here that you created in Chapter 4. Enter Process Message Category “PP20” and the relevant message character- istic will automatically appear. Execution Steps Overview Place the cursor on any level and press (F8) to simulate the PI sheet, and it will appear on the right-hand side of the figure. Tips and Tricks Use the drag-and-drop option on any element in XSteps to move it to the desired loca- tion. For example, if Table Entries should appear earlier than you want in the XStep tree, simply select it and drag it to your desired new location. © 329

sections.title: Process Message Evaluation
sections.en: 7.8 Process Message Evaluation 7.8 Process Message Evaluation The system offers process message evaluation as a report that you can use to access information on process messages. Process message evaluation integrates with Micro- soft Excel and offers the option to save the evaluated information either as a pivot table or simply as an Excel worksheet. You can use all the standard and self-defined process message characteristics in the evaluation. You can also self-define the layout for evaluation—that is, decide how the system should display the information during evaluation. But it’s important to first create an evaluation version before using it to evaluate process messages. For process message evaluation, use menu path Logistics  Production—Process  Pro- cess Management  Evaluation  Process Messages or use Transaction CO52. On the initial screen, enter the Plant as “1710” and the Evaluation version as “SAPS4” and then choose the Create icon. But first, create an evaluation version before using it in process message evaluation. You can also create a new evaluation version with refer- ence to an existing version. To create an evaluation version, simply enter the Plant and choose the SAP XXL icon. The system will then bring up the screen shown in Figure 7.52, wherein a user can check the relevant boxes and save. The system will then ask the user to enter the Evaluation version and its Description. Figure 7.52 Selection Screen of Process Message Evaluation For this example, shown in Figure 7.52, you use the previously created Evaluation ver- sion SAPS4 and check the boxes in the line characteristics (Line char.) and column char- acteristics (Col. char.) columns for the characteristics (process messages) that need to be evaluated. For the Line char. option, check the requisite boxes so that the data 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 330 appears in horizontal lines (in rows). For the Col. char. option, check the requisite boxes so that the data appears in columns. In this example, the control recipe status appears in a column, whereas the control recipe destination appears in a row. If you select the Evaluate option, the system downloads the information either in an Excel spreadsheet or in a pivot table format. Alternatively, you can use the Preview option. The first column and the related rows will show the control message ID. In the second column, the first row pertains to the control recipe status, whereas the second row reflects the control recipe number.

sections.title: Process Manufacturing Cockpit
sections.en: 7.9 Process Manufacturing Cockpit Building on the configuration made in Chapter 4 for the process manufacturing cockpit, PP10, we now show how it appears when a user wants to use it for data entry and report- ing purposes. To access the process manufacturing cockpit, use menu path Logistics  Production—Process  Process Management  Manufacturing Cockpit  Start or use Transaction COPOC. On the initial screen, enter the Plant as “1710” and the cockpit Name as “PP10.” Then, choose Execute. Figure 7.53 shows manufacturing cockpit PP10, in which the required details are main- tained. All details are optional, except for the Material quantity consumed field, which is mandatory, as denoted by the red asterisk. When you exit the process manufacturing cockpit, the process messages automatically become available in the message monitor (Transaction CO54XT). From there, you can send them to the predefined destinations. Figure 7.53 Process Manufacturing Cockpit, PP10 © 331

sections.title: Resources Network
sections.en: 7.10 Resources Network 7.10 Resources Network A resource network is a group of individual resources used in the production process. The ability for a user to pick and choose from the available or even required resources during process order creation ensures smoother production. Using a resource network also eliminates the need to create a production version for every variant of the production. We’ll show you a real-time example in which several resources are available and usable for similar production processes, but the business process entails a user deciding which resource to use just before production begins. For this purpose, a planning resource is defined that is only used for planning and that represents several resources that can be used in the same way. There’s a case of three equivalent resources (mixes), and the business requirement is that the user wants to dynamically (and in real time) select the actual resource while creating a process order level (before release). To map this business requirement in SAP S/4HANA, you use the resource network functionality. You begin by creating a specific task list usage type and checking the rel- evant box. Then, you create the relevant master data (including the resource network), create a resource hierarchy, and assign them to a master recipe. Then, when a process order is created and ready to be released, the system will prevent the user from releas- ing it until the user selects one resource from a resource network. Let’s go through this step by step. First, you access the screen shown in Figure 7.54 via Transaction OP45 and create a new task list usage, Z08, by copying standard task list usage 008. In the detailed view, check the until rel. box to denote that this task list usage will only allow for release of a pro- cess order after one of the resources from the resource network is selected. Figure 7.54 Configuration Settings for Task List Type 2 Then, using Transaction CRC1, assign the previously created task list usage Z08 to Resource MIX_PLAN, as shown in Figure 7.55. Simultaneously, create a few additional resources (Mix1, Mix2, and Mix3) and assign standard task list usage 008 to them. 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 332 Figure 7.55 Assigning Task List Usage in Planning Resource Next, access Figure 7.56 via Transaction CR24 to create Resource network MIX_RN. Here, assign all the previously created resources, including planning resource MIX_PLAN, by first choosing New Assignment and then entering individual resources in the popup that appears. Be sure to also check the Assignment Indicator box for each of the resources you’re adding to the resource network. To create relationships among these assigned resources in the resource network, choose Graphic in Figure 7.56, which brings up Figure 7.57. Figure 7.56 Resource Network and Assignment of Resources © 333 7.10 Resources Network The three resources in the resource network MIX_PLAN shown in Figure 7.57 appear connected to the planning resource MIX_PLAN. To connect a resource, first select the relevant resource and then choose Connect (shown on the right-hand side of Figure 7.57). Choosing a resource will also enable currently disabled options (such as Duplicate, Modify, and even Delete) to delete a resource from a resource network. Figure 7.57 Creating Resource Network Next, access the screen shown in Figure 7.58 via Transaction C201 or C202 (create or change a master recipe). Then, in the Recipe Header tab, enter “MIX_RN” for Resource netwrk to assign it. Figure 7.58 Assigning Resource Network in Master Recipe 7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing 334 Next, choose the Operations tab, which opens the screen shown in Figure 7.59. Then, assign only planning resource MIX_PLAN at the operation and phase levels and main- tain any additional details if required. Figure 7.59 Assigning Planning Resource in Operation and Phase Figure 7.60 shows the process order creation screen (Transaction COR1). While you’re trying to release the process order, the system sends an error message (BS013—System Status WCRE Is Active) to indicate that the process order release has failed. You will now need to select a resource from the resource network before the process order can be released. To select a resource, choose Operations on the screen shown in Figure 7.59, which takes you to the screen in Figure 7.61. Figure 7.60 Error Message when Releasing Process Order Figure 7.61 shows the operations view of a process order and consists of the same plan- ning resource, MIX_PLAN. To choose an actual resource from the resource network, first select Operation 0010 and then choose Execute resource selection, which will cause the screen shown in Figure 7.62 to appear. Figure 7.62 shows the popup that enables you to select one of the resources from the resource network. Place the cursor on the relevant resource and click Choose, and plan- ning resource MIX_PLAN shown in Figure 7.61 will be replaced by the resource you’ve chosen. To choose a different resource from the resource network, choose Execute Resource Selection on the screen in Figure 7.61 again. © 335

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 7.11 Summary Figure 7.61 Resource Selection Figure 7.62 Choosing Resource from Resource Network 7.11 Summary In general, the production processes in process industries are no different from those in discrete manufacturing, except that PP-PI offers options to record greater details of the processes. However, PP-PI also offers functionalities such as process management, which is unique to PP-PI only. With the introduction of XSteps, the process manage- ment functionality not only becomes easier to implement in PP-PI but also finds usage in discrete manufacturing industries. The next chapter covers production planning in repetitive manufacturing. ©

sections.title: Overview
sections.en: 8.1 Overview Companies that work with high-volume and mass-produced materials (e.g., pens, pen- cils, keyboards, batteries) find that REM (rather than discrete manufacturing or process manufacturing) best reflects their production processes. If your actual business pro- cess is simple and straightforward, you should consider implementing the lean REM production type, as long as it can fulfill your business needs from a functional perspec- tive. The SAP S/4HANA system supports and offers make-to-order (MTO) REM, which enables handling of configurable materials. REM also supports handling unit manage- ment, in which goods and packaging are mapped as a logistic unit. Serial number assignment for unique identification is also possible in REM, and make-to-stock (MTS) REM is equally possible. The following sections will cover how REM plays a central role in production planning and production execution, and they will also discuss the REM process flow.

sections.title: Roles of Repetitive Manufacturing in Planning and Production
sections.en: 8.1.1 Roles of Repetitive Manufacturing in Planning and Production Figure 8.1 provides an overview of the role of REM in the planning and production areas of the SAP S/4HANA system. The planning process starts with requirements coming from SAP S/4HANA Sales or from the material forecast, and it then becomes a part of demand management. The system runs the MRP based on the figures given in the demand program, and the outcome of the MRP run (the planning run) is the creation of procurement proposals. These procurement proposals can be planned orders (order proposals), purchase requisitions for external procurement, or delivery schedules for scheduling agreements with vendors. In REM, the planned orders can’t be converted because these are sufficient to trigger the production process. In fact, the REM planned orders take on the new order type of the run schedule quantity when the system assigns a production version to them. After actual production execution, the system records the relevant information, such as components consumption, activities consumption, and production yield. Finally, because REM is a period-based manufacturing type, the product costing team proceeds to the settlement process, in which it settles variances and overheads during cost col- lector settlement. © 339 8.1 Overview Figure 8.1 REM in Production Planning

sections.title: Repetitive Manufacturing Process Flow
sections.en: 8.1.2 Repetitive Manufacturing Process Flow Figure 8.2 illustrates the end-to-end process flow in REM. The process flow sequence is as follows: 1. The process begins when requirements from MRP (through planned independent requirements [PIRs]) or SAP S/4HANA Sales flow in, based on which the production must be initiated. The system covers the infinite requirements with planned orders during the MRP run. 2. The system performs the line loading and checks the capacity requirements to see if it’s able to produce the required quantities. If it can’t, it’s overloaded. 3. The system checks for the availability of the components needed to produce the material. 4. The operational methods sheet that contains detailed instructions for producing the material can be printed. 5. It’s also possible to print the production list, which contains quantities to produce and important dates. A production list specific to a material or a production line can also be printed. Following the lean manufacturing principle, these are the only two shop floor papers. 6. The material needs to be staged by undertaking a transfer posting from the main store to the production floor, from where the components are issued to the produc- tion lines. Sales and Distribution Material Forecast Costing Capacity Requirements Planning Demand Program Material Requirements Planning (MRP) Repetitive Manufacturing Pl. Ind. Rqmts. Sales Orders Planned Orders (PE) Planned Orders Production Backflush • Goods Issuance • Confirmation • Goods Receipt Cost Collector Settlement 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 340 7. The production execution step is the actual and physical production of the material. 8. If the business process entails working and reporting with reporting point back- flush (milestone confirmation), then the WIP can be calculated. 9. The production yield, record issuance, consumption of components, and postpro- duction activities are recorded. Excess component consumption, excess activities such as machine hours or labor hours consumed (as compared to standard dura- tions), and scrap can also be recorded. It’s also possible to record any deviation in consumption. 10. The last step reflects the integration of CO with PP, in which the product costing team performs settlement using the product cost collector. Figure 8.2 Process Flow in REM Note QM processes can also be integrated into the REM production process to ensure that all products produced in-house will go through quality checks before being dispatched to customers. Requirements Planning Line Loading and Capacity Checking Availability Check Print Operational Method Sheet Print Production List Material Staging Production Execution WIP Determination (Optional in Case of Reporting Point Backflush) Final Confirmation Goods Receipt Backflushed Component Withdrawal Settlement Using Product Cost Collector © 341

sections.title: Master Data
sections.en: 8.2 Master Data 8.2 Master Data Before running processes and jobs in the SAP S/4HANA system, you must set up com- plete and comprehensive master data. REM has its own unique master data that often overlaps with other production types, such as discrete manufacturing and process manufacturing. If master data is set up in the right sequence, it’s much easier and more logical to interconnect because the predecessor-successor relationship is already taken care of. Table 8.1 provides an overview of the master data that is needed in REM. It begins with the material master of the product (a finished good or an assembly), which you specifi- cally mark as the REM type in the MRP 4 view of the material master by checking the relevant box. You also assign it the REM profile that you previously configured (in Chapter 5). You then create a BOM of the product that needs to be produced, and you assign the components together with the quantities needed to produce that product. If needed, you can also define a scrap percentage at the operation or component level. Then, you create a work center or production line, followed by a rate routing for the material, in which you assign the previously created work center. Finally, you assign the material’s BOM and rate routing to a production version for the material. You then check the REM box to denote that the material produced will follow the REM process. When all of the logistics master data is in place, the CO team can create product cost col- lectors of the material. This can be specific to or independent of the production version. Materials Production Versions  Activating REM indicator  Assigning REM profile  Assigning production supervisor in Work Scheduling  Activating REM indicator  Rate-based planning  BOM assignment  Production line  Receipt storage location Bill of Materials Cost Object Controlling  Material BOM  Product cost collector or even product cost estimate Work Center (Production Line) Rate Routing  Production line as work center  Assign work center (production line)— usually one operation  Production rate (quantity per time) Table 8.1 Master Data of REM 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 342 Note We strongly recommended that you maintain close coordination and liaison with the CO team to ensure that when they’re working with PP, both teams are completely aligned in terms of their working and reporting needs. For example, each work center (production line) must be assigned a cost center, which the CO team should provide. It may provide one cost center for multiple work centers or one cost center for an individual work center, depending on how the team wants to see the cost center reporting and evaluation. One possible approach to having several cost centers within one production line is to incorporate additional cost-relevant operations (in the rate routing) and still adhere to the principle of just one work center for production (and PP). Let’s now discuss how to set up each of the REM-specific master data areas.

sections.title: Material Master
sections.en: 8.2.1 Material Master The first step in creating REM-specific master data begins with creating the necessary settings in the material master of the product (an assembly or a finished good). To change the material master, you follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master  Material  Change  Immediately or use Transaction MM02. On the resulting screen, enter Material number “2256” and press (Enter) to bring up the orga- nizational level, wherein you enter Plant “1710” and Stor. Loc. “171A.” Choose Continue to bring up Select Views, in which you select MRP 4 and press (Enter) again. On the screen shown in Figure 8.3, you maintain the details related to REM production processes in the MRP 4 view of the material master. Then, you check the Repet. Manu- facturing box to denote that the material will undergo the REM production process. Then, select PP10 in the REM Profile field. Note This is the same REM profile that you configured in Chapter 5. A REM profile is a four-digit code that determines the following:  The movement type that the system uses to post GRs and GIs  How the system reduces planned orders and run schedule quantities in the confir- mation process  How the system makes the correction in the assembly’s components (according to BOM), which enables you to define whether GI posting isn’t possible in the system due to forgotten GR postings or whether the system should process the same later on in a separate backflush processing transaction © 343 8.2 Master Data  How the system deals with production activities during confirmation, such as setup or machine hours You should then save the settings in the material master and assign a production supervisor in the Work scheduling tab of the material master. Figure 8.3 REM Indicator in MRP 4 View of Material Master

sections.title: Bill of Materials
sections.en: 8.2.2 Bill of Materials The process you use to create a material BOM for REM is no different from the process in discrete manufacturing. Refer to Chapter 6, Section 6.2.2, which covers the details of material BOM creation.

sections.title: Work Center (Production Line)
sections.en: 8.2.3 Work Center (Production Line) The alternative term for a work center in the SAP S/4HANA system is a production line. Work center is a term specific to discrete manufacturing, whereas production line is spe- cific to REM. Production lines are usually created as simple work centers in the system (and because the SAP system refers to these as work centers, we’ll use both terms throughout this chapter to maintain a logical connection between the SAP terms and real-world business scenarios). In the work center, you define the availability of the production line. In REM, following the lean manufacturing principle, you don’t model all workstations in the production line as individual work centers. Rather, you model the entire production line (with all of its work centers) as one work center. You enter the production line you create as the work center in the Production Line field in the pro- duction version, and you specify this same work center in the single operation of the 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 344 rate routing. You can represent production lines that need to include or model more than one work center in a line hierarchy. To create a work center (production line), you follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data  Work Centers  Work Center  Create or use Transaction CR01. On the ini- tial screen you use to create the work center, enter Plant “1710” to create a production line. It’s mandatory to assign a plant while creating a production line. Enter “KBASSY” for Work Center. This is an alphanumeric identification to denote a production line. A production line can be a production facility, a processing unit, a machine or group of machines, or a laborer or group of laborers directly involved in the production process. For this example, set the Work Center Category field to “0007.” This control function ensures that only the relevant screens will be eventually available during production line creation. Work center category 0007 denotes a production line. Other important functions in this screen are Copy from Plant and Copy from Ref. Work Center. These functions help reduce data entry efforts during new production line cre- ation by automatically making available all of the necessary information from the refer- ence production line in the new production line, which you can then change as needed. In the following sections, we’ll cover the five tabs for a work center and the information that you need to maintained. Basic Data Figure 8.4 shows the Basic Data tab of a production line. Here, you enter a short descrip- tion of the production line in the text box at the top-right of the screen. In the Usage field, enter “009.” A usage is a control function that controls whether this production line eventually becomes available for various other tasks, such as during the creation of rate routing or the production version. A usage of 009 denotes that this production line can subsequently be used in all task list types. In the Standard Value Key field, enter “SAP3.” A standard value key denotes activities performed on a specific production line, as well as the option to record them. Some of the activities include production time, setup time, processing time, and labor time. For REM, the SAP S/4HANA system already provides the SAP3 standard value key, which you can used. Next, enter the production time as a rule for maintenance. If you assign the Production Time as Must Be Entered from the dropdown list, then users must enter the actual dura- tion of the activity during confirmation of the REM planned order. Otherwise, the sys- tem won’t allow the production supervisor to save the confirmation. Other mainte- nance options are May Not Be Entered, May Be Entered, and Should Be Entered. These offer various degrees of control, from an information message to a warning message, if the requisite information (duration, in this case) isn’t recorded during confirmation. © 345 8.2 Master Data Figure 8.4 Work Center: Basic Data Default Values Tab Choose the Default Values tab to see the screen shown in Figure 8.5, wherein you assign the PP01 control key to the work center. A control key is a control function that man- ages whether a production supervisor can perform an activity or a task associated with the work center. Place the cursor on the Control Key field and press (F4). While you have the cursor on the Control Key field, if you choose the Detailed Informa- tion button, the system will show the details in the lower half of the same screen. Note that in REM, only the following checkboxes are included: Scheduling, Det. Cap. Reqmnts., Cost, Insp.char. required, and Confirmations. No other checkboxes work for REM. If a given box isn’t checked, then that step or function can’t be performed on this work center (KBASSY). For example, if the Scheduling box isn’t checked in the control key, then the system won’t perform scheduling on this work center. In addition, note that in the Confirmations field, you can use option 1 (milestone confir- mation) to report point backflush during confirmation. Note You set up control key by using Transaction OPJ8. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 346 Figure 8.5 Work Center: Default Values Capacities Tab Choose the Capacities tab in the work center (production line) creation screen to see the options screen shown in Figure 8.6. In the Capacities tab, enter “SAPR01” in the Setup Formula field; this denotes the setup formula that the system will use during capacity calculation. For Processing Formula, enter “SAPR02.” This is for Capacity cate- gory 001 for Machine (shown at the top of the screen). Note See Chapter 16 for information on capacity requirements planning. Scroll down and choose the Capacity Header button at the bottom of the Capacities tab (not shown). This brings up the header screen of the capacity for Work Center KBASSY, as shown in Figure 8.7. The capacity header contains comprehensive details about the capacity: the start and end time of the shift, the length of breaks, the overload percent- age allowed for capacity, shifts and intervals, and the factory calendar. Note Refer to Chapter 2 for information on how to set up the factory calendar and capacity planner group. © 347 8.2 Master Data Figure 8.6 Work Center Capacities Figure 8.7 Work Center Capacity Header 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 348 For this example, enter the following in each field or check the relevant box:  Capacity Category short text (not shown): “Keyboard Assembly Line-1” This short text describes the capacity category—for example, the specific produc- tion line or machine.  Capacity Responsible: “A” It’s important to assign a capacity planner group to a production line, so that during evaluation of capacities, the capacity planner group can be one of the evaluation cri- teria. The capacity planner group shouldn’t be the name of the person but instead the area or domain of responsibilities.  Capacity Base Unit: “Min” (for minutes) This is the unit of measure (in minutes) in which the system displays the capacity of a production line.  Start Time: “08:00:00” This is the start time of a production line.  End Time: “17:00:00” This is the end time of a production line.  Length of Breaks: “01:00:00” This is the total length of breaks during the start and end of shifts. Break time reduces the availability time of the capacity of a production line. For example, if a machine runs 12 hours a day, with two hours of daily breaks, then the available capacity of the production line is 10 hours. Note that this fact is considered in lead time scheduling of orders.  Capacity Utilization: “100” (percent) This is the actual capacity utilization factor. This factor has to be as realistic and as close to the actual capacity of a production line as possible to enable the system to calculate the available capacity of the production line. The capacity utilization is reduced accordingly if the production line can’t be used for a certain percentage of time—for example, due to machine failure.  No. Ind. Capacities: “1” This is the number of individual capacities associated with a production line. For example, four individual ice cream mixers are created as one production line, so the individual capacities are four in number and directly multiplied by the timings of the capacity of the production line. For example, if the available capacity of a pro- duction line is 10 hours a day, and if there are four individual capacities, then the total available capacity of the production line is 40 hours.  Relevant to Finite Scheduling Checking this box requires the system to consider capacity overload during capacity evaluation.  Overload % A capacity overload of a certain percentage directly increases the capacity availabil- ity of the production line by the defined percentage. If, for example, the total capac- © 349 8.2 Master Data ity of a production line is 40 hours with 10% overload, then the available capacity is 44 hours. A 10% overload for 40 hours is 4 hours, which adds to the 40 hours to show the total available capacity as 44 hours. Because you’re considering an overload, maintain “5” here to denote that a 5% capacity overload is allowed.  Long-term planning Checking this box enables the system to consider production line availability during long-term planning, which is simulative planning of material and production lines over a long period of time. Scheduling Choose the Back icon and then choose the Scheduling tab (see Figure 8.8). Then, enter the standard formulas available for REM (these are covered in detail in Chapter 6). Figure 8.8 Work Center Scheduling Note See Chapter 16 to learn more about capacity requirements planning. Costing Because PP comprehensively integrates with the cost object controlling functionality in product cost controlling, each work center needs to have a cost center. You can assign a single cost center to multiple work centers in the Costing tab, but you can’t assign multiple cost centers to a single work center. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 350 Note We strongly recommend that you to coordinate with your CO person to ensure that each cost center and associated activity type for the given controlling area has price planning. At the same time, you need to ensure that activity types and cost centers are also in place so they can be assigned in the rate routing. On the screen shown in Figure 8.9, enter the following details:  Start date This is the start date of the availability of the cost center for the production line.  End Date This is the end date of the availability of the cost center for the production line.  Cost Center You must directly assign a cost center to every production line so that when you per- form work using a production line, the system can accumulate all of the costs in the designated cost center. Figure 8.9 Work Center Costing © 351 8.2 Master Data  Activity Type (production time) The activity type denotes the predefined rate at which the system calculates the work performed by a production line.  Formula Key (production time) A formula denotes the parameters that the system considers while calculating the cost incurred in performing work at a production line. Then, save the work center.

sections.title: Rate Routing
sections.en: 8.2.4 Rate Routing Rate routings are designed for the maintenance of production rate−based task lists and are used in REM, among other business scenarios. In a standard routing, the base quan- tity (e.g., pieces) generally remains constant, and the time-related data, such as the pro- cessing time, is maintained using standard values. Rate routing works in exactly the opposite way. The production quantities are maintained on the basis of individual items, and the reference time is constant. Thus, a rate routing describes the quantity of an item that must be produced within a given period of time. A rate routing can contain a series of operations. The production rate is defined for each operation based on the production quantity, production time, and associated unit of measure. The production rate is determined as the quotient of the production quan- tity and production time. This enables a production planner to determine the produc- tion quantity in terms of tons per shift, for example. Usually, only one operation is pro- duction relevant, and the others may be controlling or print relevant. Using line hierar- chies, several production-relevant operations can support capacity leveling in different work centers along the production line. All other aspects of the functionality and maintenance of standard routings and rate routings are identical. Work centers are assigned to the rate routing, which should also contain the standard value key in which the standard values for production time, setup time, and teardown time are predefined. A reference routing is used as a reference or template in routings. A reference rate rout- ing contains a sequence of operations. As with a rate routing, the production rate for an operation is defined on the basis of the production quantity and reference time. The functionality and maintenance of reference routings are similar to those of reference operation sets. To create a rate routing, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data  Line Design  Rate Routing  Create or use Transaction CA21. On the initial screen where you create a rate routing, enter Material “2256” and Plant “1710.” In the Key Date field, enter “07/01/2024” and denote the date when the rate routing becomes available in the pro- duction process. Because the rate routing validity starts on July 01, 2024, this rate rout- ing won’t be available if, for example, a production planner creates an REM planned order for the month of April 2024. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 352 If you provide no date during rate routing creation, then the system will automatically assign the rate routing’s creation date as the valid from date. In the following sections, we’ll cover the various elements of rate routing. Header Details Choose the Header icon. In the header details of the rate routing, enter the following:  Usage: “1” (for production) This makes the rate routing available during subsequent production activities, in which the system will use the rate routing during the creation of the REM planned order.  Status: “4” (released) This makes the rate routing available during subsequent production activities, in which the system will use the rate routing during the creation of the REM planned order. If the status of the rate routing is set to Being Created (Status 1), it won’t be available when a REM planned order for the material is created.  Planning Work Center: “KBASSY” This is the production line that you previously created and is now used in the plan- ning and production of the material.  From Lot Size: “1” PC This denotes that the smallest quantity of the REM planned order must be at least 1 PC for the system to consider using this rate routing. In other words, this rate rout- ing is applicable when you create the REM planned order of a quantity greater than or equal to 1 PC.  To: “999,999” PC This denotes that the maximum quantity of the REM planned order is 999,999 PC for the system to consider using this rate routing. In other words, this rate routing is applicable when a REM planned order with a quantity greater than or equal to 1 PC but less than or equal to 999,999 PC is created. Note Because QM also integrates with REM, you need to engage the QM resource for this activity. Next, choose the Operations button. Figure 8.10 shows the resulting screen, in which KBASSY is the work center (in the Work Ctr column). This denotes assigning a previ- ously created work center (production line) to an operation, which is used in the pro- duction of a material and for which you’re creating the rate routing. An operation defines a single level of detail for a production step. © 353 8.2 Master Data Figure 8.10 Routing: Operation Overview Operations Select and double-click the first line item, Act. 0010, and the screen shown in Figure 8.11 will appear. Figure 8.11 Routing: Operation Details Here you see the operation’s detailed view, in which you can enter the following:  Production time: “2.0 HR” (hour) On the production line, you defined that recording duration information in rate routing is mandatory (by using the Must Be Entered maintenance control); hence, you must define the time it will take to complete the production line step of the manufacturing process. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 354  Act. Type: “3” An activity type is a result of direct integration of PP with product cost controlling. In fact, PP completely integrates with product cost controlling. An activity type contains the financial rate (value-based) to perform an activity during production. So, five hours of production time has an associated cost of producing the material. Any devia- tion in production time, either positive or negative, has a direct impact on the COGM.  Setup: “1.0 HR” This is the time it takes to set up the machine or the production line for this task. Any deviation in setup time, either positive or negative, has a direct impact on COGM.  Teardown: “2.0 HR” This is the time it takes to dismantle or tear down the machine or production line. Any deviation in teardown time, either positive or negative, has a direct impact on COGM. Components Allocation Next, press (F6) or choose menu path Goto  Operations Overview, and the system will bring up the Operations Overview screen. On this screen, choose the Components Allo- cation icon. Figure 8.12 shows the component allocation in rate routing. These components are directly taken from a material’s BOM, which you created in the previous step. Figure 8.12 Material Component Overview in Routing If you don’t assign a specific component to a specific operation, the system will assign all components to the first operation of the rate routing. This means that all compo- nents must be available at the time of the first operation so that during backflush, the system can consume all components in the first operation. If a business process entails that a few components will be used or needed in the later stages of the production process, then you select them and use the Reassign button to assign specific components to the operation. © 355 8.2 Master Data Because there is only one operation in REM, the preceding scenario of material assign- ment is broadly more applicable in discrete manufacturing than in REM. Material Assignment Going back to the rate routing header screen (Figure 8.12), choose the New Assignment button. A production planner can assign several materials, together with their respec- tive plants, to this rate routing group (50000003). All such materials belonging to this rate routing group will have the same group but with an ascending group counter number. In other words, many of the materials undergoing the same production process will need separate routings. To accomplish this, just create one rate routing and assign rel- evant materials (with plants) to it. This saves time and effort in creating an important part of the master data (rate routing) and also eliminates or reduces redundant data entry efforts. Finally, save the rate routing.

sections.title: Production Version
sections.en: 8.2.5 Production Version Production versions denote different ways in which a company produces a material— for example, a material with one rate routing but multiple BOMs. Therefore, for each BOM and the same rate routing, the system needs a separate production version to denote its uniqueness during REM planned order creation. The requirement to create a separate production version also applies when there is one BOM for a material but multiple routings. In the REM process, it’s mandatory to create a production version and make it available. The production version can also be specific to the quantity range, thus acting as a control function. For example, you can create one production version that has a quantity limita- tion of 1 to 1,000 PC and another production version for a quantity greater than 1,000 PC. The production version can also be specific to the validity period, thus acting as a con- trol function. For example, a production version can be valid from April 1, 2025, to March 31, 2025, and from April 1, 2025, on, a new production version will be effective. When the system has to assign the production version to the REM planned order, it searches for specific validity dates and automatically assigns the relevant production version when it finds it. If the system can find multiple production versions that fulfill the search and applica- tion criteria, it assigns the first available production version to the REM planned order. The first available production version refers to the list that can be called from the mate- rial master (in the MRP 4 or Work Scheduling views). To create a new production version, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Pro- duction  Master Data  Production Versions or use Transaction C223. Then, in the Selec- tion Conditions area of the screen shown in Figure 8.13, enter Plant “1710” and Material 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 356 “2256” and then press (Enter). If the system is unable to find a production version for the material, then the lower half of the screen will appear blank. If the system is suc- cessful, then it will display the relevant production versions. Figure 8.13 Production Version Creation Initial Screen A menu bar is available at the bottom of the production version creation screen that has just about all of the options you’ll normally need while creating production version master data, including copying, deleting, copying from a template, and so on. Choose the Create Production Version icon and an almost empty screen will appear for maintaining details, as shown in Figure 8.14. Figure 8.14 Production Version of Material 2256 © 357 8.2 Master Data Then, enter the details as noted in Table 8.2. Field Value Additional Details/Remarks Material 2256 – Production Version 0001 – Minimum Lot Size 1.000 This field sets the minimum REM planned order quantity for which the system offers this production version for selection. Leaving this field blank entails that there is no minimum quantity limit. Maximum Lot Size 99,999,999.000 This field sets the maximum planned order quantity for which the production version is applicable. Valid from 07/01/2024 This field denotes that if the REM planned order cre- ation starts on or after 07/01/2024, the system will offer this production version for selection. If the start date of the REM planned order is before 07/01/ 2024, then the system won’t make this production version available for selection. If this field is left blank, the system automatically incorporates the current date as the validity start date of the produc- tion version. Valid To 12/31/9999 This field sets the end validity date of the production version. Detailed planning Rate Routing Task list type R denotes that it’s a rate routing. Each task list type represents a different function; for example, task list type Q represents quality inspection. For this example, enter rate routing Group “50000003” and Group Counter “1.” Alternative BOM 1 This is the BOM that you created in the previous step. BOM Usage 1 In this field, 1 refers to the usage production. REM allowed Check the box Checking this box is mandatory to denote that the production version is applicable and becomes avail- able during the REM process. Production Line KBASSY Because REM is generally a simpler production pro- cess, only one production line (work center) is involved. Receiv. Location (not shown) 171A Entering a value makes it a default setting, and the system automatically proposes it during GR or con- firmation of the REM planned order. Table 8.2 Parameter Entry in Production Version 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 358 The Lock field in the Production Version area of the screen provides the flexibility to lock or unlock the production version anytime to allow or restrict access to the produc- tion version during REM planned order creation. A locked production version won’t be available for selection during REM planned order creation. Finally, click the Check button for a consistency check to evaluate whether the details incorporated in the production version are correct and valid. The system confirms that it’s able to find a relevant task list (a rate routing) as well as the BOM, which meets the other criteria of the production version. This includes hav- ing validity dates for the rate routing and the BOM, which falls within the validity spec- ified in the production version, as well as the lot sizes mentioned in the production ver- sion. Then, choose the Cancel icon. The green traffic light for the task list and the BOM indicates that the consistency check was successful and that this production version will be available to the user during REM planned order creation. If the system returns a warning or error during the consistency check, it will indicate it with a yellow or a red traffic light, respectively. Note Performing a consistency check isn’t a prerequisite for using a correct production ver- sion, but it can give you confidence that eventually, a valid production version will be available to work with. Finally, choose Close Detailed Screen and Adopt Changes and save the production ver- sion. Note If detailed scheduling and capacity planning functionalities are required in the REM business processes of your company, then we recommend that you use a routing (instead of a rate routing) and assign it to the production version. Details of how to cre- ate a routing were covered in Chapter 6. Later in this chapter, we’ll cover detailed scheduling and capacity planning in REM while using a rate routing. The formulas used in rate routing (such as SAPR02, in this example) should allow you to perform scheduling and capacity calculation, and you can do this by checking the relevant boxes in the formula you’re using in the rate rout- ing (Transaction OP55).

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning in Repetitive Manufacturing
sections.en: 8.3 Material Requirements Planning in Repetitive Manufacturing Extensive coverage of MRP is provided in Chapter 13. In this section, however, we will show you how to use MRP to preplan REM-based manufacturing types. The preplanning © 359 8.3 Material Requirements Planning in Repetitive Manufacturing process entails that a production planner has already planned to produce predeter- mined quantities of a product and use MRP for comprehensive planning, including the components (the BOM) of the product. It’s important to note that there are no REM-spe- cific details for MRP; REM uses standard MRP. For example, preplanning isn’t a prereq- uisite for REM. REM based planning can also be used on sales orders only (MTO). For this example, we use strategy 40 (the MTS production method), which is a typical case in REM. You can use any planning strategy in conjunction with REM (MTO and MTS), and we’ll focus on a typical real-world example in the keyboard manufacturing industry. For normal and standard planning, the planning process starts with either a sales order forming the basis of planning or quantities calculated from forecasting. Any or all of these quantities are forwarded to demand management, which ends up forming the PIRs. The MRP considers PIRs and generates procurement proposals, including planned orders. A production planner then evaluates the planning results and converts them into procurement elements for external procurement; these don’t need to be con- verted any further or for REM types. The steps are as follows: 1. Maintain planned independent requirements You use Transaction MD61 to enter the PIRs into the screen shown in Figure 8.15. Enter Material “2256,” Plant “1710,” and Version “00,” and then define the planning horizon from “07/01/2024” to “08/05/2025.” Press (Enter). Next, enter the planning quantities as maintained in Figure 8.15 and save the PIR. The next step is to run the MRP. Figure 8.15 Planned Independent Requirements 2. Run material requirements planning In this step, the materials planner runs MRP Live by using Transaction MD01N. Figure 8.16 shows the initial screen to run MRP. You set the parameters as shown and choose Execute (not shown) to begin the process of running MRP. As soon as the sys- tem completes the planning process (the MRP run), it displays the planning results. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 360 Figure 8.16 Initial Screen of MRP Live 3. Display planning results Figure 8.17 shows the planning results for both material 2256 and its associated com- ponents. The next step is to evaluate the planning results of material 2256 by either double-clicking the material directly or first saving the planning results and then using Transaction MD04 for the material and plant combination to see the stock/ requirements list. For this example, use the first option: double-click the planning results of material 2256. Figure 8.17 Planning Results of MRP Run The system also creates multiple planned orders (PldOrd) to cater to the PIRs (see Figure 8.18). © 361

sections.title: Planning Table
sections.en: 8.4 Planning Table Figure 8.18 Planning Results of Material 2256 Note In principle, manual changes to the REM planned orders should only be made in Trans- action MD04. Save the MRP results and all of these planning details will become available on the REM planning table. 8.4 Planning Table The planning table in REM is highly interactive and enables the planner to perform many functions and activities with all details automatically updated. For example, if you create a new planned order in the planning table, the system automatically updates the capacity requirements in the upper half of the planning table screen. The planning table also provides comprehensive visibility into material and capacity avail- abilities. In fact, important planning in REM, such as line loading planning, is generally undertaken in the planning table. In the following sections, we’ll discuss the planning table parameters and how to create orders.

sections.title: Parameters Selection
sections.en: 8.4.1 Parameters Selection To access the planning table in REM, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Planning  Planning Table  Change Mode or use Transaction MF50. Figure 8.19 shows the initial screen for the selection criteria, which allows you to enter important selection parameters. Based on the input parameters you define on 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 362 the parameter selection screen, the ensuing planning table will display the relevant information. Figure 8.19 Initial Screen of Planning Table In REM, the system refers to the main information with reference to the production version of the material; therefore, there’s an option to enter data specific into the pro- duction version on the selection screen. Some of the other selection options are Pro- duction Line, Material, Product Group, MRP Controller, and so on. For this example, enter Plant “1710” (entering either the MRP area or the Plant in the parameter selection of the planning table is mandatory). Select the Production Line radio button and enter the work center (Production Line) “KBASSY,” for which the sys- tem brings up the relevant data and other information in the planning table. Let’s review the four tabs and the options available on the REM planning table. In Figure 8.20, for Start 1, enter “07/01/2024,” and for End, enter “12/31/2024.” The sys- tem will then restrict the display of information in the planning table based on these start and finish dates. The system also won’t show planning information that lies out- side the planning horizon. Assign Week in the Period field and the system will show planning details in a weekly basket. A production planner can also choose to display planning information on the planning table, either in days or in weeks. © 363 8.4 Planning Table Figure 8.20 Period of Examination and Scheduling in REM Table Next, choose the Scheduling tab 2. The scheduling options available on this screen will enable the selection options and make them available on the planning table. Choose whether the system will perform rate-based planning and select whether it should con- sider basic dates or production dates during planning. The system can also bring up Capacity Planning and leveling options if you check the relevant box. Note We suggest that you make the same selection for the planning table as you did for the rate routing when you maintained it in the production version—except that in this case, the capacity requirements can be evaluated properly during planning. We also suggest checking on the configuration in Transaction OPU5 to see if the relevant set- tings are in place. We cover the settings for Transaction OPU5 in Chapter 3. In Figure 8.21, choose the Control tab 1 and choose whether the system should show the Material Overview, the Line Overview, or both. You can also limit the information display in the planning table if you check the Select Production Only box and then choose from the Selection Rule dropdown list options. In the Selection Rule list, you can choose to display only receipts, requirements and stocks, or PIRs. Next, choose the MRP Segments tab 2 and check the relevant boxes to choose what information to display for various MRP elements, such as Direct production. You can even input the sales document number (in SD Document) for MTO production. All of the parameter selection options are the same as configured in Chapter 5, Section 5.3, but there’s flexibility to change parameters, as we’ve done with Scheduling Strategy in the Scheduling tab of the Planning Table Initial Screen: Change Mode. In the config- uration for the scheduling strategy, we defined the scheduling strategy as Forward Scheduling, whereas in the Scheduling tab, we changed it to Backward Scheduling. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 364 Figure 8.21 Control and MRP Segments in REM Table Next, click the Execute button located at the bottom right-hand corner of the Planning Table Initial Screen shown earlier in Figure 8.19, and the screen shown in Figure 8.22 will appear. The upper half of the screen displays the complete capacity data, including the required and available capacities of the KBASSY production line that was maintained in the production version previously. Figure 8.22 Planning Table The lower half of the screen displays material-specific information. In the Preplanning field, the system displays the same PIRs that were created in an earlier section (refer to Section 8.3). The planning table also displays the settings you made in Chapter 5, Section 5.3, includ- ing available quantity, total production, total requirements, and the actual coverage as target stock. © 365 8.4 Planning Table Tips and Tricks If a production planner can’t see editable fields such as KBASSY or Not Assigned, as shown in the planning table in Figure 8.22, double-click the Not Assigned field under Material Data and these fields will appear.

sections.title: Creating a Repetitive Manufacturing Planned Order
sections.en: 8.4.2 Creating a Repetitive Manufacturing Planned Order As a planner, you have to make changes to existing planning elements, such as planned orders, and you also have to create new planned orders to account for production con- straints or other practical realities, such as an unplanned plant or machine shutdown. Because the REM planning table is interactive, you can perform all of these important planning functions and much more within the REM planning table. When you exit the REM planning table, save the interactive planning to enable the system to update the information accordingly. For this example, you create a new planned order by placing the cursor in the KBASSY field and entering a quantity of “350” for any week (in column W 30 (07/24)). Then dou- ble-click the field in which you entered the quantity of 350. Here, the system has given a temporary planned order number (Plnd order: 9999999006, not shown), and it enables you to make changes to the production ver- sion, production line, or production start or finish dates. You can also check the Fxd box, which will ensure that no changes are made to the planned order during the MRP run. Choose the Adopt button and the details will be copied to the planning table. Next, double-click the Quantity field, and this time, the system will assign a permanent planned order number to which you can make a large number of requisite changes, if desired. To make changes to the planned order, choose the Change icon, and the screen shown in Figure 8.23 will appear. This screen shows that the REM planned order is referred to as the Run Schedule Quantity, which is due to the fact that it has created the new quantity directly on the production line. You can change the Order Quantity and add the Scrap Quantity to account for any production losses (see Figure 8.24). The sys- tem automatically copies all other details, such as storage location data and the produc- tion version number, from the REM master data. Scroll down and you’ll see that the sys- tem also automatically checks the Repetitive Mfg box from Version 171A (not shown). The Planned Order checkbox in the Firming area (see Figure 8.24) indicates that during the MRP run, the system won’t make automatic changes to this REM planned order. The system automatically firms all manually created REM planned orders, according to the settings you made in the REM profile in Chapter 5. Choose Schedule Planned Order at the top of the screen and the system will shows the Detailed Scheduling tab. When you perform detailed scheduling for the material, you can choose menu path Goto  Capacity Leveling in the planned order, and the screen shown in Figure 8.25 will appear, showing the capacity requirements. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 366 Figure 8.23 Header Screen of Planned Order (Run Schedule Quantity) Figure 8.24 Scheduling Planned Order © 367 8.4 Planning Table Figure 8.25 Capacity Planning Choosing Component ATP (available to promise) in Figure 8.25 will bring up the Avail- ability Check screen shown in Figure 8.26, which indicates that the system has found that the two components you checked are available. Figure 8.26 Component Availability 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 368 Tips and Tricks On the Change Planned Order screen, you can perform a large number of activities and functions—for example, components availability checks, capacity checks, and re- exploding the BOM for the material. As an MRP planner, you must decide whether to use Transaction MD04 or Transaction MF50 for your daily work. In Transaction MF50, you can manage work on a period basis, whereas in Transaction MD04, all work is order-based.

sections.title: Capacity Planning
sections.en: 8.4.3 Capacity Planning Go back to the REM planning table (Figure 8.22) and set up graphics for capacity plan- ning by choosing Graphical Planning Table. In the REM planning table shown in Figure 8.27, evaluate the capacity situation (which is displayed in the upper half of the screen). Figure 8.27 Capacity Planning In general, at this stage, the capacity situation at the work center is agreed upon and already known by all involved, except in cases of urgent or immediate business need, such as sequence planning (within shifts or in days). The capacity planning function in the REM planning table is similar to the graphical planning table used in discrete manu- facturing, so it’s an exception rather than the norm to plan capacity in the planning table. You can select a planned order located in the lower half of the screen and dispatch it to the production line so that it will appear in the upper half of the screen (in Dispatched Production). You can perform all of the capacity requirements planning and capacity lev- eling functions in the REM planning table. Some of these functions include the following:  Rescheduling  Dispatching  Performing deallocation  Making changes to the REM planned order  Making changes to rate routing operations © 369 8.4 Planning Table

sections.title: Changes and Evaluations
sections.en: 8.4.4 Changes and Evaluations You can make any changes and evaluations in the planning table, and the system will update all the information on a real-time basis. Some of these functions are listed in Table 8.3.

sections.title: Range of Coverage
sections.en: 8.4.5 Range of Coverage If you’ve already made the necessary configuration for the range of coverage profile and you’ve assigned this coverage profile to the material master in the MRP 2 view of the material master, then you have the option to evaluate the coverage in the planning table. However, you must first activate this in the View menu. The range of coverage profile contains the parameters for calculating the dynamic safety stock, and this is a statistical calculation on the basis of average daily requirements. The range of coverage profile includes, for example, maximum stock level, minimum stock level, target stock, and the statistical and actual range of coverage. If you made the configuration to display Minimum Target Stock in the Coverage field (refer to Chapter 5, Section 5.3.2, showing the Coverage field and the Target Stock Level value), then the system will display Minimum Target Stock as a default setting in the REM planning table. To change this display in the REM planning table, choose menu path View  Actual Range of Coverage Profile (see Figure 8.28). If needed, the maximum stock level can also be displayed in the planning table. Functions Function Details Create New Planned Order Place the cursor on the Not Assigned field in the planning table and then choose this icon to create a new planned order. Expand Periods Switch from the “months” planning periods to “weeks.” Collapse Periods Switch from the “days” planning periods to “weeks.” Days are the shortest periods, whereas months are the longest. Select Material Display more planning materials associated with the produc- tion line. Select (Production) Line Display production lines from the rate routing. Hide Material Hide the already-displayed materials. Hide (Production) Line Hide the already-displayed production lines. Graphical Planning Table Display a graphical version of capacity planning. Table 8.3 Selected Functions in REM Planning Table 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 370 Figure 8.28 Actual Range of Coverage in REM Planning Table Note We encourage you to explore the features and functionalities of the planning table for REM. For example, choosing the Situation icon in the REM planning table will bring up the screen shown in Figure 8.29. Figure 8.29 Planning Situation © 371

sections.title: Material Staging
sections.en: 8.5 Material Staging 8.5 Material Staging Material staging is the step in which the components from the replenishment storage location are transferred to the issuing storage location. These components are con- sumed during the actual production process. This step involves the stock transfer transaction with standard movement type 311, and it transfers stock from the replen- ishment storage location to the issuing storage location. The system issues or back- flushes the components of the material from an issuing storage location. Tips and Tricks The alternative term used for material staging is pull list. A pull list provides details on which material’s components are faced with shortages. At the same time, it also trig- gers stock transfers. You can also use storage location MRP or Kanban as the pull list. You can view the current or existing material situation of the planned orders before proceeding to stage the materials. Evaluating the current material situation gives a materials planner a better perspective, and it helps them decide which components should be staged first. Material staging in REM is typically carried out with reference to a certain time horizon or a certain time interval—for example, a shift or until the end of the week. Except for MTO, materials aren’t staged with reference to any specific planned order. Similarly, the GR of the material produced is period based, with or with- out reference to planned orders. In the following sections, we’ll explain how to view the current situation, trigger replenishment, evaluate the material documents that the system created for material staging, and perform collective availability checks.

sections.title: Current Situation
sections.en: 8.5.1 Current Situation To view the current situation in the pull list, you follow menu path Logistics  Produc- tion  Repetitive Manufacturing  Material Staging  Pull List—Current Situation or use Transaction MF63. On the initial parameter selection screen, you choose from a large number of selection options and also define the end date for requirements in the Selection Horizon for Rqmts field. This is the date until which the system will consider the requirements of materials. There are also selection options for when a company uses the Kanban produc- tion process or has integrated with either warehouse management in SAP S/4HANA or embedded extended warehouse management (embedded EWM) in SAP S/4HANA. For this example, you define the Plant as “1710” (this is a mandatory entry requirement) and set the Selection Horizon for Rqmts as “12/02/2025,” which is the end date until which the system will consider all materials requirements. If the materials require- ments lie outside the horizon, they won’t show up in the current list of material staging. 8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing 372 Then, you limit the selection to Production Line (work center) “KBASSY” and choose Execute or press (F8). This brings up the screen shown in Figure 8.30. Figure 8.30 Pull List This screen shows the current stock and requirements situation of the planned orders in detail. It also shows the available stock, as well as the shortage quantities for the period under evaluation. The screen also shows the shortage quantity of each individ- ual material.

sections.title: Trigger Replenishment
