When you set a kanban to FULL, the system posts the goods receipt for the last control cycle. All control cycles work with the same material number. Figure 9.9 Status Sequence and Controls of Kanban Containers A kanban can also be automatically printed when it attains the desired status. This cre- ates a kanban card that contains barcode information and can be pasted on the physical kanban container. To set actions (such as goods receipt or kanban printing) for a particu- lar status or range of statuses, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Kanban  Status  Define Activities for Status Change. 9 Kanban 416 On the screen shown in Figure 9.10, choose from the range of kanban statuses for the INH status sequence and then select the desired action for the status, such as goods receipt or printing. Figure 9.10 Container Statuses to Trigger Automatic Actions For this example, select 5 (Container full) for both the Status From and Status To fields and check the Action: Goods Recpt box. These choices ensure that as soon as the kanban status is set to FULL, the system will automatically post goods receipt for the replenishment element.

sections.title: Kanban Alerts
sections.en: 9.4.9 Kanban Alerts Kanban alerts are used to facilitate efficient reactions in critical situations. In the stan- dard system, alerts occur for any of the following events:  A Kanban status change encounters an error.  The maximum number of empty Kanbans is exceeded.  A replenishment element is deleted.  A replenishment element is delayed (monitored via Transaction PKAL). To use this functionality, you activate the Activate Alerts flag via Transaction OM12. In the standard system, the Kanban category is supplied for the alerts. We recommend that you first check the standard alert settings to see if they meet your business needs. If they don’t, you can create a custom alert category. You can also check the standard category to define a custom alert by using the BADI_Kanban_MODIFY_ALERTS BAdI. Seek help from an ABAP resource to implement this BAdI. © 417

sections.title: Setting Up Kanban Master Data
sections.en: 9.5 Setting Up Kanban Master Data Tips and Tricks To display the errors accumulated during the Kanban processes, use standard Transac- tion PK50 (Error Display). We’ll cover this in more detail in Section 9.6. 9.5 Setting Up Kanban Master Data Master data is the backbone of every successful replenishment process. This section will cover how to set up necessary and Kanban-specific master data in the SAP S/4HANA sys- tem, such as production orders, purchase orders, scheduling agreements, and process or planned orders. This example uses material 2262 (a semifinished material) in plant 1710. In the following sections, and as a part of Kanban master data maintenance, we’ll cover the specific settings needed. We’ll also show how to maintain Kanban-specific master data for manufacturing-specific business scenarios.

sections.title: Kanban Control Cycle
sections.en: 9.5.1 Kanban Control Cycle The control cycle is the most important master data used for the Kanban process, and it defines the relationship between the demand and the supply sources. The control cycle contains the following control data for kanban production:  Kanban circulation (the number of kanbans and the kanban quantity)  Replenishment strategy (external procurement, in-house production, or stock transfer)  Printing kanbans  The delivery address  The basic data required for automatic kanban calculation (if necessary)  The process control data A few examples of the process control data include the indicator for a separate goods receipt, the status sequence key, and the indicator for the logic for triggering replenish- ment for one-card Kanban. Control cycles for classic Kanban can be maintained. The system uses these control cycles for Kanban with quantity signals or for one-card or event-driven Kanban. In event-driven Kanban, the system only creates a kanban when required; for example, a certain event based on actual material consumption triggers the Kanban process. You can create the control cycle by using Transaction PK01. (There is no menu path available.) On the initial screen that appears, enter “2262” for Material, “1710” for Plant, 9 Kanban 418 and “Kanban” for the Supply Area, for which the control cycle is defined. Choose the Classic Kanban radio button and press (Enter), and the screen shown in Figure 9.11 will appear. On this screen, you enter a short text in the Storing Position field to denote the physical location in which to place the kanban container. For this example, enter “4” for No. of Kanbans and “2” for Maximum Empty; this indicates that there will be four kanbans at this supply area and that a maximum of two kanban containers can remain empty at any time. Maintain the printer settings that the system will use to print the kanbans’ barcodes, and maintain the replenishment quantity in the Kanban Quantity field as “50” PC. The system will automatically create a production order of quantity 50 each time a kanban is set to EMPTY status. The system always prompts the user to choose the replenishment strategy for a Kan- ban. In this example, choose in-house production as the replenishment strategy by selecting the In-House Prod. radio button (not shown). After selecting the replenish- ment strategy, choose In-house Prod. replenishment strategy 0006 from the drop- down list (see Figure 9.11) and maintain the data in the Person Respons. (responsible) and Prod. (production) Version fields. Figure 9.11 Replenishment Strategy (External Procurement) in Control Cycle © 419 9.5 Setting Up Kanban Master Data Next, choose the Flow Control tab (see Figure 9.12). Figure 9.12 Flow Control in Control Cycle On the screen shown in Figure 9.12, check the Separate GR (goods receipt) box if your company doesn’t want to link a kanban FULL status with automatic goods receipt for a production or a purchase order. This setting may be useful in the following situations:  Your company posts goods receipts for externally procured materials in a central warehouse.  The goods receipt posting is automatically linked to the backflush, as is always the case in repetitive manufacturing. Here, for example, a production supervisor can separate the backflush posted by the supply source from the status change initiated by the demand source.  If your company uses planned orders (REM), manual Kanban, and scheduling agree- ment schedule lines, then you must post the goods receipt before you can set the status to EMPTY again. With the Indep. Supply Source indicator, it’s not the demand source but the supply source that triggers replenishment. The demand source sets the kanban status to EMPTY, but the system doesn’t create a replenishment element and sets the kanban status to WAIT. While remaining in the Kanban board screen, the supply source triggers replenishment for these kanbans, thereby setting the status of such kanbans to EMPTY. The independent supply source thus determines the time of replenishment by itself. Next, you assign the previously configured INH status sequence in this step to control how the system does or doesn’t allow the user to set various statuses. To trigger replen- ishment when the kanban status is IN USE or WAIT or upon reaching a trigger quantity, you maintain the relevant details in the Flow Control tab (see Figure 9.12). Next, choose the Kanban Calc. tab and the screen shown in Figure 9.13 will appear. Figure 9.13 shows the Kanban calculation details. Entering a value of “1” into the Calcu- lation Type field enables the system to calculate the number of kanbans necessary to fulfill the production requirements. In the Calculation Profile field, you can maintain a number for the data to use for the automatic calculation of the Kanban circulation. The system uses the calculation profile if the replenishment lead time is less than one day. 9 Kanban 420 Figure 9.13 Kanban Calculation in Control Cycle The system uses the following formula for automatic Kanban calculation: K = ((AC × RT × SF) ÷ Cont.) + C The variables are defined as follows:  K = Number of kanbans  AC = Average consumption per unit of time (from dependent requirements)  RT = Replenishment time per Kanban (from control cycle or material)  Cont. = Quantity per Kanban  SF = Safety Factor  C = Constant Save the control cycle after you make the necessary inputs. Tips and Tricks You can also maintain the control cycle by using Transaction PKMC or following menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Control Cycle  Control Cycle Maintenance.

sections.title: Material Master/Production Version
sections.en: 9.5.2 Material Master/Production Version Although this example primarily covers Kanban replenishment via discrete manufac- turing (production orders), you maintain the following settings if your company uses repetitive manufacturing (planned orders or run schedule quantities):  Using Transaction MM02, choose the MRP 2 tab of the material master and assign the Proposed Supply Area as “Kanban,” which is the same production supply area you previously configured in Section 9.4.6 (see Figure 9.14).  Using Transaction C223, maintain information for Default Supply Area in a produc- tion version. © 421

sections.title: Replenishment with Kanban
sections.en: 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.14 Assigning Proposed Supply Area in Material Master

sections.title: Bill of Materials
sections.en: 9.5.3 Bill of Materials If one or more components of a material BOM are replenished via Kanban, then you maintain the production supply area in the material BOM. To do so, use Transaction CS02 and navigate to the item details of the component. Then, choose the Status/Lng Text tab to maintain the production supply area (which you previously configured in Section 9.4.6) in the Prodn Supply Area field for each of the components that will undergo replenishment via Kanban. 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban In the following sections, we’ll cover the impact of Kanban configuration and Kanban master data on the three kanban replenishment strategies: production, procurement, and stock transfer. We’ll also cover several associated topics, such as how to print a kanban, how to handle Kanban errors, and how to manually post a Kanban goods receipt. Regardless of the Kanban strategy you use, we suggest that you read all the sec- tions; there’s a significant amount of information common to all three Kanban replen- ishment strategies. For example, regardless of the Kanban replenishment strategy you use, a user will most likely face Kanban errors that require processing or rectification. We’ll also cover how to address scenarios in which you encounter errors. 9 Kanban 422

sections.title: Kanban Replenishment via Production
sections.en: 9.6.1 Kanban Replenishment via Production With the necessary Kanban configuration in place and the master data set up, we can move forward with the business processes involved in replenishing a kanban through a procurement process. To begin using the Kanban board (in the Demand Source view), you use Transaction PK13N or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Con- trol  Demand Source View. Figure 9.15 shows the initial parameters screen; here, you enter “1710” for Plant, choose Production Supply Area and enter “Kanban,” and enter “2262” for Material. Then, check the Tabular View box and press (Enter), and the screen shown in Figure 9.16 will appear. Figure 9.15 Kanban Board Demand Source Overview Initial Screen Note Although our example uses the Demand Source view of the Kanban board, you can also use the Supply Source view by using Transaction PK12N or following menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Control  Supply Source View. As soon as the system creates the control cycle, the kanbans attain WAIT status, as shown in Figure 9.16. © 423 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.16 Kanban Board in SAP GUI If you choose the tabular view to display the Kanban board, all information will appear on a table. But if you don’t choose the tabular view, then the left-hand side of the screen will show details such as supply area, material number, and kanban quantity, and the right-hand side will show the actual statuses of various kanbans through unique iden- tification (using the internal number range defined in the configuration) by means of various color codes. Here, you can see four kanbans (with Kanban IDs 88, 89, 90, and 91) with the WAIT sta- tus. To learn which Kanban status each color code denotes, choose Legend, shown in the lower half of Figure 9.16. Figure 9.17 shows the Kanban Board app, displaying the same information as in Figure 9.16. A user has the option to use the SAP Fiori app or SAP GUI to access the Kanban board, as preferred. We’ll use both interfaces. Before delving into replenishment via Kanban, let’s also take a quick look at the stock/ requirements list for material 2262 and plant 1710. The purpose of evaluating this list is to show how the system continuously updates the stock/requirements list based on various activities and actions performed during the Kanban process. To navigate to the stock/requirements list, you use Transaction MD04 or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Evaluations  Stock/Require- ments List. 9 Kanban 424 Figure 9.17 Kanban Board App On the initial screen that appears, enter “2262” for Material and “1710” for Plant and press (Enter). The resulting screen is shown in Figure 9.18. The first thing to notice is that there isn’t any stock at the plant and storage location level. Because the Kanban process hasn’t started yet, there aren’t any replenishment elements, such as produc- tion orders or purchase orders. Figure 9.18 Stock/Requirements List without Any Replenishment Elements Now, let’s start the Kanban process! Using Transaction PK13N (see Figure 9.16), you set two kanbans to EMPTY for the first time. To do so, select two of the orange WAIT kan- bans (88 and 89) and choose the Set to Empty button that’s located on the right-hand side of the upper tool bar of the Kanban board. This step is a signal from the demand source to the supply source to begin the replenishment process. The system then updates the kanbans’ status to EMPTY, as shown in Figure 9.19, and the color of the empty kanbans changes to red. © 425 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.19 Two Kanbans with EMPTY Status Tips and Tricks To select multiple kanbans, choose the first kanban and then hold (Ctrl) and select additional kanbans. Setting the kanbans to EMPTY triggers creation of two production orders, as main- tained in the control cycle. Then, double-click Kanban 88 in Figure 9.19 and the screen in Figure 9.20 will appear. It shows detailed information for Kanban ID Number 88, which links it to production order 00008000012. Figure 9.20 Kanban Information: Detailed View 9 Kanban 426 Using Transaction MD04 again, let’s take a look at the new replenishment elements that were created after two kanbans were set to EMPTY (see Figure 9.21). The system cre- ated two production orders: 00008000012 and 00008000013, which are marked Prd- Ord in the MRP Element column. Figure 9.21 Stock/Requirements List after Replenishment Elements Creation Let’s also take a quick look at production order 00008000012. Using Transaction CO03, Figure 9.22 shows the production order. Note the checked Kanban Indicator box in the Assignment tab, which reflects how the production process integrates with Kanban. Figure 9.22 Production Order with Checked Kanban Indictor © 427 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.23 shows the updated status of four kanbans in the Kanban Board app. The pur- ple color of two kanban IDs denotes that they are in process, which is the next target status based on the configuration. If there’s a colorless kanban ID, then it denotes that an error occurred when the user tried to set the next kanban status. We’ll show how to handle Kanban error processing shortly. Figure 9.23 Kanban Board with Updated Kanban Statuses Next, choose kanban 88 on the screen in Figure 9.23, and choose Set to FULL to denote that replenishment of kanban ID 88 has been completed (goods are received). The kanban’s color then changes to green to denote that it’s full (see Figure 9.24). The sys- tem will display a message (not shown) that confirms the number of kanbans pro- cessed. At the same time, and on successful replenishment, the system changes the kanbans from EMPTY to FULL on the Kanban board. Figure 9.24 Setting Kanban to FULL Status If a user is using the SAP GUI version of the Kanban board, then double-click the rele- vant kanban to view the details. The material document number against which the sys- tem posted the goods receipt when the status of the kanban was set to FULL will also be displayed. There are three ways to set the Kanban to full:  Scan the barcode that denotes the FULL status.  Use the Kanban board’s Set to Full button. 9 Kanban 428  Execute a normal goods receipt, which will automatically set the kanban to green (FULL). Using Transaction MD04 for the stocks/requirement list, Figure 9.25 shows that the replenishment element (production order 10000082) is no longer in the system now that the Kanban has been set to FULL via automatic goods receipt. However, the second replenishment element (production order 000080000013) is still there and awaits a goods receipt. Figure 9.25 Replenishment Element (Production Order) Reduced Let’s now discuss the business processes of Kanban replenishment via a procurement process.

sections.title: Kanban Replenishment via Procurement
sections.en: 9.6.2 Kanban Replenishment via Procurement To set up a replenishment strategy for stock transfer, follow menu path SAP IMG  Pro- duction  Kanban  Replenishment Strategies  Define External Procurement Strategies. On the screen that appears, enter “1710” for the plant and “0001” (“Working with pur- chase orders”) in the External Procurement field, as shown in Figure 9.26. Here, you have nearly all of the same Control Type options that were available for in-house pro- duction. Because this replenishment strategy is for external procurement, the configu- ration settings here relate to kanban replenishment via the procurement process. Here, Order Type refers to the purchase order type that the system will create in kanban replenishment. The next step is to maintain the setting that will exclude the storage location from MRP. To do so, you set up the MRP area for a specific storage location as discussed in Section 14.4 of Chapter 14. © 429 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.26 Control Types for Replenishment Strategy for External Procurement The procurement department creates a quantity contract with the supplier for the materials that the production department needs to consume in Kanban processes. To create a quantity contract, you use Transaction ME31K or follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Purchasing  Outline Agreement  Contract  Create. Figure 9.27 shows the main details, including the Supplier (enter “USSU-VSF06”), Material (enter “2259”), target quantity (Targ. Qty), and price (Net Price). When you save, the system will create quantity contract number 4600000040. We’ll use this quantity contract num- ber in the control cycle for kanban replenishment. Figure 9.27 Quantity Contract 9 Kanban 430 The control cycle is the most important master data used for the Kanban process, and it defines the relationship between the demand and the supply sources. The control cycle contains the following control data for kanban replenishment via procurement:  Kanban circulation (the number of kanbans and the kanban quantity)  Replenishment strategy (external procurement, in-house production, or stock transfer)  Printing kanbans  The delivery address  The basic data required for automatic Kanban calculation (if necessary)  The process control data Control cycles can be maintained for classic Kanban. The system uses these control cycles for Kanban with quantity signals or for one-card or event-driven Kanban. In event-driven Kanban, the system only creates a kanban when required; for example, a certain event based on actual material consumption triggers the Kanban process. To create a control cycle, you use Transaction PK01. On the screen shown in Figure 9.28, enter “2259” for Material, “1710” for Plant, and “Kanban” for the Supply Area. Then choose the Classic Kanban radio button and press (Enter). Figure 9.28 Control Cycle Creation On the screen shown in Figure 9.29, you enter short text in the Storing Position field to denote the physical location in which to place the kanban container. For this example, © 431 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban enter “4” for the No. of Kanbans and “2” for Maximum Empty; this indicates that there will be four kanbans at this supply area and that a maximum of two kanban containers can remain empty at any time. Maintain the printer settings that the system will use to print the kanban’s barcode. Next, choose the replenishment strategy for a kanban. In this example, we chose exter- nal procurement as the replenishment strategy by selecting the Ext. Procurement. radio button (not shown). After selecting the replenishment strategy, choose Replen- ishment Strategy 0001 (not shown). Figure 9.29 Replenishment Strategy (External Procurement) in Control Cycle On the screen shown in Figure 9.29, enter specific details to replenish kanbans via external procurement. Enter “1710” for Purchasing Org., “USSU-VSF06” for Supplier, and “4600000040” for Agreement (quantity contract), along with item number “10” from the quantity contract that you created in the previous step. Next, choose the Flow Control tab, which brings up the screen shown in Figure 9.30. Then, check the Separate GR box if your company doesn’t want to link a kanban FULL status with automatic goods receipt for a purchase order. 9 Kanban 432 Figure 9.30 Assignment of Quantity Contract in Control Cycle If you check the Independent Supply Source box, then it’s not the demand source but the supply source that will trigger replenishment. The demand source will set the kanban status to EMPTY, but the system won’t create a replenishment element and will set the kanban status to WAIT. While remaining in the Kanban board screen, the supply source will then trigger replenishment for these kanbans, thereby setting the status of such kanbans to EMPTY. The independent supply source thus determines the time of replenishment by itself. Also, you assign the previously configured external status sequence in this step to con- trol how the system does or doesn’t allow the user to set various statuses. To begin using the Kanban board (in the Supply Source view), you use Transaction PK12N or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Con- trol  Supply Source View. Figure 9.31 shows the initial parameters screen; here, you enter “1710” for Plant (in the first Plant field), choose Supplier and enter “USSU-VSF06,” and enter “2259” for Mate- rial. Then, press (Enter); the resulting screen is shown in Figure 9.32. Figure 9.31 Kanban Board: Demand Source © 433 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Note Although we used the Supply Source view of the Kanban board here, a planner can also use the Demand Source view by using Transaction PK13N or following menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Control  Demand Source View. As soon as the system creates the control cycle, the kanbans attain WAIT status, as shown in Figure 9.32. The left-hand side of the screen shows details such as supply area, material number, and kanban quantity, and the right-hand side shows the actual sta- tuses of various kanbans through unique identification (using the internal number range defined in configuration) by means of various color codes. Here, there are four kanbans (84, 85, 86, and 87) with the WAIT status. To learn which Kanban status each color code denotes, choose Legend, as shown in the lower half of Figure 9.32. Figure 9.32 Kanban Board Legend: Identifying Kanban Status Colors Before delving into replenishment via Kanban, let’s take a quick look at the stock/ requirements list for material 2259 and plant 1710. The purpose of evaluating this list is to show how the system continuously updates the stock/requirements list based on various activities and actions that you perform during the Kanban process. To navigate to the stock/requirements list, use Transaction MD04 or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Evaluations  Stock/Require- ments List. 9 Kanban 434 On the initial screen that appears, enter “2259” for Material and “1710” for Plant and press (Enter). The resulting screen is shown in Figure 9.33. The first thing to notice is that there isn’t any stock at the plant and storage location level. Furthermore, the sys- tem indicates that storage location 171A isn’t planned. This is the result of the storage location level setting you previously established in the material master to exclude stor- age location 171A from all forms of planning by creating an MRP area for storage loca- tion 171A. (MRP areas are discussed in detail in Chapter 13, Section 13.4.) Because the Kanban process hasn’t started yet, there aren’t any replenishment elements yet, such as purchase orders as production orders. Figure 9.33 Stock/Requirements List for Procured Materials Now, let’s start the Kanban process! Using Transaction PK13N (see Figure 9.34), you set the kanbans to EMPTY for the first time. Then, select the two purple WAIT kanbans (84 and 85) and navigate to Edit  Set Status to Empty. This signals the system to begin the replenishment process via procurement, and the system will update the kanbans’ sta- tuses, as shown in Figure 9.34. Figure 9.34 Available and Unused Kanbans © 435 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Tips and Tricks To select multiple kanbans, choose the first kanban, hold (Ctrl), and select additional kanbans. To select several kanbans that are next to each other, choose a kanban at one end of the board and drag the mouse cursor to the other end. Figure 9.34 shows the status of kanbans 84 and 85 as EMPTY. The EMPTY status triggers the kanban replenishment process, as defined in the control cycle of the material-plant combination. Setting the kanbans to EMPTY triggers generation of two purchase orders, which are basically contract release orders for the quantity contract maintained in the control cycle. A contract release order is a subset of the outline agreement (quantity or value contract) and is functionally the same as a purchase order. The difference between a contract release order and a purchase order is that the former links outline agreements and the latter does not. Using Transaction MD04, let’s take a look at the new replenishment elements that were created after two kanbans were set to EMPTY (see Figure 9.35). The system created two purchase orders (4500001883 and 4500001884, marked POItem in the MRP element column) as soon as the status of the two kanbans was set to EMPTY. Figure 9.35 Stock/Requirements List with Replenishment Elements Figure 9.36 shows information for kanban 84 that links it to purchase order 4500001883. Using Transaction ME23N, the screen shown in Figure 9.37 shows purchase order 4500001883, which links to quantity contract 4600000040. Note the kanban indicator (Kanban Indicat.) in the Delivery tab, which reflects how procurement integrates with Kanban. 9 Kanban 436 Figure 9.36 Detailed Kanban Information Figure 9.37 Contract Release Order (Purchase Order) To post goods receipts, you select the kanban to fill (in this case, 002) and then navigate to Edit  Set Status to Full (see Figure 9.38). The system will display a message that confirms the number of kanbans processed. At the same time, and on successful replenishment, the system changes the kanbans from EMPTY to FULL and changes the color to green. © 437 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.38 shows the status of kanban 84 as it appears after you double-click it. Notice the material document number, 5000002523, against which the system posted the goods receipt when the status of the kanban was set to FULL. Figure 9.38 Empty Kanbans Awaiting Replenishment Now, let’s now take a look at the stock overview report for material 2259 in plant 1710. To start, you use Transaction MMBE or follow menu path Logistics  Production Plan- ning  Production  Kanban  Evaluations  Stock Overview. Figure 9.39 shows the Unit of Measure for material 2259 as PC (piece), the Unrestricted use stock as 50, and the On- Order Stock (open purchase orders) as 50. Figure 9.39 Stock Overview 9 Kanban 438

sections.title: Kanban Replenishment via Stock Transfer
sections.en: 9.6.3 Kanban Replenishment via Stock Transfer Finally, let’s discuss the third kanban replenishment method: replenishment via stock transfer. Based on the configuration settings you’ve made, this section covers two-step stock transfers using movement types 311 and 313. When Stock Transfer is set to “0001” (two-step) in the Replenishment Strategy tab of a material’s control cycle (Transaction PK01 or PK02; see Figure 9.30), the system brings up the screen shown in Figure 9.40. Figure 9.40 Stock Transfer as Kanban Replenishment Strategy The screen shown in Figure 9.40 requires you to maintain a Storage Location (enter “171B”) from which the system will post goods issuance for stock transfer. Remember that you defined the goods receipt’s storage location (171A) when you created the pro- duction supply area (in Transaction PK05). Figure 9.41 shows the Kanban board displaying four waiting kanban containers, which will now be replenished with a stock transfer. In Figure 9.42, choose Kanban 95 and then choose Edit  Set Status to Empty from the top menu items. After changing the kanban status to EMPTY, double-click Kanban 95 to open the screen shown on the right-hand side of Figure 9.42. © 439 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.41 Kanban Board for Managing Stock Transfer Replenishment Figure 9.42 Kanban Information for Stock Transfer with Reservation Figure 9.42 shows that the system will replenish kanban 95 in two steps. In the first step, once you mark the kanban as EMPTY, the system creates stock transfer reservation 0000016740. In the second step, you set the status of Kanban 95 to FULL, which not only updates the kanban’s status and color to green but also updates the stock transfer’s goods move- ments’ details (see Figure 9.43). Figure 9.43 shows a goods receipt quantity (GR Quantity) of 50 while creating Material Document 4900007779, and the Kanban Status reads Container full. 9 Kanban 440 Figure 9.43 Kanban Information with Material Document

sections.title: Printing Kanban Cards
sections.en: 9.6.4 Printing Kanban Cards There are three ways to print kanban cards: single print, collective kanban print, and renewed kanban print for each control cycle. In single print, cards are either only printed once or printed for replacement purposes using the create control cycle and change control cycle functions. In this option, you use the same kanban card over and over again. Here, there are three options:  The kanban card circulates between the supply source and the demand source with the container.  The kanban card is passed from the demand source to the supply source without a container but returns to the demand source with one. The transport of the cards to the supply source has to be organized.  The kanban card remains at the demand source and is attached to the full kanban or is stored without a kanban at a predefined location. There is no information on the circulating kanbans, and the information on empty kanbans is kept at the demand source. Information is passed on to the supply source by means of the Kanban board. When a kanban is delivered, the card has to be retrieved at the demand source. © 441 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban With collective kanban print, kanban cards can be printed across the control cycle. In other words, collective kanban print allows collectively or mass printing several kan- bans in one step. In the case of a print error, you can repeat the printing of certain kan- bans (e.g., all kanbans that should have printed on a certain printer during a certain time period). In a renewed kanban print for each control cycle run, the card is thrown away at the end of each cycle. The following options (using the Kanban Print field in the control cycle) are then available:  1 Card Printed when Replenishment Is Triggered With in-house production or stock transfer, the card is printed at the supply source. This means that the card doesn’t have to be transported to the supply source. The card can be used as an extra written request for delivery (e.g., for in-house produc- tion with planned orders or manual kanbans). For production orders, printing is car- ried out using the print control for production orders; in all other cases, it’s carried out using the Kanban transaction.  2 Card Printed when Goods Receipt Is Posted It makes sense to use this option for external procurement with central goods receipts. If a company uses a strategy in which the goods receipt is carried out auto- matically in the background as soon as the status is changed to FULL, then you shouldn’t use this print control technique. The system prints the kanban on GR post- ing for a purchase order or a production order (or a stock transfer reservation) by using the GR function in IM.  Blank The kanban card is printed instead of the GR papers and is attached to the kanban. To print a kanban card, use Transaction PK17 or follow menu path Logistics  Production  Kanban  Control Cycle  Print Kanban. On the initial screen that appears, enter appropri- ate criteria to search for the kanbans that need to be printed and then choose Execute. The system then displays a list of kanbans that you can print. Choose the appropriate kanbans by checking their boxes and then choose Print Selected Kanbans. The system will print to the local network printer (see Figure 9.44). Figure 9.44 Print Preview of Kanban 9 Kanban 442

sections.title: Kanban Error Processing
sections.en: 9.6.5 Kanban Error Processing Using Transaction PK50, you can access the initial screen shown in Figure 9.45 that dis- plays and rectifies kanbans with processing errors. Here, you enter parameters to ensure that the system only displays kanbans experiencing processing errors. For this example, enter “1710” for Plant and “Kanban” for Prodn Supply Area. Then, choose Exe- cute (not shown) and the screen shown in Figure 9.46 will appear. Figure 9.45 Initial Screen for Kanban Error Processing Figure 9.46 shows that control cycle 16 has one kanban, 92, in need of error processing. Check the relevant box for the kanban to correct it and then choose Kanban Correction. This will bring up the screen shown in Figure 9.47. Figure 9.47 shows the details for kanban correction for control cycle 92. Here, the Status of Kanban ID number 1 is ERROR. Double-click the ID of the kanban with an error, which in this example is 92, and the system will open the error correction options shown in the lower half of the screen. © 443 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.46 Kanban Error Processing Figure 9.47 Detailed Kanban Error Processing In the lower half of Figure 9.47, some of the error correction options are intended to correct the kanban’s status or its quantity/batch or even to reverse the processing of an 9 Kanban 444 already processed kanban. In this example, the production order wasn’t released and the kanban’s status couldn’t be set to FULL, and that led to the Kanban error. Therefore, choose the Kanban Corr. button and the system will prompt you to confirm the action. Choose Yes, and the system will update the status of the kanban to the correct setting. In this example, Kanban 1 is correctly set to FULL, as this is the next status according to the status sequence. As a rule, when a status change is incorrect, the kanban is always set to ERROR, and the kanban can’t be used again until the kanban error correction is ensured. It’s possible to define cases in which the system issues an error message that doesn’t change the status of the kanban, but in all other cases, the system sets the kanban status to ERROR. For example, if there’s an invalid cost collector for the backflush of a run schedule quantity, it’s good business practice to set the ERROR status so that the user must com- plete the missing data. In other cases—for example, if the data is only temporarily locked by another user—an error message is sufficient. The user can repeat this action at any time, and the kanban retains its status. Referring to Figure 9.47 again, in the case of a posting error, the user can reverse the last Kanban signal (e.g., changing the status from EMPTY to FULL). To do so, you can choose Reversal, and the system will perform the following actions:  It will reset the current container status to the previous status.  It will reverse the actions linked to the status change. That is, it will flag purchase orders, reservations, and production orders with a deletion indicator, and it will delete planned orders and schedule lines. It will also reverse the document from the goods receipt, and so on. On the other hand, if a replenishment element for a kanban (e.g., a planned order, pro- duction order, or purchase order) is deleted or you set a deletion indicator for it, the system will set the relevant Kanban to WAIT status. In the following cases, it’s not possible to reverse the Kanban signal:  If the system has already processed the kanban correction, it’s no longer possible to carry out a reversal.  If a kanban has already been reversed, it can’t be reversed a second time. Now, refer to Figure 9.48 wherein this specific kanban error stipulates that the error was encountered when a user tried to change the kanban status from EMPTY to FULL, possi- bly due to the fact that there wasn’t enough stock available at that time. To fix this kanban error, you choose the Kanban Corr. (correction) button, and the system will bring up the popup where you can confirm whether the user would like to change the status of the kanban again. Choose Yes. © 445 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban Figure 9.48 Resolving a Kanban Error Figure 9.49 shows that kanban 92 is now set to FULL and has simultaneously triggered a goods issuance. Double-clicking Kanban 92 brings up the detailed information shown on the right-hand side of the screen. Figure 9.49 Updated Kanban ID 92 after Successful Error Processing 9 Kanban 446 Figure 9.49 also shows that when you set the Kanban status to FULL, the replenishment element triggered the GI process, which resulted in the creation of material document 4900007880/2024.

sections.title: Separated Post Goods Receipts
sections.en: 9.6.6 Separated Post Goods Receipts There’s another Kanban scenario in which you undertake goods receipts for produc- tion orders as a separate step (via Transaction MIGO) instead of setting the kanban sta- tus to FULL. Separately posting goods receipts meets the business needs of companies that have slightly longer lead times or that have some physical distance to cover to bring the kanban containers to the shop floor for use in production or assembly. Choosing to separately post GRs still integrates with the Kanban process; the system shows kanban containers with different identifications to denote cases in which GR has been undertaken or is awaited. Once you’re back at the Kanban board (using Transaction PK13N), the screen will show the statuses of various kanban containers. In a separate GR, trying to change the kanban status to Set to Full does not adhere to the predefined status sequence of the control cycle and thus creates an error. The error message explains that there’s a need to first set the mandatory In Process status before updating the status to Set to Full. A FULL status set for a kanban highlights it with green shading. However, a small dash sign to the right of kanban ID indicates that although the status of the kanban was marked as Set to Full, the system still needs to update its actual GR. Although the GR for the production order was performed separately in the previous step, this delay in the kanban status update is due to the configuration settings that determine the system will only update a kanban’s status after a predefined time interval (five minutes).

sections.title: Kanban Signal: Two-Kanban Process
sections.en: 9.6.7 Kanban Signal: Two-Kanban Process Instead of triggering the Kanban signal by setting a kanban to EMPTY, you can with- draw quantities from kanbans in a control cycle by using the quantity signal. In this case, the system reduces the actual quantity by the quantity specified in the quantity signal. At the same time, the system recognizes when the actual kanban quantity is zero and automatically sets the kanban to EMPTY. When the quantity is withdrawn from a container for the first time, the system sets the status to IN USE. If the with- drawal quantity exceeds the actual quantity of the kanban, the system reduces the actual quantity of the next kanban. This means the system first reduces kanbans with the IN USE status and then reduces kanbans that have had the FULL status for the lon- gest amount of time. When the quantity signal is used, the system updates only the actual quantity of the kanban and does not carry out stock postings. The system carries out the stock postings when backflushing the material. It’s also possible to combine the kanban and the quantity signals. © 447 9.6 Replenishment with Kanban The procedure is similar to the previous procedures. However, in this case, the kanban is set to EMPTY when the remaining quantity in the kanban reaches or exceeds the trig- ger quantity. In this process, the parts are withdrawn with the quantity signal, but it’s mandatory to maintain the trigger quantity and the logic in the control cycle. The with- drawal quantity can be posted either with the quantity signal or using a production data control system. The biggest disadvantage of using the quantity signal is the need to individually report quantities. Kanban only manages the status of the kanbans and controls material flow and produc- tion using the Kanban signals. In the case of underdelivery, remaining quantities of the production/purchase order remain. Now, let’s examine the Kanban signal through an example. First, you should create a control cycle (Transaction PK01) for a new material, 2263, and define the total number of kanbans as two, each of which should have a quantity of five. At any given time, only one kanban can remain empty. Also, specify that the kanban quantity should trigger replenishment and that the trigger quantity is three. When the kanban quantity signal indicates that a kanban quantity of three or less remains in a container, it will automat- ically trigger replenishment. The Kanban board in Figure 9.50 shows that the status of Kanban 97 is WAIT and Kan- ban 96 is EMPTY. Select Kanban 96 and then choose To Full so that system updates the kanban status as shown in Figure 9.51. Figure 9.50 Two-Kanban Replenishment Using Quantity Signal Figure 9.51 Kanbans with WAIT and FULL Statuses Let’s use the Kanban quantity signal to denote withdrawal or consumption of material 2263 from Kanban 96. We’ll also show how this automatically impacts the status of Kanban 97. 9 Kanban 448 To record quantity consumption of material using the quantity signal, use Transaction PK22 or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Con- trol  Kanban Signal  Quantity Signal. Figure 9.52 shows the screen for entering the Kanban ID Number (enter “96”) and the Withdrawal Quantity (enter “4”). Save your entries. The system will update the infor- mation as shown at the bottom of the screen. You’ll see that now, the actual quantity that remains in Kanban 96 is 1, because four have been withdrawn. Figure 9.52 Kanban Quantity Signal In this way, the system continuously rotates the EMPTY, WAIT, and FULL kanban sta- tuses for two kanbans by using quantity signals (see Figure 9.53). Figure 9.53 Updated Status of Kanban 97 Tips and Tricks With Kanban, it’s possible for the system to automatically update statuses and quanti- ties. However, due to human error, such updates often need adjustment. To make © 449

sections.title: Evaluating and Reporting Kanban
sections.en: 9.7 Evaluating and Reporting Kanban adjustments, you use Transaction PK21 or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Control  Kanban Signal  Manual Entry.

sections.title: Logical Stock and Physical Stock
sections.en: 9.6.8 Logical Stock and Physical Stock Setting a kanban to EMPTY doesn’t lead to a goods issue posting. In general, the requested replenishment order is confirmed and kanban materials are backflushed simultaneously (via goods issuance for components). The backflush has no influence on the kanban’s status. Upon consumption of relevant material, the kanban is set to EMPTY. This action is based only on the physical stock and is completely independent of any stock posting in the system. However, with insufficient control or monitoring, considerable variances can occur between physical stock and logical stock because goods movements aren’t posted simultaneously. In conventional production control, the lot size calculation determines production quantities. With Kanban, fixed container quantities are deter- mined (the lot size is always the same). The procured quantity within a period of time depends on the frequency with which the kanbans are set to EMPTY. Replenishment frequency is based on actual consumption. The more of a material that is required, the faster the kanbans flow between supply and demand sources. If material consumption decreases, the flow of kanbans slows down. There is never more material in circulation than the quantity defined by the number of kanbans in the control cycle. If a material isn’t required for a certain period of time, all kanbans remain at the demand source. Use the Kanban calculation to adjust the number of kanbans to suit the current requirements situation. 9.7 Evaluating and Reporting Kanban Timely, accurate, and comprehensive information helps a planner make better- informed and overall better business decisions. In this section, we’ll cover a few evalu- ation and analysis reports that are specific to Kanban. It’s important to note that the Kanban board only offers an overview of the kanbans at a certain point in time. The standard analyses in LIS and in SAP Fiori apps provide tools for creating evaluations of kanban circulation for a specified interval.

sections.title: Control Cycle and Kanban Evaluation
sections.en: 9.7.1 Control Cycle and Kanban Evaluation To access the control cycle and Kanban evaluation, use Transaction PK18 or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Evaluations  Control Cycles/Kanbans. 9 Kanban 450 Figure 9.54 shows the initial screen of the control cycle and Kanban evaluation. Enter the desired selection parameters so that the system only presents the information rel- evant for evaluation. Enter “1710” for Plant and “KANBAN” for Prodn Supply Area. Choose Execute (not shown) and the screen shown in Figure 9.55 will appear. Figure 9.54 Initial Screen for Control Cycle and Kanban Evaluation Figure 9.55 Control Cycle and Kanban Evaluation © 451 9.7 Evaluating and Reporting Kanban Figure 9.55 shows the list of kanbans, their statuses, the replenishment elements (e.g., production order, purchase order, transfer posting), and the material documents that were created as a result of any inventory posting.

sections.title: Kanban Analysis
sections.en: 9.7.2 Kanban Analysis The standard analyses available in the SAP S/4HANA system enable users to draw infor- mation from the standard information structures. The difference between the reports and standard analysis is that in information systems, the information is dynamic and changes when the business user performs relevant business functions. In standard analysis, the system draws from historical information to enable the business user to make better business decisions. In other words, information from information sys- tems flows into analyses. To access the Kanban analysis, use Transaction MCQ. or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Evaluations  Information System. Figure 9.56 shows the initial screen of the Kanban analysis. Enter “1710” for Plant, “KAN- BAN” for Prodn Supply Area, and a date range for Kanban analysis. Choose Execute and the screen shown in Figure 9.57 will appear. Figure 9.56 Initial Screen for Kanban Analysis Figure 9.57 shows some of the key Kanban figures for plant 1710. As in all standard reports and analyses, you can display or hide the available fields. Double-click a key fig- ure, such as Total Act. Qty, and the screen shown in Figure 9.58 appears. It shows all of the important Kanban key figures associated with plant 1710. From various lead times to quantities to additional information, the Kanban analysis has them all covered. 9 Kanban 452 Figure 9.57 Kanban Analysis Figure 9.58 Key Figures for Kanban Replenishment

sections.title: Delayed Replenishment Elements
sections.en: 9.7.3 Delayed Replenishment Elements It’s important for a Kanban planner to have visibility into kanbans facing delayed replenishment so that the planner can implement necessary alerts or actions to pre- vent supply chain disruptions. The evaluation of delayed replenishment should pro- vide not only the necessary visibility but also the option to issue timely alerts. To evaluate or issue alerts for delayed replenishment elements, you use Transaction PKAL or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Eval- uations  Delayed Replenishment Elements. © 453 9.7 Evaluating and Reporting Kanban On the initial screen that appears, enter the plant, the delivery date (and the time), and other selection parameters in the relevant fields so that the system displays the desired information. If needed, check the Trigger Alerts box to enable the system to issue alerts for all delayed kanbans. Choose Execute and the screen shown in Figure 9.59 appears. In this example, at least three kanbans are awaiting replenishment. The kanbans’ statuses are set to 2 (container empty, with replenishment). Figure 9.59 Delayed Replenishment Elements

sections.title: Kanban Plant Overview
sections.en: 9.7.4 Kanban Plant Overview To get a plant-wide overview of the kanbans’ replenishment statuses, you use Transac- tion PK11 or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban  Evaluations  Plant Overview. On the initial screen, enter selection parameters so that the system only displays the information needed. The screen shown in Figure 9.60 shows the status of each mate- rial, the supply area, the number of kanbans, and their statuses. For example, for mate- rial 2262, there are four kanbans, each with a quantity of 50. The system shows that two kanbans (50%) have a WAIT status, one kanban (25%) is FULL, and one kanban (25%) is IN TRANSIT. Figure 9.60 Kanban Plant Overview Figure 9.61 shows the Set Kanban Container Status app. You can use this app to update the status of various kanbans. To do so, place the cursor in the Enter Barcode field, and in the ensuing dropdown, choose from a list of all the available kanbans. After you make the relevant selection, the right-hand side of the screen will enable you to make 9 Kanban 454 the desired changes or updates, such as changing from one status to another. Once you’ve successfully saved the entries, they’ll appear in the Saved tab. Figure 9.61 Set Kanban Container Status App In addition, you can use the Schedule Kanban Control Cycle Status Change app to schedule regular updates to the statuses of various kanbans without making manual entries.

sections.title: Kanban Control Cycle Analysis
sections.en: 9.7.5 Kanban Control Cycle Analysis While Chapter 21 is focused on discussing many SAP Fiori apps for production plan- ning, we’ll discuss one app here that is specific to Kanban: the Kanban Control Cycle Analysis app. The Kanban Control Cycle Analysis app evaluates the Kanban control cycles with focus on the status of the circulating kanban containers. It identifies critical situations early and quickly, for example, Kanban control cycles containing errors or having too many open replenishments (visualized by the number of empty kanban containers). You can use it to get insights into the overall Kanban replenishment situation at the aggregated level (for example, at the plant level) and then drill down to the details at the Kanban control cycle level. A range of chart types are available for evaluations (see Figure 9.62). Note Refer to SAP PRESS E-Bite Kanban in SAP S/4HANA for a detailed understanding on this topic. © 455

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 9.8 Summary Figure 9.62 Kanban Control Cycle Analysis app 9.8 Summary Implementing Kanban isn’t restricted to the production process; it can be imple- mented in procurement, stock transfer, and interplant transfer processes. In this chap- ter, we covered the Kanban configuration that needs to be in place so that configured objects can be assigned to the master data. We discussed the entire production process using Kanban, including the use of the Kanban Board app. We also discussed handling Kanban errors, as well as Kanban evaluation and reporting. The next chapter is about batch management. ©

sections.title: Batch Management Overview
sections.en: 10.1 Batch Management Overview The definition of a batch differs among companies, industries, and countries. For exam- ple, in the pharmaceutical industry, strict guidelines and regulations determine what a batch is. These regulations on batches and batch control include the ANSI/ISA-88 stan- dard and the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) 21 CFR Part 11 specifications in the United States. 10 Batch Management 458 Although there is no one exact definition of a batch, the following definition from ExxonMobil Aviation may help: A batch is the specific quantity of a material produced in a single manufacturing process (i.e., under the same conditions, thus featuring identical properties). Each batch of material is given a batch number. Each batch of a material is tested with regard to relevant characteristics to ensure it meets the values or is within the range for those characteristics. A second definition of a batch is from Marathon Oil Company, and it significantly dif- fers from other definitions: “A batch is a shipment of a single product that is handled through the pipeline without mixing with preceding or following shipments.” This third definition of a batch is from the Hawaiian Coffee Association: A batch refers to a quantity of coffee coming to the roaster. Quantities of the same coffee arriving at different times would be viewed as separate batches. Changes from batch to batch—even of the same variety of bean—must be detected by the roaster if he is to produce coffees that are consistently the same. Whatever the definition, the fact is that the batch has to be identified by a batch record. This can be as simple as identifying bags of coffee beans as they arrive at the plant or as complex as identifying a batch by numerous qualifying characteristics to ensure qual- ity and safety. A batch of material can either be purchased from a supplier or produced internally. We’ve discussed the need to manage materials by batch; however, in SAP, the material must be identified as batch relevant. The identification of a batch record is especially important for the pharmaceutical industry, due to the regulations set down by the FDA in the United States and other reg- ulatory bodies around the world, such as the Drugs Controller General of India, Bundes- gesundheitsamt in Germany, Health Canada, and the Medicines and Healthcare Prod- ucts Regulatory Agency in the United Kingdom. These regulatory bodies are primarily interested in public safety. Regulations such as FDA 21 CFR Part 11 in the United States are aimed at improving the efficiency of quality control and the quality assurance process. Each batch produced has to be quality tested, with the results stored electronically against the batch number. The most critical use of batch numbers in the pharmaceutical industry is for product recall. Batch numbers also can be used as tracking devices for companies in case of errors or contamination. Manufacturers publish product recalls every day, but for the pharmaceutical industry, product recalls can save lives. A pharmaceutical company can voluntarily recall a product. If the company finds that a result from a test on a batch was incorrect, putting the batch out of tolerance, then the product made from that batch could be hazardous. © 459

sections.title: Batch Level and Batch Status Management
sections.en: 10.2 Batch Level and Batch Status Management The errors could go all the way back to the supplier, if any of the material was pur- chased. If a supplier informs the company that a batch of purchased material was out of tolerance, then that batch must be traced through the production process to find all finished goods batches that may have used components or ingredients from the faulty batch. In the United States, the FDA has the power to request that a company initiate a recall when it believes that a drug violates the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act. The FDA will request a recall when it concludes the following:  A drug that has been distributed presents a risk of illness, injury, or gross consumer deception.  The manufacturer or distributor hasn’t recalled the drug.  FDA action is necessary to protect public health. In a recall, the manufacturer informs the retailer, wholesaler, or even consumer about how to identify the batch number on the product and which batch numbers are a part of the recall. Let’s begin with the configuration basics of batch management. 10.2 Batch Level and Batch Status Management In the following sections, we’ll follow a step-by-step approach to the configuration steps required to set up batch management in the SAP system.

sections.title: Defining the Batch Level
sections.en: 10.2.1 Defining the Batch Level A batch can be defined at different levels, but it’s defined at the plant level by default. At the beginning of an SAP S/4HANA implementation project, an MM consultant helps the company decide the batch level at which it requires batch monitoring and report- ing. Use the following guidelines to determine that batch numbers are unique at the follow- ing three levels:  Plant and material combination level At this level, the plant and material combination have unique batch numbers. Dif- ferent materials in the same plant can be assigned the same batch number, and the same material in different plants can also be assigned the same batch number. In this context, the material is transferable from one plant to another, and although the batch number is the same, the specification of the destination batch will remain unchanged.  Material number level As batch numbers are created at the material level, the same batch numbers can be 10 Batch Management 460 used for other materials as well. After setting the batch level as the material in the system, a material can have the same batch number even though the materials are in different plants with the same specifications. The same batch number can be reas- signed to other materials with different specifications.  Client level The batch level is set at the client level, which is the highest level. The same batch number can’t be used for different materials or different plants. The batch number assigned is unique and can be assigned only once; thus, every material will always have a new or different batch number. The system’s default setting is the plant level. As mentioned earlier, this can be custom- ized to the batch level as needed. However, it’s important to note that while switching to a higher level is always possible, it’s only possible to lower the batch level from the client level to the material level. This is because the batch data is organized so that it has to be converted whenever it’s switched to another batch level. To define the batch level, you follow these steps: 1. Go to SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Specify Batch Level and Activate Status Management. Choose the batch level as we just discussed (client, plant, or material level) and then save. 2. Choose Batch Status Management to activate batch management status in the cli- ent, check the Active box, and then save. 3. Choose Plants with Batch Status Management and check the Batch Status Manage- ment box in the resulting list for those plants where batch status management has to be active. Check the boxes and save. 4. Choose Initial Status of a New Batch, specify which initial new batches are to be assigned for each material type, and check the Initial Status box for each material type that will be batch managed.

sections.title: Assigning a Batch Number
sections.en: 10.2.2 Assigning a Batch Number In this step, you define the batch number range and assignments. To do so, follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Number Assign- ment  Activate Internal Batch Number Assignment. On the resulting screen, select the Active radio button to activate the internal batch number assignment. You can also define whether internal batch number assignment is allowed for goods receipts that have an account assignment via menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Number Assignment  Internal Batch Number Assignment for Assigned Goods Receipt. In the next step, define the number ranges for internal batch number assignment by following menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Num- ber Assignment  Maintain Internal Batch Number Assignment Range. © 461 10.2 Batch Level and Batch Status Management In the standard SAP S/4HANA system, you can set the number range object BATCH_CLT. Number range 01 from 0000000001 to 9999999999 has already been defined. As with internal batch number assignment, you can assign a number range for external number assignment. Follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Manage- ment  Batch Number Assignment  Maintain Number Range for External Batch Number Assignment. Maintain the external number range by clicking the Number Ranges button and then the Change Intervals button. Here, you can use the already-defined number range or create a new number range by clicking the Inset Interval button. Enter the number (No.), From number, and To number, and then choose Save. Note You can use SAP enhancement SAPLNOIZ for internal batch number assignment. This enhancement comprises two function module exits to define number ranges or tem- plates for batch numbers:  EXI_SAPLVO1Z_001 This exit is used to replace the number range object and/or interval proposed with its own number range object. It’s also used to stop the system from assigning an internal number based on the material or plant. This exit can be used to stop the dialog box from appearing.  EXIT_SAPLVO1Z_002 This exit helps assign its own number or even change the number assigned by the system.

sections.title: Creating New Batches in the Production Process
sections.en: 10.2.3 Creating New Batches in the Production Process This configuration step is how the system creates new batches for processes and process orders. To define the new batch creation for processes and process orders, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Creation of New Batches  Define Batch Creation for Production Order/Process Order or use Transaction CORW. You can make specific settings in batch management for the existing production con- trol profiles. You can also choose the production control profile for making the settings by selecting the required option (see Figure 10.1) for automatic batch creation:  Automatic batch creation at order creation  Automatic batch creation at order release  No automatic batch creation in production/process order Then, you select the batch classification option in the Batch Classification field. In addi- tion, check the Extended Classification box so that more batch-specific functionalities become available. 10 Batch Management 462 The production scheduling profile (PS Profile), which in this example is YB0001, is then assigned in the Work Scheduling view of a material master. Figure 10.1 Batch Management in Production Process

sections.title: Creating Batches for Goods Movement
sections.en: 10.2.4 Creating Batches for Goods Movement You can define the new batch creation settings for movement types. To do so, follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Creation of New Batches  Define Batch Creation for Goods Movements. For instance, when batch numbers aren’t determined by the system automatically, the user may want to assign a batch number for goods receipt on a production or a process order (movement type 101) manually. To achieve this, you choose the Automatic/man- ual without check option for Movement Type 101, as shown in Figure 10.2. Figure 10.2 Batch Management with Movement Type © 463

sections.title: Master Data in Batch Management
sections.en: 10.3 Master Data in Batch Management For each material type, you can also define whether a new batch can be created via the batch master creation transaction (Transaction MSC1N). To do so, follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Creation of New Batches  Define Initial Creation of Data for Batch Master Transactions. If a company only wants the sys- tem to assign the batch numbers automatically and doesn’t want users to have the option to assign them manually, especially for finished products, then you choose Automatic / no manual creation for material type FERT. This completes our discussion of the basic configuration settings needed for batch management. In the next section, we discuss how to set up master data in batch man- agement. 10.3 Master Data in Batch Management A first step when working with batch management is to identify the material that needs to be batch managed and then update the material master record by setting the batch management requirement. In the following sections, let’s go over the different master data management processes and steps specific to batch management.

sections.title: Activating Batch Management in the Material Master
sections.en: 10.3.1 Activating Batch Management in the Material Master In the SAP S/4HANA system, batch master records always depend on material master records. Therefore, the first step is to define batch management requirements in the material master. Go to Transaction MM02 (Change Material Master), enter all the required details, and check the Batch mgmt (batch management) box to denote that the material has to be handled in batches (see Figure 10.3). The following views of the material master contain the checkbox for batch management:  Sales: General/Plant  Plant Data/storage 1  Purchasing  Work scheduling All views are the same, and any changes and updates in one view appear in all views. Tips The indicator for the material can be changed from batch-managed to nonbatch- managed only if there has been no stock for the current period and the previous period. This is to allow for transaction posting of batch-managed materials in any previous period. In the SAP S/4HANA system, batches are posted for a material, so the batch master records are always dependent on material master records. If there is a mismatch between subjected material and the batch requirement, then you need to post all stocks from the previous fiscal year, the previous period, and the current period. To do this, uncheck the Batch mgmt box and repost the stock in batches into the system. 10 Batch Management 464 Similarly, you can cancel the batch requirement for any material that your company doesn’t want to batch manage anymore. If there’s a need to reorganize the batch master records, then you must uncheck the Batch mgmt box, post the required batch requirement, and then post the stock back into the SAP S/4HANA system using Transaction MIGO. Figure 10.3 Activation of Batch Management in Material Master

sections.title: Batch Classification
sections.en: 10.3.2 Batch Classification To differentiate batches of material depending on their individual properties, you use characteristics to define the properties. For example, the paint industry uses color, cov- erage, and viscosity as characteristics to classify different batches. The cold-roll coil industry uses finish, thickness, and quality to classify different batches. These characteristics are created in the SAP S/4HANA system and assigned to the mate- rials. The same classification concept as in materials classification is applied in defining batch classification. Say that a company that deals with various types of paints wants to capture the charac- teristics of paint: shine, color, and viscosity. A class has to be created with the character- istics for coverage, color, and viscosity wavelength, and this class has to be assigned to a material. Also, every time a new batch is created for the material, the characteristic values need to be entered. Let’s briefly define characteristics and assign them to a class specific to batch management. © 465 10.3 Master Data in Batch Management Defining Characteristics First, you create the various characteristics for the materials, such as thickness, viscos- ity, density, and so on. To create characteristics, follow menu path SAP Menu  Cross-Application Components  Classification System  Master Data  Characteristics or use Transaction CT04. In the resulting screen, enter the Characteristic name and choose Create. Then, enter a description, as shown in Figure 10.4. The Status field has In Preparation, Locked, and Released options. A Released status means that the characteristic can be used; a status of In Preparation or Locked means that it can’t be used. Figure 10.4 Characteristic In the Value assignment area, select either Single-value or Multiple Values. Clicking the Single-value radio button specifies that only one value can be assigned to this character- istic; you use the Multiple Values radio button when more than one value is possible. Next, select the Values tab and enter the possible values for this characteristic, as shown in Figure 10.5. Checking the Additional Values box allows you to enter character- istic values that aren’t defined. After defining the values, save the characteristic. In this example, we’ve set the Thickness characteristic, and the value of thickness can be within a range of 8.00 to 12.00 mm. 10 Batch Management 466 Figure 10.5 Characteristic Values Now that you’ve created a characteristic, the next step is to assign existing characteris- tics to the class, which we explain in the following section. Defining a Class and Assigning Characteristics A class represents a group of characteristics. Classes are defined based on the material classification, such as garments, oil and grease, or metals. To define a class, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Cross-Application Components  Classification System  Master Data  Classes or use Transaction CL02. Enter the Class type as “023” (Batch) and fill in the appropriate Class name, which in this example is “ZPP.” Figure 10.6 Assignment of Characteristics to Class © 467 10.3 Master Data in Batch Management Then, choose New. Enter a description, set the Status to Released, and enter the validity dates. On the Char. tab, assign the characteristics to the class, as shown in Figure 10.6. In this case, we’ve assigned three characteristics: MM02 (Thickness), MM10 (Product Class), and MM11 (Date of Manufacture).

sections.title: Assigning a Classification in the Material Master
sections.en: 10.3.3 Assigning a Classification in the Material Master After you’ve defined a class, the next step is to assign it to the material master via Transaction MM02. Choose the Classification view for Material 2264 and enter Class Type “023” to assign the previously created ZPP class. Doing so will automatically bring up all three characteristics assigned to the class, as shown in Figure 10.7. Here, you can maintain specific characteristic values pertaining to this material. Because this is a master data step, note that characteristic values assigned are for the material master only and not for batches of a material. Figure 10.7 Classification View of Material Master Now that you’ve created the requisite master data for batch management, the next sec- tion discusses the business processes associated with batch management. 10 Batch Management 468

sections.title: Business Processes of Batch Management
sections.en: 10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management In the following sections, we’ll cover the business processes of batch management in the SAP S/4HANA system. Some of these business processes include manually or auto- matically creating a batch, changing or deleting a batch, using the classification func- tion in a batch, and attaching documents to a batch.

sections.title: Creating a Batch Master Manually
sections.en: 10.4.1 Creating a Batch Master Manually A batch master record for a material can be created either manually by a user or auto- matically in the background by the system when goods are received for the first time. A manually created batch of a material can then be assigned during stock posting, such as during goods receipt of a material from the procurement or production processes. To create batch master records manually, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Create or use Transaction MSC1N (see Figure 10.8). Figure 10.8 Manually Creating Batch of Material Enter a material, plant, and storage location to create a batch master record. If an internal number range is defined, then the system will create the batch number automatically © 469 10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management after you press (Enter). The data in a material master applies in general to all the batches assigned to this master record. By contrast, a batch master record contains data that uniquely identifies the corresponding batch and characterizes the unit as one that can’t be reproduced. Various types of information are stored in the batch master record. Some can be updated manually, and some can be updated automatically. We’ll discuss some of the relevant information in different tabs in the following sections. Basic Data 1 Important fields and checkboxes in the Basic Data 1 tab shown in Figure 10.8 are as fol- lows:  Date of Manufacture You can enter the date when the batch was produced into this field. In some indus- tries, this field is also used to enter the date when the material was tested or retested. If a material is found to be still in tolerance after the shelf life date has expired, the material can be retested, and the date of the retest is entered into this field, in addi- tion to a new Shelf Life Exp. Date. We recommend that you check with clients to determine whether they need to use this field.  SLED/BBD The SLED/BBD (shelf life expiration date/best by date) field is where you enter the date when the shelf life of this batch will expire. The shelf life of a product can vary between plants. This date can be used in the sales process, as customers may have set a requirement for the acceptable number of days of shelf life remaining. Some companies use this field to indicate the date on when a batch needs to be retested. If the company has implemented the QM functionality, then the system uses this field to see if the material’s batch expiration is about to be reached so that the system can create a quality inspection lot for retesting the material.  Available From This field indicates when the batch will be available. For example, if a material needs to remain in the quality inspection process for a certain number of days after test- ing, then the quality department can enter a date here to inform other departments of when the batch is expected to be available.  Batch Status These radio buttons allow you to classify the batch as having restricted or unre- stricted use. If you click the Unrestricted radio button, then the batch has no restric- tion placed on its use. If you click the Restricted radio button, the batch will be treated like blocked stock in planning but can be selected by batch determination if the search includes restricted-use batches. You can change the Batch Status to restricted from unrestricted by changing the checkbox in the batch record. A material document will be posted that shows the movement of stock between the two statuses. 10 Batch Management 470  Next Inspection (not shown) This date field allows the quality department to enter the date of the next quality inspection of the batch, if applicable to this material. This field is helpful when a company must retest a material’s batch after every predefined period. This material retesting process is known as recurring inspection.  Supplier Batch (not shown) If the material is purchased, then you can add the batch number assigned by the sup- plier to the batch record. It’s important to any product recall procedure to note the supplier batch number here. The Supplier Batch field allows you to enter a 15-charac- ter string. Basic Data 2 In this tab, you can maintain the Texts and Freely Definable Date fields. There are six date fields, and maintaining them is optional and only necessary if a user wants to use these dates somewhere in the process. The other fields on the screen are six date fields: Date 1, Date 2, and so on. These fields don’t have any standard functionality. The six Date fields can be used for whatever purpose is defined by the client. For example, these fields could contain the dates on which the material was inspected by the quality department. Classification In this tab, you use the class that was assigned to a material during material master classification, and you maintain the characteristics values here by clicking the New button (not shown). After clicking the New button, you maintain the values of all the characteristics listed based on the material class, as shown in Figure 10.9. Figure 10.9 Classification Tab of Batch Master © 471 10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management Material Data In this tab, you use the information from the material master record. Choosing the All Changes icon will display the change history of the batch. This tab won’t appear when a batch is created for the first time but will appear in the change or display batch transac- tions (Transaction MSC2N and Transaction MSC3N, respectively). Tips and Tricks If there’s also a business need to link documents to a batch, then you must complete another configuration step. Follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Master  Activate Document Management for Batches or use Transaction ODOC. The configuration in Transaction ODOC consists of simply switching on the field to allow SAP Document Management service for batches. The options are Active and Inactive.

sections.title: Changing a Batch
sections.en: 10.4.2 Changing a Batch After you’ve created the batch, there may be occasion to amend the batch record, either to modify a characteristic or to add a new linked document.
