To change a batch record, follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master  Batch  Change or use Transaction MSC2N. You can make changes to the batch record, but these changes will be recorded and available to review. The Change Batch screen has an extra tab in change mode, and it accesses a screen to view all changes made to the batch record. The Changes screen (refer to Figure 10.8) shows the changes made to the batch record. The information recorded includes the user who created and changed the record, as well as the fields that have been changed, including their values. This information is important to some companies because a strict audit record is needed to show compliance with federal or local regulations. In the pharmaceutical industry, companies manufacturing items that are to be consumed are under strict regulations from the FDA. Companies should at all times ensure that their record keeping is compliant with FDA regulations.

sections.title: Deleting a Batch
sections.en: 10.4.3 Deleting a Batch There is no transaction used solely for deleting a batch, but a batch can be deleted through Transaction MSC2N. The screen shown back in Figure 10.8 shows the initial screen of the change batch transaction, which has a Batch Deletion Flag box that you can check if the batch is to be deleted. However, checking the deletion flag box doesn’t immediately delete the batch. Checking the box allows the batch to be processed by an archiving program that will determine whether the batch can be deleted. If the batch can’t be deleted, the deletion flag will remain until either the archiving program deter- mines that the batch can be deleted or until the deletion flag is removed. 10 Batch Management 472

sections.title: Automatic Creation of a Batch in Goods Movement
sections.en: 10.4.4 Automatic Creation of a Batch in Goods Movement It’s possible to define the automatic creation of batch numbers in the SAP S/4HANA sys- tem, whether for movement types or production and process orders. For instance, the system can be customized to automatically create unique batch numbers whenever goods are delivered against a process order. This means that every time goods with movement type 101 are received, the unique batch number for the material received is created by the system automatically. Here, we present an example of how the system automatically creates a batch master upon goods receipt with reference to a process order (movement type 101). To receive goods with reference to a process order and via movement type 101, you go to Transaction MIGO (see Figure 10.10). Select Goods Receipt and R08 Order from the two dropdowns located in the top-left corner of the screen, and then enter process order number “1002203.” In the item details, there’s one more tab: Batch. Notice that the system has automatically created batch 0000000127. Choose the Classification button (not shown but located further down the same screen) in the Batch tab in Figure 10.10 to enter the values of the characteristics, as shown in Figure 10.11. Figure 10.10 Goods Receipt of Batch-Managed Material On the screen shown in Figure 10.11, maintain characteristic values such as Thickness, Product Class, and Date of Manufacture. © 473 10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management Figure 10.11 Classification View of Batch during Goods Receipt

sections.title: Stock Overview
sections.en: 10.4.5 Stock Overview You can use the stock overview report to view how stock is kept under different batches. Use Transaction MMBE, enter the plant code and material number, and execute the report. In the report, you can see the stock overview of a material and the respective batch numbers, such as batch number 127 (which was just received in the previous step). Figure 10.12 shows batch 000000127 of material 2264 with a quantity of 10.0 PC. When a batch-managed material needs to be issued, if there are multiple batches of the same material in stock, the system automatically lets the user choose the batch num- ber from the material to be issued. If there’s a business need to assign a batch number based on some strategy, you should configure the batch determination strategy. We cover the entire batch determination topic in the next section of this chapter. Batch usage transactions can be used to track batches and for analysis. Transaction MB56 will give the details of a batch where-used list. SAP S/4HANA also provides the Batch Information Cockpit, a central tool for tracking batches, which is also discussed in this chapter. 10 Batch Management 474 Figure 10.12 Stock Overview Let’s discuss batch determination in the following section.

sections.title: Batch Determination
sections.en: 10.5 Batch Determination The batch determination process can be implemented in one or more logistics and sup- ply chain components of SAP S/4HANA, including in PP, QM, MM, SAP S/4HANA Sales, and embedded EWM. Batch determination uses strategy types, search strategies, and search procedures to identify a batch in the relevant area. In a real-world scenario, a warehouse supervisor receives one material in a number of batches, and this will be kept in storage location stock or in warehouse storage bins. When the material needs to be issued to production, a strategy needs to be in place for the system to choose different batches. Batch determination is the process of defining the strategy in the system by which the system determines and offers the right batch to pick. Batch determination uses the condition technique. Strategy records of the respective application—such as PP, QM, MM, SAP S/4HANA Sales, or WM/embedded extended warehouse management (embedded EWM)—determine batch determina- tion. Different strategy records in the system have to be created for different purposes, such as goods issue to production, goods issue to customer, goods issue to cost center, © 475 10.5 Batch Determination and so on. Batch determination can be used in production order, process order, goods movement, sales order/delivery, cost center, and transfer order functions. Configurations for each application—such as production, inventory, sales, and stock transfers—are stored in search procedures, which run the batch determination. After the batch determination procedure is set up, the system automatically accesses the corresponding entry of the application, which further refers to the relevant entries in configuration. Figure 10.13 shows how the system searches for the batch number by using the batch determination procedure. In any transaction, the system will first determine the appli- cation area—which will be PP and PP-PI, MM, WM and embedded EWM, or SAP S/4HANA Sales—and then, it will determine the search procedures, which are assigned based on different combinations (1 and 2). In the case of PP or PP-PI, the system will determine the order type and plant from the transaction and then find a search proce- dure (3 and 4). For MM, the system will find a search procedure based on movement type. For SAP S/4HANA Sales, the search procedure will be based on sales organization, distribution channel, division, and sales document type. The search procedure helps the system determine the search type 5, and the search type helps to identify the condition types and condition tables 6. The system will find the appropriate batch number based on the access sequence system. Figure 10.13 Batch Determination Flow in Logistics and Supply Chain Material Issue Mat Price UOM Mat-1 1000 50.00 pcs. Batch No B201 Determine the application area, i.e., PP/PP-PI, MM, WM, or SD PP/PP-PI MM WM SD Determine from document: ▪ Order type ▪ Plant Determine search procedure from order type and plant Determine search pro- cedure from WH number and WH movement type Get strategy type from search procedure Condition type: CO01 Access sequence: CO01 Condition type: ME01 Access sequence: ME01 Condition type: WM0001 Access sequence: WM0001 Condition type: SD02 Access sequence: SD02 Get strategy type from search procedure Get strategy type from search procedure Determine from document: ▪ Movement type Determine from document: ▪ Warehouse number ▪ WH movement type Determine from document: ▪ Sales organization ▪ Distribution channel ▪ Division and sales doc type Determine search procedure from movement type Get strategy type from search procedure Determine search procedure from sales org, DC, and division 10 Batch Management 476 In the following section, we start out with the necessary configuration steps required to set up automatic batch determination in the production process of PP-PI for process manufacturing (process order). However, the same logic and process steps we cover here can be used to set up automatic batch determination in other applications, such as PP (discrete manufacturing), MM, SAP S/4HANA Sales, and WM and embedded EWM. Then, we cover the necessary master data required for batch determination. Finally, we show how the configuration and the master data setup eventually are reflected in the batch determination business process. Next, let’s perform the following configuration steps in their logical and sequential order.

sections.title: Defining Condition Tables
sections.en: 10.5.1 Defining Condition Tables The batch determination condition table includes a number of fields that you can select and use to record and assign values. There are five options for condition table creation, depending on which business process your company wants to use batch determination for:  Production order (Transaction OPLB)  Process order (Transaction OPLB)  Inventory management (Transaction OMA1)  SAP S/4HANA Sales (Transaction V/C7)  WM (Transaction OMK4) To define the condition table of a process order, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistic—General  Batch Management  Batch Determination and Check  Condition Tables  Define Process Order Condition Tables or use Transaction OPLB. There are also options to define condition tables separately for each application area— that is, production order, process order, inventory management, SAP S/4HANA Sales, and WM and embedded EWM. For this example, as shown in Figure 10.14, we created a new table (table 550) by copying it from standard condition table 030. Copying a table allows you to use the batch deter- mination procedure specific to the Order Type/Plant/Component combination. Tips and Tricks We recommend that you always copy any new configuration object from an existing one and then make the desired changes to the newly configured object. Doing so not only saves you time and effort but also reduces the chances that you’ll miss important configuration settings that will then be unable to give the desired results in transac- tion processing. © 477 10.5 Batch Determination Figure 10.14 Condition Table in Batch Determination Figure 10.15 shows newly created condition table 550, in which the three selected fields are Order Type, Plant, and Material (which is basically the component), as shown on the left-hand side of the figure. You can also choose additional fields from the catalog shown on the right-hand side of the same figure so that they too will be available for selection during the batch determination procedure. For example, if you place your cursor on Valuation Type from the field catalog and choose the Select Field button, then during batch determination, the system will only suggest batches of materials that are available with specific valuation types. Figure 10.15 Creating a New Condition Table 10 Batch Management 478 Next, click the Generate button, and the system will create the condition table, as shown in Figure 10.16. Here, you can see the log generated on the successful creation of new condition table 550. Figure 10.16 Log after Condition Table Creation

sections.title: Defining the Access Sequence
sections.en: 10.5.2 Defining the Access Sequence For each batch strategy type, there is a batch determination access sequence. This allows the batch strategy type to access the condition tables in the correct sequence. To define an access sequence for PP-PI, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics— General  Batch Management  Batch Determination and Batch Check  Access Sequences  Define Process Order Access Sequences. Figure 10.17 shows the access sequence for a process order. First, you select the access sequence and choose Edit. Here, you define the sequence of tables that the system should follow. For example, the system will first search the Order Type/Plant/Material combination, then the Order Type/Plant/Component combination, and so on. Figure 10.17 Access Sequence in Batch Determination In the standard system for process orders, access sequences CO01 and CO02 are defined (just as in the standard SAP S/4HANA system for inventory management) and access sequences ME01 and ME02 are also defined. In the standard system for SAP S/4HANA Sales, access sequences SD01, SD02, and SD03 are defined. We recommended that you make a copy of the standard access sequences available and then make changes in the condition table sequences to meet any unique business requirements. © 479 10.5 Batch Determination Here, you select standard access sequence CO01 and make a copy of it by choosing Copy As. On the screen that appears, you define the new access sequence as “ZPP” while con- firming that you want to copy all dependent entries from access sequence CO01 to ZPP. However, if you want to use standard access sequence CO01, then you don’t have to perform this step. Because this example uses standard access sequence CO01, condition table 550 (which we created previously) has already been assigned (see Figure 10.18). Access sequence CO01 has three fields that the system will consider during batch determination: Order Type, Plant, and Material. Figure 10.18 Newly Created Access Sequence CO01

sections.title: Defining Strategy Types
sections.en: 10.5.3 Defining Strategy Types The batch strategy type is the specification that tells the system what type of criteria to use during the batch determination process. You can define a batch strategy in the five areas we’ve already mentioned. You define the strategy types for each application area by following menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Determination and Batch Check  Strategy Types  Define Process Order Strategy Types. Then, choose the New button or copy the standard strategy type provided by SAP. We recommend that you to copy the standard strategy type and make the required changes, then save the new strategy type. In this example, we created new strategy CO03 and also assigned previously configured access sequence CO01, as shown in Figure 10.19. We also assigned Class Type 023 (Batch) and the previously created associ- ated Class ZPP. This is the same class you created in the previous section for the batch determination processes, and you’re now assigning it in newly created strategy type CO03. In the Selection area, there’s an option to define the values of certain characteristics within a class. The values can be maintained by clicking the Maint. button. In addition, the following information is needed: 10 Batch Management 480  Selection type This field allows the user to determine how the batches are selected at the com- mencement of the batch selection. If the Selection type field is left blank, then the system will display the batches that meet the selection criteria.  Sort sequence This field allows the user to choose a sort that will define how the batches are sorted if they’re selected. Sort sequence can be maintained on this screen if desired. Later in this section, we’ll also cover how to create a sort sequence and assign it to a condi- tion record.  Batch split This area contains the No. of splits field that defines the number of batch splits that are allowed during batch determination. Figure 10.19 Strategy Type

sections.title: Defining the Batch Search Procedure
sections.en: 10.5.4 Defining the Batch Search Procedure In this configuration activity, you’ll define search procedures for batch determination in a process order. Follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Manage- ment  Batch Determination and Batch Check  Batch Search Procedure Definition  Define Process Order Search Procedure or use Transaction OPLG (see Figure 10.20). You choose New Entries to define the search procedure or define it by copying the SAP- provided search procedure. As always, we recommend that you to copy the standard © 481 10.5 Batch Determination procedure (so that you don’t miss any required settings), make the required changes, and save the entries. In Figure 10.20, we created new batch search procedure Z00001 by copying standard procedure CO0001. After copying, you can select newly created batch search procedure Z00001 and then choose the Control folder located on the left-hand side of the screen. Figure 10.20 Defining the Batch Search Procedure The previously created Condition Type CO03 is now assigned to the newly created batch search Procedure Z00001, as shown in Figure 10.21. Figure 10.21 Assigning the Condition Type to the Batch Search Procedure

sections.title: Allocating Batch Search Procedures and Activating Checks
sections.en: 10.5.5 Allocating Batch Search Procedures and Activating Checks In this step, you assign the batch search procedure to specific movement types, as shown in Figure 10.22. During the batch determination process, this search strategy will help to search the relevant batches for a particular movement type, such as 261 (goods issue against a process order). Next, follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Determination and Batch Check  Batch Search Procedure Allocation and Check Activa- tion  Assign Search Procedures to Production and Activate Check  Assign Search Proce- dure to Process Order and Activate Check or use Transaction CORC. 10 Batch Management 482 On the screen shown in Figure 10.22, previously configured batch search procedure Z00001 is assigned to Plant 1710 and Order Type YBM2. Be sure to check the Check Batch box so that the system will trigger automatic batch determination when a process order of this plant-order type combination is created in the SAP S/4HANA system. Figure 10.22 Assigning the Batch Search Procedure to Movement Type 261

sections.title: Batch Search Procedure for Process Orders
sections.en: 10.5.6 Batch Search Procedure for Process Orders In this final configuration step, you assign previously configured batch search proce- dure ZC0001 to the plant-order type combination. Follow menu path SAP IMG  Produc- tion Planning for Process Industries  Process Order  Master Data  Order  Define Order- Type-Dependent Parameters or use Transaction COR4. Figure 10.23 Assigning the Batch Search Procedure to the Plant-Order Type Combination © 483 10.5 Batch Determination Figure 10.22 shows that plant 1710 and order type YBM2 are assigned batch search pro- cedure Z00001. On the screen shown in Figure 10.23, you check the Check: Specified Batch Active box so that the system will use this assigned batch search procedure during process order creation. With these steps performed, the necessary configuration required for batch determina- tion is complete. Next are two master data management steps required to set up auto- matic batch determination.

sections.title: Creating Sort Rules
sections.en: 10.5.7 Creating Sort Rules A sort sequence enables the system to choose batches that meet the user-defined sort criteria of characteristics. For example, batches of a material expiring first are prime candidates to be sorted in ascending order so they can be consumed on the earliest possible date. This sort strategy is known as first expiration, first out. Other sort strate- gies include first in, first out (FIFO) and last in, first out (LIFO). Any other type of sort sequence is also possible, which we’ll soon show with an example. First, you access the screen shown in Figure 10.24 by following menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Determination  Selection and Sort  Sort Rule  Create or by using Transaction CU70. Next, you create new sort sequence ZPP, assign previously created characteristic MM02, and check the Ascending box. Doing so will lead to the system suggesting batches of material with values in ascending order during the batch determination process. For example, say there are four batches of a material with thicknesses (characteristic MM02) of 0.6 mm, 0.5 mm, 0.9 mm, and 0.35 mm. To adhere to the ascending order of thickness during the batch determination procedure, the system will first suggest batches of material with a thickness of 0.35 mm, then suggest batches with a thickness of 0.5 mm, and so on. Figure 10.24 Creating a Sort Sequence in Batch Determination 10 Batch Management 484

sections.title: Maintaining Condition Records
sections.en: 10.5.8 Maintaining Condition Records As a last step in automatic batch determination, you maintain the relevant condition records, which will then be picked by the system. To maintain a condition record, you fol- low menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Deter- mination  Batch Search Strategy  For Process Order  Create or use Transaction COB1. Next, select the strategy that you defined in the earlier configuration steps (in this case, CO03). In the popup that appears, choose the OrderType/Plant/Component radio but- ton because you want to use the batch search procedure for the component that will be used in the production process. On the next screen, select the Order Type (YBM2) and Plant (1710) combination. Then, enter Material “2265,” which is a batch-managed com- ponent that will be used or consumed in the production process, as shown in Figure 10.25. Then, select the first line item and choose the Details button to open the screen shown in Figure 10.26. Figure 10.25 Batch Search Strategy Figure 10.26 shows the details view of the batch determination condition record. Here, you can control how many batches the system splits components into, if changes and overdelivery to system-determined batches are allowed. With the dropdown list from the SelType batch det. field, you can also choose how the system interacts with the user during the batch determination process. The choices available in the dropdown list were shown previously in Figure 10.25. Next, on the screen shown in Figure 10.25, you first select component 2265 and then choose Sort so that the screen shown in Figure 10.27 appears. The complete details of the condition record are shown, including the fact that previously created sort rule ZPP was assigned and marked with characteristic MM02 (Thickness) in ascending order (per the Ascending checkbox). © 485 10.5 Batch Determination Figure 10.26 Details of Batch Search and Split Strategy Figure 10.27 Assigning a Sort Sequence in the Batch Search Strategy 10 Batch Management 486 The configuration and master data setup required for the batch determination proce- dure is now complete. Let’s now run the business process that entails creating a process order of order type YBM2 for plant 1710 for finished good 2264. In this process, the com- ponent is material 2265, which is undergoing the batch determination process. Tips and Tricks Before running the business process to test batch determination of a component, you should follow the steps in this checklist: 1. Make sure the component is managed in batches (i.e., the Batch Management box is checked in the component’s material master). 2. Ensure that the component is part of the material’s BOM, for which the process order is created. 3. Ensure that along with sufficient stock of the component’s batches, there are also multiple batches with different characteristic values (in this example, thicknesses) so that the completeness and correctness of the batch determination process can be tested. 4. Reconfirm the entry in the Batch Entry field in the Plant/Storage 1 view of the com- ponent’s material master. For example, choose whether you want to automatically or manually trigger batch determination upon release of a process order. 5. If the same material is batch managed in one plant but not in another, then check or uncheck the Batch Management (Plant) box based on your business needs.

sections.title: Business Process
sections.en: 10.5.9 Business Process First, you use Transaction COR1 to create a process order of order type YBM2 for plant 1710. Then, enter “2264” for the header material and maintain other basic process order details, such as order quantity and dates. Next, navigate to the Component Overview shown in Figure 10.28 and take note of the fact that component 2265 needs a total of 100 PC to produce main material 2264. Select component 2265 and then choose Exe- cute batch determntn (batch determination). Then, the system will not only determine the relevant batches that meet the sort sequence of the thickness characteristic but also split the total required quantity of 100 PC into multiple batches. It will also display a popup to inform you that while 100 PC of component 2265 are needed, only 96 PC of various batches are available. Here, the system has automatically determined and then assigned four batches of material 2265: 130, 141, 143, and 144. Let’s evaluate this in more detail. The system first evaluates the thickness sort criteria to see which batch has the lowest thickness value and assign that batch’s quantity to the process order. However, if the assigned batch’s quantity isn’t sufficient to cater to the complete requirement (in this case, 100 PC), the system proceeds to the next batch with the next-highest thickness value and assigns the batch’s quantity to the process order. The system continues in this way until multi- ple batches in the total required quantity are assigned to the process order. © 487 10.5 Batch Determination Figure 10.28 Batch Determination Let’s now also quickly check whether the batch determination for the process order is correctly configured. Figure 10.29 and Figure 10.30 show that batch 130 has the lowest characteristic (thickness) value of 8.10 mm but is only available in a quantity of 16 PC, which was assigned to the process order. Next, the system found that batch 141 had the next highest thickness value of 8.99 mm in a quantity of 10 PC. This batch was also assigned to the process order while the system continued searching for more batches to meet the total required quantity of 100 PC. Next, the system found that batch 143 with a thickness value of 9.136 mm had a quantity of 60 PC, which it therefore assigned to the process order. Finally, for the available quantity of 10 PC for the fourth batch (144) with the highest thickness value of 9.55 mm, the system accordingly assigned the same to the process order. These settings validate the ascending sort sequence of the thick- ness characteristic that was set up for batch determination. Figure 10.29 Batch Determination According to Sort Sequence, Part 1 10 Batch Management 488 Figure 10.30 Batch Determination According to Sort Sequence, Part 2 Choose Copy, shown in Figure 10.29, and all four batches will be copied and assigned in the process order, as shown in Figure 10.31. Figure 10.31 Multiple Batches Assigned Now, let’s discuss recurring inspection and the expiration dates of batches.

sections.title: Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates
sections.en: 10.6 Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates The ability to inspect batch-managed goods—whether procured raw materials or pro- duced semifinished or finished goods—before their expiration dates ensures that there will be sufficient time and flexibility for companies to respond to any unforeseen situations. © 489 10.6 Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates Recurring inspection (also called repeat inspection) at regular intervals allows business users to take advantage of standard functionalities to automatically create inspection lots when the expiration dates of batch-managed goods approach. The details related to the shelf life of materials are managed in individual batches. The batches nearing expiration can go through quality control checks of results recording and usage deci- sions to help managers decide whether they can still be used. This functionality offers time and cost savings by enabling the business to act proactively and thus optimizes the full logistics and supply chain operations of the company. A significant challenge for any organization is to keep track of batch-managed materials with shelf-life expiration dates. This is especially the case in process-based industries for slow-moving materials or materials with a short shelf life. Tracking this information allows an organization to ensure that those materials are consumed first in the case of raw materials and sold first in the case of finished goods. This minimizes the material and financial costs associated with the need to destroy and scrap goods or materials. At the time of goods production, the system is able to calculate and suggest a best before date. Similarly, upon procurement of goods, the system is able to calculate and suggest a shelf-life expiration date (SLED). Therefore, it makes sense to ensure that recurring inspection takes place before the goods are about to expire. Note Although this example shows the integration between PP and QM in the SAP S/4HANA system, a company can still use several batch-specific functions even if QM isn’t yet implemented. We recommend that you engage a PP consultant to ensure end-to-end integration among MM, PP, and QM functionalities for the batch-managed business processes cov- ered in this section. This section outlines the important master data needed to successfully set up recurring inspection. We’ll show how to manually create an inspection lot that triggers the recur- ring inspection program, and we’ll also touch on an automatic trigger option. For the QM part of the master data setup, we recommend that you engage a QM consultant to cover the following QM-specific master data setup.

sections.title: Quality Management Master Data Checklist
sections.en: 10.6.1 Quality Management Master Data Checklist For recurring inspection to work correctly, you need to set up the following QM master data:  Activation of recurring Inspection Type 09 in the material master  Inspection plan usage 9 (Material Check)  Status set to 4 (Released) 10 Batch Management 490 In the following sections, we’ll discuss how to activate the recurring inspection type, as well as the steps required to set up an inspection plan and set its status to Released. Activation of Recurring Inspection Type in the Material Master Although the material may have been created previously, its QM view may still need to be activated. If the QM view is already activated, then you need to activate the new inspection type for recurring inspection at the organizational level (plant). To activate the recurring inspection type, follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master  Material  Create (General)  Immediately or use Transaction MM02. Then, enter material “2264” and choose the Select View(s) button. Select the Quality Management view and then choose Organizational Levels. Enter plant “1710” and choose Continue. This leads to the Quality Management view of the material master Here, you should maintain an Inspection Interval of 10 days to denote that material inspection is due every 10 days. In the case of recurring inspection, the system refers to the information maintained in this field to calculate the next inspection date. Choose Insp. Setup, and in the ensuing popup, maintain Inspection Type 09 for recurring inspection. Also make sure to check the Active box to activate this inspection type. Back in the Quality Management view, this time, you should choose the Plant Data/ Stor. 1 view. The screen shown in Figure 10.32 will appear, and it will show the Shelf life data area, in which you should maintain the shelf-life data, including the minimum, maximum, and total shelf life of a material. While calculating or updating the SLED, the system looks for the date of manufacturing against the production/process order or the date of goods receipt against the purchase order. It then adds up the days or weeks in the period, such as the days or weeks from the total shelf life, to come up with the SLED or the best before date. In this example, the system adds 20 days from the Total shelf life field to the date of manufacture to suggest a SLED or best before date. For the SLED or best before date calculation, the system considers calendar days. Figure 10.32 Shelf-Life Data in Plant Data/Stor. 1 View of Material Maser © 491 10.6 Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates Tips and Tricks The maximum storage period of a material should have the longest duration. The total shelf life of a material must be greater than its minimum remaining shelf life. Further- more, it makes sense for an inspection interval (see Figure 10.33) to be less than the total shelf life of a material. Figure 10.33 Inspection Interval in QM View of Material Master Batch Master Let’s look at some of the details of the material’s batch, which is about to expire. When materials are batch managed, it’s always the batch—not the individual material—that expires. To change the batch, you follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master  Batch  Change or use Transaction MSC2N. Enter Material “2264,” Plant “1710,” “171A” for the Storage Location where the batch is stored, and material Batch number 129. Then, press (Enter) and the screen shown in Figure 10.34 will appear. The Next Inspection field is of paramount importance for recurring inspection. Together with the inspection interval that we previously covered (in the QM view of the material master), the system calculates the next inspection date whenever a business user records the usage decision of an inspection. Here the next inspection date is 07/ 24/2024, and inspection should occur every 20 days. Upon executing the recurring inspection program, which we’ll cover next, the system adds another 20 days to this date. In reality, the system comes up with a date that is one day earlier than the date maintained in the Next Inspection field. This is to ensure that the quality inspector can inspect the material, record the results, and decide on the material’s usage (make the usage decision) at least a day before the product is set to expire. 10 Batch Management 492 Figure 10.34 Shelf-Life Expiration Date and Next Inspection Date The Date of Manufacture field in this case shows 07/24/2024. For in-house production of finished or semifinished goods, this is the date when the user records goods receipt against a process or process order. If this batch contains material (e.g., raw material) received against a procurement process, then at the time of recording its goods receipt, the system prompts the warehouse person to enter the date of manufacturing mentioned with the accompanying supplier’s quality certificate or supporting documents, such as a packing list. On executing the recurring inspection program, the system looks for the data main- tained on shelf-life expiration to come up with a new SLED or best before date. Note If you have any unique business need, the SAP S/4HANA system also provides user exit QEVA0003 to let you define a different logic for calculating the next inspection date for a recurring inspection. You need to engage an ABAP expert for this activity.

sections.title: Business Processes
sections.en: 10.6.2 Business Processes To set the initial parameters for recurring inspection, you follow menu path Logistics  Quality Management  Quality Inspection  Worklist  Inspection Lot Creation  Deadline © 493 10.6 Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates Monitoring  Trigger Manually or use Transaction QA07. On the screen shown in Figure 10.35, you enter Material “2264” and Plant “1710” and define other parameters as needed. In this example, we define Lead Time in Days as “30” and that the material should trans- fer to quality inspection stock at the time of inspection lot creation (by clicking the To Insp. Stock at Insp. Date radio button) by the recurring inspection program. Therefore, all materials whose inspection dates fall in the next 30 days (from today’s date or the date on which this transaction is run) are considered during the deadline monitoring program run. After such materials are identified, the system not only creates inspec- tion lots for them but also moves the stock from unrestricted stock to quality inspec- tion through the transfer posting process. See Table 10.1 for information about the var- ious fields and functions, which also eventually need to be mapped with a company’s business processes. Figure 10.35 Deadline Monitoring (Recurring Inspection) Fields Functions/Descriptions Lead Time in Days The number of days until the next inspection date for the system to consider for recurring inspection purposes. If five days are maintained, and if today is December 15, 2025, then the system looks for all the material batches whose next inspection date is on or before Decem- ber 20, 2025. Table 10.1 Details of Fields and Functions in Recurring Inspection Manual Run 10 Batch Management 494 Next, choose Execute to see the detailed log shown in Figure 10.36. The system will inform you that the batch’s expiration date has been reached and the material’s batch needs a recurring inspection, for which the system has automatically created inspec- tion lot number 9000000000. Figure 10.36 Log Display of Deadline Monitoring Tips and Tricks To bring about organizational efficiency and optimization, a company can automate and schedule the recurring inspection process via scheduling jobs at regular intervals by using Transaction QA06. The first step is to define a variant, and the next step is to maintain the frequency with which the system runs the scheduled program for recur- ring inspections. It’s best to schedule these programs during slow working hours (i.e., on weekends or at night) to conserve the system’s resources. Lot Creation Only The system only creates an inspection lot, but the batch is still in unrestricted-use stock. To insp. Stock at Lot Creation The system creates an inspection lot and transfers the batch to quality inspection stock. The status of the batch changes to Restricted. Block Batch at Lot Creation An inspection lot is created, and the batch is blocked for any usage until the usage decision is made. To Insp. Stock at Insp. Date The system transfers the batch to inspection stock on the date when the user needs to perform quality inspection. Block Batch at Inspection Date The system transfers the batch to blocked stock on the date the user needs to perform inspection. Fields Functions/Descriptions Table 10.1 Details of Fields and Functions in Recurring Inspection Manual Run (Cont.) © 495

sections.title: Active Ingredient Management
sections.en: 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Transaction MB5M, which is the shelf life list, can also be used to gain comprehensive visibility into materials whose shelf lives have ended or are about to end (see Figure 10.37). Figure 10.37 Shelf Life Expiration List The next section is on the configuration, master data, and business process of active ingredient management in SAP S/4HANA. 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Active ingredient management in batch management enables organizations to take advantage of material valuation at the batch level, as well as the potency or active propor- tion in the overall quantity of material. This functionality is especially helpful when you’re using an active element or ingredient of a material that contains carrier materials, including impurities. If the total physical quantity of a material is a mixture, then the active or potent material within the mixture is known as the active ingredient quantity. This section begins by covering configuration basics and important master data that forms an integral part in running the end-to-end business processes that involve active ingredient management. Finally, for business processes in active ingredient manage- ment, we run an entire procurement process that consists of creating a purchase order for an active ingredient material, moving to goods receipt, recording results, making usage decisions, and posting stock. 10 Batch Management 496

sections.title: Configuration Basics
sections.en: 10.7.1 Configuration Basics In this section, we’ll list the most important settings and steps necessary to implement batch ingredient management in procurement. Activating Active Ingredient Management To activate ingredient management functionality at the client level, you use Transac- tion OMWS or follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch-Specific Material Units of Measure  Activate Batch-Specific Material Units of Measure. You choose the Active radio button to ensure that batch management is active and then save your settings. Creating a Unit of Measure for Proportion Units For batch-specific units of measure, you need to add a characteristic to the batch class that carries the conversion factor for deriving the alternative batch-specific unit of mea- sure. You’ll likely need to create this unit of measure before proceeding further, but if the unit of measure already exists in the system, then you can continue to the next step. To create a unit of measure for an active ingredient that should act as a proportion unit of measure, you use Transaction CUNI or follow menu path SAP IMG  SAP NetWeaver  General Settings  Check Units of Measurement. Choose the Units of Measurement (no dimensions) button and then choose Create. For this example, on the screen shown in Figure 10.38, we create a unit of measure called AIU and enter minimal details, such as its commercial and technical names, its description, and its decimal places, if any. Figure 10.38 Details of Unit of Measure for Ratio Unit © 497 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Proportion Unit The next step is to maintain an entry of a batch-specific unit of measure and create a relationship with an available standard unit of measure. To do so, you use Transaction BMA1 or follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch- Specific Material Units of Measure  Edit Batch-Specific Material Units of Measure. Cre- ate a new entry for the unit of measure called AIU, which the system should be able to use as a batch-specific unit of measure. Enter “KG” for the unit of measure (UoM) as the reference unit of measure (see Figure 10.39). Figure 10.39 Batch-Specific Unit of Measure Proportional Factors You can maintain a relationship between the characteristic’s unit of measure, batch- specific unit of measure, and base unit of measure. To do so, you use Transaction BMA3 or follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch-Specific Material Units of Measure  Calculate Proportional Factors  Define Calculation of Propor- tion Quantity from Base Quantity. Choose New Entries and enter the Commercial format, Batch-specific UoM, and Base Unit of Measure information, as shown in Figure 10.40. Figure 10.40 Calculation of Proportion/Production Quantity from Base Quantity 10 Batch Management 498 Tips and Tricks You can also have a separate Batch-specific UoM in Figure 10.40 that may differ from both the base unit of measure and the characteristic unit of measure. The steps to cre- ate a batch-specific unit of measure are the same as those we’ve already covered. Once you’ve created this unit of measure, you can assign the batch-specific unit of measure as shown in Figure 10.40.

sections.title: Master Data
sections.en: 10.7.2 Master Data Let’s now cover the master data setup that you need to perform to ensure that active ingredient management integrates with your business processes. Defining a Ratio Unit of Measure/Conversion Factor Characteristic We’ll now maintain the conversion factor characteristic for the batch class. Use Trans- action CT04 to create a characteristic and then assign the same characteristic to the batch class of the product that requires the batch-specific conversion. For this example, you create characteristic P03 with the description “Fat Content” and with the unit of measure set as “%.” The characteristic that you create should be in numeric format and can’t have multiple values or an interval. All the data you need to define for this char- acteristic is defined in Figure 10.41. Figure 10.41 Class Characteristic © 499 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Choose the Values tab in Figure 10.41 to bring up the screen shown in Figure 10.42, where you enter a characteristic value range of “0.00–100.00” and check the box to set the default (D) indicator. In the Addnl data (additional data) tab, choose Display Values. Finally, in the Restrictions tab, enter classes “022” and “023” so that this class character- istic (P03) is available for use only in these two classes. Figure 10.42 Values Tab in Characteristic Assigning a Characteristic to the Class Use Transaction CL02 and assign the characteristic (conversion factor characteristic P03) as shown in Figure 10.43. Figure 10.43 Class for Active Ingredient Management with Organizational Area 10 Batch Management 500 In addition to assigning the characteristic to the class, you need to classify the batch class in the header as a special batch class by entering a value of “S” (substance steel) in the class header of the Organizational area field. Next, choose the Char. (characteristic) tab. Here, you also need to add the value “S” in the Org. Areas column for characteristic P03 in the characteristics screen of the class (see Figure 10.44). This step ensures that the system uses this characteristic in carrying out the batch-specific units of measure functionality. Additionally, be sure to also check the Prop./Prod. Unit Rel (proportional/production unit relevant) box to denote that characteristic P03 will be used for the purpose of calculating the active ingredient or potency of a material. Figure 10.44 Characteristics Tab in Class Inspection Setup: Class Characteristic Next, create a master inspection characteristic and link it to class characteristic P03. Use Transaction QS21 or follow menu path Logistics  Quality Management  Quality Planning  Basic Data  Inspection Characteristic  Create. On the initial screen that appears, enter master inspection characteristic “AIUMIC13” and plant “1710,” and in the Class Charc field, enter “P03,” which is the class characteris- tic you created in the previous step. Figure 10.45 shows that the system automatically copies all the information from Class Charc P03 to Master IC AIUMIC13, including con- trol indicators and upper and lower values. Next, choose Control Indicators and then Quant. Data to verify the completeness and correctness of the information. Figure 10.46 shows the quantitative data indicating that the system automatically picks up values from class characteristic P03, not just for the Decimal Places (2 places) and its unit of measure (Msmt Unit %) but also for the upper and lower limits that we main- tained previously (shown in Figure 10.41). © 501 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Figure 10.45 Class Characteristic in Master Inspection Characteristic Figure 10.46 Define Master Inspection Characteristics Maintaining Active Ingredient Data for a Material Next, assign batch class P03 to the material master, which requires the functionality of batch-specific units of measure. For this example, assign class 023 and then assign batch class P03 to material 2242. 10 Batch Management 502 To maintain data for a material, you update the classification view of material master 2242 (fatty acid concentrate that is managed in batches) with Transaction MM02 or menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Environment  Material Master  Material  Change  Immediately. Go to the classification view and assign batch class P03 to material 2242, as shown in Figure 10.47. You don’t need to assign any char- acteristic value range in the Value field: the system automatically picks up this infor- mation because earlier, you set the Default value settings (shown in Figure 10.41). Figure 10.47 Active Ingredient Class Defined in Material Master’s Classification View Maintaining Unit of Measure Data in the Material Master You maintain the Additional Data view to maintain the conversion factor between the active ingredient unit of measure (AIU) and the base unit of measure, but also with pro- portion or product units of measure. Use Transaction MM02, enter material 2242, choose the Basic data 1 view, and navigate to Additional Data. Figure 10.48 shows the Units of measure tab of material 2242. Here, you maintain a con- version factor between base unit of measure (kg) and AIU. For this example, we main- tain that for every 100 kg of material, we have 85 AIU; that is, 100 kg = 85 AIU (or 85 AIU for every 100 kg). Next, choose the Proportion/prod.unit tab on the far right of the screen, and the screen in Figure 10.49 will appear. Here, first select A for Units meas. use from the dropdown list that denotes the proportion units. Then, enter previously created class characteristic © 503 10.7 Active Ingredient Management P03 and assign a plan value of 85%, which is within the allowed range for the class char- acteristic. This will indicate to the system that every 100 kg of fatty acid concentrate material should contain 85 units of the active ingredient. Then, for the proportion/prod- uct unit, enter the AIU value, which is the new proportion unit in which the system mea- sures or calculates the active ingredient. Also, check the V-R box, which is for valuation at the unit of measure, so that the system knows that all valuations will be in this unit of measure (AIU). These are still all planned values; we’ll show you later how the inspection results in QM enable the system to make adjustment entries to account for the actual content of the active ingredient in the material. Lastly, choose Main Data and then the purchasing view. Figure 10.48 Units of Measure Conversion Figure 10.49 Proportion/Product Unit of Measure Tab in Additional Data of Material Master 10 Batch Management 504 Tips and Tricks If the Proportion/prod.unit tab shown in Figure 10.49 doesn’t appear in your SAP S/4HANA system, then you need to first maintain the step shown in the Purchasing view in the material master (see the next subsection) and then come back to Figure 10.49, wherein the Proportion/prod.unit tab will appear. This is because the Proportion/ prod.unit tab will only appear when the Plant organizational level is selected for a material master at the time of material master creation or change. Purchasing View in Material Master In the purchasing view (see Figure 10.50), check the boxes for both of the Batch Management indicators. Then, enter “AIU” for the Order Unit so that you can place the purchase order with the supplier in the active ingredient unit of measure. Also, enter “1” for Var. OUn to enable the system to allow the creation of purchase orders in the alternative unit of measure. Figure 10.50 Purchasing View of Material Master Valuation Category of Material Figure 10.51 shows the Accounting 1 tab. Here, you enter “X” in the Valuation Category field to ensure that the system valuates the material at the batch level. © 505 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Figure 10.51 Valuation Category Next, set the Price control indicator to “V” to denote a moving average price. With a moving average price, the system updates the material’s price with every goods receipt and with invoice verification. Tips and Tricks If you need to activate valuation category X as shown in Figure 10.51, follow menu path SAP IMG  Materials Management  Valuation & Account Assignment  Split Valuation  Configure Split Valuation and follow these steps: 1. Choose Execute and then choose Cancel. 2. Choose Global Categories. You’ll see valuation category X Automat (Batch); choose X and then choose the Change button. On this screen, check the Determine Val. type automat. box. By default, this box is checked—but if it isn’t, check it and save. 3. Click Back twice. 4. Choose Local Definitions. You’ll see your plant, so choose the plant number (in this example, this is plant 1710) and click the Cats  OU button. 5. Choose X and then click the Activate button. The system will activate valuation cat- egory X for the requisite plant, and you’ll see Active in the Status column beside Valuation Category X. 6. Save your settings. 10 Batch Management 506 QM Inspection Setup: Inspection Type In the Quality Management tab of the material master, you need to maintain the set- tings shown in Figure 10.52 to trigger automatic inspection lot creation based on a goods receipt for a purchase order of this raw material. The activated InspType (inspec- tion type) should be of type 01. Check the Insp. with Mat. Spec. box to denote that the system will look for a material specification at the time of inspection lot creation for goods receipt for the purchase order. Alternatively, you can also use an inspection plan (by checking the Insp. with Task List box) for this material for sample calculation, results recording, and usage decisions. Save your entries. Figure 10.52 Inspection Setup QM Inspection Setup: Material Specification To create a material specification, use Transaction QS61 or follow menu path Logistics  Quality Management  Quality Planning  Inspection Planning  Material Specification  Edit. On the initial screen, enter material 2242 and press (Enter) to view the screen shown in Figure 10.53. The system automatically copies all information from class characteristic P03 and mas- ter inspection characteristic AIUMIC13, including upper and lower limits. Because all information in the material specification refers to either the class characteristic or the master inspection characteristic (look for the lock Ref icon in Figure 10.53), you can make hardly any changes to the material specification. © 507 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Figure 10.53 Material Specification of Material 2242 with Master Inspection Characteristic AIUMIC13 Standard Price for Batch Apart from the material’s standard or moving average price, you can also maintain a standard price for the batch-specific unit of measure that the system keeps updating with each transaction, such as goods receipt or invoice verification. To maintain a stan- dard price for the batch-specific unit of measure, use Transaction MWB1 or follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch-Specific Units of Measure  Standard Price  Create. Enter the standard price and price unit. In the initial screen that appears, enter Material “2242,” Plant “1710,” and proportion/prod- uct (Prop./Prod.) “AIU.” Then, press (Enter), and the screen in Figure 10.54 will appear. Figure 10.54 Proportion Valuation 10 Batch Management 508 Here, the system refers to the data from the Accounting 1 view of the material master. In the Current Proportion Valuation area, you can maintain the standard price and price unit.

sections.title: Business Processes
sections.en: 10.7.3 Business Processes Let’s now proceed to the actual business processes of active ingredient management. We first create a purchase order of the same material (2242) and record its goods receipt, and then, we perform results recording, which consists of recording the details of the actual ingredient proportion, making a usage decision, and posting the mate- rial’s stock. We also show how the SAP S/4HANA system keeps updating the informa- tion in each step of the business process. Purchase Order Before you create a purchase order for material 2242, quickly refer to Figure 10.50, which shows the purchasing view of the material where you set the (purchase) Order Unit to be the AIU. If you create the purchase orders in active ingredient quantities, the system updates the purchase order quantity and physical quantity in the purchase order history. In this way, you can ensure greater control of and visibility into the degree of order quantity fulfillment based on active ingredient quantities. To create a purchase order for material 2242 that contains an active ingredient mea- sured in the AIU proportion, use Transaction ME21N or follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Purchasing  Purchase Order  Create  Supplier/Supplying Plant Known. Enter Supplier “EWM17-SU01,” Material “2242,” a PO Quantity of “100 AIU,” a Net Price of “100 USD,” and a Plant of “Plant1 US.” (You can also enter plant code 1710, in which case, the system will use the plant’s description instead of its code.) In the Quantities/Weights tab, the system automatically converts not just the purchase order quantity into a stock-keeping unit (which is kg, and in which you maintain the mate- rial’s inventory) but also shows the conversion factor between order units and stock keeping units. This is the same information that you maintained previously in Figure 10.50. Choose the Delivery tab and the screen in Figure 10.55 appears. It accounts for the fact that the quantity of an active ingredient that the supplier actually delivers may vary slightly from the quantity ordered. Therefore, you need to maintain the tolerances for under- and overdelivery. For this example, check the Unlimited box to denote unlim- ited overdelivery; for underdelivery, enter 20%. Then, you save the purchase order, and in this example, the system creates a purchase order with the number 4500001882. © 509 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Figure 10.55 Delivery Tolerance in Purchase Order Tips and Tricks You can maintain the under- and overdelivery tolerance for a material by assigning the purchasing value key in the purchasing view of the material master (see Figure 10.50). Goods Receipts for Purchase Orders You can post the goods receipt with reference to the purchase order in the base unit of measure. To do so, use Transaction MIGO or follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Inventory Management  Goods Movement  Goods Receipt. Choose Goods Receipt (A01) and then Purchase Order (R01) and then enter the purchase order number you created in the previous step. The system will then bring up all the requisite information directly from the purchase order (see Figure 10.56). The base unit of measure for material 2242 is kg. You can determine the physical quan- tity of the material only at the time of goods receipt. In this example, the supplier deliv- ers 117.647 kg. If the active ingredient proportion of the batch is not known at the time of goods receipt, then you record the goods receipt per the planned proportion; that is, you assume that the supplier’s delivered material of 117.647 kg does indeed contain 100 AIU. In active ingredient management, the actual proportion is taken from the usage decision, and the system prompts you to make a subsequent debit or credit entry (adjustment entry) for the purchase order, which we’ll show later. Now, save your entry, and the system will create a material document to record the goods receipt for this pur- chase order. 10 Batch Management 510 Figure 10.56 Goods Receipt of a Purchase Order Displaying Purchase Order History To display the purchase order history, use Transaction ME23N or follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Purchasing  Purchase Order  Display. On the Item Detail screen, choose the Purchase Order History tab and the system will display the goods receipt (see Figure 10.57). The system first assumes that the ordered quantity was delivered (100 AIU). Figure 10.57 shows that the system updates the history of goods receipt for the purchase order. Figure 10.57 Purchase Order History © 511 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Displaying the Active Ingredient Valuation Price Because the active ingredient of material 2242 has its own valuation price, you can check whether the system updated its valuation record based on the latest or last goods receipts. Use Transaction MWB3 or follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch-Specific Units of Measurement  Standard Price  Display. The screen shown in Figure 10.58 shows that the system updates the material valua- tion, especially the moving average price (V), because we have set a “V” indicator for Price Control. Figure 10.58 Displaying Active Ingredient Valuation Price Displaying the Material’s Price To check whether the system updates the price per the ingredient management based on the latest goods receipt, you can display the average price, standard price, and total stock of the active ingredient material in the Accounting 1 tab, in which the valuation type represents the batch level. Use Transaction MM03 to display the material’s price. The system displays the price per base unit of measure, and this price assumes that the base unit of measure contains the same active ingredient proportion as specified in the planned value. The Val. (valua- tion) Type of the material is at the batch level and is for batch number 122. 10 Batch Management 512 Quality Inspection: Results Recording The goods receipt for material 2242 now appears as an inspection lot. By this time, it makes sense for you to have measured the potency, or the actual quantity of the active ingredient in the delivered material. You can enter the results of the inspection lot with Transaction QE01 and enter the inspection lot number (which in this example is “10000000725”) and batch number “122.” On the screen in Figure 10.59, enter a value of “65” (for 65%) as the result for this inspec- tion lot and save it. This results recording denotes that although you expected the sup- plier to deliver material consisting of 85% active ingredients, the actual percentage of active ingredients found (after necessary tests) was 65%. Save your entries. Figure 10.59 Results Recording Quality Inspection: Usage Decision To record the usage decision, use Transaction QA11 and enter inspection lot number 10000000725. In the Characteristics tab, enter an acceptance without further action (UD code A and code group 01). Choose the Inspection Lot Stock, and the screen in Figure 10.60 will appear. Here, post the goods receipt quantity (117.647 KG) to unre- stricted stock and save it. Once you’re done saving, the system brings up an information message that stipulates that you need to correct the purchase order history for inspection lot 10000000725 by using Transaction MWBQ. The system brings this message up each time it finds a differ- ence between the theoretical (planned) and actual values of an active ingredient (see Figure 10.61). © 513 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Figure 10.60 Inspection Stock Posting Figure 10.61 Information Message to Correct Purchase Order History Correcting the Purchase Order History To correct the purchase order history for the inspection lot, use Transaction MWBQ or follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch-Specific Units of Measure  Subsequent Adjustment f. Goods Receipt  Enter with Ref. to Insp. Lot. Enter inspection lot number 10000000725, the document date, and the posting date, and then choose Execute. Then, the system will bring up a message confirming successful posting to the account, and it will correct the purchase order history based on the inspection lot results and the usage decision (see Figure 10.62). 10 Batch Management 514 Figure 10.62 Material Document Creation on Purchase Order History Update Displaying the Purchase Order History Let’s now look at the updated purchase order history and check out the physical and financial adjustment entries. To display the updated purchase order history, use Transaction ME23N. On the Item Detail subscreen, choose the Purchase Order History tab (see Figure 10.63). The system displays not just the original goods receipt with movement type 101 but also subse- quent adjustments of the purchase order using movement type 142. It also updates the purchase order quantity to 76.471 AIU by subtracting (deducting) a quantity of 23.529 AIU from the ordered and delivered quantity of 100 AIU. Similarly, the financial value is reduced by 2,352.90 USD to come up with the revised value of 7,647.10 USD. Figure 10.63 Displaying Purchase Order History Let’s now delve into how and why the system made these stock and financial adjust- ments. As explained earlier, 100 kg of material is expected to have a planned propor- tion of 85 AIU (85%). In this case, and after results recording, the actual percentage of © 515 10.7 Active Ingredient Management the active ingredient was found and recorded to be 65%. Hence, the system undertakes the following calculation: ((100 AIU × 0.65) ÷ 0.85) = 76.471 AIU This is the quantity of the active ingredient that the supplier has delivered and should accordingly be paid for. Because it costs 100 USD for each unit (AIU) of material, we get 100 USD/KG × 76.471 KG = 7,647.10 USD. Also, the delivered percentage of the active ingredient for the material is less than expected (65% actual, as compared to 85% expected), so the system uses movement type 142 to make necessary quantity and financial adjustments. Had this been the other way around, with the supplier deliver- ing a greater proportion of the active ingredient than ordered (any active ingredient percentage greater than 85.00%), the system would have made use of movement type 141 to make positive adjustment entries. Batch Master Record During the inspection process, the system valuates the batch based on batch characteris- tics. To display the batch classification, you use Transaction MSC3N or follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch  Display and then choose the Classification tab (see Figure 10.64). The batch characteristic data shows the transfer of inspection results as 65%. The batch has a value that differs from the planned value for the active ingredient characteristic that we maintained as 85% in the material master. Figure 10.64 Display Batch Master with Active Ingredient Value 10 Batch Management 516 Tips and Tricks You can also implement a batch search strategy (or batch determination) in your pro- duction or sales processes so that the system automatically selects the batches with the requisite or predefined active ingredient content. We already discussed batch determination in Section 10.5. Stock Overview Figure 10.65 1 shows the stock overview of material 2242 of batch number 122, and you can access it by using Transaction MMBE. Here, you can display the current stock of material in the base unit of measure (kg) as well as in the AIU of measure 2. Figure 10.65 Stock Overview in Base Unit of Measure and Active Ingredient Unit of Measure © 517 10.7 Active Ingredient Management Logistics Invoice Verification and Release You can post and release the supplier invoice for the delivered quantity of material and for the active ingredient. To post the supplier invoice, use Transaction MIRO or follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Logistics Invoice Verification  Docu- ment Entry  Enter Invoice. Choose the Basic Data tab and enter data for the purchase order document number, invoice date, reference, amount, and tax data. The system will accept the invoice even though the amount specified is higher than planned because there was an overdelivery for the purchase order; the overdelivery was made possible due to the tolerance entered for the purchase order item. Then, save your entry and the system will issue an invoice number as well a warning message to inform you that the invoice is blocked for payment. Figure 10.66 shows the purchase order history, which also now includes invoice post- ing. Figure 10.66 Invoice Posting To release the invoice so that you can pay the supplier for the delivered material, use Transaction MRBR or follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Logistics Invoice Verification  Document Entry  Enter Invoice. Enter the invoice document num- ber and run the report. The system will show the details report, from which you can select the relevant line item and choose Release to release the invoice. Due to subsequent adjustment for goods receipt in the purchase order value for mate- rial 2242, the system will correspondingly update the moving average price of material 2242 of batch number 122. This is shown in Figure 10.67, wherein the Val. type field is basically the batch number. 10 Batch Management 518 Figure 10.67 Price Update after Invoice Verification The next section is on batch derivation, which entails an automatic transfer of the clas- sification data of one batch to another batch of the same or different materials, thereby saving you time and effort, as well as minimizing data entry errors.

sections.title: Batch Derivation
sections.en: 10.8 Batch Derivation With batch derivation, a user can efficiently and automatically transfer a batch’s charac- teristics values from the sender material’s batches to the receiver material’s batches, thereby saving time, data entry effort, and cost. Batch derivation enables a user to main- tain one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-one relationships between the sender’s and the receiver’s materials for complete visibility of information and data. Whenever a batch-managed material is processed in the production process, its charac- teristics change and the batch-managed material attains different physical and chemi- cal attributes. These changed attributes are maintained as characteristic values of the © 519 10.8 Batch Derivation batch-managed materials. With batch derivation, you can control how the system man- ages the characteristic values flowing from a sender material to a receiver material. In this section, we’ll start by covering the basic configuration settings needed to activate batch derivation. Then, we’ll move on to the master data you need to set up, including settings in the material master, classification, and batch derivation–specific settings. Finally, we’ll cover the end-to-end business process, in which we show how the settings made for master data are reflected in the batch derivation process. For this example, the sender material is 2265 (a raw material that is consumed during the production process to produce finished good 2264) and the receiver material is 2264 (a finished good). Let’s start with the configuration basics of batch derivation.

sections.title: Configuration Basics
sections.en: 10.8.1 Configuration Basics You activate batch derivation in the configuration settings by following menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Derivation of Batch Data  Activate Batch Derivation or by using Transaction DVSP. On the screen that appears, select the Derivation Active radio button and save the settings. In the following sections, we’ll cover the end-to-end business process of setting up batch derivation master data, which entails setting up the details of the sender and receiver materials of a batch’s data. We’ll then use an example to show how the sender material’s batch data automatically flows into a receiving material’s batch data through the batch derivation process.

sections.title: Classification in Material Master
sections.en: 10.8.2 Classification in Material Master In the material master of the sender material and receiver material, you can maintain the classification view, which incorporates class type 023, which is specific to batch- managed materials. To maintain the classification view or make changes to the already maintained classification view in the material master, follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master  Change  Immediately or use Transaction MM02 (see Figure 10.68). In Figure 10.68, you can see ZPP of Class Type 023 (Batch) and three characteristics whose descriptions are given as Thickness, Product Class, and Date of Manufacture. On this screen, you simply assign an already-created class, which in this example is ZPP. After you assign an already-created class, the system automatically assigns the charac- teristics associated with that class. There’s no need to maintain any specific value for the characteristics (in the Value column) because you’ll do that during the transac- tional data entry. 10 Batch Management 520 Figure 10.68 Classification View in Material Master Note The classification settings you’ve made for the sender material also apply to the recei- ver material, especially the common characteristics whose values the system should transfer during batch derivation. Although the preceding example shows classification details of sender material 2265, we’ve made similar classification settings for receiver material 2264. In the following sections, we’ll cover the steps involved in setting up batch derivation, including maintaining details of sender and receiver materials’ batch details and how a sender material’s batch data flows into a receiver material’s batch. Classification: Class with Characteristics It’s important to understand how a class and characteristics are linked together. To view the structure of class ZPP and the characteristics associated with it, follow menu path Cross-Application Components  Classification  Master Data  Classes or use Trans- action CL02. On the initial screen, enter Class “ZPP” and Class Type “023” (Batch) and then choose Change. © 521 10.8 Batch Derivation Figure 10.69 shows three characteristics—MM02, MM10, and MM11—whose descrip- tions appeared or were visible in Figure 10.68. We refer to several instances when the system interchangeably displays the characteristics or their descriptions. The data type of characteristic MM10 is CHAR and consists of 1 character, the data type of MM11 is DATE and has 10 characters, and the data type of MM02 is NUM and has 4 characters. MM02 also has a unit of measure of mm (millimeters) and two decimal places. For this example of batch derivation, we’ll use all three characteristics to transfer their values from a sender material’s batch to a receiver material’s batch. Figure 10.69 Characteristics Assignment in Class Batch Derivation: Sender Material For batch derivation settings for the sender material, including the characteristics, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Derivation  Sender Condition Records  Create or use Transaction DVS1. On the initial screen, in which the standard Strategy Type option is available, the available key com- binations only offer the options to choose the Sender Material Number radio button or the Receiving material + Sending material radio button. This key combination enables you to maintain not just the sender material’s information but also the receiver’s material. Place the cursor on the Strategy Type field, press (F4) to see the dropdown list, select condition type BDS1, and press (Enter). Then, the system will open a popup screen to provide radio buttons to let you select a combination of both receiving and sending material information on the same screen, plus only sender material number information. For this example, select the first radio button (Receiving Material + Send- ing Material), and choose Continue. On the screen shown in Figure 10.70, enter “2264” in the Receiver Material field and validity dates in the Valid From and Valid To fields. If validity dates aren’t entered, then the system automatically sets the validity as from the date of creation of the condition 10 Batch Management 522 record until December 31, 9999. If there’s a business need to use a different validity than what the system proposes, you should use the Validity option (not shown). Then, enter Sender Material “2265” in the lower half of the screen. In this section of the screen, you can enter multiple sender materials for one receiver material. Select Sender Material 2265 and then choose Details from the top menu bar. Figure 10.70 Defining Sender-Receiver Relationship Figure 10.71 shows details about both Sender Material 2265 and Receiver Material 2264. Here, enter characteristics “MM02,” “MM10,” and “MM11” in the lower half of the screen. That tells the system to send the characteristic values of these three character- istics from material 2265 (the sender material) to material 2264 (the receiver material). These are the same characteristics that you previously maintained in the Classification view of material masters 2264 and 2265 (refer to Figure 10.68). Figure 10.71 Attributes of Sender Material © 523 10.8 Batch Derivation The next step is to define the event or instance when the system performs batch deri- vation for the receiver material. Automatic batch derivation is possible at the time when a production or process order is released, at goods receipt of a production or pro- cess order, at goods receipt of a PO, during a usage decision from a quality inspection process, or at transfer posting between two batches of different materials with com- mon characteristics. You can also perform manual batch derivation if there is any such business need. Choose Condition Usage or press (F7). Select Event 200 (see Figure 10.72) by clicking it once to highlight it; this will enable the system to perform batch derivation on the release of a production or a process order. Then, choose Continue and save the settings. Tips and Tricks Referring to Figure 10.72, if you are not able to see the event (such as a usage decision, purchase order, or process order) for which you’d like the system to perform batch der- ivation, then you can navigate to the configuration menu path to add and activate the required events. The menu path is SAP IMG  Logistics - General  Batch Management  Derivation of Batch Data  Define Derivation Events. Figure 10.72 Choosing Event for Batch Derivation So far, we’ve covered batch derivation for the sender material while also associating it with the receiver material. However, the setting for the receiver material is signifi- cantly more detailed than the setting for the sender material, especially in terms of how the system responds if batch derivation can’t be performed on the receiver mate- rial or if one of the characteristic values derived from batch derivation is missing. 10 Batch Management 524 Batch Derivation: Receiver Material For batch derivation settings for receiver material, including the characteristics, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Derivation  Recipient Condition Records  Create or use Transaction DVR1. On the initial screen, the Strategy Type option is available for the receiver material key combination. This key combination enables a user to enter not just the receiver material’s informa- tion but also that of the receiving material type. Place the cursor on the Strategy Type field and press (F4); the system then brings up Condition Type BDR1 and BDR2 to select from. Select Condition Type BDR1, press (Enter), and choose the Material Number radio button in the popup that appears. Next, enter Receiver Material “2264.” If validity dates aren’t maintained, then the sys- tem automatically sets the validity from the date of creation of the condition record until December 31, 9999 (infinity). Then, choose Receiver Material—2264 and choose Details so that the screen shown in Figure 10.73 appears. Here, enter the same three characteristics or attributes of class ZPP: “MM02,” “MM10,” and “MM11.” Next, select all of these characteristics one by one and choose the Rules button located at the bottom of the screen.
