Figure 10.73 Attributes for Characteristics of Receiver Material © 525 10.8 Batch Derivation On the screen shown in Figure 10.74, define the rules for every individual characteristic that the system needs to follow if it can’t perform batch derivation. In this example, we set the rules for the first characteristic, MM02, in which we set the control level to W Warning in case the system finds the Sender field empty. An empty Sender field means that there is no characteristic value in the sender material’s classification, so nothing transfers to the receiver material during batch derivation. These warning mes- sages can also be turned into error messages if you want to ensure handling the batch derivation–related issues as soon as an error message appears. The second control set- ting for the same characteristic (MM02) stipulates how the system reacts if it’s able to find an already maintained value in the characteristic. If the system is to overwrite the value of this characteristic, it will do so with a warning message or bring up an error message that doesn’t allow overwriting. Figure 10.74 Rules for Characteristics of Receiver Material Repeat the same rules setting for the remaining two characteristics, MM10 and MM11.

sections.title: Business Processes
sections.en: 10.8.3 Business Processes The business process of batch derivation begins with creating a process order for the receiver material (2264, the finished good) in which the system automatically assigns the sender material (2265) as one of its components. To create a process order, you use Transaction COR1. Enter receiver Material “2264,” Plant “1710,” and Order Type “YBM2” and then press (Enter). Maintain the order header details, such as the process order quantity and dates, and then navigate to the Material List shown in Figure 10.75. In the 10 Batch Management 526 Material List view of the process order for receiver Material 2264, the system assigns a specific Batch number (0000000130) of the sender material or Component 2265. Then, double-click Batch 0000000130 so that Figure 10.76 appears. Figure 10.75 Production Order Creation Figure 10.76 shows the already-maintained characteristic values of Batch 0000000130 of Material 2265, in which the Product Class characteristic is assigned a value of A, the Thick- ness characteristic has a value of 8.10 mm, and the Date of Manufacture is 08/04/2025. Figure 10.76 Characteristic Values of Sender Material’s Batch 130 © 527 10.8 Batch Derivation Figure 10.77 shows the process order header screen and the Goods Receipt tab. To test whether batch derivation is correctly set up, you first manually create the goods receipt batch of the receiving material (2264), which, in this example, is Batch 0000000134. To manually create the batch, simply choose the Create Batch icon next to the Execute Batch Derivation icon. Don’t forget to release the process order at this time because you made the settings to trigger the batch derivation functionality only for a released pro- cess order (refer to Figure 10.72). Figure 10.77 Receiver Material upon Production Order Release Next, choose the Execute Batch Derivation icon to bring up the screen shown in Figure 10.78, which shows that the system has successfully performed batch derivation on receiver Material 2264 and its associated Batch 0000000134. Choose Details so that the screen shown in Figure 10.79 appears. Figure 10.78 Results of Batch Derivation 10 Batch Management 528 Figure 10.79 shows the automatic batch-derived values of the three characteristics, which are the same as shown previously in Figure 10.76. Figure 10.79 Batch Derivation−Based Characteristic Values of Receiving Material’s Batch 134

sections.title: Reporting
sections.en: 10.8.4 Reporting Transaction DVMO (Batch Derivation Monitoring Report) shows the status of batch derivations of different materials. The statuses with different-colored traffic lights (red, yellow, and green) denote the current batch derivation status of various batches of sender and receiver materials. While Figure 10.79 showed the batch derivation details of the just-concluded example, Figure 10.80 shows a range of materials that have either successfully or unsuccessfully gone through the batch derivation process. Figure 10.80 Batch Derivation Monitor Cockpit © 529

sections.title: Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
sections.en: 10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches 10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches Batch traceability is a key functionality that’s used in almost all industries, including in discrete manufacturing. SAP delivers the batch where-used list (Transaction MB56) in the SAP S/4HANA system. This where-used list enables business users to trace the batch from the finished product to the raw material (from the top down) or from the raw material to the finished product (from the bottom up). The WIP batch is used to keep the batch traceability for a process order when several batches of raw material are consumed and several batches of a finished product are generated. Transaction MB56 works well in most cases. However, there is a limitation on a process order with N:N batches. In a standard process order, there is no link between the raw material batch consumed and the finished product’s batch received in the warehouse; therefore, the batch where-used list determines all the batches of raw material for each batch of the finished product. Imagine, for example, that a problem was detected in a raw material batch after production. In this case, it isn’t possible to identify exactly which batch of finished product was produced with this problematic batch of raw material. In previous releases, the only solution for this scenario was to create separate process orders for each raw material batch, but that required more data entry, ineffi- cient business processes, and a significant amount of manual work. In this example, we focus on the WIP batch functionality that is available in the system for production and process orders in SAP S/4HANA. More specifically, we explain how to enhance the batch where-used list to show a link between each raw material batch consumed and the respective finished product batch produced in a process order. Our example involves a simple process order with two operations and a few components. In the following sections, we start with the necessary configuration needed to set up WIP batches, followed by the required master data setup. Then, we transition to the business processes involved in WIP batches, and we conclude by using the Batch Infor- mation Cockpit reporting tool to trace WIP batches.

sections.title: Configuration Settings
sections.en: 10.9.1 Configuration Settings The following are the configuration steps you need to perform before creating the con- firmation profile. Activating Work-in-Process Batch Business Functions Two business functions are related to the WIP batch functionality that you must acti- vate by using Transaction SFW5: LOG_PP_WIP_BATCH and LOG_PP_WIP_BATCH_02. For the basic use of the WIP batch and for the scenario covered here, you only need to activate the first business function. However, you should activate the second business function as well because it brings additional functionalities for the WIP batch, such as inventory valuation, use with documentary batches, and use on an order split. 10 Batch Management 530 To activate the business function using the Switch Framework (Transaction SFW5), select the business function and choose the Activate Changes button. Warning You can’t deactivate these business functions after they’re activated. Activating the Work-in-Process Batch After you activate the business function, you also need to activate the WIP batch func- tionality in configuration. To complete this step, use Transaction OMCWB, choose the Active radio button, and then save. Warning Once you activate this configuration setting, it can’t be reversed. In other words, when a user allows the use of the WIP batch functionality in the SAP system, it isn’t possible to get back to the original state in which WIP batches can’t be used. Activating the Original Batch The next configuration step is to activate original batch reference material. To com- plete this step, you execute Transaction OMT0. On the initial screen that appears (see Figure 10.81), check the Original Batch Reference Material box and then save. Doing this enables the system to use this functionality in master data. Figure 10.81 Activating Original Batch Reference Material © 531 10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches Confirmation Now that the WIP batch and original batch management functionalities are activated, the next configuration step is to create a new confirmation profile. Because the WIP batch information must be posted during the process order confirmation, the confir- mation profile will include the WIP batch–specific subscreens. To make the necessary configuration settings for the confirmation profile, you use Transaction OPK1 and choose standard confirmation profile SAP101. On the next screen (see Figure 10.82), in the Area Selection tab, you need to add areas 8285 WIP Batch: Goods Receipts, 8290 WIP Batch: Goods Issues, and 8295 WIP Batch: WIP Batch Entry to the standard Profile SAP101. Use Transaction OPK0 if the WIP batch process is being implemented in discrete man- ufacturing (in a production order). Figure 10.82 Confirmation Profile Finally, use Transaction OMBB to switch on the option for a synchronous batch where- used list. On the screen that appears, check the BaWU synchron.posting box for the rel- evant plant, which in this example is plant 1710. 10 Batch Management 532

sections.title: Master Data Setup
sections.en: 10.9.2 Master Data Setup Let’s now delve into the requisite master data that you need to set up for WIP batches. Confirmation Profile in the User Profile To set the confirmation profile created in the previous step as the default while posting a confirmation using Transaction COR6N, you check the Standard Prof. (standard pro- file) box on the initial screen of Transaction OPK1. However, because WIP batching is a very specific functionality that may not be used in all scenarios, here’s an alternative. For each business user who posts a confirmation using WIP batch, you can add this con- firmation profile to the user parameters. User parameters are used to store user-specific settings. To add a new user parameter, use Transaction SU3, enter “CORUPROF” as the SET/GET Parameter ID, and enter the previously created confirmation profile (“SAP101”) as the Parameter value (see Figure 10.83). Figure 10.83 Confirmation Profile in User Profile Original Batch in the Material Master As previously explained, checking the Original Batch Reference Material box in config- uration activates the OB Management (original batch management) field in the mate- rial master’s Work scheduling tab (see Figure 10.84). You must set the value of the OB Management field to 1 (Allowed) for the finished prod- uct, but you don’t need to set any value for the raw material. Also, note that the Batch mgmt box has been checked. © 533 10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches Figure 10.84 Allowing Original Batch Management in the Material Master Let’s now move to the business processes of WIP batches.

sections.title: Business Processes
sections.en: 10.9.3 Business Processes The business processes in WIP batches entail creating a process order and then perform- ing confirmation, in which the WIP batches are created. The system automatically posts the goods issue and goods receipt. We’ll cover these processes in the following sections. Process Order Consider the example shown in Figure 10.85 of a regular process order that appears after executing Transaction COR2. This screen shows the Material List, in which one component, 2265, is assigned batch number 0000000130. Figure 10.85 Process Order with Material List 10 Batch Management 534 To post the confirmation using WIP batches, you need to create a link between the raw material and the finished product. You don’t need to make any changes to the process order header or components, and you can directly proceed to order confirmation. Confirmation and Goods Issuance When you’re posting a confirmation using the WIP batch confirmation profile, you’ll notice some of the following details:  The button to display the Goods Movements overview isn’t displayed.  The goods receipt goods movement is always proposed, even if automatic goods receipt isn’t set for the process order.  The goods issue goods movements are always proposed, even if the Backflush box isn’t checked for the components.  All the goods movements are proposed without quantity. Use Transaction COR6N to access the screen shown in Figure 10.86 and then enter pro- cess Order “1002208” and its first Phase, which in this example is “0020.” Scroll down the screen to evaluate the information provided here. Figure 10.86 Confirmation with WIP Batches The same three areas—WIP Batch: GI, WIP Batch: GR, and WIP Batch Entry—that you set in the confirmation profile are displayed here. In the WIP Batch: GI area, the system may propose several components, of which we choose raw Material 2265 and its © 535 10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches associated batch 130 for consumption (using Mvmnt Type 261). Select this line item and then choose the WIP Batch button. On the popup screen that appears (see Figure 10.87), enter the name of the WIP batch in the WIP Batch field (e.g., “WIP_B_006”). In this process, you establish a link between the raw material batch consumed and the WIP batch. Then, choose Continue. Figure 10.87 WIP Batch The screen shown in Figure 10.88 prompts you to maintain the characteristic values of WIP batch WIP_B_006. Figure 10.89 shows the newly created WIP Batch WIP_B_006. Figure 10.88 Maintaining Characteristic Values of the WIP Batch 10 Batch Management 536 Figure 10.89 Yield Entry for the WIP Batch Next, you maintain the standard confirmation details, such as the yield, scrap, rework, and associated activities (e.g., machine hours, labor hours), as you normally do in the confirmation process. Work in Process Batch Goods Receipt As explained previously in this section, the goods receipt movement is proposed by default in the WIP Batch: GR section; however, it’s proposed without a quantity, so you’ll need to input the quantity. After that, select the goods receipt item and the respective WIP Batch, as shown in Figure 10.90, where you first select WIP_B_006 and then choose the WIPB->GR button. (If the batch for the finished good was already cre- ated at the time of creating or releasing the process order, then you don’t need to per- form this step.) This will bring up the screen shown in Figure 10.91. In Figure 10.91, notice that the system has created GR Batch number 0000000136 for finished good Material 2264 in the WIP Batch: GR section. You can now save this confir- mation, and because the WIP batch was already linked to the raw material, a link between the raw material and the finished product material is created. © 537 10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches Figure 10.90 Assigning the WIP Batch to the Finished Good Batch Figure 10.91 WIP Batch Goods Receipt This is how the system links a raw material’s batch to a WIP batch and then to a finished good’s batch for process order 1002208: Raw material 2265 (batch number 130): WIP batch (WIP_B_006): Finished good 2264 (batch number 136). We’ll see the same linkup when we cover reporting in Section 10.9.4. 10 Batch Management 538 When you’re posting goods receipt of a finished goods batch, a few more screens or popups may appear where you can maintain additional details, such as the date of manufacture or the characteristic values of the finished good’s batch that are created by the system at the time of goods receipt. Note Now that you have a basic understanding of WIP batches and how they work, we sug- gest exploring several additional features and functionalities that are available to han- dle all business processes. For example, in Figure 10.90, we showed you how to link the WIP batch with a goods receipt, but what if a user mistakenly creates a wrong link? To undo or delink this link- age, you use the WIPB><GR icon to correct the error.

sections.title: Reporting
sections.en: 10.9.4 Reporting Access the Batch Information Cockpit screen (see Figure 10.92) by using Transaction BMBC to view the report on WIP batches. Next, enter Material “2264,” its associated Batch “0000000136,” and Plant “1710,” and then choose Selection (not shown but located in the bottom right hand corner of the screen). Figure 10.92 Batch Information Cockpit Figure 10.93 shows Batch 0000000136 for Material 2264, which meets the selection cri- teria. Choose Batch 0000000136 on the left-hand side of the screen, then select Usage from the Details dropdown icon located in the top-right corner of the screen. © 539 10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches For batch 0000000136 for the finished good 2264, Figure 10.93 shows the Top-Down Analysis of process order 1002208. However, these screen details still don’t show the associated WIP batch that was created during confirmation. To view the details of the WIP batch, choose process order 1002208 and then choose WIP Batch. Figure 10.93 Batch Usage The lower half of the screen in Figure 10.93 shows the same WIP batch’s linkage that we previously covered: Raw material 2265 (batch number 130): WIP batch (WIP_B_006): Finished good 2264 (batch number 136). Note Refer to SAP Note 1473025, which answers frequently asked questions about WIP batches. Also, note the following:  Transaction BMOBPRO is a report that shows original batches in production.  Transaction BMOBPUR is a report that traces original batches in procurement.  As a troubleshooting tip, you can use Transaction MB57 once if the batch where- used list doesn’t appear in the Batch Information Cockpit. The next section looks at the Batch Information Cockpit and how you can use it to ana- lyze and monitor batches. 10 Batch Management 540

sections.title: Batch Information Cockpit
sections.en: 10.10 Batch Information Cockpit In process industries, such as food and pharmaceuticals, traceability of the ingredients or components within a production or procurement process is of paramount impor- tance. Batch traceability across logistics and supply chain processes is also a legal requirement in many industries and a good manufacturing practice to maintain in case there is a batch recall. Companies often develop complex custom reports to view and analyze batch traceability when the Batch Information Cockpit can serve the same purpose. With the Batch Information Cockpit, you can do the following:  You can perform top-down and bottom-up traceability analysis. Top-down traceabil- ity analysis of a finished good traces all the raw, packaging, and semifinished mate- rials that were used in making the finished product’s batch. With bottom-up trace- ability analysis, you trace the ingredients or raw materials and trace all the finished goods that were made using these ingredients or raw materials batches.  You can access the batch master directly from the Batch Information Cockpit to dis- play and change information related to the batch while performing the traceability analysis of the batch.  You can display the stock information for the batch.  You can display the stock position of a material and its associated batches. To access the Batch Information Cockpit, follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Information Cockpit or use Transaction BMBC. Figure 10.94 shows the initial screen where you set parameters that the system then considers to bring up relevant batches of materials. Notice the various tabs available on the right- hand side, which you can use to enter specific information. The left-hand side of the screen provides search functionality. For this example, enter Material “2264” and Plant “1710.” Then, choose Selection and then Execute Selection. This brings up the screen shown in Figure 10.95, where the left- hand side shows that the system is able to find the relevant material-plant-batch com- bination. The right-hand side appears when a user selects Usage from the Details drop- down list. Figure 10.95 also shows the bottom-up analysis of batch 130 of material 2264 in plant 1710. It shows that this batch was consumed in process order 1002208, which in turn produced batch 136. The raw materials procured externally from suppliers will show up with the material document numbers in the same top-down analysis. Note We suggest that you explore several features and functionalities of the Batch Informa- tion Cockpit to take advantage of the large amount of information it stores. © 541 10.10 Batch Information Cockpit For example, in the Usage tab of Figure 10.95, you can choose whether the system should bring up a top-down list or bottom-up list. Similarly, you can gain comprehen- sive visibility into all batches that have expired or whose expiration dates are approaching. Figure 10.94 Batch Information Cockpit Figure 10.95 Bottom-Up Analysis 10 Batch Management 542 In Figure 10.96, the Batch Information Cockpit has been run, and several batches have been selected. The materials are shown, and you can display the matches by highlight- ing the material. The system will then display information regarding the batch on a main screen. The tabs represent the number of different screens with information on this batch. Figure 10.96 Central Area of the Batch Information Cockpit

sections.title: Electronic Batch Record
sections.en: 10.11 Electronic Batch Record In highly regulated industries, such as pharmaceuticals, there’s often a legal require- ment to maintain a complete trail and record of all the batches used in the logistics and supply chain of manufacturing a product. Often, it’s not just important to keep records on the batches received from the procurement process or batches consumed in the production process; you must maintain a complete record of various quality inspec- tions that took place in the manufacturing process. The electronic batch record, or batch record for short, captures these inspection details. You access the screen shown in Figure 10.97 by following menu path Logistics  Central Functions  Batch Management  Tools  Batch Record or using Transaction COEBR. On the left-hand side, you can see a complete trail for material 2264 and its associated Batch 136. As you drill down in the same node, the related process orders will appear: (1) the two types of quality inspections (04 for finished goods inspection and 03 for in-pro- cess inspection) if the PP-QM integration is in place and (2) the Material List (BOM) used in the production process. In the upper-right corner of the same screen, you can only approve an electronic batch record for archiving with the Approve button if the statuses of various elements (e.g., process or process order, inspection lots) are set as required for electronic archiving. Here, the system also guides you to check all the prerequisites for electronic batch records. After approval, you can archive an electronic batch record by clicking the Archive button. © 543 10.11 Electronic Batch Record Figure 10.97 Electronic Batch Record The lower-right corner shows comprehensive details of the electronic batch record in PDF format. Note We recommend that you engage an SAP NetWeaver consultant to ensure that the nec- essary authorizations required for archiving electronic batches are granted. The SAP NetWeaver consultant will also set up a document repository required to store the electronic batch records being archived. In some industries, such as consumer products and automotive suppliers, there are legal requirements to store where-used data of materials used for production and delivered to customers. Enabling recall actions is a mandatory and critical issue for these industries. Data can be recorded by managing all relevant materials in batches, but this method has a negative impact on data volume. When turning on batch man- agement, entering a batch number becomes obligatory for all goods movements. Then all inventory postings must be executed on the batch level, and labor costs for inven- tory management will increase. With the documentary batch record, it’s important to ensure that a partial stock of a material is traceable without having to maintain a batch’s data. This reduces the effort and complexities involved in maintaining batch data. The documentary batch record finds greater use in automotive and consumer goods industries but is unsuitable for use in dangerous goods. You can perform documentary batch processing in the following ways:  You can enter documentary batches during goods movements.  In a process order, you can enter documentary batches only at backflush.  In embedded EWM, you must enable entering documentary batches during trans- port order confirmation. 10 Batch Management 544

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 10.12 Summary Batch management is important to a large number of process-based industries. It has evolved from just the identification of groups of items into a process that allows com- panies to perform product recalls, select and sell by batch characteristics, and identify expiring stock. As the drive for competitive advantage continues, companies will fur- ther investigate how batch information can lower production time and hasten delivery materials to customers. Chapter 11 will discuss sales and operations planning. ©

sections.title: Standard Sales and Operations Planning
sections.en: 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning The following sections give an overview of S&OP and the similarities and differences between standard S&OP and flexible planning. They then move on to cover forecast- ing, and they conclude with coverage of standard analysis in flexible planning.

sections.title: Overview
sections.en: 11.1.1 Overview In standard S&OP, a planner can plan individual materials or a group of materials known as a product group. A product group consists of individual materials or other product groups, and it enables a planner to define the proportion factor (percentage) for each material in the overall product group. It also offers the option to aggregate and dis- aggregate at various planning levels. If the planning processes in a company are rela- tively simple and straightforward—that is, if they are restricted to individual materials or a group of materials—then standard S&OP can fulfill the company’s business needs. Example Consider the following business scenario: Before each financial year begins, the sales and production teams spend countless hours working on planning figures that are acceptable to both. The sales team uses forecasting tools to arrive at next year’s sales figures (targets), based on historical data. The production team looks at things differ- ently. They evaluate whether they are able to meet the demand for sales with existing production capacities or not. The procurement planner needs a better understanding of how the production figures will impact the procurement process: do the vendors even have the capabilities to meet the supply requirements of the company? The inventory controller (of the warehouse) is concerned about whether there is enough space in the warehouse to manage and store the produced quantities. The manage- ment of the company is interested in not only increasing the profitability of the prod- uct (and at the same time, reducing costs) but also gaining a broader understanding of the capital tie-up involved and whether new investment (e.g., capacity enhancement, increasing the number of working shifts) is warranted. Furthermore, knowing which products (or groups of products), regions, or markets can bring in greater revenue for the company also helps in the decision-making process. This is where S&OP can help. In general, the entire planning exercise entails the cre- ation of various planning versions in the system and adjusting sales or production fig- ures until there is a mutual consensus. The finalized planning figures, which usually reflect a production plan, are sent forward to demand management, where they appear as planned PIRs with an active version. When the system runs the MRP, it con- siders the active PIRs to arrive at procurement proposals (for in-house production and external procurement). In the planning process, the teams need a planning table in the SAP S/4HANA system that can take all of the important planning considerations into account and at the same time account for the dependencies of various factors on others. © 547 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning Flexible planning functionality, while very sophisticated, is also a tool that is slightly complex to manage. But if a planner can gain a better understanding of flexible plan- ning, they can reap higher dividends from it, and it will help them produce much more realistic planning figures, which their company can use to reap greater financial bene- fits. For example, with flexible planning, a planner can configure a planning layout (known as a planning table) of important key figures; include self-defined macros to manage complex calculations; perform forecasting (which is also possible in standard S&OP); take into account special events (e.g., trade shows, the Olympics) for increases in sales or take into account natural calamities (e.g., droughts, floods) to factor in decreases in sales (or increases, if their company manufactures relevant products); and gain a broader understanding of commitments (known as pegged requirements), such as capacities, materials, and PRTs. When you’re deciding which S&OP option (standard S&OP or flexible planning) to use, it makes sense for you to evaluate business requirements and at the same time strive to maintain simplicity and a straightforward approach to business processes. As men- tioned already, standard S&OP offers a very limited set of functions, but being almost completely predefined, it enables you to immediately start taking advantage of those functionalities without any configuration. In general, we recommend that first, you should try to cover your business process with standard S&OP, and then, if important functions are still missing, you should consider using the rather complex and sophisti- cated flexible planning tool instead. Flexible planning offers full functionality but requires configuration, many presettings, and definitions. SAP offers several standard analysis reports in all logistics areas, and they consist of characteristics and key figures for a period. A characteristic value combination is the combination of characteristic values with which a planner wants to plan. Characteris- tics can be materials, plants, sales organizations, distribution channels, or purchase organizations. Key figures (values or quantities) can be quantity produced, quantity procured, operations quantity confirmed, production scrap (quantity), invoiced value, or purchasing value. The period can be an interval, such as six months. For example, standard analyses bring forth information such as the purchase and invoiced values (key figures) of all the materials (characteristics) during the past six months (the period). The standard analysis reports that SAP offers for each logistics functional area have predefined information structures with no option to add new key figures. However, you can create your own self-defined information structure in flexi- ble planning by choosing the desired key figures from several available catalogs. A cat- alog consists of a large number of characteristics of and key figures from a specific application area, such as PP QM. Furthermore, you can even define how frequently the system should update the values of key figures in flexible planning standard analysis. Hence, standard analysis in flexible planning isn’t just applicable to the PP functional- ity; the concepts and details we cover in this chapter are equally applicable to other functionalities such as QM, PM, MM, and SAP S/4HANA Sales. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 548 Note See Chapter 19 for more on reporting, including standard analyses in PP. Our example of flexible planning from both perspectives (flexible planning and stan- dard analysis in flexible planning) in this chapter remains primarily focused on SAP S/4HANA Sales and its integral correlation with PP processes. Note Regardless of whether your company implements standard S&OP or flexible planning, we encourage you to read the entire chapter because several features and functional- ities are applicable to both planning types, and we eventually cover them all (not neces- sarily in the same section). For example, we cover the forecasting functionality within flexible planning, but it’s also available and can be used in standard S&OP for a material or material group. We’ve also dedicated a separate section to forecasting. This is also true for events and rough-cut planning. An event tends to have an impact on planning figures, and a rough-cut planning profile provides better visibility into capacity, mate- rial, and PRT situations. Similarly, we cover aggregation/disaggregation in standard S& OP but not in flexible planning, although the option is available in both planning types. Figure 11.1 Process Overview of Sales and Operations Planning Demand Management Planned Independent Requirements in Demand Management Sales and Operations Planning Profitability Analysis (CO-PA) Sales and Profit Plan Sales History Sales and Operations Planning Transfer to Demand Management Sales Information System (SIS) © 549 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning Figure 11.1 shows that S&OP can include key figures from one of the following three available options:  Sales information system (SIS) Planners use the SIS to take information from the sales history to propose a sales plan.  Profitability analysis Planners use information from profitability analysis to help them make sales plans. The system derives this information from sales and profit planning.  Forecasting Planners use historical data to come up with a sales plan. We cover forecasting in Section 11.4. The figures from S&OP are eventually transferred to demand management in the form of PIRs, which form the basis of MRP. We list the objects used in S&OP (which are standard S&OP and flexible planning) and illustrate their logical relationships in Figure 11.2. The objects in S&OP form an integral part in the planning process, so it’s important to have a comprehensive understanding of them:  Information structure This is the data structure that stores important planning parameters. The planning data is stored in key figures for the characteristic value combinations. As we previ- ously explained, the characteristics can be the selection criteria based on which the system brings up relevant key figures. Key figures can be quantities or values—for example, the number of quality inspection lots, the total purchase value of raw materials, operation quantities, or production quantities.  Planning method The storage, aggregation, and disaggregation of data with regard to the planning level occur either as consistent planning or as level-by-level planning. In S&OP, the system looks for the planning method that is defined in the information structure.  Planning hierarchy The planning hierarchy contains the characteristic value combinations for the char- acteristics of the information structure.  Planning table This is where the planner carries out actual and interactive planning.  Planning type The planning type defines the layout or format of the planning table. See Figure 11.2 to see how each of the objects is linked in S&OP. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 550 Figure 11.2 S&OP Objects Figure 11.3 provides a graphical comparison of the planning methods available for stan- dard S&OP and flexible planning. Figure 11.3 Standard S&OP, Flexible Planning, Level-by-Level Planning, and Consistent Planning Field Catalog (Characteristics) Basic Technical Settings Characteristics Information Structure Field Catalog (Key Figures) Key Figures Planning Hierarchy Planning Table Profitability Analysis (CO-PA) Planning Type Planning Methods (Consistent/Level-by-Level) Planning Method Standard Planning Table Information Structure S076 Consistent Planning Customized Planning Table Standard S&OP Flexible Planning Level-by-Level Planning Standard S&OP Flexible Planning Customized Information Structure © 551 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning Now let’s discuss the details of the objects used in standard S&OP and flexible plan- ning.

sections.title: Information Structures
sections.en: 11.1.2 Information Structures To create or change information structures, you use menu path SAP IMG  Logistics General  Logistics Information System (LIS)  Logistics Data Warehouse  Data Basis  Information Structures  Maintain Custom Information Structures. Alternatively, you can use Transaction MC21 to create information structures and Transaction MC22 to change them. Figure 11.4 shows Info Struct. S076, which is used in standard S&OP. The planning result is stored for each characteristic value combination among the six key figures listed in the figure. Values for other key figures, such as special production and sales orders, can’t be stored in this (standard) information structure. In standard S&OP, you can’t change many settings, but we’ll review them to help you better understand the parameters used in standard S&OP for information structures and key figures. Figure 11.4 Standard SAP Information Structure S076 11 Sales and Operations Planning 552 To view or set parameters for information structures and key figures, follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP)  Master Data  Set Parameters for Info Structures and Key Figures, or use Transaction MC7F. On the screen shown in Figure 11.5, double-click the information structure (Table) S076 or select the same and choose Details, which opens the Change View “Info structure planning parameters”: Details screen. This area stipulates that the planning method used in standard S&OP is level-by-level planning (denoted by I in the Planning method field). Figure 11.5 Planning Parameters of Information Structure Choose the Key figure planning parameters folder on the left-hand side of the screen, and the screen shown in Figure 11.6 will appear. The lower half lists the key figures avail- able for standard information structure S076. Double-click the ABSAT field and a popup will appear (see Figure 11.7). Here, you can control whether the key figure can be used for forecasting, and you can determine the type of aggregation (summation) of the entered data in the planning table. © 553 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning Figure 11.6 Key Figure Planning Parameters Figure 11.7 Details of Key Figure ABSAT 11 Sales and Operations Planning 554

sections.title: Planning Methods
sections.en: 11.1.3 Planning Methods Near the beginning of this chapter, we mentioned that in standard S&OP, the configu- ration and other settings are predefined, with limited options to customize parameters to meet the business requirements. For example, in standard S&OP, the configuration for information structure S076 is preset with characteristics and key figures, and so is the planning table in which a planner maintains the planning figures. Planning is based on either individual materials or groups of materials (i.e., product groups), in which a planner can define proportional factors. On the other hand, with flexible planning, a planner can set up self-defined informa- tion structures with desired characteristics and key figures, and planning is based on planning hierarchies. A planner can also set up self-defined planning tables and per- form either level-by-level planning or consistent planning. Table 11.1 compares the planning methods available in standard S&OP and flexible planning. With standard S&OP, a planner can only plan by using level-by-level planning (see Table 11.2), whereas with flexible planning, a planner can use both level-by-level and as consistent planning. In level-by-level planning, the planning data is maintained at the specified level only. Because the system doesn’t automatically aggregate or disaggre- gate planning data, the planner will have to manually perform these functions. Table 11.2 provides a detailed comparison of consistent planning and level-by-level planning. To use consistent planning, it’s important for the planner to define the plan- ning hierarchies in the system. A planning hierarchy enables a planner to define pro- portional factors for each characteristic. For example, if there are three sales organiza- tions, then the planner can define how much (as a percentage) each sales organization (a characteristic) will contribute to the overall planning hierarchy. Similarly, changes made to any planning level automatically update and account for changes in other planning levels. For example, say that initially, a planner defined 10 materials, each of Standard S&OP Flexible Planning Preset configuration Individual configuration based on business processes Planning based on product groups/propor- tional factors Planning based on planning hierarchies Standard planning table for entry of key fig- ures Customized planning tables Level-by-level planning Consistent planning or level-by-level plan- ning Table 11.1 Standard S&OP versus Flexible Planning © 555 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning which constituted a 10% proportion of the planning results. Now, if a new material is added, the system automatically updates the proportional factor (100% ÷ 11 materials = 9.09% for each material). The system also automatically aggregates (adds up) and dis- aggregates (divides up) the planning data to present a consistent planning position in the planning table.

sections.title: Planning Types in Standard Sales and Operations Planning
sections.en: 11.1.4 Planning Types in Standard Sales and Operations Planning The planning type defines the layout or the format of the planning table and thus rep- resents the link between the planning table (where the actual planning is carried out) and the information structure (in which the planning data is stored). It’s possible to cre- ate several planning types for one information structure. For example, two different planning types may contain planning data for two seasons (autumn and winter). The data is stored in the information structure, so the different planning types depend on each other because they use the same set of data. If you use level-by-level planning, then it’s important to create a separate planning type for each planning level. Standard S&OP has been configured with three planning types, which are used auto- matically in standard S&OP planning:  SOPKAPA Planning type for the planning of individual product groups  SOPKAPAM Planning type for the planning of individual materials  SOPDIS Planning type for dual-level planning of product group hierarchies In the following sections, we’ll go through the steps you need to perform to create a product group that you can use in standard S&OP. We’ll also show you how to transfer the planning results of a product group to demand management as PIRs. Creating a Product Group Figure 11.8 shows that in this example, we’re using information structure S076 in stan- dard S&OP to create a product group called TURBINE_SOP for plant 1710. This product Consistent Planning Level-by-Level Planning Planning hierarchy Product group Storage at the lowest level; planning data at all levels Storage at each level; planning data only at the maintained level Automatic aggregation and disaggregation Aggregation and disaggregation as a plan- ning step Table 11.2 Consistent Planning and Level-by-Level Planning 11 Sales and Operations Planning 556 group has two materials, 2262 and 2264, with proportional factors of 60% and 40%, respectively. We explained aggregation and disaggregation earlier, but the propor- tional factor is specific to level-by-level planning only and can either be maintained manually or proposed automatically by the system. To create the product group in the system, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  SOP  Product Group  Create or use Transaction MC84. For Product group TURBINE_SOP and Plant 1710, enter materials “2262” and “2264” and their proportional factors, “60” and “40” (their percentages). Figure 11.8 Creating Product Group Turbine_SOP Then, choose the Product Group Graphic button to view the product group graphically (see Figure 11.9). Figure 11.9 Graphical View of Product Group © 557 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning In addition to using interactive entry, you can calculate proportional factors on the basis of historical data by following menu path Edit  Calculate Proportional Factors. Use this option to see how well the self-defined (manual) proportional factors compare with what the system proposes. That may help you prepare a realistic plan. Moreover, you can equally distribute the proportional factors via menu path Edit  Distribute Pro- portional Factors. Then, save the product group. Creating a Plan for the Product Group To plan the product group in standard S&OP, you use menu path Logistics  Production  SOP  Planning  For Product Group  Create or use Transaction MC81. Figure 11.10 shows the initial screen of the rough-cut plan for the product. Enter Product group “TURBINE_SOP” and Plant “1710” and then press (Enter) to go to the Define Ver- sion dialog box. On this screen, enter planning Version “A00” and a version descrip- tion. Then, you again press (Enter) to go to the Create Rough-Cut Plan screen. Figure 11.10 Planning in Standard S&OP If the historical key figure data is already available in the system, then you can import it to create a sales plan. Alternatively, you can manually enter the Sales quantities in the planning table, as we’ve done in this example, by entering sales data for the next nine months. On the screen shown in Figure 11.11, choose the More dropdown list to see the following options for creating a sales plan for the product group (or for an individ- ual material, where applicable):  Transfer Plan from SIS  Transfer CO-PA Plan  Forecast  Transfer Product Group Proportional from Production or from Sales To use any of these options, the system must have significant historical data to help with effective and reliable planning. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 558 Figure 11.11 Sales Plan in Standard S&OP Options for Creating a Production Plan Automatically Remain in the planning table (Transaction MC81). Figure 11.12 shows how to create a production plan with the following options by choosing More from the top menu bar:  Synchronous to Sales Sales figures are used as operations plans.  Target Stock Level The operations plan is configured in such a way that the target stock level is reached in each period.  Target Days’ Supply The operations plan is configured in such a way that the target days’ supply is reached in each period.  Stock Level = Zero The operations plan is configured in such a way that the entire stock level is con- sumed. Depending on the business requirement, you can choose whether the production plan should be equal to the target stock level or whether the target stock should be zero at the end of the period. For this example, you create the production plan as being syn- chronous with the sales plan. This production plan also takes the target stock levels into account at the end of each period. It’s important to note that these production figures have no direct implications for pro- duction or procurement. The purpose of entering or incorporating these numbers (which are key figures, such as sales or production) is to come to a common under- standing, wherein relevant stakeholders, be they from sales or production, can transfer the finalized key figures to demand management in the form of PIRs. For example, say several planning versions are created in the system, from which input and feedback © 559 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning from sales and production are taken. The sales or production figures are then revised either in the same planning version or after you create a new version. The finalized ver- sion eventually transfers to demand management and is reflected as PIRs with the Active box checked. Figure 11.12 Options for Creating Production Plan in Standard S&OP The next step is to transfer the production plan to demand management, where the requirements of individual members (materials) of the product group are reflected as PIRs. When the transfer of the product group quantities to demand management takes place, the system disaggregates them at the material level according to the proportional factor defined previously. In this example, proportional factors of 60% and 40% are maintained for the two materials. The next section will show how the system disaggre- gates (divides up) the total planning quantities of the product group in a 60 to 40 ratio. Transferring to Demand Management You can directly transfer the planning figures from the planning table to demand man- agement by selecting the relevant key figures (e.g., production) and using menu path Extras  Transfer to Demand Management. Alternatively, you can use menu path Logis- tics  Production  SOP  Planning  For Product Group  Transfer PG to Demand Manage- ment or Transaction MC75. Figure 11.13 shows the initial screen where you assign Version “A00” of the product group and plant for transfer to demand management. You select the Prod.Plan for Mat. or PG Members as Proportion of PG radio button to transfer the production plan to demand management. Also, you must specify the requirement type and the version of PIR within which the system transfers the values. In this example, enter “VSF” in Requirements type, enter “00” in Version, and then choose Transfer now. A message log will then appear after the transfer is made. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 560 Figure 11.13 Transferring Product Group to Demand Management Planned Independent Requirements in Demand Management To check and confirm whether the production figures of the product group were suc- cessfully transferred in demand management as PIRs, you follow SAP menu path Logis- tics  Production  Demand Management  Planned Independent Requirements  Display or use Transaction MD63. Figure 11.14 PIRs in Demand Management © 561 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning On the initial screen, enter the selection parameters, such as Product Group “TURBINE_ SOP,” Plant “1710,” Requirements Type “VSF,” and Selected Version “00,” all of which you previously defined on the screen shown in Figure 11.12. Then, press (Enter) and you’ll see the screen shown in Figure 11.14, which shows how the product group breaks down into individual members (materials), with the transferred quantity divided in a ratio of 60% to 40%.

sections.title: Distributing Key Figures
sections.en: 11.1.5 Distributing Key Figures During the course of planning the key figures, you often need to make mass changes to several key figures at once. This can be tedious if done one by one or manually, so you should use the Distribute functionality to make mass changes to a key figure in the same row or column. The following are some of the changes that you can make:  Perform mathematical calculations, such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, and so on, on all values (or a selection of values) of a key figure.  Increase or decrease all the values (or a selection of values) of a key figure by a defined percentage.  Distribute a value across the time series and replace the old values. Figure 11.15 shows that you can distribute key figures from the menu bar by choosing Distribute on the planning table. In the Distribution Functions dialog box that appears, enter a number in the Val. Field and then select the right operand (OP). The list of avail- able operands will then appear (and is shown on the right-hand side of Figure 11.15). Figure 11.15 Distribution Functionality: Key Figures in S&OP 11 Sales and Operations Planning 562 Figure 11.16 shows a graphical representation of key figures—including sales, produc- tion, and stock level—that gives materials planners the visibility they need into the inward and outward movements of materials. Figure 11.16 Graphical Representation of Key Figures in S&OP The following sections cover aggregation and disaggregation, as well as tools and fea- tures that are available not only in standard S&OP but also in flexible planning. Aggregation and Disaggregation During planning, available tools such as aggregation and disaggregation of key figures help the planner to plan at one organizational (characteristics) level and transfer the values (key figures) to other characteristics levels, based on the defined proportions. Automatic aggregation and disaggregation are tools the planner can use to help pre- pare a reliable and realistic sales (or production) plan. For example, say the planner wants to consolidate (aggregate) the production quantities (key figures) at a plant level (characteristic). In this scenario, the planner can use the aggregation tool to quickly consolidate the desired information. Another example of aggregation occurs when the sales organization is at the highest level, then the distribution channel level, then the division level, then the material level, and finally the plant level. In consistent planning, automatic aggregation and disaggregation are consistently available at all planning levels, whereas in level-by-level planning, a planner needs to manually trigger them. Because standard S&OP doesn’t offer consistent planning, a planner only uses level-by-level planning in S&OP. The system enables a user to drill down data from higher levels to lower levels (using disaggregation) or progressively sum up the data from the lower levels to the higher levels (using aggregation of the sum or average). The ultimate objective of planning, either in standard S&OP or in flexible planning, is to create a plan at the lowest level of © 563 11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning the characteristic combination (e.g., at the product group or material level) and forward the plan to PP. The planning hierarchy consists of characteristic value combinations and their disag- gregation percentages. For example, if there’s a characteristic value combination of the sales organization, distribution channel, division, material, plant, statistics currency, and document currency, then a planner can view the historical sales data or a forecast plan for given key figures of the characteristics. The system then disaggregates if the data is viewed from the lower levels of the hierarchy, and it aggregates the same if the data is viewed from the higher levels using the same set of disaggregation proportions. Automatic Calculation of Proportional Factors When the planning hierarchy is maintained, you use Transaction MC9B (Using Plan- ning Hierarchy) for consistent planning to allow the system to calculate the propor- tional factors based on the disaggregation proportions. These proportional factors become the basis for aggregation and disaggregation in consistent planning. Time-Based Disaggregation When rough-cut S&OP transfers the data in demand management, the system requires time-based disaggregation. In S&OP, planning is carried out in periods of weeks or months, whereas the PIR needs a current date for its planning activities. If the business process is simple, then the requirement of the period is scheduled for the first working day of the period without any further configuration. However, if there’s a need for an in-depth and complex distribution of quantities, then a planner will need to define dis- tribution strategies and period splits. The planner assigns the distribution strategy to the period split for weekly and monthly distribution, and the planner assigns the period split, in turn, to the plant and the planning group. Planning Hierarchy in Standard Sales and Operations Planning The planning hierarchy in standard S&OP defines the proportion of one material with respect to others. For example, having a material with a 40% proportional factor enables the system to divide all planning figures of this material into 40% of the total planning figures. You create planning hierarchy master data in standard S&OP by maintaining product groups. You can also generate the master data automatically by using Transaction MC8P for information structure S076. Alternatively, you can use program RMCPSOPP (by using Transaction SE38) to generate the master data for infor- mation structure S076. When you, as a planner, are in the product group as in standard S&OP or are in a planning hierarchy in flexible planning, you need to define propor- tional factors. The system then uses these defined proportional factors either in level- by-level planning or in consistent planning. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 564 Table 11.1 provides an overview of the usage of proportional factors in consistent plan- ning and level-by-level planning. To aggregate or disaggregate planning figures in standard S&OP, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  SOP  Disaggregation  Break Down PG Plan or use Transaction MC76. Once you select the product group, plant, and version, the system will redirect you to a screen where you can make changes to the proportional factor of each mem- ber of the product group and where you can check the relevant boxes to run the stan- dard macros when the planning table screen for standard S&OP appears.

sections.title: Working with Macros
sections.en: 11.1.6 Working with Macros Macros enable you to automate the linking of key figures in the planning table by using calculation rules. In standard S&OP, the system provides macros for the calculation of the projected stock level and for creating an operations plan on the basis of the sales plan. You can also use macros in aggregation and disaggregation during level-by-level planning. However, you can’t define your own macros in standard S&OP. In the planning table, you first select the Sales line item and then choose Macro, which brings up the list of standard macros available to select from. For this example, choose Aggregation: Sales, which aggregates the individual sales figures for a material. Note Self-defined macros in flexible planning are covered in greater detail in Section 11.2.6.

sections.title: Flexible Planning
sections.en: 11.2 Flexible Planning It’s amply evident that standard S&OP offers limited options in terms of solutions to diverse and complex business scenarios and processes. If your company intends to carry out very lean and production-focused forecasting of sales figures, then we recommend that you check whether standard S&OP can fulfill your business needs. Planning Modes Calculation Methods Usage of Proportional Factors Number of Levels Level-by-level planning – Direct Single Consistent planning Calculation based on the planning hierarchy Multiplication of pro- portional factors of multiple levels Multiple Consistent planning Calculation based on actual data – Multiple Table 11.3 Usage of Proportional Factors in Disaggregation © 565 11.2 Flexible Planning In more complex planning scenarios, flexible planning can help. In flexible planning, a planner can plan and process planning data from any organizational unit, whether it’s a sales organization, a distribution channel, or a production plant. Flexible planning begins with designing an information structure, which is a structure with organiza- tional units and business planning values—for example, forecast values in different periods, their relationships, and different views of planning values from the perspec- tives of different organizational units (e.g., sales, production, procurement). To success- fully implement flexible planning, it’s very important for a planner to gain an in-depth understanding of how to define an information structure to fulfill business planning requirements. You can also use information structures for product allocation. For example, in the automobile industry, it’s common to assign sales quotas to different regions and auto- motive dealers to ensure that a company’s automobile is sold in every region the com- pany serves. In an information structure for production allocation in this example, you would maintain a dealer-wise sales quota. During sales order creation for a specific dealer, the system would then check the information maintained in the information structure for product allocation to see whether any given dealer is still eligible to place orders. And similar to standard S&OP, in which everything is preconfigured, in flexible planning, you would design a table by using the basic steps of configuring a self- defined information structure, setting the parameters for the information structure and key figures, and configuring the planning type. This section will show you not only how to successfully set up flexible planning but also how to specifically cover the update fields (key figures) involved in flexible planning. These update fields enable you to better use the flexible planning functionality when features such as macros, aggregation or disaggregation, events, and rough-cut planning aren’t involved. The update fields apply to standard analysis in flexible planning. S&OP can create a planning hierarchy and maintenance of disaggregation percentages for the characteristic value combinations in a hierarchy. As we mentioned previously, a planning hierarchy is available in flexible planning only. The section also covers self-defined macros, aggregation and disaggregation, events, and rough-cut planning profiles. All of these features hold significant value for planners.

sections.title: Creating a Self-Defined Information structure
sections.en: 11.2.1 Creating a Self-Defined Information structure Because we already covered the salient features of the information structure for flexi- ble planning in Section 11.1.2, we’ll now create the self-defined information structure in flexible planning. To create and change these structures, you follow menu path SAP IMG  General Logis- tics  LIS  Logistics Data Warehouse  Data Basis  Information Structures  Maintain Cus- tom Information Structures. Alternatively, you can use Transaction MC21 to create information structures and Transaction MC22 to change them. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 566 Figure 11.17 shows the initial screen where you can create an information structure. For this example, you define the Info Structure as “S655” and enter the description as “SOP: Info_Structure_Turbine.” It’s important to select the application because the resulting catalog (consisting of characteristics and key figures) will then appear accordingly. For this example, enter “01” as the Application for SAP S/4HANA Sales. It’s also important to check the Planning Possible box to enable the user to perform planning functions in flexible planning. Figure 11.17 Self-Defined Information Structure Creation in Flexible Planning Then, press (Enter) and the screen shown in Figure 11.18 will appear. Next, click the Choose Chars button, which will bring up the option to select characteristics from the catalogs. For this example, select the following characteristics from three catalogs:  SD: Sales Organization (order) – Sales Organization – Distribution Channel – Division  SD: Article (order) – Material – Plant  SD: Units (delivery) – Document Currency – Local Currency If the selections are made in the correct order, the resulting screen will look as shown in Figure 11.18. © 567 11.2 Flexible Planning Figure 11.18 Characteristics and Key Figures in Flexible Planning Next, to select the key figures, choose Choose Key Figures and then select the key fig- ures from the following catalog. For this example, select the following key figures from two different catalogs:  Key Figures: SOP – Sales – Production – Stock Level – Target Stock Level – Available Capacity  SD: Values, Prices (Order) – Net Price – Net Value All the key figures that are selected in Figure 11.18 are then copied to a temporary area, and you confirm the selection by choosing Copy + close. If you’ve made the selections in the correct order, then the resulting screen will look like the Key Figures area in Figure 11.19. Next, choose the Generate button to open the Log screen shown in Figure 11.20. Here, information structure S655 is successfully created in the form of an SAP table. If there are any errors, a warning or error message will appear, thus enabling you to address the problem to avoid issues during the planning activity phase. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 568 Figure 11.19 Characteristics and Key Figures in Flexible S&OP Figure 11.20 Information Structure in Flexible S&OP © 569 11.2 Flexible Planning The next step in creating the information structure for flexible planning is to define the planning method. The SAP S/4HANA system offers two types of planning methods, as we’ve already discussed:  Consistent planning  Level-by-level planning The planning method determines the method of disaggregation of data and the storage of data at various levels of planning. Refer to Section 11.1.5 for details. To set up a planning method for the self-defined information structure, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Sales & Operations Planning (SOP)  Master Data  Set Parameters for Info Structures and Key Figures or use Transaction MC7F. On the over- view screen that shows the various information structures that are available, double- click information structure (Table) S655. The screen shown in Figure 11.21 will then appear, wherein you enter “K” for the Planning method (for consistent planning) and “1710” for the Planning Plant so that the system will automatically use this plant during transfer of key figures to demand management. Figure 11.21 Planning Parameters in Information Structure Two other important fields are Statistics Currency and Base Unit of Measure. For this example, you enter “USD” and “PC” (respectively) into these two fields. Entering “USD” into the Statistics Currency field enables the system to use US dollars as a base currency, whereas the Base Unit of Measure is the smallest unit of measure in which the system will show inventory figures. If a material has a different base unit of measure than what you’re using in planning, then you must define the conversion factor. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 570 You also enter “PRODU” into the Capacity figure field so that you can work with pro- duction line values from the planning table while carrying out rough-cut capacity plan- ning. Similarly, by entering “PRODU” into the Figure to Dem. Mgmt field (not shown), you ensure that the system will use the production line values while transferring the planned values to demand management. Finally, you enter “US” into the Factory calendar field, which will enable the system to refer to working days and holidays during rough-cut capacity planning. Note You can perform rough-cut capacity planning in a planning table to see whether the planned forecast values are within the constraints—for example, available capacity at a critical work center. If they aren’t, then you need to adjust the planned forecast val- ues until the capacity load value is shown within permissible limits. For rough-cut capacity planning, you use the production quantity from the planning table. The sys- tem will then select the production quantities of different periods from the PP line in the planning table to calculate the capacity requirement and display the capacity load as a percentage value. Next, select the Key figure planning parameters folder shown in Figure 11.21 and the screen shown in Figure 11.22 will appear. Figure 11.22 Key Figures in Information Structure © 571 11.2 Flexible Planning The lower half of Figure 11.22 lists all the key figures you’ve selected during information structure creation. Next, double-click ABSAT, and on the screen that appears, choose between the following two options:  Forecast Check this box to allow forecasting. If you don’t check the Forecast box for the key figures, then forecasting won’t be possible for the key figures in the planning table.  Time-Based Disaggregation This determines the type of aggregation with three possible options: the average value of the total key figures value of a lower level is aggregated to the higher level; the total of the key figure values of the lower level is aggregated to the higher level; or no aggregation is to be carried out at all from the lower level to the higher level. We recommend that you always use the total aggregation method.

sections.title: Planning Hierarchy
sections.en: 11.2.2 Planning Hierarchy In standard S&OP, only level-by-level planning is possible. Although the characteristics of the information structure already fine-tune the planning levels, a planner must still assign characteristic values to them to carry out planning at those levels. For this example, the custom information structure S655 contains the sales organization, dis- tribution channel, division, material, plant, document currency, and statistics currency characteristics. The purpose of a planning hierarchy is to enable the planner to define the proportional factor of each of the characteristics and in the sequence in which they are supposed to be planned. A planner creates a planning hierarchy for the following important reasons:  To let the SAP S/4HANA system know how the characteristic values are involved in S&OP.  To let the SAP S/4HANA system know the percentage proportional weight of each of the characteristic values in the organizational hierarchy. This is known as the disag- gregation percentage, which the system uses to disaggregate or aggregate the data across various levels during planning and consolidation. We covered aggregation and disaggregation in Section 11.1.5. In level-by-level planning, you use the disaggregation percentage directly for disaggre- gation/aggregation purposes. In consistent planning, you use the disaggregation per- centages to calculate the proportional factors used in aggregation and disaggregation. You must fulfill the following prerequisites to successfully create a planning hierarchy:  Create an information structure with an active status.  Configure the planning parameters for an information structure.  Create the update rules and activate them (if applicable). You can only create one planning hierarchy for a given information structure. In addi- tion, note that you can only use one information structure in consistent planning, whereas in level-by-level planning, you can use one or multiple information structures. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 572 To maintain the planning hierarchy for self-defined information structure S655, use SAP menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning Hierarchy  Cre- ate or use Transaction MC61. Next, enter Info structure “S655” as shown in Figure 11.22. In the ensuing Characteristics popup, enter all the details. Note that if the details aren’t maintained at a specific level, the system triggers the planning hierarchy to maintain the proportional factor. For example, if you don’t enter any details for Division, then the next three characteristics, Material, Plant, and Document Currency, will enable you to reflect the planning hierar- chy in which, for example, you can enter multiple material numbers with a proportional factor for each. Next, on the Create Planning Hierarchy: Statistics Currency screen, enter “USD” as the Local Cur. with a Proportion of “100”, as shown in Figure 11.23. Figure 11.23 Creation of Planning Hierarchy Next, navigate from the higher level to the next level by selecting the characteristic val- ues and choosing the Next Level icon (not shown) or by just double-clicking a character- istic value. Also, check out the graphical representation by choosing the Hierarchy Graphic button on the Maintain Planning Hierarchy screen or by using Transaction MC67. Tips and Tricks You can also use Transaction MC9A (Generate Master Data) to have the system auto- matically create the planning hierarchy along with the disaggregation percentages based on the historical data available in the information structure and its version. You can also use the program to simulate the creation of a planning hierarchy.

sections.title: Planning Type
sections.en: 11.2.3 Planning Type Once you have the planning hierarchy in place, the next step is to maintain the plan- ning type, which then becomes an underlying template or layout in the planning table. © 573 11.2 Flexible Planning As mentioned previously, in maintaining a planning type for flexible planning, a plan- ner can define their own macros, assign events, allocate rough-cut capacity planning, and deploy planning versions. The planning type then becomes a format, layout, or template of the planning table, containing key figures and arranged in the desired way. A planning type is similar to a Microsoft Excel program, in which a user can, for exam- ple, include the headings on the left-hand side of the worksheet and the data for the headings on the right-hand side of the worksheet. A planner can also create multiple planning types for a given information structure. Each planning type arranges the key figures of the information structure as desired on a planning table that has its own macros to convert or translate the available data of an information structure. Having multiple planning types enables the planner to plan the same data arranged in different templates and from different perspectives to arrive at a feasible operations plan. Alternatively, if a planner is going to use only one planning type, then they should save the data under different planning versions and then final- ize one final planning version, activate it, and transfer the finalized planning data to demand management. Note In flexible planning, you can only transfer the finalized planning to demand manage- ment if you have the plant characteristic and the material characteristic included in the different characteristics defined in the planning hierarchy. You create the planning types with reference to an information structure (in this exam- ple, it’s S655). The name of the planning table must start with a Z or a Y only. The name of the planning type can be up to 11 characters long. To create a planning type, you use SAP menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flex- ible Planning  Tools  Planning Type/Macro  Create or use Transaction MC8A. On the Planning Type: Create screen (see Figure 11.24), enter Planning Type “Z655,” check the Line Layout and Macros boxes, and press (Enter). Figure 11.24 Planning Type 11 Sales and Operations Planning 574 When the popup appears, enter Info structure “S655” and then press (Enter) again to open the Define Planning Type dialog box (see Figure 11.25). Figure 11.25 Creation of Planning Type Next, define the planning horizon by entering the Planning start date, the Future peri- ods involved in forecasting, and the Historical data that needs to be pulled in from the information structure. The planning start date is the date or the period from which you want to begin plan- ning. This is the start of the future periods; that is, if you’ve opted for six future periods starting on 07/01/2024 (July 01, 2024), and if you’ve set the period as monthly in the configuration for the information structure, then the planning table will show six monthly columns, starting with 07/2024 and running to 01/2025. Similarly, if the historical data is set for six months, then the system will only pull in the his- torical data for the past six months. This historical data will be used to forecast for future periods. Press (Enter) or choose Continue to open the screen shown in Figure 11.26. You can add key figures to the planning type by choosing the Structure button to add the key figures, choosing the Add row button to insert a blank row, and choosing the Actual data button to add a row that will bring into the planning table the actual data that belongs to version A00 (which contains the actual data) of the information struc- ture. To bring in the actual data for a specific key figure, first keep the cursor on that key © 575 11.2 Flexible Planning figure and then choose the Actual data button. For example, if you place the cursor on the Sales key figure and then choose the Actual data button, then the system will add a row called Actual Sales below the Sales key figure. Figure 11.26 Structure Assignment in Planning Type You can’t edit the actual data row, although you can use it in formulas (macros) to arrive at the data of other rows. You can use the actual data row to compare the actual data to the planned data. Figure 11.27 shows how the planning type will look after you successfully incorporate all of the needed key figures into the planning table in the desired sequence. You will need to repeatedly choose Structure to bring each key figure into the planning table in the desired order or sequence. When you’re finished, save the planning type. Figure 11.27 Planning Table in Flexible Planning 11 Sales and Operations Planning 576 Tips and Tricks You can also bring in external data for a key figure by using the EXIT_SAPMMCP6_A00 user exit. To create a row for the external data, keep the cursor on a key figure and then go to Edit  User Exit Data.

sections.title: Working with Self-Defined Macros in Flexible Planning
sections.en: 11.2.4 Working with Self-Defined Macros in Flexible Planning Next, create self-defined macros in the planning type by using menu path Macro  Cre- ate. Follow these steps: 1. In the Define Macro window, enter the Macro name and the Description of the macro and then press (Enter). 2. Check the Display and Calc. selected area boxes on the screen shown in Figure 11.28. 3. Place the cursor on the first Operand field on the screen shown in Figure 11.28 and then either use drilldown or press (F4) to select a key figure in the ensuing window. For this example, select the key figure Sales and choose Continue. Figure 11.28 Self-Defined Macro 4. Use the drilldown for the Op field, and on the screen in Figure 11.29, all of the normal operands (addition, multiplication, etc.) will be available for you to choose from to perform mathematical calculations. 5. Enter the value “0.90” in the second Operand field on the screen shown in Figure 11.28. Then, the calculated results (output) should appear as the Production key figure in the Result field. Thereafter, whenever a planner manually enters values or makes available the data in the Sales key figure of the planning table and executes the ADJPROD macro, the Sales key figure will be multiplied by 0.9, and the results will be shown in the Production key figure. 6. Save the macro. © 577 11.2 Flexible Planning Figure 11.29 Available Operands For the second example of using macros, you define a second macro, TOTAL, which multiplies the Sales key figure by the Net Price to produce a Net Value key figure, as shown in Figure 11.30. Figure 11.30 Second Self-Defined Macro 11 Sales and Operations Planning 578 After manually entering the Sales quantity on the screen shown in Figure 11.31, you simulate the self-defined macros by following menu path Macro  Execute, which brings up the option to select a macro to execute.
