Execute both macros one by one and then examine the results (see Figure 11.32) to see if they conform to the formulas defined in the macros. Figure 11.31 Executing Macros Figure 11.32 Testing Self-Defined Macros Tips and Tricks You can record the macros by following menu path Macro  Record. If you need to, you can delete obsolete or incorrectly created macros or edit them via the same menu path within the planning type. © 579 11.2 Flexible Planning

sections.title: Row Attributes in a Planning Type
sections.en: 11.2.5 Row Attributes in a Planning Type To set the properties or attributes of any key figure, you can simply double-click the key figure or select it and choose the Row Attribute icon, as shown in Figure 11.33. You can also mark a key field as a Planning line (input required), an Output line (display only), a Text line (information-only key figure), or an Auxiliary line (which you can use as a vari- able in macros). You can also determine the aggregation type of the key figure by choosing the relevant radio button. In Figure 11.33, we changed the standard descrip- tion of the Production key figure to Adjusted Production. Figure 11.33 Line Attributes in Planning Type

sections.title: Planning in the Planning Table
sections.en: 11.2.6 Planning in the Planning Table Apart from entering sales, calculating production quantities, and running macros, a planner can perform the following functions (all of which we cover in this chapter, with the exception of transfer to profitability analysis):  Forecasting historical data in the planning table (see the Executing the Forecast sec- tion ahead, as well as Section 11.1.4)  Transferring to demand management (Section 11.1.4)  Capacity planning (rough-cut planning; Section 11.1.5)  Events (Section 11.1.6)  Transferring to profitability analysis 11 Sales and Operations Planning 580 To create a new plan in flexible planning for the self-defined information structure, you use menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Cre- ate or use Transaction MC93. Follow these steps: 1. Enter the planning type as “S655” for this example. Alternatively, you can use “Z655” for the planning type to clearly indicate a link with information structure Z655. 2. Enter the characteristics combinations for which to create the planning table. 3. Enter the Version number, “A00,” for the information structure data that is to be used in the planning table. Planning version A00 is an active version. 4. Press (Enter) to open the planning table and then maintain the data from the infor- mation structure for the key figures. Instead of manually entering the Sales key figure, you can use the SIS, which we cov- ered in Section 11.1. In the following sections, we’ll cover how to execute a forecast, check and validate the self-defined macros, and transfer the results of the forecast to demand management. Executing the Forecast First, select the Sales key figure row and execute the forecast from the menu in the planning table by choosing Edit  Forecast. Next, as shown in Figure 11.34, you select the Period intervals radio button and manually maintain both Forecast and Historical data. Figure 11.34 Executing Forecast in Flexible Planning © 581 11.2 Flexible Planning For this example, you use historical data from the past six months to execute the fore- cast for the next six months. Alternatively, you can maintain the same details by choosing No. of periods and entering data into No. of forecast periods and No. of histor- ical values. Next, in the Forecast execution area, you select the forecast model, such as Seasonal models. You can change or update the historical values by choosing the Historical but- ton. A planner also can manually enter or correct the historical values, as we’ve done in this example. When choosing the Forecasting button, depending on the forecast model you’ve selected, you may need to enter further details, such as the Alpha Factor and Gamma Factor (see Figure 11.35). Figure 11.35 Additional Forecast Parameters Tips and Tricks To save yourself the work of manually entering all of the preceding details, you can use the Forecast Profile button shown in Figure 11.34. We discuss creating forecast profiles in Section 11.4.3. You execute the forecast by choosing the Forecasting button on the screen shown in Figure 11.35. This brings up the Forecast: Results screen, which shows the forecast values for the next few months (see Figure 11.36). Choose Continue and all of the forecast fig- ures will automatically be copied to the Sales key figure on the planning table. The result is a graphical comparison of the historical and forecast figures for the period under consideration—that is, the defined months of historical figures and forecast fig- ures. Note Because we’ve separately covered forecasting in sufficient detail in this chapter, we’ve intentionally limited the discussion here to only the basic steps involved in forecasting in the planning table. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 582 Figure 11.36 Results of Forecast Validating Self-Defined Macros After you’ve put the Sales figures in place from the forecasting activity, the next step is to manually enter the Net Price and then execute both of the macros (see Figure 11.37). The system will then automatically fill in the Net Value and Adjusted Production key figures (see Figure 11.38). Figure 11.37 Executing Macros © 583 11.2 Flexible Planning Figure 11.38 Planning Figures after Executing Forecast and Running Macros With the Adjusted Production figures also now in place in the planning table, let’s trans- fer the same to demand management, in which the system will reflect the quantities of the production key figures as PIRs. Transferring Key Figures to Demand Management Next, while remaining within the planning table, select the Production row and choose Extras  Transfer to Demand Management. Figure 11.39 Transfer of Planning Data to Demand Management 11 Sales and Operations Planning 584 Alternatively, you can follow menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Plan- ning  Environment  Transfer Material to Demand Management or use Transaction MC90 (see Figure 11.39). Then, assign Version “A00” of Info structure “S655” for transfer to demand manage- ment. The Key figure for this example is PRODU (production), and the transfer horizon begins on 07/09/2024 (July 09, 2024). Furthermore, you must specify the requirement type and the version of PIR within which the system should transfer the values. For this example, assign Requirement Type “VSF” and Version “00” and then choose the Transfer now button in the menu bar. A message log will then appear after the transfer of key figures to demand management. Transferring Results Validation in Demand Management Next, to check and confirm whether the production figures were successfully trans- ferred to demand management as PIRs, you follow SAP menu path Logistics  Produc- tion  Demand Management  Planned Independent Requirements  Display or use Transaction MD63. On the initial screen that appears, enter the selection parameters, such as Material “2264,” Plant “1710,” Requirements Type “VSF,” and Version “00,” all of which you previ- ously defined on the screen shown in Figure 11.39. Then, press (Enter), and on the screen shown in Figure 11.40, check whether the transferred quantities are complete and correct. You can also check the correctness of the transferred data for a product group by entering the relevant product group, which in this example is “TURBINE_ SOP,” in the initial parameters selection screen in Transaction MD63. Figure 11.40 Validation of Transferred Planning Data as PIRs This completes our review of the standard steps involved in flexible planning. Next, we discuss a few salient features of the planning table, especially those related to the plan- ning hierarchy. © 585

sections.title: Additional Features of Planning Tables
sections.en: 11.2.7 Additional Features of Planning Tables The following list reviews some additional features related to planning tables:  Checking header information To check the header information, which shows the planning hierarchy levels, choose menu path Settings  Choose Header Info in the planning table.  Firming/unfirming key figure data During consistent planning and to ensure that aggregation and disaggregation won’t change the key figure data, you can firm or unfirm the key figure data. Follow menu path Settings  Firming  Switch On. We recommend that you then follow this sequence in the firming process: – Activate fixing – Change the value – Deactivate fixing You can also deactivate fixing by following menu path Settings  Firming  Unfirm Period.  Determining decimal places and rounding To determine the number of decimal places and also allow rounding of key figures, press (F8) or choose Settings  Places after Decimal Point.  Copying key figure data from another information structure To copy data for a key figure from another information structure, choose Extras  Copy Data. The copy of the key figure data then goes from the source information structure for a given period to the target information structure.  Showing row totals To show the row totals, choose Settings  Show Row Totals.  Transferring all planning table data To transfer the entire data of the planning table to Excel, choose Extra  Microsoft Excel.  Moving up or down the planning hierarchy Use the following options to move up or down the planning hierarchy within the planning table: – Member – Owner – Next Member – Previous Member – All Members 11.3 Version Management In the information structure, the planning data is retained in versions. This enables the planner to carry out simulations. A planner can assign any number to a version, and 11 Sales and Operations Planning 586 these remain inactive, with the exception of two specific versions with defined mean- ings and functions: version 000 (which always contains actual data) and version A01 (which is the active version). You can directly update all of the following in the info structure against the respective version numbers:  Every version that is created and for which data is stored in the planning table  All of the values for each of the key figures  Characteristic combinations that are either entered manually or evaluated by mac- ros, events, or forecasts You can also copy one version’s data to another version and delete obsolete or unwanted versions, as we’ll discuss in the following sections.

sections.title: Copying a Version
sections.en: 11.3.1 Copying a Version You can use different menu paths or transactions to copy one version to another, depending on whether you’re using standard S&OP or flexible planning:  In standard S&OP Choose Logistics  Production  SOP  Planning  Version Management  Copy or use Transaction MC78.  In flexible planning Choose Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Version Management  Copy or use Transaction MC8V. Figure 11.41 Copy Planning Version © 587

sections.title: Forecasting
sections.en: 11.4 Forecasting To copy the PIR details of the source version to the target version of an information structure, you enter the values for the Version into the Source version and the Target version areas and then choose Copy at the top of the screen (see Figure 11.41). In this example, the values you enter in the Source version and Target version fields are “001” and “A00,” respectively. Alternatively, you can maintain the date range (the Period) to copy the PIR details of the source version to the target version.

sections.title: Deleting a Version
sections.en: 11.3.2 Deleting a Version You can use different menu paths or transactions to delete planning versions, depend- ing on whether you’re using standard S&OP or flexible planning:  In standard S&OP Choose Standard SOP: Logistics  Production  SOP  Planning  Version Management  Delete or use Transaction MC78.  In flexible planning Choose Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Version Management  Delete or use Transaction MC8W. To delete one or more versions of an information structure, check the relevant boxes next to the version numbers and choose Delete at the top of the screen.

sections.title: Scheduling a Copy Version or a Delete Version
sections.en: 11.3.3 Scheduling a Copy Version or a Delete Version To schedule a background job to delete or copy a planning version or activate an exist- ing planning version, use Transaction MC8K. To schedule a copy from one target ver- sion of one information structure to another information structure, use Transaction MC8Q. 11.4 Forecasting A forecast is a prediction of future requirements and is based on the past consumption trend over a given period of time. These consumptions can be, for example, material withdrawals against the production order, process orders, or even cost centers. When- ever a new material is created in the system, the system automatically incorporates the info in the Consumption tab in the Additional Data area of the material master. The consumption of finished goods can be against the sales order. Hence, forecasting offers enormous benefits for planning any material type—whether it’s a raw material, a con- sumable, a packing material, a semifinished good, or even a finished good—based on historical consumption figures. While each withdrawal or consumption updates the information in the system, a planner can also manually enter the past consumption figures for a material to help drive and facilitate forecasting. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 588 A detailed analysis of consumption figures provides a pattern for or other valuable information on consumption patterns—that is, a constant consumption model, a moving average model, or a seasonal trend model. Therefore, the system offers several standard forecasting models to choose from. If a planner isn’t sure which forecast model to select, then the system allows the planner to select Automatic Model Selec- tion to facilitate forecasting. However, using this option has its disadvantages. Warning SAP doesn’t recommend using Automatic Model Selection for forecasting because it eventually ends up incorporating one of the many forecast models (e.g., constant, mov- ing average, seasonal) available in the material master. The SAP-recommended fore- casting model may not be in line with the actual business scenario. Tips and Tricks When using the forecasting tool, you should take a couple of materials as test cases and run the entire forecasting cycle. This forecasting cycle includes maintaining histor- ical values, selecting the right forecasting model, and maintaining additional forecast- ing parameters. Finally, you should execute the forecast and see if the forecast results closely match what the system is expected to do. If it doesn’t, then consider evaluating other available forecasting models or refining the forecasting parameters. After gain- ing the necessary experience with a limited number of materials, you should replicate the same steps for other similar materials. Be sure to periodically review and update the forecasting parameters to ensure reliable material planning results. This section covers the forecasting options available in S&OP (which are standard and flexible planning), as well as the options that are available in the material master in case you want to run forecasting on a consumable material or a packing material, which isn’t part of S&OP. We’ll begin by covering the fields and options that are avail- able in the forecasting view of the material master (e.g., forecast models, configuring and using the forecast profile). Finally, we’ll cover the weighting group and splitting indicator that can be used in forecasting.

sections.title: Forecasting View in the Material Master
sections.en: 11.4.1 Forecasting View in the Material Master You can maintain the forecast parameters in the forecasting view of the material mas- ter by using Transaction MM02. Figure 11.42 shows the Forecasting view of Material 2264 for Plant 1710. Details of all the important fields on the screen are given in the sub- sequent sections of this chapter. © 589 11.4 Forecasting Figure 11.42 Forecasting View in Material Master When you run the forecast for a material-plant combination (Transaction MP30) or for the entire plant (Transaction MP38), the system uses information from the material master and offers no option to make changes to the forecast parameters. Note Navigate to the Forecast menu in SAP S/4HANA system via menu path Logistics  Pro- duction  Production Planning  Materials Forecast. You can also execute forecasting in the following planning areas:  S&OP for materials or for product groups  Flexible planning When you’re executing a forecast in either of these areas, the system provides you with the options to select the forecast profile and make changes to the default data defined in the forecast profile. You can also carry out forecasting for consumption-based mate- rials, and essentially, the concepts and fundamentals remain the same. Table 11.4 covers the fields shown in Figure 11.42. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 590 Fields Descriptions Forecast model This indicates the chosen forecast model. For example, forecast model N (no forecast/external model) enables the SAP S/4HANA system to create forecasts from external sources, such as an Excel file or even a legacy system. (See Section 11.4.2, in which we cover this topic in detail.) Period Indicator These are the periods in which the consumption values and the forecast values are managed. Fiscal Year Variant This is the fiscal year in which the consumption values and the forecast values are managed. RefMatl: consumption This is the consumption data on a material that the system uses to forecast values for a new material. If two or more materials have similar consumption histories, then one material can form a reference material for another, and yet, a multiplier can still apply to an individual material to account for any increase or decrease in consumption values and forecast values. (See the definition of Multiplier below.) Date to This is the date up to which the system uses the consumption values of the reference material to calculate new material fore- cast values. RefPlant:consumption This is the plant of the reference material. Multiplier This is the multiplication factor that the system uses for the ref- erence material’s historical values to calculate new material fore- cast values. Hist. periods This indicates the number of historical values the system uses for a material forecast. Forecast periods This indicates the number of period splits for which the forecast values should be created. Periods per season This indicates the number of periods that belong to a season. Use this option only if the forecast model is a seasonal forecast model. Initialization pds. This is the number of periods used for initialization. Initializing is resetting the system’s settings, which can be automatically or manually enabled. Initialization takes place when the material is forecast for the first time or when there are structural changes to a material’s time series, such as a change in a material’s forecast model, a change in historical values, or a change in forecast values. Table 11.4 Parameter Details of Forecasting Screen in Material Master © 591 11.4 Forecasting Fixed periods This is the number of periods for which the system won’t recalcu- late the forecast values in the next forecast. This option helps you prevent fluctuation in a forecast’s calculation and can help when the production can no longer react to changed planning figures within the planning horizon. Model selection This specifies whether the system should check for the values for a trend or seasonal fluctuation or for a trend and seasonal fluctu- ation. You should set this indication if you want the system to help with automatic forecast model selection. Optimization level This specifies the increment (rough, middle, or finer) by which the system will optimize the forecast parameters. Alpha factor This specifies the smoothing factor for updating the basic values. If you leave this field blank, the default value will be 0.2. Gamma factor (not shown) This specifies the smoothing factor for updating the seasonal index. If you leave this field blank, the default value will be 0.3. Tracking limit This specifies the amount by which (or the number of times) the forecast values may deviate from the actual values. Every time a forecast runs, the system compares the tracking limit with the tracking signal. The tracking signal, which is calculated internally, is the quotient and the mean average deviation. The system uses the tracking signal to control the accuracy of the forecasts. If the tracking signal is greater than the tracking limit, then the system issues an exception message and asks the planner to (re)check the selected forecast model. Selection procedure This defines the procedure by which the system selects the opti- mum forecast model. In the standard system, there are two pro- cedures that the system uses:  1: This uses a significant test to determine whether a trend or a seasonal pattern is present, and then, it selects the forecast model based on the results.  2: This uses more precise and granular calculation than proce- dure 1 but consumes more system resources. This procedure carries out the forecast by using all models, optimizes the parameters, and then selects the model with the smallest mean average deviation. Weighting group This indicates how many historical values are used to calculate the forecast and what weighting is given to each of the historical values. Fields Descriptions Table 11.4 Parameter Details of Forecasting Screen in Material Master (Cont.) 11 Sales and Operations Planning 592 Tips and Tricks When you implement SAP S/4HANA is implemented for the first time, there aren’t any historical consumption values available in the system that can form the basis of fore- casting. In such scenarios, you can manually enter the unplanned or total consumption quantities of a material. When you’re maintaining consumption values for a large number of materials, creating an upload program is a more practical approach. To manually enter the total consumption values into the material master (Transaction MM02), select the Additional Data button and then choose the Consumption tab. Also, define the plant for which the consumption values are maintained and for which fore- casting is being executed.

sections.title: Forecast Models
sections.en: 11.4.2 Forecast Models The purpose of forecast modeling is to aid in forecasting particular events. The forecast model is designed around factors that the client believes are important in influencing the future use of a material. The client also uses its past consumption of a material to determine the material’s future use. Both of these methods should produce a reliable forecast. A number of forecast models are available within SAP S/4HANA, as follows: Beta factor This specifies the smoothing factor for the trend value. If you leave this field blank, then the default value will be 0.1. Delta Factor (not shown) This specifies the smoothing factor for the mean average devia- tion. If you leave this field blank, then the default value will be 0.3. Reset automatically If you check this box, then the system automatically resets the forecast model if the tracking limit is exceeded. The system also selects a new model during the next forecast run. Param.optimization If you check this box, then the system optimizes the smoothing factors needed by the given forecast model. Also, parameter opti- mization is carried out for both the first forecast and subsequent forecasts. The system calculates a number of different parameter combinations and selects the one that produces the lowest mean average deviation. Correction factors If you check this box, the system calculates the forecast values by taking into account the correction factors, as defined in the con- figuration. Fields Descriptions Table 11.4 Parameter Details of Forecasting Screen in Material Master (Cont.) © 593 11.4 Forecasting  Constant model The constant model assumes that the use of material is constant. This isn’t to say that the use of material is the same each month but that the variation in material usage fluctuates little and a constant mean value has been calculated. This forecast model can apply to electricity consumption in an office, for example. Although elec- tricity consumption increases in summer months due to increased air conditioning use, the consumption doesn’t vary a great deal from the mean value over a longer period of time, such as a year.  Trend model The trend model is used when there is an identifiable increase or decrease in use of a material over a period of time. The trend may include areas of movement away from the trend, but the overall movement follows the trend. For example, a down- ward trend over time may represent the use of printer cartridges for top-selling printers that become obsolete over a short period of time, perhaps only 12 to 18 months. As the purchase and use of such printers decrease, the cartridges used in such printers will also slow.  Seasonal model The seasonal model affects many businesses due to the weather, holidays, and vaca- tions. The seasonal model is defined as a pattern that repeats during each period. For example, the annual seasonal pattern has a cycle that is 12 periods long, if the peri- ods are months. A seasonal model may be applicable to a company that makes patio furniture, which experiences a greater demand in the months of May through Sep- tember, and this pattern is repeated each year.  Seasonal trend model The seasonal trend model is similar to the seasonal model, except that instead of the same pattern occurring each period, the pattern is moving further away from the mean value, either positively or negatively. For example, California sparkling wine manufacturers can see a positive seasonal trend. They have a seasonal pattern in demand for their products, and for them, the seasonal pattern has a positive trend, as sales have continued to rise. A negative seasonal trend can be shown for beer manufacturers, who have a seasonal market, but the overall trend continues to be negative as sales slow each year. Tips and Tricks If a client hasn’t developed forecast models in the past or doesn’t know which forecast model fits best, it may want to allow the SAP S/4HANA system to analyze the historical data and determine an appropriate forecast model to use. Forecast model J (automatic model selection) is available in SAP S/4HANA. After the system selects a model, the cli- ent can use it as a starting point and make modifications in the future. However, it’s equally important to revisit the system-proposed-and-selected forecast model to ensure that it closely matches the consumption trend. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 594

sections.title: Forecast Profile
sections.en: 11.4.3 Forecast Profile A forecast profile consists of user-defined values (parameters), which then become avail- able during the execution of forecasting. You can, however, still make changes to these values (parameters) in the forecast profile before executing the forecast. To create a fore- cast profile, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Sales & Operations Planning (SOP)  Functions  Forecasting  Maintain Forecasting Profiles or use Transaction MC96. Figure 11.43 and Figure 11.44 show a standard available forecast profile, SAP, which you can copy to create a new profile and make desired changes. Figure 11.43 SAP Forecast Profile Details of the important fields in the forecast profile are given in the following list:  Frm Period/to Period This denotes the period or intervals for the historical data and forecast data that you’ll use during forecasting.  Forecast strat. This is the forecasting strategies/models that the system will use to carry out the forecast. Forecasting strategies are covered in Section 11.4.4.  Period Ind. This is the period or interval of the required future periods the forecasting will trigger. © 595 11.4 Forecasting Figure 11.44 SAP Forecast Profile: More Options  Periods/Season This is the number of periods in a season if there is a seasonal trend.  Factory Calend. The factory calendars specify holidays or the fiscal year variant. For this example, you assign factory calendar “US.”  Max.hist.values This is the maximum number of historical periods. For example, if you enter “12,” the system will consider the past 12 months of historical values to suggest forecast values.  Consumpt. You use this option to forecast consumption, as in standard S&OP’s level-by-level planning forecast. It is based on the material’s consumption history.  Forecast parameters This corrects historical values for the following (see Figure 11.44): – Alpha factor: This is used to smooth the basic value. – Beta factor: This is used to smooth the trend value. – Gamma factor: This is used to smooth the seasonal index. – Sigma factor: This is used to correct the historical figures that lie outside the pre- defined upper and lower tolerances. The lower the sigma factor, the greater the control of forecast figures. SAP recommends using a value between 0.6 and 2.0. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 596

sections.title: Forecast Strategy
sections.en: 11.4.4 Forecast Strategy Table 11.1 lists the relationships among various forecast models and their correspond- ing forecast strategies. For each forecast strategy (or model) selection, you maintain the details of the parameters, such as the alpha factor, beta factor, or gamma factor, which are highlighted in the Additional Information column of the table. For example, if demand for a product is generally constant throughout the year, then you select the constant model. Similarly, to maintain an average quantity of material in the ware- house, you use the moving average model, which adds up the total quantity of the material from historical figures and then divides this total quantity by the period under consideration to arrive at an average number. So, it’s important to evaluate the consumption figures for a given historical period that the forecasting model fits best to meet your business requirements. Time Series Development Forecast Models Forecast Strategies Additional Information Constant progression Constant model 10 The end results are constant figures. Constant progression Constant model with first-order exponen- tial smoothing 11 Maintain an alpha factor to smooth the basic values. Constant progression Constant model with automatic alpha smoothing factor adoption 12 This uses first-order exponential smoothing. Constant progression Moving average model 13 This averages out the values based on the number of historical values. Constant progression Weighted moving average model 14 Maintain a weighting group. Trend-like pat- tern Linear regression 20 This evaluates the trend in values to come up with plausible trend values. Trend-like pat- tern First-order exponential smoothing 21 Maintain alpha and beta factors to smooth out the basic and trend values. Trend-like pat- tern Trend model of second-order expo- nential smoothing 22 Maintain alpha and beta factors to smooth out the basic and trend values. Trend-like pat- tern Automatic alpha smoothing factor adoption 23 Maintain second-order exponential smoothing with or without model parameter optimization. Table 11.5 Forecast Models and Forecast Strategies © 597 11.4 Forecasting Figure 11.45 shows the dropdown list of some of the forecast strategies that you can choose from and assign in the forecast profile. Figure 11.45 Forecast Strategy Season-like pattern Seasonal model (winters procedure) 31 Maintain alpha factors, gamma factors, and periods per season for basic and seasonal smoothing. Season-like pattern Seasonal model of first-order exponen- tial smoothing 40 Maintain alpha, beta, and gamma fac- tors and periods per season. Automatic model selection 50 The system takes its own calculation and factors into account and determines/ suggests the appropriate model. Automatic model selection 52 This is more time and resource consum- ing, but it provides more reflective results. Historical data way 60 The system will use past or historical values as is, without automatically making any corrections or adjustments. Time Series Development Forecast Models Forecast Strategies Additional Information Table 11.5 Forecast Models and Forecast Strategies (Cont.) 11 Sales and Operations Planning 598

sections.title: Using the Forecast Profile
sections.en: 11.4.5 Using the Forecast Profile If you select Forecast Profile while executing the forecast, the Forecast: Forecast Profile popup will appear, and in it, you can select the relevant forecast profile. The system con- siders all of the forecast parameters during forecasting. If you still need to make any last- minute changes to the values defined in the forecast profile, then choose the Change option to make the desired changes and execute the forecast. To configure a new forecast profile, use Transaction MP80. We already covered how to execute a forecast in Section 11.2.6, so we’ll just summarize some of the more important topics of forecasting here. When you execute a forecast, the system calculates the mean average deviation, the trend value, and the error total of the forecast values. The mean average deviation option helps you identify the degree of variability and make a subsequent decision on buffering strate- gies to avoid stock-outs. For example, the system divides an error total of 44 by the mean average deviation value of 13 (44 ÷ 13 = 3.385) and compares it with the tracking limit set at 4.000 (in the material master) to decide whether the forecast model is still applicable or whether the situation warrants selecting a new or different forecast model. The error total is the sum of all errors in the past consumption series (refer to Figure 11.36). Tips and Tricks When you’re executing a forecast, be sure to check out the forecast error messages by double-clicking them and attending to them as needed. The graphical versions of the forecast figures show the following:  In yellow, the details of the historical corrected consumption values  In blue, the details of the historical originating consumption values, if any  In red, the originating forecasting values  In green, the corrected forecasting values, if any Tips and Tricks The forecast model is another important parameter because it tells the system what to look for: a trend pattern, constant consumption from period to period, or seasonality. You can also choose to let the system perform a number of tests so that it can auto- matically identify the pattern and set it after executing a forecast run. Here are three different ways you can perform forecasting on individual materials or a group of materials:  At a single material and plant level, use Transaction MP30.  At the total materials and plant level, use Transaction MP38.  At a single material and plant level, use Transaction MM02. © 599 11.4 Forecasting Tips and Tricks To view or print the consumption values and forecast values of individual materials or all materials in a plant, here are the transaction codes:  Individual material: Transaction MP32  Total forecast: Transaction MPDR

sections.title: Weighting Group
sections.en: 11.4.6 Weighting Group The weighting group defines how many of the consumption values the system takes into account during the forecast run and what weight to give to each value. Often, recent consumption values get higher weights than past values. The weighting group works only for the weighted moving average forecast model or forecast model W. You can also use this forecast model to set up the delta factor to smooth out the forecast values. To set up a weighting group, follow menu path SAP IMG  Materials Management  Forecast  Weighting Groups for Weighted Moving Average.

sections.title: Splitting Indicator
sections.en: 11.4.7 Splitting Indicator In forecast-based planning, it’s mandatory to execute material forecasts first. Based on the results of the forecast, the system calculates and plans future consumption quanti- ties. You can select the forecasting periods to be days, weeks, months, or entire years. The system can consider safety stock in the net requirement calculations. The system also takes receipts into account to ensure the demand from the forecast is met. If the demand isn’t met, then the system creates procurement proposals. You can specify the number of historical periods that the system will use in forecasting, and you can also specify the future forecast horizon (periods) for each material. After the planning run, the system makes material available at the beginning of the period. You have the option to further divide or split these material requirements into finer detail by using the Splitting Indicator option in the MRP 3 view of the material master. You can also separately define the period pattern for the forecast (day, week, month, or posting period) and the number of prediction periods for each material. However, the period pattern of the forecast may not be detailed enough for MRP because it may not provide the required granularity. For this reason, you should use the Splitting Indicator to define for each material how the forecast requirement values may further be distrib- uted to a more detailed period pattern for MRP purposes. You maintain the Splitting Indicator option in configuration by following menu path SAP IMG  Materials Management  Forecast  Define Splitting of Forecast Requirements for MRP. The splitting indicator contains the number of periods to be considered and the number of days and/or weeks for which the splitting is to be carried out. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 600 The following are the three possible forecast requirements:  No. of Days This is the number of periods that are to be calculated to the day in MRP.  No. of Weeks This is the number of periods that are to be calculated to the week in MRP.  No. of Periods This is the number of periods—in addition to the periods to be split—for which the system is to carry out the net requirements calculation in the planning run. Note As per SAP Note 2268100, SAP plans to replace the current material forecast solution in the future. The future solution will perform the forecasting algorithm in SAP HANA by utilizing SAP HANA’s forecasting library, thus improving material forecast perfor- mance. In the meantime, you can still use the material forecast transactions, such as Transaction MP30, as there is no functional equivalent yet in SAP S/4HANA.

sections.title: Rough-Cut Planning Profile
sections.en: 11.5 Rough-Cut Planning Profile To carry out capacity analysis in standard S&OP or in flexible planning, you need to maintain master data for the capacity requirements for production of a base quantity of the material or product group. This master data is known as a task list, and it can be a routing, rate routing, master recipe, or rough-cut planning profile. Normally, you cre- ate a rough-cut planning profile for the material-plant combination because it provides a rough estimate of the capacity requirements. Warning An important point to consider is that not every information structure created or used in S&OP has a plant as a characteristic. This makes capacity planning very difficult. For this reason, you should enter a planning plant into the Set Parameters for Info Struc- ture and Key Figures configuration screen (Transaction MC7F). Also, without reference to a plant and material, transfer of key figures to PIRs isn’t possible. Because capacity evaluation is performed at the plant level, we advise you to include it in the information structure and the percentage splits given to the information struc- tures for disaggregation and aggregation purposes. Also, on the Set Parameters for Info Structure and Key Figure screen (Transaction MC7F), you enter a key figure in the Capacity Figure field; the numbers for this key fig- ure in the planning table will be considered in capacity planning. For this example, we used the PRODU value in the capacity figure (refer to Figure 11.21). © 601 11.5 Rough-Cut Planning Profile Based on the details given in the rough-cut planning profile, the resource leveling cal- culates the resource load for the operations plan and thus enables you to quickly esti- mate feasibility in terms of capacity. Next, we’ll cover the rough-cut planning profile and how the system reflects pegged requirements.

sections.title: Creating a Profile
sections.en: 11.5.1 Creating a Profile To create a rough-cut planning profile, use SAP menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Tools  Rough Cut Planning Profile  Create or use Transaction MC35. On the initial screen of a rough-cut planning profile, enter Info structure “S655” and choose Execute, which opens the popup shown on the right-hand side of Figure 11.46. Enter all of the characteristic details and press (Enter), and the General Data screen shown in Figure 11.47 will appear. Figure 11.46 Initial Screen for Characteristic Assignments On this screen, enter “22” in the Time span field and enter “1” PC in the Base quantity field. Set the Status to “4” (for Released), set the Usage to “1” (for production), and then press (Enter). Tips and Tricks You can change the parameters on the General Data screen when needed. For exam- ple, you can change the number of working days to account for any national or unan- nounced holiday(s) in a month. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 602 Figure 11.47 General Data in Rough-Cut Planning Profile On the screen that appears, shown in Figure 11.48, double-click the first line of the Resource Table column. Then, in the Resource Type dialog box, choose Work center. The Resource popup will then appear. Enter “Turbine” for Work Center, “1710” for Plant, and “HR” for Unit. Repeat the same steps for another work center, “assembly.” Also add Material “2265” and a cost element to the rough-cut planning profile. Maintain the val- ues as shown in Figure 11.49 and save the entries. Figure 11.48 Rough-Cut Planning Profile © 603 11.5 Rough-Cut Planning Profile Figure 11.49 Rough-Cut Planning Profile with Relevant Data In Transaction MC94, the planning table displays the available capacity and capacity requirements via menu path Views  Capacity Situation  Rough-Cut Planning  Show (see Figure 11.50). Figure 11.50 Rough-Cut Planning View in Planning 11 Sales and Operations Planning 604 In the Planning Table area, the system shows the capacity requirements against the available capacity at the work center, based on the monthly production figures. The system immediately calculates the capacity utilization when the plan changes, and that facilitates the interactive adjustment of the operations plan to the capacities. How- ever, the resource-leveling process doesn’t provide any standard function for adapting the production plan to the available capacity. If a capacity is overloaded, a corresponding message is displayed in the footer. For this example, this is the case for 07/2024 (July 2024). There’s also a capacity overload for the next several months, as shown in the blue font in the monthly columns in the Capacity load row. If you scroll down in the same section, the capacity requirement details of the second work center will also appear. Use the same menu path—Views  Capacity Situation  Rough-Cut Planning  Show—to view the costs and material values and choose the relevant option.

sections.title: Pegged Requirements
sections.en: 11.5.2 Pegged Requirements Within the planning table, pegged requirements offer you a quick way to gain a better understanding of a workload on a resource—whether it’s a work center, a material, or a PRT—to evaluate pegging. The term pegging is synonymous with association or attach- ment, which means one planning element is attached to another. To view the pegged requirements within the planning table, you can choose Views  Pegged Requirements to open the Source of requirements screen (see Figure 11.51). Alternatively, you can double- click the Capacity Reqmt field shown in Figure 11.50 to make the screen shown in Figure 11.51 appear. Figure 11.51 Pegged Requirements © 605

sections.title: Events
sections.en: 11.6 Events 11.6 Events Major industry events, national holidays, sporting events, planned promotions, and festivals can affect sales quantities (with increases or decreases), so understanding events in advance will help you with planning efforts. You can increase or decrease the sales quantities using a functionality called events. The events functionality is applied to the sales key figures in all possible cases or to any other key figures that give sales quantities or sales values. The system provides the option to assign events across all characteristic levels or for a specific characteristic level to which the event should be applied. For example, let’s say that initially, a planner used the forecasting tool and seasonal model to come up with planned sales figures. Later, the company decides to participate in an important and relevant trade show that should have a positive impact on the company’s sales. To attend to this anticipated surge in product sales (or demand), a planner can incorporate this event (the trade show) into the planned sales figures to enable the system to account for the increase. The events are assigned to the planning types and thus come into play in the planning table. Note Creating an event isn’t a mandatory activity in the planning process, so a planner can opt out of the event option in the planning situation, if needed. The SAP S/4HANA system provides two ways of modeling an event: cumulative model- ing and percentage modeling. For cumulative events, the sales quantity affected by the event is maintained as a difference quantity in absolute figures. The overall sales quan- tity is represented as the total of normal sales and includes the event. For percentage events, the sales quantity affected by the event refers to the quantity planned in the Sales key figure. In the following sections, we’ll discuss how to create events and assign them to plan- ning. We’ll also show how an event’s assignment impacts materials planning.

sections.title: Creating Events
sections.en: 11.6.1 Creating Events To create events, you use menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning  Tools  Events  Create or use Transaction MC64. The initial screen on which to create an event is shown in Figure 11.52. For this example, you enter the Event Number as “S655: SOP EVENT” and then press (Enter). In the General Information popup, select the Proportional radio button and then press (Enter) again. On the Proportional Event screen shown in Figure 11.53, enter the monthly Change in percentage and then choose Assignment, which opens the Info Structure popup. In this popup, assign the Info Structure as “S655.” 11 Sales and Operations Planning 606 Figure 11.52 Creation of Event Figure 11.53 Assigning Event to Information Structure Now that an event is created, the next step is to assign the event.

sections.title: Assigning Events
sections.en: 11.6.2 Assigning Events When you’re done creating the event in the previous section, the screen shown in Figure 11.54 appears. On this screen, the characteristics of the information structure (such as Sales Organization and Division) automatically appear and are filled in, along with the other information shown. © 607 11.6 Events For this example, select the Sales key figure radio button and then select the A00 radio button for the active version. Finally, the system will propose the current data for the event, which you can change. Also, check the Status box to indicate that the event sta- tus is active. Finally, choose Assignments, which provides the list of event assignments that can be changed or even deleted (see Figure 11.55). Be sure to check the box located on the far left-hand side of this event so as to ensure correct assignment with the infor- mation structure. Figure 11.54 Assignment of Event Figure 11.55 List of Event Assignments 11 Sales and Operations Planning 608

sections.title: Events in Planning
sections.en: 11.6.3 Events in Planning Now that you’ve made all the necessary settings and assignments for the event, let’s test to see whether the planning results are reflective of the event in the planning type. The planning type table is a frame designed to enable the planner to plan the planning figures. Therefore, it makes sense to link the events in the planning type itself, so that when the planning type is used to plan on a planning table (using the key figures), the events come into play automatically. Figure 11.56 shows the planning type change screen (Transaction MC8B) for Planning type Z655 and Info structure S655. For this example, select the Sales radio button and choose the Event button from the menu bar so that the Stock level key figure appears for selection. Save the planning type. Figure 11.56 Events in Planning Type The next step is to change the existing plan (or create a new plan) for the same planning type Z655 and information structure S655 and see how the entered stock figures reflect the impact of an event (see Figure 11.57). In this step, you use a key figure from the existing set of key figures to represent events in the planning type. Normally, such a figure is an unused or unwanted key figure from the list. If a company already knows that it’s going to use events in planning, we suggest that you have one or two unwanted key figures that aren’t related to any planning activities and that you’ll exclusively use for purposes such as an event. If all of the key figures are already used in the planning type, then you can delete a key figure (by using Transaction MC8B) and then use it to add the event so that when you click the Event icon, the key figure will be shown as available for addition. © 609 11.6 Events In the planning type, place your cursor on a key figure (in a location where a planner wants the event-related key figures to appear—preferably below Actual Sales or Sales Quantities) and then choose Event. This action shows a list of unused key figures from which you can choose. Select one and press (Enter); the system will then draw in four other new event-related key figures in its place. In short, the system will require you to choose a key figure that can be overwritten with the four new key figures required for planning events. Figure 11.57 Events in Flexible Planning: Part 1 Figure 11.58 shows the change mode screen (Transaction MC94), on which you can manu- ally enter quantities for each month in the Sales field. Notice that the Proportional Event -> Sales field reflects the details of the event (event number: S655:SOP Event; not shown). Figure 11.58 Events in Flexible Planning: Part 2 11 Sales and Operations Planning 610 Note It’s very important to note that the impact of an event won’t be visible on the planning table unless a planner makes changes to quantities in the Sales field and saves the planning table. Then, the next time that a planner enters the planning table, the plan- ning table values that are populated in the event-based key figures will appear. For this example, those values are in the Corrected -> Sales field, which takes the percentage change (proportional) from the Proportional Event -> Sales row. Note Refer to SAP Note 2380568 for details about the simplification item that changed the scheduling parameters in configuration.

sections.title: Mass Processing in Sales and Operations Planning
sections.en: 11.7 Mass Processing in Sales and Operations Planning As a planner, you must maintain a large and diverse range of materials and products during the planning phase. In many planning steps, it’s crucial for you to perform the requisite functions online and interactively; however, you can automate a few plan- ning steps in the mass processing functionality of S&OP. Some of the mass processing steps that you can perform are listed here:  Forecasting  Determining the opening stock level  Running a macro  Transferring planning data to demand management  Copying key figure information from one information structure to another  Transferring S&OP key figure data to profitability analysis Because these steps eventually become routine work for a planner, it makes sense to use the mass processing function. In mass processing, you define activities that describe the individual steps that are car- ried out, as well as their assignment to mass processing jobs. Before creating a mass processing job in S&OP, you must create a planning activity. The following sections will discuss planning activity and setting up and scheduling mass processing jobs.

sections.title: Planning Activity
sections.en: 11.7.1 Planning Activity A planning activity is a function that a planner will execute through a given mass processing batch job. A planning activity consists of function profiles, which define © 611 11.7 Mass Processing in Sales and Operations Planning functions in detail. A planner will need to create each of the function profiles separately before creating the planning activity in SAP S/4HANA. To create a planning activity, you use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Sales & Operations Planning (SOP)  Functions  Mass Processing  Define Activities or use Transaction MC8T. In the Key F.plng.activity field, enter the name of the planning activity (for this exam- ple, “SOP”), the Planning Type (“Z655”), the planning activity type, and the correspond- ing activity profile that needs to be executed through this planning activity. For a given function that requires mass processing execution, choose the relevant activity type. After you select the relevant activity, the corresponding activity profiles, (such as Macro, Transfer Profile, and Copy Profile) become available to select from.

sections.title: Setting Up a Mass Processing Job
sections.en: 11.7.2 Setting Up a Mass Processing Job To set up the mass processing job, you need to perform the following steps in the given sequence: 1. Assign a name to the mass processing job. 2. Assign the information structure and version. 3. Assign the planning type. 4. Assign a name to the variant. 5. Select the selection screens. 6. Define the characteristic values, activity, and aggregation level. 7. Save the variant. To create the mass processing job, follow SAP menu path Logistics  Logistics Control- ling  Flexible Planning  Planning  Mass Processing  Create or use Transaction MC8D. (Use Transaction MC8E to change the job and Transaction MC8F to delete it.) Then, perform the following steps: 1. Create a planning job. For this example, enter the Job Number as “SOP_SAP” and a short description of the Job Name as “SOP_SAP_Flex_Plan.” 2. Choose Execute and maintain Info Structure “S655” and Version “A00” to apply the mass processing job to this information structure and version. 3. Choose Execute and maintain Plng.Type “Z655,” which is the specific planning type within the information structure with which the system will perform the mass pro- cessing function. 4. Select a planning type and then maintain the Variant Name for the selection screen details. 5. In the Create Variant field, enter the variant name, which in this example is “SOP_ Flex_Plan.” Then, choose Create to create the variant and enter the selection screen details. 1. Select the characteristic levels on which the system will run the mass processing job: the Aggregation Level and the Planning Activity. You configured the planning activ- ity in Section 11.7.1, and it consists of functions that the system performs through mass processing. It also contains the profiles of the respective functions that are configured. 2. Click the Attributes button to go to the Variant Attributes popup. 3. Check the relevant box to select whether to run the mass processing in the back- ground only. Save the variant attributes. 11 Sales and Operations Planning 612 6. Check the Created box and choose Continue to proceed with maintaining the selec- tion screen details. After you have finished creating the planning job, perform the following functions:

sections.title: Scheduling the Mass Processing Job
sections.en: 11.7.3 Scheduling the Mass Processing Job You can schedule the mass processing job by using Transaction MC8G. Here, you spec- ify the variant name and use the standard batch scheduling function. You can monitor mass processing jobs by using Transaction SM37. Use Transaction MC8I to obtain an overview of a specific job. To delete a mass processing job, use Trans- action MC8F.

sections.title: Standard Analysis in Flexible Planning
sections.en: 11.8 Standard Analysis in Flexible Planning Now that we’ve discussed most of the options and features available in flexible plan- ning, we’ll cover a lesser-known functionality of standard analysis in flexible planning. In Chapter 20, we’ll discuss how to take advantage of the large number of standard analysis reports that are available by using predefined and preconfigured information structures in all the logistics areas of the SAP S/4HANA system. However, this section covers a user-defined information structure (not a predefined information structure) that can still be used in standard analysis. User-defined standard analysis helps when a planner wants to evaluate all the desired key figures in one report (analysis report) by using one information structure. This eliminates the need to refer to two or more pre- defined information structures to obtain the desired information. These are the steps you perform to create a standard analysis in flexible planning: 1. Maintain a self-defined information structure (with Transaction MC21). 2. Set parameters for the information structure and key figures (with Transaction MC7F). 3. Create update rules for the information structure (with Transaction MC24). Maintain an update rule for each key figure and then choose Activate Updating. You may also use the Check icon to simulate update rules. Finally, choose the Generate icon to generate the update rules for the information structure. © 613 11.8 Standard Analysis in Flexible Planning In flexible planning, you update the rules for key figures within the information struc- ture so that when you’re running the standard analysis report, the system will bring up the relevant information. The step in which you define the update rule occurs right after you create and activate the information structure. Using Transaction MC24, you create the update rules for the characteristics and key figures of the information structure. The update rules enable the system to do the following:  Know which characteristics and key figures it must update when the user performs the business transactions.  Know the SAP tables from which it needs to fetch the data for given key figures and characteristics. They also include information for the dates the system should use for records or data that is populated in the structures. The update group is equally important because it defines the business areas and the tables that will be used for updating the information structure. You can maintain multiple update rules for a given key figure by copying an existing rule. The SAP S/4HANA system offers functional areas such as materials management, SAP S/4HANA Sales, production planning, quality management, project system, and plant maintenance, among others. Warning To ensure that the system isn’t overburdened, you should only include the key figures for which there is a need to update the information structure from SAP S/4HANA sys- tem tables in the application area. Note To update groups in configuration, follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics Information System (LIS)  Logistics Data Warehouse  Updating  Updating Definition  Specific Definition Using Update Rules  Maintain Update Rules. Before you complete the activation, check the consistency of the set update rules by choosing the Generate button to check for any errors or warning messages and elimi- nate them. The system won’t generate an information structure with errors, and no data will be populated in such an information structure. After you attend to the update rules, the next step is to configure the parameters to update data in the information structure. The period split must match the period for planning—that is, Set Planning Parameters for the Information structure (see Section 11.2.2). To configure the parameters, follow these steps: 11 Sales and Operations Planning 614 1. Perform the business transactions, such as creation of a sales order or production order. 2. Create the necessary transaction data in the SAP S/4HANA system to test the update of the information structure in the SAP tables. For example, you can create a sales order with an order quantity and order value (if these two key figures are used in the self-defined information structure). You can then see whether the data is automati- cally updated in the information structure (table) by using Transactions SE16N or SE11 and giving the details of the self-defined information structure. 3. Run a standard analysis of the self-defined information structure (Transaction MC9C). On the initial screen, enter self-defined information structure “S655” and version “A00” (actual data). Then, press (Enter), and on the next screen, the defined characteristics will be available to choose from (see Figure 11.59). Figure 11.59 Standard Analysis for Self-Defined Information Structure S655 Tips and Tricks Check the update log by using Transaction MC30. Enter the user ID and choose Exe- cute. Keep the cursor on the information structure number and choose the Details but- ton to check the data updated in detail. Then, choose the Analysis button to check the programs used in updating the information structure. Note All of the master data and transactions for flexible planning are listed in one place, under menu path Logistics  Logistics Controlling  Flexible Planning. © 615

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 11.9 Summary 11.9 Summary S&OP offers the potential for significant optimization of business processes by helping the planner use standard S&OP or flexible planning. For standard S&OP, the planner uses the product group to define the percentage or the proportional factor of each material within the group or hierarchy, whereas in flexible planning, the planner uses planning hierarchies. A planner can extensively use standard or self-defined macros and also create large numbers of planning versions. The final operations plan gets transferred to demand management via PIRs. The forecasting tool is also available in standard S&OP and in flexible planning to facilitate proposing realistic planning figures. Events help a plan- ner take any specific spike or dip in planning figures into account, and the rough-cut planning profile provides better visibility into capacities and materials commitments. A planner can perform a large amount of routine planning work by using the mass pro- cessing functionality, and they can also use flexible planning to perform standard analy- ses for the self-defined information structure. The next chapter covers demand management. ©

sections.title: Elements of Demand Management
sections.en: 12.1 Elements of Demand Management The main elements on which demand management is based can be broadly catego- rized into the following four areas:  Consumption modes and logic  Determining the requirement type  Availability checking  Total replenishment lead time We’ll discuss each of these in the following sections.

sections.title: Consumption Modes and Logic
sections.en: 12.1.1 Consumption Modes and Logic A consumption mode determines whether consumption of planned production quan- tities is to be carried out as backward or forward consumption, or whether both con- sumption types are allowed. In backward consumption, the system looks for the planned independent requirement (PIR) quantity that exists immediately before a sales order. In forward consumption, the system looks for the PIR quantity that exists immediately after a sales order. There’s also a need to maintain the consumption period in the MRP 3 view of the material master or in the configuration for demand management. The consumption period (forward or backward) is specified in workdays and is valid from the current date. Sales orders, material reservations, and dependent requirements then consume the PIR quantities that lie within the consumption period and after (for forward consumption) or before (for backward consumption) the require- ments date. In the consumption process, the system compares the PIR quantities and dates of materials with actual customer requirements or demand, such as sales orders. The con- sumption process is defined for every planning strategy by combining a requirements type from demand management and a requirements type from sales order manage- ment. This step means that the requirements type entered when maintaining PIRs is compared to the customer requirements type specified in configuration. To use a consumption mode, you must first maintain the Consumption Mode field in the material master record (in the MRP 3 view, as shown in Figure 12.1). © 619 12.1 Elements of Demand Management Figure 12.1 Consumption Mode in MRP 3 View of Material Master The Consumption Mode field offers the following five options, which are available as dropdown options when you place your cursor on the field and press (F4):  1: (backward consumption only)  2: (backward/forward consumption)  3: (forward consumption only)  4: (forward/backward consumption)  5: (period-specific consumption) The customer requirement consumes the PIR quantity that either directly precedes or follows it. If the PIR quantity directly preceding the customer requirements doesn’t cover the customer’s requirement, then the system looks for the next-closest PIR in the future or past. During this process, the system refers to the consumption periods main- tained in the material master or in configuration for demand management. The con- sumption periods determine the intervals (for backward or forward consumption) within which customer requirements consume PIRs. Customer requirements that lie outside the consumption period aren’t consumed. If no consumption periods have been maintained, then you can only consume requirements that are planned on the same day. The allocation of customer requirements to PIRs is carried out dynamically. This step means that if sales orders or planning are rescheduled, the allocation is deleted and redefined. In period-based consumption, sales orders, dependent require- 12 Demand Management 620 ments, or material reservations consume planned independent requirements that lie within the consumption period of the planned independent requirement. Tips and Tricks Although this isn’t always applicable, we suggest that the number of days entered for each period must realistically account for consumption to avoid overproduction to meet customer requirements. On the other hand, overproduction is sometimes encouraged to ensure maximum use of resources. The consumption intervals relate to the time granularity of PIRs.

sections.title: Determining the Requirements Type
sections.en: 12.1.2 Determining the Requirements Type A requirements type contains the control parameters to plan and consume PIRs and customer requirements. Different requirements types or combinations of require- ments types from PIRs and customer requirements are defined for each planning strat- egy (see Figure 12.2). Figure 12.2 Configuration Settings for Strategy 40 © 621 12.1 Elements of Demand Management Note There may not be a need for you to define any new strategies because SAP’s standard delivery provides all strategies to meet most business needs. There may only be a few cases that require you to define customized planning strategies, as covered in Section 12.7. If you want the system to automatically determine the requirements type when you’re creating the PIRs, then you should enter strategy groups for the corresponding materi- als in the material master record. If the strategy group allows a number of planning strategies, then the system proposes the requirements type from the main strategy. Referring to Figure 12.1, notice that Strategy Group 40 is assigned to the material. Figure 12.2 shows that for Strategy 40, the associated Reqmts Type of Indep.Reqmts is “VSF” (Planning with Final Assembly). The associated Reqmt Type of Customer Reqmt is “KSV” (Sales Order with Consumption). Planning strategies represent the business procedures planners use to plan and pro- duce a material. In the system, planning strategies are recorded in configuration by a combination of requirements types from demand management and sales order man- agement (refer to Figure 12.2). This combination determines either the consumption of PIRs by customer requirements or how the availability check is to be carried out. By assigning a planning strategy to a particular material, the system determines the correct requirements type (and all the control parameters of the requirements class) when creating PIRs or sales orders. The following assignments must exist so that the system can assign the requirements classes to PIRs and sales orders:  A planning strategy must be assigned to a strategy group (from demand manage- ment) so that a material can be planned with various planning strategies.  One main strategy and up to six alternative strategies must be assigned.  A strategy group must be assigned to the material (in the material master record) so that the system can automatically determine the correct requirements type.  A planning strategy must be assigned to the MRP group (assigned in the configura- tion for demand management) in which the MRP group is assigned to the material in the material master record. Figure 12.3 shows how various elements of requirement classes and planning strategy link up with each other in. In Section 12.7, we’ll cover the step-by-step approach to con- figuring the elements shown in Figure 12.3. 12 Demand Management 622 Figure 12.3 Relationships among Requirements Type, Requirements Class, and Planning Strategy Table 12.1 shows how requirements types for independent requirements link up with requirements types for customer requirements. Refer to Figure 12.2 and the associated strategy 40; the same details of strategy 40 are covered in Table 12.1. Strategies Requirements Types for Independent Requirements Requirements Types for Customer Requirements 10 LSF MTS production KSL Sale from stock without independent requirement reduction 11 BSF Gross PIRs KSL Sale from stock without independent requirement reduction 20 KE Individual sales order without con- sumption 2264 KEK MTO with configurable material 26 KEL MTO for material variants 30 LSF MTS production KL Sales order manufactured by lot size 40 VSF Planning with final assembly KSV Sales order with consumption 50 VSE Planning without finalassembly KEV MTO with consumption 52 VSE Planning without finalassembly KSVS Sales order with consumption Table 12.1 Planning Strategy Assignments Requirement Class Planned Independent Requirements Requirement Class Customer Requirements Requirement Type Planned Independent Requirements Requirement Type Customer Requirements Planning Strategy Planning Strategy Group Material © 623 12.1 Elements of Demand Management

sections.title: Availability Check and Total Replenishment Lead Time
sections.en: 12.1.3 Availability Check and Total Replenishment Lead Time A crucial element of effective production planning is the way in which a material’s availability is checked in the sales order and how the demand is transferred from the delivery proposal to the production schedule. To set up this process, you maintain an availability check and a total replenishment lead time in the MRP 3 view of the material master that also includes the strategy group and the consumption rules. The availabil- ity check with its associated rule and scope of check will drive what happens when a sales order is entered using Transaction VA01. On sales order creation, an availability check is triggered; if the customer’s requested delivery date can’t be met, a view with a delivery proposal pops up. The delivery proposal attempts to address the following questions: 54 VSE Planning without finalassembly KEKT MTO with consumption 55 VSE Planning without finalassembly KELV MTS with consumption without final assembly 56 VSE Planning without final assembly KEKS MTO with consumption of character- istics planning 59 VSEB Planning; phantom assemblies 60 VSEV Planning the plan- ningmaterial KEVV Individual customer with planning material consumption 63 VSEV Planning the plan- ningmaterial KSVV MTS with consumption of planning material 65 VSEV Planning the plan- ningmaterial ELVV MTO variant with planning material 70 VSFB Planning for assemblies 81 KMSE Assembly planned order 82 KMFA Assembly with production order 89 VSE Planning for assemblies KMSE Assembly planned order Strategies Requirements Types for Independent Requirements Requirements Types for Customer Requirements Table 12.1 Planning Strategy Assignments (Cont.) 12 Demand Management 624  Is a one-time delivery in full on the customer-requested delivery date possible?  When is the complete delivery of all requested items possible?  When can partial deliveries be made? The availability check in a sales order shows two dates separated by a forward slash (/). The first date is the ship date, and the second is the material availability date that is rel- evant for MRP and will show in Transaction MD04 if some other conditions are met and the dates and quantities are fixed. Depending on what proposal is selected and agreed upon with the customer, you can check the box next to the delivery proposal to confirm the dates. Those dates will then be relevant for production and materials plan- ning, and everything in planning will work toward meeting these dates. How these dates are determined depends on the scope of the check. A combination of the availability check (set in the MRP 3 view of the material master) and the checking rule (set according to the option chosen to carry out the availability check) determines the delivery date to the customer. Other factors that can be defined are objects, parameters, and elements that the system considers (or doesn’t consider) in the procedure to check for availability. One of the most relevant settings in the scope of the check is the planner’s decision to check with or without total replenishment lead time. Total replenishment lead time is a measure primarily used in MTO because it’s the time it takes to replenish the product in question if there is nothing readily available in stock. If the system checks with total replenishment lead time (if the Scope of Check box is unchecked), the availability check only looks out to the end of the lead time. If it doesn’t find enough quantity to fulfill the demand in that period, it confirms any quantity just after the total replenishment lead time. Subsequently, the next MRP run will generate an additional order proposal to ful- fill the demand. In the next sections, we’ll discuss various planning strategies for MTS and then do the same for MTO. We’ll also cover strategies relevant to ATO, planning components, and planning strategies relevant to variant configuration.

sections.title: Strategies for Make-to-Stock Production
sections.en: 12.2 Strategies for Make-to-Stock Production The planning strategies explained in this section are designed for planning procure- ment (production or purchasing) of components by planning the final products. You should choose an MTS strategy if the following are true:  The materials aren’t segregated; that is, they aren’t assigned to specific sales orders.  Costs need to be tracked at the material level and not at the sales order level. If the stock is produced independently of sales orders, you should always use MTS produc- tion because it’s then possible to immediately provide goods to customers from that stock. You may even want to produce goods without having sales orders in anticipa- tion of the fact that there may be customer demand in future. This means that MTS © 625 12.2 Strategies for Make-to-Stock Production strategies can support a close customer-supplier relationship because the objective is to quickly provide customers with goods from the stock. Returns that have passed quality inspection and other unexpected GRs can be used for other sales orders. You can also avoid maintaining unreasonably high stock levels by doing the following:  Create a production plan in advance (in demand management) to plan the stock. If you use this option, then you should also decide whether sales orders exceeding the production plan are to affect the actual production or not.  Receive sales orders as early as possible (e.g., using scheduling agreements). MTS strategies generally consist of up to five stages. Table 12.2 illustrates which stages are involved in which strategies: 1 denotes allocations and 2 denotes reduction of PIRs. A PIR of a finished goods material is a planning quantity that the company expects to sell in the future but wants to preproduce earlier on. Let’s now discuss the MTS planning strategies in detail.

sections.title: Strategy 40: Planning with Final Assembly
sections.en: 12.2.1 Strategy 40: Planning with Final Assembly This strategy is probably the most widely used MTS strategy. If a planner can forecast production quantities for the final product, then it makes sense for the planner to use this planning strategy. PIRs are consumed by incoming sales orders so that the master plan is always adjusted to suit the current requirements situation. Therefore, the important feature of this planning strategy is that a planner can react quickly to a cus- tomer’s requirements. The smoothing of the master plan is less important here. More- over, strategy 40 has the following qualities:  PIRs are entered at the finished product level, and they trigger the procurement and production of the necessary assemblies and components before receipt of the sales orders. Stages in Order Planning Strategies Strategy numbers 40 30 10 11 52 63 Demand management (creation of PIRs) Yes No Yes Yes Yes Yes Procurement before sales Yes No Yes Yes 2 Yes Yes Sales order Yes 1 Yes Yes Yes Yes 1 Yes 1 Procurement after sales No Yes No No Yes Yes Goods issue (GI) for delivery and reduction of PIRs Yes Yes Yes Yes 2 Yes Yes Table 12.2 Five Stages of MTS Strategies 12 Demand Management 626  It’s possible to use information from the sales forecast, information from the sales information system, or other planning tools to plan production quantities.  As soon as the sales order is received, it consumes the PIRs.
