Figure 12.19 Creation of Independent Requirements © 663 12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management When you drill down in the Requirements type field (by pressing (F4) while keeping the cursor on the field), the system reveals a large number of standard requirement types to choose from that are specific to the customer. If you’ve assigned a strategy group in the MRP 3 view of the material master, the system automatically assigns a requirement class to the customer-independent requirements. Note While the system automatically fills in the Requirements type field whenever you assign a strategy group to the material master, you can overwrite what’s in the field if you’ve defined alternative strategies in the strategy group. Press (Enter) and the screen in Figure 12.20 appears. Here, you enter Material number “2264” and an Order Quantity of “100” for Plnt “1710.”. Then, save the customer inde- pendent requirements, and the system will generate an internal number for the cus- tomer requirement, which in this example is 50000008. Figure 12.20 Independent Requirements with Requirement Type KSV Next, run the MRP to see how the system accounts for the customer requirements in the planning run.

sections.title: Planning and Stock/Requirements List for Independent Requirements
sections.en: 12.8.3 Planning and Stock/Requirements List for Independent Requirements To plan for the independent requirement, run the MRP on material 2264 and plant 1710 by using Transaction MD01N. To evaluate the stock/requirements list, including independent requirements after the MRP run, you use the menu path Logistics  Production  Production Planning  Demand Management  Environment  Stock/Requirements List or use Transaction MD04. As shown in Figure 12.21, the customer requirement is shown as CusReq in the MRP Ele- ment column. 12 Demand Management 664 Figure 12.21 Results of MRP with Customer Requirements

sections.title: Total Independent Requirements: Evaluation
sections.en: 12.8.4 Total Independent Requirements: Evaluation At any given time, you can evaluate the total PIRs of a material and its consumption. The system displays the Planned Qty. as available in the system, whereas the Total Assignment column shows the consumption figures. Use menu path Logistics  Produc- tion  Production Planning  Demand Management  Evaluations  Display Total Reqmts or use Transaction MD73. Figure 12.22 shows the initial view. Here, you enter Material “2264,” enter Plant “1710,” and select the Output List 1 option from the dropdown list. Press (Enter) and the Display Ind Requirements with Assigned Cust. Requirements view shown in Figure 12.23 will appear. Here, the monthly planned quantity previously entered is visible in the upper half of the view, along with the customer requirement. When a planner has maintained only the dynamic consumption, then this is shown in the Total Assgmnt column. If the entire process is finished—that is, if the sales order has been delivered—then the system shows the quantity in the Withdrawal Qty col- umn. © 665 12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management Figure 12.22 Initial Screen for Total Requirements of Material 2264 Figure 12.23 Total PIR and Requirements for Material 2264 12 Demand Management 666

sections.title: Total Independent Requirements: Reorganization
sections.en: 12.8.5 Total Independent Requirements: Reorganization Although the PIRs are automatically reduced, a planner must regularly and periodically evaluate the PIRs in the system to see if they need to be eliminated (or adjusted). This helps ensure that old and irrelevant planning data no longer becomes part of the plan- ning process during the MRP run. To evaluate the PIR, you use menu path Logistics  Production  Production Planning  Demand Management  Environment  Independent Requirements: Reorganization  Adjust Requirements or use Transaction MD74. In the initial view, you maintain the ini- tial parameters so that when the system is executed, it only brings up the relevant results for consideration and action. Notice that you have the flexibility to evaluate the results of the organization in Test Mode (No database changes) (see Figure 12.24). This lets you evaluate the results in simulative mode before performing actual reorganization. Figure 12.24 Initial Screen for Reorganizing PIRs The Key Date field refers to the date until which the system should bring up the PIR data for reorganization. For this example, if 07/01/2025 (July 01, 2025) is maintained, then the system should consider all of the PIRs until the month of July in 2025. It’s important to highlight that such PIR reorganization is only ensured for past and old PIRs, ones the © 667 12.8 Business Processes in Demand Management company no longer wants to plan or account for in demand management. The Rel. Key Date field specifies the relative key date, with reference to today, for which the demands will be processed. For example, you enter if −200 days, then the system selects and processes all demands that lie 200 days in the past and earlier. Tips and Tricks To define the plant-level PIR reorganization, you use Transaction OMP8, wherein you define the number days is defined. The system then subtracts these days from today’s date to come up with the reorganization date, and then it reorganizes all PIR data older than the reorganization date. After you maintain the initial parameters, choose Execute to open the view shown in Figure 12.25. This view brings up the data for the last 200 days in which the system was able to find PIRs for the materials and in plant 1710, which contains quantities and other materials and their associated quantities. After you perform evaluation, you can organize (delete) the quantities of the relevant materials. Figure 12.25 Simulative Results of PIR Organization

sections.title: Planned Independent Requirements: Reduction
sections.en: 12.8.6 Planned Independent Requirements: Reduction PIRs are reduced during GI, and unlike during consumption, the PIRs are actually reduced and not just consumed. During MTS production using strategy 10, the oldest PIR is reduced first. However, the reduction can also affect PIRs in the future if relevant 12 Demand Management 668 strategies are used with consumption, such as reduction in consumption strategies 40, 50, 60, and so on. Here, reduction simply takes place according to the current consumption situation when you post the GI. For this strategy to work, you need to set the configuration data accordingly. You perform the configuration using Transaction OMJJ. For nonconsump- tion strategy 10, this setting is available in the requirements class. This transaction is in the domain of MM, wherein you make relevant settings per movement type. For non- consumption strategy 10, these settings are available in the requirements class. After executing Transaction OMJJ, you provide the movement type—for example, “601” (GI against delivery)—in the Updating Control area, as shown in Figure 12.26. Check the Ind. Rqmts Reduction box to enable the system to reduce the PIRs at the time of GI (e.g., movement type 601) against a sales order. During assembly planning, the PIR is reduced when goods are withdrawn for the production order. Figure 12.26 Configuration Settings of PIR Reduction Table 12.6 provides an overview of common (selected) planning strategy types for which the system reduces the PIRs, based on specific transactions, such as GI against a sales order or production order. © 669

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 12.9 Summary 12.9 Summary Demand management acts as a central point to account for both planning for produc- tion processes and consumption for sales processes. If you use the right strategy type for each material based on its production process, demand management can optimize business processes not only by helping you enter realistic planning quantities such as PIRs but also by showing you how they relate to the automatic and dynamic adjust- ment of PIRs upon receipt of sales orders and their further processing, including GI. The flexibility to assign different planning and consumption strategies broadly mitigates the insecurities and other risks involved. At the same time, the system keeps a realistic check on the quantities entered in PIRs compared to the actual demand situation by making appropriate adjustments. The next chapter covers MRP. Planning Strategies Planning Strategy Descriptions PIR Reductions 10 MTS production GI (sales order, MTS) 20 MTO production GI sales order 30 Production by lot size GI (sales order, MTS) 40 Planning with final assembly GI sales order 50 Planning without final assembly (MTO) GI sales order 52 Planning without final assembly (MTS) GI production order 60 Planning with planning material (MTO) GI sales order 63 Planning with planning material (MTS) GI production order 70 Subassembly planning GI production order 82 Assembly planning with production order GI sales order Table 12.6 Relationships between Selected Strategy Types and PIR Reductions ©

sections.title: Process Overview
sections.en: 13.1 Process Overview MRP consists of the following steps, as shown in Figure 13.1: 1 Check the planning file to avoid using planning materials that aren’t needed. 2 Determine the material shortage quantity (or surplus quantity) by carrying out a net requirements calculation. 3 Calculate the procurement quantity to include lot sizes for planned orders or pur- chase requisitions. 4 Schedule basic dates or lead times (for in-house production only). 5 Select the source of supply by determining the procurement proposal. 6 Determine the requirement for subordinate parts on the basis of the BOM explosion by carrying out a dependent requirements determination. Figure 13.1 Process Steps in MRP Production Order Creation Capacity Requirements Planning Purchase Requisition Check Planning File Scheduling Dependent Requirement Determination Procurement Quantity Calculation Material Requirements Planning Schedule Lines Planned Order Sales Order Planned Independent Requirements Long-Term Planning Demand Management Sales and Distribution Net Requirements Calculation Fixed Planned Order Determine Procurement Proposal © 673 13.1 Process Overview During an SAP S/4HANA system implementation project—or even later on, when your company wants to start taking advantage of planning tools such as MRP—you should follow a step-by-step approach to identifying materials to plan with MRP. You can plan just about any kind of material with MRP. Broadly speaking, as a planner, you should consider the following factors and take the following steps: 1. To begin with, prepare a comprehensive list of all materials in a Microsoft Excel file. This Excel file may be a template that the SAP S/4HANA system consultant will make available. Materials can be finished goods, components, assemblies, raw materials, packing materials, consumables, or maintenance spares. You can also plan materials with special procurement types, such as subcontracting materials, phantom assem- blies, pipeline materials, procurement from another plant, production in another plant, stock transport orders (for interplant transfer), and so on. 2. Separate the materials that you want to plan from those that aren’t worth your time and effort or the cost involved in planning. Then, delete the latter from the Excel file. 3. Once you’ve got the materials that you want to include in planning, separate them further by classifying them into A, B, and C types. Type-A materials are high-value ones and generally include finished goods, semifinished goods or assemblies, raw materials, and packaging materials. Type-B and type-C materials are medium- to low-value materials, such as consumables and routine maintenance spares. Using deterministic replenishment procedures for all type-A materials, you individually evaluate each and every important factor involved in MRP to see how these fit into plan- ning to provide the desired results. For example, as a planner, you should ask the follow- ing questions:  What should the safety stock of this material be?  How many days in the planning window are needed so that the system doesn’t make any automatic changes to the production (or procurement) plan?  When there is a requirement for a material, how much should the system plan (exact quantity, minimum quantity, maximum quantity, etc.)?  How should the incoming sales orders consume the planning figures?  Is this material MTS or MTO?  Is there a need to produce this material for a specific project or customer (via MTO)?  Is this assembly produced in house or at another plant of the company?  How much scrap (as a percentage) does the production of this material generate for a product or subassembly, and therefore, can the scrap be incorporated into plan- ning?  Does it make sense to first forecast material requirements by using a forecasting tool and then adjusting the forecast values before proceeding with material plan- ning? 13 Material Requirements Planning 674 You need to ask these (and many more) questions for each material and correspond- ingly enter or update the information in the Excel file. For raw materials or packaging materials, which are externally procured, you need to get answers to questions such as the following:  What is the planned delivery time of this material?  What is the GR processing time?  How much underdelivery and overdelivery of a material is allowed?  Is there a scheduling agreement or a contract (for quantity or value of a material) with a supplier?  Is there a quota arrangement in place if there are multiple vendors of the same material? We suggest involving procurement personnel from MM to help with answering these (and many more) questions because they have firsthand knowledge of and experience with procurement. For type-B and type-C materials, you should consider using consumption-based plan- ning or stochastic replenishment procedures. In consumption-based planning, you choose whether to use reorder-based planning, which involves manually or automati- cally suggesting the reorder point of each material based on historical consumption. For manual reorder point planning, you enter the reorder point quantity, which may be based on past experience or consumption figures available from another planning program (not the SAP system). Furthermore, you can take advantage of the forecasting tool to help you plan future consumption. Forecasting also uses historical consump- tion data that’s available in the system. Because planning is a continuously evolving and ongoing process, we recommend that you continue fine-tuning the planning parameters of materials to help the system come up with planning results that can be used in the production and procurement processes almost immediately (and without need for significant adjustments). For example, say that when you first entered the planned delivery time of a material, it was five days, but as the system built up a significant database of planned delivery times in the past year, the average delivery time turned out to be seven days. You must update this information in the MRP view of the material master so that the future planning results of MRP are more reflective of this reality. The system also makes available a tool (Transaction WPDTC) to provide a comparison between the information maintained in the material master and the actual planned delivery time that the system records for each delivery. Several such analysis reports and tools are available in the SAP S/4HANA system. Whether you’re using MRP or consumption-based planning—that is, deterministic or stochastic replenishment procedures, respectively—a large number of factors are defined in the four different MRP views of the material master to influence the plan- © 675 13.1 Process Overview ning results of MRP. In fact, the system refers to this detailed information in the MRP views to come up with procurement proposals. Some of these factors may be how the system performs the net requirements calculations, what percentage of safety stock you should consider in planning, the lot-sizing procedures to use, whether forecasting is a necessary prerequisite to planning, and how to manage special procurement types. In the following sections, we’ll cover the important factors and parameters you’ll need to set up to achieve the desired planning results from MRP, which you can subse- quently use in production and procurement activities. Figure 13.2 provides an overview of the role of MRP in the planning and production areas of the SAP system. Figure 13.2 Production Planning The planning process starts with requirements from SAP S/4HANA Sales; from the next-generation cloud-based SAP HANA tool known as SAP Integrated Business Plan- ning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP); and from the S&OP planning process as a forecast, which eventually becomes a part of demand management. Companies can also use master production scheduling for planning finished products, important semifinished products, and products that will use bottleneck production lines. After the master pro- duction scheduling planning run, you can use the capacity leveling functionality in Demand management Purchase requisitions Purchase orders Planned orders Production orders Production planning and detailed scheduling (PP/DS) Forecast Capacity requirements planning (CRP) Master production scheduling (MPS) Material requirements planning (MRP) Sales and operations planning (S&OP) SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain (SAP IBP) 13 Material Requirements Planning 676 capacity requirements planning to ensure that the plan proposed by master produc- tion scheduling will not exceed the capacity of critical resources. An alternative to mas- ter production scheduling is to use the PP/DS tool, which is now embedded into the SAP S/4HANA digital core. PP/DS supports advanced planning features that are not available in master production scheduling or MRP, such as planning with finite capac- ity (i.e., considering capacity restrictions on the work centers), planning considering batch shelf life, planning with complex heuristics, and even using the PP/DS optimizer tool to make a feasible plan based on cost considerations. The system also takes customer requirements into account and reflects all demand from demand management as PIRs. A PIR with an active version consists of quantities of a material for which a planner wants to run MRP. The system runs MRP based on the details given in the demand program, and the outcome of the MRP run is the creation of procurement proposals. These procurement proposals can be planned orders (order proposals), purchase requisitions for external procurement, or delivery schedules for scheduling agreements with vendors. Procurement proposals are divided into two sep- arate yet interlinked areas: procurement for externally procured materials and produc- tion for in-house produced materials. Figure 13.3 shows how to categorize materials for planning purposes into two major subareas: deterministic and stochastic replenishment procedures. Figure 13.3 MRP and Consumption-Based Planning Material Requirements Planning Forecast Procedures Stochastic Replenishment Procedures (Type B and C Materials) Deterministic Replenishment Procedures (Type A Materials) Complete BOM Explosion No BOM Explosion Master Schedule Items Reorder Point Planning Forecast- Based Planning Time- Phased Materials Planning MRP Consumption- Based Planning © 677 13.1 Process Overview Tips and Tricks Transactions MC40 and MC41 are quite helpful for letting you as a materials planner separate materials into A, B, and C types based on their usage or consumption values (from the past) or requirement values (in the future). For example, you can use ABC analysis to classify those customers that generate 75% of sales volume as A customers, the next 15% as B customers, and the rest as C customers. Similarly, you can classify materials by aggregating their consumption values (or sales volumes) of materials until they reach 80% (or whatever percentage you define) of the total value and then designate all those materials as type-A items. You can then incorporate these ABC analyses into the ABC Indicator field in the MRP1 view of the material master. Note the A, B, and C indicators that can be made part of their respective material masters in Figure 13.4. You access the first screen shown in Figure 13.4 with Transaction MC40, and it shows an ABC analysis based on consumption/usage values 1. You access the second screen with Transaction MC41, and it shows an ABC analysis based on requirements values 2. Figure 13.4 ABC Analysis Using Transactions MC40 and MC41 13 Material Requirements Planning 678 Let’s now discuss both types of material replenishment procedures—deterministic and stochastic (or consumption-based)—in greater detail, along with an overview of the MRP types associated with them:  Deterministic replenishment procedures and MRP types (type A materials) You use deterministic planning to plan for the future; that is, the MRP run will create order proposals in a quantity and for a time period exactly as required, without tak- ing any elements of probability or randomness into account. For example, a material that is planned using MRP type PD (see Section 13.2.1) will always create a proposal for the exact quantity to meet the material demand and at the time it’s needed. This MRP type usage is most prevalent due to its easy setup because it doesn’t involve any automation. To add some quantity buffer to this plan- ning type to ensure enough stock will be available during the replenishment lead time, as well as to prevent stock-out, you maintain a safety stock. But because the planning of each individual material demands equal and individual consideration of various other factors, such as whether the material’s demand is high or low and whether the lead time to replenish is long or short, this MRP type isn’t effective when used across large numbers and diverse types of materials. MRP type PD is planning deterministically. (SAP calls this MRP.) PD simply looks at demand in the future and covers it with a procurement proposal that has a delivery date just before the demand occurs. Other deterministic MRP types are P1, P2, P3, P4, and D1. They require you as a planner to set a planning time fence, and they help you by keeping planned orders that are generated by the MRP run outside that period of time. These MRP types can also protect a short-term production program and are fixed automatically when they fall within the frozen zone. You use MRP type M0 to plan important finished (or unfinished) products separately from the rest. It has its own master production scheduling planning run and is meant to cut down on pro- cessing time before the plan is released to the BOM structure. As in the P series, the M1, M2, M3, and M4 types allow for the protection of a planning time fence and auto- matic fixing of procurement proposals. MRP types D1 and D2 (which are based on demand-driven replenishment) work the same as MRP type M1, with the difference that stocks are replenished to the maximum level.  Consumption-based replenishment procedures and MRP types (type B and type C materials) These procedures are also called stochastic and have some degree of randomness and an element of probability. The future planning of their demand and correspond- ingly the supply availability of these materials is based on past consumption pat- terns. Hence, there is a probability and randomness to this process that can be buff- ered with safety stocks, capacity, or time. In stochastic planning procedures, the system tries to cover future requirements by making stock levels available that fulfill these future requirements with the least amount of inventory investment. That is why consumption-based replenishment © 679

sections.title: Deterministic Replenishment Procedures
sections.en: 13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures policies underlie an estimating process. Here, the safety stock settings for these pro- cedures play an equally important role. Because future stockholdings are predicted to the best of the procedure’s capabilities and because there is some level of random- ness and variability in demand, a buffer in the form of safety stock in most cases is indispensable to avoid stock-outs. The need for a buffer level depends on the length of the replenishment lead time, the variability of that lead time, the variability in demand, and a desired, stipulated ser- vice level that communicates the planner’s percentage of acceptable stock-outs to the system. Using these parameters, the system—or for manual procedures, the plan- ner—can calculate a safety buffer that should deliver the expected results in terms of availability and stockholding. The first groupings of consumption-based replenish- ment procedures are the reorder point types. Reorder-level planning is a consump- tion-based method because it requires inventory to be available at all times and doesn’t wait until there is demand before triggering replenishment. The next group- ing is forecast-based planning procedures, such as MRP type VV. Finally, the third grouping is time-phased planning procedures, such as MRP types R1 and R2. In the next section, let’s discuss the deterministic replenishment procedures and the associated MRP types available in the SAP S/4HANA system. The section after that will cover stochastic replenishment procedures and their associated MRP types. 13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures When you as a planner decide to plan a material using MRP, one of the first parameters to define in the material master is the MRP type. An MRP type is a control function you use to control several subsequent steps. For example, if you assign the MRP type ND, for no planning, to a material, then the system won’t plan the material at all. If you use MRP types that start with P (e.g., PD, P1, P2), they control how the system takes the net requirements calculations into account and how it firms procurement proposals for MRP during MRP runs. The net requirements calculation takes the plant stock and all receipts into account and then subtracts the requirement quantities. If the net require- ments calculation comes up with a shortage or deficit quantities, then the system cre- ates procurement proposals during the MRP run. In the net requirements calculation, the system determines the available stock based on the following formula: Plant stock – Safety stock + Open order quantity (purchase orders, production orders, fixed procurement proposals) – Requirement quantity (PIRs, customer requirements, material reservations, forecast requirements for unplanned additional requirements) = Available stock If it evaluates that there is no shortage, then the system doesn’t create procurement proposals. 13 Material Requirements Planning 680 Note We’ll cover only the standard materials replenishment procedures and their associated MRP types, to help you develop the necessary foundations and understanding required to use them before opting for customization.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type PD
sections.en: 13.2.1 Material Requirements Planning Type PD MRP type PD is planning deterministically. In its initial configuration, PD may also include a forecast of unplanned consumption (see the Forecast Ind. and Cons. Ind. Fore- cast fields). When a forecast model is maintained in the Forecasting screen, the PD will forecast unplanned consumption (the unplanned consumption can be seen in the con- sumption history) and display additional requirements (Unpld. Cons.) in the stock requirements list (via Transaction MD04). PD simply looks at demand in the future and covers it with a procurement proposal that has a delivery date just before the demand occurs, as shown in Figure 13.5. Figure 13.5 MRP Type: PD Because PD is deterministic, it waits for demand so as to arrange supply. If there is demand, and if the MRP run is executed, then a supply proposal is generated to cover that demand. This MRP type is well suited for expensive purchased parts that are costly to store, for which consumption is highly variable and unpredictable, and for which the lead time to procure them is short. MRP run generates order proposal Period 1 Period 2 Period 3 Actual demand PD | Deterministic Planning © 681 13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures This type of planning is carried out for the quantities planned in PIRs or quantities planned in incoming sales orders (MTO or MTS), dependent requirements, and stock transfer requirements—that is, any requirement that is MRP relevant. In this type of net requirements calculation and its planning, the following apply:  The system doesn’t consider or ask for any forecast information or historical con- sumption values. Material planning is ensured only on the basis of absolute require- ments or demands in hand.  The system determines the available stock in the planning run, which is required to meet the demands, with the following logic: Plant stock + Scheduled receipts from production and purchase – All of the demands (e.g., from sales orders, material reservations, and PIRs) If the available stock is insufficient to fulfill the demand, then depending on the set- tings of the planning run, PD creates planned orders for in-house production, as well as purchase requisitions, delivery schedules and scheduling lines, or purchase requisi- tions for external procurement. As mentioned earlier, other deterministic MRP types are P1, P2, P3, and P4, which require you as a planner to set a planning time fence. They help you by keeping planned orders that are generated by the MRP run out of that period of time. The differ- ences among these four MRP types lie in whether they firm or don’t firm the procure- ment proposals and what requirements in which period they consider. Similarly, you use MRP type M0 to plan important finished (or unfinished) products separately from the rest. As in the P series, the M1, M2, M3, and M4 types allow for the protection of a planning time fence and automatic fixing of procurement proposals. Let’s consider an example of firming type P1 to explain how firming works. In firming type P1, the system automatically firms the existing procurement proposals so long as these are within the defined planning time fence. It also creates new order proposals when it evaluates that there is going to be a shortage of material within the planning time fence. However, the system pushes the new procurement proposals outside the planning time fence to ensure that the production plan isn’t disturbed. When the newly created procurement proposals begin to fall within the planning time fence, the system automatically firms them and makes them a part of the production plan. To ensure greater stability in the production and procurement plans, a planner can take advantage of firming logic and the planning time fence. The firming logic defines how the system should handle the existing or new procurement proposals by automat- ically firming the planned orders, allowing an option for manual firming, or doing nei- ther of those things. The planning time fence allows for the definition of a range within which MRP doesn’t create, change, move, or delete any procurement proposals. This can be a great reliability factor for the production department if you use a planning 13 Material Requirements Planning 682 time fence such as the following: for the next five days starting from today, the produc- tion plan will not be changed, not even if there are new sales orders. You can maintain the planning time fence in the MRP 1 view of the material master or in configuration. The planning time fence is the number of workdays (not calendar days) within which a planner can protect the master plan. The system firms the pro- curement proposals to protect them from any changes. However, a planner still has the flexibility to make any last-minute manual changes, if needed. Firming of the procure- ment proposals (planned orders, purchase requisitions, and delivery schedules) falling within the planning time fence prevents the proposals from being adapted in the next planning run. In other words, inside the planning time fence, the system doesn’t create or delete any procurement proposals, nor does it change the existing proposals. The system calculates the planning time fence from the current date and adds up the number of working days incorporated as the planning time fence in the material master (see the MRP 1 view). You can also define the planning time fence in the configuration settings of the MRP group because it’s easier to just assign the MRP group to the mate- rial master. When the system moves forward in the time scale, it’s logical that any pro- curement proposal that was previously outside this planning time fence will start to move into the planning time fence. When that happens, the proposal is automatically firmed for protection from changes. That means the horizon keeps rolling with time. There are two firming options for procurement proposals: automatic firming and man- ual firming. There are ten MRP types with firming available in the system (M0, M1, M2, M3, M4, P1, P2, P3, P4, and D1), and they differ with regard to the automatic firming of elements within the planning time fence and also with regard to the creation of new elements. It’s important to note that firming type M0 behaves just like MRP type PD; that is, the planning time fence is inactive for this master production scheduling time. Note Firming type M refers to master production scheduling, whereas firming type P refers to MRP. Technically, master production scheduling and MRP in SAP S/4HANA are almost the same thing, and you can use the same transaction for them (MD01N). However, because neither master production scheduling nor MRP takes capacity restrictions into account, we recommend that you split the planning run between master production scheduling and MRP and run capacity leveling for the products that you’ve planned with master production scheduling before running MRP. © 683 13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures Table 13.1 shows the similarities among firming types for both MRP (firming type P) and master production scheduling (firming type M). A planner can also set how the system should account for roll-forward. A roll-forward defines the number of workdays starting from today (both forward and backward) during which the system can delete the firmed planned orders if they aren’t converted to either production orders or purchase requisitions. For example, you may want to delete all of the firmed planned orders older than five days during the next MRP run. To do this, you select an MRP type with firming (e.g., P1) and select the Delete Firm Planned Orders option from the Roll Forward field. Next, define “–5” in the Roll Forward field in the MRP group parameters for a specific plant (using Transaction OPPR). The negative value (the number of days from today) is for deletion of the past and firmed planned order, and a positive value is to manage deletion of the firmed planned orders in the future. In the same MRP group settings (in Transaction OPPR), you assign the number of workdays (from today) that the system can use to firm the planned orders for the rel- evant firming type. Figure 13.6 shows the MRP 1 view of the material master and shows various MRP Type(s) in the dropdown menu, as well as the Planning Time Fence field. Firming Types 0 1 2 3 4 MRP Types PD M0 P1 M1 D1 P2 M2 P3 M3 P4 M4 Firming Methods of Existing Procurement Proposals (within the Planning Time Fence) No firming of procurement proposals within the planning time fence.  Automatic firming of procurement proposals within the planning time fence.   Manual firming of procurement proposals within the plan- ning time fence (no automatic firming of procurement pro- posals).  Firming Methods of New Order Proposals (within the Planning Time Fence) Automatic creation of new procurement proposals to cover shortages within the planning time fence. However, the sys- tem pushes the dates of these new procurement proposals outside the planning time fence.   No new procurement proposals created to cover material shortages within the planning time fence.   Table 13.1 Firming Types in Planning 13 Material Requirements Planning 684 Figure 13.6 Planning Time Fence

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type VB
sections.en: 13.2.2 Material Requirements Planning Type VB VB is the simplest and most manual of the reorder point MRP types. When using MRP type VB, you as a planner have to calculate and set the reorder point manually and maintain it in the Reorder Point field in the material master. You can base the reorder point on historical consumption values or even your best guess. Although it’s not required, we recommend that you to maintain safety stock when using this MRP type. Furthermore, we suggest that you to look at the past consumption of the material in question and derive an estimated, future daily consumption quantity from that amount. Then, simply multiply that quantity by the number of working days (the days on which your company consumes the material) within the total replenishment lead time. You should maintain that lead time in the MRP 2 screen of the material master record (in the Planned Delivery Time + GR Proc.Time + Purchasing Time fields). The product of future daily consumption with the lead time gives an inventory point that, if reached, will enable the system to reorder so that your company doesn’t run out of stock before the next receipt comes in. Depending on the variability in the estimated consumption or lead time, you may need to raise the reorder point by a safety stock when the reorder point shifts below the safety stock (see Figure 13.7). © 685 13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures Figure 13.7 Reorder Point Planning Tips and Tricks There are many ways to calculate and guess future daily consumption. Transaction MC.9, for example, gives that history. However, when you’re looking at the table with key figures and characteristics, you’ll see that only monthly totals are available, and even though you can divide the monthly total by the number of working days in that month, you’ll likely have some sense of doubt about consistent consumption day by day. But the consumption graphic is displayed in the LIS, where you can apply a number of helpful tools to get all the information you need. This way, you can see whether there’s consistency in consumption over time and figure out how much inventory your company needs to get through the lead time without any stock-outs. Then, you can add a safety stock and maintain it in the material master’s MRP 2 view. Subsequently, you can maintain MRP Type VB and also Reorder Point in the MRP 1 view of the mate- rial master. The reorder point quantity is the estimated consumption within the total replenishment lead time plus the safety stock quantity. We’ve mentioned that even though you’re not required to maintain a safety stock at all in MRP 2, you should have that kind of information readily available (see Figure 13.8). The system generates exception message 96 (Section 13.13.3 on exception messages) every time the stock falls into that range, which gives an indication over time of whether the reorder point is too low or there’s an opportunity to lower it (and with it, the average stockholding of this material). The more often exception message 96 appears, the more likely it is that the reorder point is too low. But if exception message 96 appears only rarely, it’s time to look into lowering that reorder point. We’ll show you an example of reorder point planning in Section 13.15.2. VB | Manual Reorder Point Reorder Point Safety Stock Manually set by the planner Manually set by the planner 13 Material Requirements Planning 686 Figure 13.8 MRP Type VB You can configure the MRP Procedure that the system uses in the MRP Type on the screen shown in Figure 13.9. Figure 13.9 Reorder Point Planning VB | Manual Reorder Point Reorder Point Safety Stock Manually set by the planner Manually set by the planner © 687 13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures Place your cursor on the MRP Procedure field and then make a selection from the drop- down option or press (F4). Figure 13.9 shows the configuration screen for manual reor- der point planning (MRP Type VB). Notice that you as a planner can take advantage of the system’s functionality to automatically calculate safety stock and reorder points. For configuration of the MRP type, refer to Section 13.4. Tips and Tricks Use the fixed lot-sizing procedure for reorder point based planning so that the system procures the fixed lot size instead of only creating a proposal for the deficit quantity.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type VM
sections.en: 13.2.3 Material Requirements Planning Type VM To achieve more automation and greater accuracy in the reorder point procedure, you can use MRP type VM, which eliminates guesswork and lets the system do the figuring. But the system can only figure and calculate reliable and authentic values if the under- lying basic data and specifically the historical consumption figures are accurate. This procedure requires timely and accurate GR/GI postings because it uses that data to fig- ure mean absolute deviation and therefore consistency in consumption, lead time deviation, and various settings (e.g., planned delivery time, service level) to come up with a reorder point. If any of this information isn’t maintained correctly, VM will pro- duce reorder points that either cause stock-outs or drive the inventory to potentially very high levels. In any case, VM uses the material master’s Forecasting screen to auto- matically calculate the reorder point and a safety stock quantity via the following pro- cedure: 1. On the Forecasting screen, VM uses its settings and historical consumption to calcu- late a past MAD. The system does so by comparing an ex-post forecast to the actual past consumption. 2. It uses the service level being maintained in the MRP 2 view of the material master and a standard formula to calculate a safety stock quantity. 3. It calculates the future anticipated daily consumption by running a forecast in the material master record. 4. It multiplies the working days in the total replenishment lead time with the future anticipated daily consumption. This is the basic value that will appear in the fore- casting results. 5. It adds the safety stock quantity to the basic value. This is the calculated reorder point. 13 Material Requirements Planning 688 6. During this procedure, VM uses the Forecasting screen for simulation purposes only. The forecasted quantities won’t become MRP relevant. VM uses the forecast to sim- ply perform an ex-post forecast that allows for the determination of MAD, which it then uses to determine the degree of variability for the calculation of reorder point and safety stock (see Figure 13.10). Figure 13.10 MRP Type VM When deciding whether to use this planning procedure, you should take the following factors into consideration:  The higher the degree of variation (MAD), the higher the safety stock requirement and the higher the reorder point will be.  The longer the replenishment lead time, the higher the reorder point needs to be to get through the replenishment lead time.  The longer the replenishment lead time, the greater the chance of unforeseen con- sumption. This increases variability and, with it, safety stock requirements and the reorder point.  The higher the service level percentage maintained in MRP 2, the higher the safety stock requirement and, with it, the reorder point.  The higher the daily consumption, the higher the reorder point. At the same time, the forecast is obligatory and uses total consumption. Figure 13.11 shows the automatic Reorder Point calculation, the Safety Stock calculation, and other calculations. VM | Automatic Reorder Point Reorder Point Safety Stock Auto-calculated in the forecasting screen Auto-calculated in the forecasting screen © 689 13.2 Deterministic Replenishment Procedures Figure 13.11 Safety Stock and Reorder Point Calculation Using MRP Type VM

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type V1
sections.en: 13.2.4 Material Requirements Planning Type V1 MRP type V1 acts exactly like VB, except that it takes external requirements into con- sideration. You as a planner have to manually set and calculate the safety stock and reorder point this procedure. You can choose and configure what the external require- ments are, such as order reservations, released stock transport orders, and released purchase requisitions. If there is a sales order reservation within the replenishment lead time (set Ext. Reqmts as “2”), then the order is triggered early because the amount of demand from the reservation is taken into account for the determination of when the reorder point is broken. While you’re using this MRP type, it’s helpful to use a lot-size procedure that adds those external requirements to the ordering quantity within the lead time. Because these demands are already known, it makes sense to add them to the order size so that at the time of receipt, the consumption can be replenished in full. Therefore, the V1 type offers an advantage over both the VB type and the VM type in that it’s not purely driven by the past but is a little more proactive and includes future requirements in the plan- ning process.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type V2
sections.en: 13.2.5 Material Requirements Planning Type V2 MRP type V2 provides the most sophisticated and advanced reorder point procedure so far. Not only does it include external requirements, but it also calculates the safety 13 Material Requirements Planning 690 stock and a reorder point automatically by using the Forecasting screen in the material master record. As a word of caution, beware of using too much automation too soon, unless you fully understand this material planning procedure. As shown in Figure 13.12, MRP type V2 automatically calculates a reorder point based on length of lead time, variability in the consumption, and the service level you set. So, the V2 type combines automatic calculation of the planning parameters with consider- ation of external demand. This procedure is based on a simple reorder point methodol- ogy that is sophisticated enough to automate the planning process and optimize inventory holdings. Figure 13.12 MRP Type V2

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Types D1 and D2
sections.en: 13.2.6 Material Requirements Planning Types D1 and D2 In demand-driven replenishment (DDR), the planning run creates a new supply element (such as a purchase requisition, a planned order, or a stock transfer requisition) when the net flow position of a material falls below the reorder point maintained in the material master. The aim of the planning run is to create supply elements to fulfill demand by using a better quality of demand signal. This means that it’s possible to cre- ate supply elements that meet real customer demand more accurately, which in turn achieves improved service levels on the one hand while lowering inventory levels and bound capital on the other. As this MRP type uses firming type 1 (like MRP type M1), the procurement proposals within the planning time fence are firmed automatically as soon as their date is at least one day before the end date of the planning time fence. The date of new procurement proposals created in the planning time fence is then moved to the end date of the plan- ning time fence. As a result, these new procurement proposals aren’t firmed. Reorder Point Safety Stock V2 | Automatic Reorder Point with External Demand Demand within replenishment lead time Auto-calculated in the forecasting screen Auto-calculated in the forecasting screen Demand within replenishment lead time shifts ordering point and increases ordering quantity © 691

sections.title: Stochastic Replenishment Procedures
sections.en: 13.3 Stochastic Replenishment Procedures The equation for generating supply elements is the net flow equation: On-hand stock + Open supply – Open past (including today’s) demand – Qualified spike demand In this equation, the quantity of stock on hand is added to the quantity of open supply, and then then the total quantity of open demand (including today’s demands) plus the qualified spike demand is subtracted. This results in the net flow position. If the day’s net flow position is below the reorder point, a supply element is created for the amount that will bring the stock level back up to the maximum stock quantity. SAP S/4HANA 2023 has introduced a new MRP type, D2, to cater to the DDR require- ments of the repetitive manufacturing industry. (Refer to Chapter 8 on repetitive man- ufacturing.) Note See Chapter 14 for more information on DDR. 13.3 Stochastic Replenishment Procedures Some of the stochastic replenishment or consumption-based MRP procedures require you to execute and save the forecast before the system carries out MRP. In such cases, the Forecasting indicator in the relevant MRP type is marked as mandatory/obligatory, rather than optional. When a forecast on a material is executed, the system looks for the forecasting parameters that are maintained in the forecasting view of the material master, such as the forecasting model and historical or forecast (future) periods. Fur- thermore, depending on the model you select, it also considers smoothing factors, such as alpha, beta, gamma, or delta. In forecasting, the system considers the planned and unplanned consumption quantities of the material for the periods under evalua- tion and comes up with forecast (future) figures. When the forecast results are saved, the MRP for forecast-based consumption planning uses these forecast figures to create procurement proposals after the MRP run. The following stochastic replenishment procedures require you to execute a forecast as a prerequisite to an MRP run:  Forecast-based planning  Time-phased materials planning In the following sections, we cover forecast-based and time-phased materials planning. 13 Material Requirements Planning 692

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Type VV
sections.en: 13.3.1 Material Requirements Planning Type VV The system updates the consumption data in the material master as total consumption (planned consumption and unplanned consumption) or unplanned consumption. To view the consumption details of the material, you go to the Basic Data view of a mate- rial master (Transaction MM02) and choose the Additional Data icon. Of the many tabs available, the Consumption tab holds consumption quantities. Per the MRP reserva- tions, the system updates the consumption data of the planned consumption. If con- sumption isn’t planned per MRP reservations, or if the material issuance quantity is greater than the reservation quantities, then the system updates the unplanned con- sumption. In MRP type configuration, you choose the type of historical consumption of a material to plan whether the system uses total consumption or unplanned consumption. Also, choose the reduction method for forecast requirements. The default system setting is reduction by consumption. Since we’ve already covered forecasting in detail in Chapter 11, Section 11.4, we’ll only briefly cover the details that are relevant to forecast-based consumption planning here. The consumption forecast for components is based on material withdrawals. You should maintain the forecast parameters in the Forecasting view of the material mas- ter, as shown in Figure 13.13. Figure 13.13 Forecasting View of Material Master The parameters include the granularity of the forecast—that is, whether it’s carried out on the basis of monthly, weekly, or daily values. You can define the granularity by using © 693 13.3 Stochastic Replenishment Procedures the Period Indicator field. Other entries specify the number of historical periods based on which the forecast is carried out, as well as the number of forecast periods. You can either specify the forecast model directly or have it determined automatically. Some of the possible forecast models include the following:  Constant model (D)  Trend model (T)  Seasonal model (S)  Seasonal trend model (X)  Moving average (G) Clicking the Execute Forecast button, which is located further down the screen shown in Figure 13.13 (but not shown in it), enables you to carry out a forecast in the material master, while clicking the other two buttons, Consumption vals and Forecast values (also not shown), allows you to edit the historical values and the forecast values, respec- tively. You can also run an individual forecast for a material or an overall forecast for several materials. To run an individual material forecast, use Transaction MP30. After execut- ing the forecast, you can change the forecast results, and you can also correct the fore- cast values interactively by using Transaction MP31. To display the forecast, use Trans- action MP32. For the overall forecast, use Transaction MP38 to restrict the planning scope by plants, materials, and ABC indicators. You can maintain the ABC indicator in the MRP 1 view of the material master. In MRP type VV, you carry out the forecast at an individual material level. In fact, each material can have its own forecasting screen activated in the material master. You can also use MRP type VV for finished goods, and there are many situations that call for set- ting up a finished product as VV. MRP type VV is a consumption-based replenishment strategy in that it maintains inventory in anticipation of actual demand. The inventory is replenished to a forecast, which is based on the material’s own consumption history— hence, the name material forecast. This is a good strategy when demand is predictable but the lead time to replenish is long. By putting “artificial” demand out there by using the forecasting functionality, MRP can generate all supply elements far ahead of time. All that you need to do is convert the requisition into an order on the date that the sys- tem suggests (see Figure 13.14). Warning VV doesn’t take demand spikes into consideration. Any changes in demand will flow into the consumption pattern and eventually be picked up by the forecast module. The system might increase or decrease the forecast or state that the current underlying model isn’t applicable anymore. So, as with all the other strategies, you should use MRP type VV if and when it meets your business needs. 13 Material Requirements Planning 694 Figure 13.14 MRP Type VV For MRP type VV to function, you must maintain all the parameters in the Forecasting screen of the SAP S/4HANA system. You can identify the periodicity by which to run the forecast to create demand with the Period Indicator in the Forecasting view of the material master. This setting will eventually determine with what frequency the fore- cast is run automatically for the material in question. The options are listed here:  Daily (D) This option results in daily demand records for every working day being visible in Transaction MD04 and relevant to the MRP run. Be aware, however, that a daily fore- cast will also result in daily replenishment, which may be an unnecessary consump- tion of the system’s resources.  Weekly (W) This is the most often used periodicity, and it allows for relatively low average inven- tory holding by bringing in less inventory than in a monthly periodicity. It’s also flexible and quick to detect spikes and periods of low consumption.  Monthly (M) This is used for less important or less expensive items because it brings in large quantities with few orders.  Posting period (P) You can freely define this according to a period that you set up in financial account- ing. After you set the Period Indicator, you should decide how many periods from the past to include in the forecast calculation. You can also set aside a number of periods for an ex-post forecast, and you can identify the number of periods for which the forecast run generates demand. VV | Forecast-Based Planning Receipts Period 1 Period 2 Period 3 Forecasted demand Actual consumption Period of stock-out © 695 13.3 Stochastic Replenishment Procedures To maintain the SI (splitting indicator) details, use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Forecast  Define splitting of Forecast Requirements for MRP. The Splitting Indicator contains the number of periods to be considered, as well as the number of days and/or weeks for which splitting is to be carried out (see Figure 13.15). Figure 13.15 Distributing Forecast Values to Different Periods In the MRP 3 view of the material master, you assign the previously configured splitting indicator in the Period Indicator field (see Figure 13.16). Figure 13.16 Splitting Indicator in Material Master (MRP 3 View)

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Types R1 and R2
sections.en: 13.3.2 Material Requirements Planning Types R1 and R2 Materials that are replenished using a time-phased planning method are scheduled so that the MRP run considers them for planning only on specific dates in the week. This is determined by the planning cycle in which the MRP run is only triggered on specific days of the week. Time-phased planning can be carried out with consumption-based— stochastic—procedures or with deterministic ones. MRP type R1 simply plans for an MRP run on the days scheduled by the planning cycle, which might lead to stock-outs when large consumption happens on a day not included in the planning cycle. This is 13 Material Requirements Planning 696 where MRP type R2 is the better choice because it calculates a reorder point automati- cally and triggers the planning run whenever the reorder point is broken. For example, if a supplier always delivers a material on a certain day of the week due to its own internal route and delivery planning, then it makes sense for a company’s plan- ning run to follow the same cycle and be displaced by the delivery time. Similarly, if the company procures or produces certain material during specific times of the year only, then the company should use time-phased planning. For example, chocolate manufac- turers in a few hot and humid climatic countries cease production during the summer months. In such cases, all production and procurement planning is confined to the defined planning cycle only. To plan a material based on time phases, you use Transaction MM02 to enter the MRP type for time-phased planning and the planning cycle in the material master (see Figure 13.17). You also use a forecast in this planning concept, and the general procedure is very similar to forecast-based planning, with the difference that the procurement intervals are predefined. Figure 13.17 Time-Phased Materials Planning The configuration basics of setting up a planning cycle are the same as those for setting up a planning calendar, which is described in Section 13.14.

sections.title: Configuring Material Requirements Planning Types
sections.en: 13.4 Configuring Material Requirements Planning Types To configure an MRP type, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Master Data  Check MRP Types. Here, the detailed view of MRP Type PD is shown (see Figure 13.18). We’ve previously discussed almost all options available on this screen, such as the firming type, forecast indicator, and consumption indicator. To control the MRP procedure that the system uses in the MRP type, you place the cur- sor on the MRP Procedure field and choose the dropdown option or press (F4). Figure 13.19 shows the resulting available list of MRP procedures. Note MRP procedure X Without MRP, with BOM Explosion is used in the production plan- ning and detailed scheduling (PP/DS) area of SAP S/4HANA. We cover the use of this © 697 13.4 Configuring Material Requirements Planning Types MRP procedure and its associated MRP type X0 (see Table 13.2) in Chapter 17, which dis- cusses PP/DS. Figure 13.18 Configuration (Transaction SPRO) of MRP Type PD Figure 13.19 MRP Procedures Table 13.2 is a side-by-side comparison of the available MRP types in a standard (blank) SAP client and SAP Best Practices client. For the sake of providing as much coverage of different MRP types as possible, we chose to cover the SAP Best Practices client in this chapter as well as in this book. It’s also possible that the SAP Best Practices client that you may be using may have all the standard MRP types available (i.e., it may be a blank client). 13 Material Requirements Planning 698 SAP S/4HANA 2023: Blank Client SAP S/4HANA 2023: SAP Best Practices D1 Demand-driven replenishment, fixing type -1- D1 and D2 Demand-driven replenishment, firming type -1- M0 MPS, fixing type -0- M0 MPS, FCST consumption, no firming M1 MPS, fixing type -1- M1 MPS, FCST consumption, auto firming, new orders after PTF M2 MPS, fixing type -2- M2 MPS, FCST consumption, auto firming, no new orders in PTF M3 MPS, fixing type -3- M3 MPS, FCST consumption, manual firming, new orders after PTF M4 MPS, fixing type -4- M4 MPS, FCST consumption, manual firming, no new orders in PTF ND No planning ND No planning P1 MRP, fixing type -1- P1 Forecast consumption, auto firming, new orders after PTF P2 MRP, fixing type -2- P2 Forecast consumption, auto firming, no new orders in PTF P3 MRP, fixing type -3- P3 Forecast consumption, manual firming, new orders after PTF P4 MRP, fixing type -4- P4 Forecast consumption, manual Firming, no new orders in PTF PD MRP PD Forecast consumption, no planning time fence R1 Time-phased planning VB Reorder point planning R2 Time-phased w. auto. reord. point RE Replenishment planned externally RF Replenish with dyn. target stock RP Replenishment Table 13.2 Available MRP Types © 699

sections.title: Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.en: 13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures 13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures After performing a net requirements calculation, when the system determines a mate- rial shortage, it looks to information maintained in the Lot Sizing Procedure field in the MRP 1 view of the material master. The lot sizing specifies the quantity that the system should propose when it comes across a quantity shortage of a material. When the sys- tem creates the correct lot size as a procurement proposal, it also looks for other infor- mation, such as the safety stock, maximum or minimum lot size, static or dynamic safety stock, scrap percentage, and rounding value. It’s very important to determine lot sizes to ensure optimum inventories and good ser- vice levels. Suppliers occasionally demand minimum order quantities, which end up as excess inventory levels, especially for slow-moving items. It’s possible to maintain inventory controls or gates that are then easier to manage. Between procurement or purchasing and materials planning, there is a gate, which means that materials plan- ning should generate the most economical lot size, generate an order proposal with that optimal lot size, and initiate the purchasing process. The advantage of having these kinds of gates is that a company can build a basis for negotiations with suppliers. For example, if the materials planner applies the condi- tions in the Minimum Lot Size field in the material master, then the MRP run will always generate order proposals using the minimum quantity. Lot sizing procedures in the SAP S/4HANA system help the planner find the most optimal quantities to order. An MRP run not only automatically generates the order proposal but also suggests the perfect lot size to bring in so that availability is assured without the company holding too much inventory. There are several lot sizing procedures already preconfigured and available in the standard SAP system. V1 Manual reord. point w. ext. reqs. V2 Autom. reord. point w. ext. reqs. VB Manual reorder point planning VI Supplier managed inventory VM Automatic reorder point planning VS Seasonal MRP VV Forecast-based planning X0 W/O MRP, with BOM Explosion SAP S/4HANA 2023: Blank Client SAP S/4HANA 2023: SAP Best Practices Table 13.2 Available MRP Types (Cont.) 13 Material Requirements Planning 700 You assign the lot sizing procedure in the MRP 1 view of the material master (Transac- tion MM02). For the example in Figure 13.20, assign the Lot Sizing Procedure as “EX” (Lot-for-lot order quantity). Figure 13.20 Lot Sizing Procedure in Material Master There are three different lot sizing procedures in the SAP S/4HANA system:  Static lot sizing procedures  Periodic lot sizing procedures  Optimum lot sizing procedures We’ll discuss these procedures in the following sections before moving on to configur- ing lot sizes.

sections.title: Static Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.en: 13.5.1 Static Lot Sizing Procedures Static lot sizing procedures can further be categorized as exact lot sizing, fixed lot siz- ing, fixed lot sizing with splitting, and replenish to maximum stock level, as described in the following sections. Lot Size: Exact Lot Sizing The system creates planned orders or procurement proposals to cover the exact short- age requirement only (EX). For example, if a net requirement quantity of a material is © 701 13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures 110 units, then the system creates the procurement proposal for 110 units only (see Figure 13.21). Figure 13.21 Lot Size: EX Lot Size: Fixed Lot Sizing The system proposes the total requirement in multiples of fixed lots (FX). For example, if the lot size is fixed as 60 units, and the shortage quantity is 240, then the system cre- ates four planned orders to cover the shortages. Lot Size: Fixed Lot Sizing with Splitting The system proposes the total requirement in multiples of fixed lots with overlap (FS). This lot sizing is generally preferred if a company can’t procure a large quantity at once and hence must split the quantity with an overlap. Using this lot sizing procedure entails assigning a fixed lot quantity together with a rounding value. The rounding value must be a multiple of the fixed lot quantity. For example, if the lot size is fixed as 100 units, and if the rounding value is 20 units, then the system creates five planned orders to cover the shortages (see Figure 13.22). Figure 13.22 Lot Size: FS 2 4 6 6 4 2 Lot - For- Lot (EX) 2 4 6 6 4 2 Fixing and Splitting (FS) = 4/2 with Takt = 2 Days 13 Material Requirements Planning 702 You can also shift the orders by a certain takt time, which means using a control param- eter for scheduling based on takts (i.e., physical areas of a production line). This proce- dure avoids the simultaneous scheduling of numerous orders and thus ensures that the actually required lead time is observed. If you use splitting in combination with a fixed lot size, then the rounding value must be a multiple integer of the fixed lot size. The takt time indicates the offset between order dates in terms of workdays. The differ- ent orders overlap in time and are shifted against each other by the takt time. For example, you can assign Lot Sizing Procedure as “FS” (Fixed Lot with Splitting) and give a Fixed Lot Size of “100.” Next, set the Rounding Value as “25” (it should be a multi- ple of the fixed lot quantity). Finally, maintain a Takt Time of “3” days (workdays). When the system runs the MRP, assuming there is no existing stock of the material in the plant, it will create four planned orders of 25 each for the total quantity of 100 and will also delay the start date of each planned order by three workdays (takt time). Lot Size: Replenish to Maximum Stock Level The system creates planned orders to fill to the maximum stock level (HB). The maxi- mum stock level has to be defined in the relevant field of the MRP 1 view of the material master. For example, if the maximum stock level is 300 units, and the demand quantity is 50 units, then the system creates a planned order of 350. It first ensures that the 300 units of stock fill up, and then, it adds the demand quantity to it. When the reorder point is broken, the MRP run figures the difference between the cur- rent stock level and the maximum stock level desired and creates a replenishment quantity correspondingly. When you’re using this lot sizing procedure, some consumption occurs during the replenishment lead time, and when the replenishment quantity is received, the stock level might be considerably lower than at the time you calculated the replenishment quantity. One way to address this issue is to include demand in the replenishment lead time by making relevant configuring settings in a lot sizing procedure, as covered in Section 13.5.4.

sections.title: Periodic Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.en: 13.5.2 Periodic Lot Sizing Procedures Periodic lot sizing procedures can be further categorized into daily lot sizing, weekly lot sizing, monthly lot sizing, and lot sizing of any other periodicity. For example, if the lot sizing procedure is daily, then the system considers demand with all the receipts for that day and creates a daily shortage proposal in case a shortage exists. If it’s a weekly lot size, then the system combines all the requirements for a week and comes up with one procurement proposal. In other words, several demands can be covered with a single supply element. These procedures all collect demand in a specified period and create exactly one supply © 703 13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures element to cover the total demand quantities in that period. However, it’s important to mention that the longer the period, the more inventories need to be maintained. This is because the MRP run generates an order proposal that brings in the entire quantity needed in that period to the date of the first demand. For example, if a planner is using a three-month period, and if there’s a demand of one piece on the first date of the first month, then the order proposal would bring in the entire quantity needed for three months’ worth of demand on the first day. This would result in higher inventory, which would carry a cost if the item in question is of high value. Periodic lot size procedures are effective when used with cheap items with low inventory carrying costs. Tips and Tricks Use Transaction OMI4 to set the availability date as either the start or the end of the period (see Section 13.5.4).

sections.title: Optimum Lot Sizing Procedures
sections.en: 13.5.3 Optimum Lot Sizing Procedures In the optimum lot sizing procedure, the production or purchase of materials entails fixed lot size costs (such as setup costs for machines and purchase order costs) and vari- able costs (which are referred to as capital tie-up due to stockholding). To minimize variable costs, you must procure quantities that are as small as possible—but to mini- mize fixed costs, you must procure quantities that are as large as possible. Therefore, optimum lot sizing procedures consider variable and fixed costs and determine opti- mal lot sizes according to different procedures based on these costs. Example Phosphoric acid is the main component used in the production of diammonium phos- phate, which is a specialized fertilizer. The company wants to replenish to the maxi- mum stock of phosphoric acid at the time of placing new orders, but there are supplier requirements regarding minimum and maximum order limits for shipment. To cater to this business need, you start by creating a new MRP lot size (which may also be a copy of MRP lot size HB: replenish to maximum stock level) and checking the Check Min. Lot Size and Check Max Lot Size boxes. You then assign this self-defined MRP lot size to the MRP 1 view of the material master. The system will then prompt you to enter the mini- mum and maximum lot sizes of the material. Thereafter, not only will the system make sure to replenish the material to the maximum stock level, but it will also take the min- imum and maximum lot sizes into account when creating procurement proposals. To illustrate this example further, say that the storage tank capacity for phosphoric acid is 40,000 metric tons, the minimum lot size is 20,000, and the maximum is 25,000. Currently, the stock in the storage tank has dropped to 10,000 metric tons. The system creates one planned order for 25,000, which ensures that this is the maximum 13 Material Requirements Planning 704 lot size it can go up to and still remain within the maximum storage tank capacity of 40,000. If the minimum and maximum lot sizes had not been maintained, then the system would have created a planned order of 30,000 metric tons to replenish the existing stock of 10,000 to the maximum of 40,000. Optimum lot sizing procedures strive to strike a balance between ordering cost and stockholding cost, and they therefore optimize the total cost of inventory. For exam- ple, let’s look at the EX lot sizing procedure, which brings in—in its simplest version— exactly as much as is needed at exactly the time that it’s needed. In other words, there’s no need to carry inventory forward to meet any other demand; whatever comes in is consumed immediately. However, there’s certainly an ordering cost that needs to be paid every time there is a demand. So, if there are five demands in one month, then a company will end up paying for five orders. This is different with a monthly lot sizing procedure, in which there’s only one order per month. But if you as a planner consider the same situation with five demands, then the order will bring in the total quantity to meet all demands throughout the months to fulfill the first demand, and you will have to carry forward the stock that isn’t consumed by the first demand. On the other hand, there’s only the cost of one order. But if you’re using an optimum lot sizing procedure instead, then the system (the MRP run) will figure out automatically—in any given demand situation—what the opti- mum order quantity and order frequency are to arrive at the minimum between the ordering cost and stockholding cost. The system does so by using various formulas, such as the least unit cost formula, the Groff formula, or the part-period balancing for- mula. These all aim to strike the optimum balance between ordering and stockholding costs. To determine the variable costs, you maintain the storage costs percentage specified in the storage costs indicator (Storage Costs Ind.). You enter the storage costs indicator along with the ordering costs in the MRP 1 view of the material master, and the system will calculate the variable costs according to the following formula: Variable costs = Requirement × Price × Storage costs [%] × Storage duration ÷ 365 In the MRP 1 view of the material master, you maintain the Storage Costs Ind. as well as the Ordering Costs.

sections.title: Configuring Lot Size
sections.en: 13.5.4 Configuring Lot Size Table 13.3 is a summary of a selection of standard MRP lot sizes with lot sizing proce- dures. When you’re selecting a certain lot size, some of them require additional infor- mation, and you must maintain them in the material master, as noted in the last col- umn. © 705 13.5 Lot Sizing Procedures MRP Lot Size Lot Sizing Procedure Lot Size Indicator Fields EX: Exact lot size calculation Static lot size E: Exact lot size FK: Fixed lot size for customer MTO produc- tion Static lot size F: Fixed lot size FS: Fixed/splitting Static lot size S: Fixed with split- ting Fixed Lot Size, Rounding Value, Takt Time FX: Fixed order quantity Static lot size F: Fixed lot size Fixed Lot Size HB: Replenish to maxi- mum stock level Static lot size H: Replenishment to maximum stock level Maximum Stock Level MB: Monthly lot size Lot size according to flexible period length M: Monthly lot size PK: Lot size according to flexible period length and planning calendar Lot size according to flexible period K: Period based on planning calendar TB: Daily lot size Lot size according to flexible period length T: Daily lot size WB: Weekly lot size Lot size according to flexible period length W: Weekly lot size WI: Least unit cost procedure Optimization lot size W: Least unit cost procedure Ordering Costs, Storage Costs Ind. DY: Dynamic lot size creation Optimization lot size D: Dynamic planning Ordering Costs, Storage Costs Ind. GR: Groff lot-sizing procedure Optimization lot size G: Procedure according to Groff Ordering Costs, Storage Costs Ind. SP: Part-period balancing Optimization lot size S: Part-period bal- ancing Ordering Costs, Storage Costs Ind. D1 or D2: Demand- driven replenishment Static lot size H: Replenishment to maximum stock level Reorder point, Maximum stock level, Safety stock Table 13.3 Selection of MRP Lot Sizes and Lot-Sizing Procedures 13 Material Requirements Planning 706 Although standard lot sizes available in the SAP S/4HANA system are generally suffi- cient to fulfill most business needs, you can configure the MRP lot size to attend to any specific business process. To configure the MRP lot size, you use menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Planning  Lot Size Calculation  Check Lot Sizing Pro- cedures or use Transaction OMI4. Figure 13.23 displays MRP lot sizing procedure EX for an exact lot size calculation. Figure 13.23 Lot Size Configuration You can also set different applicable lot sizing parameters for short-term and long-term horizons. Procurement/production always takes place with the lot size of the short- term horizon, while the long-term horizon enables the forecast function, which is mostly aggregated (e.g., via monthly periodic lot sizing). This is often useful in reducing the number of requirement coverage elements, especially if you use fixed lot sizes that are smaller than the average requirement quantity. Typically, a period-based lot size is used for the long-term horizon. Tips and Tricks If you use fixed lot sizes for financial or practical reasons, without any technical need, then you can avoid surplus procurements by checking the Last Lot Exact box in the © 707

sections.title: Rounding
sections.en: 13.6 Rounding configuration of the MRP lot size. This is particularly applicable to use with higher- value goods. 13.6 Rounding In some cases, it’s useful to round the quantities of the procurement proposals. In the simplest business scenario, you can use a rounding value because the order quantity of the requirement coverage element always represents a multiple of the rounding value. In more complex scenarios, in which the rounding value depends on the requirement quantity, you can use a rounding profile, which enables you to, for example, model dif- ferent pack sizes. The system offers static and dynamic rounding profiles. The static rounding profiles round up the value after a threshold value has been reached. If the rounding value exceeds the threshold, the system continuously rounds up the value until it reaches the next threshold. You can create rounding profiles by using Transaction OWD1. Example If the rounding value is 30 units, the procurement quantity is 75 units, and the mini- mum lot size is 15 units, then the system always rounds up the planned order to a multiple of 30 units, specifying the rounding to a quantity of 30 units. In this example, the system rounds up the procurement quantity of 75 units to 90. You can use the Rounding Value option in the MRP 1 view of the material master in almost all lot sizing procedures. The static rounding value method may not fulfill all business scenarios in which you’re using a given rounding value and the procurement proposals are rounding to that value.
