There may also be business processes in which the rounding value may change based on the size of the procurement proposal. In such instances, you can use a static rounding profile. For example, you can configure the system to have a rounded value of 10 units if the procurement quantity is at least one unit. For a procurement quantity of 31 units or more, the system should round it off to 50 units. Using Transaction OWD1, the example in Figure 13.24 1 shows that a Rounding Profile PP01 has been created for Plant 1710. Here, you choose the Static button, and the next screen 2 will pop up. Enter the threshold values and the rounding values and then click the Simulation button to bring up the Simulation Selection dialog box 3. Enter a Quan- tity simulation of “1.000” unit, enter a to qty of “150.000,” and click Continue. The sys- tem will then display the simulation results 4. If there’s a business need to specify how the system should act when it reaches a certain threshold value by either adding up or reducing the quantity to the defined percentage, 13 Material Requirements Planning 708 you can do that too. Go back to the first screen shown in Figure 13.24 1, and this time, choose the Create qty t/b added/subtracted button. The screen shown in Figure 13.25 will appear, and there, you can define the threshold values and then enter the percent- age that the system should add to or subtract from the procurement quantity. So, if the procurement quantity is less than or equal to 100 PC, the system will add up to 10% of it. When the procurement quantity is greater than 100 but less than or equal to 200, it will reduce the quantity by 15%. Figure 13.24 Rounding Profile (Static) Figure 13.25 Quantity Addition/Subtraction Profiles In a dynamic rounding profile, you define the rounding off method as well as the rounding rule. The rounding off method may be a multiple of the order unit or sales order. Also, you should consider using an available customer exit to define any unique © 709

sections.title: Scrap
sections.en: 13.7 Scrap rounding method. For a rounding rule, you round the threshold value either up or down by the defined percentage. 13.7 Scrap Companies are often faced with problems relating to materials they can’t use, which are known as scrap. They can come from defective finished goods produced, excess con- sumption of raw material due to wastage in the production process, and operational inefficiencies. The system offers several options for maintaining scrap or waste details at each level—the assembly level and the component (raw material) level—so that pro- duction and procurement planning are closely synchronized with actual and practical situations. Based on historical data or practical experience, you should maintain assembly and component scrap as master data, which subsequently facilitates production and pro- curement processes. Scrap is treated differently in the SAP S/4HANA system. Scrap in real life can be available for use or even sold, but it isn’t treated as inventory in the SAP S/4HANA system. The two types of scrap described here are as follows:  Assembly scrap This is completely unusable finished or semifinished product for which the raw material was issued in accordance with a BOM. Assembly scrap is different from coproducts or by-products, which are inventory managed in the SAP S/4HANA sys- tem.  Component scrap This allows the system to increase the issuance quantity of the component against a production order by the defined percentage to account for scrap or wastage during production. Successfully assigning assembly and component scrap in master data enables the sys- tem to consider scrap not only during production planning—either by increasing pro- duction quantity to account for assembly scrap or increasing the issuance quantity to account for component scrap—but also during procurement planning. During the MRP run, the system takes the scrap percentage into account and reflects it in the plan- ning proposals it generates. To maintain the details of assembly or component scrap in the MRP view of the mate- rial master, you follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master  Material  Change  Immediately or use Transaction MM02. In the following sections, we’ll provide additional details about the different types of scrap and also explain how to maintain details of their different uses. 13 Material Requirements Planning 710

sections.title: Assembly Scrap
sections.en: 13.7.1 Assembly Scrap You maintain assembly scrap in the MRP 1 view of the material master. Here, you enter the assembly scrap in the form of a percentage in the Assembly Scrap (%) field. If this material is then used as an assembly, the system considers it during scrap calculation by correspondingly increasing the production order quantity. For example, say an assembly scrap of 10% is defined for the material. When a production order is created, say for a quantity of 100 PC, the system automatically increases the production order quantity to 110 PC to account for 10% scrap. At the same time, it also issues an informa- tion message. Because the production order quantity has increased to 110 PC, the sys- tem accordingly increases the components’ quantities as well, as it reads this informa- tion from the material’s BOM (and multiplies the production order quantity, which in this case is now 110 PC with the components’ quantities).

sections.title: Component Scrap
sections.en: 13.7.2 Component Scrap You maintain component scrap in the MRP 4 view of the material master in the form of a percentage. If this material is then used as a component in the BOM, the system con- siders it during scrap calculation. For example, entering 10% scrap for 100 M of a com- ponent material increases the component issuance quantity in the production order to 110 M (see Figure 13.26). Figure 13.26 Component Scrap

sections.title: Operations and Component Scrap in Bill of Materials
sections.en: 13.7.3 Operations and Component Scrap in Bill of Materials In Transaction CS02, to change a BOM for a material, you choose the Item Overview col- umn. On the screen that appears (see Figure 13.27), you maintain operation scrap and component scrap in percentages. When maintaining operation scrap as a percentage, you also check the Net ID box. © 711 13.7 Scrap Figure 13.27 Operation and Component Scrap in BOM If both operation scrap and component scrap are maintained in the BOM, then the sys- tem takes both of them into account. For example, for the component quantity of 1.00 M, the system calculates the final component issuance quantity as 1.21 M, after first tak- ing operation scrap and then component scrap into account. That is, it makes the calcu- lation 1.00 M × 1.10 = 1.10 M and then makes the calculation 1.10 M × 1.10 = 1.21 M. Tips and Tricks If you maintain a component scrap percentage in a material master (as in Figure 13.26), it will enable the system to consider the same scrap percentage whenever and wher- ever this component is used in any material’s BOM. However, if there’s a business sce- nario in which you maintain different scrap percentages of the component used in dif- ferent products, then it makes sense to maintain the component scrap details in each material’s BOM (as in Figure 13.27). For example, raw tomatoes are used as a component in producing different products. If the same quantity of raw tomatoes is wasted (as scrap) in producing different prod- ucts, then you should maintain this scrap percentage in the material master for fresh or raw tomatoes. However, if raw tomato wastage percentages are different for differ- ent products, such as tomato ketchup, tomato paste, tomato puree, and tomato soup, then you should maintain these individual wastage (scrap) percentages in each of the four tomato-based products so that the SAP system correctly will calculate the raw tomato quantities required to procure and/or produce each of the four products and their associated wastages.

sections.title: Scrap in Routing
sections.en: 13.7.4 Scrap in Routing You use Transaction CA02 to change a routing for a material. In the Item Overview col- umn, go to the General Data area (see Figure 13.28), where you can maintain operation scrap as a percentage. Then, when a production order is created, the system will increase the component issuance quantity by the percentage you define to account for operation scrap in the routing. 13 Material Requirements Planning 712 Figure 13.28 Scrap in Routing

sections.title: Safety Stock
sections.en: 13.8 Safety Stock Safety stocks are quantity or time buffers that companies use to account for variability in supply and demand. The system offers two types of safety stock: static (i.e., absolute) safety stock and dynamic safety stock (i.e., safety days’ supply). In the following sections, we’ll cover how safety stock availability can be used in net requirements calculation and also the selection method that the system uses for receipts.

sections.title: Static Safety Stock
sections.en: 13.8.1 Static Safety Stock The system can subtract static or absolute safety stock from material availability calcu- lations (net requirements calculations). The safety stock must always be available to cover for unforeseen material shortages or unexpected high demand (see Figure 13.29). Figure 13.29 Static Safety Stock Receipts Forecasted demand Safety stock 30 80 120 Actual demand Dead stock © 713 13.8 Safety Stock Static safety stock certainly has its uses, especially when the objective is to guarantee 100% availability of a cheap part. However, in most situations, the system will drive up dead stock because variability in demand may lead to constant availability of stock that may never really be used. The simplest buffering strategy of all is to use a static safety stock that you can main- tain manually in the Safety Stock field (see Figure 13.30). If you maintain a safety stock quantity, the MRP run will ignore that quantity for planning purposes and set it aside for a rainy day. In fact, the Stock/Requirements List area in Transaction MD04 subtracts the safety stock on the first line and then works without it for everything it does and looks at. Figure 13.30 Safety Stock and Safety Days’ Supply Figure 13.31 (Transaction MD04) shows that the safety stock quantity is immediately subtracted from free available stock and will simply be ignored in all future planning. Figure 13.31 Not Considering Safety Stock for the Available Quantity

sections.title: Safety Stock Availability
sections.en: 13.8.2 Safety Stock Availability To avoid the unnecessary creation of planned orders for a smaller quantity, which can otherwise be covered by the safety stock, you can define the percentage (share) of safety stock that the system can use in such business scenarios. For example, during the MRP 13 Material Requirements Planning 714 run, say the system assesses a shortage of 2 PC of a material. However, a safety stock of 40 PC is maintained for this material, which the system doesn’t consider in the MRP run. If a 10% safety stock has been defined to account for smaller shortages—which, for this example, means that 4 PC come from safety stock—then the system can cater to this small requirement or shortage of 2 PC from the 4 PC available from safety stock and will no longer create a procurement proposal. To define the safety stock parameters, you use menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Produc- tion  Material Requirements Planning  Planning  MRP Calculation  Stocks  Define Safety Stock Availability. For this example, the system can consider 10% of the safety stock for Plnt 1710 and MRP Group 0000 for net requirements calculation (see Figure 13.32). Next, you assign this MRP group in the MRP 1 view of the material master. Figure 13.32 Share of Safety Stock

sections.title: Dynamic Safety Stock with Range of Coverage Profile
sections.en: 13.8.3 Dynamic Safety Stock with Range of Coverage Profile You can employ a dynamic safety stock in the SAP S/4HANA system by using the Range of Coverage profile. With the receipts-forecasted demand safety stock profile, you can set a target dynamic safety stock, a minimum level, and a maximum level. If effectively implemented, the MRP run will always try to keep the target dynamic safety stock available for planning. But, other than with the static safety stock, no additional quan- tities will be brought in unless the safety stock falls below the minimum. Therefore, if the minimum low in planning is set, then all safety stock is used as a buffer. In addition, if the actual demand turns out to be less than what was planned, the maximum level in the coverage profile will keep the stock from blowing up in case of underconsumption of the planned demand. If there is a fixed lot size or a minimum order quantity, then the target coverage may be exceeded. However, the coverage profile also has a maximum coverage due to a fixed lot size or a rounding value. In a coverage profile, you don’t set the levels (minimum, target, and maximum) in quantities, but you manage them in days of coverage. That fact provides the dynamic part of the safety stock because the objective is to hold three days of future demand in reserve to buffer demand and supply variability. That means that if demand goes up, there’s more safety stock, and if demand goes down, there will be less. © 715 13.8 Safety Stock To set up the range of coverage profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Planning  MRP Calculation  Define Range of Cover- age Profile (Dynamic Safety Stock) or use Transaction OMIA (see Figure 13.33). Figure 13.33 Range of Coverage Profile You use the Determine Range of Coverage area to calculate the future daily demand. You do this by collecting demand over a horizon (you have to set the horizon here in the first section) and then dividing the total by the number of days within that horizon (working days or calendar days must be identified in this section) to arrive at a future average daily demand. You then multiply this future average daily demand by the lev- els set in the Determine Average Requirements area. Therefore, at the end of this calcu- lation, you as a planner will end up with quantities for the minimum target and the maximum target. For example, if the levels are Minimum = 1, Target = 5, and Maximum = 20, and if the future average daily demand is 10 pieces for the next three months, then the levels will be 10 pieces minimum, 50 pieces for the target dynamic safety stock, and 200 pieces for the maximum desired stock level. It’s important to note that these numbers are only valid for any period when the future average daily demand is 10 pieces. If that changes, the levels will change also. Figure 13.33 shows the Coverage Profile set to “001”. The dynamic safety stock is calculated with this formula: Dynamic safety stock = Average daily requirement × Range of coverage 13 Material Requirements Planning 716 In this equation, the average daily requirement is calculated with this formula: Requirements in the specified periods ÷ Number of days in the total period length For the calculation of the average daily requirements, you must maintain the following parameters:  Period Indicator This indicator determines the calendar period for which average daily requirements need to be carried out. You can maintain the following values: weeks (W), months (M), and periods according to the planning calendar (K). For this example, you main- tain “M” for a monthly coverage of safety stock.  Type of Per.Length You use this indicator to determine the length of the individual periods in detail. You can maintain the following values: workdays, calendar days, and standard days. If you select standard days as a period length, then you must maintain the number of days determined per period in the field.  Number of Periods You define and use the number of periods to calculate the average daily require- ments.  Range of Coverage Profile in the First Period The Number of Periods is three months. The No. of Days per Period is twenty days.  Range of Coverage in the Second Period The target safety stock (Tgt) is enough to cover five days.  Range of Coverage in the Rest of the Horizon You must plan a minimum stock of “1” day, a target stock of “6” months, and a max- imum target safety stock of “8” days. Figure 13.34 shows the MRP 2 view of the material master, wherein the previously con- figured Range-of-Coverage Profile 001 for plant 1710 is assigned. Figure 13.34 Assigning Coverage Profile in Material Master With these configuration and master data settings in place, let’s now demonstrate the dynamic safety stock calculation with an example. Figure 13.35 shows the monthly requirement (PIRs, via Transaction MD61) shown in the Planned qty column for the next five months for Material 2267 and Plant 1710. © 717 13.8 Safety Stock After you run MRP Live (in Transaction MD01N), you go to the stock/requirements list (in Transaction MD04). Let’s evaluate the planning results in detail. Because it’s already July, the system calcu- lates safety stock requirements for the next month (August) by considering the average daily requirement for the next three months (August, September, and October). Here, the system considers three months because in the coverage profile, the period main- tained was three months. Figure 13.35 Independent Requirements Note All requirements and calculations are in PC (pieces), and all calculations are rounded off to two decimal places. Because the target days of the first period in the coverage profile is maintained as three days of daily average requirements (6.16 PC), the amount to cover this requirement of three days of safety stock (dynamically calculated) is calculated as follows: 6.16 (average daily requirement) × 3 (three days of target safety stock) = 18.51 PC of safety stock (period 1) Figure 13.36 shows that in August 2024, the system created a planned order for a quan- tity of 120 PC. Figure 13.37 shows the summarized view, in which the Targ. (target) Stock column vali- dates the safety stock calculations shown earlier. 13 Material Requirements Planning 718 Figure 13.36 Procurement Proposals Created after MRP Run Figure 13.37 Actual Coverage and Days’ Supply

sections.title: Safety Stock and Safety Time
sections.en: 13.8.4 Safety Stock and Safety Time For days’ supply and safety time, the system plans the GR in advance by the period specified as the safety time. Thus, the planned days’ supply of the stock actually corre- sponds to the number of days specified as the safety time. The system shifts back the date of the receipts by the number of working days and also takes the factory calendar into account. In the MRP 2 view of the material master, the Safety Time/Act.Cov. field is available. The system uses the number of days maintained in this field to simulate the requirements of an earlier date, displaced exactly by that number of days. By using this field, a plan- ner pulls the requirements in so that the MRP run creates the receipts for replenish- ment order proposals for an earlier date. Therefore, the safety time is a time buffer and © 719 13.8 Safety Stock doesn’t increase any quantities. Because the actual requirement doesn’t change, the only difference is that the receipt comes in earlier and provides additional coverage for the time it was pulled forward. The fact that the safety time brings the receipts in earlier, when they’re actually needed, means that when there’s a supply delay, the safety time provides a time float and thus works alongside the safety stock, which acts as a quantity float. You make the relevant entries in the MRP 2 view of the material master. The system then refers to the planning of safety days’ supply as the safety time. To consider the safety time, you set the Safety Time Ind. to “1” (for independent requirements) or “2” (for all requirements) and then also give the Safety Time/Act.Cov. in days. Furthermore, you define deviations from the constant safety time by using a safety time period profile (in the STime Period Profile field).

sections.title: MRP Safety Stock Method
sections.en: 13.8.5 MRP Safety Stock Method Because SAP Fiori is the preferred user interface of SAP S/4HANA, some of the fields are only available in the relevant SAP Fiori apps to support the desired functionalities. The MRP Safety Stock Method field is only available in the Manage Product Master Data app. An innovation introduced in the SAP S/4HANA 2023 system is the availability of time- dependent stock levels that you as a planner can use to define a time-dependent safety stock and time-dependent days of supply. This flexibility enables you to define safety stock values that are valid for specific periods of time as compared to the fixed value maintained in a material master. The same concept is valid for safety days of supply. To maintain the relevant data, first, you use the MRP Safety Stock Method option that’s available only in the Manage Product Master Data app. Then, you maintain the MRP time-dependent stock levels in the Maintain Time-Dependent Stock Levels app. The three possible methods to define MRP safety stock are as follows:  Static safety stock This is the fixed safety stock that will not change over time. This method is available in SAP ERP and even SAP S/4HANA releases.  Time-dependent safety stock (MB) This method allows you to create custom intervals and define a different value of the safety stock for each interval.  Time-dependent days of supply (MZ) In this method, you define how many days of requirements are to be met by the safety stock. 13 Material Requirements Planning 720 Tips and Tricks The Maintain Time-Dependent Stock Levels app supports the upload of data via spreadsheet with intervals so as to reduce the time and effort required to maintain the relevant data. You can simply export the table from the app to download the template and fill in the data in the downloaded template. Then, you can upload the data by click- ing the Upload Intervals button. Note You cannot use time-dependent safety stock levels in combination with a coverage profile, as discussed in Section 13.8.3.

sections.title: Scheduling
sections.en: 13.9 Scheduling For the system to automatically schedule procurement proposals during the MRP run, you must maintain the requisite information in the MRP views of the material master. In the following sections, we’ll discuss your options for maintaining the lead time details for in-house and external procurement that then enable the system to ensure effective planning and scheduling of both in-house and externally procured materials.

sections.title: Scheduling In-House Production
sections.en: 13.9.1 Scheduling In-House Production You as a planner can schedule in-house production in the planning run, and the system can then create planned orders in two different ways:  Basic date determination It schedules the dates based on the production time defined in the material master.  Lead time scheduling It schedules the dates (and times) based on the information in the routing (or the master recipe). In addition to a more detailed scheduling result, the MRP run can generate capacity requirements for the planned orders and perform capacity plan- ning, sequencing, leveling, and scheduling with planned orders. In an MRP run, you select the option for the system to schedule based on basic date determination or lead time scheduling.

sections.title: Basic Date Determination
sections.en: 13.9.2 Basic Date Determination Basic date determination is a type of scheduling that determines the basic dates within which production is to take place. For this reason, the in-house production time defined by the basic dates should be greater than or equal to the in-house production © 721 13.9 Scheduling time defined by the routing explosion. In an MRP run, the system first calculates the basic order dates from the data in the material master. Then, in detailed planning, it uses the data in the routing. In the context of basic date scheduling, no operation dates are defined and no capacity requirements are generated. The determination of the in- house production time can be carried out as quantity dependent or quantity indepen- dent. In both cases, the system refers to the entries in the material master and also takes the factory calendar into account. Tips and Tricks You use Transactions CA96 and CA97 to update the In-House Production parameter in terms of days in the MRP 2 view of the material master (see Figure 13.38). At the same time, Transaction CA97 helps you with updating the Assembly Scrap field in the mate- rial master, which is taken from the total operation scrap in the routing of material. Figure 13.38 In-House Production Time and Scheduling Margin Key Note The scheduling margin key is the float, which consists of the number of days it takes to release a production order and the number of days before and after production when the material finally becomes available. Scheduling margin keys are buffer times for scheduling. The next two sections cover in-house production times that are either dependent on or independent of the quantity produced. Quantity-Independent In-House Production Time In this case, the in-house production time doesn’t depend on the order quantity; that is, small quantities require the same amount of time as large quantities. This assumption is valid for planning if the order quantity is limited by the lot sizing procedure, if expe- rience has shown that order quantities usually don’t differ very much, or if the daily granularity can cover fluctuations of the order quantity. Quantity-Dependent In-House Production Time If you can no longer ignore the influence of the order quantity on the in-house produc- tion time, then you can calculate the in-house production time as quantity-dependent. 13 Material Requirements Planning 722 For this purpose, you carry out a detailed evaluation of the in-house production times and maintain the Setup time, Interoperation, and Processing time fields as well as the Base quantity in the Work Scheduling view of the material master (see Figure 13.39). The interoperation time represents a summary of move times, wait times, and safety times, and only the processing time is quantity dependent because it depends on the base quantity. The setup and interoperation times are quantity independent and are added to the processing time. The following formula describes the quantity-dependent in-house production time: In-house production time = Setup + (Order quantity ÷ Base quantity) × Processing time + Interoperation time You maintain these parameters for either the quantity-independent or the quantity- dependent in-house production time (see Figure 13.39). Figure 13.39 In-House Production Time in Days

sections.title: Planned Order Dates
sections.en: 13.9.3 Planned Order Dates In backward scheduling, a planner can derive the dates for the planned order from the requirement date. To do this, they must deduct the various periods—including the opening period, the in-house production time, and the GR processing time—from the requirement date. In forward scheduling, the system shifts the order start date to the current date, and the opening period is omitted. Forward scheduling occurs in the MRP run only if the requirement date is too close to the current date. Figure 13.40 shows the Dates/Times area for the planned order for the material, in which it takes the various dates into account to arrive at the start and finish dates of the order. Figure 13.40 Basic Dates Determination in Planned Order © 723 13.9 Scheduling Because this is backward scheduling, you enter the End (order finish) date as “09/30/ 2024.” The system then uses SMK 001 to come up with the basic date calculations. All dates are on workdays (Monday through Friday), per the factory calendar. While remaining on the Change Planned Order screen, you also perform scheduling by choosing the relevant icon. In scheduling, the system accurately calculates the time it takes to perform each activity at every operation of the work center.

sections.title: Scheduling External Procurement
sections.en: 13.9.4 Scheduling External Procurement When planned orders for externally procured materials or purchase requisitions are created, the system refers to the Planned Delivery Time information in the material master to calculate how long (on average) it will take for the supplier to deliver the req- uisite material (see Figure 13.41). Figure 13.41 Scheduling for Planned Delivery Time The planned delivery time refers to the workdays that appear on the factory calendar, provided a planning calendar hasn’t been specified. As an alternative to determining the planned delivery time from the material master, a planner can determine the planned delivery time from the purchasing info record. To do this, the scheduling type must be allowed in the plant parameters. Moreover, the source list for the material must contain a relevant entry. Tips and Tricks You use Transaction WPDTC to calculate and compare the planned delivery time of a material (or group of materials) in the material master, purchasing info record, and supplier. The scheduling process for external procurement is carried out similarly to that of in- house production, except that it uses four periods (for backward scheduling):  Opening period  Purchasing department processing time  Planned delivery time  GR processing time 13 Material Requirements Planning 724 These periods are deducted from the requirement date, and the purchasing depart- ment processing time is maintained in the Plant parameters area (see Figure 13.42). These options are described in Section 13.11.3. Figure 13.42 Purchase Processing Time in Configuration

sections.title: Forward and Backward Scheduling
sections.en: 13.9.5 Forward and Backward Scheduling You can make a distinction between the scheduling types regarding whether to use backward scheduling or forward scheduling, and you can choose whether production takes place in-house or externally. Similarly, the following interdependencies exist for different MRP procedures:  Materials planned in forecast-based planning  MRP For the two MRP procedures, the start dates are known, and the planning should ensure that materials are available on those dates. The determination of basic dates for in- house production and of the release date for purchasing therefore occurs as backward scheduling in these MRP procedures. Only if the basic date determined during schedul- ing is in the past does the system automatically switch to forward scheduling from today’s date (when the MRP run took place). In reorder point planning, the dates are defined by means of forward scheduling. After falling short of the reorder point during the MRP run, a planner needs to initiate pro- curement activities. The availability date of the materials is determined based on the date of the material shortage. © 725

sections.title: Procurement Proposals
sections.en: 13.10 Procurement Proposals For external procurement, the order finish date corresponds to the delivery date, while the order start date corresponds to the release date. Here, the processing time in pur- chasing, the GR processing time in terms of workdays, and the planned delivery time of the material in terms of calendar days are all considered. For external procurement, the system creates either a planned order or a purchase requisition. Purchase requisitions for external procurement plan the external procurement quantity. After the system completes the planning, you as a planner can convert the planned order into a pur- chase requisition, and then, the purchase requisition is converted into a purchase order. On the initial screen of the planning run, you use the Creation indicator for pur- chase requisitions to define whether the system should directly create purchase requi- sitions or whether it should first create planned orders. If a delivery plan exists for a material, and if the source list contains an MRP-relevant entry, then you can directly generate delivery schedules in MRP. This applies if there’s a scheduling agreement for materials with the supplier. You do this by specifying the Creation indicator for delivery schedules on the initial screen of the planning run. 13.10 Procurement Proposals When the MRP is run in the system, the system looks for procurement type informa- tion to decide whether the ensuing procurement proposals entail in-house production, external procurement, or both. The procurement type in the MRP 2 view of the material master defines which of the alternatives applies to the material in the plant (see Figure 13.43). If both in-house production and external procurement are allowed, MRP assumes in-house production by default. For example, the raw material is generally not sold or produced in house, so it makes sense to maintain the procurement type as external. Similarly, the finished product isn’t procured but instead is produced in house, so it makes sense to assign it an in-house procurement type. Finally, the semi- finished good may be produced in house as well as procured externally, so you can assign the procurement as both. Figure 13.43 Procurement Type in Material Master 13 Material Requirements Planning 726 In the MRP run, the system creates planned orders. These planned orders are converted into a production order (or process order) for in-house production. For external pro- curement, the purchase requisitions can be converted into a PO or PO item. In the following sections, we’ll discuss how to interactively create planned orders from a stock/requirements list. We’ll also show you how to independently create a purchase requisition for a material and the planned order profile it uses. Finally, you’ll see how to check for component availability, including available-to-promise logic.

sections.title: Planned Orders
sections.en: 13.10.1 Planned Orders Planned orders contain an order quantity, a start date, and a finish date. The start and finish dates are the result of the scheduling process and are based on the factory calen- dar. The availability date can occur after the finish date if a GR processing time has been maintained. In addition, the planned order contains the dependent requirements from the BOM explosion, as well as organizational data. There are a couple of ways in which you can manually create planned orders:  While remaining in the stock/requirements list (via Transaction MD04)  By using Transaction MD11, which is the planned order creation transaction For this example, we’ll show you how to create a planned order in the stock/require- ments list. We’ll also show you how to manually create a planned order by using Trans- action MD11. Planned Order Creation with Interactive Planning To carry out interactive planning by specifically creating planned orders, you can use Transaction MD11 or you can use interactive MRP for material and plant via Transac- tion MD43. In the latter case, the system displays the current requirement and stock sit- uation. By clicking the Planning button, you can trigger automatic MRP (see Figure 13.44). The system then displays the planning result, which is still only a simulation at this point. The system won’t create the planned order until you save the planning results. More- over, Transaction MD43 enables you to manually create and reschedule specific pro- curement proposals, when you choose the Procurement Proposal, Production Order, or Rescheduling button. Figure 13.44 shows the planning results for Material 2267 and Plant 1710. While remain- ing in interactive planning, you should choose Create procurement proposal. Then, in the Select Procurement Proposal popup (see Figure 13.45), you select the procurement proposal, which for this example is a Purchase requisition with the Purchase.requisition key (or document type) NB. Chose Continue or press (Enter) to open the Purchase © 727 13.10 Procurement Proposals requisition creation screen (see Figure 13.46). Then, enter the details, such as order quantity, start date, and so on, and click the checkmark button to continue. Figure 13.44 Planning Results Figure 13.45 Interactively Creating Planned Order Figure 13.46 Planned Order: Detailed Screen 13 Material Requirements Planning 728 The system then incorporates a purchase requisition for the material (see Figure 13.47). For this example, it creates purchase requisition number 100001495. Figure 13.47 Purchase Requisition in Stock/Requirements List Manual Creation of Planned Order You use Transaction MD11 to manually create a planned order. On the initial screen that appears, enter “LA” for Plnned Order Profile, which supports MTS production. Then, press (Enter) and provide the Material number, Plant number, Order Quantity, and Basic Dates, as shown in Figure 13.48. Figure 13.48 Planned Order Creation © 729 13.10 Procurement Proposals A manually created planned order is always firmed, and the system denotes it with an asterisk (*) in the stock/requirements list (Transaction MD04). Checking the Conversion Ind. box ensures that the planned order can eventually be converted into either a pro- duction order (or a process order) for in-house production or a purchase requisition for external procurement. Next, we perform three functions: checking the availability of the material’s compo- nents to ensure smooth production, checking the components’ ATP, and also checking for any capacity overload. Figure 13.49 shows the BOM (component) details of Material 2265. Here, you can make any desired changes, if needed. Figure 13.49 Components Availability in Planned Order Next, increase the planned order quantity of material 2267 to 10,000 units (or any large quantity) and then choose Component ATP (see Figure 13.48) to open the Availability Check screen (see Figure 13.50). Notice that the available component quantities are shown with green lines (if any), while the system reflects the shortages with yellow lines. Figure 13.50 Components Availability and ATP Check 13 Material Requirements Planning 730 Next, choose the Back icon, which leads you to the planned order header screen. Here, the system can only commit to a material’s planned order quantity of 10,000 because it uses an available-to-promise check, which enables the system to advise you of the quantity that it can commit to or promise, based on the current available stock and other reservations or issuances. While you’re in the planned order creation screen, you should choose Schedule Planned Order to make the system perform detailed scheduling activity; the results will appear in the new Detailed Scheduling tab in the planned order. Then, choose Edit  Capacity Check to bring up the screen shown in Figure 13.51, which denotes many days of capacity overload in the red rows. Figure 13.51 Capacity Check

sections.title: Collective Availability Check on Planned Orders
sections.en: 13.10.2 Collective Availability Check on Planned Orders You can use Transaction MDVP to perform a collective availability check on a large number of planned orders (see Figure 13.52). When you execute the transaction, the sys- tem will display a list of planned orders along with the results of the last availability check, including the Confirmation Factor (the percentage of the total order quantity that could be committed), Committed Date, and Committed Quantity values. A traffic light in the first column indicates the results of the availability check:  A green light means the availability check was carried out and the total order quan- tity was committed.  A yellow light means the availability check has not yet been carried out.  A red light means the availability check was carried out, but there were missing com- ponents. © 731 13.10 Procurement Proposals Figure 13.52 Collective Availability Check on Planned Orders Note To run the mass availability check as a background job, you can schedule a job with program RLD05000. In this case, the relevant parameters are in the Check Mode (Back- ground Processing) box. You can also carry out collective availability checks for planned orders by using Trans- action COHV.

sections.title: Planned Order Profile
sections.en: 13.10.3 Planned Order Profile As you’ve already seen during planned order creation, a user has to enter a planned order profile. To define the order profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Procurement Proposals  Planned Orders  Define Order Profile. The order profile is used for both planned orders (Object Type 1) and pur- chase requisitions (Object Type 2), and it contains information on the permitted pro- curement types and special procurement types. Other available object types include the following:  Reservation  Production order  Simulation order To create a new planned order profile, you choose New Entries, enter a name for the planned order profile, and select the object type. Finally, in the detailed screen, you can enter the procurement type or even special procurement control parameters. Let’s now discuss some of the important MRP configuration settings before delving into discussing how to run MRP and evaluate the results. 13 Material Requirements Planning 732

sections.title: Configuration Settings for Material Requirements Planning
sections.en: 13.11 Configuration Settings for Material Requirements Planning In the following sections, we’ll discuss the configuration settings you need to ensure that MRP runs at the plant level. They are as follows:  MRP activation  Scope of planning configuration  Plant parameters configuration  MRP group configuration

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Activation
sections.en: 13.11.1 Material Requirements Planning Activation This configuration step allows you to choose the plant for which to activate the MRP. To do so, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  MRP  Planning File Entry  Activate MRP and Set Up Planning File or use Transaction OMDU.

sections.title: Scope of Planning Configuration
sections.en: 13.11.2 Scope of Planning Configuration When you’re running plant-level MRP with Transaction MD01N, the scope of MRP gen- erally refers to a specific plant. However, you can also refer to several plants they are defined in the corresponding planning scope. To define the planning scope, you use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Planning  Define Scope of Planning for Total Planning.

sections.title: Plant Parameters Configuration
sections.en: 13.11.3 Plant Parameters Configuration You can confiture MRP to the following three levels:  Plant parameters  MRP groups  Material master (MRP views) Material master settings have higher priority than MRP group parameters, which in turn have higher priority than plant parameters. No setting can be found on all three levels, but they can be on up to two of them. Therefore, plant parameters are usually used to set defaults, which you can overrule if necessary. The plant parameters of MRP contain a view of numerous configuration settings that are relevant to MRP. The plant parameters screen shows whether certain configuration settings have been maintained or whether they are still in their initial status. Using this screen enables a user to navigate directly to the relevant configuration area. In the plant parameter configuration step, the following buttons are some of the con- figurations:  Reference Plant  Number ranges © 733 13.11 Configuration Settings for Material Requirements Planning  Direct Procurement  Available Stock  Rescheduling  External Procurement  Conversion of Planned Orders  Dependent Requirements Availability  Floats (Schedule Margin Keys)  BOM Explosion  BOM/Routing Selection To configure plant parameters, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Plant Parameters or use Transaction OPPQ. Then, choose the Maintain button and a popup will appear. There, enter the Plant as “1710” and then choose the Maintain icon. A detailed screen will then appear in which you can maintain the individual plant parameters.

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Group Configuration
sections.en: 13.11.4 Material Requirements Planning Group Configuration You can group materials together by using MRP groups and then assigning the MRP groups in the MRP 1 view of the material master. Each of the MRP groups can have its own set of MRP parameters. When working with an MRP group, the following options that are specific to a material or group of materials are available:  Strategy group  Settlement type and horizon  Rescheduling horizon and planning horizon  Production storage location selection  Conversion order types of planned orders  Planning horizon  Planning time fence and roll-forward periods  BOM and task list selection IDs  Direct procurement parameters  Planned order scheduling parameters  Start number of days allowed in the past  Availability checking groups  Period split for distributing the independent requirement  Maximum MRP intervals  Availability of safety stock for MRP  BOM explosion  Direct procurement of nonstock items  Creation indicators for purchase requisitions  Scheduling of external procurement according to the information record  Checking rule for dependent demand 13 Material Requirements Planning 734 To configure MRP groups, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  Material Requirements Planning  MRP Groups  Carry Out Overall Maintenance of MRP Groups or use Transaction OPPR. On the initial screen, enter the Plant as “1710” and choose the Maintain button. In the popup that appears, enter MRP Group “0001” and choose the Maintain button. Figure 13.53 and Figure 13.54 show parts of the detailed MRP Group screen where you maintain individual parameters of the MRP group. Figure 13.53 Maintain MRP Group, Partial Screen 1 Figure 13.54 Maintain MRP Group, Partial Screen 2 © 735

sections.title: MRP Live
sections.en: 13.12 MRP Live Once you’ve configured and saved an MRP group, assign it in the MRP 1 view of the material master (see Figure 13.55). Figure 13.55 MRP Group in Material Master Note While this example covers the maintenance of an MRP group, you can also create a new MRP group, delete an existing MRP group, or copy an MRP group from an existing one. You can also assign the MRP group to a material type by following menu path SAP IMG  Logistics  Production  Material Requirements Planning  MRP Groups  Define MRP Group for Each Material Type. You can now put to use the material requirements planning foundations you built in the previous sections by running MRP Live and evaluating the planning results. You can also tailor storage location planning with MRP area functionality, and we’ll discuss that as well. 13.12 MRP Live In MRP Live, the entire MRP logic—including database selection— is carried out directly in SAP HANA. This means that all the MRP steps, from net requirements calcu- lation to the creation of the replenishment proposal, are carried out in SAP HANA. Let’s first discuss how MRP Live differs from classic MRP transactions, including the main functionalities, restrictions, and transactions available for MRP Live, compared with classic MRP. 13 Material Requirements Planning 736 The new Transaction MD01N was created specifically to run MRP Live. In classic MRP, there were different transactions available to execute a total MRP run (Transaction MD01); a single-item, multilevel MRP run (Transaction MD02); and a single-item, single- level MRP run (Transaction MD03). Transaction MD01N replaces all of those classic MRP transactions and provides more flexible selection criteria with more fields and options for selection, as shown in Figure 13.56. However, it’s not possible to replace multilevel MTO planning (via Transaction MD50) and individual project planning (via Transaction MD51) with MRP Live. For such busi- ness scenarios, you should use the classic MRP transactions, or you should plan the header material within MRP Live, making sure to check the BOM Components box so that all the components are also checked. Transaction MD01N was also designed via the simplification concept. Therefore, it includes the following changes from the classic MRP transactions:  The creation indicator for purchase requisitions and schedule lines doesn’t exist in Transaction MD01N. This means that MRP Live directly creates purchase requisi- tions for materials procured externally or creates a schedule list.  The creation indicator for MRP lists is also not available in MRP Live, for perfor- mance reasons. This means that MRP lists aren’t created by MRP Live. If you as a planner need to create MRP lists, then you should plan the material with the classic MRP transactions (e.g., Transaction MD01). Otherwise, use the stock/requirements list (Transaction MD04) to analyze the MRP results.  The scope of planning is no longer necessary because it’s now possible to select one or more plants in Transaction MD01N. MRP Live can also automatically determine the sequence of plants for planning, so the scope of planning becomes obsolete with MRP Live.  Processing key NETPL (net change in the planning horizon) isn’t available in Trans- action MD01N. This processing key was created to restrict the MRP run on the plan- ning horizon for a faster MRP run, and because MRP performance is much better with MRP Live, this processing key and the planning horizon concept are now obso- lete.  The flag to determine whether parallel processing should be used isn’t available in MRP Live; parallel processing is automatically triggered by the system. In addition, it’s no longer required to define the destinations for parallel processing in Transac- tion OMIQ because they will be determined automatically, based on the server group.  The user exit key and user exit parameter fields aren’t available. In the classic MRP transactions, user exits SAPLM61C_001 and EXIT_SAPMM61X_ 001 allowed the user to select which materials would be planned by MRP, and their most common use was to plan all the materials of a specific MRP controller. In Transaction MD01N, the MRP controller is already available as a selection criterion. If you need to use a different © 737 13.12 MRP Live selection criterion, you can use the product group or the multiple material selection as a selection criteria. Figure 13.56 MRP Live In addition to the changes related to the transaction design, there are the following sig- nificant changes to the MRP process:  Total requirements aren’t supported on MRP Live. Just like the classic MRP transactions, total dependent and sales requirements aren’t supported on MRP Live.  Subcontracting on MRP Live is only supported with MRP areas. In classic MRP transactions, a separated planning segment is created for each supplier in a subcontracting scenario. In MRP Live, however, this functionality is no longer available. This means that the subcontracting scenario must be mapped with MRP areas. Therefore, you need to create an MRP area for each supplier in configuration and assign each such MRP area to the materials that will use the subcontracting sce- nario. See Section 13.15 for more on MRP planning areas.  Replenishment proposals aren’t reusable. In classic MRP transactions, unfirmed planned orders and purchase requisitions weren’t necessarily changed during the MRP run in the case of a quantity or date change. Now, in MRP Live, if the date or quantity of a replenishment proposal must 13 Material Requirements Planning 738 be changed by MRP, the replenishment proposal is deleted and a new one is created. Basically, this means that the number of existing planned orders and purchase req- uisitions may be changed more frequently in MRP Live than in classic MRP. Note As MRP Live is a relatively new material planning tool, some very specific settings aren’t yet implemented; a complete overview of those restrictions can be found in SAP Note 1914010. Under very specific scenarios, the number of replenishment proposals generated by MRP Live can differ from the number of replenishment proposals generated by classic MRP. This happens, for example, when you’re using a reorder point MRP type with external requirements (e.g., MRP type V1) or a safety stock. This happens because, in classic MRP, the safety stock (or reorder point) quantity is added to the replenishment proposal created to cover the first requirement. In MRP Live, on the other hand, a sepa- rate replenishment proposal is created to cover the safety stock. Even though there may be more replenishment proposals in MRP Live, the total quantity of the replenish- ment proposals generated should be the same as the total quantity in classic MRP. We’ll demonstrate this in an example in Section 13.15. Note A new set of MRP applications collectively called the MRP simulation cockpit was deliv- ered in the latest versions of SAP S/4HANA. These applications are enhanced, and new applications were created as an alternative way to evaluate MRP results. The SAP Fiori applications of the MRP simulation cockpit place a strong focus on identifying and resolving shortages that may lead to supply chain disruptions. Refer to Chapter 21 for detailed discussions of these apps. To access MRP Live, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Production  MRP  Planning  MRP Live or use Transaction MD01N (see Figure 13.56). There are far more materials planning options available in MRP Live, such as selecting a specific group of materials and saving them as a variant. Planning can happen via product group and MRP controller, and you can even choose a material scope to decide whether MRP Live is to run on master production scheduling materials only, MRP materials only, or both. You can also plan BOM components. Enter the parameters shown in Figure 13.56 and choose Execute. Whenever MRP Live finds a material planning setting that is still not supported, it auto- matically switches to the classic MRP logic to plan without further problems. However, this isn’t the best approach to achieving optimal performance with MRP Live. A planner can identify for which materials a restriction was found and classic MRP was executed © 739 13.12 MRP Live by analyzing the MRP Live results in Transaction MD01N. On the MRP Live results screen, the system displays how many materials were planned directly in SAP HANA and how many were planned using the classic MRP logic. By choosing the Materials with Messages button on the results screen (see Figure 13.57), a planner can see exactly which materials could not be planned with MRP Live and which restrictions caused them to be planned via classic MRP logic. Figure 13.57 Planning Results from MRP Live Figure 13.58 shows that materials (those with 000 in the Cde column) were planned at the finished goods level via MRP Live, while more materials were planned at code level 001. Two materials were planned using classic MRP, and the details are shown in Figure 13.59. Figure 13.58 Materials Planned via MRP Live and Classic MRP Figure 13.59 also shows the materials with messages after the MRP Live execution. If you choose the Solve Issue button, it will redirect you to the master data configuration where this restriction is found (generally, the material master) so that you can change the settings to avoid this restriction and ensure that the material is planned with MRP Live. Figure 13.59 Materials with Messages from MRP Live 13 Material Requirements Planning 740 After running MRP, a materials planner can spend more time in the stock/require- ments list evaluating the supply-demand situation of the material and taking actions where required.

sections.title: Stock/Requirements List
sections.en: 13.13 Stock/Requirements List We’ll now briefly explain a few of the available analysis and evaluation functions of the stock/requirements list after an MRP Live run. The stock/requirements list is not only a materials evaluation tool but also an intuitive dashboard that a materials planner can use to create, change, or delete planning elements. By accessing the planning, production, sales, or procurement details of either an indi- vidual material or several materials collectively, the stock/requirements list details the parameters—such as MRP type, lot size, and the replenishment lead time—that are used in material planning. The logical and structured grouping of exception messages enables a materials planner to immediately see which materials need urgent action, such as expediting procurement or production to meet customer orders or even can- celing or delaying procurement or production orders that are no longer needed as early as originally planned. Evaluation tools, such as a stock overview or a day’s supply, pro- vide planners with complete visibility into the demand and supply situations of mate- rials. Stock Overview There is another important report—the stock overview report—that isn’t discussed in this section. The stock overview report provides the current stock situation of the material and is quite helpful for material planners. You can use Transaction MMBE to access the stock overview report. In the initial parameter screen, enter the Material number, together with the Plant, and select other parameters, such as Storage Loca- tion and Special Stock Indicator. With the stock overview report, you can only view the stock details of one material at a time. Choose Execute or press (F8). If you need to view the warehouse stock of a large number of materials all at the same time, then use Transaction MB52.

sections.title: Overview
sections.en: 13.13.1 Overview Figure 13.60 shows the Stock/Requirements List: Initial Screen (Transaction MD04). The stock/requirements list describes all MRP-relevant elements for a material within a plant. These include the stock, customer requirements and PIRs, dependent demands, planned orders, and production orders. In addition, it shows parameters that are rele- vant to MRP, such as the safety stock and the planning time fence. For all elements, you can display and change the details, if this is permitted. Usually, the system displays the © 741 13.13 Stock/Requirements List availability date of a GR, but you can display the GR date instead—for example, to plan actions in case of delays. We’ll also discuss the following options that are available in the stock/requirements list, along with several other options:  Displaying exception messages  Jumping into the detail view  Jumping into the order report  Displaying the GR date or availability date  Showing the replenishment lead time  Changing the MRP elements Figure 13.60 Initial Screen of Stock/Requirements List, Individual Access Figure 13.61 shows the stock/requirements list, and choosing the Expand header details icon in the top-left corner of the screen will reveal all the parameters used by the sys- tem in planning the material. In other words, there’s no longer a need to separately view the MRP parameters in the material master; you can do so while remaining in the stock/requirements list. Let’s evaluate some more details shown in Figure 13.61. On 07/13/2024 (July 13, 2024), the opening Stock of Material 2264 in the Available Qty column was 111. There are quite a few planned independent requirements (IndReq), as well as a few process and planned orders. To interactively convert a planned order into a production order, you choose the Ele- ment Details icon (the magnifying glass) next to MRP element 2947 in Figure 13.61, and the popup shown in Figure 13.62 will appear. 13 Material Requirements Planning 742 Figure 13.61 Stock/Requirements List Figure 13.62 Converting Planned Order into Production Order Figure 13.62 offers more details about system-generated (automatic) planned order 0000002947, which is for a quantity of 10,900. The MRP controller can both display this planned order and make any manual changes if needed. Furthermore, you can also convert this planned order into a production order. To do so, choose -> Prod.Ord. and the screen shown in Figure 13.63 will appear. Figure 13.63 shows a production order, and choosing the Assignment tab here will show you the automatically assigned planned order number 2947 in the lower half of the screen (not shown). Next, save the production order, and the system will create a pro- duction order number, which in this example is 80000015. Figure 13.64 is the stock/requirements list (from Transaction MD04) after conversion of planned order 2947 into a production order. Production order 000080000015 is dis- played for a quantity of 100. © 743 13.13 Stock/Requirements List Figure 13.63 Production Order Created from Planned Order Conversion Figure 13.64 Converted Production Order in Stock/Requirements List Next, refer to the initial screen of Transaction MD04, as shown in Figure 13.60, but this time, choose Collective access and enter any of the several selection parameters, such as MRP Controller 101. Doing so will bring up a list of materials for which MRP controller 101 is responsible for planning. You can also use transaction MD07 for collective access to the stock/requirements list. 13 Material Requirements Planning 744

sections.title: Traffic Lights for Days’ Supply and Receipt Days’ Supply
sections.en: 13.13.2 Traffic Lights for Days’ Supply and Receipt Days’ Supply The red, yellow, and green traffic lights in both Transaction MD06 and Transaction MD07 in Figure 13.65 give a quick indication of potential overstocking or understocking in the list view:  A red light will appear if a demand from the past is still unfulfilled. How far in the past this demand element lies is specified by the negative days of supply shown in a column in the list display.  A green light doesn’t mean everything is in order. It can also mean that there is more stock than needed to fulfill requirements in the future. Green lights, therefore, can alert a planner if there is too much inventory held.  Yellow lights are assigned to materials for which demand and supply are in perfect balance. Figure 13.65 Stock/Requirements List, Collective Access SAP S/4HANA allows you to make changes to the traffic light settings to tell the system when a material’s planning should be alerted with a red or green light and what range is acceptable for an item with balanced demand and supply. You can also develop a traffic light system to provide relevant and useful information. For example, you could consider setting up the list so that traffic lights are sorted with the most urgent material handling on top and long-term opportunities at the bottom. One such scenario could involve setting the monitor so that materials with stock days of supply below safety levels (and not only after they are overdue) would have a red light. Anything that has receipts coming in to cover demand within an acceptable time frame would get a yellow light, and anything that has too many receipts coming too early—and which therefore needs to be expedited out—would get a green light. © 745 13.13 Stock/Requirements List You can also use the traffic lights for exception messages: the exception groups consid- ered more important can be above the ones that aren’t so urgent, combined with the ranges of coverage. In the stock/requirements list or in the MRP list, the system calculates the number of days on the time axis for which the existing stock of material can fulfill the require- ments. Receipt days’ supply indicates how long the current stock and the expected receipts can cover the requirements. The system offers two different types of receipt days’ supply:  Receipt days’ supply 1  Receipt days’ supply 2 You can select the receipt elements for both types of receipt days’ supplies by following menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Define Receipt Elements for Receipt Days’ Supply. In this step, you select which procurement elements the system should consider in or exclude from its calculation for days’ supply. For example, you can define two receipt days’ supplies and instruct the system to take the less binding receipt elements into account for the first receipt day’s supply and take only the binding receipt elements (such as production orders and shipping notifica- tions) into account for the second day’s supply. The Safety Stock field controls whether the days’ stock supply considers safety stock while calculating the number of days until the warehouse stock falls below the safety stock. If the indicator 1 is maintained, then the system uses the number of days until the stock falls below 0.

sections.title: Exception Messages
sections.en: 13.13.3 Exception Messages The system generates exception messages in MRP when it encounters alerts during the planning run. The system displays exception messages for each relevant procurement element in the stock/requirements list (refer to the Exception column in Figure 13.64). You can also view the exception elements by choosing Edit  Find in the List. The main elements of exception messages are priority, assignment to group, suppress message, create MRP list, and text of exception message. Exception messages are dis- played primarily in four transactions:  Transaction MD04 displays the dynamically created stock requirements situations for an individual material  Transaction MD05 displays the MRP list, which is a snapshot from the time when the MRP run last planned the material  Transaction MD06 displays a collective list of materials with their respective MRP lists  Transaction MD07 displays a collective list of materials with their respective stock/ requirements situations 13 Material Requirements Planning 746 Tips and Tricks You can change text of exception messages to denote the importance of an exception. For example, changing the text to “Warning: stock fallen below safety stock level” will help a planner quickly separate exception messages with a warning from exception messages with information. To make limited changes to the existing exception mes- sages, follow configuration menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Exception Messages  Define and Group Exception Messages. Let’s now briefly discuss the eight different exception message groups. Group 1 Exceptions An MRP run generates exception messages in group 1 when the date that was deter- mined using the opening period is in the past. For example, the start date of a planned order might be November 15. That is the date when the planned order must be con- verted into a process order so that the goods are produced on time to fulfill a demand. From this date, the system then subtracts the working days—for example, four days— from the opening period (the number of days maintained for the opening period in the scheduling margin key) and arrives at the opening date of the planned order, which in this example will be November 11. If the planned order isn’t converted into a produc- tion order after November 11, the system will display a message in this group. In other words, it’s an extra time buffer to alert the planner to convert an order proposal into a fixed supply element. Group 2 Exceptions This group contains exception messages that inform you about missing the release date for converting a purchase requisition or planned order into a purchase order or a production order, respectively. The release date can be seen in Transaction MD04. There are actually two hidden columns—one for the opening date and another for the release date. If the Start/Release Date and Opening Date columns aren’t visible in Transaction MD04, then you need to be sure to make them visible. Move the cursor so that the two lines can be seen and then drag the columns open. These columns provide valuable information about opening and conversion dates for the order proposals. The release date was calculated by subtracting the lead time from the requirements date (backward scheduling); therefore, it represents the latest date by which an order proposal (fixed, converted, a PO sent out to supplier, or a production order sent to the shop floor) has to start so that the material can be received on the required date. If this date is missed, on-time material availability might not happen, thereby causing service levels to degrade. As in group 1, an exception informs the user of a new order proposal, 03, and an excep- tion informs the user of an existing order proposal. This time, however, it’s the start (or release) date that is relevant. To address why the 03 New, and start date in the past © 747 13.13 Stock/Requirements List message is issued, you must evaluate possible lead time errors in the master data or look at the availability checking rule, which might have allowed a requirement to drop in within the replenishment lead time. It’s also possible to maintain configuration set- tings so the system switches to forward scheduling from today’s date onward if the start date falls in the past. The 06 Start date in the past message informs the user of an existing order proposal that has missed its conversion date. To address these errors, you must get all these ele- ments up to date and release them on time with the correct lead time, which will deter- mine the correct start/release date. If you need to reschedule an order proposal instead of releasing it, then check the basic data setup to see if it’s faulty and doesn’t generate the correct dates automatically. When exception 63 Production start before order start appears, it’s due to incorrect configuration settings. The system has suggested a production start date that lies before the order start date in lead time scheduling. You can fix this message by select- ing the option to adjust the basic dates in the scheduling parameters for the order pro- posals (via Transaction OPU5). Group 3 Exceptions Group 3 messages reflect a more serious problem as they not only inform you about missing the start/release date of the order but also indicate that the finish date has passed as well. With the 04 New, and finish date in the past message, the MRP run keeps trying to adjust the proposals to match supply with demand. Dates are adjusted with new order proposal creation, which creates a bullwhip effect in the planning pro- cess. You need to prioritize the exception monitoring to ensure finish dates in the past don’t happen at all or occur only minimally. The 07 Finish date in the past message is very critical because it alerts the user to an impending outage, unless there’s enough safety stock to get through the demand that’s waiting for that order to be received. Message 07 can come from a purchase req- uisition, a planned order, a PO, or a production/process order. In all cases, immediate action is warranted. Under no circumstances should there be orders in the system for which the finish date has passed. This actually means that the finish date (or the GR date, in the case of a purchase order) lies in the past. Another part of group 3 for late finishing is exception message 64 Production finish after order finish, which informs the user of an inconsistency in scheduling. The pro- duction finish, which was calculated using the routing’s lead time, lies after the basic date (calculated from the material master’s lead time). Again, this is because the basic dates aren’t adjusted—due to a setting in configuration Transaction OPU5. Group 4 Exceptions Exceptions from group 4 are general messages and are for informational purposes only to address master data or configuration inconsistencies. These exceptions can only be in Transaction MD06 (Collective MRP Lists), not in Transaction MD07 (Collective 13 Material Requirements Planning 748 Stock/Requirements Situation), because the system can’t tell when the last planning run was. This is because the dynamically created stock/requirements situation (in Transaction MD07) has no time stamp, and therefore, the system doesn’t know the new from the old. The following are some of the exception messages in group 4:  01 Newly Created Order Proposal An order proposal was newly created in the last planning run. This message is gener- ated if there is new demand in the system or when MRP runs use planning mode 3 (delete planning dates).  42 Order Proposal Has Been Changed The order proposal was changed in the planning run, either because of a change in the demand situation or because of changes made in the master data settings after the last planning run.  44 Order Proposal Re-exploded The order proposal’s BOM was re-exploded in the planning run, and new dependent requirements were created.  46 Order Proposal Has Been Manually Changed The order proposal was manually changed in the single-item planning run. Group 5 Exceptions Group 5 exception messages result from errors in the BOM explosion during the MRP run. Message 50 No BOM Exists means there is most likely no BOM for the material, or, in sales order processing, a sales order BOM could be missing. If the MRP run generates a planned order to cover demand but it can’t find a BOM suitable for the order, message 52 No BOM Selected appears. It could be that there are BOMs assigned to the material, but the selection criteria can’t assign the appropriate one to the planned order. If exception message 53 No BOM Explosion Due to Missing Config. appears, it’s due to missing configuration and customization because the line items in the BOM didn’t receive dependent requirements. Message 54 No Valid Run Schedule is an exception when using repetitive manufactur- ing. A run schedule is like a planned order, and the correct quantities couldn’t be deter- mined by the MRP run. Group 6 Exceptions Group 6 exceptions result from an availability check that evaluates stock shortages or excess stock. All messages in this group are related to quantities and stock levels. For example, message 25 Excess Stock points to the planned available stock’s coverage being greater than the maximum stock coverage specified in the range of coverage pro- file. Because the range of coverage profile is used to keep a check on minimum, target, or maximum stock levels, the MRP run checks to see inventory below the defined © 749 13.13 Stock/Requirements List levels. However, if there is a rounding value, fixed lot size, or minimum order quantity maintained in the policy, this maximum will be exceeded. For customer individual stock segments to manage individual customer stock, excep- tion 26 Shortage in Individual Segment will alert the user to higher stock levels than planned for in a range of coverage profile. When you’re using a planning time fence with fixed types (P2 or P4 in deterministic planning), message 56 No Requirements Coverage can alert you to a shortage within the planning time fence. Message 57 Disc. Matl (discontinued material) describes a situation wherein a material is partly replaced by a follow-up or replacement material used in the discontinuation process. As a planner can replace a part with a follow-up material in the discontinua- tion process, there may be a business need to receive an alert if the discontinued part was only partially replaced with the substitute material. Another exception in discon- tinuation is message 58 Uncovered Reqmt after Effective-Out Date, which is generated if a requirement exists after the effective-out date for a part that is to be discontinued and therefore can’t be covered by planned stock. And if there is a receipt—maybe from a previously generated PO—that brings in stock after the effective-out date of a discon- tinued part, exception message 59 Receipt after Effective-Out Date will appear. These are very helpful alerts that help you manage discontinuation of parts without interrup- tions, stock-outs, or leftover unconsumed stock. When using quota arrangements, a planner can source parts or materials from various vendors with an equal or unequal distribution of required quantities, called a quota. For example, a planner could order a quantity of 100 pieces for the same part from three different vendors with quotas of 40%, 35%, and 25% of the required total quantity. Should the MRP run, for whatever reason, exceed the maximum release quantity of the quota, then exception 70 Max. Release Qty—Quota Exceeded would appear. An exception message that may then appear often is 96 Stock Fallen below Safety Stock Level, which informs the user that the static safety stock set in the MRP 2 screen of the material master record is low. If you as a planner never or rarely receive this mes- sage, then the inventory level is probably too high, and all the safety stock probably turns into unused dead stock. So, it’s a good thing to receive the occasional message 96 because it means you’re making good use of the buffer without binding too much cash in inventory holdings. In addition, when a material has exception message 96, its traf- fic light turns to red, and its Days of Supply turns to –999.9, which means that all mate- rials whose safety stocks are breached can be seen at the top of the list in Transaction MD07. This might help you manage priorities and effectively address exception mes- sages. Group 7 Exceptions Four exception messages are contained in this group. One is for nonfixed order pro- posals (30), and the other three are for fixed replenishment elements, such as POs and production orders. The latter three require manual rescheduling of the orders because 13 Material Requirements Planning 750 they are fixed, and the MRP run doesn’t change them anymore. The system suggests the date to reschedule to. The four messages are explained in more detail here:  10 Bring Process Forward When a demand date or quantity has changed and a certain quantity of goods is needed at an earlier time, instead of creating another order proposal, the MRP run suggests bringing the already ordered quantities in for an earlier delivery date so they can be used to fulfill the changed demand. The rescheduling has to happen manually, and the process is often called expediting because the planner will need to reach out to the supplier to see if expediting the delivery to an earlier date (before the originally agreed upon lead time) is possible.  15 Postpone Process This message informs the user that the opposite has to happen: the ordered quanti- ties aren’t needed as early as was originally agreed to. It would be better if the sup- plier could deliver later because the demand has moved out to a later date. Not post- poning as suggested by message 15 will lead to having too much inventory too early.  20 Cancel Process This message occurs when a demand is reduced or disappears altogether. This mes- sage only appears for fixed supply elements such as a PO or a production/process order. At an earlier time, there must have been a demand causing the creation of an order proposal that must have been converted to a fixed supply order. Subse- quently, the demand must have disappeared or been reduced, but because the sup- ply was fixed, the MRP run can’t delete the order.  30 Plan Process According to Schedule The MRP run isn’t able to fulfill the demand because, according to master data or routing settings, the lead time is too long to bring the materials in on time. Group 8 Exceptions As with group 4, messages out of group 8 can’t be seen in Transaction MD07 but only in Transaction MD06. If the planning run was terminated for a material, then users see message 98 Abnormal End of Requirements Planning. This message may appear due to defective master data that prevents planning or even a prerequisite not being met, such as mandatory forecasting on the material not being performed prior to the MRP run.
