When you’re dealing with exception messages, the following are some of the ways you can prioritize handling them: 1. Group 8 is the most important of all the groups, so look at the abnormal ends, where the planning run aborts. Address these materials first. 2. Next, look at group 5. These concern structural problems, and the planning run can’t even come up with a date or quantity because the demand doesn’t get exploded down to the lower level. © 751 13.13 Stock/Requirements List 3. Then check out group 3. Not only was a date to firm a receipt missed, but the date when the receipt was supposed to come in is already too late to fulfill a demand. 4. After that, attend to the group 2 problems (wherein the date was missed when a pro- posal needed to be firmed) so that a planner can receive the quantity after the regu- lar lead time. If a date is missed, rescheduling and manual expediting is the only option. 5. Group 1 exceptions are only relevant while using the opening horizon to make an additional check when external procurement is triggered first by a planned order and then by a purchase requisition. 6. Therefore, you should tackle group 7 messages next. These require expediting and may take much more time to get through, but at least you can create a list at this point. You can work on the list later in the day (call vendors, check with purchasing, check the warehouse for inventory, etc.). 7. Next, deal with group 6. Inventory excess or shortages usually require a change in strategy. Again, you should make a list and see how to reduce these exceptions over time, by applying a different MRP type, changing the lot sizing procedure, looking at safety stock levels, and so on. 8. Finally, review the general messages in group 4 to see why there is a new proposal or why they have changed. These messages are informational only and don’t necessarily require action.

sections.title: Order Report
sections.en: 13.13.4 Order Report Next, access the screen shown in Figure 13.66 via Transaction MD4C or CO46. The order report will show each element of the stock/requirements list. In this example, it shows that production order 000080000005 of main material component 25 doesn’t provide the requirements coverage, as denoted by the red traffic light. The green traffic light further down the report (not shown) represents a satisfactory stock situation, whereas the red one represents a signal for the planner to take action. Figure 13.66 Order Report 13 Material Requirements Planning 752 Note To create a custom profile that can then be used in an order report, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning  Material Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Define Profiles for Order Report.

sections.title: Pegged Requirements
sections.en: 13.13.5 Pegged Requirements The order report finds all the receipts assigned to the requirements in a multilevel, top- down pegging overview, but it’s also possible to run a bottom-up pegging analysis to find which requirements are assigned to a given receipt element. You use Transaction MD09 to determine the pegged requirements (see Figure 13.67). Choose the Order Route and it will show the pegged requirements level by level in the lower half of the screen. Figure 13.67 Pegged Requirements

sections.title: Material Requirements Planning Elements
sections.en: 13.13.6 Material Requirements Planning Elements Because the system denotes each planning element separately, you should use the dropdown or filter function so that the system will only display the desired informa- tion (see Figure 13.68). © 753 13.13 Stock/Requirements List Figure 13.68 MRP Elements in Procurement without Requirements

sections.title: Header Details of Stock/Requirements List
sections.en: 13.13.7 Header Details of Stock/Requirements List Referring back to Figure 13.61, choose the Header Details icon to find all the parameters used by the system in planning the material. In other words, there’s no longer a need to separately view the MRP parameters in the material master; you can do so while remaining in the stock/requirements list (see Figure 13.69). Figure 13.69 Header Details 13 Material Requirements Planning 754

sections.title: Interactive Conversion of Procurement Elements
sections.en: 13.13.8 Interactive Conversion of Procurement Elements When you choose the Additional Data for MRP Element icon (the magnifying glass icon on the left-hand side of the stock/requirements list) for any procurement element (e.g., a planned order), a popup appears for you to perform the interactive conversion of a planned order into either a purchase requisition for external procurement or a production/process order for in-house production.

sections.title: Summation View
sections.en: 13.13.9 Summation View Figure 13.70 shows that a planner can toggle between the summation view and a detailed view of all planning elements (receipts, issuances/requirements, etc.). This icon is shown on the left-hand side of the figure (next to Individual List). Figure 13.70 Summation View of Stock Requirement List

sections.title: Stock Statistics
sections.en: 13.13.10 Stock Statistics The screen shown in Figure 13.71 appears when you choose Goto  Stock Statistics in the stock/requirements list. The stock statistics provide comprehensive details of the stock situation of the material. The stock/requirements list also provides a complete overview of the capacity situa- tion of the in-house produced materials so that you as a planner can act or react to any unusual capacity overload in specific work centers. To view the capacity situation, nav- igate to Goto  Capacity Situation in the stock/requirements list (see Figure 13.72). © 755 13.13 Stock/Requirements List Figure 13.71 Stock Statistics Figure 13.72 Capacity Situation 13 Material Requirements Planning 756

sections.title: User Settings
sections.en: 13.13.11 User Settings You can save individual display settings in the stock/requirements list to suit your preferences or business needs. Refer back to Figure 13.61: the Settings icon is available in the top-right hand side of the screen. Note We encourage you to explore the many other features and functionalities of the stock/ requirements list by choosing different icons and choosing from the menu options.

sections.title: Planning Calendar
sections.en: 13.14 Planning Calendar There are several instances in which either the company receives goods on specific days of the week or the supplier supplies goods only on specific days of the week. In other words, the planning of materials differs from the factory calendar of the company. For example, several agricultural products used in the production process of edible prod- ucts are produced only during specific periods of the year, and the company must pro- cure them and also store them to ensure they are available for consumption during the production process. For example, red chilies, an agricultural product, are only produced from January to April. The company uses a planning calendar to ensure the system cre- ates procurement proposals for this raw material (red chilies) within that four-month time period. It’s also possible to specify a planning date in the planning run. This option is useful because it enables a planner to bring the planning run forward to an earlier date. If the planning run is set for Monday, for example, this planning run can be performed on a Friday. The planning calendar is assigned in the MRP 2 view of the material master (Transac- tion MM02). Figure 13.73 shows the Planning Calendar field for maintaining the plan- ning calendar. Figure 13.73 Planning Calendar in Material Master © 757 13.14 Planning Calendar To create a new planning calendar, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Mate- rial Requirements Planning  Master Data  Maintain Planning Calendar or use Transac- tion MD25. On the screen shown in Figure 13.74, define the Planning Calendar as “ZPP” for Plant “1710” and select the Following Working Day radio button if the period start date isn’t on a working day. Figure 13.74 Setting Up Planning Calendar Next, you define a minimum number of periods that the system must generate in the planning calendar in the Minimum Period field. When you press (Enter), a popup will appear where you can select weekdays, work weeks, workdays, work months, and so on. Next, choose the Periods button, and the New Dates popup will appear and let you define the start and end date of the planning calendar. Then, choose Continue. In Figure 13.75, notice that all period planning starts on a Monday and that this is the same as is defined in the lower half of the first screen shown in Figure 13.74. 13 Material Requirements Planning 758 Figure 13.75 Planning Calendar

sections.title: Planning Areas
sections.en: 13.15 Planning Areas Apart from running MRP at a single plant level, material level, or product group level, you can activate the MRP areas if you have a business need to run MRP for one of the following:  A single storage location or a group of storage locations  A subcontractor The advantage of setting up MRP areas is that you can plan materials at the storage location level, which allows planning of materials at the storage location level to be dif- ferent from MRP planning of the material at the plant level. For example, if there are consumable materials that are stored at several storage locations in a plant, then you can set the MRP type of this material to “ND” (no planning) at the plant level. However, for two specific storage locations with high consumption of this consumable material, you can set the MRP type to “VB” (reorder point planning) to ensure that the system will help to replenish this material as soon as it drops below the reorder point level in those two storage locations. MRP areas make this material planning possible. © 759 13.15 Planning Areas In this section, we show you how to configure MRP areas, activate MRP areas in the material master (including defining important planning parameters), run the MRP for MRP areas, and finally evaluate the planning run’s results. Warning You can’t deactivate MRP areas after activating them in the SAP system. Therefore, you must carefully consider whether to use MRP areas before implementing them.

sections.title: Configuration
sections.en: 13.15.1 Configuration The two main configuration steps involved in setting up MRP areas are checking the MRP Area Active box and defining the MRP areas, as described here for a storage loca- tion: 1. To activate MRP areas, you use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Require- ments Planning  Master Data  MRP Areas  Activate MRP for MRP Areas. Here, you check the MRP Area Active box and save. 2. To define MRP areas, use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Material Requirements Planning  Master Data  MRP Areas  Define MRP Areas for Plant/Storage Locations. 3. Then, choose New Entries, set MRP Area as “1710–171A,” and provide a description. 4. For MRP Area Type, select 02 from the dropdown menu, which is for storage location MRP. Assign the Plant as “1710” and press (Enter). 5. On the left-hand side of the screen, double-click Assign Storage Locations, which leads you to the screen shown in Figure 13.76. Note MRP area type 02 is the standard type for defining additional MRP areas on the storage location level. MRP area type 01 is for the plant MRP areas and is created automatically when you convert the planning file for MRP areas. MRP area type 03 is for subcontract- ing, in which a company’s stock is maintained at a supplier’s premises, and it ensures materials planning. 6. Assign Recg. stor. loc. (receiving storage location) as “171A” for Plant “1710” and save the entry. For this example, we used a single storage location for the plant, but you can group more than one storage location under one MRP area. This storage location (or loca- tions) will be planned separately and independently from planning at the plant level. In addition, in a subcontracting scenario involving MRP areas, you can define subcon- tracting MRP areas within a plant to enable planning and material availability at the subcontractor (supplier) level. 13 Material Requirements Planning 760 Figure 13.76 Configuring MRP Areas for Storage Location

sections.title: Set Up the Material Master
sections.en: 13.15.2 Set Up the Material Master With the necessary configuration settings for MRP areas in place, you can activate the storage location MRP for material 2267, plant 1710, and storage location 171A. Using Transaction MM02 for the change in the material master, you enter Material “2267” for Plant “1710,” and for Stor. Loc. 171A, you select the MRP 1 view. On the screen shown in Figure 13.77, you click the MRP Areas button so that the Create MRP Area screen shown in Figure 13.78 appears. Then, select configured MRP Area 1710–171A. Figure 13.77 MRP Areas in Material Master © 761 13.15 Planning Areas Figure 13.78 MRP Area Settings for Storage Location in Material Master Note If an error message reading MRP area 1710–171A does not exist as SCM location appears, then execute report /SAPAPO/CREATE_LOCATION for MRP area 1710-171A by using Transaction SE38. Next, assign the MRP Type as “VB” for manual reorder point planning at the MRP area level and set the Reorder Point to “200.” Then, assign other parameters, such as MRP Controller “001” and Fixed Lot Size “100.00” (not shown), per Figure 13.78. Finally, choose the MRP 2 tab. In the MRP 2 tab, more planning options are available at the material level, such as defining a Safety Stock of “70” at the MRP areas level (see Figure 13.79). Here, you should enter a Planned Deliv. Time in days and choose the Forecast tab. The Forecast tab page enables you to enter forecast parameters and also interactively execute a forecast of the material. Then, you choose the Consmptn values tab and 13 Material Requirements Planning 762 maintain the planned and unplanned consumption values. Finally, choose Adopt in the MRP areas and save the material master settings. Figure 13.79 MRP 2 Tab of MRP Area 1710–171A

sections.title: Running Material Requirements at the Planning Area Level
sections.en: 13.15.3 Running Material Requirements at the Planning Area Level To run the MRP for MRP areas interactively, you use Transaction MD01N (MRP Live) and enter the parameters, such as Material “2267.” Once you execute the MRP Live transaction, the screen shown in Figure 13.80 will appear. Notice that Material 2267 belongs to MRP Area 1710–171A. Choose the Selected Stock/Requirements List button (not shown) to go to the screen shown in Figure 13.81, which we’ll discuss in the next section. Figure 13.80 Planning Results from MRP Live Using MRP Area © 763 13.15 Planning Areas

sections.title: Planning Results
sections.en: 13.15.4 Planning Results Figure 13.81 shows the planning results of Material 2267 in MRP Area 1710–171A. Because there was no opening stock and the stock has fallen below the safety stock in the ware- house (the storage location of the MRP areas), the system creates new planned orders 0000002998 and 0000002999 with a fixed quantity of 100 each. (This is because we defined a fixed lot size in Figure 13.78.) Notice Exception 96, which denotes Stock Fallen below Safety Stock Level (message details in the lower corner of the screen). Figure 13.81 Planning Results of Material 2267 for MRP Area 1710–171A Note SAP S/4HANA also offers report RMMDDIBE (via Transaction SE38) for the mass main- tenance of the MRP area settings in the material master. With this report, you can copy the MRP area settings with a reference, create or change them with an MRP or forecast profile, create or change them with data, and set or reset the deletion indicator (see Figure 13.82). Figure 13.82 Mass Processing of MRP Area 13 Material Requirements Planning 764

sections.title: Classic Material Requirements Planning
sections.en: 13.16 Classic Material Requirements Planning Now that we’ve covered MRP Live and associated business processes, let’s briefly cover the basics of classic MRP and some of the important functionality it offers. First, access the screen shown in Figure 13.83 by using Transaction MD02 to run MRP for a single material at MRP area level 1710–171A. Figure 13.83 Single-Item, Multilevel MRP (Initial Screen) The following are some of the important selection parameters of MRP:  Processing key The processing key enables the system to decide the type of planning run it needs to carry out for the material. The options for the planning run are NETCH, NETPL, and NEUPL.  Purchase requisition key If external procurement is implied, the system considers this setting during the planning run. If the necessary settings are in place, then the system can directly cre- ate purchase requisitions for all externally procured materials. Alternatively, the system can create planned orders that you can convert into purchase requisitions.  Delivery schedules If a source list for the material is maintained and the same has been incorporated into scheduling agreements with vendors, the system can create schedule lines. © 765 13.16 Classic Material Requirements Planning  Creation of MRP list The system includes the results of the planning run in the MRP list after the plan- ning run. An MRP list is a static or frozen list of stock/requirements situations, with the date and time immediately after the planning run. This indicator controls how the system should create the MRP list. The available options are for the system to create an MRP list for all planned orders, create an MRP list for planned orders with exceptions, or not create any MRP list at all. Exceptions are alerts in the system that the planner needs to keep an eye on.  Planning mode During the planning run, the system plans various procurement elements, such as unfirmed planned orders, purchase requisitions, and scheduling agreements, and it does it once again in the next planning run. You can control how the system should proceed with the planning data that is already there (from the latest planning run or due to interactive activities of the MRP planner). Three options are available: – Adopt Planning Data If there is any change in requirement quantities, dates, or lot sizing procedures for unfirmed planned orders (or other procurement elements), the system re- explodes the BOM for the new quantities in the MRP run. – Re-explode BOM and Routing The system re-explodes the BOM and routing if there is a change in the BOM mas- ter data, routing, production versions, or BOM explosion numbers. This helps to ensure that the system re-explodes the BOM for the existing unfirmed planned orders. The procurement proposals created in the previous planning aren’t deleted but are adjusted to take the changes into account. – Delete and Recreate Planning data The system deletes the existing procurement proposals (i.e., the entire planning data of the previous planning run), unless they are firmed, and it re-explodes the BOM and routing, thereby creating altogether new procurement proposals.  Scheduling types For in-house-produced materials, the system offers two types of scheduling options: – Basic date scheduling – Lead time scheduling After you enter the parameters on the initial screen of Transaction MD02, press (Enter) twice (first to confirm the MRP parameters, causing the entered parameters to turn red, and then to execute the MRP run) and the system will bring up the plan- ning result screen for a large number of materials. On this result screen, not only the finished or semifinished goods (2267) are planned but also all the components of the BOM. You can view the planning result of a material by double-clicking it. 13 Material Requirements Planning 766

sections.title: Types of Planning Runs
sections.en: 13.17 Types of Planning Runs Depending on the business process, a planner can use one of several types of planning run available in the SAP S/4HANA system. A planning run denotes the level and mode at which a planner wants to run MRP. For example, a planner may want to run MRP on an individual plant or a group of plants, on an individual material or a group of materi- als (product group), in online mode, or in background mode. Some of the planning runs include the following:  Total planning online  Total planning background  Single-item planning, sales order  Single-item planning, project We discuss each of the planning runs in the following sections.

sections.title: Total Planning Online
sections.en: 13.17.1 Total Planning Online In this type of planning run, the system carries out a multilevel planning run—that is, for all the materials in a plant, for all the materials in multiple plants, or for MRP, as defined through Scope of Planning, which is available on the selection screen. Total planning online is carried out using Transaction MD01. If the business process requires you to consider and use other parameters in the MRP run, then you can either define them or use a user exit. For example, if your company wants to carry out the MRP run for a set of MRP controllers only or for a specific pro- curement type (E: In-house managed, F: Externally procured, X: Both), then you should use a user exit. The user exit enhancement is M61X00001, and we recommend that you to engage an ABAP consultant for this.

sections.title: Total Planning Background
sections.en: 13.17.2 Total Planning Background With the total planning background method, you can carry out the planning in the background, as opposed to in the foreground or online. Using Transaction MDBT, you can create a variant of the Transaction MD01N screen and schedule it in the back- ground on a periodic basis. The program name for Transaction MDBT is RMMRP000. Use Transaction SE38, give the program name, and define the variant to execute the MRP in the background.

sections.title: Single-Item Planning, Sales Order
sections.en: 13.17.3 Single-Item Planning, Sales Order For MTO production, you can use single-item planning for a sales order. Enter the sales order number together with the line item of the sales order and use Transaction MD50 © 767

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 13.18 Summary to execute the planning. The system not only creates a procurement element or receipt with the sales order reference for the finished good but also plans all the components defined in the BOM of the finished good.

sections.title: Single-Item Planning, Project
sections.en: 13.17.4 Single-Item Planning, Project You can also execute separate planning for ETO materials. If the material—a finished good or a spare part for a raw material—is assigned to a WBS element, then use Trans- action MD51 to execute planning. Note Materials planning is a very complex topic and requires you to have a much deeper understanding of how to formulate material planning rules and policies in a company that can then be mapped in the SAP system. Here, we recommend that you read Mate- rial Requirements Planning with SAP S/4HANA (www.sap-press.com/5861, SAP PRESS, 2024), which discuss this topic in great detail. 13.18 Summary A company can take enormous advantage of the MRP functionality in the SAP S/4HANA system to help it improve and optimize its business processes. A planner can plan all types of materials, including high-, medium-, and low-value materials. The various lot- sizing procedures and safety stocks ensure that there’s neither an overstock nor a stock-out situation. The planning calendar helps a planner plan materials that are spe- cific to particular calendars only. This chapter focused on some of the latest planning innovations available in SAP S/4HANA. MRP Live provides far more options for improved planning and scheduling of materials and capacity, and MRP areas can be implemented to plan materials at the storage location or subcontracting supplier level. The next chapter covers demand-driven replenishment (DDR) in SAP S/4HANA. ©

sections.title: Limitations of Current Materials Planning and
sections.en: 14.1 Limitations of Current Materials Planning and Replenishment Tools Let’s first evaluate why traditional materials planning tools are falling short of address- ing the challenges of the VUCA world. It’s important for you to understand that while many of these planning tools effectively work in isolation, they can’t work cohesively enough to reduce or eliminate the bullwhip effect. And as we evaluate each material planning tool to explain why it’s falling short, it’s equally important for you to under- stand that implementing a DDR solution doesn’t entail discarding all these planning tools, such as forecasting, MRP, safety stock, and reorder point planning. Rather, it requires you to use selected and effective features of several of these planning tools to improve the flow of materials and relevant information. For example, DDR still uses MRP for quantity calculation and scheduling of components, but it stops at stocked or buffered positions. In contrast, MRP starts at the lowest level of a BOM, which is often the highest level of the product (e.g., a finished product), and it continues quantity cal- culation and scheduling of all components of the BOM until it reaches the highest level of a BOM (often the raw materials and packing materials). Similarly, the forecasting tool is still a required planning tool in the overall context of using DDR, as is using capacity requirements planning tools in the context of overall supply chain planning. Bullwhip Effect The bullwhip effect is a cascading and incremental increase in the logistics and supply chain’s variability as a result of a small change in customer demand. The most important material planning tools and their current limitations are as fol- lows:  Forecasting Forecasting uses historical data to predict or forecast future requirements and uses forecasting models, such as constant, moving average, seasonal, and trend. All fore- casts also have a degree of error to account for deviations (mean average deviations) between forecast figures and the confidence in these figures. However, forecasts start out wrong, and they are riddled with errors that only increase with time. Fore- casts are also more inaccurate when they are made at the individual stock-keeping unit level. Imagine trying to accurately forecast or predict the number of air condi- tioners of a specific model (individual stock-keeping units) to produce a year from © 771 14.1 Limitations of Current Materials Planning and Replenishment Tools now (over a long-term horizon) when there just isn’t enough information available now and predicting how hot the weather is going to be a year from now is unreliable.  Sales and operations planning (S&OP) Forecasting often forms the basis of S&OP. A sales and operations plan ensures that the annual sales plan matches up with the production (operations) plan. The mutually agreed-upon sales and operations plan then enables a company to evaluate the ware- house space, the machines’ capacities, and the procurement commitment (value) it will need for the entire year. However, when inaccurate forecasting forms the basis of S&OP, sales and production plans are most certainly bound to change over the near- term horizon when actual sales and demand are better known.  Demand management Demand management is the last step for a materials planner to check and validate a sales or production plan to use for production and procurement planning by using the master production scheduling and MRP tools. (The plan is driven by the S&OP of the previous step.) The basis of demand management is an unreliable forecast, which leads to precisely wrong procurement and proposals generation.  Master production scheduling (MPS) An MPS program plans and schedules production of critical materials and ensures that there are no changes to the production plan within the defined time horizon, such as within a week. No changes are made to the master production scheduling after an MRP run. MPS runs before MRP so that critical products and components are planned first in the planning process. As the demand management plan is fed into MPS, it’s clear to see why MPS only adds to the supply chain chaos.  Material requirements planning (MRP) MRP explodes the BOMs of finished products to calculate the quantities of the semi- finished, packing, and raw materials required to produce finished products.  Materials planning using safety stock Safety stock is the buffer stock of materials that planners turn to in case of a sudden and unforeseen demand spike or a supply disruption. Although it’s possible to auto- matically calculate safety stock, safety stock is unable to account for the dynamic nature of supply and demand variabilities. Furthermore, the safety stock calculation method isn’t the primary materials planning tool and therefore cannot truly act as a buffer between demand and supply deviations.  Reorder point planning When stock falls below a reorder point, replenishment is triggered. The replenish- ment’s proposal quantity is often a fixed or minimum lot that’s intended to prevent the ERP system from creating an unnecessarily large number of procurement pro- posals of small quantities each time there’s a material shortage (or when a material’s stock falls below the reorder point). As with safety stock, you can calculate the reorder point of a material automatically; however, reorder point planning can’t reflect the replenishment priorities of different materials. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 772

sections.title: Material and Information Flows
sections.en: 14.2 Material and Information Flows According to George W. Plossl, author of Production and Inventory Control: Principles and Techniques (Prentice Hall, 1967), “All benefits will be directly related to the speed of flow of information and materials.” Now more than ever, we can enhance this concept by adding that the flow of materials and information must be relevant to the required out-of-market information because in today’s hyperconnected world, companies are drowning in data but starving for relevant information to help them navigate the VUCA challenges of the modern supply chain. Furthermore, the words all benefits can be broken down into the following benefits or KPIs that companies seek to achieve:  Exceptional customer service ensures timely and quality products.  Revenue increases or at least remains stable.  The quality of products and services keeps getting better.  Inventories of purchased, WIP, and produced products stay at optimal levels.  Expenses remain minimal by eliminating expedited freight costs, rework, and unplanned partial deliveries.  Cash flow is greatly improved. With these points in mind, it’s critically important for you to address distortions lead- ing to relevant information or material flows as often and as soon as possible to create a smoother supply chain. First, let’s look at why distortions of relevant material and information occur, as illus- trated in Figure 14.1. Starting at the bottom of Figure 14.1, materials planners use MRP 1 to calculate future demand for products to ensure timely production and procure- ment. However, due to its inherent logic, MRP treats everything as dependent and acts as one big calculator to suggest production and procurement quantities of materials on specific dates. In today’s complex and globalized supply chain, the time it takes to pro- cure and produce a product is much longer than it has ever been before. At the same time, customers today have far more options to choose from, and that leads to shrink- ing customer tolerance time for a company to deliver or ensure availability of a prod- uct 2. To better predict customer demand and feed that data into the MRP program, compa- nies use forecasting tools 3. Unfortunately, even the best forecasting model or pro- gram always has a degree of error. The longer the forecast horizon is—such as yearly, quarterly, or even monthly—the greater the degree of error 4. The more detailed the level at which forecasting is done, such as at the individual product or stock keeping unit level, the more amplified the forecasting error, which leads to the distortion of rel- evant information 8. Refer again to the bottom of Figure 14.1. While MRP uses forecasting to predict demand 3, when the actual customers’ demand is known, adjustment between forecasted demand and actual demand must happen 5, and that leads to nervousness 6 in the © 773 14.2 Material and Information Flows logistics and supply chain. These conflicting and often-changing demand signals 7 again lead to the distortion of relevant information 8. Using distorted or unreliable information for materials planning is one of the two major reasons for the bullwhip effect, which is the amplification of supply variability as the result of demand variability and which has cascading and negative ripple effects on the supply chain. The second effect of nervousness 6 is that companies often use materials planning, such as production or procurement planning with time horizons of one-week or even two-week buckets 9, to bring some sanity to production or procurement plans and to prevent frequent changes to these plans. In doing so, planners use latent signals j that again result in the distortion of relevant information 8. The third factor that adds nervousness 6 to the supply chain appears when materials planners attempt to flatten BOMs k to reduce material quantities or scheduling vari- abilities. In doing so, materials planners end up using oversimplified signals l for pro- curement or production planning, which further adds to the distortion of relevant information 8. When distorted information is used, it’s no surprise that the products or materials required aren’t ready or available when needed m, and that eventually cul- minates in the distortion of relevant materials n. When relevant materials are dis- torted, companies either end up with products that don’t sell (which is an overstock scenario) or with no products to deliver on time to meet demand (which is an out-of- stock scenario); both scenarios lead to missed business opportunities and lost custom- ers. Let’s now go back again to the bottom of Figure 14.1 to understand how the MRP pro- gram that treats all materials as dependent 1 leads to the same distortions of relevant materials n created by nervousness 6 in the supply chain previously. Just like a big cal- culator, the MRP program performs a level-by-level explosion of BOMs of finished goods to calculate the quantities of semifinished, packing, and raw materials that are required at specific times; the resultant MRP plans of quantities and schedules are based on all these dependencies o. Because the MRP program is designed to ensure that the quantities of all materials in a BOM eventually net to zero after taking any safety stock into account, any change in plans, variability, or disruption on the supply side has multiplying effects on all dependent requirements, leading to the accumula- tion of delays p. The delays accumulate, but the gains do not, and thus, material plan- ners have MRP-generated production and procurement plans that are never properly synchronized q between demand and supply. The end results are the same as before: companies end up with overstock of slow-moving products or products that don’t sell, while stock-outs occur for high-demand products. In the following sections, we’ll look at how decoupling can serve as a solution to this problem, and we’ll also cover the basics of demand-driven material requirements plan- ning (DDMRP) and its components. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 774 Figure 14.1 Information and Material Distortions (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission)

sections.title: Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and
sections.en: 14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components The solution to the distortion of relevant information and the quagmire of material flows lies in decoupling the logistics and supply chains to reduce the bullwhip effect. Decoupling entails disconnecting the ever-present and constantly increasing variabil- ity between demand and supply by placing buffers at strategic points, known as decou- pling points. Materials planners then regularly monitor the buffers in the material value chain—from procurement to interplant transfer to production to distribution— to ensure timely action and interventions where necessary. There are three types of buffers: Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Why? Distortions to relevant materials Distortions to relevant information Material is not ready at needed time Signals with known errors are used Latent signals are used Oversimplified signals are used Supply variability transference causes delay accumulation across dependent networks Cumulative Manufacturing and Procurement Times are much longer than customer tolerance times MRP Plans (timing and quantity requirements) are built using all dependencies MRP treats everything as dependent Weekly buckets are used BOMs are flattened Changing and conflicting signals MRP Plans never properly synchronize Adjustments must occur as actual demand becomes known MRP is loaded with forecasts Nervousness © 775 14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components 1. Stock buffer The stock situation at each buffer is closely monitored to ensure that there’s no shortage of the required materials. The icon denotes a decoupled position and maintains a stock buffer in DDR. 2. Time buffer This buffer manages demand and supply variability that may impact production or procurement scheduling. The time buffer icons are these: 3. Capacity buffer This buffer at production lines or work centers provides the cushions required for smoother flow to in-house-produced materials. The capacity buffer icons are these: The three color codes represent different buffer statuses: red points the materials plan- ners to areas that need immediate attention or urgent action, yellow requires closely monitoring the buffer situation, and green reflects a comfortable buffer situation. Each color of buffer is also accompanied by a percentage to denote the buffer remaining or unconsumed in relation to the overall buffer size. For example, a work center, such as a tablet-packing line, might show a red color in the tricolor buffer status to indicate 12% remaining (unconsumed) capacity. All reports and metrics used to represent buffers show red at the top (or highest) level to reflect the criticality or severity of the buffer sit- uation, while green gets the lowest position. Exceptional situations such as stock-outs, overstocks, and overcapacities are denoted by different colors, such as deep red for stock-outs or overcapacities and deep blue for overstocks or excess available capacities. As this chapter will discuss later, these deep-red and dark-blue zones are often the best candidates for bringing improvements to material and information flows. Note SAP introduced DDR in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 1708 and SAP S/4HANA 1709. DDR is based on the DDMRP methodology from the Demand Driven Institute (DDI). Carol Ptak and Chad Smith, partners at DDI, have committed themselves to promoting and proliferat- ing DDMRP and associated concepts and working throughout the world. In their book DDMRP: Demand Driven Materials Requirement Planning (3rd edition, Industrial Press, 2019), Ptak and Smith provide a deep dive into and authoritative resources on DDMRP and associated concepts. Figure 14.2 shows an example planning screen for DDR with several parts, with varying priorities denoted by corresponding tricolor buffer statuses. Figure 14.3 represents the supply chain. Starting from the left, a buffer is placed at the decoupling point 1, which is between the component manufacturing and subassem- bly steps. Notice the reduction in the variability wave 2 that dampens the bullwhip effect and also notice the associated lead time reduction 3. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 776 Figure 14.2 Example of DDR Planning Screen (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permis- sion) Figure 14.3 Decoupling to Reduce Bullwhip Effect (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission) In the following sections, we’ll discuss not only where to implement DDR in the logis- tics and supply chain but also the five main components of DDMRP.

sections.title: Where to Implement Demand-Driven Replenishment
sections.en: 14.3.1 Where to Implement Demand-Driven Replenishment The following are the four areas in the logistics and supply chain in which implement- ing DDR can be beneficial: Easily demonstrable RELATIVE PRIORITY is a crucial aspect of DDMRP Part# Planning Priority On-Order Qualified Demand Net Flow Position Order Recommendation Request Date Top RED Top YELLOW Top GREEN Lead Time 406P RED 19.8% 401 506 263 644 2606 4-Aug 750 2750 3250 20 403P YELLOW 43.4% 1412 981 412 1981 2579 23-Jul 1200 3600 4560 8 402P YELLOW 69.0% 601 753 112 1242 558 24-Jul 540 1440 1800 9 405P YELLOW 74.0% 3400 4251 581 7070 2486 24-Jul 1756 7606 9556 9 401P YELLOW 75.1% 2652 6233 712 8173 2715 25-Jul 2438 8938 10888 10 404P GREEN 97.6% 1951 1560 291 3220 0 1050 2550 3300 6 Today's Date: July 15 On Hand OEM Foundry Component Sub-Assembler Strategic Decoupling Point Variability Wave Decoupled Lead Time © 777 14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components 1. Purchasing Purchased parts often experience supply variability. 2. Operations Production operations that produce several common parts and work centers with bottleneck capacities are the prime candidates for DDR. For semifinished goods sold by a company directly to its customers, implementing DDR can mitigate the risks of demand variability due to customers’ changing requirements for the company’s products. 3. Distribution Finished goods or even services’ spare parts are distributed to different distribution centers and experience significant demand variability. 4. Interplant Companies with several manufacturing units or warehouses spread across various locations of a country, different countries, or even continents have materials with short, medium, or long lead times. Due to the special procurement types of such materials, some of them are prime candidates for DDR.

sections.title: Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Components
sections.en: 14.3.2 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Components The five components of DDMRP (see Figure 14.4) show a materials planner how to effec- tively position buffers at strategic inventory locations, protect the flow of materials and relevant information by evaluating buffer profiles, and then dynamically adjust these buffers. Finally, an efficient pull system is put in place to reduce variability between demand and supply through demand-driven planning. Figure 14.4 Five Components of DDMRP (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission) This is followed by execution while ensuring that the changing supply and demand dynamics adapt to the logistics and supply chain. Demand Driven Material Requirements Planning Strategic Inventory Positioning Buffer Profiles and Levels Dynamic Adjustments Demand Driven Planning Visible and Collaborative Execution Position 1 Protect 2 3 Pull 4 5 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 778 Strategic Inventory Positioning In this component (see Figure 14.4), a materials planner decides which items to stock and where. Materials that are prime candidates for consideration are those with long lead times, significant supply or demand variability, or a component used in manufac- turing several products, plus those that face a production capacity bottleneck. The planner chooses materials from the stock buffers as well as the decoupling points in the logistics and supply chain. As already covered previously, the buffers at decoupling points are denoted by the icon. The following two questions will immediately arise in your mind when you’re choos- ing a decoupling point:  What important factors should I consider while selecting a decoupling point in the logistics and supply chain?  Where should I position a decoupling point in the logistics and supply chain? The six factors you should consider when choosing a decoupling point are as follows:  Customer tolerance time The maximum amount of time customers are willing to wait for delivery or avail- ability of a product before seeking alternatives  Market potential lead time Lead time that will allow an increase in price or an opportunity to capture additional business through existing or new customer channels  Demand or sales order visibility horizon The time period during which the demand for dependent requirements is known or when there’s visibility into actual sale of products as recorded in a sales order  External variabilities – Demand variability The probability of spikes and swings in demand that may overwhelm resources such as capacity, stock, and cash flow – Supply variability The potential for and probability of supply disruptions from specific sources of supply (suppliers or interplant transfers)  Inventory leverage and flexibility The specific places in an integrated BOM structure that enable a company to have maximum options for product availability and the best lead time compression  Critical operation protection Putting specific control points in place to minimize potential points of disruptions in the supply chain, such as placing control points at work centers or production resources with bottleneck capacities or where demand/supply variabilities can quickly accumulate or amplify © 779 14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components Based on these six factors for choosing decoupling points, Figure 14.5 shows an exam- ple of strategically placed buffers. These buffers are based on the following consider- ations:  The FPG and 201 buffers let you quickly take advantage of additional business oppor- tunities.  The FPG stock position is minimized due to the 101 buffer.  The 101 and 102P buffers allow for an MTO strategy on FPH.  The 201 and 204P buffers protect the Z resource as best as possible at the product structure (BOM) level.  The 301P buffer minimizes the inventory liability at 201.  The 201 and 101 buffers can direct the shared components to actual requirements. Figure 14.5 Strategically Placed Buffers (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission) The following are the six tests you should consider when evaluating a decoupling point:  Decoupling test The lead time on the supply side should decrease when compared with the lead time chain of the nonbuffered components.  Bidirectional benefit test The demand side should show a compressed or reduced lead time and higher avail- ability of a product or a component. The supply side should get cumulative demand requirements to provide a materials planner with better and more efficient order management of the recommended order quantity.  Order independence test The stock in the buffers isn’t specific to any production order, sales order, or stock transport order (interplant transfer). This test therefore ensures that there’s as little WIP as possible while making stock available for any demand. 30 Days 20 Days 3 Days 2 Days 2 Days 5 Days 10 Days 204P 201 101 FPG FPH Z C E F C C C C B A B T S T S 102P 301P 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 780  Primary planning mechanism test The buffered part at a decoupling point should be able to manage all demand and supply requirements independently by taking the net flow position into account.  Relative priority test The buffered position needs to account for both planning and execution of not just the buffered part but also its priority in relation to other buffered parts and posi- tions.  Dynamic adjustment test The buffered part needs to adjust dynamically to account for supply and demand variations within the defined time horizon. Buffer Profiles and Levels In this component (see Figure 14.4), you as a materials planner decide how much stock to hold (the buffers) at specific decoupling points. You group together materials that share similar characteristics and attributes (e.g., raw or packing materials with long lead times, finished goods with high demand variability, several intermediate products sharing a common machine or a bottlenecked work center) and then assign them rele- vant buffer profiles. Then, you maintain buffer levels. Let’s take a look at the three buffer zones (red, yellow, and green) and how to calculate them (see Figure 14.6). You calculate the yellow zone 1, and then the green zone 2, and finally the red zone 3.  Yellow zone This is central to the demand coverage in the buffer, and you calculate it using the following formula: Average daily usage (ADU) × Decoupled lead time (DLT)  Green zone This determines the order frequency and the size of each order. There are three ways to calculate the green zone, and the largest value of the three calculations forms the green buffer zone: – Minimum order quantity (MOQ) – Desired order cycle (DOC) × DLT – ADU × DLT × Lead time factor (LT factor)  Red zone This is the embedded safety in the buffer. The red zone is the sum of the red zone base and the red zone safety, as follows: – Red zone base: ADU × DLT × LT factor – Red zone safety: Red zone base × Variability factor (VF) – Red zone = Red zone base + Red zone safety © 781 14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components Figure 14.6 Buffer Zones (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission) To get a better sense of buffer positions, consider the following four abbreviations and their meanings:  Top of red (TOR) This is the highest point of the red zone. When the stock, schedule, or capacity breaches the red zone, it signals the need for urgent action or immediate attention.  Top of yellow (TOY) This is the highest point of the yellow zone. Breaching TOY signals a warning of a buffer situation. TOY = Red zone + Yellow zone.  Top of green (TOG) This is the highest point of the green zone that reflects a comfortable buffer position until it crosses into the TOY zone. TOG = Red + Yellow + Green.  Over the top of green (OTOG) This is a situation in which the on-hand or available stock is over the top of the defined green zone (the TOG), and it signals an excess inventory position. Figure 14.7 shows an example of calculating the buffer zones. You start by calculating the yellow zone, and then you calculate the green zone, and finally the red zone. The data required for calculating all three zones is also shown in Figure 14.7. The three zones are calculated as follows: 1. TOR: 90 2. TOY: Red + Yellow = 90 + 150 = 240 3. TOG: Red + Yellow + Green = 90 + 150 + 90 = 330 Red Zone Base Red Zone Safety Yellow Green Green Zone: Determines supply order generation frequency and size – the largest quantity of either of the following three: • Minimum Order Quantity (MOQ) • Desired Order Cycle (DOC) x Decoupled Lead Time (DLT) • Average Daily Usage (ADU) x Decoupled Lead Time (DLT) x Lead Time (LT) Factor Yellow Zone: The heart of the demand coverage in the buffer • ADU x Decoupled Lead Time (DLT) Red Zone: The embedded safety in the buffer • Red Zone Base: DLT x ADU x LT Factor • Red Zone Safety: Red Zone Base x Variability Factor (VF) • Total Red Zone = Red Zone Base + Red Zone Safety Top of Yellow (TOY) Top of Red (TOR) Top of Green (TOG) Over the Top of Green (OTOG) Blue 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 782 Figure 14.7 Buffer Calculation (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission) Dynamic Adjustments In this component (see Figure 14.4), a materials planner oversees dynamic adjustments to buffers to account for product seasonality, the stage of a product maturity (such as a soon-to-be-discontinued product or a new product introduction), and capacity adjust- ments, and then, the planner prebuilds the requirements in anticipation of future demand or slowdown. Buffers, such as stock buffers, also adjust dynamically to account for supply variability, a procurement or production schedule mismatch, or a machine reaching an overload capacity situation. As already covered, the stock, schedule, and capacity buffers adjust dynamically to ensure elimination of distortions of relevant information and materials flows. Demand-Driven Planning In this component (see Figure 14.4), a materials planner uses the visual planning tools to create replenishment orders where necessary. Replenishment orders are created based on the net flow equation: Net flow equation: On hand + On order – Qualified sales order (or demand) In this equation, on hand is the available stock in the warehouse, on order refers to the quantities of materials ordered and awaiting receipt, and qualified sales order (or demand) refers to sales orders that are due today, those that are past due, and qualified future spikes. The qualified future spike considers the quantity threshold within a defined time horizon in a daily time bucket to decide if the spike qualifies to be included in net flow equation calculation. For example, a qualified future spike is included in the Part Buffer Calculation – An Example Average Daily Usage (ADU) 15 Green Zone 90 (the largest of three calculated values) Buffer Profile (Manufactured, Medium Lead Time, Low Variability) M, M (0.4), L (0.33) LT Factor: 60 (DLT (10)x ADU (15) x Lead Time Factor (0.4)) MOQ (Minimum Order Quantity) 50 Minimum Order Quantity: 50 Imposed or Desired Order Cycle (DOC) 6 days Order Cycle: 90 (6(DOC) x 15(ADU)) Decoupled Lead Time (DLT) 10 days Yellow Zone 150 (10(DLT) x 15(ADU)) Lead Time Factor (M) = 0.4 Variability Factor (L) = 0.33 Red Zone 90 (Red Base (60) + Red Safety (30)) Red Base: 60 (DLT (10)x ADU (15) x Lead Time Factor (0.4)) Red Safety: 30 (Red Base (90) x Variability Factor (0.33)) 120 150 90 0 50 100 150 200 250 300 350 400 90 150 90 © 783 14.3 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Basics and Components net flow equation calculation when the demand quantity of the material is 50 units or more (the quantity threshold) on a single day (in a daily time bucket) and within the time horizon of one week. Visible and Collaborative Execution In this component (see Figure 14.8), you as a materials planner take action to convert suggested replenishment orders into procurement orders, production orders, or stock transport orders. Furthermore, you use material lead times or scheduling synchroniza- tion alerts to quickly attend to any material or scheduling disruptions that threaten to destabilize the supply chain. DDR is a priority-based pull system that alerts planners visually whenever a situation warrants action or attention. Figure 14.8 DDMRP Execution (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission) Table 14.1 shows the DDMRP components mapped to the available DDR apps in SAP S/4HANA. The following is a brief introduction to each component, and we’ll dive into them further in later sections:  Buffer positioning DDMRP starts with the product classification using the Schedule Product Classifica- tion app. After the classification, you can use the Buffer Positioning app to choose where to position the decoupling point buffers. Finally, you classify the products Highly Visible Open Supply Priority Management …NOT priority by due date! Order # Order Type Due Date Customer MO 12379 MTO May - 12 Super Tech MO 12401 12% RED MTS May - 14 Internal MO 12465 27% RED MTS May - 12 Internal MO 12367 53% YELLOW MTS May - 12 Internal MO 12411 61% YELLOW MTS May - 16 Internal Order # Order Type Due Date Customer MO 12367 MTS May - 12 Internal MO 12379 MTO May - 12 Super Tech MO 12465 MTS May - 12 Internal MO 12401 MTS May - 14 Internal MO 12411 MTS May - 16 Internal Order # On-Hand Buffer Status PO 819-87 27% (RED) WO 832-41 42% (RED) WO 211-72 88% (YELLOW) Priority by buffer status… 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 784 according to the lead time using the Schedule Lead Time Classification of Products app. Alternatively, you can manually classify the products using the Mass Mainte- nance of Products (DD) app.  Buffer sizing In this step, you execute the actual buffer calculation with the Schedule Buffer Pro- posal Calculation app. You can also review the calculated buffers by using the Man- age Buffer Levels app.  Replenishment planning You use the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app to evaluate which products are below the reorder point.  Replenishment execution You use the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status app to check for which products the on-hand stock can’t cover the buffer and thus for which products to expedite the supply.  Analytics This is the last step of the DDMRP process. With the Planned Overview app, you can find the DDMRP-related KPIs, take action, and prevent problems. DDMRP Components DDMRP Component Descriptions DDR Apps in SAP S/4HANA Functionalities of DDR App 1 Strategic inventory positioning Schedule Product Classification (DD) This performs classification (ABC, EFG, and PQR). Mass Maintenance of Products (DD) Based on classification, you set MRP types D1 and D2 (MRP types available for DDR) manually. You can change classifications manu- ally as well. Schedule Lead Time Calculation of Products (DD) This performs calculation of decoupled lead times (DLTs) using MRP types D1 and D2 and classification according to EFG. 2 Buffer profiles and levels Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation This calculates buffers based on DLTs using MRP types D1 and D2. Manage Buffer Levels This enables users to adjust calculated values of buffers. Table 14.1 DDMRP Components Mapping with DDR Apps in SAP S/4HANA © 785

sections.title: Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup
sections.en: 14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup The first three sections of this chapter discussed DDR concepts in detail and laid the foundations that will form the basis of mapping these DDR concepts with actual setup in the SAP S/4HANA system, which we discuss in detail in the following sections. 14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup To begin with, this section will focus on configuration settings to explain how they impact DDR master data and even business processes. Then, we’ll discuss setting up the minimal DDR-specific master data to get the DDR process up and running in the system. Next, we’ll cover some transactions or real-time business processes that DDR takes into consideration to calculate its own set of parameters. These parameters calculate the three difference zones (green, yellow, and red) and set up the right classifications (ABC, EFG, PQR, XYZ, etc.). 3 Dynamic adjustments Replenish Planning by Planning Priority The Replenish Planning by Planning Priority app is used to evaluate which products are below the reorder point. 4 Demand-driven planning MRP Run This creates receipt ele- ments. Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status With the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Sta- tus app, a materials planner can check for which products the on-hand stock can’t cover the buffer and expe- dite the supply. 5 Visible and collabo- rative execution Planner Overview Projected On-Hand Alerts The Planner Overview app helps materials planners find DDMRP-related KPIs so they can take action and prevent supply chain problems. The Projected On-Hand Alerts app identifies situations where the projected stock within the decoupled lead time will not be sufficient to cover all the requirements. DDMRP Components DDMRP Component Descriptions DDR Apps in SAP S/4HANA Functionalities of DDR App Table 14.1 DDMRP Components Mapping with DDR Apps in SAP S/4HANA (Cont.) 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 786 As the entire DDR process is embedded in various stages of the logistics and supply chain—such as procurement, production, distribution, and sales—it’s best that all rele- vant stakeholders gain a better understanding of an end-to-end DDR process flow. Let’s discuss configuration settings first.

sections.title: Configuration Settings
sections.en: 14.4.1 Configuration Settings The configuration settings related to DDR are buffer profile, key terms, MRP type, and lot sizing, and these are discussed in the following sections. Buffer Profile According to the DDMRP principles, products must first be classified according to vari- ous classifications, such as the usage value (the ABC classification), lead time (the EFG classification), BOM usage (the PQR classification), and variability (the XYZ classifica- tion). Depending on the settings of the variability and the lead time classification made in a buffer profile, SAP S/4HANA will determine a variability factor (VF) and a lead time fac- tor (LT factor). Both of these factors are predetermined in a buffer profile and are used to calculate the buffer. Figure 14.9 shows the standard buffer profile delivered by SAP S/4HANA, which can be found in the Buffer Profile Maintenance app. Alternatively, the buffer profile can also be accessed using Transaction PPH_DD_BUF_PROF in the SAP S/4HANA system. Figure 14.9 Standard Buffer Profile Let’s consider an example of how the system determines the VF and the LT factor from the buffer profile. For a material purchased externally (referred to as Buy) with a low variability indicator and a short lead time, the VF is 0.30 and the LT factor is 0.80. This is noted on the first row of the buffer profile in Figure 14.9. © 787 14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup If necessary, you can create a new buffer profile with adjusted factors or assign a differ- ent buffer to each plant (in this case, Plant 1710, as shown in Figure 14.10). We will use Plant 1710 as an example throughout this chapter. Figure 14.10 Plant-Specific Settings of Buffer Profile The next section discusses some of the key DDR terms, which are helpful to refer to when considering Figure 14.9 and Figure 14.10. Key Terms The following list contains some important DDR terms and their meanings. These terms are either defined in supply chain associations, such as the Association for Sup- ply Chain Management, or by the Demand-Driven Institute:  Item type This categorizes various material types, such as procured (P), intermediate (I), semi- finished (SF), manufactured (M), and distributed (D). In the SAP S/4HANA buffer pro- file, procured materials are referred to as Buy, the produced components are referred to as Make, and the interplant transfer (stock transport orders) of materials are referred to as Transfer.  Average daily usage (ADU) This is the average consumption of a material in a given time period.  Minimum order quantity (MOQ) This is the minimum procurement quantity imposed by the supplier or the minimum production quantity or economic order quantity of a material. In SAP S/4HANA, this information is maintained in the Minimum Lot Size field in the MRP1 view of a material master.  Imposed or desired order cycle (DOC) This is the frequency of procuring a material, either imposed from the supplier side or a company’s own ordering cycle.  Decoupled lead time (DLT) This is the longest but reduced unprotected or unbuffered lead time in the BOM to procure or produce a material. In SAP S/4HANA, this information is maintained or updated in the Total Replenishment Lead Time field of a material master (in the MRP 3 view of a material master). 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 788  Lead time factor (LT factor) This denotes a factor in the lead time to procure or produce a material in the range of 0.0–1.0. Longer lead times will have smaller LT factors and vice versa. Materials planners should mutually agree to define the lead time categories (long, medium, and short) and then assign an LT factor to each lead time category. Here’s an exam- ple: – Long lead time: LT factor = 0.20 – Medium lead time: LT factor = 0.50 – Short lead time: LT factor = 0.85  Variability factor (VF) This denotes the demand or supply variability of materials in the range of 0.0–1.0. Higher variability in demand or supply will have a higher VF and vice versa. Materi- als planners should mutually agree to define the VF categories (stable, intermittent, and unstable/unpredictable demand/supply) and then assign a VF to each category. Here’s an example: – Erratic supply/demand (high variability): VF = 0.85 – Semi-stable supply/demand (significantly high variability): VF = 0.70 – Mostly stable supply/demand (medium variability): VF = 0.30  Buffer profile This is a logical grouping of materials that share similar characteristics, such as materials being procured, manufactured, or distributed across warehouses. The buf- fer profile also mentions the LT factor and VF of each material. A buffer profile con- sists of an item part, a lead time category (and an LT factor), and a variability cate- gory (and a VF). For example, a buffer profile of M, M (0.4), L (0.33) means the follow- ing: – The part is manufactured (M). – The part has a medium lead time (M) with an LT factor (0.4). – The part has low (L) demand/supply variability with a VF (0.33).  On-hand alert threshold This is defined as a percentage of the safety stock and is maintained in the Buffer Profile Maintenance app (refer to the last column of Figure 14.10). For example, if the safety stock of a material is 1,000 PC and an on-hand alert threshold is maintained as 0.10 in the buffer profile, then the system will issue a red alert in the Replenish- ment Execution by On-Hand Alert app as soon as the stock falls to 100 or below (1,000 × 0.10 = 100 PC).  Spike horizon The spike horizon is a time window that starts the day after today and comprises the DLT multiplied by a factor called the spike horizon DLT multiplier (SHT), with the addition of an offset called the spike horizon constant. The idea is that DDR will also © 789 14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup react to a demand within this time window to ensure product availability. Referring to Figure 14.10 again, and as an example, the Spike Horizon Constant is maintained as 5.00 (days), while the Spike Horizon DLT Multiplier is maintained as 0.50. Now, if the DLT calculated by the DDR process is eight days, then the calculation for Spike Horizon is Spike horizon = 5 (spike horizon constant) + (8 (DLT) × 0.5) = 9 days.  Spike threshold DDR won’t react to all the demand within the spike horizon but only to qualified demands. To be considered a qualified demand, a requirement quantity must be greater than the spike threshold, which is calculated by multiplying the safety stock by the spike threshold factor. Only those highlighted demands will be considered qualified demands because they are within the spike horizon and above the spike threshold. Let’s explore this with an example: A safety stock (red zone) of 1,000 is calculated by the DDR process. A qualified spike demand of 1,500 is taken into account by DDR because it’s greater than the order spike threshold of 1,500. An order spike threshold is a multiplication of the safety stock (in this example, 1,000) by the Spike Threshold (1.50) as defined in the buffer profile (refer to Figure 14.10). There- fore, 1,000 × 1.5 = 1,500, which is the order spike threshold. So, any sales order with quantity of 1,500 or more and that is within the spike horizon (nine days; see previ- ous bullet point) is considered a qualified demand. Material Requirements Planning Type DDR uses the MRP type D1 that has Firming Type 1—Automatic Firming and Order Pro- posals Rescheduled Out. This setting entails that any existing planned order that lies within the time fence will be automatically considered firm and won’t be changed by MRP. If there is a shortage within the time fence, a new planned order will be created, but it will be rescheduled out (i.e., it will be created outside the time fence). As soon as this newly created planned order falls within the planning time fence, the system will automatically firm it. If the planning time fence is 15 days, then in a stock/requirements list (Transaction MD04), the MRP results when using MRP type P1 will uniquely identify the End of Plan- ning Time Fence when this 15-day period ends. A DDMRP type can be any MRP type with MRP procedure C, such as the standard MRP type D1, which will be used throughout this chapter. A DDMRP type acts like a reorder point MRP type, wherein the yellow buffer zone is the reorder point, the green zone represents the maximum stock level, and the safety stock is the red zone. Note MRP type D1 can not only be used in discrete manufacturing but also in process manu- facturing. For repetitive manufacturing, you should use the new MRP type D2 available in SAP S/4HANA 2023. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 790 Lot Sizing A lot sizing procedure defines the rules for the quantity of the replenishment proposal created by MRP. The lot sizing procedures available in SAP S/4HANA can be grouped into the following categories:  Static lot sizing procedures  Period lot sizing procedures  Optimum lot sizing procedures DDR belongs to the static lot sizing procedures, in which the quantity will be calculated according to the quantity specifications defined in the material master. To achieve the correct MRP results for DDR, it’s also important to choose a lot sizing procedure that will replenish up to the maximum stock level after covering the requirements. For this, standard lot sizing procedure HB (replenish up to maximum stock level) is available in the system. Positioning a buffer for a product in DDR entails selecting a DDMRP type, such as the standard MRP type D1, as well as using standard lot sizing procedure HB so that MRP can correctly replenish up to the maximum stock level. Technically, these settings can be made directly in the material master, but SAP also offers the Buffer Positioning app (discussed in Section 14.5.5), which was specially developed for this purpose. When you’re using this application, filters are available so that you can select the products to process. Those products will be listed with their respective classifications and further details, such as the lead time and the DLT. You can use this information to help deter- mine whether a buffer should be positioned or not. Note In SAP S/4HANA 2023, SAP introduced a new configuration setting (Transaction OMDQ) to allow material planners to define additional requirements for inclusion in the net- flow position (NFP) calculation using the MRP type for DDMRP procedure. Following are the additional requirements categories that you can select for inclusion in open past demand:  Purchase requisition releases of stock transfer purchase requisitions  Scheduling agreement delivery schedules for stock transfer schedule lines  Subcontracting requirements (parts to be provided to supplier for adding value to a product) of subcontracting purchase requisitions  Dependent requirements of planned orders

sections.title: Master Data Settings
sections.en: 14.4.2 Master Data Settings The following sections list the critical master data setup required for the DDR process. © 791 14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup Material Master For material master 2269 in plant 1710, the MRP Type field is assigned “D1,” while the Lot Sizing Procedure field is assigned “HB.” This material-plant combination will be used as a DDR example throughout this chapter. Meanwhile, for the sake of this example only, you should maintain the numeric values for different fields in the material master as noted in Table 14.2, which also includes the purposes they serve. Note As this is just an example, you should simply maintain any reasonable values in all stock-related fields. The DDR process will automatically calculate and update these fields based on actual material usage or consumption data, which is discussed in Sec- tion 14.4.3. Second, for all the materials that are part of the DDR process, such as raw materials, packing materials, and semifinished materials, you should maintain the relevant data in the respective material masters. Figure 14.11 shows the Demand-Driven Replenishment tab that’s available in the Stock/ Requirements List (Transaction MD04), in which the Net Flow Position is shown along with other details. The Demand-Driven Replenishment tab will only appear in the Stock/Requirements List for those materials that are DDR managed. Also, scroll to the far-right side (by sliding the gray bar located at the center of the screen to the right side) of the screen to see some more DDR classifications, such as the variability indicator and the DLT indicator, among other information. Fields in Material Master Field Values in Material Master Purposes in DDR Maximum Stock Level “500” TOG Reorder Point “100” TOY Safety Stock “50” TOR Procurement Type “E” For materials produced in-house: value “E” For procured materials: value “F” Special Procurement Type “40” To be maintained only for interplant trans- fer materials Table 14.2 Material Master Fields Maintenance for Buffers in DDR 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 792 Figure 14.11 Stock/Requirements List for DDR Material Bill of Materials A material’s BOM consists of components grouped together with the relevant quanti- ties that are needed to produce the material in the production process. For example, if vanilla ice cream is the main product, then its BOM will consist of all the ingredients (referred to as components) needed to produce it. Similarly, producing an automobile requires an engine, a chassis, a body, and a large number of other components. To create the BOM of a material, use Transaction CS01. On the initial screen, enter Material “2269” and Plant “1710” and then maintain material numbers for the individ- ual components, together with the quantity for each. Although it contains quite a num- ber of materials, only materials 2262, 2265, and 2267 are relevant to DDR (in addition to the main material, 2269). Note Although only BOM details are covered here, it’s also important to maintain the other manufacturing data, such as the routing data for discrete manufacturing (the master recipe for process manufacturing and rate routing for repetitive manufacturing) and the production version. This master data setup will ensure that the planning elements created by DDR can be successfully converted into production elements (e.g., planned orders created in DDR can be converted into production or manufacturing orders). Similarly, for procured materials, you should at least maintain a purchasing info record, which is a one-to-one procurement relationship between a material and a supplier. This master data setup will ensure that the planning elements created in DDR can be successfully converted into procurement elements (e.g., purchase requisitions created in DDR can be converted into purchase orders). © 793 14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup For interplant transfer, you maintain the material masters in question in both the plant setting and the DDR-related setting, as already discussed in this earlier section.

sections.title: Transactions
sections.en: 14.4.3 Transactions To trigger and calculate the average daily usage (ADU) of DDR-relevant materials, let’s perform some GI and GR transactions beforehand in the system and on different dates. For example, you can do the following: 1. Receive four materials in a storage location in different quantities and on different dates in the past against the initial stock posting. 2. Issue out (consume) the materials against a cost center in different quantities and on different dates in the past so that the system can automatically determine the ADU. 3. Manually create a few sales orders for this material on different dates in the past and also in the future. 4. As DDR now also enables planning for the future to account for any forecast/pre- dicted requirements, you should maintain some values in the PIRs. Performing this step will enable the system to calculate the ADU for future consumption too. All these steps are briefly covered in the following sections. Goods Receipts/Goods Issue In this example, when the DDR and non-DDR materials are created in the SAP S/4HANA system, there are no initial stocks or any history of past receipts or consumption. To maintain some stocks of different materials on different dates in the past, you perform the GR process. Use Transaction MIGO, choose Goods Receipt from the first dropdown list, and then choose Other from the second dropdown list. Then, you ensure that movement type 561 is used for initial entry of stock balances (Init.entry of stBal.), as shown on the right- hand side of the screen. In this example, four DDR-based materials are received in a quantity of 100 each on July 15, 2024 (the Posting Date). A few more similar GR transactions are undertaken but in varying quantities and on different dates. These receipts of materials on different dates and in different quantities will enable the system to calculate the LT factor and supply VF. In a real-time, actual supply chain scenario, such GRs are undertaken with reference to purchase orders for procured materials, via production or process orders for in-house- produced materials, and via stock transport orders for interplant transfer materials. To begin GI—that is, to record consumption of materials with reference to a cost center—you make selections from both the dropdowns: choose Goods Issue from the first dropdown on the left-hand side and then select Other in the relevant options drop- down that appears. Then, make sure to use movement type 201 in the GI for Cost Center 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 794 field for consumption of materials against the cost center, as shown on the right-hand side of the screen. You can then undertake a few more similar GIs, but in different quantities and on different dates. These issuances and consumptions of materials on different dates and in different quantities will enable the system to calculate the ADU. In the real-time supply chain scenario, goods are issued with reference to production or process orders (via movement type 261), cost centers (via movement type 201), sale orders (via movement type 601), or stock transport orders (via movement type 541). Note DDMRP considers all goods movement, such as goods issue and goods receipt, during the period under evaluation. But if necessary, you can exclude certain goods issuance, such as goods issue against scrap (via movement type 551), from the DDMRP calcula- tion. To make the relevant configuration settings, you use menu path SAP IMG  Pro- duction  Demand-Driven Replenishment  Assignment of Profile for Goods Issue Selection (DD). Planned Independent Requirements You can use PIRs to project ADU in the future and allow the system to calculate a pro- jected buffer for the future. This is especially relevant, for example, if you know in advance that there will be an event that might affect product consumption, such as a marketing campaign or a seasonal increase in demand. We’ll also cover this scenario in this chapter. You can use the Mass Maintenance of Products (DD) app to define the horizon in the future for which the system will look for PIRs. To create the PIRs of a material, use either Transaction MD61 or MD64. For this example, enter Material “2269” and Plant “1710” and assign the Version as “00” (Requirements Plan). Define the Planning Horizon and the Planning period in days so that the system brings up the relevant fields for data input. Then, press (Enter) to arrive at the screen shown in Figure 14.12, which shows the day-wise future demand of material 2269 in plant 1710. Figure 14.12 Planned Independent Requirements © 795 14.4 Demand-Driven Material Requirements Planning Setup It’s important to check the box in column A (active) so that the system will consider these PIRs during the MRP run.
