Open Past Demand Open past demand refers to sales orders for which the delivery dates have already passed but that aren’t delivered yet. To create open past demand—that is, to create a sales order—use Transaction VA01. Enter the standard sales Order Type “OR” as well as the organizational data details, such as Sales Organization “1000,” Distribution Chan- nel “10,” and Division “00.” Then, press (Enter). Then, as shown in Figure 14.13, you enter the customer, which in this case is Sold-to Party “USCU S09.” At the item level, enter Material “2269” and an Order Quantity of “600” PC. Figure 14.13 Sales Order Although it’s not shown here, the Req. Deliv.Date (required delivery date) in this case is 07/14/2024 (July 14, 2024). This is the date that DDR will consider when evaluating both the open past demand (sales orders that aren’t partially or fully delivered to customers yet) and any future demand that lies within the spike horizon, to make them a part of the net flow equation. Next, you save the sales order, and, for this example, the system creates sales order number 5616. If there are several line items in a sales order, then the system considers the First Date field for scheduling and DDR calculation purposes. It’s important to note that DDR only consider sales orders for demand and not customer requirements (via Transaction MD81). 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 796 Open Orders Open orders are basically the production orders or process orders for in-house-produced materials, purchase orders for procurement-related materials, and stock transport orders for interplant transfer-related materials whose goods are partially delivered or not deliv- ered yet but are expected to be (i.e., open orders). To create a production order, you use Transaction CO01 (use Transaction COR1 for cre- ating a process order). On the initial production order screen that appears, enter Mate- rial “2269,” production Plant “1710,” and standard order Type “PP10.” Then, choose Con- tinue. The header data screen of the production order consists of information that is applica- ble to the entire order, such as the production order number, material number, plant, and order type. The Total Qty field is relevant, and the quantity of 100 is the open order quantity of material 2269 in plant 1710 that hasn’t been delivered yet and hence is con- sidered in the net flow equation of DDR. The Dates/Times section is equally important because these dates form the basis for when the system expects the goods to be deliv- ered to the warehouse. On the production order screen, you navigate to Components, which shows the BOM data and the components that will be used in the production process. Reservations The system has automatically created reservations for three components of main material 2269—including for components 2262, 2265, and 2267—along with relevant quantities. Movement type 261, which denotes the consumption of this component, will be recorded against this production order. Finally, as these three components are also DDR-related materials, DDR will use the net flow equation. The following sections will discuss how to run the DDR process in SAP S/4HANA to ensure that you protect throughput, classify products, calculate and update buffers, and ensure better planning and execution while also focusing on key DDR metrics and analytics.

sections.title: Running Demand-Driven Replenishment
sections.en: 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment The main purpose of maintaining buffers is to provide a cushion against the shock of fluctuations in supply chain management and financial activity by protecting the throughput. Throughput is defined as the rate at which material exits the manufactur- ing process. Lead time is the time it takes to move through the manufacturing process, and WIP, as mentioned previously, is the amount of inventory contained between entry and exit points of the manufacturing process. Therefore, throughput = (WIP ÷ lead time). © 797 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment The following sections will discuss how to protect throughput, classify materials based on ABC, PQR, and XYZ position buffers, and classify materials based on lead time.

sections.title: Protecting Throughput
sections.en: 14.5.1 Protecting Throughput DDR builds on the same buffer concept to achieve the following two primary objec- tives:  It provides shock absorption by providing inventory, thereby ensuring the protec- tion of downstream users from fluctuations in demand and supply.  It reduces the lead time required to deliver a product by removing anything that’s upstream of the buffer from the cumulative lead time. To achieve these objectives, you undertake a series of DDR-specific steps in SAP S/4HANA, such as classifying products for variability or usage value, positioning buf- fers, and finally classifying products based on the lead time. Classification is a central step in determining the decoupling points in a multilevel BOM structure as part of the DDR approach. You valuate the planning objects’ product/ plant or product/plant/MRP areas according to their essential characteristics and then assign them to classes on this basis. This classification helps you as a planner identify the appropriate settings for each planning object, which you should select based on these characteristics. In the context of DDR, these settings are defined in a certain way. The results of classifi- cation lead to the assignment of buffer profiles in a subsequent step: buffer positioning. As a result, classification is a central building block in the planning process of the DDR approach in SAP S/4HANA. In SAP S/4HANA, classification is carried out exclusively based on SAP Fiori apps.

sections.title: Classification
sections.en: 14.5.2 Classification The Schedule Product Classification (DD) app, along with other DDR-related apps, is available to you after you activate DDR in SAP S/4HANA. These apps are available via the SAP Fiori launchpad. You need to execute some SAP Fiori apps for DDR when you start using DDR as a solu- tion, whereas you’ll use operational SAP Fiori apps for DDR on a daily basis. This section will walk you through the DDR implementation steps, beginning with classifying the products. Technically speaking, buffer positioning may take place before classification, but product classification is an important factor for you to consider when you’re decid- ing where to position a buffer, so you as a materials planner should always start with product classification. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 798 Note Because product classification will help you identify where to position a buffer, we rec- ommend that you classify all the products that may potentially be planned with the DDR logic. After you open the app, an overview screen appears and displays the job runs of the past. Choose Create to start an application job for making the necessary settings for the classification run. The Job Template in Figure 14.14 1 is preselected, while you can maintain a user-defined Job Name to clearly distinguish the individual application jobs. Scroll down and then check the Start Immediately 2 box to begin the application job right away. To schedule an application job, choose Define Recurrence Pattern, which opens the popup shown in Figure 14.15. When you’re scheduling a new job, it’s important to first define the scheduling options that will set the job start date and the recurrence pattern. Figure 14.14 Product Classification While you’re choosing the relevant option from the Recurrence Pattern dropdown scheduling options shown in Figure 14.15, the next few fields will prompt you to main- tain the application job recurrence’s details. For this example, choose Single Run and then choose OK, and the screen in Figure 14.16 will appear. © 799 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment Figure 14.15 Scheduling Options Figure 14.16 Selection Criteria for Products After the scheduling step, the next step is to define the Selection Criteria settings that will be used to select which products will be classified in the job. Figure 14.16 shows four entries in the Product field and the relevant Plant as selection criteria, so the applica- tion job will classify all the products that meet these criteria. An important parameter included in the selection criteria is the Number of Days (Past) value. This will represent the number of days in the past during which the system will look for goods movements (goods issuances/consumptions) to calculate the ADU. In this example, the Number of Days (Past) value is maintained as “30,” so the system will look for goods issued in the past 30 days to determine the ADU. If needed, you can choose to exclude days when there was no goods issuance by checking the Exclude Non Goods Issue Days box (see Figure 14.17). Doing so will increase the ADU of the DDR materials under consideration. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 800 Scrolling down the Selection Criteria section in Figure 14.16 will bring up the screen area shown in Figure 14.17, where you can define the thresholds for the value, BOM usage, and variability classifications. As this app analyzes material movements to determine past consumption during the specified period, you can now influence the selection of material documents that form the basis of consumption. You can influence the threshold values that are used to delimit the previously mentioned classes and maintain them as percentage values in the app, as shown in Figure 14.17. Next, you maintain the relevant classification parameters for ABC, XYZ, and PQR, as shown in Figure 14.17. Then, choose the Check button to validate all the settings you’ve made, and the message noted at the bottom of the screen will appear. Finally, choose Schedule to schedule the application job. Figure 14.17 Thresholds Settings You can make settings for three different classification procedures:  ABC analysis  XYZ analysis  PQR analysis The ABC classification evaluates the relative importance of a material/plant or material/ plant/MRP area combination in relation to the other planning objects analyzed on the basis of historical consumption. The objects are divided into three groups:  A materials Relative to all objects analyzed in a run, these have had the highest consumption value in the past.  B materials These materials have a lower proportion of total consumption than A materials. © 801 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment  C materials These materials have the lowest proportion of total consumption. For classification, the consumption value is determined on the basis of the material documents. The materials involved in the classification run are then sorted in descend- ing order according to their consumption value. An A is assigned to a planning object until the threshold value of, for example, 70% is reached cumulatively across all mate- rials of the class. The subsequent materials are then assigned a B classification until, say, the next 20% of the consumption value is reached; the remaining materials are classified as C materials. The Schedule Product Classification (DD) app can perform this analysis at the level of material/plant combinations, as well as at the level of material/ plant MRP area; other levels aren’t possible. You’ll use the Value (ABC) Classification setting to define whether a product belongs to the group of products with the largest GI value (classification A), to the intermediate group with a medium GI value (classification B), or to the group of products with the lowest GI value (classification C). SAP S/4HANA will calculate the total GI value of these products within the interval defined in the selection criteria, sort materials according to this value, and then define to which group each product belongs, according to the percentages defined at these thresholds. This means that the input for these thresholds will be a percentage, and the sum of the three values should be 100%. SAP S/4HANA will calculate the total GI value within the period; that is, it will consider both the material price and the number of issues. This means that a material with a high price but without too many GIs may have the same classification as a material with a low price and a high number of GIs. ABC analysis is therefore a way of dividing the planning objects into classes according to their value-based significance. This analysis method is now being combined with two other methods that bring in further important aspects for the identification of key characteristics, thus facilitating your goal of being able to assign optimal settings to a planning object (for example, with regard to buffer configuration). One of the two addi- tional classification methods provided by the app is XYZ analysis, which is used to assess the predictability of future demand on the basis of past consumption. Analo- gous to ABC analysis, there are three classes:  X materials These materials have the most constant consumption, so it’s assumed that future consumption can be predicted with high accuracy and that therefore, buffers for the coverage of uncertainties can be reduced.  Y materials The materials in this class have greater variation in consumption than X materials.  Z materials These materials show the greatest variation in consumption, so it’s assumed that predictability accuracy is low. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 802 The criterion for assessing predictability is a statistical fluctuation factor called the coefficient of variation, which determines, squares, and sums up the deviation of each individual observed historical value from the mean value. This sum is then divided by the number of observed values and normalized to the mean value to obtain a relative fluctuation measure. As with ABC analysis, the app enables the maintenance of threshold limits for XYZ analysis. Unlike in ABC analysis, however, these are absolute values, not percentage val- ues. The Variability (XYZ) Classification setting will classify products according to the demand variability. Products with a classification of X will have a low variability, prod- ucts with a classification of Y will have medium variability, and products with a classi- fication of Z will have high variability. The total sum of sales orders overdue, sales orders due on the current date, and order spikes are considered as the actual demand. With the mean and standard deviation of the actual demand, a coefficient of variation is calculated. This coefficient is used as the threshold for variability: the higher the coef- ficient, the higher the variability. The last possible classification methodology within the framework of the app is the PQR analysis, which you can use to analyze the planning objects to determine how many BOMs they are used in. The reason for using this type of analysis is to find out whether there is a single use or multiple uses and thus how many recipients are rele- vant for the object. You use the BOM Usage (PQR) Classification setting to determine whether a material is used in a high number of BOMs (classification P), a medium number of BOMs (classifi- cation Q), or a low number of BOMs (classification R). The value defined for each thresh- old is basically the total number of material BOMs in which a product is used, and it will be classified accordingly. You can use the three classification methods we’ve mentioned here to classify material/ plant combinations or material/plant/MRP area combinations according to the follow- ing essential characteristics:  How important they are in relative terms in the material spectrum  How predictable the future demand is  How many objects are used You can then use these three characteristics to determine the central settings of the DDR process, mainly for the purpose of defining how these objects are controlled and whether you need to maintain inventory for them. Therefore, the results of the classi- fication will play an important role in the next step: buffer positioning. You can view a summary of the results of the classification run in the application log of the Schedule Product Classification (DD) app. You can assess the log directly on the ini- © 803 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment tial screen of the app or by choosing the Back button in the job settings overview. From the overview screen, you can access two more detailed views: 1. Job Log This shows messages that have been identified by the system during runtime. In this context, it’s also possible to filter by message types. 2. Job Details This provides the settings selected for the job, such as threshold values and selec- tions. EFG Analysis According to Replenishment Lead Times In SAP S/4HANA, another option for classifying objects is EFG classification, and it’s based on the replenishment lead time. However, before you decide how to classify an object on the basis of the replenishment lead time, you need to calculate the decou- pled replenishment lead time, which doesn’t exist at this early stage. Therefore, you can only execute this type of classification with a different app in a later process step. We’ll discuss this topic in the context of lead time determination in Section 14.5.4. The results of the classification are often used together with other information to make the previously mentioned decisions, which will play a role at a later stage. If, in addition to the mathematically deductible criteria of the classifications, you use additional cri- teria that can’t be mathematically derived to make appropriate decisions, it’s referred to as a grouping. For example, it can make sense to treat a simple standard part differ- ently from a more complex electronic part with a similar consumption behavior—that is, with comparable classification results. Classes and groups that are combined are often called clusters. After you finish the first step of product classification, the information you need to posi- tion the decoupling buffers of the DDR products becomes available. To position those buffers, you can consider the results of the previous classification to determine whether a product is a good candidate for a buffer. The product classification, however, isn’t the only factor you should consider: there are external factors, and you as a planner can also use your own experience to determine when a buffer is necessary. There are some situ- ations in which a buffer may be a good option:  A semifinished product is classified as P because it’s used in too many BOMs.  Because it’s a key component and it’s used to produce many products, it might be a good idea to position a buffer for this product, thus avoiding stock-outs that might have far-reaching effects.  The source of supply of a given component purchased externally isn’t reliable, and there are too many delayed deliveries. In this case, adding a buffer would mean that the demand for this product would be absorbed by the safety stock if there is a delay. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 804  An important product has a long lead time for production, so any late demand arriv- ing would mean a long delay.  In an MTO scenario, a customer can’t wait for the whole total replenishment lead time to have the product delivered. In this case, adding a buffer to strategically selected components will reduce the lead time, as the demand from this customer will be absorbed by the buffer.

sections.title: Buffer Positioning
sections.en: 14.5.3 Buffer Positioning When entering this application, you use the filters to select which products will be pro- cessed. Those products will be listed with their respective classifications and further details, such as the lead time and the DLT. You can use this information to help deter- mine whether to position a buffer or not (see Figure 14.18). Furthermore, you can select materials and choose Simulate DLT to simulate the decoupled lead times of these DDR materials. Note Notice the two tabs in Figure 14.18: Positioning -Internal and Positioning-External. You can use the former to determine whether the buffer position and the buffer sizing will be internal, that is, whether it will be executed within the SAP S/4HANA. You can use the latter to determine whether it will be external—that is, executed by an external system. Figure 14.18 Unbuffered Products without Decoupled Lead Time © 805 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment You can directly buffer or unbuffer a product on the initial screen of the Buffer Posi- tioning app by choosing the Buffer or Unbuffer button (see Figure 14.18). When you select one of these options, a popup appears to select a new MRP type, the new lot siz- ing procedure, and additional DDR-related material master settings. Any changes you make here will be saved directly in the material master. From the initial screen, you can also simulate the DLT calculation for one or more products, as is done in this example and shown in Figure 14.19 in the Simulated DLT column. You can then analyze the dif- ference between the current DLT and the proposed DLT for informational purposes and even for decision-making purposes. Figure 14.19 Simulated Decoupled Lead Time The Simulated DLT column in Figure 14.19 shows that the DLT isn’t calculated for these four selected products. When you select these four products and then choose Simulate DLT, the simulated DLT (in days) will appear as shown in Figure 14.19. Before you decide whether to position a buffer for a specific product or not, you should select a specific product (in this example, it is material 2262), and navigate to the Buffer Analysis app, where extensive details are available to support you in making your deci- sion (see Figure 14.20). To navigate from the Buffer Positioning app (refer to Figure 14.19) to the Buffer Analysis app, select the relevant material, which in this example is material 2262, and choose the forward arrow icon located on the far-right side of an individual material. The screen shown in Figure 14.20 then appears. Figure 14.20 shows all the information related to the Upstream and Downstream val- ues. In this situation, upstream refers to product components, for which there are the longest path or the whole BOM. Downstream refers to product parents, for which the system displays information that is related to all the BOMs in which a product is used (see Figure 14.21). 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 806 Figure 14.20 Buffer Analysis Figure 14.21 Upstream To analyze a product with many levels of components, we recommend that you switch to the Network Graph view (see Figure 14.21), where there is a graphical representation of the upstream and downstream values and multiple display options, as shown in Figure 14.22. In Figure 14.22, it’s possible to show, for example, the longest path, the product flow, or the entire BOM and BOM usage from the first dropdown, which is located in the top-left corner of the screen. There are different options to highlight specific products in the network, such as buffered products, products under an area of responsibility (AOR), products selected as favorites, and products in one of the product classifications. The example in Figure 14.23 shows a partial view of the whole BOM of a product (2269) with the characteristic (DLT Indicator) chosen from the dropdown in Figure 14.22. The BOM components are highlighted in red, yellow, or green, depending on the BOM usage. © 807 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment Figure 14.22 Network Graph Figure 14.23 Partial View of BOM with Lead Time Variability Tips and Tricks To expand or collapse details of materials in a network, you simply select the relevant material to highlight it and then choose the relevant option, as shown in Figure 14.23. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 808

sections.title: Lead Time Classification
sections.en: 14.5.4 Lead Time Classification The Schedule Lead Time Classification of Products (DD) app uses the DDR planning characteristics maintained in the previous step of buffer positioning to determine the DLTs. These DLTs are the basis for buffer level determination. The second main task of the Schedule Lead Time Classification of Products (DD) app is to carry out a fourth classification method: the EFG classification by replenishment lead time. Unlike the three classification methods explained earlier, this classification can only be carried out after the DDR-dispatching characteristics have been main- tained because the decoupling points aren’t available in the system at the beginning of the process. After you call up the Schedule Lead Time Classification of Products (DD) app, a selection screen appears where you can create a new analysis run. In addition to the job name and start time, it’s important for you to define the objects—for example, Product, Prod- uct Group, Plant, and MRP Controller—for which the system should perform the analy- sis. Another central setting is the horizon for historical replenishment (see Number of Days (Past) in Figure 14.24). Figure 14.24 Job Settings for EFG Analysis © 809 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment Figure 14.24 shows an overview of the possible settings regarding the classification thresholds of the EFG analysis. This app classifies the products according to the lead time (EFG) classification. Just as in the Schedule Product Classification app, the initial screen of this app will show an over- view of the jobs that are already completed, in process, and scheduled. On this screen, you can create a new job, copy an existing job, or check the results of the finished jobs. The thresholds defined here are only for the EFG classification, but a materials planner can define different thresholds depending on the procurement type. The following options are available:  DLT Threshold for Buy This is used for products procured externally—that is, with procurement type F.  DLT Threshold for Make This will be considered for products manufactured internally—that is, products with procurement type E.  DLT Threshold for Transfer This is used for products with a stock transfer special procurement type, which are supplied by means of a stock transfer from another plant. In these thresholds, you as a materials planner can define how many days is considered a short, medium, or long lead time for products manufactured, purchased, or trans- ferred. This differentiation of thresholds depending on the procurement type happens because the lead time to manufacture a product may be very different from the lead time to buy the components. In addition, you must specify appropriate thresholds for the EFG classification. This clas- sification methodology is executed for material/plant combinations with a DDMRP type based on their DLTs, which are also determined in this run together with the single- level replenishment lead times. You can use different threshold values in calendar days for in-house production, external procurement, and stock transfers. In all three cases, the materials are divided into three groups:  E materials Materials with short replenishment lead times according to the respective value definition  F materials Materials with average replenishment lead times according to the threshold value definition  G materials Materials with long replenishment lead times according to the threshold value defi- nition 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 810 While the EFG classification and, by its very nature, the determination of the DLTs is only carried out for decoupled materials with a DDMRP type, the single-level replenish- ment lead times are determined for all selected objects, including those for which no buffering is planned. For in-house production, the system determines the production or process orders over the selected analysis horizon. For all identified objects, the time period between order creation and final confirmation is determined as a single-level replenishment lead time and then aggregated as an average value on the material/plant combination. If no element is found within the selected horizon, the system uses the in-house production time of the material master, which is independent of lot size. The procedure for external procurement is the same, which means the purchase orders within the horizon are selected accordingly, and the period between purchase order creation and the date on which the final delivery indicator is set is determined. As is true for in-house production, there is a fallback scenario for external procurement in case no element is found within the selected horizon. In this instance, the system first uses the planned delivery time of the info record or contract that the MRP run selects as the preferred source of supply according to the settings. The system uses an explo- sion with lot size 1 and the explosion date of the run. If the system can’t determine a replenishment lead time, it uses the planned delivery time of the material master. For interplant transfer materials, the system evaluates the time when the goods/mate- rials were issued from a supplying plant against a stock transport order until these goods were received at the receiving plant. The determination of the DLTs is based on the single-level replenishment lead times determined as described earlier, and it uses the multilevel sum of the longest critical path in each case. The system also delivers an active BAdI implementation that enables the automated maintenance of the values in the material master. Maintenance takes place in the mas- ter data fields that were specified previously as a fallback option for determining replenishment lead times—that is, into the lot-size-independent in-house-production time for in-house production and into the planned delivery time of the preferred source of supply or the material master in the case of external procurement. The DLT is updated in the Tot. Repl. Lead Time (total replenishment lead time) field (in the MRP 3 view) of the material master.

sections.title: Buffer Level Determination
sections.en: 14.5.5 Buffer Level Determination You now use these areas to derive buffer levels. The following areas are distinguished:  TOR This area marks the upper end of the red buffer zone. © 811 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment  TOY This buffer level is the sum of the red and yellow buffer zones.  TOG This buffer level is the sum of the red, yellow, and green buffer zones. If the inventory position is lower than TOY, a replenishment to TOG will be triggered in the planning run. If the stock is above TOG, it’s in the blue zone. This depiction of the buffer zones and their levels is shown in Figure 14.25. Figure 14.25 Buffer Zones and Levels in DDR The following sections will discuss how to schedule buffer level calculation, position buffers, evaluate buffer levels and average daily usage, and adjust buffers. Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation The last step in DDR implementation is the actual buffer calculation, which you can also schedule as a background job by using the Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation app. On the initial screen, there are options to manage the already scheduled and fin- ished jobs and to schedule a new background job execution. The selection screen pro- vides the scheduling parameters and allows you to select which products should be included in the buffer calculation, as shown in Figure 14.26. The Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation app performs the calculation of the buffer zones according to the DDR logic, which means that it calculates the central buffer zones and levels. After calling up the Schedule Buffer Level Calculation app, you as a planner can configure an analysis run, including selecting whether to calculate the ADU—that is, the average demand per day. It’s based on the previous step of classifica- tion, which used the material movements (GIs and GRs). Figure 14.26 shows the possi- ble settings in the Schedule Buffer Proposal Calculation app. You also need to specify here whether the results should be saved or just adopted within the defined tolerances. Min Safety Stock [MARC-EISLO] Safety Stock [MARC-EISBE] Reorder Point [MARC-MINBE] Maximum Stock [MARC-MABST] Red base Red safety Yellow Green Blue TOG – Top of Green TOY – Top of Yellow TOR – Top of Red = Average Demand x Replenishment Lead Time = Yellow x Lead Time Factor = Red Base x Variability Factor = Yellow x Lead Time Factor OR = Min Order Quantity OR = Average Demand x Cycle Time TORS – Top of Red Safety 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 812 Furthermore, you can choose the option to reuse or recalculate the DLT from the mate- rial master by selecting the relevant radio button located in the bottom-right corner of the screen. Figure 14.26 Schedule Buffer Level Calculation App There are also options to choose whether the buffer will be adopted only if the new value is within a tolerance interval (Adopt with Tolerance), whether it will be always adopted (Always Adopt Proposals), or whether it will never be adopted (Do Not Adopt Proposals). If you choose either Adopt with Tolerance or Do Not Adopt Proposals, you’ll then need to review the calculated buffer later in the Manage Buffer Levels app. Furthermore, you can choose whether the ADU will be calculated based on a fixed interval or on a rolling interval and whether the DLT will come from the material master values or will be recal- culated. If needed, you can choose to exclude the days when there was no goods issu- ance by checking the Exclude Non Goods Issue Days box. Doing so will increase the ADU of the DDR materials under consideration. For this example, choose Do Not Adopt Proposals. After you choose Check and then finally Schedule, the system will create a log for the scheduled job. Note You can also use the PIRs to project an ADU in the future and allow the system to also calculate a projected buffer for the future. This is especially relevant, for example, if you know in advance that an event—such as a marketing campaign or a seasonal © 813 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment increase in demand—might affect product consumption. You can define the future horizon during which the system will look for planned independent requirements in the Mass Maintenance of Products (DD) app, which we discuss later in Section 14.5.5. On the initial screen, a flag will appear in the Log column for jobs already completed, and it will show the status of the background job execution. If you double-click this flag, the log details will appear, including any warning or error messages that may prevent the buffer calculation. Position Buffers Next, you use the Manage Buffer Levels app to evaluate the results. Figure 14.27 shows an example of buffer level management by manual intervention. The system proposes that you increase the Current stock of 500 PC to Proposed 1160 PC. The Buffer Levels field first shows the current buffer levels and then shows the proposed buffer levels directly below that (in a tricolor horizontal bar). The Average Daily Usage (Historical and Future) column provides insight into the ADU of material 2269, in the yellow zone (96.667 PC). The lower half of the screen shows the zone-wise suggestions for the pro- posed buffers, and we’ll discuss them in detail soon. Figure 14.27 Buffer Calculation To analyze material 2269. choose the forward arrow located on the far-right side of the screen to go to the next screen shown in Figure 14.28. While selecting a specific DDR product in this application, you can scroll down into a more detailed view of the buffer, ADU, and DLT, as shown in various tabs in Figure 14.28. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 814 Figure 14.28 shows the current lower buffer levels and the system-proposed buffers to be maintained on a daily basis. If you choose a horizontal bar on a specific date, it will reveal the date and details of the respective zones, which, in this example, are that as of July 19, 2024, the Yellow Zone is 483 PC, the Green Zone is 242 PC, and the Red Zone is 435 PC. Figure 14.28 Planning View of Redefined Buffer Levels If you select another view from the Planning View dropdown, such as the Comparison View, the system will show comparisons of various stock levels. The Settings icon lets you switch between viewing the details as zones or as stocks, while using the Legends icon provides details of this visual representation. These icons are located in the top- right corner of the screen. Buffer Levels You can review the calculated buffer levels in the Manage Buffer Levels app. When you enter this application, you’ll see a list of products and the respective buffer levels calcu- lated by the system, including a graphical representation of the historic and projected buffer levels and the ADU. Scroll down the screen shown in Figure 14.28 until the screen shown in Figure 14.29 appears (or choose the Average Daily Usage tab in Figure 14.28). © 815 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment Average Daily Usage The blue bars in Figure 14.29 represent the ADU during the past 30 days, based on actual GIs. The orange bar represents future ADU based on the planned independent require- ments maintained for material 2269 in plant 1710, as well as the settings maintained in the screen for mass adjustments (shown later in Figure 14.34). The past and future hori- zons are also shown as Horizon for Past and Horizon for Future, respectively, in the top- left corner of Figure 14.29. Figure 14.29 Past and Future Average Daily Usage Classifications Figure 14.30 shows any proposed changes in classifications, such as, DLT, BOM usage, and VF, among other options. Here, the DLT for Proposed Buffer is 5.00 Days, and a Change in DLT of 4.00 Days is proposed. Figure 14.30 Decoupled Lead Time Figure 14.31 is the summary of all the current versus proposed changes (if any) to the classification of a DDR-based material. Here, the Proposed Variability Factor is 0.80 and the Proposed DLT Factor is 0.50. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 816 Figure 14.31 Classifications Buffer Adjustments You also can switch to edit mode and make manual adjustments to the calculated buf- fer level by adding an adjustment factor for each zone. Then, the value originally calcu- lated for each zone will be multiplied by this factor within the period defined for this buffer adjustment. After entering edit mode, you select the Show Adjustments option to view all the existing zone adjustments per period. There’s also an option to add a new adjustment or delete an existing one. Figure 14.32 shows an example in which an adjustment was created from July 14 to July 27, 2024, with factors of 1.50, 2.50, and 0.80 for the red, yellow, and green zones, respectively. When you add a new zone adjust- ment, choose Simulate Changes to see the changes reflected in the chart. Based on the information and data that are available, let’s first summarize the classifi- cation of material 2269 undertaken through the DDR process and then understand how the three buffer zones were calculated. All data and calculations are based on a date of July 18, 2024.  ADU = 96.667 PC  DLT = 5 Days  VF = 0.8  DLT Factor = 0.5 Therefore, on July 19, 2024, the yellow zone = ADU × DLT = 96.667 × 5 = 483 PC, which is rounded off because the unit of measure is PC (pieces), and the calculated value is dis- played without decimal places. You can validate this on the screen shown in Figure 14.28. © 817 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment Figure 14.32 Manual Adjustment of Buffer Levels The green zone is calculated as the yellow zone × DLT factor = 483 × 0.5 = 242 PC, which you can validate in Figure 14.28. Because the red zone consists of a red base and a red safety, let’s calculate them first:  Red base = Yellow zone × DLT factor = 483 × 0.5 = 241.5 PC  Red safety = Red base × VF = 241.5× 0.8 = 193 PC  Red zone = Red base + red safety = 241 + 193 = 435 PC (You can validate this in Figure 14.28.) Next, let’s discuss how these three calculated zones update the various fields in the material master (or product):  TOR = Safety stock or the red zone = 435 PC (see the Safety stock (DD) field in Figure 14.33 1)  TOY = TOR + yellow zone = 435 + 483 = 918 PC (see the Reorder Point (DD) field in Figure 14.33 1)  TOG = TOY + green zone = 918 + 242 = 1,160 PC (see the Maximum Stock (DD) field in Figure 14.33 1) Note that the data in Figure 14.33 1 is only updated after you adopt the buffer proposal by choosing Save and Adopt on the screen shown in Figure 14.30. Adopting a buffer pro- posal automatically updates the relevant fields of the material master with updated values. These fields are Safety Stock, Reorder Point, Maximum Stock, and Total Replen- ishment Lead Time. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 818 Note Because product characteristics, such as lead time or usage, may change over time, it’s a best practice to periodically review the product classifications. In addition, you should also periodically review the buffer and recalculate it because the ADU varies over time. For this, you can use the scheduling options available in the relevant DDR apps. Once you’ve adopted the buffer proposal, the next step is to run MRP Live (Transaction MD01N) so that the system creates replenishment proposals. After MRP Live runs, the stock/requirements list (Transaction MD04) is updated accordingly, as shown in Figure 14.33 (see the upper half of the stock requirements list 1). All stock fields, such as Max- imum Stock, Reorder Point, and Safety Stock, are updated along with other demand- driven fields. Recall that all these fields were blank when the DDR process wasn’t initi- ated on material 2269 and for plant 1710 in Section 14.3. Figure 14.33 Buffer Levels Redefined More fields and associated calculations shown in Figure 14.33 2 are discussed in the next section. Finally, when a buffer proposal is created and adopted, the system sets the following values as defaults for a product:  Individual lead time = (Planned delivery duration or in-house production duration, based on the procurement type) + GR processing time © 819 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment  DLT = Individual lead time or total replenishment lead time  ADU = (Reorder point – safety stock) ÷ DLT  LT factor = (Maximum stock – reorder point) ÷ (reorder point – safety stock)  VF = (Safety stock ÷ (maximum stock – reorder point)) – 1 As a self-learning DDR exercise, you can use the safety stock (545), reorder point (1,151), maximum stock (1,453), and decoupled lead time (eight days) data to calculate and val- idate the ADU, LT factor, and VF of 75.7, 0.5, and 0.8, respectively, from the preceding three equations. It’s also important to note that running MRP Live is one way of replenishing the stock. The next section shows how to interactively manage all DDR planning with SAP Fiori apps without needing to run MRP Live. Mass Adjust Buffers In some situations, products can’t be classified because there isn’t enough information in the system. For example, in a new implementation, in which there’s a lack informa- tion about the past consumption of products, the system can’t classify a product according to the value or the variability. The same thing happens during the introduc- tion of a new product, as there isn’t enough information available to classify the prod- uct. In this case, we suggest that you use the Mass Maintenance of Products (DD) app to manually set the product classification. Within this application, you can manage mass changes for any of the product classifi- cations by selecting products and choosing the Change button shown in Figure 14.34. Figure 14.34 Mass Maintenance of Products 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 820 You can also change additional DDR-related fields, such as MRP Type, Maximum Stock Level, Reorder Point, and Safety Stock. The two sections of Figure 14.35 1 and 2 show the popup screen where you implement the mass changes after choosing the Change button. Here, you select which fields you want to changed and which fields should keep their existing values. For example, you can manually define values in the Variability Indicator and Value Indicator dropdowns because these can’t be calculated automatically if enough GIs haven’t been posted in the past. In this application, you can also define the Lead Time calculation methods for the Hori- zon for Past (in Days) and Horizon for Future (in Days) settings. By default, the system will calculate the lead time based on the values defined in the material master, but dif- ferent methods are available in which the lead time will be calculated based on con- firmed production orders. These fields will define the method and the number of days the system will use to find confirmed production orders and calculate the lead time. For this example, you can maintain a value of “15” in the Horizon for Future (in Days) field 2 so that the system will consider the future planning horizon of 15 days with demand, which is basically PIRs, during buffer level determination. Figure 14.35 Mass Adjustments of Values of Products Note The ILT Calc. Method field (not shown but located further down screen 2 in Figure 14.35) is used to calculate the lead time. Lead time can be calculated based on its historic average, which is the default method. Lead time, when predicted, is calculated using © 821 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment machine learning and will grow more accurate over time. Note that in the initial stages, there might be a mismatch between the predictions and the actual lead times. To maintain relevant configuration settings so that they appear in the ILT Calc. Method field in Figure 14.35 2, you use the following configuration menu path in the SAP S/4HANA system: SAP IMG  Production  Demand-Driven Replenishment  Define Lead Time Calculation Method.

sections.title: Planning and Execution
sections.en: 14.5.6 Planning and Execution Now, we’ll discuss the procedures that have been defined in SAP S/4HANA’s automated planning to generate procurement proposals such as purchase requisitions and planned orders. This is followed by interactive planning executed by the user to post- process the results of the planning run. Here, there are two substeps involved. Priority key figures support the decision to convert a procurement proposal (e.g., a planned order) into a fixed element (a production order or a process order) or to convert a pur- chase requisition into a purchase order. Finally, a production order is released for pro- duction again on the basis of a priority key figure in a comparable step. This means that the planning and execution procedure for DDR doesn’t differ from the process flow of a classic MRP run. However, as already described in the previous sec- tions, there is a significant difference in the control logic for the elements to be buff- ered, and the planning and execution part of the DDR process is now also characterized by specific features. We discuss the functions available for these three steps in the fol- lowing two sections. The following sections will also discuss how to use the decoupling points set previously together with the buffer levels to generate appropriate procurement and in-house pro- duction proposals. Furthermore, we’ll discuss three DDR-specific apps: Replenishment Planning by Priority, Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status, and Planner Over- view. Automated Demand-Driven Replenishment Planning in the MRP Run You can carry out the planning in DDR with SAP S/4HANA by using the normal MRP run, which starts with running Transaction MD01N or using the Material Requirements Planning app, for example. Using the MRP type previously maintained, the planning run identifies the materials for which the DDR logic is to be used. These must have planning procedure C indirectly via the MRP type. The net flow equation is used for generating receipt elements: On-hand stock + Open supply – Open past (including today’s) demand – Qualified spike demand 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 822 In the planning run, the net flow position calculated as described is compared with the TOY buffer level. If the net flow position is lower than TOY, then a receipt element is cre- ated that raises the stock quantity to TOG. Figure 14.36 shows the results of a DDR plan- ning run as an example. Figure 14.36 Results of DDR Planning Run (Example) The following input factors are used for the calculation:  On-hand stock The quantity available for the planning run that would also be used in other plan- ning procedures in the MRP run  Open supply All receipts that have already been ordered but aren’t yet available  Open demand (past, including current day): – Dependent requirements – Sales and distribution requirements (schedule lines, outbound deliveries, and open sales order quantities) – Stock transfer order requirements – Stock transfer reservations (via movement type 541) This explicitly excludes dependent requirements, PIRs, and stock transport requisi- tions.  Qualified spike demand In particular, you should take high demand into account when determining the qualified spike demand. This is a future daily requirement that lies above the spike Red Top of Red (ToR) = 4.200 Units Yellow Top of Yellow (ToY) = 8.000 Units Green Blue Top of Green (ToG) = 12.000 Units Created Proposal = 12.000 – 7.350 = 4.650 Units Net Flow Position = 7.350 Units © 823 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment threshold within the spike horizon (see Figure 14.37). The spike threshold is defined by a factor of the TOR, which is maintained at the plant level. The spike horizon is cal- culated as follows: Today + Factor × DLT + Constant offset The input values for the factor and constant offset are also maintained at the plant level and in the Buffer Profile app (see Figure 14.38). Figure 14.37 Usage of Spikes in DDR Planning Run Figure 14.38 Buffer Profile Settings at Plant Level (Plant 1710) You apply forward scheduling by using the DLT, whereby the GR processing times and the safety times of the horizon key are also included in the same way as for normal MRP. We recommend that you eliminate these times to avoid multiple consideration. To calm the planning results, you should use a planning time fence with firming type 1 (MRP Type D1 for DDR) so that a new element that has been moved by one day can’t be created in the subsequent planning run. Evaluating the DDR Results after MRP Run Figure 14.39 and Figure 14.40 are part of the same Header Details screen, and they show an example of the MRP results for Material 2269 in the Stock/Requirements List screen (via Transaction MD04). On expanding the screen, notice the Demand-Driven Replen- ishment tab and associated DDR information. TOG TOY TOR Time Sales Order Quantity Spike Horizon Today Spike Spike Threshold = 50% x TOR (Past) Due SO’s Demand, in particular Spikes 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 824 Figure 14.39 Stock/Requirements List Figure 14.40 Stock/Requirements List: Detailed View Let’s discuss and evaluate the DDR results for Material 2269 in Plant 1710. The mate- rial’s Stock on Hand is 60, which is below the reorder point. The Open Supply quantity is 100 and is basically a production order for which the goods aren’t delivered to the warehouse yet. The Open Past Demand quantity (from the sales order) is 600, whereas the Qualified Spike Dmd (the customer order for which the delivery date is within the Spike Horizon Date of 07/22/2024) is 652.500. The spike horizon is a time window that starts the day after today and comprises the DLT multiplied by a factor called the spike horizon DLT multiplier, with the addition of an offset called the spike horizon constant. The purpose here is that DDR will only react to a demand within this time window to ensure product availability. So, in this example, the Spike Horizon Constant is 5 (days), and the Spike Horizon DLT Multiplier is 0.5. Referring to Figure 14.39 again, the DLT is 7 days. So, the calculation for the spike horizon is as follows: Spike horizon = 5 (Spike horizon constant) + (5 (DLT) × 0.5) = 7 days This is shown in the Order Spike Horizon field in Figure 14.39. © 825 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment However, MRP won’t react to all the demand within the spike horizon—only to quali- fied demands. To be considered a qualified demand, a requirement quantity must be greater than the spike threshold, which is calculated by multiplying the safety stock by the spike threshold factor. Only those highlighted demands will be considered quali- fied demands because they are within the spike horizon and above the spike threshold. The qualified spike demand of 600 is not considered by DDR because it’s smaller than the Order Spike Threshold quantity of 652.500. An order spike threshold is a multiplica- tion of the safety stock (in this example, 435.000) by the Spike Threshold (1.50) as defined in the buffer profile (refer to Figure 14.38). Therefore, 435.000 × 1.5 = 652.500 is the order spike threshold. Note The Spike Horizon Constant, Spike Horizon DLT Multiplier, Spike Threshold, and On- Hand Alert Threshold settings are maintained at the plant level in the Buffer Profile Maintenance app (see Figure 14.38 again). However, there are additional scenarios in which MRP may generate a new replenish- ment proposal for a DDR product even if the stock isn’t yet below the reorder point. During the MRP calculation, the system will try to calculate the net flow position, using the following equation: On-hand stock + open supply – open past (including today’s) demand – qualified spike demand Referring to Figure 14.39 and this equation, the net flow position of 160 is calculated as follows: Net flow position: 60 + 100 = 160 Because the open past demand and any qualified spike demand are also considered in the equation, the available quantity would be below the reorder point. Therefore, MRP will create a new replenishment proposal to replenish up to the maximum stock level. In the equation, the qualified spike demand, as well as Figure 14.40, shows that we have a line with the End of Spike Horizon, so it’s important to define those terms for better understanding of the MRP results for a demand-driven product. Replenishment proposals created for a DDR product will always be forward scheduled, considering today as the start date and using the DLT to calculate the finish date for both externally and internally procured products. As shown earlier in Figure 14.39 and Figure 14.40, in which the stock/requirements list for a demand-driven product is shown, there’s also a planning time fence when using a demand-driven MRP type. This time fence is always equal to the DLT, and it uses fixing type 1 by default. This time fence exists to prevent the dates of a planned order created by MRP for a demand- driven product from being changed in the following MRP run. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 826 The MRP run is usually executed daily as a background job, and it should ensure that there are enough replenishment proposals to cover the DDR buffer. In addition to the MRP run, a materials planner can use the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app and the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status app to monitor the buffer coverage and take actions to avoid shortages, such as manually creating a new replen- ishment proposal or expediting an existing order. These two apps are discussed in the following sections. Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority You can postprocess the planning run by using the Replenishment Planning by Plan- ning Priority app. After you call up the app, a buffer level overview will be displayed. The planning priority app is used for sorting, which results in a percentage comparison of TOG with the following value: On-hand stock + Receipts – Relevant requirement elements You can use the displayed results to convert planned orders into production orders and process orders, or to convert purchase requisitions into purchase orders. The Replen- ishment Planning by Planning Priority app displays a list of products within the user’s AOR for which the net flow position is below the reorder point. However, you can change this by using the filter option to add more planning priority statuses if you need to see additional products. All those products will be sorted according to the plan- ning priority, which is the percentage of the buffer covered by the net flow position. The lines are highlighted in color according to the respective planning priority status. Figure 14.41 shows an example in which the first three lines are highlighted in red because the net flow position is below the safety stock and the fourth line is high- lighted in blue because the net flow position is above the maximum stock level. Figure 14.41 Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority App © 827 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment There are five different planning priority statuses:  Above maximum stock  At 0 or below with demand  Below reorder point  Below safety stock  Below or at maximum stock Figure 14.41 also shows an example of a planning run result sorted according to plan- ning priority. The screen shown in Figure 14.41 displays a Create Supply button on those lines for which the net flow position is below the reorder point or the safety stock. If you choose this option, DDR will create a new planned order to replenish this product up to the maximum stock level. The scheduling logic for this planned order will be similar to the logic used by MRP: it uses forward scheduling, considering today as the start date and using the DLT to calculate the finish date. Next, select a specific line in Figure 14.41 and then choose the forward arrow located on the far-right side of the screen. This brings up the Supply/Demand List tab and addi- tional information about the product, as shown in Figure 14.42. Figure 14.42 Supply/Demand List The Supply/Demand List tab consists of information about the existing demand and replenishment elements. You can edit or delete an existing replenishment element or manually create a new planned order by defining the quantity, date, and source of supply. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 828 The Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app shown in Figure 14.42 focuses on the planning side because it’s based on the comparison of the net flow position with the reorder point. Meanwhile, the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status app (see Figure 14.43), discussed next, is used to manage short-term execution. It focuses on the comparison of the on-hand stock with the safety stock. Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status Replenishment execution according to the DDR logic is performed with the Replenish- ment Execution by On-Hand Status app. In this app, the current warehouse stock situ- ation is displayed, and the on-hand status is used for prioritization, which is calculated as follows: On-hand stock (without receipt and requirement elements) compared to TOR (in %) This app will show, by default, a list of products for which the on-hand stock is below the safety stock (the red zone), but you can include additional products by changing the default filters. Figure 14.43 shows this list of products, where the icons in the On-Hand Buffer Status column highlight products with stock below the safety stock. Whenever there is an open replenishment proposal for a product for which the stock is below the safety stock, the Expedite Supply button will appear in the Execute Action column. By choosing this option, you can change the dates of an existing replenish- ment element to ensure that the safety stock is covered as quickly as possible, thus avoiding any potential product shortage. Figure 14.43 Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status App © 829 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment Note By default, the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app will only show the option to create a new supply, and the Replenishment Execution by On-Hand Status app will only show the option to expedite an existing supply. However, you can change the column settings (via the filter option) in both applications to include a column for the missing action. You can also include additional columns with important informa- tion, such as the reorder point or the safety stock. When you choose the option to Expedite Supply in Figure 14.43, a popup appears (see Figure 14.44) that displays a list of the existing replenishment proposals and additional information, such as the source, status, and order progress (in case of production orders). You can also change the date in the Planned Availability Date column to expe- dite the replenishment proposal. Figure 14.44 Expedite Supply Alternatively, instead of choosing the option to expedite a replenishment proposal as shown in this example, you can simply choose one of the lines to see the Supply/ Demand List and additional information about the demand-driven product, exactly as in the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app shown earlier in Figure 14.41. You as a materials planner can also use the Projected On-Hand Alerts app (see Figure 14.45) to identify situations in which the projected stock within the decoupled lead time will not be sufficient to cover all the requirements. The following are the alerts to choose from:  Stockout Alert This alert appears when the requirement will lead to a shortage within the decou- pled lead time.  Safety Stock Alert This alert appears when the requirement causes the projected stock to fall below the safety stock within the decoupled lead time. 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 830  Threshold Alert This alert appears when the projected stock falls below the safety stock threshold as defined in configuration. You can navigate to the detailed screen not only to get more information about the var- ious alerts but also to create new replenishment proposals. Figure 14.45 Projected On-Hand Alerts Planner Overview The Planner Overview app displays an overview of all materials that need to be treated manually. Therefore, it considers one or both of the following situations:  Buffer level calculation results need attention or action.  Planning status and on-hand status need attention or action. There are three different tiles in this application, as shown in Figure 14.46:  Buffer Level Management This tile shows the products with deviating buffers that a planner should approve.  Replenishment Planning Similar to the Replenishment Planning by Planning Priority app, this tile shows products with a net flow position below the reorder point.  Replenishment Execution Similar to the Replenishment Execution by Planning Priority app, this tile shows products with a stock level below the safety stock level. The Planner Overview app enables you to choose the breakdown by procurement type or by each DDR product classification (ABC, EFG, PQR, or XYZ). You do this by choosing the relevant option from the dropdown available in all three tiles. Within this app, you can also choose any of the bars in the charts to drill down into the application and see © 831 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment the respective list of products. For example, in Figure 14.46, there is one product with the Buy procurement type on the chart for the Replenishment Planning tile. Choosing this bar will branch you into the Replenishment Planning app, which will display this specific product. Therefore, you can use this application as a central point of entry from which you can monitor the main DDR KPIs and take actions to correct problems and deviations. Figure 14.46 Planner Overview Historical Buffer Performance The Historical Buffer Performance app (see Figure 14.47) is a new app that shows the his- torical data of a material buffer stock within a specified time period. Figure 14.47 Historical Buffer Performance The app compares the material stock with the buffer in the previous month and shows the number of days a material was outside the outlier area (red zones). You can change the Evaluation Horizon to different time period or granularity—such as last week, last 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 832 month, last quarter, or even a custom period as long as it is within a maximum of 92 days. If you need to, you can even drill down into the historical buffer position to every single day of the week to see how that view compares with the actual stock situation. The next section will discuss important KPIs and metrics relevant to DDR, including inventory position and inventory sizing. Furthermore, it will discuss the factors that affect the DDR process.

sections.title: Metrics and Analytics
sections.en: 14.5.7 Metrics and Analytics As discussed in Section 14.2, the key to ensuring success in materials planning and a smoother supply chain using the DDR approach is to focus on protecting and promot- ing relevant material and information flows. Doing so is only possible by setting up metrics (KPIs) and leveraging the power of analytics to bring constant improvements. The three factors responsible for smoother and more efficient demand-driven plan- ning are demand signal reliability, stability, and velocity, and we’ll cover those later in this section. Let’s first discuss the key metrics of DDR. Key Metrics DDMRP requires setting up inventory buffers at strategic points in the supply chain to independently plan, schedule, and execute material supply based on actual demand. This is a practice known as inventory positioning. Not only is the inventory position critical, but so is determining how much inventory to place to bring harmony between material demand and supply. This is a practice known as inventory sizing. Effective inventory positioning and inventory sizing immediately lead to business benefits (such as reduction in working capital, storage costs, and material expediting costs) while increasing production efficiencies through effective machine capacity and resource utilization. These key metrics are discussed in the following sections. Inventory Positioning A standard graphical representation of inventory positioning will show the on-hand stock position of a buffered material wherein the x-axis is the time range, and the y-axis is the stock position. The black dotted line reflects the time series of material replenishment. It’s important to note the instances when this buffered material pene- trated deep into the alert zones, meaning the yellow zone and even the red zone. Evalu- ating such instances by addressing the root causes that led to these zone penetrations often reveals the prime areas for improvement. Some of the root causes for such situa- tions can be the fact that the materials planner didn’t place a timely replenishment order (i.e., waited too long), an shipment from a supplier was inordinately delayed, or there was a production breakdown. Similarly, companies often evaluate a supplier’s per- formance based on the number of times there were stock-outs due to that particular supplier’s delayed material delivery, how long such delays lasted, and how many parent © 833 14.5 Running Demand-Driven Replenishment materials were affected. Along with analytics, these key supplier metrics (KPIs) help the company make informed decisions on whether to continue with the same supplier, work on an improvement plan, or simply look for alternative sources of supply. Figure 14.48 is another view of key metrics for stock buffers (on the left side of the fig- ure) and capacity buffers (on the right side). Again, the dashed lines in both figures are situations worth evaluating for improvement for understock, overstock, and capacity. To provide a more holistic view of such alert zones, such as dark red, red, and yellow, it often makes sense to evaluate them over a longer-term horizon rather than over a shorter one. Doing so will ensure that the analytics can depict a broader trend over a relatively longer period of time instead of showing a sudden erosion of zones due to a one-time supplier delay or an unforeseen machine breakdown. Figure 14.48 Focusing on Outliers (©Demand Driven Institute, Used with Permission) Inventory Sizing Similarly, evaluating a group of materials with similar characteristics (e.g., materials with long lead times, several materials ordered from the same supplier or a group of suppliers, and production materials facing delays due to bottleneck capacities at spe- cific work centers) provides greater insights for improvement initiatives than evaluat- ing individual materials’ replenishment situations at decoupling points. Here’s an example of typical stages or phases wherein you can use analytics to monitor the replenishment’s critical metrics and then undertake improvement initiatives to monitor the progress of these critical metrics. Stage 1 shows a bimodal distribution of material stock wherein there were many deep penetrations into the yellow, red, and dark red zones for both stock-outs and overstock situations. This alarming situation is reflected by the dashed lines at both ends of the two curves. At stage 2, the DDR’s imple- mentation takes place, and the situation improves significantly. However, notice that there are still outliers on both sides of the curve, as shown by the dashed lines. There’s still some room for improvement at both ends, by eliminating the stock-out and over- stock situations. Additional buffer improvement initiatives, such as procuring materials Dark Red Dark Red 0 Optimal Range Warning Y R Warning Y R Too Little Too Much Optimal Warning (High) Warning (Low) Extreme High Extreme Low Too Much Too Little 14 Demand-Driven Replenishment 834 from alternative sources that may be slightly more expensive but can greatly reduce the replenishment lead time, finally result in stage 3. Factors Affecting Demand-Driven Replenishment Finally, let’s discuss the three factors that play a critical role in measuring demand-driven metrics and providing reliable analytics:  Signal integrity Signal integrity in the supply chain monitors whether there’s complete, correct, and timely communication of the required data and relevant information to ensure timely replenishment. In other words, signal integrity status is a measure of how rapidly SAP S/4HANA and the planners respond to the demand signals generated by SAP S/4HANA. This assessment is made by comparing each day’s net flow position against TOG, which is for order recommendation and not order execution. This replenishment should not only be timely but also based on the recommended quantity suggested by the DDR approach. A delayed placement of a replenishment order or a quantity mismatch will definitely lead to buffer erosion that will lead you into one of the alert zones.  Decoupling point integrity A decoupling point is an independent stock position in the supply chain that is strate- gically positioned to absorb the effects of demand variability or supply disruptions— factors that are all too common in today’s volatile and complex supply chain. To ensure the integrity and reliability of a decoupling point, it’s important to constantly evaluate the reasons for, factors behind, and causes of deviations in relevant material and information flows. Using analytics, you can determine the materials that exhibit a large variability between planned and actual supply order management and make them immediate candidates for attention and corrective action. With analytics, you can compare the on-hand inventory value with TOY (the execution zone) to generate a status expressed as a percentage.  Decoupling point velocity The velocity at a decoupling point measures the frequency of planned and actual placement of supply orders. This measurement is known as order frequency vari- ance. For example, say that after you implement DDR planning, 10 purchase orders for a material are planned to be placed with a vendor on a monthly basis. However, in actuality, 5,616 purchase orders were placed within the same time period. This deviation between planned and actual purchase orders points to an area where order frequency needs to increase. Similarly, a group of materials belonging to the same classifications or attributes are measured for flow index, meaning how their ordering frequencies compare with their ADU. In addition, although the focus is often on materials that move too slowly or are ordered too infrequently, an equally important factor to look into is those materials that are ordered very frequently. They mask the actual velocity of materials’ movements and therefore are candidates © 835

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 14.6 Summary for improvement in materials flow. With analytics, you can monitor such critical material flow metrics and take action to improve the flow index. 14.6 Summary DDR shifts the entire paradigm from the “supply” side of planning to the “demand” side, wherein only the materials in demand are procured and produced to reduce stock- outs and overstock situations. This chapter covered the necessary DDR configuration and master data setup, and it then reviewed a few relevant transactions to accurately and correctly reflect them in the DDR business processes. The DDR business processes consist of classifying products, positioning buffers, and calculating buffers. We also dis- cussed the planning and execution steps of DDR. The next chapter covers predictive material requirements planning (pMRP), which is an innovative materials and capacity planning simulation tool that can help you improve your logistics and supply chain management. ©

sections.title: Basics
sections.en: 15.1 Basics The pMRP functionality doesn’t require any configuration settings at all. This means that you can use pMRP right away as long as the proper operational MRP configuration is in place, such as a valid plant, MRP controllers, and so on. The pMRP functionality does, however, need the following basic master data and transactional data to already be in place:  Material master Any MRP-relevant material can be used for a pMRP simulation. Define scenario Create simulation Adjust simulation Analyze Process simulation Copy and simplify master data = reference data Implement changes to operations © 839

sections.title: Setup
sections.en: 15.2 Setup  BOM A valid BOM with the BOM usage set to 1—Production should exist for a material that will be used in a simulation.  Work center The work center used in a simulation should have at least one capacity relevant for finite scheduling.  Routing A valid routing should exist, and the operation in the bottleneck resource operation should be relevant to capacity planning.  Production version At least one valid production version referring to the BOM and routing should exist. If there is more than one valid production version, then you can change the source of supply during the simulation.  Purchasing info record A purchasing info record is a one-to-one material-to-supplier relationship that is needed for the pMRP process so that the system can assign at least one source of supply to each material that is procured.  Planned independent requirements PIRs for the relevant materials must be maintained. All of this data is also used by MRP for planning materials. Before you start to use pMRP, all of the relevant master data must be available. As one of the main objectives behind pMRP is to allow the user to check whether there is enough capacity to produce the forecasted quantities, it’s very important to correctly set up the routing and the work center with the standard values and the proper formu- las. From a transactional data perspective, the main input for pMRP is a demand plan, which is represented by PIRs (Transaction MD61). The planning strategy used isn’t rele- vant because it supports any requirement type, and you can use an active or an inactive PIR version. When choosing an inactive version, the capacity planner will need to select the version number during simulation creation. 15.2 Setup The first step in the pMRP process is to create a pMRP simulation, wherein you as a planner can choose to restrict a set of master data for simulation purposes. When you create a simulation, you can select which top-level materials, components, or work cen- ters will be considered for pMRP. In pMRP, the planning will happen during the simula- tion creation itself. With pMRP, you will be able to process the simulation immediately after the creation is finished and will not have to wait for a planning run. The pMRP 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 840 process uses simplified planning logic, in which all materials use a deterministic MRP and a lot-for-lot procedure. Before creating the simulation, you must ensure that the relevant master data has been created. You must also have created the PIRs, since they will be the main input into the pMRP simulation. The system will plan the selected materials and determine the work center capacity requirements based on those PIRs. A word of caution here: when a simulation is created, master data will be copied into it, so subsequent changes to master data may not be considered. Creating a pMRP simulation is basically a background job that you can schedule through the Schedule pMRP Simulation Creation app. You can also use this application to manage background jobs that are already finished, scheduled, or in process. As shown in Figure 15.2, an icon in the Log column will show the status of the finished background executions, and another icon appears in the Results column. Choosing the icon in the Log column will show the details of success, warning, or error messages that may have been triggered during each step of simulation creation. You can create, copy, and delete a simulation creation (although deletion is only possi- ble for scheduled jobs). To create a new application job, choose Create on the screen shown in Figure 15.2, which will bring up the screen shown in Figure 15.3. Figure 15.2 Application Jobs On the screen shown in Figure 15.3. you can use the dropdown list in the Job Template field to choose from the following three job templates, which have different selection criteria:  Creation of pMRP Data via Top-Level Materials In this template, object selection is based on the material master, so the selection cri- teria will be focused on material master information, such as material number, MRP controller, and low-level code. The system will look for PIRs for the relevant materi- als and run simplified MRP to determine the required quantity for each level and the capacity allocated in the work center. © 841 15.2 Setup  Creation of pMRP Data via Components Selecting this template will enable you to select components as inputs for creating the simulation. In this case, pMRP will use the BOM to find relevant upper-level materials and look for demands for these materials.  Creation of pMRP Data via Work Center In this template, object selection is based on the work center, so the selection criteria will be focused on the work center. The system will look for capacity requirements in the selected work centers and then identify the top-level materials and the respec- tive PIRs. For this example, you choose the first option from the dropdown list in Figure 15.3 1 and then click the Step 2 button. Figure 15.3 Scheduling pMRP Then, in the Scheduling Options section 2, you define whether the job will be executed immediately or scheduled to run on a future date. The creation of the pMRP simulation will be executed only once; therefore, it should not be executed as a periodic job, and the Recurrence Pattern value will be set to Single-Run by default. Then, click the Step 3 but- ton on the screen shown in Figure 15.3 to make the screen shown in Figure 15.4 appear. In the Parameter Section of the screen shown in Figure 15.4, fill in the ID for Reference Plan and Simulation ID fields, which are used to identify the simulation. You should use unique values; the reference ID and simulation ID cannot be the same.
