The ID for the 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 842 reference plan will represent the set of master data selected according to the selection criteria, and you can add new simulations later under the same reference ID. Then, in the Parameter Section, select the Bucket Category (daily, weekly, or monthly buckets) and the start and end date for the simulation. Figure 15.4 Parameters Selection in pMRP Scheduling, Part 1 In the Limitations section, there are more fields you can use to restrict the selection. You can use these fields to ensure that pMRP will be executed only for materials with a specific BOM, routing, or material status, or for a BOM or task list usage. Then, scroll down the screen shown in Figure 15.4 until the screen shown in Figure 15.5 appears. The Object Selection section of the screen shown in Figure 15.5 contains the fields that you will use to select materials or work centers. Depending on the job template you’ve selected, you’ll see material-related fields (such as the Material, Plant, MRP Controller, Material Type, and Low-Level Code) or work center-related fields (such as Work Center, Work Center Category, and Person Responsible) here. You can also define whether stock transfers will be considered in the scenario and which plants and which work centers from those supplying plants should be considered in the simulation. Finally, in the Document Data section, you choose whether the opening stock will be zero, whether it will be the safety stock or the actual material stock at the plant, whether an inactive PIR version will be considered instead of the active version, and whether the sim- ple discontinuation will be considered. You define all of these settings in the reference plan while creating the simulation. You cannot change them later, when you’re adding new simulations to the same ref- erence plan. Once you click the Schedule button, the background job to create the new simulation will be scheduled. Depending on the number of materials you select for the simulation, the job may take some time because it executes an actual planning © 843 15.2 Setup run. We highly recommend that you first check the completeness and correctness of the data you’ve maintained by choosing Check; the message that will appear at the bottom of the screen will confirm whether the application job can be scheduled. Figure 15.5 Parameters Selection in pMRP Scheduling, Part 2 Figure 15.6 shows the details of the simulation creation logs, including the success and error messages that have been triggered. When you choose the icon in the Results col- umn shown previously in Figure 15.2, the screen will jump into the spool, which dis- plays a list of the materials and work centers selected for the simulation. Figure 15.6 Application Log After you create the pMRP simulation, you will be able to process the simulation in the Process pMRP Simulations app, as discussed in the next section. 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 844

sections.title: Managing pMRP
sections.en: 15.3 Managing pMRP In the Process pMRP Simulations app, you as a planner will actually work with the sim- ulation, analyze the planning results, identify and resolve capacity issues, and validate the forecast you’ve created. Once the simulation is processed, you can release the results to operational planning in the form of PIRs. Then, the forecast can become rele- vant to MRP. When you open this app, it will display a list of all the created simulations, including the total number of capacity issues, the delivery performance, and the simulation sta- tus. These appear in separate columns. The number of capacity issues represents the total number of buckets in which a capacity issue was identified, and the delivery per- formance represents the percentage of the originally planned forecast that can be ful- filled. For example, if a capacity issue is found and the forecast quantity was reduced to resolve the issue, then the delivery performance will be adjusted accordingly. The Simulation Status column is related to the results of the simulation creation; if the status is Created with Warnings or Fatal Errors, you can refer to the simulation creation logs shown in Figure 15.7. Figure 15.7 Processing pMRP Simulations In this application, you also have the option to copy, rename, or delete an existing sim- ulation or override errors. When you copy a simulation, it will be linked to the same ref- erence data as the original simulation. Also, you can choose whether to copy data from the simulation or from the reference data, as shown in Figure 15.8. If you choose to copy from the simulation, then changes already made to the simulation will also be copied; if you choose to copy from the reference data, changes made to the simulation will not be considered and the original forecast quantities from the reference data will be cop- ied. You can also add a percentage for the Overload Tolerance value so that pMRP will not consider a work center overload until reaching the tolerance. © 845 15.3 Managing pMRP When you choose one of the simulations, the Demand Plan Simulation screen will appear (see Figure 15.9). This screen shows the lines with the materials selected for the simulation, and each column represents the forecast quantity for a specific bucket. Some cells are highlighted in red because there is an issue with a capacity overload in the work center: the forecasted quantities will require more time to be produced than is available with the current shift schedules in the work centers. The capacity issue may be related to either top-level materials or a semifinished product in the BOM’s lower levels. Figure 15.8 Copying pMRP Simulation Figure 15.9 Demand Plan Simulation The Demand Plan Simulation screen shows the number of capacity issues, but there’s no issue with delivery performance. The number of capacity issues will not necessarily match the number of highlighted cells; two or more cells may be highlighted due to the same capacity issue in a specific work center. In this example, the delivery performance is still 100% because the originally forecasted quantities have not yet been changed, but this number may change when a supply chain planner tries to resolve the capacity issues caused by the forecasted quantities. We’ll cover that scenario later in this chap- ter. On this screen, you choose from the following options in the Quantities Displayed dropdown list: 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 846  Simulation Only This column shows the forecast quantities related to the simulation.  Reference/Simulation Two columns will appear: the original quantity from the reference and the simula- tion quantities. With this option, you can compare the changes made to the simula- tion with the original quantities to form a better estimation of the impact of the changes over the originally forecasted quantity.  Delta of the Reference and Simulation This column only shows the difference between the reference and the simulation. This view also helps you estimate the impacts of the changes you’ve made in the simulation. If you choose Capacity Plan Simulation from the Simulation Views dropdown list located in the top right-hand corner of Figure 15.9, a different screen will appear. It focuses on work center capacity, as shown in Figure 15.10. The upper part of the screen shows a list of the work centers selected for this simulation, with the total number of hours allocated and the remaining capacity of the work centers for every month. In the lower part of the screen, a chart shows a graphical overview of the work centers’ possi- ble load and the overload. In the chart bars, the green area represents the feasible load (the capacity requirements that can be fulfilled with the current work center capacity), and the red area represents the capacity overload that is causing the issue. Figure 15.10 Capacity Plan Simulation In the capacity plan simulation, two buttons correspond to the two alternatives for dealing with the capacity issues:  Disregard Capacity Issues The pMRP process will simply ignore all the existing capacity issues for the selected work center. The total number of capacity issues will be reduced, and the cells will no longer be highlighted in red. © 847 15.3 Managing pMRP  Change Available Capacity You can change the work center’s available capacity to allow for a capacity overload. When you choose this option, a popup will appear (see Figure 15.11), wherein you can choose months and the percentage of overload allowed. Months with a capacity issue will be highlighted in red, and the system will also propose a capacity utiliza- tion that can resolve the capacity issue for each month. Be sure to select one or more relevant months from the Months to Show dropdown so that the system brings up the option to make changes to the existing capacities. Figure 15.11 Changing Available Capacity On both the Capacity Plan Simulation screen and the Demand Plan Simulation screen, if you choose the Inspector view, the system will display a side panel with additional information about the selected object (see Figure 15.12). When you’re using the Inspec- tor on the Capacity Plan Simulation screen, the inspected objects will be the work cen- ter and the capacity allocated for the selected month, and the Inspector will also show the top-level materials related to the capacity allocated in the work center. Figure 15.12 Inspector Panel 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 848 If you use the Inspector tool from the Demand Plan Simulation screen, then the inspected objects will be the material and the quantity forecasted for the selected month. The tool will also display the capacity issues related to the forecast created for this specific top-level material, even if they’re caused by a material in the lower BOM levels. Also, when you’re using the Inspector from the Demand Plan Simulation screen, a link will appear that, when clicked, will bring up the multilevel material simulation for the selected demand. Figure 15.12 shows the Inspector view for a demand plan simulation. It includes links to the Multi-Level Material Simulation and the capacity plan simulation for the work cen- ters with capacity issues. (The latter is not shown, but scrolling down will reveal it). The screen in Figure 15.13 will appear when you select the Simulation Components option from the Simulation Views dropdown list in Figure 15.12. This view is a summary of various components and their availability in the chosen time bucket. (Here, it’s the monthly time bucket.) Figure 15.13 Simulation Components If you click the Multi-Level Material Simulation link, the system will bring up a screen with a complete BOM for a specific bucket, including the details of the required and missing quantities for each material in each BOM level (see Figure 15.14). There may be a number of issues and a number of sources of supply available for each material; therefore, it’s possible to identify on exactly which BOM level there is a capacity issue. Figure 15.14 shows the details of the multilevel material simulation, and it indicates that several materials have capacity issues. In the multilevel material simulation, it’s also possible to resolve the capacity issues for top-level and lower-level materials. For materials with several sources of supply (and thus with several valid production versions), you can select the Change Source of © 849 15.3 Managing pMRP Supply option and then select a different production version. When you choose this option, a popup appears (see Figure 15.15) in which you can distribute the forecasted quantity among the different production versions that are available for the selected material. Here, you’ll see an overview of the available capacity for each source of supply and a proposed distribution of the forecast quantity among the different production versions. Also, you can manually define the quantity distribution or select the Adopt Proposal option (see Figure 15.15) to automatically adopt the proposed distribution. Figure 15.14 Multilevel Material Simulation Figure 15.15 Changing Source of Supply Figure 15.16 shows that the demand for the month of January 2025 is being shifted to Feb- ruary 2025 (preproduction) to balance the capacity overload at the given work center. 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 850 Figure 15.16 Shifting Top-Level Demand The Pull In Quantity option shown in Figure 15.14 will enable you as a capacity and materials planner to either preproduce an in-house-produced material or request early procurement of an externally procured material (see Figure 15.17). Figure 15.17 Preproduction or Early Procurement of Components If there are still any unresolved issues that need attention or the action of a capacity planner, then you can evaluate these issues in the Issue List (see Figure 15.18). Here, you can choose to disregard the capacity overload issues you face. © 851 15.3 Managing pMRP Figure 15.18 Issue List Figure 15.19 is the Demand Plan Simulation screen, which shows that reducing the orig- inal PIRs leads to poorer delivery performance. For this example, previously, delivery performance was 100%, but after a reduction of the PIRs for a few months, delivery per- formance has correspondingly decreased to 86%. The right-hand side of the screen shows the complete log or history of all the changes made to this pMRP plan. Figure 15.19 Delivery Performance Impacted Tips and Tricks If you choose one of the many KPIs displayed at the header level in Figure 15.19, a popup will appear and show you more details about the issues facing the KPI. From this popup, you as a planner can navigate to the detailed list, which is basically an issue list, to better understand and address the issues in hand. 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 852 On any of the main screens of the Process pMRP Simulations app, the Simulation Sum- mary button is available, and clicking it gives you an overview of all the changes made while you’ve been processing the simulation. The following change categories will be shown:  Changes in Demand  Changes in Capacity  Preproduction or Early Procurement  Changes in Source of Supply  Unresolved Issues  Disregarded Capacity Issues Figure 15.20 shows the Simulation Summary screen, which has tabs for the different change categories and the total number in each category. You can also export this table to a spreadsheet so that you can further discuss and validate the forecast changes. Figure 15.20 Simulation Summary

sections.title: Releasing Results to Operative Material Requirements Planning
sections.en: 15.4 Releasing Results to Operative Material Requirements Planning Once the simulation process is complete and you’ve identified and resolved all of the capacity issues, or if you as a capacity planner decide to disregard them or compromise on delivery performance, then you can release the pMRP simulation. You accomplish this by choosing the Release button on the initial screen of the Process pMRP Simula- tions app. This brings up a popup (see Figure 15.21) in which you can confirm whether the simulation can be released, and the forecast will be transferred to the top-level materials in an inactive version 01, even if you created the simulation with active PIRs as references. The option to transfer PIRs into an active version also exists in the same © 853 15.4 Releasing Results to Operative Material Requirements Planning popup (see Version Active: Yes). Also, as mentioned at the beginning of this chapter, in the latest version of SAP S/4HANA, DDMRP can also consider PIRs for the buffer calcu- lation. This means that the pMRP simulation results will be an input for DDMRP. When the simulation is released, if there are DDMRP materials in the BOM’s lower levels, then PIRs will be created for DDMRP materials, even if there was no PIR for those materials before the simulation creation. Therefore, the buffer calculation will take into consider- ation any demand variability that can be anticipated by the forecast. Furthermore, you can copy the inactive PIRs created by DDMRP to the active version in Transaction MD70. Alternatively, you can simply check the Active flag in Transaction MD62 to activate PIRs. When you release a simulation, the Show Log option (not shown) will provide a complete log of the PIRs that are transferred. If you need to, you can check the Subassembly Components box to bring up the option to transfer the sub- components of the main assembly in the chosen version (see Requirements Version* in Figure 15.21). Furthermore, the pMRP tool in SAP S/4HANA 2023 offers even more options for the system to consider materials that are managed via Kanban (discussed in Chapter 9) and even non-MRP materials. Figure 15.21 Releasing pMRP Simulation Figure 15.22 shows the updated status of the pMRP simulation that has been processed, and the Simulation Status denotes that it is Released. 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 854 Figure 15.22 Processed Simulation Released to Operative MRP You access the screen shown in Figure 15.23 via Transaction MD62. It shows that Mate- rial 2269 in Plant 1710 now has updated PIRs transferred through pMRP simulation. Because this PIR is now in operative planning, it’s ready for the MRP run. Figure 15.23 Updated Planned Independent Requirements Next, refer to Figure 15.21 again. If you checked the Capacity Changes Proposals box during pMRP release, then the system will be able to adopt the capacity changes you made during the simulation and also update the work center capacity using the Mass Maintenance of the Work Center Capacities app (see Figure 15.24). When you open this app, you can select the pMRP Change Proposal flag to see a list of all the changes pro- posed by pMRP simulations. When you select one of the lines, you’ll be able to see the details of the capacity change proposed by the pMRP simulation, including the month and the proposed capacity utiliztion. Or, you can simply reject the proposal if it’s not possible to implement it. Figure 15.24 Changes to pMRP in the Mass Maintenance of Work Center Capacities App © 855

sections.title: Using Flexible Constraints
sections.en: 15.5 Using Flexible Constraints 15.5 Using Flexible Constraints The pMRP functionality allows you as a planner to define flexible constraints for prod- ucts. For example, you can define that a maximum quantity of a certain material can be produced or purchased in any given time period (week or month). If this quantity is exceeded, pMRP will generate an alert when processing the pMRP simulation. You can also define flexible constraints for a single material or multiple materials, and you can used them for materials produced in house, externally procured materials, or stock transfer materials. The available periodicities are weeks and months. To define a flexible constraint, you use the Define Flexible Constraints app (see Figure 15.25 1). In this example, we use the same material, 2269, in plant 1710, and we have defined a constraint that whenever the production quantity is more than 75 pieces (PC) during the period from August 2024 to February 2025, then the system should consider this constraint while processing pMRP. When you’ve scheduled the pMRP and you pro- ceed to process it, the system will show the Violated Constraints (see Figure 15.25 2). Figure 15.25 Flexible Constraint 15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning 856 Since pMRP is an interactive tool, any changes made during processing will immediately be reflected. For example, in 2, if you reduce the quantity of Material 2269 below 75 PC, then the system will accordingly update (reduce) the status of the violated constraints. Clicking on the Violated Constraints field will bring up detailed information on all the constraints that have been violated.

sections.title: Summary
sections.en: 15.6 Summary The pMRP functionality is the latest innovation in SAP S/4HANA, and it offers simula- tion capabilities that can be used by production and capacity planners to make fore- casting from a work center capacity point of view both practical and achievable. With pMRP, a supply chain planner can change forecasted quantities and evaluate the results, test different sources of supply for different products, and consider the prepro- duction of certain critical products to avoid a capacity overload in a specific month. The pMRP functionality is still in the development phase, and more innovations are expected to be delivered in future releases of SAP S/4HANA. The next chapter covers capacity requirements planning, which includes capacity eval- uation and capacity leveling. © 857 Chapter 16 Capacity Requirements Planning Apart from materials availability, capacity availability plays a central role in the entire production process. Capacity requirements planning helps companies ensure that the relevant resources become available at the required time for optimum and uninterrupted production. Building on the concepts and understanding that you developed in Chapter 3 and Chapter 6 for capacity requirements planning, this chapter focuses on the short-term capacity planning after a production planner creates or releases production orders. Medium- to long-term capacity planning was covered by the chapters on sales and operations planning (S&OP in Chapter 11) and predictive material and resource plan- ning (pMRP in Chapter 15). The capacity requirements planning details for discrete manufacturing that we cover in this chapter are equally relevant and applicable to pro- cess manufacturing and, to a certain extent, repetitive manufacturing (REM). For example, when you decide to assign relevant profiles for capacity evaluation or capac- ity leveling, the system offers profiles with REM in the naming convention for REM and with PI in the naming convention for process manufacturing. For discrete manufactur- ing, the profiles have a naming convention that includes SFC (for shop floor control). Because the system uses the same principles that we cover in this chapter in plant maintenance and project systems, the available profiles have naming conventions that include PM and PS, respectively. We’ll start this chapter with an overview of the processes involved in capacity planning and then cover capacity requirements and capacity evaluations. We’ll cover both stan- dard and variable capacity evaluations. Next, we’ll cover how capacity requirements at different work centers can be combined and how to perform real-time checks on capac- ity availability, either during order creation or order release. Then, we’ll move on to cover dispatching and the associated profiles, the dispatching sequence, sequence-dependent setup, and midpoint scheduling. We’ll also cover how to use the mass processing function to expedite business processes, such as dispatch- ing and deallocation. Finally, we’ll cover some of the more important features that are available in the graphical and tabular versions of capacity planning tables. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 858 16.1 Process Overview The objective of capacity requirements planning is to schedule operations within the availability capacity and to check their feasibility when creating logical sequences of operations. You can implement capacity requirements planning for production orders and for planned orders from MRP. You can also make the relevant configuration settings to enable the system to consider planned orders in capacity planning. This option works best if your company’s business processes are mature enough and there’s significantly high confidence in the planning results that MRP creates. Because there are often fre- quent and at times high numbers of last-minute changes between planning and actual production and execution, it may not be wise for you to book capacities based only on planning results. If you do that, you may compromise your company’s capacity avail- ability, which in turn may have an impact on your company’s actual production sched- ule. At the same time, you may have a genuine business need to convert planned orders into production orders earlier so as to book capacities based on production orders. Still, others may prepare a comprehensive and feasible production plan based only on planned orders. Apart from scheduling operations, capacity requirements planning involves two important processes:  Capacity evaluation A capacity evaluation compares available capacities with capacity requirements, with reference to the work center or specific production order.  Capacity availability check A capacity availability check uses the same information to check whether the avail- able capacity at the requisite work center is sufficient to manage the operation of the production order. Depending on the configuration settings, the system can perform a capacity availability check during the creation of a production order or during its release. If there is insufficient capacity, three options are available: – The system won’t create or release the production order. – The system will create or release the production order. – The system will prompt the production planner to decide on creating or releasing the production order, despite the capacity shortage. Note Refer to Chapter 6, in which we covered available capacity. If there is insufficient capacity, or to account for capacity restrictions, the system per- forms finite scheduling to look for periods when sufficient capacity is available. Insuffi- cient capacity at the work center leads the system to rescheduling the operation, but © 859 16.1 Process Overview you as a production planner will still have to perform the dispatch function to give a formal go-ahead to proceed with production at the given work center. The dispatch function can take place either from the capacity planning table or from the tabular capacity planning table. If there is an incorrect or inadvertent dispatch, you can inter- actively deallocate the operation. Upon dispatch, the system performs the following four functions:  It determines the dispatch dates.  It creates the dispatching sequence.  It performs finite scheduling of the operation to be dispatched.  It performs midpoint scheduling of nondispatched operations of the production order. Upon dispatch of an operation, the system assigns it the status DISP (dispatched) and no longer schedules the operation automatically. Note Implementing capacity requirements planning makes greater sense in companies that have a diverse range of products and that also want to ensure that all of their products are produced in sufficient quantities for availability in the market. If a company pro- duces products that use up all of its available capacities or produces individual prod- ucts such as fertilizer, cement, caustic soda, or iron bars, then implementing capacity requirements planning may not add much value to its business processes. During SAP S/4HANA system implementation, you should discuss and evaluate how implementing capacity requirements planning will improve your business processes and then decide whether to implement capacity requirements planning or not. Capacity management consists of a sequenced system of repeatable tasks. Each of these tasks serves to balance resource availability with the capacity load it has to cope with. Capacity management can be broken down into the following phases (see Figure 16.1, in which the broad arrows denote the capacity management process):  Planning  Sequencing  Leveling  Scheduling  Evaluation Say that a production scheduler is tasked with scheduling a pool of orders within a cer- tain time frame. The scheduler must first put the orders into a sequence according to some sort of criteria (maybe heijunka or setup optimization) and then level them on the work centers or production line as available hours permit. For sequencing, many 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 860 options are preconfigured and available in the SAP S/4HANA system. Orders can be sorted according to setup optimization, first in, first out (FIFO), or heijunka, and there are many more options that can be customized. For any given period, the scheduler saves a sequence record, which is used in the next step for distribution within available capacity. Leveling uses the order’s load and the work center’s availability to figure out at what specific point in time the order will be placed. Figure 16.1 Capacity Management Two Invaluable Rules of Planning For production and capacity planners, following two specific rules in capacity planning will bring tremendous sanity to the production process:  There is a point in time at which planning activities must end.  Plan resources in the medium and long term and manage demand. Plan resources and sequence in the short term. Then, schedule and manage the supply of materi- als required for production. Now that you have a basic understanding of how capacity requirements planning works, let’s delve into the details you need to know to understand how capacity requirements planning works in the system, alongside the relevant business require- ments. 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation The system reflects the day-to-day business processes in place, including creation and release of production orders, confirming them, delays in production, and the extra time it takes to set up or process the product in capacity requirements. Capacity evaluation Planning Scheduling Execution Stock Delivery Pooling Leveling Demand Entry Sequencing © 861 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation enables you as a capacity planner to view the capacity load or overload at various work centers with a large number of standard and variable evaluation options. It’s essential for you to understand these two business processes—capacity requirements and capac- ity evaluation—before you get started with the actual capacity requirements planning process. We’ll discuss them in more detail in the following sections. We’ll cover how to understand the capacity requirements that the system creates, how to evaluate them, and the steps that you can take for capacity leveling. 16.2.1 Capacity Requirements As already mentioned, the system uses the settings that you already configured in Chapter 3 for capacity requirements planning and also the information that you entered in the PP master data, such as the work center and routing from Chapter 6. Here, we briefly cover these details. The system determines the capacity requirements during lead time scheduling if the Det. Cap. Reqmnts option in the control key has been set. Furthermore, the system takes into consideration the formulas that were entered in the work center, the default values in the routing, and the order quantity. It’s important to emphasize that the for- mulas for scheduling aren’t used in capacity requirements planning. For each compo- nent of an operation, such as setup, processing, and wait times, there is an earliest and latest date. The schedule formula is set to one of the operation dates. In the standard settings, this is the latest setup start date or processing start date, if the operation doesn’t provide for any setup time. An operation can span several time periods, and because the system bases the capacity evaluation on this information, the distribution key determines how the system distributes capacity to different periods. The distribution key itself consists of the distribution strategy and the distribution function. The distribution key defines the operation dates and the basis upon which it carries out the distribution—for example, the factory calendar, the Gregorian calendar, or the operation time. You can maintain the distribution key, distribution strategy, and distribution function through menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Require- ments Planning  Operations  Capacity Requirement  Distribution. The system determines the capacity requirement during lead time scheduling and has to go through the capacity availability check and finally the dispatching process. If the system confirms the operation during the capacity availability check, it assigns the capacity requirement with the system status BSTKZ. It does the same when an opera- tion is dispatched. In this way, the system converts the capacity requirement into a component called a basic load. The basic load has an important role to play in the capacity availability check because available capacity represents the difference between the available capacity and the basic load. The system reduces the capacity requirements when any of the following tasks are performed: 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 862  Confirming an operation  Setting the status of a production order to technically complete  Locking the production order  Setting the deletion flag in the production order The system also updates the capacity requirements when you partially confirm an operation, and it correspondingly reduces part of the capacity requirements for the operation. 16.2.2 Standard Evaluation of Capacity Utilization The system offers several methods of capacity evaluation. Each standard evaluation has its own transaction code, whereas for variable evaluation, a user selects the rele- vant profile to enable the system to display the requisite information. In standard evaluations, the system displays the capacity requirements in direct com- parison to the available capacities. The following are some of the standard capacity evaluations:  Standard overview (Transaction CM01) The system aggregates the load on a weekly basis.  Detailed capacity list (Transaction CM02) The system shows the load per order and week.  Pool of orders/operations (Transaction CM03) The system presents only the load of released operations.  Overload (Transaction CM05) The system only shows work centers with overload.  Extended selection, work center view (Transaction CM50) The system displays an aggregated view of the daily load.  Extended selection, order view (Transaction CM52) The system displays an aggregated view of the weekly load for all work centers that the selected orders occupy. To access the capacity planning evaluation, you follow menu path Logistics  Produc- tion  Capacity Planning  Evaluation  Work Center View  Load or use Transaction CM01. Figure 16.2 shows the standard overview (load) of the Work center ASSEMBLY and Plant 1710 combination. It shows the capacity requirements for each calendar week that result from the creation or release of production orders. The initial screen also shows the available capacity (AvailCap.) of the work center and the resulting percentage of load (CapLoad), as well as the remaining available capacity (RemAvailCap). For example, in calendar week 28/2024, a capacity requirement of 166.23 hours leads to a utilization (or an overload) of 415.6% based on an available capacity of 40.00 hours. The system displays the periods with an overload in red. © 863 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation Figure 16.2 Capacity Evaluation of Work Center Warning The utilization rate on the production lines should never exceed 100% for an extended period of time. It shouldn’t even get close to that because the closer it gets to full utili- zation (100%), the less a capacity planner can cope with variability in the process. It’s a fact that when capacity utilization approaches 100%, variability multiplies. However, the urge to let it approach 100% comes from the standard model of finance or costing departments that asks for maximum utilization of resources because the business should make as many products or units as possible, with the available capacity used to spread the overhead costs thinner over more units and make the individual product cheaper. If you use Transaction CM02, the screen shown in Figure 16.3 appears. It shows the eval- uation list of the capacity requirement of each operation, including the production order number, the header material, and the order quantity. The Choose fields button lets you display additional fields of the operation, such as its start time. You can also directly jump into the production order from here. On the Select fields screen shown in Figure 16.4, the system lists already-displayed fields on the left-hand side. You can also choose from a large number of fields available on the right-hand side. Simply select the fields that need to appear in the standard 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 864 capacity evaluation and choose the Display button (with the back arrow <) to move them from the right-hand side to the left-hand side. Then, choose Continue. Figure 16.3 Detailed Capacity Evaluation for Week Figure 16.4 Fields for Selection in Capacity Evaluation © 865 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation Because a user can select many fields, we recommend that you use the filter options to narrow down the available choices. For example, if you use the Quantities filter that’s available in the dropdown list on the right-hand side of the screen, the system will bring up the quantities fields to choose from, and it will eventually display them in the standard capacity evaluation. 16.2.3 Variable Evaluation of Capacity Utilization In variable evaluations, you as a capacity planner can base the capacity evaluation on individual requirements. In this process, the system requires an overall profile that defines the evaluation. Standard profiles are available for this purpose, or you can con- figure profiles to meet specific business needs. For a variable evaluation of capacity utilization, you follow SAP menu path Logistics  Production  Capacity Planning  Evaluation  Variable or use Transaction CM07. The Planning screen in Figure 16.5 shows the variable evaluation using overall profile SAPS- FC010. In contrast to the standard overview (in Transaction CM01 or overall profile SAPX912), this profile displays only the requirements of the basic load. Figure 16.5 Selection of Variable Evaluation, Overall Profile SAPSFC010 Here, you select Overall Prof SAPSFC010. On the ensuing screen, you enter Work Center “ASSEMBLY” and Plant “1710,” and then, you press (Enter) or choose Variable Overview. The Variable Overview screen that appears (see Figure 16.6) is similar to the standard evaluation, but it displays only those requirements that affect the basic load. The sys- tem shows the capacity requirements that have been dispatched. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 866 You can define variable evaluation by using profiles, which we’ll discuss next. Figure 16.6 Variable Evaluation Using Overall Profile SAPSFC010 Overall Profile The overall profile consists of four profiles: the selection profile, option profile, list pro- file, and graphic profile. These profiles, in turn, contain additional profiles. Figure 16.7 provides an overview of the profile structure. We’ll discuss the first two profiles here; the remaining two profiles (list profile and graphic profile) contain additional settings to display a standard evaluation. Figure 16.7 Profiles for Variable Evaluation Overall Profile Capacity Evaluation Selection Profile Option Profile Graphic Profile List Profile Distribution Initial Set Work Center Set Order Set Capacity Category Set © 867 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation To create or make changes to an overall profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Pro- duction  Capacity Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Profiles  Define Overall Profiles or use Transaction OPA6. Figure 16.8 shows the assignment for Overall prof. SAPS- FC010. Figure 16.8 Overall Profile for Variable Evaluation Selection Profile The selection profile determines which capacity requirements the system should read. To create or make changes to a selection profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Pro- duction  Production  Capacity Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Profiles  Define Selection Profiles or use Transaction OPA2. Figure 16.9 shows Sel. profile SAPSFCA010, which it assigns to overall profile SAPSFC010. Figure 16.9 Selection Profile for Variable Evaluation 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 868 The objects for which the system reads the capacity requirements are determined on the basis of the set combinations for the work center, capacity category, and order. These can also be determined on the basis of the order categories—that is, whether it’s a production/work order or a planned order. The initial set determines which restric- tions the system makes during the call. This information is output on the Selection Pro- file screen just shown in Figure 16.9. You can create or change the set combinations by using menu path SAP IMG  Produc- tion  Capacity Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Selection Set  Define Sets, Trans- action CMS1 (create), or Transaction CMS2 (change). The sets often use input variables that you can create or change by using menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Selection Set  Define Variables, Transaction CMV1 (create), or Transaction CMV2 (change). Option Profile The option profile contains items to include in backlogs, to cumulate the capacity requirements and the available capacity (Section 16.2.4), and to distribute the capacity requirements. Figure 16.10 shows Option profile SAPB020, which is also used for stan- dard evaluation. Figure 16.10 Option Profile for Variable Evaluation © 869 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation To create or make changes to an option profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Pro- duction  Capacity Requirements Planning  Evaluation  Profiles  Define Options Pro- files or use Transaction OPA3. You can set up the list profile by using Transaction OPA4 and the graphic profile by using Transaction OPA5. Both profiles contain additional settings to display the stan- dard evaluation. 16.2.4 Cumulating the Capacity Requirements For evaluation purposes, especially when large numbers of orders at various work cen- ters are taken into consideration, it’s often helpful to cumulate the capacity require- ments. This provides a broader overview of the capacity situation at each work center. Capacities can be cumulated either statically or dynamically from work center hierar- chies. When statically cumulating the capacity, the system only refers to the work cen- ter and its available capacity. To carry out capacity evaluation in cumulated form—that is, to cumulate both available capacity and capacity requirements—capacity require- ments planning must be cumulated dynamically. In the Hierarchy area shown previously in Figure 16.10, options to cumulate capacities dynamically and the Hierarchy work center for the plant are available. 16.2.5 Checking Capacity Availability When a production order is created, the system can check capacity availability in the order before you dispatch it. This step helps a production planner to decide whether it’s possible to dispatch the order or not. The system considers period-based information by checking it to find out whether the available capacity in the period of operation is greater than or equal to the capacity requirement of the operation. The length of the period is set in the period profile, which we’ll cover later in this chapter. The shortest possible period is one day. When the system performs a period-based check, it only indicates that the capacity is available (or unavailable) within the given period, not on the planned operation date. The available capacity represents the difference between the permitted capacity over- load defined in the work center and the basic load. The basic load doesn’t contain all of the capacity requirements. By default, only dispatched operations and those for which the capacity has been confirmed affect the basic load. You can display the basic load by using the variable evaluation (Transaction CM07) and profile SAPSFC010. Tips and Tricks If no capacity availability check has been carried out over a long period of time, or if it’s being used for the first time, then we recommend that you create the basic load on the 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 870 basis of the existing (unchecked) capacity requirements by using Transaction CM99. In this case, all capacity requirements will be confirmed. Often, due to delays in actual production, the system creates a backlog (e.g., noncon- firmed operations from the past). Check the Overall Capacity Load box in the strategy profile and the backlog dispatching date (BacklogDispDate 0; refer to Figure 16.10) to define whether to include them in the determination of the available capacity. We cover strategy profiles in Section 16.4.2. If these are included, the capacity requirements of the past will be temporarily scheduled forward into the current period and, if necessary, into subsequent periods and added to the basic load. An operation can last for several periods. In this case, the distribution key determines the way in which the system distributes the capacity requirement to different periods. The capacity check uses the distribution key that is assigned to the evaluation profile (Section 16.4.2). The capacity availability check is carried out for each individual order, and all operations are checked one after the other, according to their numerical order. A restriction on this capability is that only those operations are checked that exist in work centers that are relevant to finite scheduling. Therefore, it makes sense to define critical work centers (work centers with bottleneck capacities) as finite to ensure that there are enough degrees of freedom in the capacity planning. You can check capacity availability interactively while creating a production order or releasing it. Alternatively, you can perform an interactive capacity availability check from the production order, as shown in Figure 16.11. Figure 16.11 Interactive Capacity Availability Check © 871 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation The Check Capacity Availability popup appears when you choose the Capacity button in the production order header, and the system will check for capacity during produc- tion order creation in the turbine manufacturing process. In the example in Figure 16.11, there is at least one operation in which the work center doesn’t have sufficient capacity. More detailed information is available, as well as the option to use finite scheduling to shift the operations into periods with sufficient capacity. Choose the Detailed Info button to see the detailed information for this capac- ity availability check, which is provided in the Capacities with Overload screen that appears (see Figure 16.12). Figure 16.12 Detailed Information on Available Capacities, Including Overload Here, the capacity problem exists in the first Operation at Work center ASSEMBLY in Period 28/2024 because the requirement of 135.76 hours far exceeds the available capacity of 40.00 hours. Because this situation leads to a load of 339.4%, the system can’t confirm the operation. The system determines the load threshold on the basis of the permitted overload spec- ified in the capacity master data, and it can exceed 100%. However, you as a planner can choose to confirm the capacity requirement interactively by checking the Capacity confirmed box (on the left-hand side of the screen) and then choosing Adopt. Let’s now discuss category-specific check control and the various capacity profiles available in the system. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 872 Category-Specific Check Control In Chapter 3, we covered the fact that the check control settings that are specific to the plant and order category determine whether the system carries out the capacity avail- ability check at the creation and/or the release of the production order (using Transac- tion OPJK). We briefly cover it again here. The availability checks during order creation and release represent different availabil- ity operations (business function 1 for order creation and business function 2 for order release), which is why different sets of parameters exist for the creation and release functions. In addition to deciding whether the system carries out the capacity availabil- ity check, you need to assign the overall profile for the capacity availability check. If any overall profile isn’t assigned at this stage, then the system uses standard profile SAPSF- CG013. Furthermore, you can define whether production order creation or release is allowed with unconfirmed capacity requirements. The screen shown in Figure 16.13 displays the check control at order creation (using Transaction OPJK) in the Capacity availability area of the screen. If you have no busi- ness need to perform any availability check during order creation, then check the No check box. Figure 16.13 Check Control for Capacity Availability Check © 873 16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation If the system is unable to confirm the capacity requirement of an operation, then you make the following settings in the production scheduling profile (using Transaction OPKP) and assign them in the Work Scheduling view to the material master:  Define whether the confirmed capacity requirements are transferred to the basic load or whether all capacity requirements are discarded.  Define whether finite scheduling should be carried out. Figure 16.14 displays the cor- responding fields in production scheduling profile (Prod. Sched. Profile) YB0001 for Plant 1710. These settings are only relevant to collective processing. Here, you set the Overall Profile for capacity leveling and define the parameters for the Availability Check. Figure 16.14 Production Scheduling Profile Profiles Note that the system doesn’t use the overall profile for capacity leveling for the capacity availability check but instead uses it for dispatching. The overall profile for the capacity availability check has the same structure as the overall profile for dispatching. In con- trast to dispatching, however, the capacity availability check doesn’t use the overall pro- file from the production scheduler. Instead, it uses the overall profile from the capacity check control; if there is no profile available there, it uses overall profile SAPSFCG013. Figure 16.15 provides an overview of the profiles involved in the capacity availability check. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 874 Figure 16.15 Profiles for Capacity Availability Check Because we cover individual profiles in greater detail in Section 16.4.2, which deals with dispatching, we’ll only briefly explain each profile here:  Selection profile This defines the selection of objects for the capacity availability check. These include the work centers of the selected operations and the capacity requirements of the basic load.  Control profile This defines the preparation of the data.  Evaluation profile This assigns the distribution key for distributing the capacity requirements, among other parameters.  Strategy profile This contains the sequence and search direction for finite scheduling, among other parameters.  Period profile This defines the length of the period for the check.  Time profile This defines the time fences for the capacity requirements in question.  List profile This contains settings for the results output. The result of the capacity availability check is the confirmation of the capacity require- ments, if the available capacity is sufficient. If the available capacity within the period is insufficient, then the finite scheduling process can be triggered to find a period with Check Control Overall Profile Capacity Availability Check Selection Profile Object Set Filter Set Requirements Grouping Selection Key Distribution Profile Dispatching Sequence Control Profile Evaluation Profile Strategy Profile Period Profile Time Profile List Profile © 875 16.3 Finite Scheduling a sufficient capacity and to confirm the capacity requirement in that new available period. The system doesn’t automatically dispatch the operation by either the capacity availability check or by finite scheduling. A capacity planner will have to manually dis- patch the operation, either individually or in the mass processing step. We’ll cover these options later in Section 16.4.1 and Section 16.4.6, respectively. 16.3 Finite Scheduling If the available capacity is insufficient to meet the capacity requirement of an opera- tion on the requested date, you can use the finite scheduling option to find the next possible date on which the available capacity is sufficient for the operation. You can perform finite scheduling during either the capacity availability check or dispatching. While performing finite scheduling, the system refers to the strategy profile to search for the direction in which to perform the scheduling (backward or forward scheduling). A search in the direction of today’s date is based on backward scheduling and rep- resents the standard case, whereas a search into the future is based on forward sched- uling. However, much of this also depends on the company’s business processes and needs, in which only forward scheduling is considered to look for free or available capacity in the future. If insufficient capacity is found in one direction, then the search direction can be reversed. The screen shown in Figure 16.16 appears during the capacity availability check. In the next capacity overload popup, you choose the Finite scheduling icon (not shown). Figure 16.16 Finite Scheduling Results (after Capacity Availability Check) Then, the system will propose the new latest date (NewLtStart) and time (NewStTim) after it performs finite scheduling. At this stage, you as a capacity or production planner can confirm the capacity by checking the Cap.confirm. box and then choosing Adopt. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 876 When an operation (shown in the lower half of Figure 16.17) is dispatched at the work center (shown in the upper half), the system automatically performs finite scheduling and assigns the operation immediately next to the already assigned operation. Figure 16.17 Dispatching Orders in Capacity Planning Table 16.4 Dispatching In the dispatching process, the system creates a workable or feasible production plan in which sufficient capacity is available. For this process to be successful, there needs to be a sequence of dates set up for the individual operations in the work centers. The non- dispatched operations form the pool of orders and operations for the capacity planner/ production scheduler to work on. To dispatch an operation, the capacity planner selects an operation that is ready for dispatch from the capacity planning table or from the tabular capacity planning table. They then initiate the dispatching function. In this section, we’ll go over the process steps for dispatching and then get into the details of the different profiles, sequencing, and setup. 16.4.1 Process Steps When dispatching takes place, the system carries out the following steps:  Determine dispatching dates The standard setting uses the operation dates as dispatching dates. However, a plan- ner can also enter dates interactively or define the dispatching date using drag and drop in the capacity planning table. © 877 16.4 Dispatching  Create dispatching sequence If a planner wants to dispatch several operations in one step, then they create a dis- patching sequence based on specific sorting criteria (Section 16.4.3) or optimal setup times. The default settings stipulate dispatch at the latest date.  Finite scheduling if no capacity is available If no capacity exists on the dispatching date, then the system needs to run finite scheduling to come up with a feasible dispatching date.  Dispatch operation The system dispatches the operation and assigns it the DISP status (if successful).  Midpoint scheduling A planner needs to adjust the dates of the nondispatched operations in such a way that the system carries out the midpoint scheduling process for them. In midpoint scheduling, the system schedules the nondispatched operations so that earlier ones are scheduled backward and later ones forward. Midpoint scheduling is covered later in Section 16.4.5. Note It may be impractical from a particular practice or business point of view, but you as a planner can also base the dispatching processing on an infinite capacity assumption. To do this, you deselect the finite scheduling option in the strategy profile. However, with bottleneck capacities at critical levels, work centers hardly ever permit this option. During dispatching, you as a planner may realize that it’s not possible to meet the basic dates of the order. Because the system carries out dispatching for each operation, you can also compromise on the sequence of operations for the dispatch. For this, you use the strategy profile to define the extent to which the system allows inconsistencies within the operations of an order. However, the capacity planner will need to monitor the external dependencies, such as the date-based dependency between orders and customer requirements, to ensure timely production. Whenever a planner performs the dispatch or the deallocate function, the system updates the planning and the scheduling logs. In fact, it issues a message to inform the capacity planner to check the planning and/or scheduling logs. The planning log can be called from the respective application—for instance, from the capacity planning table or from finite scheduling during the interactive capacity availability check. Figure 16.18 displays a sample planning log that includes two information messages and one warn- ing message. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 878 Figure 16.18 Planning Log Summary after Finite Scheduling Double-click the information or warning messages and a log that displays information messages will appear. For instance, it may say that the system successfully dispatched operation 0010. It provides the dispatching date and time as well (in the second infor- mation message), as shown in Figure 16.19. Figure 16.19 Detailed Scheduling Message Log after Finite Scheduling 16.4.2 Profiles for Dispatching Capacity leveling uses a large number of profiles, which are primarily interconnected, to perform capacity leveling and differentiate between different tasks. In this section, we briefly cover some of the most-used profiles and the associated settings in each. Later in this chapter, when we cover the business processes of capacity leveling, we’ll cover more details of the associated profiles and explain their roles where necessary. Building on the same principles as for profiles in capacity evaluation, the system cumu- lates profiles for capacity leveling in an overall profile. Figure 16.20 illustrates the rela- tionships of various profiles with each other. We explain some of the more important profiles in the following sections. © 879 16.4 Dispatching Figure 16.20 Finite Scheduling Results (Triggered by Dispatching) Overall Profile To create or make changes to an overall profile in capacity leveling, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Requirements Planning  Capacity Leveling and Extended Evaluation  Define Overall Profile or use Transaction OPD0. The screen shown in Figure 16.21 displays the standard overall profile SAPSFCG011 and various subprofiles. Figure 16.21 Overall Profile for Dispatching Production Scheduler Overall Profile Capacity Leveling Selection Profile Object Set Filter Set Requirements Grouping Selection Key Distribution Profile Dispatching Sequence Control Profile Evaluation Profile Strategy Profile Time Profile Profile for Capacity Planning Table Tabular Planning Tabular Profile List Profile Production Scheduling Profile 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 880 There’s no need to maintain the Period profile field for the overall profile because it’s relevant for a capacity availability check only. The following sections describe some of the individual profiles shown on this screen. Strategy Profile It’s very important to comprehensively maintain the strategy profile because the sys- tem refers to it during dispatching and rescheduling functions. To create or make changes to the strategy profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Requirements Planning  Capacity Leveling and Extended Evaluation  Strategy  Define Strategy Profile or use Transaction OPDB. Figure 16.22 displays strategy profile SAPSFCT001, including its numerous parameters. The important parameters of the strategy profile control the functions for dispatching, scheduling, and creating a sequence of operations. Figure 16.22 Strategy Profile © 881 16.4 Dispatching The Scheduling control parameters shown at the top of the screen allow you to make the following settings:  Set whether to carry out finite scheduling during dispatching. If the Finite schedul- ing parameter isn’t set, the dispatch process assumes that unlimited capacity is available. If there is insufficient available capacity, then the dispatching attempt becomes unsuccessful.  When the Dispatch at earliest point in time parameter is used, the system doesn’t consider interdependencies with other operations and dispatches the operation as early as possible.  The Dispatch at best time for setup parameter chooses the dispatch date on the basis of the existing setup sequence in such a way that the increase in the overall setup time is kept as small as possible. This entails the system having the provision to shift the already dispatched operations. To attend to this need, you also set the Insert operation parameter. The system supports this function in the capacity planning table, and it makes sense only if setup matrices for sequence-based setup are in place. We explain the setup matrices later in Section 16.4.4.  The Date entry when dispatching parameter ensures that instead of the system using the operation date during dispatching, it will bring up a popup window and prompt the user for an interactive date entry.  The default setting for the planning direction is backward from the dispatch date. However, this setting can be changed when the Plan. direction forwards parameter is activated. When the system is unable to find available capacity within the plan- ning horizon, and when the Change planning direction parameter has also been set, the system will continue to search for available capacity in the opposite direction.  The Planning in non-work periods parameter overrides the nonwork periods in the work center and suggests 24-hour capacity availability with a full rate of capacity uti- lization in the dispatch process.  The Close gaps parameter enables the system to automatically close gaps that are caused by deallocations or rescheduling. This again entails rescheduling of already dispatched operations and is only possible on the capacity planning table. The parameters in the Dispatching functions area at the bottom of the screen define which options are available for dispatching:  Sort operations to be dispatched determines how to create the right sequence of operations if a planner wants the system to dispatch several operations simultane- ously.  The Consider operation sequence in the order parameter enables the system to adhere to the sequence of operations in the production order. When this parameter is used with forward scheduling, the system doesn’t dispatch the operation prior to its predecessor. Similarly, with backward scheduling, the system makes sure that it doesn’t dispatch the operation after its successor. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 882  If the Operation date check is used, a planner can check whether the new operation dates lie within the floats that can be selected using the following parameters: – Use operation floats – Use float bef. prod. (use before production) – Use float aft. prod. (use after production)  If you as a planner want the system to immediately perform the midpoint schedul- ing process, then you select the Midpoint scheduling parameter. When you select this parameter, depending on the operations, the system immediately carries out the midpoint planning function; otherwise, it carries it out when you save the plan- ning results.  Setup time optimization creates the dispatching sequence according to the setup time minimization criterion.  If the Cancel dispatching due to error parameter is set, the system doesn’t allow dis- patch of operations outside the floats. Selection Profile The selection profile enables you as a planner to define which capacity requirements the system should consider for dispatch. To create or make changes to the selection profile, you follow configuration menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Require- ments Planning  Capacity Leveling and Extended Evaluation  Define Selection Profile or use Transaction OPD1 (see Figure 16.23). Figure 16.23 Selection Profile for Dispatching © 883 16.4 Dispatching Control Profile The control profile defines how the system presents the data. It can be presented con- tinuously over time for the capacity planning table, or it can be based on periods for the tabular capacity planning table. An important parameter of the control profile defines whether a planner can modify the operations and at which stage the system should set the locks to avoid any further changes to the operations. To create or make changes to the control profile, you follow the configuration menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Requirements Planning  Capacity Leveling and Extended Evaluation  Define Control Profile or use Transaction OPDE (see Figure 16.24). Figure 16.24 Control Profile for Dispatching Evaluation Profile The evaluation profile enables a planner to maintain the distribution keys for the capac- ity requirements, the units of measure, and the details to cumulate the capacity require- ments. To create or make changes to the evaluation profile, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Requirements Planning  Capacity Leveling and Extended Evaluation  Strategy  Define Evaluation Profile or use Transaction OPD3. Time Profile The time profile contains the time horizons for importing data records, as well as for evaluation and planning. The planning horizon must be shorter than or equal to the evaluation period, which in turn must be shorter than or equal to the database read 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 884 period. Another entry concerns the date for dispatching the backlog to which the sys- tem dispatches all backlog capacity requirements—provided it considers the backlog. To create or make changes to the time profile, you follow menu path, SAP IMG  Produc- tion  Capacity Requirements Planning  Capacity Leveling and Extended Evaluation  Define Time Profile or use Transaction OPD2. Figure 16.25 displays the details of time profile SAPSFCZ002. Figure 16.25 Time Profile for Dispatching 16.4.3 Dispatching Sequence In general, a planner will carry out the dispatching function manually, individually, and interactively. But when there’s a large and drastic change in the production plan, which also greatly impacts the operations involved, it makes sense to dispatch several operations according to specific rules. The easiest and most straightforward rule that most companies follow is to perform dispatching according to the start dates and times of operations. Similar rules can be implemented by using the sorting function, whereas the layout key enables a planner to implement sorting from a large number of available fields. You can set the layout key by using Transaction CY39 in Customizing (see Figure 16.26). On this screen, you select the fields that you as a planner want to sort and then eventu- ally make available in dispatching. After selecting the fields, choose the Sequence/ Heading button. The field details allow you to manage the sorting in ascending or descending order. Then, the layout key is assigned to the strategy profile. © 885 16.4 Dispatching Figure 16.26 Layout Key for Sequence Creation during Dispatching Tips and Tricks In addition to the layout key, you can use user exit CYPP0001 to sort the capacity requirements. We recommend that you engage an ABAP resource to define the param- eters in the user exit. The sequence in which the operations are dispatched may not necessarily match the sequence in the work center, as certain conditions of the dispatching process can trig- ger the system to dispatch some operations differently. For example, if a long-duration operation doesn’t fit into any gap that has been created on the basis of operations already dispatched, the system will dispatch that long-duration operation further in the planning horizon. 16.4.4 Sequence-Dependent Setup In some production processes, the setup time depends on the sequence in which the product is produced or on the physical state of the machine left behind after an opera- tion. For example, if an automotive company has a paint shop and it paints doors and hoods in a light color such as gray or white first and then a dark color such as dark blue or black afterwards, this might not be that critical because the setup time for dark 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 886 might be of standard length. But if the dark color is painted first and the light color after- ward, the setup time for gray might be much higher because workers may need to clean the paint shop machines very thoroughly to get rid of the dark color. You can configure the dependency of setup work on a processing sequence by assign- ing the setup groups in respective operations of the routing. The assignment takes place at two levels: the setup group category and the setup group key. A setup group category can contain several setup group keys; for example, a setup group category can be color, and the setup group keys can be black, white, and yellow. The fact that the assign- ment occurs at two different levels has the advantage of enabling both detailed main- tenance between setup group keys and less detailed maintenance between setup group categories. Setup Group Categories You can configure group categories and setup group keys by using Transaction OP43. Figure 16.27 shows the list of setup groups that are available for Plant 1710. Figure 16.27 Setup Group Category Select the Setup group category folder and then double-click the Setup group key folder on the left-hand side of the screen. Finally, in the operations details of the routing (in Transaction CA02), you assign the setup group categories and keys (see Figure 16.28). You can maintain the setup time between the setup group categories and the setup group key in a setup matrix via menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Require- ments Planning  Master Data  Routing Data  Define Setup Matrix or by using Transac- tion OPDA. The setup matrix is unique for each plant. © 887 16.4 Dispatching Figure 16.28 Assignment of Setup Group Category and Setup Group Key in Routing Figure 16.29 illustrates an example in which the setup time from a previous operation (with setup group category 1 and setup group key 10) to a subsequent operation (with setup group category 1 and setup group key 20) is 100 minutes. Figure 16.29 Setup Matrix However, the setup time in the opposite direction—that is, from setup group category 1 and setup group key 20 to setup group category 1 and setup group key 10—amounts 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 888 to 25 minutes. Maintaining a setup matrix enables you to assign individual operations in the routing, saving you time and effort in master data maintenance. The last column (not shown) shows the standard value (activity type) that the system can use for setup time. Examples of activities in the standard value key are setup time, machine time, and labor time. Note that we covered the standard value key in Chapter 3. Setup Time When a planner is using the capacity planning table, the system offers the following planning functions for planning with sequence-dependent setup times:  Interactive change of default values  Automatic adjustment of default values  Dispatch at best time for setup  Setup time optimization In the capacity planning table, you as a planner can also make automatic or manual changes to the setup time by following menu path Functions  Adjust Setup Time  Man- ually. In the screen that appears, make changes to the setup time in the Setup field. The setup time can also be increased or decreased. When making this setup time adjustment, the system carries out a new scheduling process. If an operation was already dispatched, the system deallocates it when the setup adjustment takes place. With automatic setup time adjustment, the system calculates the setup times of oper- ations on the basis of their sequence and reschedules them accordingly. To carry out the automatic setup function, you first set the Plan. Direction Forward and Insert Ope- ration parameters in the strategy profile. To close gaps that occur when the system reduces the setup times, you also set the Close Gaps parameter in the strategy profile. When you’re using the Dispatch at Best Time for Setup function, the system doesn’t try to dispatch the operation at the predefined dispatch date. It will do this at a point when the overall setup time is minimal for all operations increases. To use this function, you set the Dispatch at Best Time for Setup and Insert Operation parameters in the strategy profile and make sure that the parameter for the Setup Time Optimization dispatch function is not set. To enable itself to propose an optimum time for setup, the system compares the dis- patch times of operations with each other. If several optimal setup times exist, the sys- tem uses the latest of the optimal setup times. During dispatch, the system doesn’t adjust the setup time of operations according to the sequence of operations. Instead, it requires an automatic setup time adjustment in the next step. To dispatch several operations at the same time, the system uses the dispatch sequence defined in the strategy profile. Setup time optimization establishes an optimal setup sequence for a group of ope- rations. The system dispatches the sequence in the work center without gaps and also © 889 16.4 Dispatching automatically adjusts the setup times. The setup time optimization function is useful for redesigning the planning because it ensures that the sequence of operations is opti- mal even after the dispatch. But if the dispatch period contains operations that have already been dispatched, the system deviates the new sequences from the previously determined optimal sequence. For setup time optimization, you select the Setup Time Optimization and the Plan. Direction Forward parameters in the strategy profile. 16.4.5 Midpoint Scheduling Midpoint scheduling behaves in the same way as lead time scheduling, except that the basis of scheduling isn’t the order start or finish date. Instead, it starts with the date of the dispatched operation of the order and carries out a backward or forward scheduling process from the operation, according to the planning direction that you’ve defined in the strategy profile. The system considers the other dispatched operations of the order as fixed, and the dispatch occurs for each individual operation. During the dispatch of an operation, the dates of the operation can change. In such cases, you can use mid- point scheduling to adjust the dates of nondispatched operations to the changed dates of a dispatched operation. For example, if the dispatch of only the second of three operations took place, then midpoint scheduling can adjust the date of the nondispatched operations, which are the first and third ones. See Figure 16.30 for midpoint scheduling of three operations of an order. Figure 16.30 Midpoint Scheduling 16.4.6 Mass Processing Often, if there are several operations or orders for which you want to perform a mass function, such as dispatch or deallocation, it’s best to use the mass processing function- ality that’s available in capacity planning. To maintain the mass processing settings, 10 20 30 10 20 30 Old scheduled dates Operation shift Forward and backward scheduling 20 TS TE ES EE TS TE 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 890 you follow SAP menu path Logistics  Production  Capacity Planning  Leveling  Varia- ble  Background  Execute or use Transaction CM40. On the initial screen that appears, you can assign, for example, Overall profile SAPSF- CG011 for capacity leveling. Enter the Job name as “PP10” and choose Save Parameters in the Call Up Function area of the screen. Next, click Save or press (Ctrl)+(S). When you save the parameters for the first time, the system prompts you enter work center parameters, in which you can maintain text related to the Work Center, Plant, Capacity Category, and Capacity Planner Grp fields. Then, the next time you use Transaction CM40, you can use one of the three available actions—such as Dispatch, Deallocate, or User Exit (i.e., user exit CY190001)—in the Action area of the screen. In the Call Up Function area of the screen, you can define whether to dispatch the job at a later stage (Save Parameters), dispatch it directly, or execute it directly. Use Transaction CM41 to display the logs of the dispatch jobs. 16.5 Capacity Planning Table Two types of capacity planning tables are available in the system: graphical and tabular. Our focus will primarily remain on the graphical capacity planning table, but all of the concepts and functions available in the graphical capacity planning table are also avail- able in the tabular capacity planning table. The highly interactive and intuitive nature of the graphical capacity planning table enables a capacity planner to perform several activities and functions:  Make changes to an order.  Make changes to an operation.  Make changes to capacity.  Dispatch operations.  Deallocate operations. While we’ll discuss the final two functions in the preceding list in individual sections, there are relevant options available on the graphical capacity planning table for all the remaining change functions. Some of these change functions that we’ll discuss in the following sections are making real-time changes to production or process orders, mak- ing changes to operations, and making changes to available capacities. 16.5.1 Changing Orders, Operations, or Capacity To provide an example of the change functions, let’s say you as a planner want to make changes to a specific order. First, you choose Change Order, and the system will navi- gate you to the production order change screen (Transaction CO02), on which you can © 891 16.5 Capacity Planning Table make the desired changes. Options to change operations and capacity details will also be available. You can also make changes in the strategy profile in the capacity planning table to per- form the desired functions and achieve the requisite planning results. To access the capacity planning table, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  Capacity Planning  Leveling  Work Center View  Planning Table (Graphical) or use Transaction CM21. Figure 16.31 shows the capacity planning table that appears after you enter the initial parameters, such as the Work Center and Plant. Then, choose Continue. Figure 16.31 Capacity Planning Table Tips and Tricks You can also use transaction CM25, which offers even more options for capacity leveling. If the system is able to find capacity requirements in the given period for work centers or orders, it displays the capacity information. The upper-left pane displays the work centers, and the upper-right pane displays the dispatched operations. The lower-left pane displays the pool of operations and the corresponding production orders, and the lower-right pane displays nondispatched operations.
