You can define the sort profile of the pane to enable the system to sort from the pool of orders and operations. The system uses the order start date in the standard setting for sorting. However, the sort sequence gets disturbed when a planner deallocates the operations because the system adds new objects to the end of the list. You can choose Edit  Sort New to reestablish the previous sort sequence. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 892 You can modify the time-based scaling of the planning table by choosing Settings  Scale or by pressing the (Ctrl) key and simultaneously dragging a section of the screen. The search function helps you navigate through the planning table, especially when you’re trying to find relevant information and there is a large amount of data or infor- mation available. You can call up the search window via Edit  Search or by pressing the key combination (Ctrl)+(F). The system facilitates navigation in the capacity planning table by highlighting objects that belong together. Let’s say you as a planner want the system to highlight the orders that belong to the same material (in this example, 2258). To do this, you choose Edit  Highlight  Objects That Belong Together (see Figure 16.32). The lower-right pane will then show all the objects that belong together in dark green. Figure 16.32 Highlighting All Objects that Belong Together You can also highlight the objects that belong together based on other criteria, such as order start date, target quantity, or planner group. To do this, choose Edit  Highlight  Selection. On the resulting screen, check the selection field to group and enter the field value that matches the purpose. Choose Continue and the groupings will again appear in dark green. 16.5.2 Dispatch Operations On the capacity planning table, there are two ways to dispatch operations. You can call the dispatch function by clicking Dispatch from the menu bar, or you can drag and drop the operation using the mouse. In both cases, you first select the operations that need to be dispatched from the pool of orders/operations. The screen shown in Figure 16.33 dis- plays the dispatch using the dispatch function in the menu bar. In the lower-right pane, © 893 16.5 Capacity Planning Table you select the operation to dispatch, and then, the horizon bar turns to light blue. Then, you choose Dispatch from the top menu bar and the system moves it to the upper-right pane (see Figure 16.34). Figure 16.33 Dispatching in Capacity Planning Table Figure 16.34 shows the production order (in light blue), which has just been dispatched from the orders (pool) and is scheduled immediately after the operation for the already dispatched production order. Figure 16.34 Dispatched Operation The dispatch date is the scheduled operation date, which by default is the latest start date. If capacity is available on that date, the system dispatches the operation on the 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 894 dispatch date. If capacity is unavailable on the dispatch date, finite scheduling deter- mines the next possible date on which capacity is available, and the system then accordingly dispatches the operation on that date. If you’re using the drag-and-drop method for dispatching the operation, the “drop” date represents the dispatch date. 16.5.3 Deallocation The deallocation process is similar to the dispatching process. You can deallocate an operation either through Deallocate in the menu bar (which is located right next to the Dispatch option on the screen shown in Figure 16.33) or by using drag and drop in the pool of orders/operations pane. 16.5.4 Options in the Graphical Planning Table In the sequence-dependent setup, we’ve already covered the fact that the system requires specific settings in the strategy profile to enable it to plan effectively on the capacity planning table. The capacity planning board allows you as a planner to change the strategy profile by navigating to Settings  Strategy. You can also make temporary changes to the selected strategy profile, and these changes then apply to the dispatches that are carried out immediately after that. However, the system won’t store these set- tings when you leave the capacity planning table. If the available capacity is insufficient, you can increase it from within the capacity planning table—for example, by adding another shift or an interval. You can adjust the available capacity from the capacity planning table by choosing Goto  Capacity  Change. Similarly, you can make changes to the production order or to the operations from within the capacity planning table by clicking the relevant options that are avail- able in the menu bar. The planned increase in material stock can be displayed in the capacity planning table. To display the Material Stock pane for selected materials (see Figure 16.35), choose Extras  Material Stock  Show. This pane appears toward the bottom of the capacity planning table. Depending on how the capacity master data is set up, several operations can occupy a work center at the same time. In such cases, a green line below the operations indicates a multiple commitment. Choose Edit  Multiple Commitment  Show to display the multiple commitments of the selected work centers. Note The system enables you as a planner to leverage objects and layouts for display in the capacity planning table. We encourage you to explore these options. Here, you can find options for Scale, Increment, Shift Calendar, and Time Line, among others. © 895 16.5 Capacity Planning Table Figure 16.35 Material Stock Pane 16.5.5 Material Availability before Dispatching To ensure smoother production, you should also check to see whether the components required to produce a product are available prior to dispatching an operation to a work center. Figure 16.36 shows that a production order has two missing parts (components) out of the four components required. Figure 16.36 Material Availability in Capacity Planning Table As mentioned earlier, in addition to the graphical capacity planning table, the system provides a tabular capacity planning table that contains nearly all the functions of the capacity planning table. The only exceptions are the functions for sequence-dependent setup and the associated settings for the planning strategy. Figure 16.37 displays the ini- tial screen of the tabular capacity planning table. 16 Capacity Requirements Planning 896 To access the tabular capacity planning table, you use menu path Logistics  Production  Capacity Planning  Leveling  Work Center View  Planning Table (Tabular) or Transac- tion CM22. Figure 16.37 Tabular Capacity Planning Table Due to the extensive similarities between the capacity planning tables in graphical form and tabular form, and with almost all of the properties and functions covered in this chapter, a planner can easily conduct the planning process in the tabular capacity planning table if necessary. Note Chapter 21 will discuss a few SAP Fiori apps available for capacity planning. 16.6 Summary Capacity requirements planning helps a planner ensure that the system plays its part in making important business decisions, from dispatching the right operation in the right work center to evaluating the possibilities of increasing a shift or an interval. It can also help ensure that the system will perform finite scheduling if there is a capacity shortage or midpoint scheduling to account for on-the-ground production and capac- ity realities. The next chapter discusses the production planning and detailed scheduling function- ality. © 897 Chapter 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling The primary objective of the production planning part of production planning and detailed scheduling (PP/DS) is to ensure that the right product is available at the right time and in the right place to be deliv- ered to customers and to ensure that the raw materials are available for production. The detailed scheduling part of PP/DS supports the sequenc- ing and scheduling of production operations by taking into consider- ation the available resource capacity (machines and human resources) and the availability of materials. In addition, detailed scheduling ensures optimal usage of resources. SAP S/4HANA offers a new PP/DS area, which means that the PP/DS functionality is now part of the SAP S/4HANA core and there’s no longer a need to have an SAP Advanced Planning and Optimization (SAP APO) system to use PP/DS. The embedded PP/DS func- tionality offers most of the features present in PP/DS in SAP APO. All PP/DS transactions can be accessed directly in SAP S/4HANA. In addition, new SAP Fiori apps for capacity monitoring, capacity planning, and detailed scheduling are available, and most existing PP/DS transactions can be accessed through the SAP Fiori launchpad. Embedded PP/DS also ensures master data harmonization, and creating sources of supply has been sim- plified. In embedded PP/DS, the core interface (CIF) has also been simplified. The CIF is respon- sible for integrating PP/DS with SAP S/4HANA. Originally, CIF integration occurred through remote function calls (RFCs) and inbound and outbound queues in which data was processed when received or sent. But now, because PP/DS runs on an SAP S/4HANA core, there’s no longer a need to use RFCs to integrate data between systems, which means simplified integration, synchronous commits, and consistent data between the two systems. Immediate error handling in most situations is possible. The new MRP Live, introduced in SAP S/4HANA and already covered in Chapter 13, is now also capable of planning with PP/DS heuristics. Due to the embedded nature of PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA, the material master and work center creation is also synchronous. In other words, a material or a work center that has just been created in SAP S/4HANA can be immediately available for planning in PP/DS. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 898 In the following sections on PP/DS, it will instantly become clear that many of the con- cepts, foundations, and pieces of knowledge developed in this book will be equally applicable to PP/DS processes. For example, by now, you’re familiar with concepts related to work centers, BOMs, routings, and production versions. Similarly, you’re familiar with pegging, PIRs, demand management, stock/requirements lists, and MRP Live, among other concepts. So, in the interest of giving maximum coverage to the topic under discussion—that is, PP/DS—we won’t review these topics here. Instead, we’ll only discuss topics or definitions that are relevant to PP/DS and that require more detailed coverage to provide you with understanding of PP/DS itself. Furthermore, while it’s true that PP/DS is now embedded in SAP S/4HANA, you still need to set up and configure the CIF, even when the source and the target SAP systems are the same—that is, SAP S/4HANA. Similarly, to use the PP/DS optimizer tool to opti- mize production flow, you still need to set it up in SAP S/4HANA. Finally, due to the fact that SAP S/4HANA and PP/DS use the same or similar terminology (such as planned orders and PIRs), the following sections will specify whether any given element belongs to SAP S/4HANA or PP/DS. The following are some of the key features in PP/DS that we’ll cover in this chapter:  Production planning heuristics  Interactive planning tools  Detailed scheduling heuristics  The PP/DS optimizer  Interactive, detailed scheduling tools  Background planning and scheduling tools  Planning procedures  Pegging relationships  The alert monitor  The plan monitor In the following sections, we adopt the same approach to covering PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA that we’ve followed throughout. We’ll cover the basic configuration needed to set up PP/DS, and then we’ll cover master data specific to PP/DS. Next, we’ll put the PP/DS master data to use by covering the associated PP/DS business processes. We’ll also discuss heuristics, the production planning run, and the optimizer. In the last sec- tion, we’ll cover the alert monitor and the plan monitor. Finally, we’ll discuss reporting, including SAP Fiori apps that are relevant to PP/DS. Let’s begin with the necessary PP/DS configuration. 17.1 Configuration You need to perform a number of steps before you can start using embedded PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA. These include checking the relevant business functions and other con- figuration settings. © 899 17.1 Configuration Embedded PP/DS was introduced as business function SCM_PPDS_ON_ERP, and it should be active by default on an SAP S/4HANA system. Still, we recommend that you confirm whether this business function is active by executing Transaction SFW5 and checking whether the traffic light is green on the screen the system displays. To activate embed- ded PP/DS, you need to check the Advanced Planning box by following menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Planning  Basic Settings  Activate Advanced Planning and Scheduling. Note All PP/DS configuration options are available via menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Plan- ning. With embedded PP/DS, there’s no need to create integration models to transfer trans- action data between SAP S/4HANA and PP/DS or vice versa. You can activate the trans- fer of the transaction by following menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Planning  Basic Set- tings  Activate Advanced Planning and Scheduling  Settings for Data Transfer. Then, you can set the order types that are relevant for advanced planning as relevant for transfers between PP/DS and SAP S/4HANA (see Figure 17.1). Figure 17.1 Order Types for Transfer Embedded PP/DS needs to have the model and version set up. Model 000 and version 000 are the locations in which the active planning data products and resources relevant to advanced planning are stored. You dan also create additional simulated 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 900 planning versions for assessing the planning situation. You create the model and ver- sion via Transaction /SAPAPO/MVM (see Figure 17.2). Here, you maintain the PP/DS Horizon in Days as a time frame during which the system will automatically be able to create, change, or delete receipts. Outside of the PP/DS horizon, you can only plan receipts manually. Figure 17.2 Model and Planning Version Combination Tips and Tricks The system doesn’t recognize any PP/DS transaction that’s entered in the command bar. Hence, you must precede all PP/DS transactions with “/N” and then enter the PP/DS transaction code. For example, you should enter “/N/SAPAPO/MVM” in the command bar instead of “/SAPAPO/MVM.” To avoid repetition, we’ll denote the relevant PP/DS transactions in this chapter without including “/N.” Next, check the Change Planning Active box and then make the following key settings:  PP/DS Horizon in Days The PP/DS horizon in days defines the number of calendar days within which the PP/DS system is allowed to create, change, and delete procurement proposals during a planning run execution. Beyond the PP/DS horizon, you can create procurement proposals manually by using the PP/DS transactions. There is no option to set any material-specific planning horizon in embedded PP/DS, and all the advanced plan- ning products will use the PP/DS horizon defined in the planning version settings. © 901 17.1 Configuration  Determine Priority In PP/DS, you can assign priorities to the procurement proposals (e.g., planned orders). These order priorities are used in PP/DS functions such as scheduling heu- ristics to reduce the delays in manufacturing an order that has higher priority.  Take Safety Stock into Account Safety stock is the requirement element used in the plan to cover any unplanned demand that may result in a stock-out situation. PP/DS supports safety stocks in planning.  No Order Without Source of Supply In PP/DS, when orders are created, the source of supply, such as a production data structure or an external procurement relationship (e.g., an info record), is assigned to the orders. If no suitable sources of supply are found, the orders can still be cre- ated, but the order dates can’t be correct because the source of supply determines the time it takes to procure or produce a material. In the planning version, you define whether to create orders when no suitable source of supply is available and how the system should react when no source of supply is determined. Next, you activate the business transfer event (BTE) application indicator. You must activate this indicator to trigger the transfer of the relevant data into the embedded PP/DS. In Transaction BF11, you check the ND-APO box to set the flag (see Figure 17.3). Figure 17.3 Activating ND-APO Checkbox Although you’re not required to create integration models for the transaction data transfer, you still need to maintain basic settings for the CIF. In addition, you need to create certain master data objects such as purchasing contracts, info records, and scheduling agreements in an integration model to send those objects to the PP/DS side. Therefore, maintaining the basic CIF settings is an important step of using embedded PP/DS. You can change basic settings for creating the system landscape and for includ- ing SAP S/4HANA with system type SAP_APO and the corresponding release so that an integration model can point to itself (act as its own system and client) and logical sys- tem and queue types can be maintained. You maintain basic CIF settings by following menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Planning  Integration via Core Interface. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 902 You must also maintain global settings and defaults for PP/DS, and you do it via Trans- action /SAPAPO/RRPCUST1 or by following menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Planning  Global Settings  Maintain Global Parameters and Defaults (see Figure 17.4). Figure 17.4 Global Parameters and Default Values Let’s discuss some of the important fields and data transfer options shown in Figure 17.4:  Number Range Number This optional setting determines whether orders created in PP/DS contain a number that corresponds to the PP/DS number range. As soon as an order is transmitted to SAP S/4HANA via CIF, the order receives an order number in SAP S/4HANA that is based on the SAP S/4HANA number range. The order number is then transferred to PP/DS. You need to define a number range for PP/DS orders in advance, and you can assign it here. © 903 17.1 Configuration  Transfer to R/3 In-House Production Here, you define whether planned orders or purchase requisitions are transferred to SAP S/4HANA or whether the transfer occurs only after conversion in production orders or purchase orders.  Planning Run The settings you make here are relevant if no other explicit settings are made within specific applications. Note Notice the available-to-promise (ATP) categories for PP/DS. You use these to define the receipt and requirement elements of production and detailed planning. They are already present in the delivery system, so you specify a category only if the executing system isn’t an SAP S/4HANA system (see the ATP Categories tab in Figure 17.4). The MRP-based detailed scheduling scenario is a special form of process modeling that uses PP/DS in the context of production order processing in a targeted manner. Before the advent of embedded PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA, basic material requirements planning (MRP) was supposed to be done in SAP S/4HANA, while PP/DS was supposed to be used only for detailed scheduling. Against this background, MRP-based DS in master data modeling doesn’t require the transfer and mapping of routings and BOMs in produc- tion data structures or production process models, and it can omit the associated implementation effort (in production versions and associated integration with change transfers). You can consider manufacturing order networks from SAP S/4HANA (in the direct pro- curement/in-house production special procurement key) in a PP/DS capacity planning in this scenario. Within the scope, you can implement scheduling and retransfer of the new time schedule to SAP S/4HANA when converting planned orders into production orders. A special feature is that this conversion is done locally in SAP S/4HANA by way of exception; but when the production orders are posted, the earlier PP/DS scheduling takes effect nevertheless. Additional changes and transfers can follow iteratively. To make the relevant configuration settings, you access the screen shown in Figure 17.5 via Transaction CFDS and select the relevant options to automatically transfer or not transfer some or all planning and scheduling data from PP/DS to SAP S/4HANA. You can also set another retransfer of the planning result between PP/DS and SAP S/4HANA. These settings are relevant if the quantity of a production order (or any other data element) that originates from PP/DS is changed in SAP S/4HANA, for example. The changed quantity is transferred to PP/DS, where the order is rescheduled. The new dates are retransferred to SAP S/4HANA only if you configure this function in Transaction /SAPAPO/CP3 (see Figure 17.6). Here, you choose elements such as in-house production, external procurement, PIRs, and reservation data to retransfer between PP/DS and SAP S/4HANA in a bidirectional manner. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 904 Figure 17.5 Activating MRP-Based DS Figure 17.6 Retransfer of Data © 905 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS This completes the minimal configuration required to set up and run PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA. The next section is on maintaining the necessary PP/DS master data so that you can run the associated business processes. 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS The following sections will cover the master data required to run PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA. 17.2.1 Location A location can represent a plant, a business partner, a shipping or receiving point, or an MRP area. To create locations in PP/DS, you use Transaction SE38 and then execute report /SAPAPO/CREATE_LOCATION. This report immediately creates a location, with no integration queues, and an error message will be displayed immediately if an error occurs (see Figure 17.7). Figure 17.7 Creating Locations, Business Partners, Plants, and Shipping Points 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 906 You can also use ABAP program /SAPAPO/UPD_ LOC_SP_PL to update the location data in embedded PP/DS when changes are made to the corresponding data in SAP S/4HANA. Note You can access transactions pertaining to the master data, business processes, and reporting of PP/DS via SAP Menu  Logistics  Advanced Planning. After you transfer the plant location, a plant location of location type 1001 (production plant) is created in embedded PP/DS. You can access the location master from Transac- tion /SAPAPO/LOC3 or via menu path Logistics  Advanced Planning  Master Data  Location  Location. After you enter the Location and Location Type, choose Change to change the information and add additional PP/DS information to the location master. 17.2.2 Material Master When you plan an advanced planning material in MRP Live, the system uses the default heuristic and further settings defined in the Advanced Planning tab of the material master. To plan materials with PP/DS heuristics, you define MRP type “X0” in the MRP1 tab. Whenever you want to plan a semifinished material with PP/DS heuristics, you should also plan the parent material with PP/DS. Therefore, when designing a BOM, you must ensure that the parent material is an advanced planning material if at least one of its components is an advanced planning material. This design is necessary to ensure that all the dependent requirements are relevant for PP/DS. In SAP S/4HANA, the Advanced Planning tab has been introduced into the material master. In this new tab, you define a material as relevant to the PP/DS planning process by simply checking the Advanced Planning box, as shown in Figure 17.8. Note We’ll be using the material 2269 and plant 1710 combination throughout this chapter while discussing PP/DS. In addition, the example here is based on discrete manufactur- ing, but the same concepts and understanding of PP/DS apply if the manufacturing type is either process or repetitive. Furthermore, as the focus of this chapter is on covering the production processes in PP/DS, procurement and sales processes specific to PP/DS are beyond the scope of this discussion. Let’s discuss some of the important fields of PP/DS. Figure 17.8 shows the Advanced Planning view of the material master. There are many important fields, as follows (note that only a few of them are shown in Figure 17.8): © 907 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS  PP Plng Procedure For each planning-relevant material in PP/DS, and for any event occurring at a loca- tion product level (that is a material), an action is triggered by PP/DS. For customer requirements, the PP planning procedure also determines whether the desired quantity or the confirmed quantity of a schedule line is relevant for pegging. For example, say a planning-relevant event such as a goods movement for a product or a change in the product master takes place. Possible actions by PP/DS can include immediately calling up a product heuristic for the product or creating a planning file entry. A heuristic is a planning function in PP/DS that executes planning for selected objects, such as products, resources, operations, or line networks. Figure 17.8 Advanced Planning Tab in Material Master Tips and Tricks Planning in Planning Run (planning procedure 111) should always be the standard pro- cedure you select for planning products in PP/DS. It corresponds to the procedure used in SAP S/4HANA. When a planning-relevant change occurs, a planning file entry is writ- ten that is processed in the planning run with the planning of the product. Planning procedure 111 contains heuristic SAP_PP_002 (Planning Standard Lots), and this heuris- tic corresponds to the net requirements and lot-size calculation that you’re familiar with from SAP S/4HANA. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 908  Plan Explosion This field specifies the task list type, such as a BOM and routing, that the system uses to create receipts in PP/DS.  Product Heuristic Just as there are MRP types in SAP S/4HANA that control the type of planning a material will have, product heuristics are the planning methods for PP/DS.  Avoid Alerts When you check this box, the system will attempt to create the pegging relation- ships between requirements and receipts without quantity or date alerts. The sys- tem first links receipts and requirements that are compatible on a time and quantity basis and then links the remaining receipts and requirements in a second step. Warning This method can reduce system performance.  Alert Threshold for Earliness The system creates a date/time alert for a fixed or dynamic pegging relationship if the earliness exceeds the alert threshold—that is, if the availability date/time is ear- lier than the specified time frame before the requirements date. To specify the time frame, you use format HHHHHH:MM (hours:minutes). For example, 1000:10 means 1,000 hours and 10 minutes and 20:20 means 20 hours and 20 minutes. If no value is specified, then the system uses the value 100000:00. Therefore, the system only creates an alert if an availability date/time is more than 100,000 hours before the requirements date.  Alert Threshold for Lateness As with the alert threshold for early receipt, the system creates a date/time alert for a fixed or dynamic pegging relationship if the delay exceeds the alert threshold— that is, if the availability date/time is later than the specified time frame after the requirements date.  Pegging Strategy Using the pegging strategy, you can determine in which time sequence the system should cover requirements for the product with dynamic pegging and in which time sequence the system should use the product receipts in the pegging interval to cover a requirement. Thus, the pegging strategy controls the following: – Which requirement the system should cover first – Which receipts the system should use first In general, for dynamic pegging, the system first covers the earliest requirement, then processes the next requirement, and so on. Because there may be several receipts for the availability dates and times within the pegging interval, you must © 909 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS specify which receipt the system should use first. You can choose from the following options: – Use current receipts Here, the system uses the current receipt where possible to cover a requirement. Starting from the requirement date, the system first searches to the beginning of the pegging interval. If there are no receipts in this direction, the system searches to the end of the pegging interval. – Use the earliest receipt (FIFO) Here, the system uses the earliest receipts in the pegging interval to cover a requirement—that is, it uses the first receipt in the pegging interval, then the sec- ond, and so on. With this strategy, excess receipts only become available later.  Res. Network Name This indicates the name of the resource network. A resource network consists of the physical links between resources in a plant, such as processing units, reactors, and vessels. It facilitates the flow of materials through a plant.  Product Alerts In this field, you specify which of the following is true: – The system determines direct alerts for a requirement or a receipt of a product – The product is relevant to network alerts If a product is relevant to network alerts, then the system evaluates the direct alerts that it determines for this product for receipts or requirements at the superordinate levels of the pegging structure as well. You can also hide alerts for less critical com- ponents and thus increase the clarity and transparency of network alerts and improve performance. If you select the Avoid Alerts option, the system won’t determine any direct alerts for the product. Thus, the product also won’t be relevant in regard to the network. Note You as a planner need to specify whether and which alerts are actually displayed by the system in the planning interface or in the alert monitor in the PP/DS alert profile to be used for planning. We discuss the alert monitor in detail in Section 17.7.  Lot Size Unit This indicates the valid unit of measure (UoM). The following options are available: – No lot size units are maintained: The base UoM maintained for the product applies. – You can maintain a unit of measure (an alternative or base UoM) and then con- vert it to the base UoM. If you enter an alternative UoM, you must maintain the 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 910 base UoM on the Units of Measure tab. You must be able to convert the alterna- tive units of measure to the base UoM for the product; otherwise, the unit won’t be accepted. For example, say the alternative UoM is kilograms (kg) and the base UoM is pieces (PC). In this case, 5 kg corresponds to 3 PC.  Target Days’ Supply The standard PP/DS SAP_PP_002 (Planning of Standard Lots) heuristic and the sup- ply network planning (SNP) heuristic take into account the target days’ supply if one of the following target stock level methods is selected: – No entry: Target days’ supply from the product master – 111: Target stock level equals maximum stock level + safety stock – 5: Maximum from maximum stock level/target days’ supply (product master) – 6: Total from maximum stock level/target days’ supply (product master) – 7: No target stock level To plan location products using target stock level methods, you use the standard SAP_PP_002 heuristic (planning of standard lot sizes). Heuristic-based planning uses the SNP heuristic, which plans the demand over the entire supply chain network (via cross-location planning) and creates a medium- term production and distribution plan. It doesn’t take into account any constraints or costs, so the plan created won’t necessarily be feasible. In a second step, after the heuristic run, you as a planner can then adjust the plan using capacity leveling in interactive SNP planning to create a plan that is feasible.  Reorder Days’ Supply You specify the reorder days’ supply if Reorder Point Method 2 for the location prod- uct has been defined.  GR Processing Time This is the time in between the delivery or the production of a product and its avail- ability as stock. You use this time, for example, as handling time or a time for quality checks, and you add it to the transportation duration or the production time of a product.  GR Handling Cap. Cons. This is used by the heuristic and PP/DS optimizer to calculate how much handling resource capacity is consumed by the product for a particular plan. For example, if the handling resource can handle 1,000 liters per day and the handling capacity con- sumption of 10 liters per piece is defined, then the maximum rate is 100 pieces per day.  Loc. Shelf Life In this area, you as a planner can control whether the system should consider the resource location-dependent shelf life of a product and, if so, the standard, mini- mum, and maximum shelf lives a product needs to have. A location in PP/DS can be © 911 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS a plant, a distribution center, a storage location, an MRP area, a customer, a transpor- tation lane, or a business partner (supplier).  Proposed Strategy The strategy group maintained in the MRP 3 view of the material master is directly linked to the PP/DS strategy, as shown in Table 17.1. If you enter other strategies or keys into SAP S/4HANA, then the relevant field in the PP/DS product master will remain blank or unchanged. After you check the Advanced Planning box for an existing material, you use Transac- tion SE38 to execute report /SAPAPO/PPDS_DELTA_ORD_TRANS for the material. This report allows the integration of existing planning elements for this material into PP/DS without the creation of an integration model. The only input parameters to add are the material and the plant. You enter those parameters in the Material and Plant fields shown in Figure 17.9. Figure 17.9 PP/DS Delta Order Transfer Strategy Descriptions SAP S/4HANA Strategy Groups PP/DS Strategies Anonymous make-to-stock (MTS) 10 10 Planning with final assembly 40 20 Subassembly planning 70 20 Planning without final assembly 50 30 Planning with planning product 60 40 Make-to-order (MTO) production 20 Blank Table 17.1 Different Strategy Keys in SAP S/4HANA and PP/DS 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 912 Tips and Tricks You use the mass maintenance transactions that are available for the material master, such as Transaction MASSD and MM17, to maintain the PP/DS fields in the material master. After you save a material master for which the Advanced Planning option has been selected, the changes will be immediately reflected in the product master. You can review the product master with Transaction /SAPAPO/MAT1; Figure 17.10 shows prod- uct master 2269, which was created for this material. Recall that these same details were maintained in the material master and are now visible in the product master. Figure 17.10 Product Master in PP/DS Note Directly creating a material in PP/DS isn’t supported, which means that you should always generate the product master by checking the Advanced Planning box in the material master. However, you can make limited PP/DS-specific changes in the product master, as shown in the various available tabs in Figure 17.10. 17.2.3 Work Center/Resource The work center or resource is also simplified in embedded PP/DS. As with maintain- ing settings in the material master, you can define that a work center is relevant for © 913 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS capacity planning in PP/DS. You don’t need to create an integration model to generate the resource in PP/DS. During work center creation or maintenance (in Transaction CR01), you simply check the Advanced Planning box as shown in Figure 17.11, and a cor- responding PP_DS resource will then be automatically created. Figure 17.11 shows Work Center ASSEMBLY for Plant 1710 with the Advanced Planning option selected in the Basic Data tab. Figure 17.11 PP/DS Work Center Note We’ll be using the same work center ASSEMBLY and plant 1710 combination through- out this chapter while discussing PP/DS. Next, choose the Capacities tab shown in Figure 17.11 and then choose the Capacity Header, which brings up the screen shown in Figure 17.12. In Figure 17.12, choose the APO Resource button located in the top-right-hand corner and maintain the PP/DS-specific details on the popup screen. The time stream validity is determined by the Days - (days in past) and Days + (days in future) fields, and the same is used to create the SAP liveCache time stream in PP/DS. Just as the material master created in SAP S/4HANA is reflected as a product master or a product in PP/DS, each work center created in SAP S/4HANA with the Advanced Plan- ning box checked is reflected as a resource in PP/DS. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 914 Figure 17.12 APO Resource in Work Center In Figure 17.13, the PP/DS resource created as a work center has the prefix “W.” That’s fol- lowed by the resource name, then the associated plant for the resource, and finally the associated resource category (such as machine or labor). So, in this example, for the just- created work center ASSEMBLY for plant 1710 with resource category 001 (machine) in SAP S/4HANA, the corresponding resource that’s automatically created in PP/DS is WASSEMBLY_1710_001. Figure 17.13 Resources in PP/DS: Initial Screen © 915 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS Next, you access the newly created PP/DS resource screen shown in Figure 17.13 via Transaction /SAPAPO/RES01 and enter the details as shown. Then, you choose Display Resources to make the screen shown in Figure 17.14 appear. Figure 17.14 Resource in PP/DS As already mentioned, embedded PP/DS provides synchronous commit, which means the PP/DS resource information will always be consistent with the SAP S/4HANA work center information. As shown in Figure 17.14, the PP/DS resource is created immediately after the creation of the work center. In the resource, the Planner Gr (group) field is important. The PP/DS planner is location independent and is created in the Specify Per- son Responsible (Planner) configuration activity. You also use the planner to select the area of responsibility for PP/DS reporting, including in SAP Fiori apps. Note As with the material master-product combination, manually creating PP/DS resources isn’t allowed, and you can only create a PP/DS resource together with a work center. However, you should delete a PP/DS resource manually whenever the respective work center is deleted. Creating a work center won’t be allowed if the respective resource already exists on the PP/DS side. It’s important for you to enable target system−independent settings in the CIF for the resource transfer in Transaction CFC9. Values defined in the Days in Past and Days in the Future fields and resource type will be used when a PP/DS resource is created. In addition, the no change transfer change transfer mode isn’t supported in embedded PP/DS (see Figure 17.15). 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 916 Figure 17.15 Configuration Settings for Data Transfer Note Remember to also create the remaining PP master data, such as the BOM of material 2269 and its routing that uses work center ASSEMBLY. Finally, you should also create a production version that combines the routing and BOM details of material 2269. 17.2.4 Sources of Supply Sources of supply define how the materials are procured. In the following sections, we examine sources of supply for materials produced in house (via internal procurement) and sources of supply for materials sourced from a supplier or another plant (via exter- nal procurement). Internal Procurement For a material produced internally, the PP/DS source of supply is the production data structure, which represents a combination of a BOM, routing, and production version. Although creating a production data structure in PP/DS isn’t as simple as creating a product (i.e., a material in SAP S/4HANA) and a resource (i.e., a work center in SAP S/4HANA), there’s still no need create an integration model to do it. By using Transac- tion SE38 and executing report PPDS_ERP_CUSLNTRTO_CIF_REPORT, you can gener- ate the production data structure based on production versions. Figure 17.16 shows the selection screen of report PPDS_ERP_CUSLNTRTO_CIF_REPORT 1. The results of the report execution 2 show a successful transfer of the production data structure. © 917 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS Figure 17.16 Product Data Structure As shown, this transaction offers flexible selection criteria, allowing you to choose, for example, which materials or plants for the production data structure will be generated. You can also choose between an Absolute Transfer, in which everything will be trans- ferred, and a Change Transfer, in which only the production data structure for which the master data was changed since the last transfer will be transferred. Because produc- tion data structure transfers are synchronous, the results will be visible immediately after you run the transaction. Any errors that may have occurred during the transfer will also be revealed. Figure 17.16 (2) shows the results screen of production data struc- ture generation for Material 2269, Plant 1710, and Production Version 0001. Note You can also use transaction CURTOADV_CREATE to generate the production data structure. Use Transaction /SAPAPO/CURTO_SIMU to view production data structure. External Procurement For external procurement, you can generate integration models for scheduling agree- ments, contracts, and info records in PP/DS, and an integration queue will still exist. You can generate an integration model via Transaction CFM1 (or execute report RIMODGEN via Transaction SE38), and then, you can activate the integration model via Transaction CFM2 (or execute report RIMODAC2 via Transaction SE38). 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 918 The upper part of Figure 17.17 1 shows the initial screen for creating integration models via Transaction CFM1, and the lower part 2 shows the successful creation of an integra- tion model that lists scheduling agreements, contracts, and purchasing info records for external procurement. Be sure to choose Generate IM 2 to generate an integration model. Figure 17.17 Creating Integration Model After you create the integration model in Transaction CFM1 1, you use Transaction CFM2 2 (see Figure 17.18) to activate the model. Note In addition to creating and activating the integration model, you can use Transaction CFM4 to display it. You can use Transaction CFM5 to search for the integration model that a particular master data object is part of, and you can use Transaction CFM7 to delete integration models. © 919 17.2 Master Data in PP/DS Figure 17.18 Activating or Deactivating Integration Model Note Due to the highly integrated nature of working between production and procurement, we recommend that you engage an MM consultant to ensure that you correctly create the necessary master data for correct and smooth planning and scheduling of procured materials that are planned through PP/DS. Table 17.2 summarizes the master data mapping between SAP S/4HANA and PP/DS. Master Data in SAP S/4HANA Master Data in PP/DS Plant and business partner (customer and supplier) Location (with location type) Purchasing info record, scheduling agree- ment, and outline agreement External procurement relationship, including transportation lane Special procurement key in material master Transport relation or interpretation as a special scenario Table 17.2 Master Data Mapping in PP/DS 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 920 17.2.5 Stock and Batches The inventory data in SAP S/4HANA includes stocks and the associated batches. Stock information is transferred to embedded PP/DS along with batch information if the material is batch-managed. Stocks are maintained at the plant level, and for planning purposes, stock is considered at the MRP area level in SAP S/4HANA if an MRP area is assigned to the plant. Plant-level stock will be transferred as stock to the PP/DS plant location, and stocks in the MRP area in SAP S/4HANA are transferred to the MRP area location in embedded PP/DS. Batches associated with stock are transferred to embed- ded PP/DS; they are referred to as versions in the PP/DS planning tools. In embedded PP/DS, the stock update counters are activated by default and can’t be turned off. The stock data that’s transferred to embedded PP/DS uses relative stocks, which refers to the delta between the current stock and the stock to be received or issued. The initial transfer of stock can be triggered by the CIF reconciliation tool (Transaction /SAPAPO/ CCR) or the initial transfer report (Transaction /SAPAPO/PPDS_DELTA_ORD_TRANS). Whenever a stock posting happens in SAP S/4HANA, it’s immediately transferred to embedded PP/DS, along with its batch and storage location information. This completes our discussion of PP/DS master data. In the following sections, we’ll dis- cuss the important business processes of PP/DS. 17.3 Business Processes The PP/DS product view is the equivalent of an MRP transaction: the stock/require- ments list (Transaction MD04), which we covered in Chapter 13, Section 13.2. You can monitor the demand/supply situation for one specific product in the product view, and you can also create different planning versions for simulation purposes. To access the product view, you use Transaction /SAPAPO/RRP3. If a popup appears asking you to enter a variant, you simply enter a dummy number such as “1” and press (Enter). Figure 17.23 Pegging Structure in Product Overview Three purchase requisitions (PurRqs) are automatically created for external procure- ment of three corresponding components. The details of these automatically created purchase requisitions can also be checked via Transaction ME53N. Pegging involves a link that creates a logical relationship between the receipt elements and requirements elements of a product within a location. The system uses these peg- ging relationships to assign requirements to receipt elements. The links always result from availability and requirements dates within a pegging area, in which product, loca- tion, and account assignment (MTS or MTO, etc.) correspond to each other. Pegging involves an analysis that refers to existing orders and requirements, and it occurs inde- pendently of requirements planning. The primary applications of pegging include analysis of production planning, capacity planning, and optimization. © 925 17.3 Business Processes There are two types of pegging relationships: fixed and dynamic. Fixed pegging enables a planner to fix a pegging relationship that will not be changed automatically by the system, while in dynamic pegging, the system attempts to adjust the dates and quanti- ties to closely meet the production and procurement plan. For pegging relationships to be created, the product, location, account assignment (MTS and MTO production), and planning versions must be the same. Figure 17.28 Display Logs Screen 17.4 Heuristics A heuristic is a planning function that can be parameterized and used to execute plan- ning or a planning step for selected objects. The planning objects of heuristics can be products, resources, orders, and operations. The concept of heuristics is a central con- cept in planning with PP/DS. A number of planning steps can be executed by heuristics. Accordingly, you can easily design the planning steps in various ways—by selecting the appropriate heuristic. In PP/DS, you as a planner simply have to select a different heu- ristic or create a new heuristic that then becomes part of the overall planning process. You can use the heuristics of a given group for the same task or issue; they represent different planning and scheduling alternatives. Two heuristic options are available:  Product heuristic You use a product heuristic to plan products with heuristics that are defined via the planning procedure or product masters.  Variable heuristic With the variable heuristic, you as a planner can perform planning interactively with a heuristic other than the one stored in the product master and without having to change the entry in the product master. Furthermore, you can change the heuristic settings before executing planning. In any case, execution of the heuristic first treats data only in a transactional simula- tion, and the data is written to the database only when you save the planning results. You use the heuristic profile (configuration in PP/DS) to define which variable heuris- tics can be selected. Three standard heuristic profiles are available: 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 928  Profile SAP001, for the detailed planning board  Profile SAP002, for the product view  Profile SAPREM, for the product planning table With interactive heuristic planning, you as a planner execute planning for a product. You can also perform this function from the product view, the product planning board, the detailed planning board, and so on. You can also use it when the planning situation of a product has been changed manually and you want to determine the effects of the change on planning right away. Selecting the Product Heuristic option will run the heuristic assigned to the product master; however, you can directly execute other heuristics assigned to the heuristics profile within the product view by selecting the More  Variable Heuristic option. To execute the variable heuristic, you go to the screen shown in Figure 17.20 and choose More  Execute Variable Heuristic from the top menu bar. On the next screen that appears (see Figure 17.29 1), you select the relevant heuristic, which in this example is SAP_PP_002 Planning of Standard Lots. Figure 17.29 Executing Variable Heuristics in PP/DS Then, make any other desired planning or scheduling changes by scrolling down the screen and choosing relevant options. In addition, to cross-check which materials are © 929 17.5 Production Planning Run being planned via a heuristic, you choose the Products tab. Next, choose the Heuristics button from the top menu bar to see the heuristic results 2. As already mentioned, product heuristics define the planning of a product, and you can enter them explicitly in the PP/DS tab in the product master. If no entry is present in the product master, the standard heuristic of the planning procedure is used. Table 17.3 lists some typical product heuristics. 17.5 Production Planning Run You use a production planning run initiated via Transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSB0 to exe- cute planning in interactive (foreground) mode or in batch (background) mode. Depending on the complexity of the planning or sequencing required, you can execute the heuristics, PP/DS optimization, or DS functions. In the production planning run, you can execute various planning steps in several con- secutive processing phases by using different heuristics or functions. For example, you can execute MRP heuristics followed by bottom-up rescheduling heuristics. You can execute a planning run for active version 000 or any inactive version that you as a planner specify in the Planning Version field. In the Time Profile field, you define the planning period, which can be a period in the past or the future. The Propagation Range defines the restrictions on the products and resources that are included in the planning run. You can also execute the planning run on a simulation version. In the Heuristics Short Descriptions SAP_PP_002 Planning of standard lots: “normal” planning of the product based on the lot size stored in the product master SAP_PP_003 Planning of shortage quantities: planning only of shortage quantities; existing orders remain as is; relevant for capable to promise SAP_PP_004 Planning of standard lots in three horizons: planning with various lot sizes in three time horizons SAP_PP_005 Part-period balancing: optimizing lot sizing procedure SAP_PP_013 Groff procedure: optimizing lot sizing procedure SAP_PP_007 Reorder point planning: reorder point planning with a reorder point from the product master SAP_PP_C001 Planning of standard lots for continuous I/O (input/output): planning that considers continuous input/output in the production data structure/pro- duction process model Table 17.3 Typical Product Heuristics 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 930 various step tabs, you maintain the sequence of functions or heuristics to be executed. You can select functions such as firming/unfirming orders, the detailed scheduling optimizer, and all the permitted heuristics. The relevant products and resources will be controlled based on the functions or heuristics you select. For example, if you select the schedule sequence heuristic, then the system will be forced to use resources or order selections instead of products when applying the heuristic (see Figure 17.30). Figure 17.30 Planning Several Heuristics You can analyze the results of the planning run immediately and examine them inter- actively by checking the Display Logs box on the selection screen. Tips and Tricks After you’ve executed the planning run in interactive mode or in background process- ing, the logs will be stored, and you can analyze them in evaluation transactions, such as Transaction /SAPAPO/RRPLOG1. You can use the selection variants you saved in the PP/DS production planning run in a background planning run executed via Transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSB1. In the following sections, we’ll discuss how MRP Live either creates an SAP S/4HANA planned order that links the PP/DS planned order or directly creates a production order based on the PP/DS planned order. Furthermore, we’ll discuss the PP/DS scheduling © 931 17.5 Production Planning Run and capacity planning options available either in tabular form or on the DS planning board. Finally, we’ll discuss the PP/DS optimizer, which strives to maintain a balance among various production, procurement, and capacity constraints. Note Heuristic SAP_PP_002 (Planning of Standard Lots) is particularly important and corre- sponds to the normal planning of a product with the use of the lot size defined in the product master. It is the standard heuristic in planning procedure 111 (Planning in the Planning Run) and therefore represents the normal case for product planning. In heu- ristic SAP_PP_002, you can set a reuse mode (e.g., for new planning or change plan- ning). If nothing is specified, the reuse mode from the planning file is used. Normal planning of a product with the lot sizes defined in the product master occurs with heu- ristic SAP_PP_002. 17.5.1 Executing Planning in SAP S/4HANA The basic rule for planning in PP/DS is that detailed production planning and schedul- ing occurs in PP/DS, while this planning is executed in SAP S/4HANA. The planned order or the purchase requisition is converted into a manufacturing order or purchase order (respectively) in PP/DS to execute PP/DS planning. Unlike SAP S/4HANA planned orders, the PP/DS planned orders already contain all the production dates. If a PP/DS planned order is transferred to SAP S/4HANA, then the SAP S/4HANA planned order contains only the basic dates and all dependent requirements. The production start and end dates are transferred as basic dates. Integration at the detailed scheduling level isn’t realized for SAP S/4HANA planned orders. For this reason, the conversion of planned orders must occur in PP/DS. Convert- ing a PP/DS planned order corresponds only to checking the box to set the conversion flag (manually for one order or via mass conversion for orders and purchase requisi- tions). If a PP/DS planned order with the conversion flag is transferred to SAP S/4HANA, then a corresponding manufacturing order is created in SAP S/4HANA. The SAP S/4HANA planned order assumes the production dates determined in the PP/DS planned order, and the integration of the orders is maintained. Status information or production confirmations that are created in SAP S/4HANA and are relevant to planning are transferred from SAP S/4HANA to the PP/DS order. All data relevant to planning must remain consistent. In addition, the SAP S/4HANA produc- tion order contains a variety of functions to execute production—functions that don’t require integration with the PP/DS order, such as the printing of order papers and con- sumption. We’ve now covered the PP/DS side of the product view of product 2269 and the receipt quantity of 100. The next step is to run MRP Live (Transaction MD01N) and see how the 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 932 planning occurs for product 2269 from PP/DS transfer to creating a planned order in SAP S/4HANA so that it can be converted into a production order. After the plan- ning run, the performance log is displayed, and it shows information about the num- ber of materials planned, the number of materials with errors, and the time spent on each step. Figure 17.35 Data Transfer Comparison and Reconciliation between SAP S/4HANA and PP/DS The following sections will discuss the scheduling options that are available via the resource planning table and the DS planning board, as well as the large number of material and capacity planning options they offer. Scheduling in Production Planning: Detailed Scheduling Performing detailed scheduling in PP/DS helps you as a planner schedule the operation of orders while considering constraints such as material and resource availability. The basic functions of the scheduling tools are to schedule, reschedule, and deallocate oper- ations. You can also use other parameters, such as scheduling direction (forward/back- ward) and order relationships, to influence detailed scheduling behavior. You can per- form detailed scheduling interactively from the scheduling tools or execute it in the planning run as a step. Scheduling via the Resource Planning Table A resource planning table is an alternative to the DS planning board that displays scheduling data in tabular format. You can use a resource planning table to handle sequencing or scheduling problems with resources. The selection screen for including 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 936 data in the resource planning table is similar to the selection screen for the DS planning board. Additional tools, such as the alert monitor, plan monitor, and PP/DS optimizer, are integrated into resource planning tables, as shown in Figure 17.36. A clipboard func- tion is available in the resource planning table so that you as a planner can copy a list of operations and sequence and schedule them on the resource. You can interactively sequence operations and activities by using drag-and-drop or pick-and-drop options. The scheduling heuristic that’s assigned to the heuristics profile used in the resource planning table enables you to execute the scheduling heuristic. Planning multiple resources or bucket resources isn’t supported by the resource planning table, but the Alert Monitor and Plan Monitor functions are available in the resource planning table. Figure 17.36 Resource Planning Table Detailed Scheduling Planning Board The DS planning board is a widely used scheduling tool in PP/DS. From the DS planning board, you can use all scheduling options, including executing various detailed sched- uling heuristics, using the PP/DS optimizer, and manual scheduling. Detailed schedul- ing is launched with a time profile that determines the time period during which data is displayed (in the Display Start and End of Display fields) and the data is available for detailed scheduling (in the Planning Start and Planning End fields). You as a planner can manually select resources, products, and orders via Transaction /SAPAPO/CDPS0 (see Figure 17.37). On the DS planning board, graphical objects repre- sent the different statuses of the scheduling of the planning objects, such as orders and operations. You can also use a work area to determine the resources and products, and it’s defined in Transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSC3. © 937 17.5 Production Planning Run The user settings and the DS planning board profile settings determine the charts dis- played on the DS planning board and also the availability and behavior of the schedul- ing and monitoring tools loaded into the DS planning board. Apart from the charts within the DS planning board, such as the Resources Chart, the Product Chart, and the Product Stock, there is a graphical representation of the network view of the orders as well as the pegging relationships among activities, operations, and orders (see Figure 17.37). On this screen, notice that a planner can Reschedule and Deallocate operations in similar ways as in capacity requirements planning in SAP S/4HANA (via Transactions CM21 and CM25), as we covered in Chapter 16. Figure 17.37 DS Planning Board Since SAP Fiori is the preferred user interface for all important business functions in SAP S/4HANA, Figure 17.38 shows the Advanced Scheduling Board that is the SAP Fiori version of the DS planning board. Here, you as a planner can not only intuitively select and deselect the desired chart types from the Select Charts dropdown list located in the upper left-hand corner of the screen, but you can also perform various business func- tions such as running a heuristic or an optimizer. The detailed scheduling optimizer application has been integrated with the Advanced Scheduling Board app. A detailed scheduling strategy drives how the scheduler treats operations and orders. The scheduling mode, the planning direction, and other parameters (e.g., the consider- ation of block planning and error handling) are defined in the strategy profile. Within the strategy profile, the parameters for dependent objects (the orders and operations that depend on other objects scheduled within the DS planning board) are also defined, as shown in Figure 17.39. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 938 Figure 17.38 Advanced Scheduling Board Figure 17.39 Strategy Settings Tips and Tricks When launching the DS planning board, the system generates a simulation version by copying the planning data from the current version. As a result, you as a planner can simulate multiple scheduling options, and after you’ve created a desirable scheduling © 939 17.5 Production Planning Run situation, you can save the data to the current planning version. You can access simula- tion versions via Transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSS0. You can also process these simulation versions further in both graphical and tabular options and adopt them into the active version. 17.5.3 Production Planning: Detailed Scheduling Optimizer The PP/DS optimizer has specific properties that distinguish it significantly from the calculation procedure of heuristics. Solving complex scheduling problems requires consideration of several constraints that the PP/DS optimizer can address. The effort needed to find an exact solution to the problem increases with the number of possible permutations. To put it more exactly, the effort needed increases by the factorial of the orders to be planned. In this case, planning even a small, two-digit number of orders would require hundreds of years of computer time to find an exact analytical solution. Therefore, the PP/DS optimizer uses selective procedures that lead to realistic comput- ing times (in minutes)—without a guarantee that the absolute optimum solution for the problem has been found. From the set of possible solutions, it’s very likely that the PP/DS optimizer will find some solutions, but not all of them. In comparison, a heuris- tic leads to exactly one solution. Using the same constraints, several optimization runs can produce different results. Based on these properties, it’s clear that the computing time entered by the user as a termination criterion for the optimization run is extremely important. In general, the higher the amount of computing time, the higher the quality of the solution. However, the reverse isn’t always true: more computing time doesn’t guarantee the improvement of a solution that has already been found. The PP/DS optimizer isn’t called as an independent transaction; instead, it’s called from within corresponding applications. It can be called interactively in the DS plan- ning board or product planning table (via Transaction /SAPAPO/PPT1). The PP/DS opti- mizer can also be run with specification of an optimization profile in the production planning run. The PP/DS optimizer in embedded PP/DS can’t create orders, but other functionalities are supported. The PP/DS optimizer can be executed in the background in a production planning run (via Transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSB1) or via interactive scheduling tools such as the DS planning board (refer to Figure 17.37). To use the PP/DS optimizer, you choose Optimize on the screen shown in Figure 17.37. In the ensuing popup, you enter the start and end dates of the optimization horizon and then choose Optimize again to open the PP/DS Optimizer screen shown in Figure 17.40. Before we discuss Figure 17.40 in detail, you should choose the Optimization profile icon (the wrench) located in the top left-hand corner of the screen to bring up the Set- tings box shown in Figure 17.41. Here, you can maintain different information—such as the total setup costs, maximum delay costs, and total mode costs—in relative terms. You can also make various optimization settings in different tabs. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 940 Figure 17.40 PP/DS Optimization Log You can select basic settings for the PP/DS optimizer through the optimization profile, by customizing the DS planning board or the product planning table, or by using Trans- action /SAPAPO/CDPSC5. When you start the PP/DS optimizer, you can adjust or change some of these basic settings based on the current planning situation (see Figure 17.41). Figure 17.41 PP/DS Optimization Profile © 941 17.5 Production Planning Run The PP/DS optimizer works based on costs, and therefore, the objective of optimization is to minimize overall production costs. You can achieve this cost reduction with an objective function made up of individually weighted key figures. Use the following weighting factors (Wi, where i = 1…6) to influence the orientation of optimization: Production costs = (W1 × Makespan) + (W2 × Total of the setup times) + (W3 × Total of the setup costs) + (W4 × Maximum delay costs) + (W5 × Total of the delay costs) + (W6 × Total of the mode costs) Now, let’s discuss the fields shown in Figure 17.41:  Makespan Minimizing the makespan, or the overall lead time, aims at a maximum load on the optimized bottleneck resources in the short term. The system attempts to minimize the overall time needed to process orders and operations with consideration of the existing constraints.  Total Setup Times Minimizing the total of the setup times is possible if the time needed to set up from one setup status to the next is dependent on exactly that sequence (setup status- dependent setup time with dynamic setup). In a paint shop, for example, the time required to set up the use of a light color after using a dark one is likely longer than the time to set up the opposite. In such cases and similar cases, an optimization objective can be to create an optimal sequence from the standpoint of the total of the required setup times. The result is that the system summarizes the operations of similar setup statuses in blocks. Long setup times would then appear only when changing between two different setup statuses.  Total Setup Costs Minimizing the total of the setup costs is necessary only when the system needs to have the costs and setup times described independently of each other. This can be the case when identical setup times differ for various resources in terms of cost- related evaluation and must therefore be decoupled from the setup time. Otherwise, the setup time criterion is sufficient.  Maximum Delay Costs The PP/DS optimizer can penalize delays that occur in terms of the defined require- ments dates/times that lie within the optimization horizon with delay costs. Two approaches are available: – You can permit short delays in similar forms, which will help you avoid individ- ual, significant delays, resulting in a minimization of the maximum delay costs. – You can formulate the total of the delays as a target value and calculate a mini- mum value across the overall delay for all orders. This approach doesn’t exclude individual, obvious delays. In general, you can define delay costs as dependent on the order priority; you would use higher costs for sales orders with a higher prior- ity. This approach should help to avoid delays for high-priority orders. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 942  Total Delay Costs This is the cost of delayed production, and it enables planners to decide which mate- rials need to be produced first so as to minimize the associated costs.  Total Mode Costs Mode costs penalize the frequent (via fixed costs) and long-lasting (as defined with variable costs) use of alternative work centers. The alternative work centers must already be available in the system in master data and contained in the propagation range. You can link them to optimization via alternative modes.  Trade-offs It should be obvious that optimization usually works within somewhat contradic- tory constraints and different interests, such as sales interests and production inter- ests. This is referred to as a trade-off. It’s also clear that the influencing factors have somewhat different natures (costs and times) and therefore possess different physical units. To enable a comparison of the individual totals within the formula for calculating the production costs, costs are con- verted into times. The following rule applies here: a standardized cost of 30 corre- sponds to 30 seconds. Referring to Figure 17.40 again, note the information about the quality of the solution that has been found both during and after successful optimization. The three bars shown here graphically display information on the status of the optimization mes- sages, the values of the related optimization parameters (including deallocation costs), and the solution process over time. From this evaluation view, you can use a button to view the parameters of the optimization and to change the parameters set by the opti- mization profile to some extent, as well as to start optimization (or restart it) interac- tively. To start the PP/DS optimizer, you choose the Start Optimization Run icon (the clock or the execute icon) in the top left-hand corner of Figure 17.40. Then, the system will start generating messages as shown on the right-hand side of the screen. Notice that the optimization run shows that quite a few orders can only be scheduled with a delay, while the two bottom-half sections show the numerical and weighted values of the optimized solution. Next, choose the Sol. tab located in the top-left-hand corner of Figure 17.40 to open the results screen shown in Figure 17.42. Figure 17.42 shows the graphical representation of the results of the PP/DS optimiza- tion run and the associated Total Delay Costs (the tallest bar) due to a delayed start of the order. You can carry out more optimization runs if needed. Alternatively, you can make changes to the settings shown in Figure 17.41 and then rerun the PP/DS optimizer. Within the Production Scheduling Board app, a planner can drag and drop operations to achieve the desired scheduling or simply run a PP/DS heuristic and let the system auto- matically optimize the operation scheduling. The following heuristics are supported within the app:  SAP001—Schedule Sequence  SAP002—Remove Backlog  SAP003—Schedule Sequence Manually  SAP004—Minimize Runtime  SAP005—Schedule Operations  SAP_DS_01—Stable Forward Scheduling  SAP_DS_02—Enhanced Backward Scheduling 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 946  SAP_DS_03—Change Fixing/Planning Intervals  SAP_DS_04—Activate Seq.-Dependent Setup Activities Within the SAP Fiori app, a planner can also create (using a template), change, or delete orders; check logs/alerts; and adopt, save, or reset results. However, campaign plan- ning, block planning, creating downtimes, and fixing intervals aren’t supported within the Production Scheduling Board app. 17.6.2 Monitor Capacity Utilization Embedded PP/DS also offers the Monitor Capacity Utilization app. When accessing this app, you as a planner will be prompted to select an Area of Responsibility, which is a combination of a plant and a planner. The Monitor Capacity Utilization app provides a quick overview of capacity utilization for all the resources within the selected area of responsibility. The next section discusses the alert monitor in PP/DS. 17.7 Alert Monitor The alert monitor in PP/DS is a tool that a planner can use during planning to manage and evaluate exception messages centrally. Messages about problems are transferred automaticallly from the application to the alert monitor, and each exception message relates to a specific problem situation. The primary task of the alert monitor is to list exception messages in a structured manner, and the messages correspond to the defined selection criteria. The alert monitor provides information on situations that require adjustments in planning. Note the distinction between the reason for the exception and its properties. Using exception messages, the alert monitor can provide general information on the problem situation itself but not necessarily on why the exception occurred. Let’s first discuss how to set up the alert monitor before showing how it works in plan- ning and scheduling. To create a new alert monitor profile, you use Transaction /SAPAPO/AMON1 and choose Alert Profile on the initial screen. Figure 17.51 shows alert profile ZPPDSALTMON, in which checking the relevant boxes in the Selection column will trigger relevant alerts. You can also define ranges or specific values so that the system brings up infor- mation, warnings, or error alerts upon meeting the specified values. In this example, the Product Too Early (Fixed Pegging) alert type shows alert thresholds for information, warnings, and error alerts. Note After you create an alert profile, you assign it to an overall profile so that the system can display alerts within an application (refer to Figure 17.27 to see how defined alert profile ZPPDSALTMON in Figure 17.51 is assigned to User Settings). Next, access the screen shown in Figure 17.52 by opening Transaction /SAPAPO/RRP3 (Product View) and choosing the Alert Monitor icon. Note the large number of error, warning, and information alerts in the top half of the screen. When you check any given box in the Selection column, details of each alert will be displayed in the lower half of the screen. For this example, resource WASSEMBLY_1710_001 has several error alerts. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 950 Figure 17.51 Alert Profile Maintenance Figure 17.52 Alerts in Transaction /SAPAPO/RRP3 Note Because the alert monitor tool is also available in several planning and execution func- tions in PP/DS, be sure to use it for better production or procurement planning. The next section covers the plan monitor tool that’s available in PP/DS. © 951 17.8 Plan Monitor 17.8 Plan Monitor The plan monitor evaluates the key figures for a plan in relation to specific objects, ver- sions, and periods, and it then assigns scores to the results. A planner can define their own calculation rules according to which the scores are to be calculated, or they can simply use standard aggregations (e.g., sum, mean value). The plan monitor can be used to do the following:  Determine the quality of the current planning situation.  Compare various planning and simulation versions with each other.  Compare planning over different periods. The plan monitor considers the key figures for which a planner has created key figure variants in the key figure schema. These key figures can be displayed in a tabular or graphical version or both. For example, say a production planner plans over a three- to four-week horizon but is faced with new expectations every day that impact many areas of the plan over the upcoming weeks. Here, the plan monitor can help. Key figures in the following areas of the plan monitor are available:  Key figures for order quantities  Key figures for order times  Key figures for pegging relationships  Key figures for stocks  Key figures for resources Let’s see how you can put the plan monitor to use in a step-by-step way: 1. In the Display area, choose the relevant display options, such as to display only the grid or the graphic or to display both. Check the relevant box to choose whether indi- vidual values in the graphic can be displayed. 4. Choose the Key Figures tab. 5. On the screen shown in Figure 17.54, select Key Figure Cat. (category) from the drop- down list (in this example, 9033 Orders: Times) and then choose the relevant key fig- ure (Open production time). The lower left-hand corner of the screen offers the avail- able calculations, such as using minimum, maximum, or average values of key fig- ures. 17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling 952 You can display key figures individually or collectively by choosing the relevant options in the Display area. Furthermore, you can check the correctness of a defined key figure by choosing the Check button before choosing Adopt.
