The PP/DS optimizer attempts to maintain a balance between costs and benefits for producing or not producing products and to determine where trade-offs are necessary. The user-defined alerts in the alert monitor and monitoring key figures in the plan monitor keep various checks and balances in place for smoother production and procurement planning. SAP Fiori apps for PP/DS not only report via dashboards but also enable intuitive planning. The next chapter discusses SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and operations. © 957 Chapter 18 Production Engineering and Operations SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and operations is one of the latest additions to the manufacturing space in SAP S/4HANA as an embedded solution. It caters to large, complex, turnkey projects, such as those in the aerospace, aircraft, shipbuilding, defense, and space industries, and it’s an extension of discrete manufac- turing production types. SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and operations is gaining significant traction in complex, low-volume, and extended manufacturing processes, and it’s an extension of discrete manufacturing processes. Due to the extensive design and development stages involved in a complex, large-scale, turnkey manufacturing process, production engineering and operations attempts to bring a greater balance between design changes (engineering bills of materials [EBOMs]) and their impact on operations/manufacturing (manufacturing bills of materials [MBOMs]). Here are some of the traits to consider when deciding whether to implement produc- tion engineering and operations:  Highly engineered products  Engineer-to-order (ETO)  Make-to-order (MTO and configure-to-order  Complex assembly production  Low volume of orders  High numbers of engineering change requests  Labor-intensive processes  Long cycle times (days, weeks, and months)  High numbers of components and operations Materials with some or all of the above traits are prime candidates to be considered for production via production engineering and operations. Figure 18.1 shows the key building blocks of production engineering and operations, and Figure 18.2 focuses on the key business processes of production engineering and operations that we’ll discuss in this chapter. 18 Production Engineering and Operations 958 Figure 18.1 Building Blocks of Production Engineering and Operations Figure 18.2 Business Processes Overview We begin this chapter with an overview of production engineering and operations and implementation concepts to help you develop an understanding of production engi- neering and operations, including production engineering and production operations. SAP S/4HANA Initiates and orchestrates the end-to-end production process Manages work queues, instructs operators and records complete as-built information Allows real-time access to all relevant data Converts product design into production process design that is the common basis for production planning, order management and execution Complex Assembly Execution Production Operations Production Engineering Production Operations Production Order Management and Control Shop Floor Order Complex Assembly Execution Production Engineering Manufacturing Change Management BOM Management Production Process Planning Manage BOMs Variant Configuration Major Assemblies/Installation Kits Disassembly Reference Designators Product Manufacturing Information (PMI) Tool Usage Tracking Action Log and Genealogy BOM/Routing Version Management Non-Conformance/ Defect Handling Manage Shop Floor Routings ▪ Operation/ Activities ▪ 3D Visuals ▪ Work Instructions ▪ Data Collection ▪ Qualification required ▪ PRTs ▪ Buyoffs Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner • Operation/ Activities • 3D Visuals • Work Instructions • Data Collection • Qualifications • PRTs • Buyoffs Model-Unit-Effectivity Manufacturing Change Record Monitor Engineering Snapshots Change Impact Analysis Work Assignment and Work Queues Perform Operations and Activities Production Monitoring Production Order Change Management Manage Standard Texts Templates and Favorites Planning Master Data Manage Buyoff Templates © 959 18.1 Key Terminology Then, we discuss the system configuration, roles, and authorizations required for using production engineering and operations. Then, we explain the core business processes of production engineering and the master data setup requirements and version manage- ment that support the production engineering and operations processes. We provide comparisons to demonstrate key differences between the master data setup required for production engineering and operations and the master data setup required for standard discrete manufacturing. We then use an example to walk you through the process of transitioning from engineering to manufacturing execution operations and using tools to hand over processes from product lifecycle management systems to SAP S/4HANA systems. Then, we discuss the business processes of production operations in a step-by- step manner. Furthermore, we briefly touch upon integration of production engineering and operations with other SAP S/4HANA areas, such as materials management, quality management, the SAP Document Management service, and SAP Human Capital Manage- ment for SAP S/4HANA. Lastly, as production engineering and operations processes entail significant engineering changes to the products being manufactured, we also dis- cuss change management and impact analysis. Note Because production engineering and operations processes significantly resemble dis- crete manufacturing processes, we recommend that you read Chapter 3 and Chapter 6 before reading this chapter. We’ll only be discussing the processes briefly here so as to stay focused on production engineering and operations. 18.1 Key Terminology In Figure 18.2 you’ll see some of the following key terminologies used in production engineering and operations:  Production engineering This bridges the gap between product engineering and production operations by converting the product design into production process design, which is the basis for production order management and shop floor execution.  Manufacturing change management This orchestrates the end-to-end production engineering process, as follows: – The Monitor Engineering Snapshots app informs you of new sets of EBOM ver- sions and triggers the production engineering process. – The Manufacturing Change Record orchestrates the whole production engineer- ing process and keeps track of all changes made to manufacturing objects when you’re incorporating a new EBOM version. 18 Production Engineering and Operations 960 – The Change Impact Analysis app identifies all objects that are impacted by a prod- uct engineering change. – BOM and routing version management control the lifecycle and usage of differ- ent revision levels.  SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner You use this to accomplish the handover from the EBOM to the MBOM by convert- ing the design-driven EBOM structure into a manufacturing, built-sequence-driven MBOM structure.  Production process planning This is used to create shop floor routings that include all information needed for production planning, production costing, and shop floor execution, as follows: – Operations are used for planning and costing purposes, as in classic SFC and dis- crete manufacturing. – Operation activities are used for shop floor execution, and they describe in detail how an operation has to be performed: with work instructions, data collection required, qualifications needed, production resources/tools (PRT), and buyoff procedures. Production process planning includes a set of “supporting” features: – The BOMs and routings used for planning (MRP and PP/DS) can be distinguished from those used for shop floor execution. The usage of BOMs and routings for dif- ferent purposes is defined in the Manage Production Version app. – Using templates and favorites significantly increases user efficiency when main- taining routings. – In the Manage Standard Texts app, a manufacturing engineer can maintain a library of reusable text elements for efficient work instruction authoring. – Buyoff templates can be defined and used during routing maintenance as an approval process. – Qualification requirements can be predefined for work centers and materials and are inherited by related operations and activities. The next section describes the basic configuration and setup activities you need to per- form so you can use production engineering and operations. 18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup In the following sections, we’ll discuss the configuration settings and the basic produc- tion engineering and operations setup that you need to put in place in the SAP S/4HANA system. © 961 18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup 18.2.1 Activating Extended Manufacturing Features In this configuration step, you activate the production engineering and operations fea- tures because they are not a part of the standard SAP S/4HANA Enterprise Manage- ment license. For each plant, you activate the following manufacturing features. As in the rest of the book, we’ll be using plant 1710 as an example. To activate the required features in pro- duction engineering and operations, you navigate to the menu path SAP IMG  Produc- tion  Manufacturing for Production Engineering and Operations  General Settings  Activate Features and Acknowledge License Obligations (see Figure 18.3). In this exam- ple, the following production engineering and operations features are activated:  Buyoffs  Change Alerts  Extended Manufacturing Engineering  Extended Production Operations  Qualifications Figure 18.3 Activating Features for Manufacturing Extensions 18.2.2 Assigning Roles and Authorizations You should engage an SAP NetWeaver expert to assign the business roles listed in Table 18.1. Business Roles Role Descriptions SAP_BR_PRODN_ENG_DISC_CAM Production engineer—discrete manufacturing (CAM) SAP_BR_PRODN_ENG_DISC_EME Production engineer—discrete manufacturing (EME) SAP_BR_PRODN_OPTR_DISC_EPO Production operator—discrete manufacturing (EPO) Table 18.1 Business Roles 18 Production Engineering and Operations 962 18.2.3 Maintaining Number Ranges To maintain the number ranges for all the objects in production engineering and oper- ations, you navigate the menu path SAP IMG  Production  Manufacturing for Produc- tion Engineering and Operations  General Settings  Define Number Ranges. Next, you enter the number range object and choose Interval Editing. For each object, you create the number range number 01 for the full range and ensure that the External box is not checked (see Figure 18.4). Figure 18.4 Number Ranges in Production Engineering and Operations SAP_BR_PRODN_PROC_SPCLST_CAM Production process specialist (CAM) SAP_BR_PRODN_PROC_SPCLST_EPO Production process specialist (EPO) SAP_BR_PRODN_SUPRVSR_DIS _CAM Production supervisor—discrete manufacturing (CAM) SAP_BR_PRODN_SUPRVSR_DIS _EPO Production supervisor—discrete manufacturing (EPO) SAP_BR_PRODN_SUPERVISOR_DISC Production supervisor—discrete manufacturing SAP_BR_QUALITY_ENGINEER_EPO Quality engineering (EPO) SAP_BR_BOM_ENGINEER BOM engineer Business Roles Role Descriptions Table 18.1 Business Roles (Cont.) © 963 18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup 18.2.4 Creating Business Partners and Manufacturing Users In production engineering and operations, all SAP users need to be manufacturing users too, regardless of their roles. In SAP S/4HANA, a business user is defined as a natural person who is represented by a business partner and is linked to a user in the SAP S/4HANA system. Business users interact with the SAP S/4HANA system in the context of a business process—for exam- ple, in the role of a production engineer, production supervisor, or production opera- tor. For more information, see SAP Note 2570961. You create a business partner by using the Maintain Employees app, and while doing so, you assign the user ID of the user from the SAP S/4HANA system. Figure 18.5 shows that the User is assigned Business Partner 9980000210, which you can confirm by using Transaction SU3. We’ll use this business partner (9980000210) in examples throughout this chapter. Because production engineering and operations entails work- ing with various teams and business functions, you should create a few additional busi- ness partners and assign their SAP user IDs accordingly. Figure 18.5 SAP User ID Mapping with Business Partner IDs To create manufacturing users, you create business partners as just discussed and then use them as manufacturing users. Figure 18.6 1 shows that a manufacturing user is cre- ated using the Manage User Settings app, in which not only previously created busi- ness partner 9980000210 is assigned but so are Work Center (PEOWC) and Plant (1710). You also use the Manage Teams and Responsibilities app to create teams that will be managing the production engineering and operations business processes and to assign team members accordingly 2. It’s also important for you to create appropriate teams and responsibilities for several functions in production engineering and operations. This is required for the following functions:  When adding a buyoff to operation activities in the routing, it’s best practice to spec- ify the appropriate team and provide information about its functions.  When creating buyoff cycle templates, it’s required to specify the appropriate teams and provide information about their functions. 18 Production Engineering and Operations 964 Figure 18.6 Creating Manufacturing Users and Setting Up Teams 18.2.5 Defining Profiles You maintain buyoffs by defining business functions and function profiles in the fol- lowing menu paths (see Figure 18.7): 1 Define the function profiles via menu path SAP IMG  Cross-Application Components  General Application Functions  Responsibility Management  Functions  Define Function Profiles. 2 Define the functions via menu path SAP IMG  General Application Functions  Responsibility Management  Functions  Define Functions. © 965 18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup Figure 18.7 Defining Profiles 18.2.6 Bills of Materials In this example, a date-effective EBOM is incorporated into a version shop floor BOM. The EBOM can be directly incorporated into a shop floor BOM (or MBOM), or the EBOM can be first incorporated into a date-effective planning BOM (EBOM), which is then used to create the shop floor BOM (MBOM) automatically. For this example, you use Transaction OS20 to create two new BOM usages, P and V, to represent the EBOM and MBOM for the shop floor BOM (see Figure 18.8). For BOM Usg P, you must ensure that Eng/des. is enabled; for BOM Usg V, you must enable Prod. by choosing the + sign. Because production engineering and operations extensively uses version manage- ment functionality in the BOM and routing, you must maintain the control data for the BOM usages to enable the creation of version BOMs. The two BOM usages (P and V) are the same ones that you configured in the last step, and you must now assign them in production engineering and operations. To do this, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Manufacturing for Production Engineering and Operations  Production Engineering  Define Control Data for BOM Usage (see Figure 18.9). 18 Production Engineering and Operations 966 Figure 18.8 BOM Usages Figure 18.9 BOM Control Usage 18.2.7 Routing To differentiate shop floor routing in production engineering and operations from standard routing in discrete manufacturing, you configure the work center usages for routing types V (shop floor routing) and P (planning routing) by following menu path SAP IMG  Production  Basic Data  Work Center  General Data  Define Task List Usage for Work Centers. Figure 18.10 shows work center usage 009 with two new task list types: P and V. This newly configured work center usage will be assigned to the work center responsible for managing production engineering and operations processes. Furthermore, it’s important for you to define material type assignments to task lists for all material types that are going to be used in shop floor routings and that have to be assigned to task list type V. To do this, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Basic Data  Routing  General Data  Define Material Type Assignments (see Figure 18.11). Similarly, you maintain the following configuration settings for task lists via menu path SAP IMG  Production  Basic Data  Routing  Routing Selection  Select Automati- cally:  Select Automatically: Selection ID 01, priority 05, TL type P, Usage 1, Status 2  Select Automatically: Selection ID 01, priority 06, TL type V, Usage 1, Status 4 © 967 18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup Figure 18.10 Task List Usage Figure 18.11 Task List to Material Type Mapping 18.2.8 Creating a Control Key Because production engineering and operations business processes also entail rework scenarios, you must create a control key that allows rework by checking the Rework box for the control key in menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Master Data  Routing Data  Define Control Key. For this example, we’ve created a new control key, PEO1, which is a copy of the standard and available control key, PP01. As our exam- ple will show only the standard business process (and not rework) for production engi- neering and operations, the Rework box in control key PEO1 isn’t checked. While we’re creating a version routing, we’ll show you the option to create a rework routing that will require a control key in which the Rework box should be checked. 18.2.9 Configuring Order Type−Dependent Plant Parameters We recommend that you to configure a separate order type for production engineering and operations scenarios so as to differentiate it from normal or regular production 18 Production Engineering and Operations 968 processes in discrete manufacturing. For this example, you should ensure that for pro- duction order type PEO1 in plant 1710, the previously configured routing type V shop floor routing is assigned. You do this via menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Master Data  Order  Define Order-Type-Dependent Parameters (see Figure 18.12). Figure 18.12 Order Type-Dependent Parameters 18.2.10 Planning Scenarios The next configuration step lets you activate or deactivate the planning scenarios for creating a planning scope alternative. A planning scenario defines the parameters and the associated scope based on which the system undertakes planning in production engineering and operations. Here are some of the important planning scenarios that you can create, activate or deactivate:  Document-based nonconfigurable This scenario allows a planner to create a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is derived from the document linked to an RH file and the target is a noncon- figurable structure. We’ll use this planning scenario as an example to demonstrate the end-to-end business process in this chapter.  Document-based purchased part This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative for a purchased part. The planning scope alternative of a purchased part is not available for planning.  Product structure manager-based configurable This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is derived from the product structure manager and the target is a configurable struc- ture. © 969 18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup  Document to nonconfiguration with network This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is derived from the document linked to an RH file and the target is a nonconfigurable structure. It also allows loading a project system network and assigning components to the network activity. The reference points are copied from network activity to the assigned MBOM components.  Document to nonconfiguration group BOM This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is derived from the document linked to an RH file and the target is a nonconfigurable structure. This scenario is plant independent. The target structure is a nonconfigu- rable group BOM.  Product structure manager to configurable group BOM This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is derived from the product structure manager and the target is a configurable struc- ture. This scenario is plant independent. The target structure is a configurable group BOM. To make changes to the relevant planning scenario, you follow the menu path SAP IMG  Production  Visual Manufacturing Planner  Configure Planning Scenario. On the screen shown in Figure 18.13, you assign the previously configured planning BOM usage V to planning scenario 01 because this is the example we’ll be focusing on. For any other planning scenario, you maintain the relevant BOM usage accordingly. With a shop floor routing, a planner can create and manage version-controlled rout- ings that are not dependent on time. A routing version determines the sequence of individual operations that are necessary to produce a product. It also contains informa- tion on where the work is performed and the work center’s resources that will be con- sumed with all the necessary technical specifications, such as the standard times, capacity requirements, and work instructions. The operation activities can be used to describe the production process in more detail. Once released, a production version for the routing version and BOM version can be used in production execution. Note that a released routing version can no longer be changed. SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner enables the handover of the engineering structure (EBOM) to the manufacturing structure (MBOM), assisted by an interactive 3D model. Figure 18.42 Visual Enterprise Structure The following are some details and functions of SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufactur- ing Planner:  The source panel contains the EBOM structure and the visual (the multilevel BOM).  The working panel is the editing area, where a single-level BOM can be extracted from the target structure and edited. The visual only shows the corresponding visual of the single-level BOM.  The target panel contains the MBOM structure and corresponding visual (the multi- level BOM).  The components in the source panel are disabled once they are fully planned and converted in the MBOM.  SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner reconciles the EBOM (source) with the MBOM (target) to check whether any component is over- or underassigned. If any discrepancy is found, it’s highlighted in the corresponding nodes. © 989 18.5 Production Operations  SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner allows restructuring of EBOMs in a different MBOM structure using a quantity or parameter effectivity-based split or merge of EBOM components into MBOMs.  Synthetic MBOMs, which are only relevant for manufacturing, can be created using SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner while ensuring the design intent in the EBOM and the EBOM component coverage.  A routing can be loaded in the working panel to allocate the components to the rel- evant operations.  Planning BOMs are created by SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner based on EBOMs that may be version controlled or date effective.  Shop floor BOMs can be created from the planning BOMs. Phantoms will be flat- tened out, and data-effectivity will be turned into versions.  SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner can be used to derive shop floor BOMs from the EBOM structure.  For various reasons, such as material availability or production process reasons, you may need to use a different part in production than the one that has been specified by the product design.  During the handover from an EBOM to an MBOM in SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Man- ufacturing Planner, the production engineer can decide to replace an EBOM material with a manufacturing substitute. If required, the quantity can also be adjusted (e.g., when substituting two small plates for a big plate).  The system will automatically remember and consider the substitution when hand- ing over future EBOM changes and during EBOM/MBOM reconciliation. After you’ve made and finalized the necessary changes between the EBOM and MBOM, you choose Save and Exit Planning from the dropdown located on the top left-side hand side. Note SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner is a user-friendly and intuitive plan- ning tool, and we recommend that you explore it in detail. Choose a component in any of the three panels (working, source, or target) and right-click it to see more options to choose from. The next section is on production operations. 18.5 Production Operations Production operations are about initiating, preparing, executing, and documenting the actual end-to-end production process. A shop floor production order is an extended 18 Production Engineering and Operations 990 version of a PP production order in discrete manufacturing. We’ll cover many additional details of shop floor production orders in production engineering and operations here. Figure 18.43 shows the end-to-end process flow that we’ll discuss in these sections, along with the following additional details:  Operations are used for planning and costing, as in classic SFC.  Activities are used for shop floor execution, and they describe in detail how an oper- ation has to be performed—that is, work instructions, data collection required, qual- ifications needed, PRTs, buyoff procedures, and so on.  Production monitoring provides a comprehensive overview of the expected and actual status and progress of production orders and operations by automatically detecting exceptions, providing decision support, and allowing for immediate actions.  Engineering change incorporates and updates production orders with the latest engineering changes.  Complex assembly execution is primarily designed for complex, low-volume pro- duction processes with high change frequency and a focus on manual work—which is typical for complex assembly and engineer-to-order businesses.  Work assignment and work queues orchestrate the actual execution of production order operations and activities. Operators can access their tasks in a work center’s work queues, or supervisors can explicitly distribute work to their team members by moving tasks to personal work queues.  The Perform Work app supports production operators in executing their tasks by guiding them through the production process with the help of interactive work instructions, 3D visual support, data collection with real-time valuations, and so on. Figure 18.43 Process Flow in Production Operations Production Holds Defects Buy Off Genealogy Production Order Operations Activities Serial Numbers Assign Work Personal Work Queue Perform Operation Action Log Production Confirmation Manage Production Hold Manage Defects Manage Buyoff My Work Queue Manage Work Center Queue Manage Production Orders/Production Operations Perform Activity © 991 18.5 Production Operations  Defect handling manages nonconformance issues by placing holds on orders or parts, creating defects to be followed up on by quality engineers, and executing final usage decisions.  Tool usage tracking documents which tool instances were used when executing an operation activity.  Serialized parts are tracked and traced during the entire production process. The genealogy and action log collects all data and creates a complete and detailed record of how a serialized part has been produced and how an order has been executed. In the following sections, we’ll create a production order for material 2291 in plant 1710 and maintain the requisite details. Supervisors can assign additional operation activities to operators while considering their qualifications and work center assignments. Some additional available functions are as follows:  Select all unassigned operation activities or check existing assignments.  Assign one or more production operators to individual operation activities to dis- tribute the workload.  Filter operators’ names by matching work centers and qualifications during an assignment.  View a number of current operators’ assignments.  Add and edit the target duration for an operation activity during an assignment.  Unassign operators from operation activities.  Assign (and unassign) not only operators but also teams to specific operations. Figure 18.46 My Work Queue 18.5.3 Performing Operation Activity Figure 18.47 shows the Perform Work—Material/Serial Number app for production order 80000043, operation 0010, and serial number 1, wherein a production operator can per- form and record work on the operation activities assigned by the production supervisor. (Note that a production operator can also use the Perform Work-Order/Operation app if the material isn’t serial number managed.) The Perform Work—Material/Serial Number app contains everything needed to carry out the work, and it gives full visibility into the tasks to be performed on the shop floor at the operation activity level. Besides displaying the key data that’s needed, the app has integrated functions that allow an operator to quickly deal with any issues encoun- tered in production and thus react accordingly. To ensure smooth processing, the app determines whether it’s possible to start the operation activity—for example, whether the predecessor activity has been completed, no holds exist, and the user has the required qualifications. It also determines the available actions according to the assigned status and action schema. In addition, it provides enhanced capabilities for 18 Production Engineering and Operations 994 collecting data on serialized materials and batches for genealogy and traceability pur- poses. You choose Start to initiate the operation activity. Figure 18.47 Performing Operation Activity 18.5.4 Work Instructions Once an operation activity is started, the operator can post the goods issues of compo- nents from the Work Instructions screen in Interactive Data Collection. The screen shown in Figure 18.47 also offers the option to record components’ consumptions, including any unplanned consumption (see the Components tab). The system allows posting the batch and serial number with inventory validations. The Work Instructions app can also do the following:  Perform and confirm activities on orders or individual parts (serial numbers).  Enforce execution and provide actions for reporting activity progress and status.  Display a dynamic 3D visualization showing the parts already assembled and parts to be added next.  Support rich text work instructions with embedded data collection and links Update the equipment hierarchy (in SAP Business Network for Asset Management) for materials with serial numbers. © 995 18.5 Production Operations  Monitor production processes on either the production order level or the opera- tional level to make decisions in the most efficient manner.  Automatically detect exceptions and issues, such as the following: – Holds – Delays – Missing history – Material availability in plant and supply areas – Operational activity details, providing a complete overview of the activity layer – Information on the inspection characteristics collected and the results in the operation activity details  Provide immediate actions: – Placing holds (for one or multiple production orders simultaneously) and releas- ing holds – Manual order adjustments, Work app ensures that the defined sequence of operational activities is considered during execution, considering any order or parallel execution of opera- tional activities. If multiple activities are available to start, an operator can choose which one to start with. The app can also do the following: 18 Production Engineering and Operations 996  It can provide a buyoff timeline, increasing the visibility of buyoff history and upcoming steps.  It can provide an option to record defects during execution against production orders or individual serial numbers.  It can provide the option of scrapping serialized parts.  The labor tracking functionality of the app can now be enabled for a work center that is later associated with an operation in the shop floor routing to track the time a pro- duction operator is logged on to work on the operation activity at that work center.  The app can provide a possibility for multiple operators to collaborate on a task that has been already started by another operator.  The system starts tracking the time spent on the serialized product or an operation activity when a production operator starts the labor tracking functionality. At the end of a shift, for example, one production operator labors off an operation activity, while another operator can still continue working on the operation activity.  The system automatically stops labor tracking for all production operators currently working on an operational activity once that operational activity is complete.  The app can record excess consumption (see Figure 18.48) Figure 18.48 Consumption Components Figure 18.49 shows a partial view of the Perform Work app after some of the business functions (such as the consumption of two components, the recording quality inspec- tion results of two characteristics, and the defect details) are recorded. All business functions can be performed by choosing the relevant options from the menu bar located in the bottom-right-hand corner of Figure 18.49. Following are some details about buyoffs:  Buyoffs verify the proper execution of an operation activity. Experts in different domains have to check the work defined for an operation activity and approve a buyoff.  Operation activities can have a buyoff procedure with multiple steps. Every step has to be approved by a different domain expert. The Manage Buyoff app shows who has already approved buyoffs for the same operation activity. © 997 18.5 Production Operations  Ad hoc buyoff lines can be added during buyoff execution. That is, the buyoff cycle of an activity can be enhanced, if necessary.  The Manage Buyoff app shows all open buyoffs that a domain expert can approve (or reject) (see Figure 18.50). Recall that we have maintained the buyoff data in the shop floor routing and these two buyoff steps have been performed. Figure 18.49 Recording Operation Activities Figure 18.50 Manage Buyoffs 18 Production Engineering and Operations 998 Let’s now discuss defect handling, production hold, yield and scrap recording, and the action log, as well as the Change Impact Analysis app. Defect Handling The defect handling functionality shown in Figure 18.49 provides an option for produc- tion operators to record defects during the execution of production activities. It also can do the following:  Support the recording of a defect with and without a hold.  Provide an option for the production supervisor to process defects (see Figure 18.51).  Support follow-up decisions, such as use as-is or scrap.  Become available for the production operator on the defects list, after a decision has been made.  Prevent the usual completion of a serialized product if a scrap decision has been made. The production operator is then forced to scrap the product.  Display the list of all recorded defects in the Perform Operation Activity app. Figure 18.51 Perform Operation Activity App Hold The hold functionality shown in Figure 18.49 provides an option to temporarily block production by placing a hold on orders, materials, operations, work centers, or serial numbers. Here are some of the business functions of the hold functionality:  When placing an object on hold, a reason code and note can be maintained.  If something is put on hold, production operators cannot continue with their activi- ties until the hold has been released. © 999 18.5 Production Operations  Holds are shown as an important exception on all supervisor and operator user interfaces. Once the issue has been solved, the hold can be released.  You can monitor all production holds in the Manage Production Holds app, using the drill-down capability to view the hold details (see Figure 18.52). Figure 18.52 Managing Production Hold Yield For a nonserialized material, choosing Post on the screen depicted in Figure 18.49 will bring up the screen to record the yield, as well as any scrap produced during the pro- duction process. (Note that the Post button is but not shown in the figure because our example uses a serialized material.) Posting the yield will also automatically lead to posting the calculated activities (setup, machine, labor, etc.) and will be based on the standard values maintained in the shop floor routing master data. Action Log Choosing Action Log on the screen shown in Figure 18.49 will bring up the action log, as shown in Figure 18.53. An action log is a complete as-built and where-used report for individual parts, and it displays serial and batch numbers for consumed components. It maintains a detailed log of activities and actions performed for an individual part, such as the following:  Date/time  User  Activities started, completed, paused, and skipped  Buyoffs approved, rejected, and canceled  Holds applied and released  Nonconformances raised  Inspection characteristics 18 Production Engineering and Operations 1000 Figure 18.53 Action Log Figure 18.54 and Figure 18.55 show the Manage Work Center Queue and Manage Pro- duction Operations apps, which show here the current status of production order 80000043—including issues in the Issues area, such as quality issues, scheduling issues, component shortage issues, and others. With these apps, an operator can have a complete overview of the operational activities in a specific operation of a shop floor order. From here, the operator can start executing the production tasks (by choosing Execute), view key information at a glance, or drill down into detailed information for each operation activity. The features available in these apps enable smooth and efficient processing of production tasks by increasing transparency into progress in production and by allowing a faster response to issues in production apps that are available for production planning. Figure 18.54 Manage Work Center Queue App © 1001 18.5 Production Operations Figure 18.55 Manage Production Operations App 18.5.5 Change Impact Analysis App Before we delve into discussing the Change Impact Analysis app, let’s briefly discuss another SAP Fiori app: Manage Change Records. You can create a change record at any stage of the production engineering and operations business process by using the Man- age Change Record app. However, we highly recommend that you to create a change record as early as possible so that all objects affected by the changes are part of the change record. The primary purpose of a change record is to contain references to all the objects that are relevant to the change cycle. The following objects are supported:  Master data: EBOM, MBOM, shop floor routing, and planning scope  Transactional data: production order and purchase order Change items can be configured depending on the change record type. You indicate the context of the object in the change record by using the change relevance setting—for example, Create, Change, New Version, Reference, and so on. The status network of the change record is also configurable. A manufacturing engineer can use the change record as a single point of entry to access all the relevant objects for the change cycle. The change record enables an engineer to navigate to the corresponding apps to view or manipulate the objects. You access the screen shown in Figure 18.56 by opening the Manage Engineering Records app or the Manage Change Records app, followed by selecting change record 200000020. This is the same change record that you created while creating the shop floor routing. More objects, such as planning scopes, manufacturing material BOMs, and even production order 80000043 were added to this change record later on to ana- lyze the impact of changes made to any or all of these objects. Then, choose Analyze Impact on the screen shown in Figure 18.56. The change impact analysis can be triggered from different master data objects: EBOMs, MBOMs, and shop floor routings. It determines the net change of the trigger objects and identifies all manufacturing objects that are impacted by those changes: MBOMs, 18 Production Engineering and Operations 1002 shop floor routings, production orders, project networks, and directly related purchase orders (direct procurement, purchase-to-order, and purchase-to-project). The change impact analysis also determines which parts (BOM items, operations, or operation activities) of an object are impacted by the change (see Figure 18.57). Figure 18.56 Change Record Figure 18.57 Analyze Change Impact © 1003 18.5 Production Operations Some additional functionalities available in the Change Impact Analysis app are as fol- lows:  You can select one or more objects from the context of the change record as the trig- ger object(s) for the analysis (see Figure 18.57 1). The analysis result can be displayed in a list or in a network view mode. While the list is useful for displaying all the impacted objects in a consolidated manner, the net- work (see Figure 18.57 2) provides insights into the relationships between the objects.  Details of the impacted objects can be visualized in the details area.  Items, operations, and operation activities affected by the change are highlighted to visualize where the impact is.  For model and unit effectivity, the change impact analysis also identifies the units impacted by the change.  You can perform some quick actions for the impacted objects—for example, you can add to a change record, hold an order, and so on. Figure 18.58 (1 and 2) shows the Manage Unassigned EBOMs and Manage Unassigned MBOMs apps. Here, an engineer can not only create a change record but also assign the unassigned EBOMs or MBOMs to respective change records. It maintains a detailed log of activities and actions performed for an individual part, such as the following:  Date/time  User  Activities started, completed, paused, and skipped  Buyoffs approved, rejected, and canceled  Holds applied and released  Nonconformances raised  Inspection characteristics © 1007 18.6 Reporting Figure 18.62 Production Action Log 18.6.5 Data Subject Information Report The Data Subject Information report is a comprehensive report that shows the activity- level details of an individual user. Note Refer to Introducing Production Engineering and Operations (PEO) in SAP S/4HANA by Jawad Akhtar (SAP PRESS, 2023) for a detailed understanding on this topic. Individual texts for each of the object types can be written and used—for example, routings, production orders, and buyoffs. Each text is assigned to a specific plant so as to allow creating different texts for different object types in different jurisdictions if required. These texts are then inserted into the report above the tables containing the objects that the user created. For some objects, if there is no data included in the table, the report doesn’t contain the texts that were created. Instead, only the table and a stan- dard text are shown. Most data is stored under the username, but if a user has a busi- ness partner name, then data can also be recorded for this. For each plant, you enter the technical names of the text modules that contain the legal texts. To print or view a preview of the report for a data subject, you enter the username or business partner name of the data subject. 18 Production Engineering and Operations 1008 To activate the required functions for each plant, you navigate to menu path SAP IMG  Production  Manufacturing for Production Engineering and Operations  General Set- tings  Get Data About Data Subjects. Use Transaction SE38 and enter “RMPE_DATA_ SUBJECT_INFO” to access this report. 18.7 Summary In this chapter, we covered some of the important configuration, master data, business processes, and reporting for SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and operations. We also discussed a business example in which an engineering draw- ing stored in the SAP Document Management service was used for production engi- neering to create a MBOM, and then we used the same in production operations. The next chapter discusses standard reporting for the production planning compo- nent. © PART V Monitoring and Evaluating Production Planning © 1011 Chapter 19 Production Planning Reporting Timely, accurate, and comprehensive information helps a company make better business decisions. A planner can immediately start to use a large number of information systems and standard reports available in SAP S/4HANA for production planning, as well as for logistics. This chapter provides detailed guidance on how to leverage a large number of standard reports that are available in all the logistics areas of SAP S/4HANA. Once you’re familiar with the features and functions for reporting, including tools and icons, you’ll find it easier to navigate to unexplored areas of reporting. 19.1 The Basics of Reporting To fully take advantage of SAP S/4HANA, it’s essential to become familiar with the icons and other features frequently available for use in reports. Because these icons and functions are the same in all the information systems and analyses, a user can nav- igate faster, get to desired information more quickly, and even find tips and tricks to manage the display and availability of data as needed. Note It’s important to note that you may find similar icons in master data and transactions such as the REM planning table (in Transaction MF50) or the stock/requirements list (in Transaction MD04), but these icons may have different meanings and functions. For example, in a standard business process, clicking the Details icon brings up the Item details screen for the object. In the reporting function, however, clicking this icon brings up a comprehensive list titled All Key Figures. Information systems present a large amount of data and information in logically struc- tured lists and sections. We provide you with the following menu paths and transac- tion codes for both discrete manufacturing and process manufacturing to make the information systems easier to use:  Discrete manufacturing Choose Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Information System  Order Infor- mation System or use Transaction COOIS. 19 Production Planning Reporting 1012  Process manufacturing Choose Logistics  Production – Process  Process Order  Reporting  Order Informa- tion System or use Transaction COOISPI. 19.2 Report Parameters When we talk about the order information system, it can be either the production order information system or the process order information system. The former is specific to discrete manufacturing reporting, while the latter is for process manufacturing report- ing. An information system consists of the most up-to-date information about a specific object, and it’s based on the transactions that the user has performed as part of the business process. For example, when a planner executes the operations list in a produc- tion order information system, the system displays the current status of various oper- ations, including those that are completely or partially confirmed. If the same report is run or executed again after six hours—for example, when the user has recorded more confirmations for production orders—at that time, the system shows up-to-date (and different) information. Another example is that when the user first uses the sales infor- mation system, it shows the creation of 10 sales order for a specific material. After six hours, when the user again runs the report (in the sales information system), the num- ber of sales orders has increased to 18. This information helps to reflect the accurate and up-to-date status of various activities and business processes. In the order information system, we’ve divided the large number of available options for parameters selection into several figures in the following sections for ease of under- standing and comprehension. 19.2.1 Selection Screen at Header Level Figure 19.1 shows the Process Order Information System screen (Transaction COOISPI). Here, you need to first select the list from the List dropdown menu; further parameter selection directly relates to the list. Based on the list option that you select, you can then define further selection parame- ters. If you select Components from the dropdown list in Figure 19.2, then you will need to give selection parameters on the screen shown in Figure 19.3 (in the next section) under Selection at Component Level. If you select Operations/Phases from the drop- down list, then you can provide greater details in the Select. at Operation Level area on the screen shown in Figure 19.3. This all-in-one report caters to the reporting requirements of all major business pro- cesses of production. Notice that a user can also provide the sales order number with a sales order item number, if a company has MTO process order (or production order) © 1013 19.2 Report Parameters scenarios. Similarly, if a process order references any work breakdown structure (WBS) element, you enter that information in the selection criteria at the header level. Figure 19.1 Selection Screen for Process Order Information System Figure 19.2 Dropdown List in Process Order Information System For the example shown previously in Figure 19.1, select Order Headers from the drop- down list, and then, on the header level, define Production Plant as “1710.” 19 Production Planning Reporting 1014 A user can select an option from 18 available lists in discrete manufacturing and 14 available lists in process manufacturing. In fact, selection of the list is the first step that a user will need to complete before running the order information system. Table 19.1 shows the available lists in the production order information system and the process order information system. 19.2.2 Selection at the Operations and Components Levels with Options Scrolling down the screen shown previously in Figure 19.1 leads to several different views in which a user can enter selection details at the operation and component levels in the Select. at Operation Level view shown in Figure 19.3. A user can also select a spe- cific system status (System Status) by checking the Excl. box to exclude information Lists Production Order Information System Process Order Information System Object Overview X X Documented Goods Movement X X Trigger Points X Production Resource/Tool X Automatic Goods Movement X X Order Headers X X Items X X Capacities X X Components X X Operations X X (phases also) Production List X X Purchase Requisitions X X Confirmations X X Sequences X Purchase Orders X X Documents Links X Goods Movements with Errors X X Control Recipes X X (control recipe) Table 19.1 Comparison of Available Lists in Information Systems © 1015 19.2 Report Parameters having the specified status. For example, if a user doesn’t want to see partially con- firmed operations or phases in the report, then they enter “PCNF” in the System Status field and check the Excl. box. Figure 19.3 Selection Options at Operation and Component Levels A user can check the Missing Part box so that the system displays the components with the missing parts status in the process orders. The missing parts status is denoted by MSPT. A user can also limit the number of process orders that the system displays so that it’s displaying only collective process orders or process orders with deletion flags. They do this by checking the relevant boxes. 19.2.3 Selection Screen for Dates Scrolling further down, there are many dates to choose from. If a user wants a report to show information from a certain start date to end date in the process order, then they can define this in basic start dates, in actual start dates, or even according to the release dates of the process orders. The Relative Dates at Header Level area (see Figure 19.4) corresponds to the time inter- val between today’s date and any other date, either in the past or future. For example, 19 Production Planning Reporting 1016 if you want to see all the process orders for the past 100 days and for the next 50 days, then you enter “100–” in the Relative Basic Start Date field and “50” in the to field. Dates in the past are denoted by adding the negative sign after entering the number (e.g., 100–). Figure 19.4 Absolute and Relative Dates in Information System 19.2.4 Multiple Selection So far, on the selection screen of a report, the option has been available to enter any parameter in a range or an interval. For example, in the Relative Basic Start Date field shown in Figure 19.4, a user can only enter a selection interval. But what if a user wants to exclude a certain period within the date range on the selection screen or include two specific dates, apart from the dates given in the interval? This and several other options are available via the Multiple Selection settings in all information systems and reports of all components. A user can click the Multiple Selection icon shown in Figure 19.5 for the Process Order fields. On the resulting screen, four tabs allow a user to include or exclude single values or even an entire range. The user enters information into each of the four tabs, and the ensuing output (report) will show the desired results. Furthermore, there are additional available icons that the user can employ to copy data from a clipboard, add or delete a line within the values, or use the Multiple Selection option. The Multiple Selection icon enables a user to select multiple values by checking the requisite boxes. © 1017 19.2 Report Parameters Figure 19.5 Multiple Selection Option in Order Information System 19.2.5 Maintain Selection A frequently used and available option is the Maintain Selection option. When you click Maintain Selection, the Maintain Selection Options screen appears, as shown in Figure 19.6. You can use any of the available options on this screen. Alternatively, you can use a shortcut to reach the Maintain Selection Options screen by double-clicking the relevant field of the selection screen of any report. Figure 19.6 Maintain Selection Option in Order Information System 19 Production Planning Reporting 1018 19.2.6 Maintain Variant Maintaining a variant in SAP S/4HANA is similar to saving a layout that is then avail- able for repeated use when needed. A user can also set a variant as a default by manag- ing the layout options. For example, say that when using the order information sys- tem, a user always uses plant 1710, order type PI01, and the date range of 30 days from today. Instead of entering all of those parameters every time to run the order informa- tion system, a user can simply save these details as a variant and subsequently select it. There are two areas in which you can maintain variants. The first one is on the Initial Parameters Selection screen of any report, information system, or analysis that you use when defining the selection parameters. You can simply enter the information once in the parameter selection screen and then save the variant by choosing Goto  Variants  Save as Variant. On the Variant Attributes screen that appears, you can give the variant a name and a short description and then choose whether to make each field required, hidden, or protected from data entry. Then, you can save the variant, and next time, you can simply choose Goto  Variants  Get to get the variant. You can also delete incorrect or unwanted variants. You can use the second option for saving a variant when an analysis report is already executed in the order information system. The system displays all list fields, and a user has made the required changes—for example, setting any filters, sorting any columns in ascending or descending order, or performing aggregation. In this case, you choose Select Layout and select the Save Layout option from the dropdown. In the ensuing popup, you enter the name of the layout and a short description. Here again, a user can change or delete the saved layouts, if needed. 19.2.7 Order Header in Process Orders When a user executes a report by pressing (F8) or choosing Execute, a report such as the one shown in Figure 19.7 appears. This is the Order Headers report for the process order information system. Quick Menu in Report Go back to the screen shown in Figure 19.7, place your cursor in any column, and right- click to display all the available options—not just for that column, but for the entire report. Aggregation Back on the screen in Figure 19.7, you can aggregate or sum up (total) any key figure or quantity. To do this, on the screen shown in Figure 19.11, select the Target Quantity column, click the Filter icon, and choose Set Filter to set the filter. Figure 19.11 Setting Filter in Order Information System 19 Production Planning Reporting 1022 In the popup that appears, either double-click the Target Quantity field or use the Main- tain Selection icon, which opens the popup window. Select Greater Than or Equal To and choose Continue. Then, enter “50” in the Order quantity field so that the report shows only target quantities equal to or greater than 50. This screen shows that the fil- ter is in place with a little black arrow pointing downward in the Target Quantity col- umn. The red arrows denote that the list is sorted in descending order (with a red arrow pointing upward) or ascending order (with a red arrow pointing downward). Note The system will interchangeably show the Target Qty and Order Quantity fields in this report. Either one is basically the production order quantity (the GMEIN field). 19.2.9 Download When executing a report or conducting a standard analysis, a user can also download the information in one of the many formats available. They do this by choosing the Download option. A user can also send the information as an email attachment or even perform an ABC analysis in the information system, although this is also available in the standard analysis reports. 19.2.10 Copy Selective Data to Microsoft Excel A user can copy selected data if they don’t want to download all the data from SAP S/4HANA into Excel or a different format. To do this, they press (Ctrl)+(Y) to select the fields or content within the report. This enables block selection, in which they select the block of fields to copy. Then, they press (Ctrl)+(C) to copy the data. In Excel or another program (Notepad or Microsoft Word), they press (Ctrl)+(P) to paste the copied data. 19.2.11 Print Users have options to create a printout of the report. They access the print option by clicking Print within the report. All the parameters used in the generation of the report in the information system also become part of the printout, including filters and the like. Note We encourage you to explore other features and functions, as well as the options avail- able in the menu of each report. We’ve covered in general some of the features and functions available in the informa- tion systems and in analyses. Now, let’s discuss the order information system in more © 1023 19.3 Order Information System Reports detail. The details covered so far have been general in nature, and they enable a user to make effective use of the information systems and analyses in any area of SAP S/4HANA. We now move on to cover some of the actual lists (reports) of the order infor- mation system in the following sections, and we encourage you to explore the others that are available. You can access all of the following reports via Transaction COOIS. 19.3 Order Information System Reports The following sections cover some of the actual order reports, such as automatic goods movement, capacities, document links, and execution steps. 19.3.1 Automatic Goods Movement In automatic goods movement, the system performs one or both of the following func- tions:  If the Backflush functionality is activated, the component’s consumption or GI is auto- matically recorded at the time of confirmation against the process order. This is denoted by movement type 261.  If the Auto-GR functionality is activated, the system automatically performs the GR for the process order of the confirmed production quantity when the user performs the confirmation for the process order. This is denoted by movement type 101. Next, from the List dropdown option shown earlier in Figure 19.1 (we’ll be referring to this List dropdown in each of the report options discussed next), you should select Automatic Goods Movements. Figure 19.12 shows the Movmt type (movement type) column for backflush and auto-GR. Figure 19.12 Automatic Goods Movements in Order Information System 19 Production Planning Reporting 1024 19.3.2 Capacities Next, from the List dropdown menu, you select Capacities. Based on the information from the routing of the material, the system automatically calculates the required capacities when the user creates a production order. When the user confirms an opera- tion of the production order, the system correspondingly reduces the capacity require- ments of the work center. This will display the work centers, operation quantities, and remaining processing time (in hours) for each of the production orders. 19.3.3 Production Resources/Tools Next, from the List dropdown menu, you select Production Resource/Tool (PRT). Although it’s not directly involved in the production process, PRT plays a pivotal role in the manufacturing process. The available PRT categories are material (M), equipment (E), document (D), and miscel- laneous (O). The details in Usage Value denote the duration for which a PRT is needed during the production process for a production order. 19.3.4 Items Next, from the List dropdown menu, you select Items. This will display the items of the production order quantities, the actual recorded scrap, the GR, and the variance, if any. This screen also contains the overdelivery or underdelivery for the material, plus col- umns containing fields such as Quantity, Scrap, and ExpVarRcpt (expected variance in receipt). 19.3.5 Document Links Next, from the List dropdown menu, select Document Links. These documents were created in the SAP Document Management service and then attached to an object, such as the material master or BOM. When a production order for a material containing an SAP Document Management service document is created or when a BOM containing such a document is used in the production order, the system automatically creates the document links. An SAP Document Management service document consists of a docu- ment number, document type, document part, and document version. 19.3.6 Execution Steps In the production order information system, a user will see the same information in the Control Recipe list as is shown in the process order information system. Execution steps (XSteps) or control recipes are used in process manufacturing, depending on the © 1025 19.4 Missing Parts Information System option the company chooses for control instructions in process management. For dis- crete manufacturing, the company can only implement the XSteps option in process management. From the List dropdown menu, you select Control Recipe. This screen shows the status of the control recipe for the specific process order (Created, Sent, Executed, or Termi- nated), the Batch details, the destination details of the control recipe (the Dest. Type, Destinatn, and Destination Address columns), and the in-process (during production) Inspection Lot details (see Figure 19.13). Figure 19.13 Execution Steps We’ve now covered a few reports from the process order information system and the production order information system, and the remaining reports follow the same pat- tern while containing information pertaining to a specific area or application. 19.4 Missing Parts Information System Having access to a standard SAP S/4HANA report that contains information about missing parts of components in the production or process order is highly valuable to a company’s production planner. Production or process orders with missing compo- nents are shown with the system status MSPT. When a user executes the report, the sys- tem not only shows the requirements quantity but also the committed or available quantity, as well as the shortage quantity. The missing parts information system can be accessed for all of the different produc- tion types—that is, discrete manufacturing, process manufacturing, and REM. The menu path/transaction for each is listed here:  Discrete manufacturing Choose Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Missing Parts Info System or use Transaction CO24.  Process manufacturing Choose Logistics  Production—Process  Process Order  Reporting  Missing Parts Info System or use Transaction CO24. 19 Production Planning Reporting 1026  Repetitive manufacturing Choose Logistics  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Environment  Shop Floor Information System  Environment System  Missing Parts or use Transaction CO24. Figure 19.14 shows the missing parts information system report for Plnt 1710. Figure 19.14 Missing Parts Information System 19.5 Summary In this chapter, we covered a large number of features and functions of standard reports, as well as the standard analyses available. We also covered the actual order reports, such as automatic goods movement, capacities, document links, and XSteps. Finally, we discussed the missing parts information system, which is a helpful tool for the production planner. The next chapter covers standard planning analysis. © 1027 Chapter 20 Standard Planning Analysis Standard analyses in SAP S/4HANA provide great insights into many important logistics and supply chain management key performance indicators, which can help companies make informed decisions. Stan- dard analyses aren’t just available in production planning but also in all logistics and supply chain management areas of SAP S/4HANA. Standard analyses enable users to draw information from the available standard info structures in SAP S/4HANA. The difference between shop floor information system (reports) and standard analyses is that in information systems, the information is dynamic and changes when the user performs the relevant business functions. In stan- dard analyses, the system instead draws from historical information to enable the user to make better business decisions. In other words, the data and information from the information systems flow into the analyses. This chapter will cover some of the standard analyses for discrete manufacturing. We also review a few features and functionalities that are available only in analyses and not in information systems, such as time series analysis and ABC analysis. The concepts underlying standard analyses for discrete manufacturing that we discuss in this chap- ter will enable users to explore standard analysis of process manufacturing and REM, for which we’ve provided the necessary menu paths as well as the associated transac- tion codes. Note Refer to Table 20.1 through Table 20.3 for the transaction codes of the various available standard analyses in discrete manufacturing, process manufacturing, and REM, respec- tively. We’ll also provide the relevant SAP S/4HANA menu paths you can use to access the standard analyses of each production type. 20.1 Discrete Manufacturing/Production Order To access the standard analyses of discrete manufacturing, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Information System  Shop Floor Informa- tion System  Standard Analyses. Table 20.1 lists the transaction codes of various stan- 20 Standard Planning Analysis 1028 dard analyses that are available for discrete manufacturing, all of which we’ll discuss in the following sections. Standard analysis for material consumption (via Transaction MCRE) provides daily consumption figures (quantity and value) of components con- sumed in producing a product. After you gain an understanding of the various stan- dard analyses we cover in this chapter, as an exercise and to reinforce the learning, we recommend that you run the material consumption analysis and explore the various options it offers. 20.1.1 Work Center You access the screen shown in Figure 20.1 via Transaction MCP7. No information has been provided on the selection criteria screen for the standard analysis for the work center; we simply executed the report. Choose Switch drilldown to open the Drilldown screen and then select the Work Center radio button to change from the plant-level to the work center−level display of data. On the Drilldown screen, there are 2 work centers listed, along with target lead time (TgtLeadTm.) and actual lead time (ActLeadTm.) details. The smallest/minimum time unit is days. (Shorter intervals can be derived via decimal format.) Reqmts (capacity requirements), ActQueueTm (actual queue time), and Wait. Time. © 1031 20.1 Discrete Manufacturing/Production Order 20.1.3 Material Use Transaction MCP5 to view the material analysis, which covers the quantity and duration details of various materials. This includes the order quantity, the GR quantity, the scrap, and the deviations. The duration details consist of delivery time deviation, lead time deviation, and durations, such as target queue time and target execution time. 20.1.4 Production Order Figure 20.5 shows the standard analysis production orders (Transaction MCP3). Here, you select the key figure, Qty of goods recvd, by placing your cursor on it and navigat- ing to Edit  ABC Analysis from the menu bar. In the popup that appears, set Strategy for ABC analysis as Total Qty of goods recvd (%) and then choose Continue. Figure 20.5 Production Order Analysis The options in ABC analysis are as follows:  Total GR Quantity (%) The GR quantity is divided by the defined percentage.  Number Material (%) The number of materials is divided by the defined percentage.  GR Quantity (Absolute) The actual GR quantity is divided into A, B, and C segments. 20 Standard Planning Analysis 1032  Number Material The number of materials is divided into A, B, and C segments. ABC analysis enables a user to select the right strategy and then divide the key figures into the top three groupings. Furthermore, there’s flexibility to define what percentages of a key figure should be listed in the A, B, and C segments. You can overwrite the system-suggested segment sizes for A, B, and C. For this example, enter the A Segment as “70,” the B Segment as “20,” and the C Segment as “10.” These three entries cause the system to divide and display the Total GR Quantity (%) in such a way that the top 70% will be categorized in segment A, 20% will be categorized in seg- ment B, and the bottom 10% will be categorized in segment C, in a sequential order. Next, click Calculate to open SAP Business Graphics as a graphical representation of the ABC analysis. Then, click Graphics in the menu bar to make the screen shown in Figure 20.6 appear. Figure 20.6 ABC Analysis 20.1.5 Product Costs In Transaction MCRI, the screen shown in Figure 20.7 shows the standard analysis of the product cost. Choose the Value COCurr field and then choose Time Series to open the Time Series dialog box. A time series denotes the breakdown of a key figure of a characteristic, which in this case is Material. © 1033 20.3 Repetitive Manufacturing Figure 20.7 Product Costs Analysis 20.2 Process Manufacturing/Process Order To access the standard analyses of process manufacturing, you follow menu path Logis- tics  Production—Process  Process Order  Reporting  Shop Floor Information System. Figure 20.9 Parameters Selection Screen Figure 20.10 Standard Analysis Icons are now mostly replaced with icon texts that enable users to quickly find the rel- evant icons required to perform specific functions. With icon texts in place, users no longer have to hover over an icon to see an icon’s text and then decide whether it accesses the function they want to use. Because icon texts take up more screen space than the icons themselves, users must always look for not just the More in the menu bar (which is usually located on the far right-hand side of the screen) but also look at the very top of the screen to search for functions that aren’t visible in the main menu bar or at the bottom of the screen. The very top of the screen shows a menu bar with texts such as Operation analysis, Edit, Goto, and Extra. 20 Standard Planning Analysis 1036 20.4.3 Other Information Structures It’s also possible to navigate to the Other Info Structure screen within the standard analysis.
